topics.title: General Classification of Manufacturing Processes. Casting - Characteristics of Sand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Classification of Manufacturing Processes. Casting - Characteristics of Sand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Manufacturing Process — Formal Definition

A **Manufacturing Process** is a sequence of scientific and engineering operations through which raw materials (or partially processed workpieces) are converted into finished goods possessing the desired geometric shape, dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and mechanical/functional properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Manufacturing processes are the methods and techniques used to transform raw materials into finished products, encompassing a wide range of operations such as casting, forming, machining, joining, and additive manufacturing.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Kitchen Chef

Imagine a chef preparing a meal. The chef can either:
- **Shape raw dough** into a roti (analogous to **forming/shaping** processes),
- **Pour melted chocolate** into a mold to set (analogous to **casting**),
- **Cut vegetables** with a knife (analogous to **machining**),
- **Weld two metal pieces** together to create a structure (analogous to **joining**), or
- **Layer material** to build a 3D printed object layer-by-layer (analogous to **additive manufacturing**).

In every case, the **input** is raw material, the **process** adds value, and the **output** is a usable product — this is the essence of manufacturing.

### 1.3 Primary Need for Classification

The manufacturing world is vast. Without a structured classification, engineers cannot:
- Select the right process for a given design,
- Estimate cost, time, and feasibility, or
- Compare alternate production routes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 scheme, students are expected to **identify, classify, and select** manufacturing processes based on the type of raw material, geometry, tolerances, and production volume.

### 1.4 Casting — The Foundational Definition

**Casting** is a manufacturing process in which a liquid or molten material (typically metal, but also plastic or ceramic) is poured into a mould cavity of the desired shape and allowed to solidify. The solidified part, called a **cast**, is then removed from the mould.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Casting** is one of the **oldest** known manufacturing processes (dating back ~5000 years) and remains the **most economical** method for producing complex shapes, large components, and small batch quantities.

### 1.5 Sand — The Backbone of Foundries

**Moulding sand** (or foundry sand) is the most widely used mould material in metal casting, primarily because it is:
- **Abundant** (silica sand — SiO₂ — is the second most abundant mineral on Earth),
- **Refractory** (withstands high molten-metal temperatures),
- **Reusable** (after reclamation), and
- **Cheap** compared to metal/permanent moulds.

> [!NOTE]
> A typical **sand mould** consists of silica sand grains bonded together by a binder (clay, resin, or sodium silicate), with additives to enhance specific properties.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

### 2.1 General Classification of Manufacturing Processes

Manufacturing processes can be broadly classified into the following major categories:

#### A. Primary Forming / Shaping Processes
These processes convert raw material (ingots, billets, powders, liquid metal) into a **basic shape**.

| Sub-Process | Input State | Output State | Example |
|-------------|-------------|--------------|---------|
| **Casting** | Liquid | Solid (near-net shape) | Sand casting, die casting |
| **Forming** | Solid | Solid (deformed) | Forging, rolling, extrusion |
| **Powder Metallurgy** | Powder | Solid (sintered) | Sintered gears, bushings |
| **Additive Manufacturing** | Powder/Wire/Liquid | Solid (layered) | 3D Printing (SLM, FDM) |

#### B. Secondary Forming / Shaping Processes
These processes provide **further refinement** to primary shapes (better accuracy, finish, geometry).

- **Machining** (material removal): Turning, milling, drilling, grinding.
- **Joining** (assembly): Welding, brazing, soldering, adhesive bonding.
- **Heat Treatment** (property modification): Annealing, quenching, tempering.
- **Surface Treatment** (finishing/coating): Painting, electroplating, anodizing.

#### C. Tertiary / Assembly Processes
Final integration of components into a complete product (e.g., fasteners, snap fits).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Point:** Casting, forming, and powder metallurgy are **near-net-shape** processes — they require minimal material removal. Machining, on the other hand, is a **subtractive** process.

### 2.2 Classification of Casting Processes

Casting itself is sub-classified based on the **mould type** and **mould material**:

| Category | Mould Type | Reusability | Typical Application |
|----------|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Sand Casting** | Sand + binder | Single-use (expendable) | Large castings, low volume (engine blocks) |
| **Shell Moulding** | Resin-bonded sand shell | Single-use | Small-to-medium precision parts |
| **Investment Casting** | Wax pattern + ceramic shell | Single-use | Jewellery, turbine blades |
| **Die Casting** | Metal (steel) die | Permanent (reusable) | High-volume non-ferrous parts (aluminium housings) |
| **Permanent Mould Casting** | Metal mould | Permanent | Pistons, cylinder heads |
| **Centrifugal Casting** | Rotating mould | Permanent or sand | Pipes, tubes |
| **Continuous Casting** | Water-cooled mould | Permanent | Steel slabs, billets |

### 2.3 Characteristics of Moulding Sand (Detailed)

For high-quality castings, moulding sand must satisfy the following **seven key characteristics**:

#### 1. **Refractoriness**
- **Definition:** Ability of sand to withstand high temperatures of molten metal **without fusion, cracking, or sintering**.
- **Measured by:** Sintering point of sand.
- **Key Metric:** Pure silica sand sinters at approximately **1700 °C**, making it suitable for casting steels (melting ~1500 °C) and irons (~1200 °C).

#### 2. **Permeability**
- **Definition:** Ability of the sand mould to **allow escape of gases and steam** generated during pouring and solidification.
- **Cause of importance:** Trapped gases cause **blow holes** and **porosity** in the casting.
- **Permeability Number (N):**
  - Determined experimentally using a **permeability meter** (standard specimen under standard air pressure).

#### 3. **Cohesiveness / Green Strength**
- **Definition:** Ability of sand grains to **stick together** and retain mould shape against the force of molten metal.
- **Measured in:** Green Compression Strength (kg/cm² or N/cm²).

#### 4. **Green Permeability & Dry Strength**
- **Green state:** Mould is moist (before drying).
- **Dry state:** Mould is oven-dried — dry strength must be sufficient to handle the mould.

#### 5. **Flowability / Plasticity**
- **Definition:** Ability of sand to **flow and pack uniformly** around the pattern, replicating intricate details.
- **Higher plasticity** → better surface finish, sharper edges on the casting.

#### 6. **Collapsibility**
- **Definition:** Ability of the sand mould to **collapse (yield)** after the casting has solidified, allowing free contraction of the metal during cooling.
- **Poor collapsibility** → causes **hot tears / cracks** in the casting.

#### 7. **Adhesiveness** *(sometimes called tackiness)*
- **Definition:** Ability of sand to **adhere** to the pattern, boxing boards, or to itself.
- Excessive adhesion makes mould stripping difficult.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Sand Constituents

| Constituent | Function | Typical % (by mass) |
|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Silica Sand (SiO₂)** | Refractory base grain | 85 – 95 % |
| **Clay (Bentonite)** | Binder — provides green/dry strength | 5 – 15 % |
| **Water (Moisture)** | Activates clay binder, gives plasticity | 3 – 6 % |
| **Additives** (coal dust, dextrin, silica flour) | Improve surface finish, reduce metal penetration | 0 – 5 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The acronym **"RPCC-FAC"** captures the seven sand characteristics — **R**efractoriness, **P**ermeability, **C**ohesiveness, **C**ollapsibility, **F**lowability, **A**dhesiveness, and (d)ry strength.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Sand casting** accounts for nearly **60 – 70 %** of all castings produced globally.
- The **automotive industry** alone consumes millions of tonnes of moulding sand annually for engine blocks, cylinder heads, and gearbox housings.
- Sand characteristics directly control **casting yield, surface finish, and scrap rate** — making this topic a frequent **KTU exam favourite**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Numerical Derivations

### 3.1 Permeability Number — Derivation and Use

The **Permeability Number (N)** of moulding sand is defined as the **rate of flow of air (in cm³/s)** through a standard sand specimen under a **standard pressure** (10 g/cm² ≈ 980 Pa).

#### Definition (Standard Formula):

$$N = \frac{V \times H}{A \times t \times P}$$

Where:
- $N$ = Permeability number (dimensionless)
- $V$ = Volume of air passed through the specimen (cm³)
- $H$ = Height of the specimen (cm)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the specimen (cm²)
- $t$ = Time of air flow (seconds)
- $P$ = Pressure of air (g/cm² or N/m²)

#### Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation

**Step 1 — Darcy's Law Foundation:**
The volumetric flow rate $Q$ of a fluid (air) through a porous medium is given by Darcy's law:

$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

Where $k$ is permeability, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, $L$ is length, and $\Delta P$ is pressure drop.

**Step 2 — Rearranging for Permeability:**

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

**Step 3 — Casting-Practice Simplification:**
For foundry testing, $Q = V/t$ and $L = H$:

$$k = \frac{V \cdot \mu \cdot H}{A \cdot t \cdot \Delta P}$$

**Step 4 — Defining the Foundry Permeability Number:**
Standardise the viscosity and pressure by fixing $P = 10 \text{ g/cm}^2$ and air at room temperature, giving the empirical **foundry permeability number** $N$:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{V \times H}{A \times t \times P}}$$

#### Typical Permeability Number Values (for KTU reference)

| Sand Condition | Permeability Number (N) |
|----------------|------------------------|
| Very low (fine, packed) | 0 – 30 |
| Low | 30 – 50 |
| Medium (typical green sand) | 50 – 120 |
| High (open, coarse) | 120 – 200 |
| Very high (open, dry sand) | > 200 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Permeability is inversely related to moisture and clay content.** Excess clay → blocks pores → low permeability → blow holes in casting.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A standard permeability test specimen of diameter **5 cm** and height **5 cm** is subjected to an air pressure of **10 g/cm²**. In **30 seconds**, **300 cm³** of air passes through. Calculate the **permeability number**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**
- Diameter, $d = 5$ cm  →  Radius, $r = 2.5$ cm
- Height, $H = 5$ cm
- Volume of air, $V = 300$ cm³
- Time, $t = 30$ s
- Pressure, $P = 10$ g/cm²

**Step 2 — Compute cross-sectional area $A$:**

$$A = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (2.5)^2 = \pi \times 6.25 = 19.635 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the permeability formula:**

$$N = \frac{V \times H}{A \times t \times P}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$N = \frac{300 \times 5}{19.635 \times 30 \times 10}$$

$$N = \frac{1500}{5890.5}$$

$$N \approx 0.2547$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

$$\boxed{N \approx 0.255 \;\; \text{(very low permeability)}}$$

Such a low permeability suggests the sand is **too fine / too moist** and is **unsuitable for ferrous castings** — the mould would trap gases, producing defective castings. The foundry operator should reduce moisture content or use coarser sand grains.

### 3.3 Sieve Analysis — Grain Fineness Number (GFN)

The **Grain Fineness Number (GFN)** is a weighted average indicating the overall fineness of moulding sand. The KTU Board frequently asks for GFN calculation.

**Standard Formula:**

$$GFN = \frac{\sum (W_i \times M_i)}{\sum W_i}$$

Where:
- $W_i$ = Weight of sand retained on sieve $i$
- $M_i$ = Multiplier of that sieve (from AFS standard)

**Step-by-Step Sieve Analysis Procedure:**

1. **Step 1:** Take a known mass (e.g., **50 g**) of oven-dried sand.
2. **Step 2:** Pour the sand through a stack of standard sieves (from coarse at top to fine at bottom).
3. **Step 3:** Weigh the sand retained on each sieve.
4. **Step 4:** Multiply each retained weight by its sieve multiplier.
5. **Step 5:** Sum the products, then divide by the total weight.

#### Example AFS Sieve Multipliers (commonly used)

| US Sieve No. | Multiplier (M) |
|--------------|----------------|
| 6 | 3 |
| 12 | 7 |
| 20 | 20 |
| 30 | 50 |
| 40 | 70 |
| 50 | 100 |
| 70 | 140 |
| 100 | 200 |
| 140 | 300 |
| 200 | 350 |
| 270 | 510 |
| Pan | (no multiplier — fines) |

> [!NOTE]
> A typical **medium-grain moulding sand** has GFN between **50 and 60**. A very fine sand (used for intricate castings) has GFN between **100 and 140**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Manufacturing Processes

```mermaid
    rootA[Manufacturing Processes] --> nodeB[Primary Forming]
    rootA --> nodeC[Secondary Forming]
    rootA --> nodeD[Tertiary Assembly]
    rootA --> nodeE[Property Modifying]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Casting]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Forming]

    nodeB --> nodeB4[Additive Manufacturing]

    nodeB1 --> nodeB1a[Sand Casting]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1b[Die Casting]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1c[Investment Casting]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1d[Centrifugal Casting]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1e[Continuous Casting]

    nodeB2 --> nodeB2a[Forging]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2b[Rolling]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2c[Extrusion]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2d[Drawing]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Machining Subtractive]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Joining]

    nodeC1 --> nodeC1a[Turning]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1b[Milling]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1c[Drilling]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1d[Grinding]

    nodeC2 --> nodeC2a[Welding]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2b[Brazing]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2c[Soldering]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Mechanical Fastening]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Adhesive Bonding]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Heat Treatment]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Surface Treatment]
```

### 4.2 Sand Casting Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    patA[Pattern Preparation] --> patB[Mould Making]
    sandA[Moulding Sand + Binder + Water] --> patB
    patB --> patC[Pouring Molten Metal]
    metalA[Melting Furnace] --> patC
    patC --> patD[Solidification in Mould]
    patD --> patE[Shakeout and Cleaning]
    patE --> patF[Finished Casting]
    patE --> sandRec[Sand Reclamation]
    sandRec --> sandA
```

### 4.3 Functional Mapping — Moulding Sand Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    charRoot[Moulding Sand Characteristics] --> refN[Refractoriness]
    charRoot --> permN[Permeability]
    charRoot --> cohN[Cohesiveness Green Strength]
    charRoot --> colN[Collapsibility]
    charRoot --> flowN[Flowability]
    charRoot --> adhN[Adhesiveness]
    charRoot --> dryN[Dry Strength]

    refN --> refN1[Withstands Molten Metal Temp]
    refN1 --> refN1a[Prevents Fusion and Sintering of Sand]

    permN --> permN1[Allows Gas Escape]
    permN1 --> permN1a[Prevents Blow Holes and Porosity]

    cohN --> cohN1[Sand Grains Stick Together]
    cohN1 --> cohN1a[Retains Mould Shape Under Pouring Force]

    colN --> colN1[Mould Yields on Cooling]
    colN1 --> colN1a[Prevents Hot Tears in Casting]

    flowN --> flowN1[Uniform Packing Around Pattern]
    flowN1 --> flowN1a[Sharp Edges and Good Surface Finish]

    adhN --> adhN1[Bonds to Pattern and Boxing Board]
    adhN1 --> adhN1a[Maintains Mould Integrity During Handling]

    dryN --> dryN1[Strength After Oven Drying]
    dryN1 --> dryN1a[Allows Dry Sand Mould Handling]
```

### 4.4 Permeability Test Apparatus — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    airA[Air Supply Tank] -->|Regulated Pressure| cylA[Cylinder with Sand Specimen]
    cylA -->|Air Passes Through| manA[Manometer Reads Pressure]
    cylA -->|Volume V in Time t| resA[Compute Permeability N]
    manA --> resA
    resA --> outA[Permeability Number N]
```

> [!NOTE]
> For a physical schematic (e.g., permeability meter, sand rammer), Mermaid cannot render actual engineering drawings. The block-level functional architecture above correctly maps the interactions of components used in standard foundry sand testing per AFS / IS standards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define **Casting** as a manufacturing process. List **any four** types of casting processes with one application each.

**Model Answer:**
Casting is a manufacturing process in which molten metal is poured into a mould cavity of the desired shape, allowed to solidify, and then removed to obtain a finished part. Four types of casting:

| Type | Application |
|------|-------------|
| Sand Casting | Engine blocks, large cast-iron parts |
| Die Casting | Aluminium housings, carburettor bodies |
| Investment Casting | Turbine blades, jewellery |
| Centrifugal Casting | Pipes, cylinder liners |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Mention the **essential characteristics** of moulding sand used in foundries.

**Model Answer:**
The essential characteristics of moulding sand are:
1. **Refractoriness** — ability to withstand high molten metal temperatures.
2. **Permeability** — ability to allow gases/steam to escape.
3. **Cohesiveness (Green Strength)** — ability of sand grains to stick together.
4. **Collapsibility** — ability to yield as the casting cools, preventing hot tears.
5. **Flowability** — ability to pack uniformly around the pattern.
6. **Dry Strength** — strength of the mould after drying.
7. **Adhesiveness** — ability to adhere to the pattern and boxing boards.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — June 2023 — 14 Marks]**
> *(a)* Classify the **manufacturing processes** in detail with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* With neat sketches, explain the **sand casting process** step-by-step. List the **advantages and limitations** of sand casting. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Manufacturing Processes**

Manufacturing processes are broadly classified into the following categories:

1. **Primary Forming / Shaping Processes** — convert raw material into a basic shape:
   - **Casting** (e.g., sand casting, die casting) — input is liquid, output is solid.
   - **Forming / Plastic Deformation** (e.g., forging, rolling, extrusion, drawing) — input is solid, output is deformed solid.
   - **Powder Metallurgy** (e.g., sintering) — input is powder.
   - **Additive Manufacturing / 3D Printing** — material is added layer-by-layer.

2. **Secondary Forming Processes** — refine the basic shape:
   - **Material Removal (Machining)** — turning, milling, drilling, grinding.
   - **Joining Processes** — welding, brazing, soldering, adhesive bonding.

3. **Property Modification Processes:**
   - **Heat Treatment** — annealing, normalizing, hardening, tempering.
   - **Surface Treatment** — plating, anodizing, painting.

4. **Assembly / Tertiary Processes:**
   - Fasteners (bolts, screws), snap fits, rivets, etc.

**(b) Sand Casting Process**

The sequence of operations is:

1. **Pattern Making** — wooden or metal replica of the final part.
2. **Mould Preparation** — pattern is placed in a flask and sand is rammed around it.
3. **Core Making** — sand cores are placed to form internal cavities.
4. **Pouring** — molten metal is poured into the mould cavity via the sprue.
5. **Solidification** — metal cools and solidifies inside the mould.
6. **Shakeout** — the mould is broken to release the solidified casting.
7. **Cleaning / Fettling** — sand, risers, sprues, and gates are removed; casting is trimmed, ground, and inspected.

**Advantages of Sand Casting:**
- Can produce **very large** castings (tonnes).
- Suitable for **low-volume / one-off** production.
- Works with **almost all metals** (ferrous and non-ferrous).
- **Low tooling cost** compared to die casting.
- Complex shapes with internal cavities possible (using cores).

**Limitations of Sand Casting:**
- **Lower dimensional accuracy** (±0.5 to ±1.5 mm) than die casting.
- **Rougher surface finish** (Ra 12.5 to 25 µm).
- **Lower production rate** — sand moulds are single-use.
- Requires **skilled labour** for moulding and ramming.

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 — 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**
> *(a)* Explain the various **characteristics of moulding sand** with their importance in foundry practice. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A standard permeability test was conducted on a sand specimen of **diameter 5.08 cm** and **height 5.08 cm** at an air pressure of **10 g/cm²**. **2000 cm³** of air passed through the specimen in **25 seconds**. Calculate the **permeability number** and comment on the suitability of the sand for casting. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Characteristics of Moulding Sand (Detailed)**

*(Provide the full 7-characteristic description from Section 2.3 above — Refractoriness, Permeability, Cohesiveness, Collapsibility, Flowability, Adhesiveness, Dry Strength — along with their importance and how defects arise if a property is inadequate.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Step 1 — Identify data:**
- Diameter, $d = 5.08$ cm  →  $r = 2.54$ cm
- Height, $H = 5.08$ cm
- Volume, $V = 2000$ cm³
- Time, $t = 25$ s
- Pressure, $P = 10$ g/cm²

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (2.54)^2 = \pi \times 6.4516 = 20.268 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply permeability formula:**

$$N = \frac{V \times H}{A \times t \times P} = \frac{2000 \times 5.08}{20.268 \times 25 \times 10}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 2000 \times 5.08 = 10160$$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**

$$\text{Denominator} = 20.268 \times 25 \times 10 = 20.268 \times 250 = 5067$$

**Step 6 — Final calculation:**

$$N = \frac{10160}{5067} \approx 2.005$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 2.0}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**

A permeability number of **~2.0** is **extremely low** (very fine and packed sand). This sand is **not suitable** for general foundry use — gases generated during pouring would not escape, leading to **blow holes and porosity**. The foundry operator should use **coarser sand grains** or **reduce moisture content** to improve permeability.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and lose marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Green Strength" with "Dry Strength":** Green strength is the strength of the moist mould; dry strength is after oven drying. Mixing them up = **1 mark lost**.
> 2. **Wrong formula for Permeability Number:** The most common error is **forgetting the pressure term $P$** in the denominator. Always re-check the formula before writing.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in numericals:** Mixing cm with mm, or using $N/m^2$ with $g/cm^2$ without conversion. **Always write units explicitly.**
> 4. **Omitting the interpretation step:** KTU examiners award **at least 1–2 marks** for the *interpretation* of a numerical result (e.g., "this permeability is low, hence sand is unsuitable for ferrous castings"). Students who stop at the number lose easy marks.
> 5. **Listing characteristics without explanation:** Simply naming the seven sand properties is not enough — examiners expect a **one-line importance** for each.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Manufacturing Process** = transformation of raw material into a finished, usable product with desired geometry, accuracy, and properties.
- **Primary classification:** Primary Forming → Secondary Forming → Tertiary / Assembly.
- **Casting** is a **near-net-shape** primary forming process using a mould.
- **Sand casting** is the **most widely used** casting process — uses expendable sand moulds.
- **Other casting types:** Die, investment, centrifugal, continuous, permanent mould, shell moulding.
- **Moulding sand characteristics** (remember the mnemonic **"RPCC-FAD"** or **"RPCC-FAC"**):
  - **R**efractoriness — withstands temperature.
  - **P**ermeability — gas escape.
  - **C**ohesiveness (Green Strength) — sticks together.
  - **C**ollapsibility — yields on cooling.
  - **F**lowability — packs uniformly.
  - **A**dhesiveness — sticks to pattern/board.
  - **D**ry Strength — strength after drying.
- **Sand constituents:** Silica (85–95 %) + Clay/Bentonite (5–15 %) + Water (3–6 %) + Additives (0–5 %).
- **Permeability Number formula:** $N = \dfrac{V \times H}{A \times t \times P}$ — **always include $P$ in the denominator.**
- **Typical GFN:** 50–60 (medium), 100–140 (fine).
- **Defects due to poor sand properties:**
  - Low permeability → **blow holes**
  - Low refractoriness → **sand fusion / metal penetration**
  - Low collapsibility → **hot tears / cracks**
  - Low green strength → **mould collapse during pouring**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "list the characteristics of moulding sand" — write all **seven** and pair each with a **one-line significance**. Examiners love this presentation and award full marks consistently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design of Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Pattern

A **pattern** is a replica of the final casting (with certain necessary modifications and allowances) used to produce the cavity in the mould sand. The mould cavity, after being filled with molten metal, solidifies to form the desired casting. The pattern is, therefore, the fundamental tooling around which the entire sand moulding process revolves.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A pattern is defined as a physical model of the casting used to create the mould cavity. It is made of suitable material and is provided with **necessary allowances** so that the resulting casting, after solidification and cooling, conforms to the required dimensions and quality.

### Functions of a Pattern
The pattern performs four critical engineering functions in a foundry:
1. **Imparts Shape:** Provides the external geometry of the casting by creating the mould cavity.
2. **Imparts Surface Texture:** Transfers a controlled surface finish from the pattern to the casting.
3. **Provides Means of Core Support:** Through the use of **core prints**, the pattern positions and supports sand cores within the mould.
4. **Provides Means of Ejecting the Casting:** Features like **draft (taper)** and **parting line** allow easy withdrawal of the pattern from the compacted sand without damaging the mould.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making a **snowball mould** in the sand at the beach. You press a plastic mould (the pattern) into wet sand, lift it out, and you get a perfect cavity. Now pour molten wax or chocolate into it, and once it cools and hardens, you get the shape of the mould.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Analogy:** The pattern is like a **cookie cutter** for castings. Just as a cookie cutter is slightly larger than the cookie (because dough spreads) and slightly tapered (so you can push it out cleanly), a pattern is made *bigger* and *tapered* compared to the final casting. The "bigger" part compensates for metal shrinkage, and the "taper" lets you pull the cutter out of the dough (or sand) without ruining the shape.

### Why Patterns Have Allowances — The Core Intuition
- **Shrinkage:** When hot chocolate cools, it shrinks. Liquid metal behaves the same way — it **shrinks as it solidifies**. So the mould cavity must be larger than the final desired part.
- **Draft/Taper:** If you tried to pull a straight-walled bucket out of compacted sand, sand would crumble. Tapering the walls lets sand "slide" past the pattern cleanly.
- **Machining:** The casting is usually rough; we leave an extra layer of metal so a machinist can cut precise features later.
- **Shake (Rap):** When the pattern is "rapped" (struck sharply) before withdrawal, it slightly enlarges the cavity.

### Standard Pattern Metrics (KTU High-Yield Values)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Material Context |
|---|---|---|
| Shrinkage allowance (Grey Cast Iron) | **1.0 %** | All dimensions |
| Shrinkage allowance (Aluminium) | **1.3 – 1.5 %** | All dimensions |
| Draft allowance (Hand moulding) | **1° – 2°** | Vertical walls |
| Draft allowance (Machine moulding) | **0.5° – 1°** | Vertical walls |
| Machining allowance (Sand casting) | **1.5 – 6 mm** | Machined surfaces |
| Shake allowance | **0.25 – 0.5 mm** | All dimensions |
| Surface roughness transferred | **12.5 – 25 μm Ra** | Depends on pattern finish |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard KTU mark distribution: A 14-mark question on **Pattern Design** typically combines (a) allowance calculations (7 marks) + (b) pattern material selection OR core print design (7 marks).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Patterns

Patterns are classified based on **construction, durability, and service life**.

### A. Based on Construction
1. **Single Piece / Solid Pattern (One-Piece Pattern)**
   - Pattern is one solid unit.
   - Used for simple shapes with no internal cavities.
   - Mould is made in one flask; pattern is withdrawn from one side.
   - *Drawback:* Cannot have complex internal features; parting line location is limited.

2. **Two-Piece / Split Pattern (Loose Piece Pattern)**
   - Pattern is divided into two halves: **cope half** and **drag half**.
   - Each half is mounted on separate match plates or plates.
   - Most common pattern type; used for castings of moderate complexity.

3. **Multi-Piece / Follow-Board Pattern**
   - Pattern divided into more than two pieces, often with a wooden base (follow board) that carries the pattern halves.
   - Used when the parting line is irregular or curved.

4. **Match Plate Pattern**
   - Both cope and drag halves are mounted on opposite sides of a single metal plate (**match plate**).
   - Plate carries **runner, gate, and sprue** details.
   - Ideal for **machine moulding** and mass production.

5. **Cope and Drag Pattern**
   - Cope and drag halves mounted on separate plates.
   - Used in high-production machine moulding lines.

6. **Gated Pattern (Stack Moulding Pattern)**
   - Several patterns mounted on a single plate.
   - Used in high-volume production.

7. **Sweep Pattern**
   - A wooden board (sweep) is rotated about an axis to sweep the mould cavity profile.
   - Used for **large circular/axisymmetric castings** (e.g., large gear blanks).

8. **Skeleton Pattern**
   - A wooden or metal frame ("skeleton") shaped to the contour of the casting, filled with sand and struck off to form the mould.
   - Used for very large castings where a solid pattern is uneconomical.

### B. Based on Durability / Material
1. **Expendable (Single-use):** Wax, foam (used in **investment casting** and **lost-foam casting**).
2. **Temporary (≤ 100 uses):** Wood.
3. **Semi-permanent (100 – 1000 uses):** Aluminium, plastics (epoxy, polyurethane).
4. **Permanent (> 1000 uses):** Cast iron, brass, steel.

### C. Based on Service / Application
- Loose piece, loose pin, gated, stripper, split, match plate, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Classify patterns based on construction with one example each."* — Always list **at least 6 types** with a **sketch description** for full marks.

## 2.2 Pattern Materials — Comparative Analysis

| Material | Working Life | Surface Finish | Cost | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wood** | < 100 uses | Moderate | Low | Prototypes, small batches |
| **Cast Iron / Steel** | > 1000 uses | Excellent | High | Mass production, machine moulding |
| **Aluminium** | 500–1000 uses | Good | Medium | Medium batch production |
| **Brass** | 1000+ uses | Excellent | High | Intricate patterns |
| **Plastics (Epoxy / PU)** | 100–500 uses | Very Good | Medium | Medium batch, complex geometry |
| **Wax / Foam (Expendable)** | 1 use | Excellent | Low | Investment / Lost-foam casting |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark questions, justify the material selection based on: **Production volume, required surface finish, dimensional accuracy, and cost of the casting.**

## 2.3 Pattern Allowances — The Engineering Core

Allowances are the **deliberate dimensional deviations** provided on the pattern to compensate for physical phenomena occurring during moulding, solidification, and post-casting operations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Students often confuse *shrinkage* with *distortion* allowance. Shrinkage is **uniform**; distortion is **directional (non-uniform)**.

### Types of Pattern Allowances

#### (i) Shrinkage Allowance (Contraction Allowance)
- **Definition:** Extra dimension provided on the pattern to compensate for the volumetric contraction of metal during solidification and cooling to room temperature.
- **Direction:** Applied to **all linear dimensions** of the pattern.
- **Magnitude:** Depends on the **casting alloy** and **restraining conditions**.

$$L_{pattern} = L_{casting} \times \left(1 + \frac{S}{100}\right)$$

where:
- $L_{pattern}$ = Dimension on the pattern (mm)
- $L_{casting}$ = Required final dimension on the casting (mm)
- $S$ = Shrinkage percentage of the alloy

**Typical Shrinkage Values (must memorise for KTU):**

| Alloy | Shrinkage Allowance (%) |
|---|---|
| Grey Cast Iron | 1.0 |
| White Cast Iron | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Malleable Cast Iron | 1.0 |
| Steel | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Brass | 1.5 |
| Bronze | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Aluminium | 1.3 – 1.5 |
| Magnesium | 1.5 – 1.8 |

#### (ii) Draft (Taper) Allowance
- **Definition:** Taper provided on the vertical faces of the pattern to enable easy withdrawal from the sand mould without damaging the mould walls.
- **Formula (in terms of taper angle):**

$$D = L \times \tan(\alpha)$$

where:
- $D$ = Draft allowance (mm) on the vertical wall
- $L$ = Vertical height of the pattern face (mm)
- $\alpha$ = Draft angle (degrees)

- **Typical values:**
  - Hand moulding: $1° \leq \alpha \leq 2°$
  - Machine moulding: $0.5° \leq \alpha \leq 1°$
  - Internal surfaces: slightly more draft (≈ 2°–3°) as sand is less compacted there.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **internal cavities** (e.g., inside of a hollow box), the draft is added so the cavity **widens upward** when withdrawn through the cope.

#### (iii) Machining (Finish) Allowance
- **Definition:** Extra metal provided on the casting surfaces that are to be machined later. This compensates for the rough surface of the casting and ensures a clean machined surface.
- **Typical values:**
  - Light castings: 1.5 – 3 mm
  - Medium castings: 3 – 5 mm
  - Heavy castings: 5 – 12 mm

#### (iv) Distortion (Camber) Allowance
- **Definition:** Allowance provided to **counteract distortion** of the casting during cooling. The pattern is made **opposite** to the expected direction of warping.
- **Example:** A flat plate that tends to warp **concave upward** at the centre is given a **convex upward** camber on the pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> Distortion is **non-uniform** and **directional**. A cast-iron plate with both ends restrained will bow **upward** in the centre during cooling — so the pattern is made slightly **bowed downward**.

#### (v) Shake (Rap) Allowance
- **Definition:** When the pattern is rapped (tapped sharply) to loosen it from the mould before withdrawal, the cavity becomes **slightly larger** than the pattern.
- **Typical value:** 0.25 – 0.5 mm on every dimension.
- *Note:* This allowance is **subtractive** in the design (i.e., compensates for the mould cavity being *bigger* than the pattern).

#### (vi) Parting Line Allowance
- **Definition:** When the parting line is **not flat** (e.g., curved or stepped), extra material is provided so the casting edge at the parting line is **clean and complete**.
- The pattern is made slightly **proud** at the parting line, so any sand imperfections or slight mismatch are compensated.

#### (vii) Core (Sand Core) Allowance / Core Prints
- **Definition:** A projection on the pattern that produces a seat (print) in the mould to **support and locate the sand core**.
- **Types:** Horizontal core print, vertical core print, balanced core print.
- Core prints are **not** allowances per se, but are critical design features.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise this table — it covers 80% of pattern design numerical problems in KTU exams.

| Allowance | Direction | Symbol | Typical Range | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shrinkage | All linear dims | $S$ | 0.8 – 2.0 % | $L_p = L_c (1 + S/100)$ |
| Draft (Taper) | Vertical walls | $\alpha$ | 0.5° – 3° | $D = L \tan(\alpha)$ |
| Machining | Machined surfaces | $M$ | 1.5 – 6 mm | $L_p = L_c + M$ |
| Distortion | Directional | — | 1.5 – 3 mm | Engineering judgement |
| Shake | All linear dims | — | 0.25 – 0.5 mm | $L_{mould} = L_p + \delta_{shake}$ |
| Parting Line | At parting line | — | 0.5 – 1.5 mm | Visual offset |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pattern design is **not** an academic exercise — it directly determines:
1. **Dimensional accuracy** of the final casting (e.g., automotive engine block, pump housing).
2. **Yield rate** in mass production (correct draft → less mould damage).
3. **Cost of post-casting machining** (machining allowance too low → scrapped parts).
4. **Core positioning** (incorrect core print → misaligned bores in hydraulic cylinders).

> [!TIP]
> Industry example: In a mass-producing **IC engine cylinder block** foundry, an error of just 0.3 % in shrinkage allowance can shift the bore centreline, causing assembly failure with the piston. Hence, accurate pattern design is a **multi-million-rupee decision** in automotive foundries.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation — Pattern Dimension from Casting Dimension

### Problem Context
A KTU student is asked: *"Design a pattern for a grey cast iron rectangular block of size 200 mm × 100 mm × 50 mm. Apply the following allowances: shrinkage = 1.0 %, draft = 1.5°, machining = 3 mm on top surface, shake = 0.3 mm on all sides. The block is to be cast horizontally (200 × 100 face down)."*

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the parting line and pattern orientation.**
The casting rests on the 200 mm × 100 mm face. The parting line lies on the mid-plane of the 50 mm height.

**Step 2 — Apply Shrinkage Allowance.**
Shrinkage is added to **all linear dimensions**.

$$L_{after\_shrinkage} = L_{casting} \times \left(1 + \frac{S}{100}\right)$$

For length (200 mm):
$$L_1 = 200 \times \left(1 + \frac{1.0}{100}\right) = 200 \times 1.01 = 202.0 \text{ mm}$$

For width (100 mm):
$$W_1 = 100 \times 1.01 = 101.0 \text{ mm}$$

For height (50 mm):
$$H_1 = 50 \times 1.01 = 50.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Machining Allowance (3 mm on the top surface).**
Machining allowance is added to the **height** on the top face only (since the bottom is the unmachined mould surface).

$$H_2 = H_1 + 3 = 50.5 + 3.0 = 53.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Draft (Taper) Allowance to vertical walls.**
The vertical walls are the **four sides of height 50 mm** (in the moulded direction). Draft is given on these walls.

For draft angle $\alpha = 1.5°$:

$$D = H_1 \times \tan(\alpha) = 50.5 \times \tan(1.5°)$$

$$\tan(1.5°) = 0.02619$$

$$D = 50.5 \times 0.02619 = 1.322 \text{ mm}$$

**Convention for draft application:**
- **External walls:** draft added outward, so the pattern is **slightly wider at the parting line** than at the top. (Top width < parting-line width by $D$.)
- **Internal walls:** draft added inward.

So the new length (parallel to parting line) becomes:
$$L_2 = L_1 + 2D = 202.0 + 2 \times 1.322 = 204.644 \text{ mm}$$

(The factor of 2 accounts for draft on both sides of the part.)

Similarly, the new width:
$$W_2 = W_1 + 2D = 101.0 + 2 \times 1.322 = 103.644 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Shake Allowance.**
Shake allowance enlarges the mould cavity when the pattern is rapped. Since the pattern itself is **unchanged** by shake (it is the cavity that grows), and shake compensates for this growth, we **subtract** shake allowance from the pattern dimension to keep the final casting size accurate.

$$L_{pattern} = L_2 - \delta_{shake} = 204.644 - 0.3 = 204.344 \text{ mm}$$

$$W_{pattern} = W_2 - 0.3 = 103.344 \text{ mm}$$

$$H_{pattern} = H_2 - 0.3 = 53.2 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exact sign convention for shake allowance is debated. **KTU convention (most accepted):** shake allowance is **subtracted** from the pattern dimension, because the shake enlarges the mould cavity, so the pattern should be made *smaller* to compensate.

### Step 6 — Final Pattern Dimensions Summary

$$L_{pattern} \approx 204.34 \text{ mm}, \quad W_{pattern} \approx 103.34 \text{ mm}, \quad H_{pattern} \approx 53.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification (reverse direction):**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{casting} &= \frac{L_{pattern} + \delta_{shake}}{1 + S/100} - M_{effective} \\
&= \frac{204.344 + 0.3}{1.01} - 0 \\
&= \frac{204.644}{1.01} \approx 202.62 \text{ mm (excluding draft correction)}
\end{aligned}$$

(Note: A full reverse verification would also de-correct the draft taper; in practice, KTU problems stop at Step 5.)

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Allowance Application Table

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the most important visual aid for KTU problems. Memorise which allowances apply to **which dimensions** and in **which direction (add or subtract)**.

| Allowance | Applies to | Direction | Adds or Subtracts? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shrinkage | All linear dims | Uniform | Adds (+S%) |
| Draft (Taper) | Vertical walls | Direction-dependent | Adds (with factor of 2 for parallel dims) |
| Machining | Surfaces to be machined | One-sided | Adds (+M) |
| Distortion | Specific to geometry | Direction-specific | Adds opposite to warp direction |
| Shake | All linear dims | Uniform | Subtracts (−shake) |
| Parting Line | At parting line | Local | Adds (+PL) |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Core Print Length

A **core print** is the projection on the pattern that forms a seat in the mould to support a sand core. Its design ensures the core is correctly located.

**Problem:** A horizontal cylindrical core of diameter 80 mm supports a casting. Calculate the core print length if the **core print diameter** is 82 mm and the **length-to-diameter ratio** is to be 1.5 for stability.

**Step 1 — Core print diameter (includes allowance for core movement):**

$$D_{print} = D_{core} + 2 \text{ mm (allowance)} = 80 + 2 = 82 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Core print length from L/D ratio:**

$$L_{print} = 1.5 \times D_{print} = 1.5 \times 82 = 123 \text{ mm}$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU often tests: *"Why is the core print length kept > 1.5 × diameter?"* — Answer: to provide **buoyancy resistance** and prevent the core from **floating** under the metallostatic pressure of the molten metal. (Core is held by **chaplets** in some cases.)

## 3.4 Composite Worked Example — Pattern with Hole and Boss

**Problem:** A cast iron flange (final dimension: outer $\phi$ 200 mm, hole $\phi$ 60 mm, height 80 mm) is to be cast. The hole is to be made by a **horizontal dry sand core** with a **core print** of 25 mm length on each side. The pattern is split at mid-height. Allowances: shrinkage = 1 %, draft = 1.5°, machining = 3 mm on top and bottom faces, shake = 0.4 mm. Calculate the pattern dimensions.

**Step 1 — Apply shrinkage to the hole diameter:**

$$D_{hole,pattern} = 60 \times 1.01 = 60.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply core print length (subtractive on pattern, as it is occupied by core):**
The pattern has a "core print" projection of 25 mm on each side of the hole. The core print diameter is slightly larger than the core.

$$D_{core\_print} = D_{core} + 2 \text{ mm} = 62 + 2 = 64 \text{ mm}$$

(where $D_{core} = 60.6$ mm after shrinkage).

**Step 3 — Apply shrinkage to outer diameter:**

$$OD_{pattern} = 200 \times 1.01 = 202.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply draft on outer walls (height 80 mm → half is 40 mm above parting line):**

$$D_{draft} = 40 \times \tan(1.5°) = 40 \times 0.02619 = 1.048 \text{ mm}$$

$$OD_{pattern,top} = 202.0 - 2 \times 1.048 = 199.9 \text{ mm (top) - actually 202.0}$$
$$OD_{pattern,PL} = 202.0 \text{ mm (at parting line)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply machining on height:**

$$H_{pattern} = (80 \times 1.01) + 2 \times 3 = 80.8 + 6 = 86.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Apply shake (subtractive):**

$$H_{final} = 86.8 - 0.4 = 86.4 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This completes the rigorous design. KTU 14-mark questions expect a labelled sketch of the pattern showing all allowances.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pattern Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Casting Drawing Received] --> B{Identify Casting Geometry}
    B --> C[Decide Pattern Type: Single / Split / Match Plate]
    C --> D[Select Pattern Material: Wood / Al / Steel / Plastic]
    D --> E[Identify Parting Line Location]
    E --> F[Apply Shrinkage Allowance to All Dims]
    F --> G[Apply Machining Allowance on Machined Faces]
    G --> H[Apply Draft Taper on Vertical Walls]
    H --> I[Add Core Prints for Internal Cavities]
    I --> J[Apply Distortion Allowance if Geometry Demands]
    J --> K[Subtract Shake Allowance from Pattern]
    K --> L[Verify Pattern Dimensions via Reverse Calculation]
    L --> M{Dimensions OK?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Generate Pattern Manufacturing Drawing]
    M -- No --> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Patterns] --> P1[Based on Construction]
    P --> P2[Based on Material]
    P --> P3[Based on Durability]

    P1 --> P1A[Single Piece]
    P1 --> P1B[Two Piece Split]
    P1 --> P1C[Multi Piece]
    P1 --> P1D[Match Plate]
    P1 --> P1E[Follow Board]
    P1 --> P1F[Sweep]
    P1 --> P1G[Skeleton]

    P2 --> P2A[Wood]
    P2 --> P2B[Metal: Al / Cast Iron / Brass]
    P2 --> P2C[Plastic: Epoxy / PU]
    P2 --> P2D[Expendable: Wax / Foam]

    P3 --> P3A[Temporary: under 100 uses]
    P3 --> P3B[Semi Permanent: 100 to 1000 uses]
    P3 --> P3C[Permanent: over 1000 uses]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Pattern Allowances Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AL[Pattern Allowances] --> AL1[Shrinkage Allowance]
    AL --> AL2[Draft Allowance]
    AL --> AL3[Machining Allowance]
    AL --> AL4[Distortion Allowance]
    AL --> AL5[Shake Allowance]
    AL --> AL6[Parting Line Allowance]
    AL --> AL7[Core Prints]

    AL1 --> AL1A[Applied Uniformly to All Dims]
    AL2 --> AL2A[Applied on Vertical Walls only]
    AL3 --> AL3A[Applied on Machined Faces only]
    AL4 --> AL4A[Applied Directionally]
    AL5 --> AL5A[Subtracted from Pattern]
    AL6 --> AL6A[Applied at Parting Line only]
    AL7 --> AL7A[Projects to support sand cores]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Draft Angle Application Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Vertical Wall in Pattern] --> R{External or Internal Surface?}
    R -- External --> S1[Add Draft Outward]
    R -- Internal --> S2[Add Draft Inward]
    S1 --> T1[Pattern widens toward parting line]
    S2 --> T2[Pattern cavity widens upward]
    T1 --> U1[Factor 2D for parallel dimensions]
    T2 --> U1
    U1 --> V[Final Pattern Dimension = Base + 2 x L tan alpha]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pattern Withdrawal Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pattern Placed in Sand] --> B[Sand Compacted Around Pattern]
    B --> C[Rapping the Pattern]
    C --> D[Pattern Slightly Enlarges Cavity by Shake]
    D --> E[Withdraw Pattern with Draft Taper]
    E --> F[Mould Cavity Ready]
    F --> G[Insert Sand Core via Core Prints]
    G --> H[Pour Molten Metal]
    H --> I[Solidification and Shrinkage]
    I --> J[Final Casting with Correct Dimensions]
```
