# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions strictly follow the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern: Part A carries 3 marks each; Part B carries 14 marks each with internal choice (i.e., students answer either OR).

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define a pattern. List any four functions of a pattern in foundry practice.**

**Model Answer:**
A **pattern** is a physical replica of the final casting, suitably modified with necessary allowances, used to create the mould cavity in the sand mould.

The four primary functions of a pattern are:
1. **Imparts Shape** — produces the external geometry of the casting.
2. **Imparts Surface Finish** — transfers its surface texture to the casting.
3. **Provides Means of Core Support** — through **core prints**, the pattern positions the sand core accurately inside the mould.
4. **Provides Means of Pattern Withdrawal** — **draft (taper)** allows clean removal of the pattern from the sand mould without damage.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**What is draft (taper) allowance? Why is it provided on the pattern?**

**Model Answer:**
**Draft allowance** (also called taper allowance) is the slight taper provided on the vertical faces of a pattern to enable its clean and easy withdrawal from the sand mould after ramming.

**Reasons for providing draft:**
- It **prevents damage** to the mould cavity walls during pattern withdrawal.
- It **reduces frictional resistance** between the pattern surface and the compacted sand.
- It enables the **mass production of castings** with consistent quality in machine moulding.
- It is particularly important for **deep patterns** where side friction is high.

Typical values: **1°–2° for hand moulding** and **0.5°–1° for machine moulding.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Allowance Calculation & Pattern Material Selection

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for Module 1]`

**Q. (a)** A grey cast iron rectangular block of size **300 mm × 150 mm × 80 mm** is to be sand cast. The block is to be cast with the **300 × 150 face as the bottom** (drag). The parting line is at the mid-height. Apply the following allowances: shrinkage = **1.0 %**, draft = **1.5°**, machining = **4 mm** on the top face only, shake = **0.4 mm** on all sides. Calculate the final **pattern dimensions** with step-by-step working. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original casting dimensions:**
$L = 300$ mm, $W = 150$ mm, $H = 80$ mm

**Step 2 — Apply shrinkage (1.0 % to all dims):**
$$L_1 = 300 \times 1.01 = 303.0 \text{ mm}$$
$$W_1 = 150 \times 1.01 = 151.5 \text{ mm}$$
$$H_1 = 80 \times 1.01 = 80.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply machining allowance (4 mm on top face):**
$$H_2 = H_1 + 4 = 80.8 + 4.0 = 84.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply draft allowance (1.5° on vertical walls above parting line):**
Vertical wall height above parting line = $H_1/2 = 40.4$ mm.

$$D = 40.4 \times \tan(1.5°) = 40.4 \times 0.02619 = 1.058 \text{ mm}$$

Apply to length and width (factor of 2):
$$L_2 = 303.0 + 2 \times 1.058 = 305.116 \text{ mm}$$
$$W_2 = 151.5 + 2 \times 1.058 = 153.616 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Apply shake allowance (subtractive, 0.4 mm on all sides):**
$$L_{pattern} = 305.116 - 0.4 = 304.716 \text{ mm}$$
$$W_{pattern} = 153.616 - 0.4 = 153.216 \text{ mm}$$
$$H_{pattern} = 84.8 - 0.4 = 84.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Pattern Dimensions:**
$$\boxed{L = 304.72 \text{ mm}, \quad W = 153.22 \text{ mm}, \quad H = 84.4 \text{ mm}}$$

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the factors governing the **selection of pattern material**. Compare wood, cast iron, and aluminium as pattern materials. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The selection of pattern material is governed by the following factors:
1. **Number of castings to be produced** (production volume).
2. **Required dimensional accuracy** and surface finish.
3. **Complexity of the casting geometry.**
4. **Type of moulding process** (hand vs. machine moulding).
5. **Cost of the pattern** vs. cost savings in casting production.
6. **Type of metal being cast** (e.g., hot metals damage wood).

**Comparative Table:**

| Property | Wood | Cast Iron | Aluminium |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | Low (0.6–0.8 g/cm³) | High (7.2 g/cm³) | Medium (2.7 g/cm³) |
| Machinability | Excellent | Poor | Good |
| Wear resistance | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Surface finish | Moderate | Excellent | Good |
| Working life | < 100 uses | > 1000 uses | 500–1000 uses |
| Cost | Low | High | Medium |
| Application | Prototypes, short runs | Mass production | Medium batches |
| Damage from moisture | Susceptible | None | None |

> **Recommendation:** For small batch (< 100 pieces) — **wood**. For medium batch (100–1000) — **aluminium**. For mass production (> 1000) — **cast iron.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Core Prints & Pattern Types

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**Q. (a)** What is a **core print**? Explain the different types of core prints with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **core print** is a specially designed projection provided on the pattern, which forms an **impression (seat)** in the sand mould. This seat serves to **support, locate, and retain** the sand core in its correct position within the mould cavity during pouring of molten metal.

**Types of Core Prints:**

1. **Horizontal Core Print** — Used for cores placed horizontally in the mould. Supported at both ends in the mould walls. Common in pipe fittings and valve bodies.

2. **Vertical Core Print** — Used for vertically oriented cores (e.g., for the central hole in a flange). Supported at the parting line and held in place by **chaplets** or by being **balanced** so that metallostatic pressure does not displace it.

3. **Balanced Core Print** — A vertical core print designed so that the **buoyancy force** of the molten metal on the core is **balanced** by the weight of the core itself, requiring no chaplets. The top and bottom core print areas are sized such that:

$$P_{buoyancy} = W_{core} + F_{bottom\_print}$$

4. **Self-Setting Core Print / Bonded Core Print** — Uses a resin or glue bond at the core-mould interface to hold the core without mechanical support.

**Design of core print length:**
- For horizontal cores: $L_{print} = 1.25 \, D_{core}$ to $1.5 \, D_{core}$
- For vertical cores: $L_{print} = 0.5 \, D_{core}$ to $1.0 \, D_{core}$ (with chaplets above)

---

**Q. (b)** Classify **patterns based on construction**. Explain **match plate pattern** and **skeleton pattern** with their applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of patterns (based on construction):**
1. Single piece / solid pattern
2. Two-piece / split pattern
3. Multi-piece pattern
4. Follow-board pattern
5. Match plate pattern
6. Cope and drag pattern
7. Gated pattern
8. Sweep pattern
9. Skeleton pattern
10. Loose piece pattern

**Match Plate Pattern:**
- Both **cope and drag halves** of the pattern are mounted on **opposite sides of a single metal plate** (the match plate).
- The plate also carries the **runner, gate, and sprue** details.
- Both halves are perfectly aligned using **pins and bushes** on the plate.
- **Applications:** High-volume production of small to medium castings in **machine moulding** lines (e.g., automotive components, plumbing fittings).
- **Advantages:** High dimensional accuracy, fast moulding cycle, no separate alignment required.
- **Limitations:** High initial cost, suitable for relatively shallow patterns only.

**Skeleton Pattern:**
- A wooden or metal **frame shaped to the contour of the casting**, with the interior filled with sand and struck off to form the mould surface.
- The "ribs" of the skeleton follow the outline of the casting.
- **Applications:** Used for **very large castings** (e.g., lathe beds, machine tool frames, large gear blanks) where a solid pattern would be **uneconomical** due to material and handling cost.
- **Advantages:** Significant material saving, easier handling of large patterns.
- **Limitations:** Lower accuracy and surface finish compared to solid patterns; requires skilled moulder.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign convention for shake allowance:** Many students *add* shake allowance to the pattern instead of *subtracting* it. Shake enlarges the mould cavity; hence the **pattern is made smaller** by the shake value. Marks lost: **2 marks per occurrence.**
> 2. **Direction of draft:** Draft is applied *opposite* for external vs. internal surfaces. Students often apply it incorrectly. Marks lost: **2 marks per occurrence.**
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for draft on parallel dimensions:** The draft is on *each side* of the part, so the total change in length is $2D$, not $D$. Marks lost: **1 mark.**
> 4. **Confusing shrinkage with distortion:** Shrinkage is *uniform* and *isotropic*; distortion is *directional*. Marks lost: **1–2 marks.**
> 5. **Forgetting to convert draft angle to its tangent:** Use the formula $D = L \tan(\alpha)$ — many students incorrectly write $D = L \times \alpha$ (treating $\alpha$ as a slope ratio). Marks lost: **1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU VIVA / ESE:**

### Core Definitions
- A **pattern** is a physical replica of the final casting with allowances.
- A **core** is a pre-shaped sand mass placed inside the mould to create internal cavities.
- A **core print** is the projection on the pattern that forms a seat for the core in the mould.

### The 7 Allowances (Mnemonic: "S-D-M-D-S-P-C")
1. **S**hrinkage — uniform, additive, on all linear dims.
2. **D**raft — additive (with factor 2), on vertical walls only.
3. **M**achining — additive, on machined faces only.
4. **D**istortion — directional, additive opposite to warp.
5. **S**hake — subtractive, on all linear dims (~0.3 mm).
6. **P**arting line — local additive, at the parting plane.
7. **C**ore prints — not an allowance, but a *feature* on the pattern.

### Pattern Materials — Decision Rule
- **Wood** → prototypes / < 100 pieces.
- **Aluminium** → 100–1000 pieces.
- **Cast iron / Steel** → > 1000 pieces (mass production).
- **Plastic (Epoxy/PU)** → complex geometry, medium batch.
- **Wax / Foam** → expendable (investment / lost-foam).

### Must-Remember Shrinkage Values
- **Grey Cast Iron: 1.0 %**
- **Steel: 1.5 – 2.0 %**
- **Aluminium: 1.3 – 1.5 %**
- **Brass / Bronze: 1.5 %**

### Master Formula
$$\boxed{L_{pattern} = \frac{L_{casting}}{1 + S/100} \pm M \pm 2L\tan(\alpha) - \delta_{shake}}$$

### Top Exam Traps
- Shake allowance is **subtracted**, not added.
- Draft angle is **in degrees** — always use $\tan(\alpha)$, not $\alpha$ directly.
- For **internal cavities**, draft is given in the *opposite* direction (cavity widens upward).
- Core print length ≈ **1.5 × core diameter** for horizontal cores.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core**: A pre-formed, bonded sand aggregate (or refractory mass) inserted into a foundry mold cavity to create **internal features, hollow passages, undercuts, or complex internal geometries** in a casting that cannot be produced by the mold itself. Cores are *expendable* — they are destroyed (by core collapse or shake-out) after the molten metal has solidified around them.

A **core box** is the tooling (analogous to a die) used to shape the core sand mixture. The protruding portions of a core that seat inside the mold are called **core prints**, which provide location, alignment, and partial mechanical support.

### 1.2 Core Sand

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Sand** is a specially formulated sand mixture used exclusively for making cores. It is *distinct* from molding sand because it must possess high strength, permeability, collapsibility, and refractoriness simultaneously.

**Typical Core Sand Composition (by mass):**
- **Silica sand** (SiO₂) — base refractory aggregate
- **Binder** — provides green/dry strength (oil, resin, sodium silicate, cement)
- **Cereal / dextrin** — improves collapsibility and surface finish
- **Silica flour / iron oxide** — improves hot strength and refractoriness
- **Moisture** — controlled (often < 4 %)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Ice Cube in a Juice Mold"**: Imagine freezing a shaped ice cube inside a jelly (the casting). The ice cube *creates* the hollow shape in the jelly. Once the jelly sets, you simply let the ice melt away (core collapse / shake-out), and the cavity remains. A foundry core plays the identical role inside molten metal — it occupies space that must later become a void.

- **Core** = shaped ice cube (pre-formed, refractory, expendable).
- **Mold** = jelly container (defines the outer shape).
- **Liquid metal** = hot liquid jelly being poured.
- **Core prints** = the small protrusions that lock the ice cube into the correct position inside the mold.
- **Chaplets** = tiny sticks used to weigh the cube down so the buoyant liquid doesn't float it away.

### 1.4 Functions of a Core

1. Form **internal cavities** (engine cylinder bores, oil passages).
2. Form **external undercuts** and recessed features.
3. Improve **casting yield** by replacing solid metal with hollow sections.
4. Provide **dimensional accuracy** to internal features that cannot be machined easily post-casting.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Cores (Step-by-Step Logic)

Cores are classified by the examiner along **three orthogonal axes**: (i) binder/curing chemistry, (ii) curing temperature, and (iii) geometric orientation in the mold.

**A. Based on Binder System**
- **Green sand cores** — bonded with clay + moisture; low strength; rarely used.
- **Oil-bonded (linseed oil) cores** — classic; oven-dried; good collapsibility.
- **Resin-bonded cores** — phenolic, furan, urethane, alkyd.
- **Sodium silicate (CO₂) cores** — hardened by passing CO₂ gas.
- **Cement-bonded cores** — Portland cement; high strength.
- **Ceramic cores** — used in investment casting for superalloys (e.g., turbine blades).

**B. Based on Curing Method**
- **Hot-box cores** — core box heated to 200–300 °C; thermoset resin cures in seconds.
- **Cold-box cores** — chemical curing at room temperature (e.g., phenolic-urethane with amine gas).
- **CO₂ (silicates) cores** — gassed with CO₂.
- **Air-dried / oven-dried cores** — slow, traditional method.

**C. Based on Orientation in Mold**
- **Horizontal cores** — axis parallel to parting plane.
- **Vertical cores** — axis perpendicular to parting plane (high buoyancy).
- **Balanced / hanging cores** — supported from upper cope.
- **Multi-part / split cores** — for complex geometry.

### 2.2 Why Each Property is Needed (the "Why" Behind the Specs)

| Property | Physical Meaning | Why it is Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Green strength** | Strength of freshly rammed, undried core | Core must survive handling & insertion into mold |
| **Dry strength** | Strength after curing | Core must resist erosion from flowing liquid metal |
| **Hot strength** | Strength at metal-pouring temperature | Core must not deform/sag under thermal load |
| **Permeability** | Ability to vent evolved gases | Prevents gas porosity in the casting |
| **Collapsibility** | Ability to disintegrate after solidification | Allows contraction of casting; easy shake-out |
| **Refractoriness** | Resistance to high temperature | Prevents fusion of sand with metal (burn-on) |
| **Surface finish** | Smoothness of baked core | Transfers to casting internal surface |

### 2.3 Buoyancy Force on a Core (the Critical KTU Concept)

A submerged core is pushed **upward** by a force equal to the weight of liquid metal it displaces, minus its own weight. This **buoyancy force** is the single most important design constraint for large cores.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buoyancy force on core | $F_b = V_c \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)$ | N | $V_c$ = core volume |
| 2 | Buoyancy for cylinder | $F_b = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot L \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)$ | N | $D$, $L$ in m |
| 3 | Volume of cylinder | $V_c = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} L$ | m³ | — |
| 4 | Area of core print | $A_{cp} = \frac{F_b}{2 \cdot \sigma_c}$ | m² | $\sigma_c$ = compressive strength of molding sand |
| 5 | Number of chaplets | $n = \dfrac{F_b}{W_{ch}}$ | — | $W_{ch}$ = load capacity of one chaplet |
| 6 | Chaplet stress check | $\sigma_{ch} = \dfrac{F_b}{n \cdot A_{ch}} \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | Pa | $A_{ch}$ = chaplet cross-section |
| 7 | Core sand: AFS grain fineness | $GFN = \dfrac{\sum (m_i \cdot n_i)}{\sum m_i}$ | — | $m_i$ = mass retained, $n_i$ = sieve number |
| 8 | Permeability number | $PN = \dfrac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot P \cdot t}$ | — | Foundry permeability test |

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` notation if you must show absolute values in any calculation row (e.g., `\vert \rho_m - \rho_c \vert`) so that the markdown table pipe is preserved.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive**: Engine blocks, cylinder heads, crankshaft journals — all use sand cores for internal oil/water passages.
- **Aerospace**: Investment-cast superalloy turbine blades use **ceramic cores** for the complex serpentine cooling channels.
- **Valves & pumps**: Internal flow passages are produced entirely by cores.
- **Sanitary fittings**: Hollow bodies are made by collapsible cores to allow easy shake-out.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Buoyancy Force on a Submerged Core

**Problem setup:** A core of volume $V_c$ is fully submerged in liquid metal of density $\rho_m$. The core material itself has density $\rho_c$. We wish to find the net upward (buoyancy) force on the core.

**Step 1 — Weight of the core (downward):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_c &= m_c \cdot g \\
    &= V_c \cdot \rho_c \cdot g
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $m_c$ is the mass of the core, and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity ($\approx 9.81$ m/s²).

**Step 2 — Weight of liquid metal displaced by the core (Archimedes' principle):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_d &= m_d \cdot g \\
    &= V_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g
\end{aligned}
$$

The displaced volume equals the core volume $V_c$ because the core is fully immersed.

**Step 3 — Net upward (buoyancy) force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= W_d - W_c \\
    &= V_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g - V_c \cdot \rho_c \cdot g \\
    &= V_c \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard KTU buoyancy formula** for cores.

**Step 4 — Special case: Cylindrical core** (diameter $D$, length $L$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= \left(\frac{\pi D^2}{4} L\right) \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (typical exam-style substitution):**

Given: $D = 0.10$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $\rho_m = 7100$ kg/m³ (cast iron), $\rho_c = 1600$ kg/m³ (oil-bonded core), $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} \times 0.30 \\
    &= 2.356 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= 2.356 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 \times (7100 - 1600) \\
    &= 2.356 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 \times 5500 \\
    &= 127.1 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A small cylindrical core of just 10 cm diameter × 30 cm length experiences **~127 N** of upward force — large enough that designers must use chaplets or extended core prints to anchor it.

### 3.2 Derivation: Sizing the Core Print Area

**Step 1 — Equilibrium condition:** The total downward reaction from the two core prints must equal the buoyancy force.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \cdot A_{cp} \cdot \sigma_c &= F_b
\end{aligned}
$$

$\sigma_c$ is the permissible compressive stress on the molding sand at the core-print interface.

**Step 2 — Solving for the required core print area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{cp} &= \frac{F_b}{2 \cdot \sigma_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — If $A_{cp}$ is too large, use chaplets:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{F_b}{W_{ch}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $W_{ch}$ is the load capacity of a single chaplet (tabulated by manufacturer based on leg area and steel grade).

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Core

| Casting Requirement | Recommended Core Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Small hole, low precision | Green-sand core (dried) | Cheap, fast |
| High dimensional accuracy | Resin-bonded / shell core | Excellent surface & strength |
| Complex internal passage | Wax / ceramic core (investment casting) | Can be leached out chemically |
| Large vertical cavity in iron | Dry-sand + chaplets | Manages buoyancy |
| Mass production of cylinder block | Cold-box (Ashland) phenolic-urethane | Fastest cycle, 30 s cure |
| Superalloy turbine blade | Ceramic core (silica-based) | Survives >1500 °C pouring |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Block Diagram (Cores)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FOUNDRY CORES] --> B[BASED ON BINDER]
    A --> C[BASED ON CURING]
    A --> D[BASED ON ORIENTATION]

    B --> B1[Green Sand Core]
    B --> B2[Oil Bonded Core]
    B --> B3[Resin Bonded Core]
    B --> B4[Sodium Silicate CO2 Core]
    B --> B5[Cement Bonded Core]
    B --> B6[Ceramic Core]

    C --> C1[Hot Box Process]
    C --> C2[Cold Box Process]
    C --> C3[CO2 Gassed]
    C --> C4[Oven Dried]
    C --> C5[Air Dried]

    D --> D1[Horizontal Core]
    D --> D2[Vertical Core]
    D --> D3[Hanging Core]
    D --> D4[Split or Multi Part Core]
```

### 4.2 Core Manufacturing Process Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Silica Sand] --> M[MIXING WITH BINDER AND ADDITIVES]
    B[Binder Oil Resin Silicate] --> M
    C[Additives Cereal Iron Oxide] --> M
    M --> R[RAMMING IN CORE BOX]
    R --> X[CURING OR DRYING]
    X --> Y[STRIP FROM CORE BOX]
    Y --> Z[INSPECTION AND STORAGE]
    Z --> P[PLACEMENT IN MOLD]
    P --> Q[SUPPORT WITH CHAPLETS]
    Q --> S[POURING OF LIQUID METAL]
    S --> T[SOLIDIFICATION]
    T --> U[CORE COLLAPSE]
    U --> V[SHAKE OUT AND CLEANING]
```

### 4.3 Core Placement & Buoyancy Support (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    COP[Cope Upper Mold Half] --> CP1[Upper Core Print]
    DRG[Drag Lower Mold Half] --> CP2[Lower Core Print]
    CP1 --> CORE[Sand Core Submerged In Liquid Metal]
    CP2 --> CORE
    CORE --> BFE[Buoyancy Force Fb Upward]
    BFE --> CHAP[Chaplets Metal Supports Take Compressive Load]
    CHAP --> COMP[Compressive Reaction Equal To Fb]
    CP1 --> CLOAD[Core Print Sand Reaction]
    CP2 --> CLOAD
    CLOAD --> BAL[Net Force Balanced Fb Equals Total Reaction]
    CHAP --> BAL
```

### 4.4 Core Print & Chaplet Geometry (Text Schematic)

```
        COPE (Upper Mold)
   +-------------------------+
   |       ___________       |
   |      |           |      |   <-- Upper Core Print (sand seat)
   |      |           |      |
   |      |   CORE    |  *   |   <-- '*' = chaplet (stays in casting,
   |      |           |  *   |       fuses with metal)
   |      |___________|      |
   |       ___________       |   <-- Lower Core Print
   +-------------------------+
         DRAG (Lower Mold)

   Fb  =  Vc . g . (rho_m - rho_c)      <-- pushes core UP
   R_cp  +  R_chap  =  Fb               <-- equilibrium
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define a "core" in foundry practice. List any four desirable properties of core sand.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *core* is a pre-formed, bonded sand (or refractory) mass placed inside a mould cavity to produce internal cavities, holes, undercuts, or passages in a casting. It is expendable and is removed by shake-out or core collapse after solidification. **Four desirable properties of core sand:**

1. **High strength** (green, dry, and hot) to resist handling, metal flow, and thermal load.
2. **High permeability** so that evolved gases can escape without causing gas porosity.
3. **Good collapsibility** so the core disintegrates easily after solidification, accommodating casting contraction.
4. **High refractoriness** to prevent fusion of sand with metal at pouring temperatures.

*(Alternate acceptable properties: low moisture content, smooth surface finish, chemical inertness with molten metal.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**What is meant by a "core print"? State its function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *core print* is a carefully shaped projection provided on the core (matching a corresponding recess in the mould) that locates, aligns, and partially supports the core inside the mould cavity. **Functions of a core print:**

1. **Locating / positioning**: Ensures the core sits in the correct geometric location within the mould.
2. **Supporting**: Bears a portion of the buoyancy force exerted by the liquid metal on the core.
3. **Venting**: The print–core gap often doubles as a vent path for evolved gases.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Cores Topic)**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks on core-related questions for the following reasons:
> 1. **Confusing "core print" with "chaplet"** — prints are sand seats; chaplets are metal supports left inside the casting.
> 2. **Forgetting the density difference** in the buoyancy formula — the correct form is $F_b = V_c g (\rho_m - \rho_c)$, *not* simply $V_c g \rho_m$.
> 3. **Omitting binder name and curing mechanism** in core-making process descriptions (e.g., not stating *"phenolic-urethane + amine gas"* for the Ashland cold-box process).
> 4. **Not labelling the parting line** on the core-box sketch — this costs easy diagram marks.
> 5. **Ignoring collapsibility** when listing core properties — many students list only strength and forget this vital property.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **Shell Core Making Process**. List its **advantages and limitations**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A cylindrical core of diameter **80 mm** and length **250 mm** is fully submerged in molten cast iron of density **7100 kg/m³**. If the core density is **1550 kg/m³**, calculate (i) the **buoyancy force** on the core, and (ii) the **minimum number of chaplets** required if each chaplet can support **25 N** of load. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Shell Core Making Process:**

- A **thermosetting phenolic resin** (3–6 %) is mixed with silica sand.
- The mixture is dumped into a **heated metal core box** (200–300 °C).
- On contact with the hot surface, the resin melts and forms a **thin shell** (≈ 6–12 mm thick) bonded to the pattern.
- The uncured sand from the centre is **dumped out** by inverting the box.
- The remaining hollow shell is **further cured** in the box for 30–60 s, then ejected by ejector pins.

**Advantages (any 4):**
1. Excellent **dimensional accuracy** and high strength-to-weight ratio.
2. Very **smooth internal surface** of casting.
3. **Fast cycle time** (30–60 s) suitable for mass production.
4. **Hollow** core → less gas evolution, less sand consumption.
5. Good **storage life** (unlimited if kept dry).

**Limitations (any 2):**
1. **High tooling cost** — metal core box required.
2. Limited to **small-to-medium cores** (large thin shells are fragile).
3. **Resin fumes** require ventilation.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $D = 0.080$ m, $L = 0.250$ m, $\rho_m = 7100$ kg/m³, $\rho_c = 1550$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $W_{ch} = 25$ N.

Step 1 — Volume of core:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot L \\
    &= \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} \times 0.250 \\
    &= 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Buoyancy force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= V_c \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c) \\
    &= 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 \times (7100 - 1550) \\
    &= 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 \times 5550 \\
    &= 68.43 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Number of chaplets:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{F_b}{W_{ch}} = \frac{68.43}{25} = 2.74
\end{aligned}
$$

Since we cannot have a fraction of a chaplet, **round up**: $n = 3$ chaplets (each rated 25 N → total 75 N ≥ 68.43 N). ✅

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Explain the **CO₂ (sodium silicate) core making process** with a neat diagram. Mention its **advantages and disadvantages**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the various **ingredients used in core sand** and the **function of each**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CO₂ Core Making Process (Sodium Silicate Process):**

- Silica sand is mixed with **5–6 % sodium silicate (Na₂SiO₃) binder** (also called *water glass*) and a small quantity of additives.
- The mixture is **rammed into a core box** (or around a core pattern) in the conventional manner.
- **CO₂ gas** is then blown through the rammed core for 10–60 seconds. The CO₂ reacts with sodium silicate to form **sodium carbonate and silica gel**, which hardens the core:

  $$\text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{SiO}_2 \text{ (gel)}$$

- The hardened core is **stripped from the box** and used immediately (no drying required).

**Advantages (any 4):**
1. **Very fast hardening** (seconds); high productivity.
2. **No baking required** → energy saving.
3. **High strength** developed immediately.
4. **Long storage life** if kept dry.
5. **Non-toxic** and environment-friendly (vs. oil/resin cores).

**Disadvantages (any 3):**
1. **Poor collapsibility** — silica gel remains rigid; causes hot tears and difficulty in shake-out.
2. **Moisture sensitive** — cores must be stored dry.
3. **Lower surface finish** compared to resin-bonded cores.
4. **Hygroscopic** nature → storage problems.

**(b) Ingredients of Core Sand and Their Functions:**

| Ingredient | Typical % | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Silica sand (SiO₂)** | 90–95 % | Base refractory aggregate; provides bulk, refractoriness, permeability |
| **Binder (oil / resin / silicate / cement)** | 2–8 % | Provides green and dry strength to hold grains together |
| **Cereal / dextrin** | 0.5–2 % | Improves collapsibility, surface finish, and green strength |
| **Silica flour** | 0–5 % | Increases hot strength and refractoriness; fills voids |
| **Iron oxide (Fe₂O₃)** | 0.5–2 % | Prevents metal penetration; improves surface finish; reduces burn-on |
| **Moisture (water)** | 2–4 % (when required) | Activates clay binder; aids in plasticity during ramming |
| **Release agent / parting dust** | trace | Prevents core sticking to core box |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Cores**

- **Definition**: A core is an *expendable, pre-formed sand (or refractory) insert* placed in a mould to create internal cavities.
- **Core sand ≠ Molding sand**: core sand has *higher* strength, permeability, and collapsibility requirements but *lower* moisture.
- **Five essential properties**: strength (green/dry/hot), permeability, collapsibility, refractoriness, surface finish.
- **Core print** is a *sand seat* (part of mould + part of core) — locates and partially supports the core.
- **Chaplets** are *metal* supports that remain in the casting — they take the *remaining* buoyancy load.
- **Buoyancy formula** (must memorize):
  $$F_b = V_c \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)$$
  For a cylinder: $F_b = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot L \cdot g \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_c)$.
- **Core-making processes** to remember for KTU: (i) Hand ramming + oven drying, (ii) **CO₂ / sodium silicate**, (iii) **Hot-box**, (iv) **Cold-box (Ashland)**, (v) **Shell core**, (vi) **Ceramic core** (investment casting).
- **Cold-box (Ashland) process** uses *phenolic-urethane resin* cured by *tertiary amine gas* — fastest production rate.
- **Hot-box** uses *thermoset resin* cured in a *heated core box* at 200–300 °C.
- **Shell core** gives the *smoothest surface* and *highest accuracy* but is the most expensive.
- **Ceramic cores** are used for *superalloy turbine blades* (investment casting) and are removed by *caustic leaching*.
- **Buoyancy direction is UPWARD** → *horizontal* cores are easy to support; *vertical* cores need chaplets.
- **Rounding rule for chaplets**: always round **up** the calculated $n$ to the next whole integer.
- **Collapsibility** is critical for ferrous castings to avoid *hot tears*; CO₂ cores fail this requirement (major disadvantage).
- **Core vents** (wax/plastic tubes) are inserted during ramming to allow gas escape — forgetting this leads to gas porosity marks.
- **Prints vs. Chaplets vs. Vents** — three different support/vent elements; examiners love testing the difference.

> **End of Module 1 — Topic: Cores**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chaplets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chaplets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chaplets** are small, pre-formed metallic supports or spacers used in **sand mould casting (foundry practice)** to position, hold, and stabilize the **core** within the mould cavity. They are placed between the core surface and the mould wall (or between two cores) to prevent displacement of the core due to gravitational sagging, fluid pressure, or **buoyancy flotation** caused by the incoming molten metal.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, chaplets belong to the category of *Core Supports* in the Foundry module. They are sacrificial elements that become permanently fused with the final casting once the molten metal solidifies around them.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **hollow, empty ball (the core)** inside a large open box (the mould cavity) and pouring water (molten metal) into the box. The hollow ball would immediately try to **float upward** because water is denser than air inside it. To stop it from moving, you would wedge small **wedges or pegs (chaplets)** between the ball and the box walls to lock it in place.

Similarly, in a casting:
- The **core** = hollow shape that forms internal cavities.
- The **molten metal** = dense liquid that exerts buoyant force.
- The **chaplets** = metallic "pegs" that resist this force and fix the core's location.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** Chaplets are NOT removed after casting. They melt and merge with the surrounding molten metal, becoming part of the finished casting. Therefore, the chaplet material must be carefully chosen so it does not introduce defects.

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- |
| Chaplet material melting point | Should be **slightly lower** than the casting metal |
| Permissible compressive stress of mild steel chaplet | **100 MPa to 140 MPa** |
| Density of molten cast iron | approx **6.8 g/cm³** |
| Density of molten steel | approx **7.2 g/cm³** |
| Density of dry sand core | approx **1.5 g/cm³** |
| Standard chaplet shapes | Round, Square, Rectangular, Cruciform |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose of Chaplets — Structured Logic

- **Support Against Buoyancy:** Molten metal is 4–5× denser than a dry sand core. The displaced volume of metal creates a vertical uplift force on the core, which chaplets must counteract.
- **Resist Lateral Movement:** As molten metal rushes into the mould, it creates horizontal冲击力 (impact). Chaplets prevent the core from being pushed sideways.
- **Maintain Dimensional Accuracy:** By locking the core in its designed position, chaplets ensure wall thickness, internal diameters, and concentricity remain within tolerance.
- **Bear Core Self-Weight:** Tall or long horizontal cores would sag under their own weight; chaplets prevent this deformation.
- **Become Integral Part of Casting:** The chaplet melts and fuses with the parent metal, so it must be of compatible composition to avoid weak joints.

## 2.2 Classification of Chaplets

### Based on Shape and Application:

**1. Flat (Button / Pad) Chaplets**
- Used for supporting **horizontal cores** from below.
- Shaped like a flat disc with a central projection that grips the core.
- Placed in recesses (prints) in the mould wall.

**2. Stalk (Pillar / Post) Chaplets**
- Used for supporting **vertical cores**.
- Cylindrical or square pillar with flared ends.
- One end rests on the mould, the other bears against the core.

**3. Double-Stud (Bipartite) Chaplets**
- Two parts: one stud is fixed on the mould print, the other on the core.
- Joined during core setting.
- Used for **vertical cores** where print accuracy is critical.

**4. Cruciform / Diaphragm Chaplets**
- Cross-shaped section, used to support long horizontal cores.
- Provides higher buckling resistance.

**5. Chaplets with Ears / Legs**
- Have small projections that bite into the sand mould for better grip.

### Based on Material:

| Chaplet Material | Used For Casting Of | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mild Steel | Steel castings | Fuses easily with molten steel |
| Cast Iron | Cast Iron castings | Matches thermal expansion; similar composition |
| Copper-Coated Steel | Steel castings | Prevents rusting, ensures better fusion |
| Brass / Copper | Non-ferrous (brass, bronze) castings | Lower melting point |
| Tin / Lead | Light alloy castings | Very low melting point |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equation is **HIGH-YIELD** and frequently asked in KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical questions.

**Step 1: Calculate Buoyant Force on the Core**

$$F_b = V_c \times (\rho_m - \rho_c) \times g$$

Where:
- $F_b$ = Buoyant force (N)
- $V_c$ = Volume of the core that is submerged / surrounded by molten metal (m³)
- $\rho_m$ = Density of molten metal (kg/m³)
- $\rho_c$ = Density of core material (kg/m³)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)

**Step 2: Calculate Load per Chaplet**

$$P = \sigma_c \times A$$

Where:
- $P$ = Safe load-carrying capacity of one chaplet (N)
- $\sigma_c$ = Permissible compressive stress of chaplet material (Pa or N/m²)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of chaplet (m²)

**Step 3: Calculate Number of Chaplets Required**

$$n = \frac{F_b}{P} = \frac{V_c \times (\rho_m - \rho_c) \times g}{\sigma_c \times A}$$

**Safety Factor:** A safety factor of **2 to 3** is generally added, so practically:

$$n_{actual} = 2 \times \frac{F_b}{P}$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $F_b$ | Buoyant Force | N (Newton) |
| $V_c$ | Core Volume | m³ |
| $\rho_m$ | Molten Metal Density | kg/m³ |
| $\rho_c$ | Core Density | kg/m³ |
| $\sigma_c$ | Compressive Stress | Pa (N/m²) |
| $A$ | Chaplet Cross-section Area | m² |
| $n$ | Number of Chaplets | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Engine blocks, cylinder heads, and gear housings use chaplets to hold sand cores in place for internal oil passages.
- **Pipe & Valve Manufacturing:** Hollow valve bodies and pipe fittings rely on chaplets for accurate internal bore positioning.
- **Heavy Machinery:** Pump casings, turbine housings.
- **Locomotive & Marine:** Large cast components with complex internal cavities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Number of Chaplets Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme often asks: *"Design the chaplet support for a sand core given casting dimensions and metal density."* The derivation below is the model solution structure.

### **Problem Setup**

A horizontal cylindrical core of diameter $D = 0.20$ m and length $L = 0.40$ m is used in a steel casting. Calculate the number of mild-steel chaplets required to support it.

**Given Data:**
- Density of molten steel, $\rho_m = 7200$ kg/m³
- Density of sand core, $\rho_c = 1500$ kg/m³
- Permissible compressive stress of chaplet, $\sigma_c = 120 \times 10^6$ N/m²
- Chaplet cross-section: circular with diameter $d = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Safety factor, $S.F. = 2$

---

### **Step 1: Volume of the Core**

The core is a cylinder, so:

$$V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times L$$

Substituting the values:

$$V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.20)^2 \times 0.40$$

$$V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.04 \times 0.40$$

$$V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.016$$

$$V_c = 0.01257 \text{ m}^3$$

---

### **Step 2: Buoyant Force on the Core**

By Archimedes' principle applied to the molten metal displacing the core:

$$F_b = V_c \times (\rho_m - \rho_c) \times g$$

Substituting:

$$F_b = 0.01257 \times (7200 - 1500) \times 9.81$$

$$F_b = 0.01257 \times 5700 \times 9.81$$

$$F_b = 0.01257 \times 55917$$

$$F_b \approx 702.96 \text{ N}$$

---

### **Step 3: Cross-Sectional Area of One Chaplet**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times d^2$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.020)^2$$

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0004$$

$$A = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

---

### **Step 4: Safe Load Capacity of One Chaplet**

$$P = \sigma_c \times A$$

$$P = (120 \times 10^6) \times (3.1416 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$P = 37699.2 \text{ N}$$

---

### **Step 5: Theoretical Number of Chaplets**

$$n = \frac{F_b}{P}$$

$$n = \frac{702.96}{37699.2}$$

$$n \approx 0.0187 \approx 0.02$$

---

### **Step 6: Apply Safety Factor**

$$n_{actual} = S.F. \times n = 2 \times 0.0187$$

Since we cannot have a fractional chaplet, and we need to safely account for uneven metal flow, **we use at least 4 chaplets arranged symmetrically** (2 on each side of the horizontal core).

$$n_{final} = 4 \text{ chaplets (practically adopted)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Judgment Override:** KTU examiners expect students to demonstrate that even if the calculated $n$ is fractional or very small, a **minimum of 3 to 4 chaplets** is always placed for **symmetry** and to handle dynamic impact forces of pouring.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Chaplet Material Selection

| Casting Metal | Recommended Chaplet Material | Melting Point (°C) | Reason for Choice |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cast Iron | Cast Iron | ~1150 | Same composition, no contamination |
| Steel | Mild Steel (Cu-coated) | ~1500 | Fuses with steel matrix |
| Brass / Bronze | Copper / Brass | ~900 | Lower melting point, easy fusion |
| Aluminum Alloy | Tin / Pure Aluminum | ~660 | Non-reactive, easy to melt |
| Magnesium Alloy | Steel (with flux coating) | ~650 | Prevents oxide contamination |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Chaplet Placement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sand Mould Cavity] --> B[Molten Metal Pouring]
    B --> C[Core inside Mould]
    C --> D{Forces Acting on Core}
    D --> D1[Buoyant Force Fb Upward]
    D --> D2[Core Self-Weight W Downward]
    D --> D3[Dynamic Impact Force Lateral]
    D1 --> E[Chaplet Support Network]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Type of Chaplet Selected]
    F --> F1[Flat Button Chaplet for Horizontal Core]
    F --> F2[Stalk Pillar Chaplet for Vertical Core]
    F --> F3[Double Stud Chaplet for Tall Vertical Core]
    E --> G[Chaplet Material Selected]
    G --> G1[Mild Steel for Steel Casting]
    G --> G2[Cast Iron for Iron Casting]
    G --> G3[Copper for Non-Ferrous]
    G --> G4[Tin for Light Alloys]
    E --> H[Molten Metal Solidifies]
    H --> I[Chaplet Fuses with Casting]
    I --> J[Final Cast Component with Internal Cavity]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Position of Chaplets in a Mould

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M[Sand Mould]
        M1[Top Cope]
        M2[Drag Bottom]
    end
    M1 --> CORE[Sand Core in Mould]
    M2 --> CORE
    CORE --> CH1[Chaplet 1 Left]
    CORE --> CH2[Chaplet 2 Right]
    CORE --> CH3[Chaplet 3 Bottom Support]
    CH1 --> WALL[Mould Wall]
    CH2 --> WALL
    CH3 --> DRAG[Drag Surface]
    M --> POUR[Pouring Molten Metal from Sprue]
    POUR --> FILL[Fills Cavity Around Chaplets and Core]
    FILL --> SOLIDIFY[Solidification]
    SOLIDIFY --> CAST[Finished Casting with Fused Chaplets]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Chaplet Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Core Design Identified] --> Q1{Core Orientation?}
    Q1 -->|Horizontal| Q2{Core Length?}
    Q1 -->|Vertical| Q3{Core Height?}
    Q2 -->|Short| R1[Use 2 Flat Button Chaplets]
    Q2 -->|Long| R2[Use 3 to 4 Cruciform Chaplets]
    Q3 -->|Low| R3[Use Single Stalk Chaplet]
    Q3 -->|Tall| R4[Use Double Stud Chaplet]
    R1 --> S[Check Buoyancy Force Fb]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    S --> T{Fb vs Chaplet Capacity}
    T -->|Fb less than Capacity| U[Design OK Apply Safety Factor 2]
    T -->|Fb more than Capacity| V[Increase Chaplet Diameter or Number]
    U --> End[Finalise Chaplet Layout]
    V --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define chaplets. State any two functions of chaplets in sand mould casting.**

**Model Answer:**
Chaplets are small metallic supports placed between the core and mould wall in a sand casting to hold the core in its correct position.

**Two functions:**
1. They support the core against buoyant force created by molten metal, preventing it from floating upward.
2. They maintain the dimensional accuracy of the internal cavity by holding the core in the design position.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What are the materials used for making chaplets? Why is the chaplet material chosen carefully?**

**Model Answer:**
Chaplets are commonly made from mild steel, cast iron, copper, brass, or tin depending on the casting metal.

**Reason for careful selection:** The chaplet must fuse completely with the molten metal to form an integral part of the casting. If the melting point is too high, it will not fuse, leaving a void or weak joint. If chemically incompatible, it may cause contamination or porosity in the final casting.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ CAREFULLY!**
> Students frequently lose marks in chaplet numericals by:
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** — Examiners expect you to multiply the theoretical $n$ by a safety factor of 2 to 3.
> 2. **Using wrong units** — Ensure density is in kg/m³, length in metres, area in m². Mixing cm and m is a common blunder.
> 3. **Ignoring Archimedes' direction** — Many students subtract $\rho_c$ from $\rho_m$ in the wrong order, getting a negative buoyant force.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** — Always mention "core is fully submerged" or "molten metal density is constant" as assumptions to gain full marks.
> 5. **Final answer without symmetry check** — Always round up and state "minimum N chaplets placed symmetrically."

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different types of chaplets used in sand mould casting. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical sand core of diameter **15 cm** and length **30 cm** is used in a steel casting. The permissible compressive stress of the mild steel chaplet is **120 MPa**. The chaplet diameter is **20 mm**. Calculate the number of chaplets required.

  - Density of molten steel = **7200 kg/m³**
  - Density of sand core = **1500 kg/m³**
  - Take safety factor = **2**  **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $D = 0.15$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $d = 0.020$ m, $\sigma_c = 120 \times 10^6$ N/m², $\rho_m = 7200$ kg/m³, $\rho_c = 1500$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Volume of Core**

$$V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.15)^2 \times 0.30 = 5.30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Buoyant Force**

$$F_b = V_c \times (\rho_m - \rho_c) \times g$$

$$F_b = 5.30 \times 10^{-3} \times (7200 - 1500) \times 9.81$$

$$F_b = 5.30 \times 10^{-3} \times 5700 \times 9.81 = 296.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Cross-Sectional Area of One Chaplet**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.020)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Safe Load per Chaplet**

$$P = \sigma_c \times A = 120 \times 10^6 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} = 37699.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Theoretical Number**

$$n = \frac{F_b}{P} = \frac{296.3}{37699.2} \approx 0.00786$$

**Step 6: Apply Safety Factor**

$$n_{actual} = 2 \times 0.00786 = 0.0157$$

**Step 7: Practical Decision**

The calculated value is far less than 1, but to maintain symmetry and withstand dynamic forces of pouring, a **minimum of 4 chaplets** are placed — 2 on each side of the horizontal core.

$$\boxed{n_{final} = 4 \text{ chaplets}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the factors considered in the selection of chaplet material. Why should the chaplet material have a melting point lower than or equal to the casting metal? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A box-shaped sand core of dimensions **20 cm × 20 cm × 40 cm** is used in a cast iron casting. The density of molten cast iron is **6800 kg/m³** and that of the core is **1500 kg/m³**. The chaplet is square in cross-section with side **15 mm** and permissible stress **100 MPa**. Determine the number of chaplets required. Take safety factor = **2**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $L = 0.20$ m, $B = 0.20$ m, $H = 0.40$ m, $a = 0.015$ m, $\sigma_c = 100 \times 10^6$ N/m², $\rho_m = 6800$ kg/m³, $\rho_c = 1500$ kg/m³.

**Step 1: Volume of Core**

$$V_c = L \times B \times H = 0.20 \times 0.20 \times 0.40 = 0.016 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Buoyant Force**

$$F_b = V_c \times (\rho_m - \rho_c) \times g$$

$$F_b = 0.016 \times (6800 - 1500) \times 9.81$$

$$F_b = 0.016 \times 5300 \times 9.81 = 831.89 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Cross-Sectional Area of One Chaplet**

$$A = a^2 = (0.015)^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Safe Load per Chaplet**

$$P = \sigma_c \times A = 100 \times 10^6 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-4} = 22500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Theoretical Number**

$$n = \frac{831.89}{22500} = 0.037$$

**Step 6: Apply Safety Factor**

$$n_{actual} = 2 \times 0.037 = 0.074$$

**Step 7: Practical Decision**

Although theoretical requirement is less than 1, the core is long and horizontal. To prevent bending and maintain uniform support, place **4 chaplets** symmetrically along the length.

$$\boxed{n_{final} = 4 \text{ chaplets}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Exam-Ready**

- **Chaplet** = metallic core support used in sand casting.
- **Primary Function:** Resist buoyant uplift force on the core caused by molten metal.
- **Secondary Functions:** Maintain core position, support core self-weight, ensure internal cavity accuracy.
- **Chaplet Becomes Part of Casting:** It melts and fuses with parent metal — never removed.
- **Types (Must know for sketch questions):** Flat (button), Stalk (pillar), Double-Stud, Cruciform.
- **Material Rule:** Chaplet melting point ≤ Casting metal melting point.
- **Mild steel chaplets** → for steel castings.
- **Cast iron chaplets** → for cast iron castings.
- **Copper/brass chaplets** → for non-ferrous (bronze, brass) castings.
- **Key Formula:** $F_b = V_c \times (\rho_m - \rho_c) \times g$
- **Number of chaplets:** $n = \dfrac{F_b}{\sigma_c \times A} \times \text{Safety Factor (2 to 3)}$
- **Always round up** and use **minimum 3 to 4 chaplets** for symmetry in actual layout.
- **Permissible stress for mild steel chaplet** ≈ 100–140 MPa.
- **Density of molten steel** ≈ 7200 kg/m³; **molten cast iron** ≈ 6800 kg/m³; **dry sand core** ≈ 1500 kg/m³.
- **Don't forget the safety factor** — most common mark-losing mistake.
- **Always state assumptions** clearly: "core is fully submerged," "molten metal density is constant," "g = 9.81 m/s²."
- **Placement rule:** Chaplets must be placed symmetrically and on both sides of the core to avoid tilting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solidification of Metals and Chvorinov's Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solidification of Metals and Chvorinov's Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Solidification

**Solidification** is the phase transformation process by which a metal transitions from the **liquid state** to the **solid state** during cooling, accompanied by the release of the **latent heat of fusion** ($H_f$). In the context of manufacturing processes, solidification is the foundational physical phenomenon upon which all **casting operations** (sand casting, investment casting, die casting, continuous casting) are built. The science of solidification governs the final **microstructure**, **mechanical properties**, **defect formation** (shrinkage porosity, hot tears, cold shuts), and **dimensional accuracy** of the cast product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Solidification is the transformation of the liquid phase of a metal into its solid phase, occurring at a characteristic temperature (freezing/melting point $T_m$) with the evolution of latent heat, and proceeds via two sequential atomic-level events — **Nucleation** (formation of a stable solid seed) followed by **Growth** (propagation of the solid-liquid interface).*

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Examiner Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Meaning | Standard Unit |
|------|----------------|---------------|
| Melting Point ($T_m$) | Equilibrium temperature at which solid and liquid coexist | K or °C |
| Superheat ($\Delta T_{sh}$) | Temperature of molten metal above $T_m$ at pouring | °C or K |
| Undercooling / Supercooling ($\Delta T$) | Temperature below equilibrium $T_m$ where nucleation actually begins | °C or K |
| Latent Heat of Fusion ($H_f$ or $L$) | Energy released per unit mass during phase change | **J/kg** or **cal/g** |
| Mold Constant ($B$) | Empirical constant in Chvorinov's rule for a given mold material | min/cm² |
| Modulus ($M = V/A$) | Volume-to-surface-area ratio controlling solidification | cm or m |
| Critical Radius ($r^*$) | Minimum radius of an embryo that can grow spontaneously | m or cm |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Frozen Pond:**
> Imagine a still pond in winter. The air above the pond is cold (the "mold wall"). As heat flows out from water to air, the water temperature drops below 0°C. Tiny ice crystals form spontaneously — these are **nuclei** (nucleation). Once a stable nucleus appears, water molecules attach to it row by row, growing into a continuous ice sheet — this is **growth**. The time for the entire pond to freeze depends on (a) its **volume** (more water → more time), and (b) its **surface exposed to cold air** (more surface → faster freezing). Chvorinov's rule captures exactly this intuition: freezing time $\propto (V/A)^n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Jaggery (Gud) Maker:**
> A traditional jaggery maker boils sugarcane juice in a wide shallow pan. Even though the same amount of juice is in the pan, it cools and solidifies much **faster** than if the same quantity were kept in a deep narrow vessel. Why? Because the **surface area exposed to air is larger** in the wide pan. The **modulus (V/A) is smaller**, hence solidification is faster. This is the *living industrial embodiment* of Chvorinov's rule — foundries design **risers** to be **modulus-poor** compared to the casting so they freeze LAST and feed liquid metal to compensate shrinkage.

### 1.4 The Solidification Sequence at a Glance

1. **Liquid metal pouring** into mold (superheat lost to mold walls first).
2. **Chill zone formation** — rapid nucleation at cold mold wall.
3. **Columnar dendritic growth** — directional heat flow produces elongated grains.
4. **Equiaxed grain formation** — independent grains in the center.
5. **Complete solidification** — centerline reaches $T_m$ and last liquid transforms.
6. **Cooling to room temperature** — solid-state cooling (no more phase change).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU emphasize this topic?**
> Solidification time directly controls **grain size** (per Hall-Petch logic) and the **size, shape, and distribution of porosity**. Designers must predict solidification time to place **risers** correctly, design **gating systems**, and choose between **sand molds (slow cooling)** and **chills/metal molds (fast cooling)**.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Atomic Events: Nucleation and Growth

#### 2.1.1 Nucleation — Birth of the Solid Phase

Nucleation is the formation of a small, stable solid particle (called an **embryo** or **nucleus**) within the undercooled liquid. There are **two distinct mechanisms**:

**A. Homogeneous Nucleation**
- Occurs **spontaneously in the bulk liquid** without any foreign surface.
- Requires **very high undercooling** (often 100–300 °C for pure metals).
- The driving force is the **volumetric free energy change** $(\Delta G_v)$ released upon solidification, opposed by the **surface free energy** $(\gamma_{SL})$ required to create the new solid-liquid interface.
- For a spherical nucleus of radius $r$, the total free energy change is:

$$\Delta G = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \gamma_{SL}$$

where $\Delta G_v$ is **negative** (favorable) and $\gamma_{SL}$ is **positive** (unfavorable).

- The **critical radius** $r^*$ and the **critical activation energy** $\Delta G^*$ are obtained by setting $\frac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = 0$:

$$r^* = \frac{-2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}$$

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3}{3(\Delta G_v)^2}$$

- Since $\Delta G_v$ is proportional to undercooling $\Delta T$, we get:

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma_{SL} T_m}{H_f \Delta T}$$

$$\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3 T_m^2}{3 H_f^2 (\Delta T)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** As $\Delta T$ increases, both $r^*$ and $\Delta G^*$ decrease sharply (inverse-square relation), so nucleation becomes easier at higher undercooling. This is the **physical reason metals undercool less in metal molds** — the foreign mold surface catalyzes nucleation, allowing it to occur with much smaller $\Delta T$.

**B. Heterogeneous Nucleation**
- Occurs on a **foreign substrate** (mold wall, oxide inclusions, impurities, deliberately added inoculants).
- The activation energy barrier is **drastically lower** because part of the embryo's surface energy is offset by the substrate–liquid and substrate–solid interfacial energies:

$$\Delta G^*_{\text{heterogeneous}} = \Delta G^*_{\text{homogeneous}} \cdot f(\theta)$$

where $f(\theta) = \frac{(2 + \cos\theta)(1 - \cos\theta)^2}{4}$ and $\theta$ is the **wetting (contact) angle** between nucleus and substrate.

- Since $0 < f(\theta) < 1$ for $\theta < 180°$, the heterogeneous barrier is always **smaller** than the homogeneous one.
- **All practical castings** solidify by **heterogeneous nucleation** — the melt is never perfectly clean, and the mold wall is always present.

#### 2.1.2 Growth — Propagation of the Solid Front

Once a stable nucleus exists, it grows by **atoms from the liquid attaching to the solid surface**. The growth morphology depends on the **thermal gradient $G$** and the **growth rate $R$** (constitutional supercooling theory):

- **Planar growth**: High $G/R$ ratio (pure metals, very slow cooling).
- **Cellular growth**: Moderate $G/R$ — cells of solute-rich liquid form.
- **Columnar dendritic growth**: Lower $G/R$ — tree-like primary arms aligned with heat flow.
- **Equiaxed dendritic growth**: Low $G/R$ — randomly oriented grains throughout.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"**GPRE** = Planar → Cellular → columnar dendritic → Equiaxed"* in order of **decreasing** $G/R$ ratio.

### 2.2 Solidification Time — The Engineering Problem

In foundry practice, the most important question is: **"How long must we wait before opening the mold?"** This time is called the **solidification time $t_s$** — measured from the moment of pouring until the **last liquid at the thermal center** has fully transformed to solid.

The total heat to be extracted from the casting is:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = \rho \cdot V \cdot H_f$$

where $\rho$ is density, $V$ is volume, and $H_f$ is latent heat of fusion.

The rate of heat extraction depends on the **heat transfer coefficient $h$** at the casting–mold interface, the **mold surface area $A$**, and the **driving temperature difference** $\Delta T$ between casting and mold.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a MUST-MEMORIZE for the KTU ESE. Every cell has appeared in past KTU question papers.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Terms | Typical Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$ | **Chvorinov's Rule** — primary equation of the module | $t_s$: min, $V/A$: cm, $B$: min/cm², $n \approx 2$ | Estimating solidification time of any casting geometry |
| 2 | $\frac{V}{A} = \frac{\text{Volume of casting}}{\text{Total surface area exposed to mold}}$ | **Modulus** $M$ of the casting | cm or m | Compare freezing tendency of different shapes |
| 3 | $M_{\text{shape factor}} = \frac{V}{A}$ — geometry-dependent | Sphere: $r/3$, Cube: $a/6$, Cylinder ($L=D$): $D/6$ | cm | Quick design calculations |
| 4 | $M_{\text{cylinder}} = \frac{\pi D^2 L/4}{\pi D L + 2(\pi D^2/4)} = \frac{DL}{4L + 2D}$ | Modulus of cylinder with diameter $D$ and length $L$ | cm | Cylindrical castings |
| 5 | $M_{\text{plate}} = \frac{a \cdot b \cdot c}{2(ab + bc + ca)}$ | Modulus of a rectangular plate ($c \le a, b$) | cm | Plate-like castings |
| 6 | $\rho V H_f = h A \Delta T \cdot t_s$ | Energy balance for ideal (Newtonian) cooling | J, kg, J/kg, W/m²K, m², K, s | Base of Chvorinov's derivation |
| 7 | $t_s = \frac{\rho H_f}{h \Delta T} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)$ | Linear form of solidification time | s | Assumes $h$ is constant |
| 8 | $t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$ with $B = \frac{\rho H_f}{h \Delta T}\left(1 + \frac{k_m \delta_m}{k_c}\right)$ | Refined Chvorinov with mold interface layer | min | Accounting for insulating mold skin growth |
| 9 | $\frac{t_{s,1}}{t_{s,2}} = \left(\frac{M_1}{M_2}\right)^2$ | Ratio form (used when $B$ cancels) | dimensionless | Compare two castings in the **same** mold |
| 10 | $M_{\text{riser}} > M_{\text{casting}}$ | **Riser Design Rule** — riser freezes last | cm | Foundry design of feeding system |
| 11 | $r^* = \frac{2\gamma_{SL} T_m}{H_f \Delta T}$ | Critical nucleus radius (homogeneous) | m or cm | Nucleation theory |
| 12 | $\Delta G^* = \frac{16 \pi \gamma_{SL}^3 T_m^2}{3 H_f^2 (\Delta T)^2}$ | Critical activation energy barrier | J | Nucleation theory |
| 13 | $\Delta G^*_{\text{het}} = f(\theta) \cdot \Delta G^*_{\text{hom}}$ | Heterogeneous barrier reduction | J | Wetting-angle analysis |
| 14 | $N_{\text{het}} = K_1 \exp\left(\frac{-\Delta G^*_{\text{het}}}{k_B T}\right)$ | Steady-state nucleation rate | nuclei/m³·s | Kinetics of nucleation |
| 15 | $\lambda = K_2 \cdot G^{-a} \cdot R^{-b}$ | Primary dendrite arm spacing (CASTING microstructure law) | m | Microstructure prediction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX warning for KTU board work:** When writing $\frac{V}{A}$ (the modulus) inside a sentence, always parenthesize it. The bar/pipe issue in tabular exam sheets has cost students marks. Use $\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)$ everywhere.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How Solidification Theory is Used |
|---|---|
| **Riser Design in Foundries** | Apply $M_{\text{riser}} > M_{\text{casting}}$ to ensure feeding channel is open until casting freezes |
| **Investment Casting of Turbine Blades** | Pre-calculate $t_s$ to determine **mold pre-heat temperature** to control grain structure |
| **Continuous Casting of Steel Billets** | Use $t_s$ to set **withdrawal speed** of the strand from the mold |
| **Directional Solidification (DS) of Superalloys** | Use $G/R$ ratio to enforce single-crystal turbine blades (planar growth maintained) |
| **Chill Placement in Sand Molds** | Use Chvorinov's rule with $B_{\text{chill}} < B_{\text{sand}}$ to **locally accelerate** solidification |
| **Inoculation of Cast Iron** | Add $\frac{1}{2}$% ferrosilicon to provide heterogeneous nuclei, refining graphite flakes |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Chvorinov's Rule

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **single most repeated Part B question** in KTU Manufacturing Processes examinations. Memorize every step. The expected answer length in a 7-mark sub-question is approximately **one full page of writing**.

**Statement to Prove:**
*For a casting solidifying in a mold, the time required for complete solidification is given by $t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$, where $B$ is the mold constant and $n$ is an exponent close to 2.*

#### Step 1 — Identify the Heat to be Removed

When a casting of volume $V$ and density $\rho$ solidifies, the **total latent heat** liberated is:

$$Q = m \cdot H_f = (\rho V) H_f$$

This is the *total energy* that must flow out of the casting into the mold for complete solidification.

#### Step 2 — Identify the Rate of Heat Extraction

By **Newton's Law of Cooling**, the instantaneous rate of heat flow from the casting to the mold is:

$$\dot{Q} = h A \Delta T$$

where:
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient at the casting–mold interface (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = effective contact area between casting and mold (m²)
- $\Delta T = T_{\text{casting surface}} - T_{\text{mold surface}}$ (K)

#### Step 3 — Equate Heat to Rate × Time

If we (for a first approximation) assume $h$ and $\Delta T$ are **constants** during solidification, then:

$$Q = \dot{Q} \cdot t_s$$

$$\rho V H_f = h A \Delta T \cdot t_s$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for Solidification Time $t_s$

Rearranging for $t_s$:

$$t_s = \frac{\rho H_f}{h \Delta T} \cdot \frac{V}{A}$$

This is the **linear form** of Chvorinov's rule — solidification time is **proportional to the modulus** $M = V/A$.

#### Step 5 — Account for Mold Interface Resistance (The Key Correction)

In practice, an **insulating air gap or solidified skin** forms at the casting–mold interface as solidification proceeds. The effective heat transfer is no longer Newtonian; it is governed by **conduction through this growing layer** of thickness $\delta_m$ and thermal conductivity $k_m$.

The instantaneous heat flux becomes:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \frac{k_m A \Delta T_{\text{eff}}}{\delta_m(t)}$$

The skin thickness grows with time, so $\delta_m \propto t^{1/2}$ (Fourier conduction). Substituting this time-dependence into the energy balance and integrating from $0$ to $t_s$ gives:

$$t_s \propto \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$$

#### Step 6 — Combine into the Final Chvorinov Equation

Combining the linear proportionality constant (now including the mold conduction term) with the squared modulus:

$$t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$$

where the **mold constant** is:

$$B = \frac{\rho H_f}{h \Delta T} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{k_m \delta_m}{k_c}\right)$$

and empirically, $n \approx 2$ for most engineering metals and molds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $n = 2$? (KTU favourite 2-mark question)**
> Because the heat-transfer resistance at the interface grows with time (the solidified skin acts as an insulator), the effective $\Delta T$ driving force diminishes, and the rate of heat extraction is not constant. The integration of the time-dependent flux yields an exponent of exactly 2 in the ideal case. In real castings, $n$ is found experimentally between 1.5 and 2.5.

### 3.2 Worked Example #1 — Classic Chvorinov Calculation

**Problem:**
A steel casting has volume $V = 500 \text{ cm}^3$ and surface area $A = 450 \text{ cm}^2$. It is cast in a sand mold with $B = 3.0 \text{ min/cm}^2$ and $n = 2$.
- (a) Find the solidification time.
- (b) If the same casting is made in a metallic chill mold with $B = 1.0 \text{ min/cm}^2$, find the new time.
- (c) Comment on the result.

**Solution:**

#### Part (a): Solidification time in sand mold

Compute the modulus:

$$\frac{V}{A} = \frac{500 \text{ cm}^3}{450 \text{ cm}^2} = 1.1111 \text{ cm}$$

Apply Chvorinov's rule with $n = 2$:

$$t_{s,\text{sand}} = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2 = 3.0 \times (1.1111)^2 \text{ min}$$

$$(1.1111)^2 = 1.2346$$

$$t_{s,\text{sand}} = 3.0 \times 1.2346 = 3.7038 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{t_{s,\text{sand}} \approx 3.70 \text{ min}}$$

#### Part (b): Solidification time in chill mold

Modulus is unchanged (same casting geometry):

$$\frac{V}{A} = 1.1111 \text{ cm}$$

Apply with $B = 1.0 \text{ min/cm}^2$:

$$t_{s,\text{chill}} = 1.0 \times (1.1111)^2 = 1.2346 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{t_{s,\text{chill}} \approx 1.23 \text{ min}}$$

#### Part (c): Engineering Comment

$$\text{Time reduction} = \frac{3.70 - 1.23}{3.70} \times 100\% = 66.7\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The metallic chill has a much higher thermal conductivity than sand, so heat is extracted far more rapidly. The casting solidifies **3 times faster**, producing a **finer grain structure**, **higher strength** (Hall-Petch), and **lower porosity** — but at the cost of higher mold expense and possible thermal shock to the casting surface.

### 3.3 Worked Example #2 — Riser Design Using Chvorinov's Rule

**Problem:**
A cylindrical casting has diameter $D = 10 \text{ cm}$ and length $L = 30 \text{ cm}$. The mold constant is $B = 3.5 \text{ min/cm}^2$. Design a **spherical riser** such that it freezes **at least 25% later** than the casting. Take $n = 2$.

**Solution:**

#### Step 1 — Modulus of the casting

$$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c} = \frac{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L}{\pi D L + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2}$$

Numerator:

$$\frac{\pi}{4}(10)^2(30) = 750 \pi \approx 2356.19 \text{ cm}^3$$

Denominator:

$$\pi(10)(30) + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(10)^2 = 300\pi + 50\pi = 350\pi \approx 1099.56 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$M_c = \frac{2356.19}{1099.56} \approx 2.143 \text{ cm}$$

#### Step 2 — Solidification time of casting

$$t_{s,c} = 3.5 \times (2.143)^2 = 3.5 \times 4.591 = 16.07 \text{ min}$$

#### Step 3 — Required solidification time of riser

The riser must freeze 25% later:

$$t_{s,r} \geq 1.25 \times 16.07 = 20.09 \text{ min}$$

#### Step 4 — Required modulus of spherical riser

$$t_{s,r} = B \left(M_r\right)^2$$

$$M_r = \sqrt{\frac{t_{s,r}}{B}} = \sqrt{\frac{20.09}{3.5}} = \sqrt{5.74} = 2.397 \text{ cm}$$

#### Step 5 — Radius of spherical riser

For a sphere, $M_r = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{R}{3}$.

Therefore:

$$R = 3 M_r = 3 \times 2.397 = 7.19 \text{ cm}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{riser}} \geq 7.2 \text{ cm} \text{ (diameter} \geq 14.4 \text{ cm)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical foundry note:** The riser diameter is **larger** than the casting diameter (10 cm) — this is correct in design intent, but in practice the riser is **placed on top** of the casting (not embedded within it), so the engineer must verify that the riser's bottom surface is **above the casting's last-to-freeze region** (typically the thermal center or hot spot).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Engineering Question | Required Formula | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| *"How long to open the mold?"* | $t_s = B (V/A)^n$ | Direct application of Chvorinov |
| *"Which freezes first — sphere or cube?"* | Compare $(V/A)$ values | Modulus comparison |
| *"What size riser do I need?"* | $M_r = \sqrt{t_{s,r}/B} = 1.25\sqrt{t_{s,c}/B}$ | Riser must freeze last |
| *"Why is my casting porous?"* | Check if $M_{\text{riser}} < M_{\text{casting}}$ | Riser froze first, no feed metal |
| *"What if I change mold material?"* | New $B$ enters directly | $B$ is mold-specific |
| *"How does grain size change?"* | $G \cdot R = $ const, and $\lambda \propto (G \cdot R)^{-1/3}$ | Microstructure law |
