## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define solidification time. State Chvorinov's rule and explain the significance of the exponent $n$ in the rule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Solidification Time ($t_s$):** It is the total time required for a casting to transform completely from the liquid state to the solid state, measured from the moment of pouring until the last liquid at the thermal center has solidified.

**Chvorinov's Rule:**

$$t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$$

where $V$ is the volume of the casting, $A$ is the surface area in contact with the mold, $B$ is the mold constant (depends on mold material, metal properties, and pouring conditions), and $n$ is an exponent.

**Significance of $n$:**
- $n$ is empirically close to **2** for most engineering castings.
- Physically, $n = 2$ arises because the heat-extraction rate is **not constant** during solidification — the growing solidified skin acts as a thermal insulator.
- The value of $n$ is found experimentally to lie between **1.5 and 2.5** depending on the metal, mold, and superheat conditions.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous nucleation. Which one is dominant in practical castings and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Homogeneous Nucleation | Heterogeneous Nucleation |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Within the bulk of the liquid | On a foreign surface (mold wall, inclusion) |
| **Undercooling Required** | Very high (100–300 °C) | Very small (< 10 °C) |
| **Energy Barrier** | High, given by $\Delta G^*_{\text{hom}}$ | Lower by factor $f(\theta) < 1$ |
| **Occurrence in Practice** | Rare | Dominant |

**Dominance in castings:** Heterogeneous nucleation is dominant in **all practical casting operations** because the molten metal is never perfectly pure (it contains inclusions, oxide films, and dissolved gases), and the **mold wall is always present** to act as a substrate. The wetting angle $\theta < 180°$ drastically lowers the activation barrier, allowing nucleation to occur with minimal undercooling.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. The student attempts **ONE of the two** questions (Choice system).

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Derive Chvorinov's rule for the solidification of a metal casting, clearly stating the assumptions. Discuss why the exponent $n$ deviates from unity in practice. (7 marks)**

**(b) A copper cube of side 8 cm is to be cast in a sand mold. The mold constant is $B = 4.0$ min/cm² and $n = 2$. Determine: (i) the solidification time of the cube, and (ii) the diameter of a spherical riser that will freeze 30% later than the cube. Assume the riser is placed in the same mold. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

[Write the full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key points to mention:]

- Total heat to be removed: $Q = \rho V H_f$.
- Newton's law of cooling rate: $\dot{Q} = hA \Delta T$.
- Equating gives $t_s = \frac{\rho H_f}{h \Delta T} \cdot \frac{V}{A}$ — linear form.

**Assumptions:**
1. Constant heat transfer coefficient $h$ at the casting-mold interface.
2. Uniform temperature difference $\Delta T$ throughout solidification.
3. Negligible superheat content (only latent heat is considered).
4. No mold-metal reaction or interfacial gap.
5. The casting is a simple geometric body with well-defined volume and surface area.

**Why $n \neq 1$ in practice:**
- An **insulating solidified skin** forms at the interface as solidification progresses.
- This skin **grows in thickness** with time, increasing thermal resistance.
- Therefore the heat flux $\dot{Q}(t)$ decreases as solidification proceeds.
- Integration of the time-dependent flux yields the squared relation, giving $n = 2$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Solidification time of the cube:**

$$V = 8^3 = 512 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$A = 6 \times 8^2 = 384 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$M_c = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{512}{384} = 1.333 \text{ cm}$$

$$t_{s,c} = B \cdot M_c^2 = 4.0 \times (1.333)^2 = 4.0 \times 1.777 = 7.11 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Diameter of spherical riser:**

Required solidification time (30% later):

$$t_{s,r} = 1.30 \times 7.11 = 9.243 \text{ min}$$

Required modulus:

$$M_r = \sqrt{\frac{t_{s,r}}{B}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.243}{4.0}} = \sqrt{2.311} = 1.520 \text{ cm}$$

For a sphere, $M_r = R/3$, hence:

$$R = 3 \times 1.520 = 4.56 \text{ cm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Riser diameter} = 2R = 9.12 \text{ cm} \text{ (minimum)}}$$

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Explain the mechanism of solidification of a pure metal. With neat sketches, describe the formation of (i) chill zone, (ii) columnar zone, and (iii) equiaxed zone in a sand-cast ingot. (7 marks)**

**(b) A cylindrical steel riser of diameter 12 cm and height 18 cm is used to feed a casting of volume 1500 cm³ and surface area 1100 cm². The mold constant $B = 3.0$ min/cm². Find: (i) solidification time of the casting, (ii) solidification time of the riser, (iii) comment on whether the riser is effective. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism of solidification (7 marks):**

The solidification of a pure metal occurs at a **single, sharp temperature** (the freezing point $T_m$) with the liberation of **latent heat of fusion**. It proceeds in **two atomic steps**:

**Step 1 — Nucleation:** Tiny clusters of atoms in the liquid (embryos) form by statistical fluctuations. Most redissolve, but those exceeding the **critical radius $r^*$** become stable nuclei. In real castings, this happens via **heterogeneous nucleation** at the cold mold wall.

**Step 2 — Growth:** Atoms from the liquid attach to the stable nuclei, and the solid-liquid interface advances into the liquid.

**Three zones in a sand-cast ingot:**

1. **Chill zone** — A thin layer of fine, randomly oriented equiaxed grains formed at the mold wall due to **very rapid undercooling** when the cold wall extracts heat at a high rate. Many nuclei form simultaneously and grow only briefly before impinging on each other.

2. **Columnar zone** — Beyond the chill zone, the temperature gradient $G$ is steep and the growth direction is constrained to be **opposite to the heat flow**. Grains with their [100] direction aligned with heat flow grow preferentially, producing **long, parallel columnar dendrites** aligned perpendicular to the mold wall.

3. **Equiaxed zone** — At the center of the ingot, the thermal gradient is low and constitutional supercooling allows fragments of dendrite arms to be carried into the bulk liquid. These fragments act as new nuclei, growing equally in all directions as **equiaxed grains** of random orientation.

> A labelled diagram of the ingot cross-section (chill–columnar–equiaxed) should be drawn, marking the mold wall, the thermal center, and the direction of heat flow.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Solidification time of the casting:**

$$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c} = \frac{1500}{1100} = 1.364 \text{ cm}$$

$$t_{s,c} = 3.0 \times (1.364)^2 = 3.0 \times 1.860 = 5.58 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Solidification time of the riser:**

Riser volume:

$$V_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(12)^2(18) = 2035.75 \text{ cm}^3$$

Riser surface area (cylinder with two ends):

$$A_r = \pi(12)(18) + 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(12)^2 = 678.58 + 226.19 = 904.78 \text{ cm}^2$$

Modulus of riser:

$$M_r = \frac{2035.75}{904.78} = 2.250 \text{ cm}$$

Solidification time:

$$t_{s,r} = 3.0 \times (2.250)^2 = 3.0 \times 5.0625 = 15.19 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) Comment on effectiveness:**

$$M_r = 2.25 \text{ cm} \quad \text{vs} \quad M_c = 1.364 \text{ cm}$$

Since $M_r > M_c$ (in fact $M_r / M_c = 1.65 > 1.25$), the riser freezes **later** than the casting. The riser is **effective** in principle — it will feed liquid metal to compensate shrinkage of the casting. However, the engineer must also confirm that the riser is **positioned above the hot spot** of the casting and that the **feeder neck** does not freeze prematurely.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to keep $V$ in cm³ and $A$ in cm² consistently. $V/A$ must come out in cm. Losing 1 mark for sloppy units is very common.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 1.25 to 1.30:** Riser design textbooks (and KTU) require $M_r \geq 1.25 \cdot M_c$ as a safety margin. If you compute a riser modulus that is *barely* greater than $M_c$, it is considered **unsafe** in foundry practice.
> 3. **Mold-constant confusion:** $B$ for sand mold is typically 2 to 5 min/cm², for die casting 0.5 to 1.0 min/cm². Mixing them gives wrong answers.
> 4. **Surface area of a hollow shape:** When a riser is open at the top, the free surface exposed to air also counts toward $A$ (it extracts heat too). Many students miss this.
> 5. **Exponent $n$:** The default is $n = 2$. Writing $(V/A)$ to the power 1 will cost full marks in derivation questions.
> 6. **Riser shape misconception:** Students often state that the *larger* the riser, the *better*. In fact, the *modulus* (V/A) controls freezing, not the absolute volume. A large hollow riser can be useless.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last 5 Minutes of Exam Revision — Memorize This List.**

- [ ] **Solidification** = liquid → solid transformation with **latent heat release**.
- [ ] **Two atomic events:** **Nucleation** (birth) and **Growth** (propagation).
- [ ] **Homogeneous nucleation** needs high $\Delta T$ and has high $\Delta G^*$; **Heterogeneous** is dominant in castings.
- [ ] **Critical radius** $r^* = \frac{2\gamma_{SL} T_m}{H_f \Delta T}$ — decreases as undercooling increases.
- [ ] **Activation barrier** $\Delta G^* = \frac{16\pi \gamma_{SL}^3 T_m^2}{3 H_f^2 (\Delta T)^2}$ — decreases as $1/(\Delta T)^2$.
- [ ] **Heterogeneous barrier reduction factor** $f(\theta) = \frac{(2 + \cos\theta)(1 - \cos\theta)^2}{4}$.
- [ ] **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$, with $n \approx 2$.
- [ ] **Mold constant $B$** depends on: mold material, metal thermal properties, superheat, and pouring temperature.
- [ ] **Sand mold:** $B \approx 2$ to $5$ min/cm² (slow cooling).
- [ ] **Metal/die mold:** $B \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$ min/cm² (fast cooling).
- [ ] **Modulus $M = V/A$** controls freezing tendency. Sphere has the **highest** modulus for a given volume — freezes slowest; thin plate has the **lowest** — freezes fastest.
- [ ] **Riser design rule:** $M_{\text{riser}} \geq 1.25 \cdot M_{\text{casting}}$.
- [ ] **Riser shape preference:** **Sphere > Cylinder > Cube** (spheres have the highest $M$ for given volume → freeze last).
- [ ] **Three zones in cast ingot:** **Chill (wall) → Columnar (middle radial) → Equiaxed (center)**.
- [ ] **Hall-Petch + Chvorinov link:** Faster cooling → smaller grains → higher strength. This is why **chills are used** at critical sections.
- [ ] **Hot spot:** The last region of the casting to solidify. The riser must be placed **directly above the hot spot**, not on the side.
- [ ] **G/R ratio controls morphology:** High G/R → planar; Low G/R → equiaxed dendritic.
- [ ] **Primary dendrite arm spacing** $\lambda \propto G^{-a} R^{-b}$ (with $a, b \approx 1/3$ to 1/2).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Word:** Chvorinov's rule is an **empirical, not exact** law. It assumes a uniform cooling rate and constant $B$. For complex 3D geometries, engineers today use **finite-element simulation software** (e.g., MAGMA, ProCAST, FLOW-3D) which numerically solve the heat-transfer equation. However, Chvorinov's rule **remains the conceptual foundation** of all such simulations, and the KTU board continues to test it rigorously in both theory and numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of Gating System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Elements of Gating System # MODULE 1 — ELEMENTS OF GATING SYSTEM ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > A **Gating System** is a precisely engineered network of interconnected refractory channels and cavities built into the sand mould that delivers, controls, and regulates the flow of molten metal from the pouring basin to the mould cavity, ensuring complete, sound, and defect-free castings. In the context of **sand casting**, the gating system acts as the *vascular system* of the mould. It is responsible not only for delivering the metal but also for: 1. Controlling the **velocity** and **flow rate** of molten metal. 2. Preventing **turbulence**, **aspiration**, and **oxidation** of the liquid metal. 3. Trapping **slag, dross, sand inclusions**, and other contaminants before they enter the cavity. 4. Minimising **shrinkage porosity** and **gas defects**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** > The performance of an entire casting — its surface finish, density, mechanical strength, and dimensional accuracy — depends more on the *design of the gating system* than on any other single mould-making variable. ### 1.2 Elements / Components of a Gating System The gating system comprises the following sequential elements (in the order the molten metal encounters them): | Order | Element | Primary Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Pouring Cup / Pouring Basin** | Receives molten metal from the ladle; smoothens initial flow; reduces splash. | | 2 | **Sprue (Down-Sprue / Up-Sprue)** | Vertical channel that conveys molten metal downwards by gravity. | | 3 | **Sprue Well (Sprue Base)** | Cushions the impact of falling metal; prevents mould erosion. | | 4 | **Runner** | Horizontal channel that distributes metal from sprue to gates. | | 5 | **Gate (In-Gate / Ingate)** | Final entry point of metal into the mould cavity. | | 6 | **Riser / Feeder** *(Auxiliary, but part of feed system)* | Supplies additional molten metal to compensate for solidification shrinkage. | | 7 | **Choke** | The narrowest cross-section in the system; controls flow rate. | | 8 | **Skimmer / Slag Trap** *(optional)* | Retains oxide films and slag. | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Plumbing of a House" > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** > Imagine pouring water from a bucket into a fish tank through a long flexible pipe. > - The **bucket lip** = Pouring cup > - The **vertical pipe (gravity drop)** = Sprue > - The **pipe elbow (cushion)** = Sprue well > - The **horizontal pipe** = Runner > - The **small nozzle tip** = Gate > - The **fish tank** = Mould cavity > - The **overflow pipe** = Riser > > If the nozzle is too wide, water splashes (mould erosion). If too narrow, flow rate is too slow (misruns). This is exactly why **gate ratio design** is critical. ### 1.4 Why is a Gating System Needed? A gating system is **mandatory** in any gravity sand casting operation because: - Molten metal cannot be placed directly into the cavity without **erosion of the sand mould**. - Without controlled flow, the metal would **entrap gases**, **oxidise**, and **trap inclusions**, leading to defective castings. - A well-designed gating system ensures **direction of solidification** is correct (e.g., progressive towards the riser). # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Molten Metal Traverses the System The sequence of fluid flow events is as follows: 1. **Filling the Pouring Cup** — Ladle pours metal; the cup's bowl-shaped geometry dissipates kinetic energy. 2. **Down-Sprue Flow** — Metal accelerates under gravity. To prevent suction (aspiration of gases), the sprue is **tapered** (typically a taper ratio of 1:100 to 1:200 on each side). The cross-section at the bottom is smaller than at the top to compensate for gravity-induced acceleration (Bernoulli's principle). 3. **Sprue Well Impact** — The well acts as a *liquid cushion*; it absorbs kinetic energy and prevents sand wash. 4. **Runner Distribution** — Metal spreads horizontally; ideally the runner is **gated from the bottom** (i.e., runner exits the sprue well from its base, not from its side) to prevent slag from entering. 5. **Gate Entry** — Final critical orifice (choke) that controls fill rate. 6. **Cavity Fill** — The cavity fills in a calculated time, *t*. 7. **Solidification** — Riser feeds shrinkage; casting cools directionally. ## 2.2 Classification of Gating Systems | Criterion | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | **By pressure condition** | Unpressurised (Open) | $A_g > A_r > A_s$ — Sprue controls flow | | | Pressurised | $A_s > A_r > A_g$ — Gate controls flow | | | Partially pressurised | $A_r > A_s > A_g$ — Balanced | | **By location on mould** | Top gating (Drop gate) | Metal enters from top — used for ferrous alloys | | | Parting line gating | Metal enters at mould parting surface | | | Bottom gating | Metal enters from bottom — used for non-ferrous, thin castings | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Concept:** > For **ferrous castings** (iron, steel) — **bottom gating** is preferred to reduce oxidation and turbulence. For **non-ferrous castings** (Al, Cu, Mg) — **top or parting-line gating** is common. ## 2.3 The "Why" — Governing Principles | Principle | Why it matters | |---|---| | **Bernoulli's Equation** | As metal falls in the sprue, its velocity increases. If the sprue were cylindrical, suction would form. Tapering restores the desired flow regime. | | **Continuity Equation** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | To keep velocity constant, cross-section must change inversely with flow velocity. | | **Chvorinov's Rule** | Determines whether the riser freezes before the casting — drives the design of the entire feed system. | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they form the basis of ≥70% of KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical questions.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = A \cdot v = \dfrac{A_t}{t}$ | Volumetric flow rate through gate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | | 2 | $v = C_d \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Velocity at sprue bottom (Torricelli's) | m/s | | 3 | $A_s : A_r : A_g = 1 : 2 : 4$ (Unpressurised) | Sprue-Runner-Gate area ratio | ratio | | 4 | $A_s : A_r : A_g = 4 : 2 : 1$ (Pressurised) | Standard pressurised ratio | ratio | | 5 | $A_s : A_r : A_g = 1.5 : 1.1 : 1$ (Partially Pressurised) | Balanced ratio | ratio | | 6 | $t = k \sqrt{W}$ (or $t = K (W)^{1/3}$) | Pouring time — empirical | seconds | | 7 | $t = C \left(\dfrac{W}{R} \right)^{1/3}$ | Refined pouring time (more accurate) | seconds | | 8 | $A_g = \dfrac{W}{\rho \cdot v \cdot t}$ | Gate area from mass, density, velocity, time | $\text{m}^2$ | | 9 | Sprue taper: $A_b = \dfrac{A_t}{2}$ (for cylindrical sprue of length $h$ with $h$-dependent correction) | Bottom area from top area | $\text{m}^2$ | | 10 | $h_s = h_c - \dfrac{h^2}{2 c}$ | Effective sprue head (height correction) | m | | 11 | Chvorinov: $t_s = B \left(\dfrac{V}{A} \right)^2$ | Solidification time of casting/riser | seconds | | 12 | Riser: $t_r > t_c \Rightarrow \dfrac{V_r}{A_r} > \dfrac{V_c}{A_c}$ | Modulus condition for feed | — | | 13 | $\text{Area ratio} = \dfrac{A_s}{A_g}$ | Unpressurised → $>1$ ; Pressurised → $<1$ | — | > Where: > - $A_s$ = sprue top area > - $A_r$ = runner cross-section > - $A_g$ = gate area > - $W$ = casting mass (kg) > - $t$ = pouring time (s) > - $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge ≈ 0.6 > - $h$ = sprue height / effective head (m) > - $V/A$ = modulus (m) > - $B$ = mould constant ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The principles you learn here are directly applied in: - **Foundry Industry** (mass production of automotive engine blocks, pump housings, gear blanks). - **Investment Casting** (jewellery, aerospace turbine blades). - **Die Casting** (high-pressure variants use similar fluid logic, but forced under pressure). - **Continuous Casting** (tundish → nozzle — geometrically analogous to a pouring basin → sprue). > [!TIP] > **Pro Tip for KTU Examinations:** In gate ratio problems, **always state the system type** before writing the ratio. Examiners award 2 marks just for correctly identifying whether the system is pressurised, unpressurised, or balanced. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sprue Taper Ratio (Why Sprue is Tapered) ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup Molten metal falls freely through a vertical sprue of height $h$. We want the metal to exit at the sprue bottom with **constant velocity** so that: - No aspiration occurs. - The mould is not eroded. - The flow regime is stable. ### 3.1.2 Governing Equation Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between the top of the sprue (point 1) and a point at depth $y$ below (point 2): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + h_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_2$$ At both points, $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$ (open to atmosphere). At the top: $v_1 \approx 0$ (pouring basin is wide), and $h_1 = h$. At depth $y$: $h_2 = h - y$. So the equation simplifies to: $$h = \frac{v_y^2}{2g} + (h - y)$$ ### 3.1.3 Solving for Velocity $$v_y^2 = 2 g y$$ $$v_y = \sqrt{2 g y}$$ This is **Torricelli's theorem**. ### 3.1.4 Implication for Cross-Section As $y$ increases, $v_y$ increases. By **continuity** $A_y v_y = A_{top} v_{top}$, so: $$A_y = \frac{A_{top} v_{top}}{v_y} = \frac{A_{top} v_{top}}{\sqrt{2 g y}}$$ Therefore, the area at depth $y$ must **decrease** with increasing $y$ — this is why the sprue is **tapered**, narrower at the bottom. ### 3.1.5 Practical Taper Calculation For a sprue of length $h$ and top area $A_t$, if the velocity at the bottom is $v_b = \sqrt{2gh}$, then for constant velocity flow, the bottom area is: $$A_b = \frac{A_t}{2}$$ A **practical taper** is specified as: top area : bottom area = **2 : 1** (i.e., each side of a rectangular sprue is tapered such that the bottom is half the top dimension along the depth direction). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Quick-Fact:** A "1:100 taper" means for every 100 mm of sprue length, the side dimension reduces by 1 mm. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pouring Time Formula ### 3.2.1 Empirical Origin The pouring time $t$ governs the soundness of the casting. A common **empirical formula** (Dieter / KTU reference) is: $$t = k \sqrt{W}$$ where $W$ is the casting mass (kg) and $k$ is a constant depending on the alloy and casting conditions. ### 3.2.2 Refined Form (More Rigorous) For thin castings, a better correlation is: $$t = C \left(\frac{W}{R}\right)^{1/3}$$ where $R$ is the average section thickness (m), and $C$ is a constant. ### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of Gate Area We start from continuity of flow through the gate: $$Q = A_g \cdot v_g = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot t}$$ Solving for $A_g$: $$A_g = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot v_g \cdot t}$$ Substituting $v_g = C_d \sqrt{2 g h_s}$: $$A_g = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot t \cdot C_d \sqrt{2 g h_s}}$$ This is the **gate area equation** tested in KTU. ### 3.2.4 Numerical Illustration (KTU Style) **Problem:** A casting of mass $W = 50$ kg is poured in $t = 20$ s. The effective sprue head is $h_s = 0.20$ m. Density $\rho = 7000$ kg/m³ (cast iron). $C_d = 0.6$. Find the gate area. **Step 1:** Compute $v_g$. $$v_g = C_d \sqrt{2 g h_s} = 0.6 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.20}$$ $$v_g = 0.6 \times \sqrt{3.924} = 0.6 \times 1.981 = 1.189 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2:** Compute $A_g$. $$A_g = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot t \cdot v_g} = \frac{50}{7000 \times 20 \times 1.189}$$ $$A_g = \frac{50}{166{,}460} = 3.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3:** Convert to cm². $$A_g = 3.00 \text{ cm}^2$$ This is a typical KTU Part B numerical answer. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Chvorinov's Rule (Riser Sizing) ### 3.3.1 The Setup Chvorinov's rule states that solidification time of a casting is proportional to the square of its **modulus** (V/A): $$t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$$ ### 3.3.2 Riser Design Condition For a riser to feed the casting effectively: $$t_{riser} > t_{casting}$$ Substituting Chvorinov's rule: $$B \left(\frac{V_r}{A_r}\right)^2 > B \left(\frac{V_c}{A_c}\right)^2$$ Since $B$ is the same for both (same mould material), we simplify to: $$\frac{V_r}{A_r} > \frac{V_c}{A_c}$$ This is the famous **"modulus of riser must exceed modulus of casting"** condition. For a **side riser**, the practical rule is: $$\frac{V_r}{A_r} \geq 1.2 \times \frac{V_c}{A_c}$$ The factor **1.2** accounts for the safety margin (head losses, hot-spot effects, surface tension). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the safety factor of 1.2. The examiner will deduct 1 mark if the safety factor is missing. # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sequential Flow of Molten Metal The following Mermaid block represents the **architectural flow** of metal through a typical top-gated, unpressurised gating system. Each block is labelled with the corresponding process step and energy/velocity transformation. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ladle / Crucible
External metal source] --> B[Pouring Cup
Energy dissipation bowl] B --> C[Down Sprue
Gravity acceleration channel] C --> D[Sprue Well
Impact cushion reservoir] D --> E[Runner Bar
Horizontal distribution channel] E --> F[Gate / Ingate
Choke - controls flow rate] F --> G[Mould Cavity
Casting shape region] G --> H[Riser / Feeder
Liquid metal reservoir] H --> I[Shrinkage Compensation
Feeds volumetric deficit] I --> J[Solidified Casting
Final product] K[Slag and Oxide Inclusions] -.->|Trapped at| B K -.->|Skimmed at| D K -.->|Filtered at| E L[Sand Mould Erosion] -.->|Prevented at| C L -.->|Mitigated at| D style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#e59866,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style G fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#f8c471,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style I fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style J fill:#85929e,color:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - **Solid arrows (A → J)** = primary path of molten metal. > - **Dotted arrows (K, L)** = *undesirable* phenomena (inclusions, erosion) that the gating system is designed to **suppress or trap**. --- ## 4.2 Schematic — Physical Cross-Section of a Gating System > [!IMPORTANT] > Since Mermaid cannot render true physical engineering schematics, the following is a **textual block-level functional architecture** mapping a typical unpressurised top gating arrangement in a green-sand mould. This representation is the standard KTU board-illustration style. ``` ┌──────────── POURING CUP ────────────┐ │ (Funnelled entry, dissipates │ │ kinetic energy of ladle) │ └──────────────────┬──────────────────┘ │ ▼ ← Tapered ┌──────────── DOWN SPRUE ─────────────┐ │ (Vertical channel, narrows toward │ │ base to maintain constant velocity)│ └──────────────────┬──────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────── SPRUE WELL ─────────────┐ │ (Cylindrical reservoir at base, │ │ acts as liquid cushion & slag trap)│ └──────────────────┬──────────────────┘ │ (Metal exits from base — not side) ▼ ┌──────────── RUNNER BAR ─────────────┐ │ (Horizontal channel along parting │ │ plane, distributes to multiple │ │ gates; area = 2 × Sprue) │ └──────────────────┬──────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────── GATE ───────────────────┐ │ (Final entry into cavity; │ │ CHOKE point; area = 4 × Sprue) │ └──────────────────┬──────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────── MOULD CAVITY ─────────────┐ │ (Casting shape, fills in time t) │ │ ↑ Riser extends upward │ │ ↑ Feeds shrinkage on solidification│ └──────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` --- ## 4.3 Logical Decision Tree — Selecting a Gating System Type ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start: Choose Gating System] --> Q1{Alloy type?} Q1 -->|Ferrous
Iron / Steel| Q2{Component thickness?} Q1 -->|Non-ferrous
Al / Cu| Q3{Fill time required?} Q2 -->|Thin section| R1[Bottom Gating
Unpressurised] Q2 -->|Thick section| R2[Top or Parting-line
Pressurised] Q3 -->|Fast fill| R3[Pressurised System
A_s greater than A_g] Q3 -->|Slow fill| R4[Unpressurised System
A_g greater than A_s] style Start fill:#fff,stroke:#333 style Q1 fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#333 style Q2 fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#333 style Q3 fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#333 style R1 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333 style R2 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333 style R3 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333 style R4 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Missing System Type Declaration** — In gate ratio problems, students write the ratio without identifying whether the system is pressurised or unpressurised. This is a **2-mark penalty zone** in 14-mark questions. > 2. **Unit Inconsistency** — Converting kg/s to m³/s requires dividing by density. Students often forget ρ. > 3. **Forgetting the 1.2 Safety Factor** in Chvorinov's modulus comparison — guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > 4. **Confusing Choke and Sprue** — The choke is the **smallest cross-section** in the gating system, which may be the gate, the sprue, or the runner, depending on the design. > 5. **Drawing the Riser at the Wrong Location** — Riser should be at the **last-to-solidify (hot spot)** region, not arbitrarily placed. --- ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Define a gating system. List its main elements.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A gating system is the network of refractory channels built into a sand mould to deliver and control the flow of molten metal from the pouring point to the mould cavity. Its main elements are: **(1)** Pouring cup, **(2)** Sprue (down-sprue), **(3)** Sprue well, **(4)** Runner, **(5)** Gate (in-gate), and optionally **(6)** Skimmer and **(7)** Riser. The system must ensure complete fill, minimise turbulence, trap slag, and prevent mould erosion. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Why is the down-sprue tapered and not cylindrical?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A cylindrical sprue would cause the molten metal to **accelerate under gravity** as it descends. This acceleration leads to **(1)** aspiration (suction) of gases, **(2)** mould erosion at the sprue base, and **(3)** turbulent entry into the cavity. A tapered sprue (typically with the bottom area = ½ of the top area) **compensates for gravity-induced acceleration** and ensures a **near-constant exit velocity**, producing a smooth, controlled flow (Bernoulli's principle applied to open-channel flow). --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme requires **internal choice** in Part B. You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]` **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the various elements of a typical unpressurised gating system used in sand casting. Describe the function of each element. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steel casting weighing **80 kg** is to be poured in a green-sand mould. The effective sprue head is **25 cm**, the discharge coefficient is **0.6**, and the pouring time is **30 s**. Density of steel = **7850 kg/m³**. Calculate the **gate area** required. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer **(a) Elements of an Unpressurised Gating System (7 marks):** | Element | Sketch Symbol | Function | |---|---|---| | **1. Pouring Cup** | Funnel-shaped at top of sprue | Receives metal from the ladle; reduces splashing; initial slag retention. | | **2. Sprue (Down-sprue)** | Tapered vertical channel | Conveys metal downward by gravity; tapered to maintain constant velocity. | | **3. Sprue Well** | Cylindrical pit at sprue base | Cushions impact; traps heavy slag particles; prevents mould sand wash. | | **4. Runner** | Horizontal channel at parting plane | Distributes metal from sprue well to multiple gates; area typically = 2 × Sprue area. | | **5. Gate (Ingate)** | Thin entry slot into cavity | Final choke; controls fill rate; area typically = 4 × Sprue area (in unpressurised systems). | | **6. Skimmer** *(optional)* | Strainers in runner | Filters oxide films and dross. | | **7. Riser** | Vertical column above hot spot | Feeds liquid metal to compensate solidification shrinkage. | **Area ratio (Unpressurised):** $$A_s : A_r : A_g = 1 : 2 : 4$$ The system is *unpressurised* because the area progressively **increases** from sprue to gate, keeping the metal under a slight vacuum at the sprue top. This forces slag to remain trapped at the sprue well. **(b) Gate Area Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** $W = 80$ kg; $h_s = 0.25$ m; $C_d = 0.6$; $t = 30$ s; $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³. **Step 1:** Velocity at gate (Torricelli + Cd correction): $$v_g = C_d \sqrt{2 g h_s}$$ $$v_g = 0.6 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.25}$$ $$v_g = 0.6 \times \sqrt{4.905} = 0.6 \times 2.2147 = 1.329 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2:** Mass flow rate: $$\dot{m} = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{80}{30} = 2.667 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 3:** Volumetric flow rate: $$Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{2.667}{7850} = 3.397 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 4:** Gate area from $Q = A_g \cdot v_g$: $$A_g = \frac{Q}{v_g} = \frac{3.397 \times 10^{-4}}{1.329} = 2.556 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Final Answer:** $$A_g \approx 2.56 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 2.56 \text{ cm}^2$$ --- ### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **(a)** Differentiate between **pressurised** and **unpressurised** gating systems. State the gate area ratios used in each, and mention one specific application for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cylindrical riser of diameter **15 cm** and height **20 cm** is to feed a casting of effective modulus **1.5 cm**. Using Chvorinov's rule, determine whether the riser will solidify before, after, or at the same time as the casting. Assume a safety factor of 1.2 is required. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer **(a) Pressurised vs Unpressurised Gating Systems (7 marks):** | Parameter | Unpressurised (Open) | Pressurised | |---|---|---| | **Area ratio $A_s : A_r : A_g$** | $1 : 2 : 4$ | $4 : 2 : 1$ | | **Choke location** | Sprue top | Gate | | **Velocity at gate** | Low (gentle fill) | High (fast fill) | | **Slag retention** | Excellent (vacuum effect holds slag at sprue well) | Poor (high velocity carries slag into cavity) | | **Pressure at sprue base** | Below atmospheric (partial vacuum) | Above atmospheric | | **Application** | Ferrous castings (iron, steel); large castings; thin sections | Non-ferrous castings (Al alloys, Cu alloys); thick sections requiring fast fill | | **Advantages** | Clean casting, low turbulence | Reduced misruns, fast cycle time | | **Disadvantages** | Slow pour; can cause cold shuts in thick sections | Slag inclusions; mould erosion | > **Key Diagrammatic Cue (for full marks):** Draw a labelled cross-section of both systems showing area progression. **(b) Riser Solidification Check (7 marks):** **Given:** Riser — cylinder, $D = 15$ cm, $H = 20$ cm; Casting modulus $M_c = V_c/A_c = 1.5$ cm. **Step 1:** Calculate riser volume: $$V_r = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot H = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 15^2 \times 20 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 4500 = 3534.3 \text{ cm}^3$$ **Step 2:** Calculate riser surface area (top + bottom + curved surface; assuming top is open and effective area is from sides + bottom for heat loss): $$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 + \pi D H = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 225 + \pi \times 15 \times 20$$ $$A_r = 176.7 + 942.5 = 1119.2 \text{ cm}^2$$ (Exposed top is hot-metal surface and not considered for heat loss from mould; in some textbook conventions all five surfaces are used; we follow the *exposed-to-mould* convention.) **Step 3:** Riser modulus: $$M_r = \frac{V_r}{A_r} = \frac{3534.3}{1119.2} = 3.158 \text{ cm}$$ **Step 4:** Compare with casting modulus + safety factor: $$M_{r,\text{required}} = 1.2 \times M_c = 1.2 \times 1.5 = 1.8 \text{ cm}$$ **Step 5:** Condition check: $$M_r = 3.158 \text{ cm} > M_{r,\text{required}} = 1.8 \text{ cm} \;\;\checkmark$$ **Conclusion:** The riser modulus is **3.158 cm**, which is significantly greater than the required 1.8 cm. Therefore, the **riser will solidify AFTER the casting** and successfully feed the shrinkage. The design is **safe**. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Save This Page.** ### ✅ Definitions - **Gating system** = the network of channels in a mould that delivers molten metal. - **Riser** = liquid metal reservoir to feed shrinkage. - **Choke** = narrowest cross-section in the gating system. - **Modulus** = Volume ÷ Surface area = $V/A$ — drives solidification time. ### ✅ Element-wise Functions - **Pouring cup** → dissipates energy, traps initial slag. - **Sprue** → vertical gravity channel, *tapered*. - **Sprue well** → cushion + slag trap. - **Runner** → horizontal distributor; *exits sprue well from the base*. - **Gate** → final entry; **choke** in pressurised systems. - **Riser** → feeds shrinkage; placed at **hot spot**. ### ✅ Critical Formulas 1. $A_g = \dfrac{W}{\rho \cdot t \cdot C_d \sqrt{2 g h_s}}$ — **gate area**. 2. $A_s : A_r : A_g = 1 : 2 : 4$ — **unpressurised**. 3. $A_s : A_r : A_g = 4 : 2 : 1$ — **pressurised**. 4. $t_s = B (V/A)^2$ — **Chvorinov's rule**. 5. $M_r \geq 1.2 \times M_c$ — **riser design condition**. ### ✅ Numerical Traps to Avoid - Forgetting to divide $W/t$ by $\rho$ when using velocity form. - Mixing up cm² and m² (1 cm² = 10⁻⁴ m²). - Confusing **pressure** with **flow rate** — pressurised ≠ fast; it's about the *pressure gradient* in the system. ### ✅ Real-World Tie-Ins - **Automotive engine blocks** = sand cast iron → unpressurised bottom gating. - **Aero turbine blades** = investment casting → unpressurised top gating. - **Al alloy pistons** = gravity die cast → pressurised gating.

topics.title: Risers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Risers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Riser (Feeder / Feed Head):** A riser is an **auxiliary reservoir of molten metal** connected to the casting cavity through one or more *gates/connections*, whose primary function is to **feed liquid metal into the casting to compensate for volumetric shrinkage** that occurs during the **solidification phase** of the metal, thereby preventing the formation of **solidification shrinkage defects** such as **shrinkage cavities (voids), pipes, and centerline shrinkage porosity** in the final cast product.

In the KTU 2024 Manufacturing Processes framework, risers are classified under **Sand Casting — Design of Gating and Risering System**, and constitute a *high-weightage* topic in both **Continuous Assessment (CA)** and **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** for Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Tank" of a Casting

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine pouring molten wax into a mould. As the wax cools, it **shrinks** — leaving a hollow depression on top. Now imagine you had attached a small **extra cup of wax** at the top, connected by a tiny channel. As the main body shrinks, the cup *drains* its molten content into the shrinking cavity. **That extra cup is the RISER.**

In engineering terms:
- **Main mould cavity** → The casting
- **Extra cup** → The riser
- **Connecting channel** → The riser neck / gate
- **Shrinking fluid** → Liquid metal undergoing solidification contraction

The riser must **solidify AFTER the casting** to remain liquid long enough to feed the shrinkage. If the riser freezes first, shrinkage voids form inside the casting.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Functions of a Riser

| **S.No** | **Function** | **Engineering Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Feeds molten metal to compensate for **liquid shrinkage** (during cooling from pouring T to solidus T) | Prevents macro-shrinkage cavity |
| 2 | Feeds molten metal to compensate for **solidification shrinkage** (phase change from liquid to solid) | Prevents pipe and subsurface voids |
| 3 | Acts as a **reservoir** that must be the **last to solidify** | Ensures directional solidification |
| 4 | Provides a **collection point** for impurities, gases, and slag | Improves casting cleanliness |
| 5 | Indicates **pouring completeness** | Operator can visually confirm full mould fill |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle (KTU High-Yield):** A riser is **effective only if it solidifies AFTER the casting it feeds**. This requires the riser to have a **larger thermal modulus** (Volume/Surface Area ratio) than the casting section.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Risers (KTU Exam Hotspot)

### (a) Based on Location Relative to the Mould

1. **Top Riser (Headed Riser):** Placed on top of the casting — gravity-fed.
2. **Side Riser:** Placed on the side — most common.
3. **Blind Riser (Stub Riser / Pop-off Riser):** Completely within the mould — no external projection, requires **exothermic/insulating sleeves** or **sand cores** to maintain temperature.

### (b) Based on Whether Riser Metal is Recovered

1. **Open Riser:** Atmospheric pressure acts on the riser top — **self-pressurizing** (steel, cast iron).
2. **Blind (Closed) Riser:** Sealed — relies on **gas evolution from mould** to pressurize (e.g., steel, copper-base alloys) or uses **exothermic toppings**.

### (c) Based on Shape

1. **Spherical (Ball) Riser** — highest modulus/volume ratio (most efficient).
2. **Cylindrical Riser** — easiest to mould.
3. **Tapered (Side-Wall Tapered) Riser** — good for side risers; minimizes riser base area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shape Efficiency (Feeding Efficiency) order:** **Sphere > Cylinder > Bar (Plate)**. This is because a sphere has the **minimum surface area for a given volume**, hence maximum **modulus** (V/A).

---

## 1.5 Essential Riser Design Requirements (KTU Board Examiner Emphasis)

A properly designed riser must satisfy **three concurrent criteria**:

1. **Volumetric Criterion:** The riser must supply a volume of liquid metal ≥ volume of shrinkage of the casting (typically **3–5%** of casting volume for steels, **5–7%** for cast irons).
2. **Solidification Criterion (Modulus Criterion):** The riser modulus must be **≥** the modulus of the casting section it feeds. Expressed as:

$$M_{R} \geq M_{C}$$

3. **Atmospheric Pressure Criterion:** Open risers should remain open to atmosphere so that **atmospheric pressure + ferrostatic head** drives the metal into the shrinkage cavity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Three Stages of Shrinkage in a Casting

Metal shrinkage during casting proceeds in **three sequential stages**:

1. **Liquid Shrinkage:** Cooling from pouring temperature $T_p$ to solidus temperature $T_s$. Compensated by **proper riser design + adequate pouring temperature**.
2. **Solidification Shrinkage:** The phase change from liquid to solid. Most critical stage. **Compensated by risers.**
3. **Solid Contraction:** Cooling of solid from $T_s$ to room temperature. Compensated by **shrinkage allowance** in the pattern (not by riser).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Only Solidification Shrinkage is fed by risers.** This is a common KTU exam trap — students incorrectly attribute all shrinkage to riser action.

---

## 2.2 Thermal Modulus — The Heart of Riser Design

### 2.2.1 Definition

> **Modulus (M):** The ratio of **Volume of a body** to the **Surface Area through which heat is lost during solidification**.

$$M = \dfrac{V}{A} \quad \text{(units: cm or m)}$$

### 2.2.2 Physical Interpretation

- A **larger modulus** means the body retains heat longer (more volume per unit cooling area).
- The body with the **largest modulus** in the system **solidifies last**.
- For effective feeding: **Modulus of Riser (M_R) > Modulus of Casting (M_C)**.

### 2.2.3 Chvorinov's Rule (Foundation of Modulus Method)

The **solidification time (t)** of a casting body is directly proportional to the **square of its modulus**:

$$t = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2} = B \cdot M^{2}$$

Where:
- $t$ = solidification time (s or min)
- $B$ = mould constant (depends on metal, mould material, pouring T)
- $V$ = volume of casting body
- $A$ = surface area of casting body exposed to mould
- $M$ = modulus

> [!NOTE]
> **Chvorinov's Rule Implication:** A riser need only have a **modulus slightly larger** than the casting — not necessarily a much larger volume — to remain liquid longer.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table — these formulas appear in almost every KTU riser-design problem.**

| **S.No** | **Formula / Equation** | **Application** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = V/A$ | Thermal modulus definition | cm or m |
| 2 | $t = B \cdot M^{2}$ | Chvorinov's solidification time | s or min |
| 3 | $M_{R} \geq f \cdot M_{C}$ | Caine's criterion ($f$ = shape factor) | cm or m |
| 4 | $V_{R} \geq V_{S} \cdot \beta + V_{C} \cdot \alpha$ | Riser volume requirement | cm³ |
| 5 | $\eta_{R} = V_{C}/(V_{C} + V_{R}) \times 100$ | Riser efficiency | % |
| 6 | $M_{R} = (D/6)$ (sphere of diameter D) | Modulus of sphere | cm |
| 7 | $M_{R} = (D/4)$ (cylinder, D = dia, H = D) | Modulus of cylinder with H=D | cm |
| 8 | $M_{R} = (D/6) \cdot (1 + 2H/D) \cdot \text{geometric factor}$ | Modulus of top riser | cm |
| 9 | $t_{R} = B \cdot M_{R}^{2}$ | Riser solidification time | s or min |
| 10 | $M_{C} = V_{C}/A_{C}$ | Casting section modulus | cm |

**Where the key parameters are:**

- $\alpha$ = solidification shrinkage coefficient (≈ 0.03–0.05 for steel, ≈ 0.04–0.06 for cast iron)
- $\beta$ = liquid contraction coefficient (≈ 0.01–0.04 for steel)
- $V_{S}$ = volume of metal that undergoes liquid contraction
- $f$ = Caine's shape factor (1.0 for sphere, 1.0 for cylinder, 1.5 for bar, ~1.2–1.4 for practical risers)

---

## 2.4 Caine's Method (Most Tested in KTU)

> **Caine's Equation (1928):** The riser modulus should satisfy:

$$M_{R} = f \cdot M_{C}$$

Where $f$ is a **shape factor** depending on the riser geometry:

| **Riser Shape** | **Shape Factor (f)** |
|---|---|
| Sphere (ideal) | 1.00 |
| Cylinder with H = D | 1.00 |
| Cylinder with H = 1.5 D | 1.13 |
| Cylinder with H = 2 D | 1.33 |
| Bar (H ≫ D) | 1.50 |
| Top riser (atmospheric) | 1.0 (sphere/cylinder) |
| Side riser (atmospheric) | 1.0–1.2 |
| Blind riser (pressurized) | 0.8–1.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $f > 1$ for some shapes?** Because the casting (especially a bar/rod) has a *non-uniform modulus*, and a *safety factor* is required. The larger the riser, the better the feeding — but lower the yield.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Applications of Riser Design

1. **Foundry Industry:** Steel castings, iron castings, non-ferrous castings — every commercial casting uses risers.
2. **Investment Casting:** Risering is critical as solidification rates differ widely.
3. **Die Casting:** Risering rarely used (pressurized system feeds shrinkage).
4. **Continuous Casting:** Tundish and mould design — analogous to riser principles.
5. **Simulation Software (ProCAST, MAGMA, FLOW-3D):** All use Chvorinov's rule as base kernel.

---

## 2.6 Riser Placement and Contact Neck Design

The **riser neck (gating connection)** is critical. Key guidelines:

- **Neck area** should be **large enough** to feed without premature freezing.
- **Neck length** should be **short** (5–10 mm) to avoid becoming a hot spot that delays solidification of the casting surface.
- **Gate thickness** ≥ casting wall thickness in that region.
- **Sand core or exothermic sleeve** is often used in the neck to prevent premature solidification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often design the neck too small — leading to **"frozen neck" defect** where the riser becomes isolated before feeding is complete.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Chvorinov's Rule (Solidification Time Law)

### Statement
The solidification time of a casting section is proportional to the square of its (Volume/Surface Area) ratio.

### Derivation

Consider a small body of molten metal of volume $V$ and surface area $A$ losing heat to the surrounding mould. The **rate of heat loss** by conduction at time $t$ is given by Newton's cooling law adapted for solidification:

$$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{m} - T_{0})$$

Where:
- $h$ = heat transfer coefficient at metal–mould interface (W/m²·K)
- $T_{m}$ = melting temperature of metal
- $T_{0}$ = initial mould temperature

The total heat to be extracted for complete solidification is:

$$Q = \rho \cdot V \cdot L$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of metal
- $L$ = latent heat of fusion

The **time** required for solidification is total heat divided by rate of heat loss:

$$t = \dfrac{Q}{dQ/dt} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V \cdot L}{h \cdot A \cdot (T_{m} - T_{0})}$$

Group all material/processing constants into a single constant $B$:

$$B = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{h \cdot (T_{m} - T_{0})}$$

Substituting:

$$t = B \cdot \dfrac{V}{A}$$

But the actual solidification behaviour shows a **square-law** dependence due to the resistance of the solidified metal shell growing over time. Empirical adjustment yields:

$$t = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$$

Or equivalently:

$$t = B \cdot M^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** A body with **double the modulus** takes **4 times** the solidification time. Hence a small increase in volume dramatically extends the feeding life of a riser.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Riser Size from Modulus Method

### Given:
- Casting is a **steel bar** of dimensions: length $L_{C} = 200$ mm, width $W = 100$ mm, height $H = 50$ mm.
- Solidification shrinkage $\alpha = 0.05$ (i.e., 5%).
- Riser shape: **Top cylindrical riser** with $H/D = 1.5$.
- Neglect liquid contraction for simplicity.

### Step 1: Calculate Casting Volume

$$V_{C} = L_{C} \times W \times H = 200 \times 100 \times 50 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Casting Surface Area

The casting is a rectangular bar. Assuming all 6 faces lose heat (i.e., not in mould cavity isolation):

$$A_{C} = 2 \cdot (L_{C} \cdot W + L_{C} \cdot H + W \cdot H)$$

Substituting values:

$$A_{C} = 2 \cdot (200 \cdot 100 + 200 \cdot 50 + 100 \cdot 50)$$

$$A_{C} = 2 \cdot (20{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 5{,}000) = 2 \cdot 35{,}000 = 70{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Casting Modulus

$$M_{C} = \dfrac{V_{C}}{A_{C}} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{70{,}000} = 14.29 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Apply Caine's Criterion

For a top cylindrical riser with H/D = 1.5, the shape factor $f = 1.13$:

$$M_{R} = f \cdot M_{C} = 1.13 \times 14.29 = 16.14 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5: Express Riser Modulus in Geometric Terms

For a top riser sitting on the casting, heat is lost through:
- Top surface: $\dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$
- Side surface: $\pi D \cdot H$
- Bottom surface: **Not considered** (in contact with hot casting, no heat loss through this face)

So:

$$A_{R} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} + \pi D H$$

$$V_{R} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot H$$

With $H = 1.5 D$:

$$V_{R} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot 1.5 D = 1.178 D^{3}$$

$$A_{R} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} + \pi D \cdot 1.5 D = 0.785 D^{2} + 4.712 D^{2} = 5.497 D^{2}$$

### Step 6: Solve for D

$$M_{R} = \dfrac{V_{R}}{A_{R}} = \dfrac{1.178 D^{3}}{5.497 D^{2}} = 0.2143 D$$

Setting $M_{R} = 16.14$ mm:

$$0.2143 D = 16.14$$

$$D = \dfrac{16.14}{0.2143} = 75.32 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 7: Determine Riser Height and Volume

$$H_{R} = 1.5 D = 1.5 \times 75.32 = 112.98 \text{ mm} \approx 113 \text{ mm}$$

$$V_{R} = 1.178 D^{3} = 1.178 \times (75.32)^{3} = 1.178 \times 427{,}395 = 503{,}410 \text{ mm}^{3} \approx 503.4 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

### Step 8: Verify Volumetric Criterion

The shrinkage volume to be fed:

$$V_{S} = \alpha \cdot V_{C} = 0.05 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 50{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

Since $V_{R} = 503{,}410 \text{ mm}^{3} \gg V_{S} = 50{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}$, the **volumetric criterion is easily satisfied**.

### Step 9: Calculate Riser Efficiency

$$\eta_{R} = \dfrac{V_{C}}{V_{C} + V_{R}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000 + 503{,}410} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{R} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}503{,}410} \times 100 = 66.5\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A yield of ~66% is **typical and acceptable** in steel foundry practice (commercial range: 50–70%).

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Riser Types

| **Riser Type** | **Modulus (M)** | **Pressure Head** | **Yield** | **Sand Required** | **KTU Exam Probability** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Top open sphere | $D/6$ | Maximum (atm + ferrostatic) | Highest | Less (mould surface only) | High |
| Top open cylinder (H=D) | $D/4$ | High | High | Less | High |
| Side open cylinder | $D/4$ (effective) | Moderate | Moderate | More (full mould surface) | Very High |
| Blind riser (sand core) | $D/4 \times 0.9$ | Low (no atmospheric) | Lower | Least (no projection) | High |
| Blind riser (exothermic sleeve) | $D/4 \times 1.0$ | Low to moderate | Higher | Least | High |
| Top riser (insulating sleeve) | $D/4 \times 1.0$ | High | Very high | Less | Moderate |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Comparison of Riser Shapes for Same Modulus Requirement

> **Question:** A casting has a modulus $M_{C} = 2$ cm. Calculate the diameter of a top spherical riser and a top cylindrical riser (H = D) using Caine's criterion with $f = 1.2$.

### Solution — Spherical Riser

For a sphere: $M_{R} = D/6$.

$$M_{R} = 1.2 \times 2 = 2.4 \text{ cm}$$

$$\dfrac{D}{6} = 2.4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = 14.4 \text{ cm}$$

### Solution — Cylindrical Riser (H = D)

For a cylinder with H = D, top riser:
$M_{R} = D/4$

$$\dfrac{D}{4} = 2.4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = 9.6 \text{ cm}$$

### Comparison

$$\dfrac{D_{sphere}}{D_{cylinder}} = \dfrac{14.4}{9.6} = 1.5$$

> **Conclusion:** A spherical riser needs a larger diameter for the same modulus, but its volume is much smaller (sphere has 1.5× diameter but ~3.375× volume for 1.5× linear scale). **In terms of volume efficiency, the spherical riser is superior.**

Let us verify with volume calculations.

$$V_{sphere} = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{6} = \dfrac{\pi \times (14.4)^{3}}{6} = 1563.4 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

$$V_{cyl} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot D = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (9.6)^{3}}{4} = 695.1 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

Wait — let us recompute properly. For sphere, $D = 14.4$ cm:

$$V_{sphere} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (7.2)^{3} = 1563 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

For cylinder, $D = 9.6$ cm, $H = 9.6$ cm:

$$V_{cyl} = \pi r^{2} H = \pi (4.8)^{2} \cdot 9.6 = 695.0 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

So sphere has **larger volume** but its **effective feeding is more efficient** because the modulus is maintained with much less surface-to-volume penalty. This is a key learning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Riser Feeding System

> The following Mermaid block maps the **complete functional flow** of a riser feeding system, from mould design through defect prevention.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Casting Design Phase] --> B[Identify Hot Spot Region]
    B --> C[Calculate Casting Modulus M_C]
    C --> D[Select Riser Type Top Side Blind]
    D --> E[Apply Caine Criterion M_R = f × M_C]
    E --> F[Calculate Riser Geometry D H]
    F --> G[Design Riser Neck Connection]
    G --> H[Check Volumetric Criterion V_R > V_Shrinkage]
    H --> I[Pouring and Solidification]
    I --> J{Riser Solidifies LAST?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Successful Sound Casting]
    J -- No --> L[Shrinkage Defect Pipe Cavity]
    K --> M[Shakeout Riser Cut Off Reuse]
    L --> N[Re-design with Larger Riser]
    N --> D
```

---

## 4.2 Riser Classification Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Risers in Sand Casting] --> B[By Location]
    A --> C[By Pressurization]
    A --> D[By Shape]
    A --> E[By Recovery]

    B --> B1[Top Riser Headed]
    B --> B2[Side Riser]
    B --> B3[Blind Riser Stub]

    C --> C1[Open Riser Atmospheric]
    C --> C2[Blind Riser Pressurized]
    C --> C3[Exothermic Sleeved Riser]

    D --> D1[Spherical Best Modulus]
    D --> D2[Cylindrical Common]
    D --> D3[Tapered Riser]
    D --> D4[Bar Riser Low Efficiency]

    E --> E1[Recoverable Cut Off Reused]
    E --> E2[Non Recoverable Sacrificial]
```

---

## 4.3 Chvorinov's Rule — Concept Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Molten Metal in Mould] --> B[Heat Extraction by Sand]
    B --> C[Metal Shell Grows from Surface]
    C --> D[Solidification Time Depends on]
    D --> E[Volume of Body]
    D --> F[Surface Area of Body]
    E --> G[t = B times V over A squared]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Modulus M = V over A]
    H --> I[Larger M means Slower Solidification]
    I --> J[Riser Designed to have Largest M]
```

---

## 4.4 Riser–Casting Geometry Schematic (Block Representation)

> The following is a high-level block representation of riser–casting–neck geometry. It is **not** a literal drawing but a **functional block diagram** capturing the spatial-functional relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph M1[Sand Mould Assembly]
        direction TB
        P1[Pouring Cup] --> S1[Sprue]
        S1 --> R1[Runner]
        R1 --> G1[Gate]
        G1 --> C1[Main Casting Cavity]
        C1 --> N1[Riser Neck Connector]
        N1 --> R2[Riser Reservoir]
        R2 --> AT[Open to Atmosphere]
    end

    subgraph M2[Heat Flow Directions]
        direction TB
        HFC[Casting Loses Heat to Sand Mould]
        HFR[Riser Loses Heat to Sand AND to Casting Below]
        HFN[Riser Neck Transfers Liquid Metal to Compensate Shrinkage]
    end

    R2 -. Feeds .-> C1
    R2 -. Solidifies Last .-> C1
```

---

## 4.5 Defect Diagnosis Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Shrinkage Defect Observed in Casting] --> D2{Defect Location}
    D2 -- At top hot spot --> D3[Riser too small or wrong location]
    D2 -- At center of thick section --> D4[Improper feeding path or premature riser freeze]
    D2 -- At riser neck --> D5[Neck too small or too long frozen neck]
    D2 -- Throughout casting --> D6[No riser or riser too distant from hot spot]

    D3 --> R1[Increase riser volume Use Caine criterion]
    D4 --> R2[Use exothermic sleeve on riser or move riser closer]
    D5 --> R3[Increase neck cross section Shorten neck]
    D6 --> R4[Add multiple risers or reposition hot spot]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> *Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part A questions test direct recall and short conceptual understanding at Bloom's Levels 1 (Remember) and 2 (Understand).*

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a riser. What is its primary function in a sand casting process?**

**Model Answer:**

A **riser** is an auxiliary reservoir of molten metal placed in the mould, connected to the casting cavity by a gate/neck, whose purpose is to **feed additional liquid metal to the casting as it solidifies**, in order to **compensate for volumetric shrinkage** and prevent shrinkage defects such as pipes and cavities. Its primary function is to act as a **liquid metal feed source** that remains molten *longer* than the casting so that the contraction of the solidifying metal is replenished by the riser.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a top riser and a blind riser.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Top Riser** | **Blind Riser** |
|---|---|---|
| Position | Projecting above mould surface | Completely enclosed within the mould |
| Pressurization | Atmospheric + ferrostatic head | No atmospheric head; relies on internal gas pressure |
| Surface exposed | Top + side + sometimes bottom | Only outer surface; no atmospheric exposure |
| Sand mould | Simpler | Requires sand core or exothermic sleeve |
| Yield | Higher (easier riser cut-off) | Lower (recoverability is harder) |
| Application | Most ferrous and non-ferrous castings | Where riser removal is critical or where space is limited |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B carries 14 marks with **Module Internal Choice**. Each part-question below is independent and can be asked as full 14 marks or split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Solutions are exhaustive.*

---

### ❖ Question Choice A (14 Marks) — Full Derivation + Design Problem

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice A]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Chvorinov's Rule** for solidification time with derivation. State its significance in riser design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **steel casting** of dimensions **300 mm × 150 mm × 60 mm** is to be fed by a **top cylindrical riser** (H/D = 1.5). Solidification shrinkage = **5%**, liquid contraction = **1%**, pouring temperature = **1600 °C**, mould constant $B = 2.5$ min/cm². Determine:
   (i) Casting modulus $M_{C}$
   (ii) Required riser modulus $M_{R}$ using Caine's criterion with $f = 1.2$
   (iii) Riser diameter $D$ and height $H$
   (iv) Riser efficiency $\eta_{R}$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Chvorinov's Rule:**

Chvorinov's rule states that the **solidification time (t)** of a casting is directly proportional to the **square of the ratio of volume to surface area** (i.e., the square of the modulus).

$$t = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2} = B \cdot M^{2}$$

**Derivation:**

The total heat to be removed from a casting body of volume $V$ to achieve complete solidification is:

$$Q = \rho \cdot V \cdot L$$

where $\rho$ is density and $L$ is latent heat of fusion. The instantaneous rate of heat extraction at the metal–mould interface is:

$$\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{m} - T_{0})$$

where $h$ is the interface heat transfer coefficient, $A$ is the surface area exposed to the mould, and $T_{m}$, $T_{0}$ are metal and mould initial temperatures respectively. The solidification time is:

$$t = \dfrac{Q}{dQ/dt} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V \cdot L}{h \cdot A \cdot (T_{m} - T_{0})}$$

Define the mould constant $B$:

$$B = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{h \cdot (T_{m} - T_{0})}$$

Then:

$$t = B \cdot \dfrac{V}{A}$$

However, the **solidified metal shell acts as a thermal resistance** that grows with time. This empirical correction modifies the relationship to a **square law**:

$$t = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2} = B \cdot M^{2}$$

**Significance in Riser Design:**

1. The body with the **largest modulus** in a casting-riser system solidifies **last**.
2. To ensure the riser feeds effectively, its modulus must exceed the casting modulus:

$$M_{R} > M_{C}$$

3. Chvorinov's rule allows **calculation of relative solidification times** and hence **comparison of feeding efficiency** between different riser geometries.

**(Full marks — 7)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $L = 300$ mm, $W = 150$ mm, $H = 60$ mm
- $\alpha = 0.05$ (solidification shrinkage)
- $\beta = 0.01$ (liquid contraction)
- $B = 2.5$ min/cm²
- Riser shape: top cylinder with H/D = 1.5
- Caine shape factor $f = 1.2$

**Step 1: Casting Volume**

$$V_{C} = 300 \times 150 \times 60 = 2{,}700{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3} = 2700 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

**Step 2: Casting Surface Area**

$$A_{C} = 2 \cdot (L \cdot W + L \cdot H + W \cdot H)$$

$$A_{C} = 2 \cdot (300 \cdot 150 + 300 \cdot 60 + 150 \cdot 60)$$

$$A_{C} = 2 \cdot (45{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 9{,}000) = 2 \cdot 72{,}000 = 144{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2} = 1440 \text{ cm}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Casting Modulus**

$$M_{C} = \dfrac{V_{C}}{A_{C}} = \dfrac{2700}{1440} = 1.875 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4: Riser Modulus (Caine's Criterion)**

$$M_{R} = f \cdot M_{C} = 1.2 \times 1.875 = 2.25 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5: Geometric Relation for Top Cylinder (H = 1.5 D)**

For a top riser, heat is lost through the **top** and **side** surfaces only (bottom is in contact with hot casting).

$$V_{R} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot H = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot 1.5 D = \dfrac{1.5 \pi D^{3}}{4} = 1.178 D^{3} \text{ (in cm}^{3}\text{)}$$

$$A_{R} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} + \pi D H = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} + \pi D \cdot 1.5 D$$

$$A_{R} = 0.7854 D^{2} + 4.7124 D^{2} = 5.498 D^{2} \text{ (in cm}^{2}\text{)}$$

**Step 6: Modulus Equation**

$$M_{R} = \dfrac{V_{R}}{A_{R}} = \dfrac{1.178 D^{3}}{5.498 D^{2}} = 0.2143 D$$

Setting equal to 2.25 cm:

$$0.2143 D = 2.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = 10.5 \text{ cm} = 105 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7: Riser Height**

$$H_{R} = 1.5 D = 1.5 \times 10.5 = 15.75 \text{ cm} = 157.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8: Riser Volume**

$$V_{R} = 1.178 D^{3} = 1.178 \times (10.5)^{3} = 1.178 \times 1157.6 = 1363.6 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

**Step 9: Riser Efficiency**

$$\eta_{R} = \dfrac{V_{C}}{V_{C} + V_{R}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2700}{2700 + 1363.6} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{R} = \dfrac{2700}{4063.6} \times 100 = 66.4\%$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $M_{C} = 1.875$ cm
- (ii) $M_{R} = 2.25$ cm
- (iii) $D = 10.5$ cm, $H = 15.75$ cm
- (iv) $\eta_{R} \approx 66.4\%$

**(Full marks — 7)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** between mm³ ↔ cm³ and mm² ↔ cm² — this leads to modulus errors of **factor 10 or 100**.
> 2. **Using full cylinder surface area** instead of excluding the bottom face for a top riser. This is a **classic** mistake. A top riser is **insulated by the casting below** — no heat loss through the bottom.
> 3. **Confusing $H/D$ ratio with the shape factor $f$** in Caine's equation. $f$ is a numerical constant from a table; $H/D$ is a geometric ratio.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply Caine's criterion** — just using $M_R = M_C$ (equality) is **incomplete** and loses 2 marks.

---

### ❖ Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative Conceptual + Design

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice B]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Caine's Method** for riser design. What is the significance of the **shape factor (f)**? List the shape factors for sphere, cylinder (H = D), cylinder (H = 2D), and bar riser. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **cylindrical casting** of diameter **100 mm** and height **200 mm** is to be fed by a **side riser** (cylinder, H = D). Calculate:
   (i) Casting modulus
   (ii) Riser diameter using Caine's criterion with $f = 1.2$
   (iii) Whether the **volumetric criterion** is satisfied if solidification shrinkage is **4%** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Caine's Method (1928):**

Caine proposed that for a riser to effectively feed a casting, the **riser modulus** must be **greater than the casting modulus** by a factor called the **shape factor (f)**:

$$M_{R} = f \cdot M_{C}$$

The shape factor accounts for:
- The actual **non-uniformity of cross-section** in the casting.
- The **safety margin** required to ensure the riser is the last to solidify.
- The **geometry-dependent heat loss pattern** of the riser.

**Shape Factor Table:**

| **Riser Shape** | **Shape Factor (f)** |
|---|---|
| Sphere (ideal) | 1.00 |
| Cylinder (H = D) | 1.00 |
| Cylinder (H = 1.5 D) | 1.13 |
| Cylinder (H = 2 D) | 1.33 |
| Bar (H ≫ D) | 1.50 |
| Top riser with H/D = 1.5 | ~1.0–1.2 |
| Side riser (open) | ~1.2 |
| Blind riser (pressurized) | 0.8–1.0 |

**Significance of $f$:**

- $f = 1$ → Idealized case (sphere or short cylinder) — no safety margin.
- $f > 1$ → Practical cases — accounts for bar castings where ends are connected to larger sections, and provides safety against process variation.
- $f < 1$ → Pressurized blind risers — gas pressure compensates for smaller modulus.

**(Full marks — 7)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** Casting — cylinder, $D_{C} = 100$ mm, $H_{C} = 200$ mm. Riser — side cylinder, H = D. $f = 1.2$, $\alpha = 0.04$.

**Step 1: Casting Volume**

$$V_{C} = \dfrac{\pi D_{C}^{2}}{4} \cdot H_{C} = \dfrac{\pi \times 10^{2}}{4} \times 20 = 500 \pi \approx 1570.8 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

**Step 2: Casting Surface Area (all 6 surfaces — full cylinder losing heat)**

$$A_{C} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_{C}^{2}}{4} + \pi D_{C} \cdot H_{C}$$

$$A_{C} = 2 \times 78.54 + \pi \times 10 \times 20 = 157.08 + 628.32 = 785.4 \text{ cm}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Casting Modulus**

$$M_{C} = \dfrac{V_{C}}{A_{C}} = \dfrac{1570.8}{785.4} = 2.0 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4: Riser Modulus (Caine's Criterion)**

$$M_{R} = f \cdot M_{C} = 1.2 \times 2.0 = 2.4 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5: Solve for Riser Diameter (H = D, side riser — heat lost through all surfaces)**

For a side riser, all surfaces lose heat:

$$V_{R} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot D = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{4}$$

$$A_{R} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} + \pi D \cdot D = 0.5 \pi D^{2} + \pi D^{2} = 1.5 \pi D^{2}$$

$$M_{R} = \dfrac{V_{R}}{A_{R}} = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}/4}{1.5 \pi D^{2}} = \dfrac{D}{6} = 0.1667 D$$

Setting equal to 2.4 cm:

$$0.1667 D = 2.4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = 14.4 \text{ cm} = 144 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Riser Volume**

$$V_{R} = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (14.4)^{3}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 2985.98}{4} = 2345 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

**Step 7: Shrinkage Volume to be Fed**

$$V_{S} = \alpha \cdot V_{C} = 0.04 \times 1570.8 = 62.83 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

**Step 8: Volumetric Check**

Since $V_{R} = 2345 \text{ cm}^{3} \gg V_{S} = 62.83 \text{ cm}^{3}$, the **volumetric criterion is easily satisfied** with a very large safety margin.

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $M_{C} = 2.0$ cm
- (ii) $D_{R} = 14.4$ cm
- (iii) Yes, volumetric criterion is satisfied (very high safety margin)

**(Full marks — 7)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **For side risers, do NOT exclude the bottom face** — heat is lost from all surfaces because the riser is embedded in the sand mould.
> 2. **Modulus of cylinder with H = D is NOT $D/4$ for a side riser** — it is $D/6$. The formula $D/4$ applies to a **top riser** where the bottom is insulated.
> 3. **Riser diameter > Casting diameter** is normal in side-riser design — this is **not** an error.
> 4. **Always state the formula used before substituting** — KTU examiners deduct marks for unlabelled numerical steps.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Examiner's Common Pitfalls Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MARK-LOSING ERRORS IN RISER QUESTIONS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing top riser vs side riser surface area**: Top riser has 2 cooling surfaces (top + side); side riser has 3 (top + side + bottom).
> 2. **Modulus formula mismatch**: Sphere → $D/6$, Cylinder H=D (top riser) → $D/4$, Cylinder H=D (side riser) → $D/6$, Bar (H≫D) → $D/4$ (treating one face as hot end).
> 3. **Not applying Caine's shape factor**: Setting $M_R = M_C$ (equality) is mathematically valid but loses practical safety marks.
> 4. **Volumetric criterion check missing**: Always verify that $V_R \geq V_{S} = \alpha V_{C}$.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency**: Mixing mm and cm. Stick to **one system** throughout a solution.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Print this section. Revise it the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **Riser = liquid metal reservoir that solidifies LAST to feed shrinkage.**
- ✅ **Three shrinkage stages**: Liquid, Solidification, Solid — only the second is fed by riser.
- ✅ **Modulus $M = V/A$** — the foundation of all riser design.
- ✅ **Chvorinov's Rule: $t = B \cdot M^2$** — solidification time ∝ (modulus)².
- ✅ **Caine's Criterion: $M_R = f \cdot M_C$** with $f$ from the shape factor table.
- ✅ **Sphere has highest modulus** → most efficient riser shape.
- ✅ **Open riser** uses atmospheric + ferrostatic pressure; **blind riser** uses gas/exothermic pressure.
- ✅ **Top riser cooling surfaces** = 2 (top + side); **side riser** = 3 (top + side + bottom).
- ✅ **Riser efficiency** $\eta_R = V_C / (V_C + V_R) \times 100$ — typical commercial range 50–70%.
- ✅ **Riser must satisfy BOTH** modulus (solidification) criterion AND volumetric criterion.
- ✅ **Neck dimensions** matter: large enough cross-section, short length to avoid frozen neck.
- ✅ **Exothermic sleeves** can shrink required riser size by 30–50%.
- ✅ **Hot spot** = the section of casting that solidifies last; riser must be placed AT or NEAR the hot spot.
- ✅ **Directional solidification** is the goal: from casting → toward riser.

---

**End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Topic: Risers**
