topics.title: Chills
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chills

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Chill** is a metallic insert (typically manufactured from iron, steel, copper, bronze, aluminum, or graphite) that is deliberately placed either inside the mold cavity or on the inner surface of the sand mold during the sand casting process. Its primary engineering function is to **accelerate the local rate of heat extraction** from the molten metal at strategic locations, thereby **promoting directional solidification** and eliminating solidification defects such as shrinkage cavities, microporosity, and centerline voids.

In the context of **cast iron metallurgy**, chills are further classified by their influence on the carbon microstructure — they either **promote** the formation of hard, brittle carbides (white iron) or **suppress** such carbide formation, depending on their composition and the alloying elements present in the melt.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Chills are covered as a sub-topic under "Sand Moulding" in Module 1 of PBMET304 (Manufacturing Processes). They form a critical component of the *feeding and risering design* framework, alongside **risers**, **padding**, and **gating system design**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heat Sink in a Cup of Tea"

Imagine you pour boiling tea (≈100 °C) into a **thick ceramic mug** versus a **thin copper mug** kept at room temperature.

- The **copper mug** absorbs heat far more rapidly because of its high thermal conductivity, density, and specific heat capacity. The tea cools down quickly, especially near the walls.
- The **ceramic mug** is a poor conductor; heat removal is sluggish.

A **chill in a sand mold behaves exactly like the copper mug**:

| Real-World Object | Foundry Equivalent | Underlying Physics |
|---|---|---|
| Copper mug walls | Metallic chill | High thermal conductivity |
| Hot tea | Molten metal (liquid steel/iron) | Source of latent + sensible heat |
| Air convection outside | Surrounding sand mold | Insulator (low k) |
| Steam from surface | Risers / open top | Heat exit path |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core takeaway for KTU exams:** A chill does not "cool" the casting; it provides a **local, rapid heat sink** that forces the solidification front to advance from the chill surface inward, ensuring that the **last region to solidify is the riser** (which acts as the feed metal reservoir). This is the essence of the **Chvorinov's Rule** applied to feeding design.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following engineering metrics are used throughout chill design:

- **Density of Steel Chill (ρ_steel):** ≈ **7850 kg/m³**
- **Specific Heat of Steel (c_steel):** ≈ **0.486 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Thermal Conductivity of Steel (k_steel):** ≈ **45 W/(m·K)**
- **Density of Cast Iron Chill:** ≈ **7200 kg/m³**
- **Thermal Conductivity of Copper Chill:** ≈ **385 W/(m·K)** *(highest, but costly)*
- **Thermal Conductivity of Sand Mold (k_sand):** ≈ **0.5 – 1.0 W/(m·K)** *(~50× lower than steel chill)*

> [!TIP]
> Always remember the **~50× thermal conductivity ratio** between a steel chill and a green sand mold — this single number justifies *why* chills are so dramatically effective in altering local solidification behavior.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The "Why & How" of Chills

The application of chills in a sand casting is governed by **Chvorinov's Rule** for solidification time:

$$t_{s} \;=\; B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{n}$$

where:
- $t_s$ = local solidification time (seconds)
- $B$ = mold constant (depends on metal and mold material)
- $V/A$ = **Volume-to-Surface-Area Ratio** (also called **modulus**, $M$)
- $n$ = exponent, typically **≈ 2** for sand casting

The **modulus** $M$ is the single most important geometric parameter in feeding design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Logic:** For a casting to be **sound (defect-free)**, every region of the casting must solidify *before* the riser does. Since solidification time scales with $(V/A)^2$, **smaller modulus ⇒ faster solidification**. Chills **artificially reduce the local V/A** of the casting region they are placed against, by providing an extra (highly conductive) surface through which heat can escape.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Identify hot spots** — regions of the casting where the modulus is locally high (e.g., a thick boss attached to a thin web).
2. **Apply a chill** at that hot spot → adds an additional cooling surface.
3. **Recompute effective modulus** with the chill in place.
4. **Verify** that the new effective modulus is **less than the riser modulus** $M_R$ (this ensures the riser freezes *last*).
5. **Validate** with Chvorinov's rule that solidification time at the chilled region is less than at the riser.

## 2.3 Classification of Chills (Theory Bullet Structure)

### 2.3.1 Based on Placement
- **Internal Chills:** Placed *inside* the mold cavity. They become part of the casting (metallurgically fused). Used when no external surface is available, e.g., inside a large boss.
- **External Chills:** Placed on the *outer mold surface* (against the sand). They do NOT become part of the casting. Used when the casting has an external surface that can be brought in direct contact with the chill.

### 2.3.2 Based on Coating
- **Bald Chills (Bare Chills):** No coating. Maximum heat extraction → strongest chilling effect. Risk of fusion/penetration.
- **Chill Coatings:** Chills coated with a refractory wash (chalk, mica, alumina). Used to **moderate** heat extraction or to prevent metal-mold fusion.

### 2.3.3 Based on Metallurgical Action in Cast Iron
- **Chill Promoters (Carbide Stabilizers):** Elements like **Bi, Te, Sb, Cr, Mn, V, Mo** increase the chill depth (encourage white iron formation).
- **Chill Neutralizers (Non-Promoters / Graphitizers):** Elements like **Si, Ni, Cu, Al, Co** reduce the chill depth (encourage gray iron formation).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for Part A and Part B questions on Chills.**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_s = B \cdot (V/A)^{n}$ | Chvorinov's Rule | $n \approx 2$ for sand |
| 2 | $M_{C} = V_{C} / A_{C}$ | Chill Modulus (V = volume, A = contact surface) | m or mm |
| 3 | $M_{C} \;>\; M_{M}$ *(for effective chilling)* | Chill modulus must exceed mold-section modulus | Dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | $Q_{abs} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat absorbed by chill | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $Q_{abs} = \rho_{C} \cdot V_{C} \cdot c_{C} \cdot (T_{m} - T_{C,i})$ | Heat absorbed = density × volume × specific heat × temp rise | $\rho_C$ in kg/m³, $V_C$ in m³ |
| 6 | $k_{chill} \div k_{sand} \approx 50$ | Conductivity ratio (steel chill / green sand) | Justifies chilling power |
| 7 | $t_{s,chill} < t_{s,casting} < t_{s,riser}$ | Sequence condition for sound casting | Seconds |
| 8 | $\text{Chill Effectiveness} \propto k_{C} \cdot \rho_{C} \cdot c_{C}$ | Effectiveness ∝ thermal conductivity × density × specific heat | Product called "**Chilling Power**" |

> **Mnemonic to remember Chilling Power ranking (high to low):** 
> **Cu > Steel > Iron > Graphite > Al** (Cu is best but expensive; graphite is used for non-ferrous low-melting alloys).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Chills are indispensable in the manufacture of:
- **Railway wheel castings** (rim chilling for white iron tread)
- **Crank-shafts, camshafts** (local chilling at bearing journals)
- **Diesel engine pistons** (internal chills in the crown)
- **Rolling mill rolls** (chilled periphery for wear resistance)
- **Track links, plow shares, crusher jaws** (chill castings with white iron surface + gray iron core)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Chill Modulus Must Exceed the Section Modulus

Consider a casting section of volume $V_M$ and surface area $A_M$, with modulus:

$$M_{M} = \frac{V_{M}}{A_{M}}$$

When a chill of volume $V_C$ and contact surface $A_C$ is attached, the **effective solidifying system** has:

$$V_{eff} = V_{M} + V_{C}$$

$$A_{eff} = A_{M} + A_{C}$$

Hence, the **effective modulus of the chilled region** is:

$$M_{eff} = \frac{V_{M} + V_{C}}{A_{M} + A_{C}}$$

For the chill to be *effective* (i.e., to draw heat away *faster* than the sand would), the chill should dominate the heat removal. The chill's intrinsic modulus is:

$$M_{C} = \frac{V_{C}}{A_{C}}$$

**Step 1 — Condition for chill dominance:**
The chill must freeze a layer of metal at its surface *faster* than the sand would on the opposite side. Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$M_{C} \;>\; M_{M}$$

This is because the sand-mold side is limited by $M_M$, while the chill side has modulus $M_C$. The **slower side dictates the freezing time**; hence to make the chill the *faster* side, we need $M_C > M_M$.

**Step 2 — Application of Chvorinov's Rule:**

$$t_{s,\,chill\,side} = B \cdot (M_{C})^{2}$$

$$t_{s,\,sand\,side} = B \cdot (M_{M})^{2}$$

The **direction of solidification** is governed by the side that *solidifies first*. To make the chill side the **frozen-end** (and the riser the **liquid-end**), we require:

$$t_{s,\,chill\,side} \;<\; t_{s,\,sand\,side}$$

$$B \cdot (M_{C})^{2} \;<\; B \cdot (M_{M})^{2}$$

$$\boxed{M_{C} \;>\; M_{M}}$$

This derivation **proves** the fundamental design rule taught in all foundry textbooks.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Heat Absorbed by a Steel Chill

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A steel chill of dimensions $100 \,\text{mm} \times 100 \,\text{mm} \times 25 \,\text{mm}$ is used in a sand mold. The chill is initially at $27\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($300 \,\text{K}$). Molten cast iron at $1300\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ is poured. Assuming the chill is heated up to $400\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($673 \,\text{K}$) and given:
> - $\rho_{C} = 7850 \,\text{kg/m}^{3}$
> - $c_{C} = 0.486 \,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
>
> Calculate: (a) the mass of the chill, (b) the heat absorbed by the chill.

### 3.2.1 Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Volume of chill:**

$$V_{C} = 0.100 \times 0.100 \times 0.025$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{C} &= 100 \,\text{mm} \times 100 \,\text{mm} \times 25 \,\text{mm} \\
&= 250{,}000 \,\text{mm}^{3} \\
&= 250 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^{3} \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of chill:**

$$m_{C} = \rho_{C} \cdot V_{C}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{C} &= 7850 \,\text{kg/m}^{3} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{m}^{3} \\
m_{C} &= 1.9625 \,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Temperature rise of chill:**

$$\Delta T = T_{final} - T_{initial} = 400 - 27 = 373 \,\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Heat absorbed by chill:**

$$Q_{abs} = m_{C} \cdot c_{C} \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{abs} &= 1.9625 \,\text{kg} \times 0.486 \,\text{kJ/(kg·K)} \times 373 \,\text{K} \\
&= 1.9625 \times 0.486 \times 373 \,\text{kJ} \\
&= 355.71 \,\text{kJ} \\
&\approx 3.557 \times 10^{5} \,\text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{abs} \approx 355.7 \,\text{kJ} \;\; \text{or} \;\; 3.56 \times 10^{5} \,\text{J}}$$

### 3.2.2 Verification of Chill Effectiveness

**Step 5 — Chill modulus:**

Contact surface (one face of the chill in contact with casting) is:

$$A_{C} = 0.100 \times 0.100 = 0.01 \,\text{m}^{2}$$

$$M_{C} = \frac{V_{C}}{A_{C}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{0.01} = 0.025 \,\text{m} = 25 \,\text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with casting section modulus:**

If the casting section has $M_M = 15 \,\text{mm}$, then:

$$M_C = 25 \,\text{mm} > M_M = 15 \,\text{mm} \;\; \checkmark$$

The chill is **effective** — the chilled face will solidify faster than the sand-mold face, satisfying the rule $M_C > M_M$.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Chill

| Casting Scenario | Recommended Chill Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Thick boss on a thin web (steel casting) | External steel chill | External surface available, easy to place |
| Hollow cylindrical hub (no external face) | Internal steel chill | No external face; chill fuses into casting |
| Thin copper alloy casting | Graphite chill | Low-melting alloy; copper chill would melt |
| Cast iron with deep white iron zone needed | Chilled iron + Bi/Te inoculant | Promoter accelerates carbide formation |
| Cast iron with soft gray iron core desired | Chilled iron + Si/Fe inoculant | Non-promoter counteracts chill effect |
| Mass-production small castings | Permanent metallic mold (die) | Chills are essentially built into the die |
| One-off heavy steel forging blank | External steel chill + refractory coating | Coating prevents fusion; reusable |

## 3.4 Common Design Pitfalls (for KTU Valuation)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1:** Forgetting that **chill volume contributes to casting volume** in modulus calculations for internal chills. Use $V_{eff} = V_{casting} + V_{chill}$.
>
> **Pitfall 2:** Assuming chills work in *all* alloys. Graphite chills melt below the pouring temperature of steel — wrong chill material choice.
>
> **Pitfall 3:** Using a chill *thinner* than the casting section. The chill will saturate thermally before solidification completes, and the casting will still develop shrinkage.
>
> **Pitfall 4:** Placing the chill at the **riser junction** instead of the **hot spot** — this starves the riser of feed metal and *worsens* shrinkage.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Taxonomy of Chills

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CHILLS] --> B[Based on Placement]
    A --> C[Based on Coating]
    A --> D[Based on Metallurgical Action]

    B --> B1[Internal Chill]
    B --> B2[External Chill]

    B1 --> B1a[Placed inside mold cavity]
    B1 --> B1b[Becomes part of casting]

    B2 --> B2a[Placed on mold surface]
    B2 --> B2b[Does not fuse with casting]

    C --> C1[Bald Chill - No coating]
    C --> C2[Coated Chill - Refractory wash]

    D --> D1[Promoters - Bi, Te, Cr, Mn, V]
    D --> D2[Non-Promoters - Si, Ni, Cu, Al]

    D1 --> D1a[Increase white iron depth]
    D2 --> D2a[Promote gray iron formation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Solidification Sequence with Chill vs Without Chill

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Pouring of Molten Metal] --> B1[Without Chill: Uniform Solidification]
    A1 --> B2[With Chill: Directional Solidification]

    B1 --> C1[Random solidification pattern]
    B1 --> C2[Hot spots develop shrinkage]
    C1 --> D1[Defective Casting - REJECT]
    C2 --> D1

    B2 --> E1[Chill extracts heat rapidly]
    B2 --> E2[Solidification front moves from chill inward]
    E1 --> F1[Riser remains liquid LAST]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Feed metal compensates shrinkage]
    G1 --> H1[Sound Casting - ACCEPT]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Heat Flow Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph CASTING_SYSTEM
        H1[Latent Heat of Fusion] --> H2[Conduction through Molten Metal]
        H2 --> H3[Conduction to Chill]
        H2 --> H4[Conduction to Sand Mold]
        H3 --> H5[Chill absorbs Q = m times c times delta T]
        H4 --> H6[Sand mold dissipates heat slowly]
    end

    H5 --> H7[Rapid local cooling]
    H6 --> H8[Slow background cooling]
    H7 --> H9[Directional solidification achieved]
    H8 --> H9
    H9 --> H10[Solid Casting with No Shrinkage Defects]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Chill Selection Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    J1[Identify Hot Spot in Casting] --> J2{Is External Surface Available?}
    J2 -->|Yes| J3[Use External Chill]
    J2 -->|No| J4[Use Internal Chill]
    J3 --> J5{Casting Material?}
    J4 --> J5
    J5 -->|Cast Iron| J6[Iron or Steel Chill + Promoter Inoculant]
    J5 -->|Steel Casting| J7[Steel or Copper Chill]
    J5 -->|Copper Alloy| J8[Graphite or Steel Chill]
    J6 --> J9[Compute M_C and M_M]
    J7 --> J9
    J8 --> J9
    J9 --> J10{M_C greater than M_M?}
    J10 -->|Yes| J11[Chill Effective - Proceed]
    J10 -->|No| J12[Increase Chill Size and Re-check]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a chill. Mention its primary functions in sand casting.**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **chill** is a metallic insert placed in a sand mold to locally accelerate the rate of heat extraction from the molten metal during solidification.

**Primary Functions:**
1. **Promotes directional solidification** — ensures the riser remains the last region to solidify, allowing it to feed shrinkage.
2. **Eliminates shrinkage cavities and porosity** at hot spots in the casting.
3. **Refines grain structure** at the chilled surface, improving local mechanical properties (e.g., wear resistance in chilled cast iron rolls).

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Internal Chill and External Chill.**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Internal Chill | External Chill |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Placed inside the mold cavity | Placed on the outer surface of the mold |
| **Fusion with casting** | Yes — becomes metallurgical part of casting | No — remains separate |
| **Application** | When no external surface is accessible (e.g., hollow bosses) | When the casting has a flat external face |
| **Modulus contribution** | Chill volume is *added* to casting volume in $V_{eff}$ | Chill volume is *not* part of casting |
| **Removal** | Cannot be removed | Can be removed and reused |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) Explain the different types of chills used in foundry practice with neat sketches. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Chills are classified into the following types:

**1. Internal Chill** — A metallic piece placed *inside* the mold cavity, embedded in the region where accelerated cooling is desired. It becomes part of the final casting.

*Sketch description:* A rectangular block-shaped steel piece is positioned inside a sand mold cavity, with the casting metal surrounding it. After solidification, the chill is metallurgically fused with the casting.

**2. External Chill** — A metallic piece placed on the *inner surface of the sand mold*, contacting the molten metal at the desired region. It does not become part of the casting and can be reused.

*Sketch description:* A chill block is held against the sand mold wall by chaplets or wires. The casting solidifies against the chill surface.

**3. Bald Chill** — An external chill with *no refractory coating*. It extracts maximum heat and is used where deep chill action is required (e.g., white iron surface on cast iron rolls).

**4. Coated Chill** — An external chill coated with a refractory wash (chalk, mica, or alumina). The coating moderates heat extraction and prevents the molten metal from fusing with the chill.

**5. Promoter-type Chill (in cast iron)** — A chill combined with carbide-stabilizing elements (Bi, Te, Cr) to increase the white iron zone depth.

**6. Non-promoter / Graphitizer-type Chill** — A chill combined with graphitizing elements (Si, Ni) to reduce the chill effect and promote gray iron structure.

---

**(b) What is chill modulus? Discuss the factors affecting the effectiveness of a chill. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Chill Modulus:**
The chill modulus $M_C$ is defined as the **volume-to-surface-area ratio** of that portion of the chill which is in contact with the molten metal:

$$M_{C} = \frac{V_{C}}{A_{C}}$$

where $V_C$ = volume of the chill and $A_C$ = contact surface area between the chill and the casting.

**Design Rule:** For a chill to be effective, $M_C > M_M$ (mold/casting section modulus).

**Factors Affecting Chill Effectiveness:**

1. **Thermal Conductivity (k):** Higher $k$ → faster heat extraction → more effective chill. Copper > Steel > Iron > Graphite.

2. **Specific Heat Capacity (c):** Higher $c$ → more heat absorbed per unit mass per °C rise → more effective.

3. **Density (ρ):** Higher density → greater mass in given volume → more total heat absorption capacity.

4. **Chill Size (Volume):** Larger volume → more heat sink capacity. Minimum chill thickness must be ≥ critical thickness.

5. **Chill Modulus ($M_C$):** Must exceed section modulus of the casting region.

6. **Initial Chill Temperature:** Pre-heated chills are less effective initially but reduce thermal shock.

7. **Coating Thickness:** Coatings reduce heat extraction rate; bald chills are most effective.

8. **Contact Quality:** Voids or sand layers between chill and metal reduce effectiveness.

9. **Pouring Temperature and Alloy Composition:** Higher superheat delays chill action; alloy conductivity affects heat flow.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the application of chills in cast iron. What are chill promoters and non-promoters? Give examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Application in Cast Iron:**
When molten cast iron is poured against a chill surface, the rapid cooling **suppresses graphite formation** and **stabilizes iron carbide (Fe₃C / cementite)**. This produces a hard, wear-resistant **white iron layer** at the chill surface, transitioning through **mottled iron** (mixed structure) to **gray iron** (with graphite flakes) in the bulk.

This technique is used to manufacture:
- **Chilled cast iron rolls** (rolling mills) — hard white iron periphery + tough gray iron core
- **Railway wheel treads**
- **Crusher jaws, plow shares, ball mill liners**

**Chill Promoters (Carbide Stabilizers):**
These elements *increase* the depth of the white iron zone by stabilizing Fe₃C and suppressing graphite nucleation. Common promoters:

| Element | Typical Addition (%) | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Bismuth (Bi) | 0.005 – 0.05 | Very strong promoter |
| Tellurium (Te) | 0.005 – 0.05 | Strong promoter |
| Chromium (Cr) | 0.5 – 3.0 | Moderate-strong promoter |
| Manganese (Mn) | 0.5 – 1.5 | Mild promoter |
| Vanadium (V) | 0.1 – 0.5 | Strong carbide former |
| Molybdenum (Mo) | 0.3 – 1.0 | Promotes pearlite + chill |

**Chill Non-Promoters (Graphitizers):**
These elements *reduce* the chill depth by promoting graphite (gray iron) formation:

| Element | Typical Addition (%) | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Silicon (Si) | 1.5 – 3.0 | Strongest graphitizer |
| Nickel (Ni) | 1.0 – 2.0 | Mild graphitizer |
| Copper (Cu) | 0.5 – 1.5 | Mild graphitizer |
| Aluminum (Al) | 0.1 – 0.5 | Mild graphitizer |
| Cobalt (Co) | 0.5 – 1.0 | Mild graphitizer |

---

**(b) Discuss the design considerations for placement, size, and material selection of chills. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Placement Considerations:**
- Chill should be placed at the **hot spot** (region of highest modulus), not at the riser junction.
- For **isolated hot spots**, use local chills; for **distributed hot spots**, use multiple chills.
- Chill surface should be in **direct contact** with the casting face; avoid sand gaps.
- Avoid placing chill where it would obstruct riser feeding path.

**2. Size Considerations:**
- Chill **thickness** must be at least equal to the **casting section thickness** to avoid saturation.
- Chill **modulus** $M_C$ must satisfy $M_C > M_M$ (section modulus of casting at the chilled region).
- General rule: chill volume = 30–50% of the volume of the casting region being chilled.
- **Sharp edges** on chills should be **radiused** to avoid stress concentration and cracking in the casting.

**3. Material Selection:**
- **Steel chills:** Most common; good balance of conductivity, cost, and machinability.
- **Iron chills:** Lower cost, slightly lower conductivity than steel.
- **Copper chills:** Highest conductivity but expensive; used for high-conductivity critical applications.
- **Graphite chills:** Used for non-ferrous alloys (aluminum, copper) where steel would melt or react.
- **Aluminum chills:** Used for zinc and magnesium alloys (lower melting point).

**4. Coating Considerations:**
- Use **refractory coating** (chalk, mica, alumina wash) when chill is to be reused or when fusion must be prevented.
- Coating thickness: typically 0.5 – 2.0 mm.

**5. Pre-heating:**
- Pre-heating the chill to 200–300 °C reduces thermal shock to the casting and improves initial contact.

**6. Support and Venting:**
- Internal chills must be **supported by chaplets or wires** to prevent displacement during pouring.
- Proper **vents** must be provided around internal chills to allow gas escape.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Chills questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "chill" with "chaplet"** — chaplets support the core; chills absorb heat. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Writing $M_C < M_M$ instead of $M_C > M_M$** for chill effectiveness. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting the role of Bi and Te as promoters** — many students only mention Si. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing "bald chill" with "chill coating"** — bald means *no* coating, not just "uncovered". (–1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping the derivation of $M_C > M_M$** in 14-mark questions — must show Chvorinov's comparison. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Not mentioning directional solidification** as the *underlying purpose* of a chill — examiners specifically look for this phrase. (–2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Chills" for KTU 2024 PBMET304**

- [x] A **chill** is a metallic insert that **accelerates local heat extraction** in a sand casting.
- [x] Main purpose: **directional solidification** to eliminate shrinkage defects.
- [x] Chills work by **reducing local V/A ratio** (modulus) of the casting region.
- [x] **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B(V/A)^2$ governs solidification time.
- [x] **Design Rule:** For effective chilling, **$M_C > M_M$** (chill modulus > mold/casting modulus).
- [x] **Internal chills** become part of casting; **External chills** do not.
- [x] **Bald chill** = no coating (maximum chilling); **Coated chill** = refractory wash (moderate chilling).
- [x] **Chill promoters** (Bi, Te, Cr, Mn, V, Mo) — increase white iron depth in cast iron.
- [x] **Chill non-promoters / graphitizers** (Si, Ni, Cu, Al, Co) — promote gray iron.
- [x] **Chilling Power ranking:** Cu > Steel > Iron > Graphite > Al.
- [x] **$k_{steel}/k_{sand} \approx 50$** — justifies chilling effectiveness.
- [x] **Chill material density:** Steel = 7850 kg/m³, Cast Iron = 7200 kg/m³.
- [x] **Chill thickness ≥ Casting section thickness** — avoid premature chill saturation.
- [x] **Use chaplets to support internal chills**; provide **vents** for gas escape.
- [x] **Always place chill at the hot spot, NOT at the riser junction.**
- [x] **Applications:** Chilled cast iron rolls, railway wheels, crusher jaws, camshafts, pistons.
- [x] **Chill modulus formula:** $M_C = V_C / A_C$ (contact surface only).
- [x] **Heat absorbed by chill:** $Q = \rho_C \cdot V_C \cdot c_C \cdot \Delta T$.
- [x] **Pre-heating chills** (200–300 °C) reduces thermal shock to the casting.
- [x] **Radiused edges** on chills prevent stress concentration and casting cracks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Numerical Problems in Manufacturing Processes (Casting)

**Numerical Problems in Manufacturing Processes (Module 1)** refer to the mathematical computations and analytical exercises performed during the design and analysis of **Casting and Moulding processes**. These problems quantitatively determine the critical parameters required to produce **defect-free castings**, including solidification time, riser dimensions, gating system dimensions, shrinkage allowances, and pouring times.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBMET304, Module 1 predominantly covers the **Casting Technology** sub-module. Therefore, "Numerical Problems" essentially translates to **design calculations in foundry practice**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 Core Areas for Numericals:**
> 1. **Chvorinov's Rule** for solidification time
> 2. **Riser Design** (Modulus Method / Caine's Method)
> 3. **Shrinkage Allowance** calculations on patterns
> 4. **Gating System Design** (Sprue, Runner, Gate ratios)
> 5. **Pouring Time** calculations

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of casting like **baking a cake in a mould**:
- The **mould (cake tin)** decides the shape of the final product.
- **Hot metal (batter)** is poured in and cools (solidifies) from the outside in.
- The **riser (extra reserve of hot metal)** acts like a "feeding straw" that supplies extra molten metal to compensate for shrinkage (the cake shrinks as it cools).

If the **riser** is too small or cools too fast, a **void (shrinkage defect / pipe)** forms inside the casting — just like a sunken centre in a poorly designed cake. Numerical problems give us the **mathematical recipe** to design the right mould, riser, and pouring conditions so the final casting is **sound (defect-free)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Constants Used in KTU Numerical Problems:**
> - Modulus safety factor for riser: **$M_r \ge 1.2 \, M_c$** (rule of thumb)
> - Mould constant **$B$** depends on mould material (sand mould ≈ **2 to 5 min/cm²**; metal mould ≈ **0.5 to 1 min/cm²**)
> - Shrinkage of cast iron ≈ **1.0%**; Steel ≈ **2.0%**; Aluminium ≈ **1.5%**; Brass ≈ **1.5%**
> - Pouring time constant **$K$** depends on casting mass and section thickness
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Casting Design Numericals are Solved

Every numerical problem in foundry design follows a structured **four-step methodology**:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Given Geometry and Material:**
   Read the casting shape (cube, sphere, cylinder, plate, etc.), its dimensions, and the alloy (cast iron, steel, brass, aluminium).

2. **Step 2 — Compute Volume ($V$) and Surface Area ($A$):**
   For each shape, calculate the total volume and the **effective heat-dissipating surface area** (areas in contact with the mould, not free surfaces that face riser/air gaps unless otherwise specified).

3. **Step 3 — Apply the Governing Equation:**
   Select the relevant formula from the cheat sheet below (Chvorinov's, Modulus, Shrinkage, etc.) and substitute values with **consistent SI/cgs units**.

4. **Step 4 — Interpret and Verify:**
   Cross-check whether the riser solidifies later than the casting, whether the shrinkage allowance is positive, and whether the pouring time matches the alloy's fluidity.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description of Terms** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chvorinov's Rule (Solidification Time) | $t_s = B \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^2$ | $t_s$ = solidification time (min), $B$ = mould constant (min/cm²), $V$ = volume (cm³), $A$ = surface area (cm²) |
| 2 | Casting Modulus | $M_c = \dfrac{V_c}{A_c}$ | $M_c$ = modulus of casting (cm) |
| 3 | Riser Modulus | $M_r = \dfrac{V_r}{A_r}$ | $M_r$ = modulus of riser (cm) |
| 4 | Caine's Criterion (Top-Fed Riser) | $M_r \ge 1.2 \, M_c$ | Riser must solidify at least 1.2 times slower than casting |
| 5 | Cylindrical Riser Volume | $V_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 H$ | $D$ = riser diameter, $H$ = riser height |
| 6 | Cylindrical Riser Cooling Area (open top) | $A_r = \pi D H + \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | Lateral + top surface (exposed) |
| 7 | Cylindrical Riser Modulus | $M_r = \dfrac{D}{4} \left( \dfrac{H/D}{1 + (H/D)} \right)$ | Function of $H/D$ ratio |
| 8 | Spherical Riser Modulus | $M_r = \dfrac{D}{6}$ | Always best — minimum surface for given volume |
| 9 | Shrinkage Allowance (Linear) | $S = \dfrac{L_m - L_c}{L_c} \times 100$ | $L_m$ = pattern dimension, $L_c$ = casting dimension |
| 10 | Pattern Dimension | $L_m = L_c (1 + S)$ | Adding shrinkage to pattern |
| 11 | Mould Dimension | $D_m = D_c + SA$ | $SA$ = shrinkage allowance |
| 12 | Pouring Time (Light Castings) | $t_p = (1.6 \text{ to } 2.0) \sqrt{W}$ | $W$ = mass of casting in kg; $t_p$ in seconds |
| 13 | Pouring Time (Medium Castings) | $t_p = (1.0) \sqrt{W}$ | Mass ≈ 50–500 kg |
| 14 | Pouring Time (Heavy Castings) | $t_p = (0.8) \sqrt{W}$ | Mass > 500 kg |
| 15 | Gating Ratio | $A_s : A_r : A_g$ | Sprue : Runner : Gate area ratio (unpressurised: $1:2:4$; pressurised: $4:2:1$) |
| 16 | Continuity Equation | $A_s v_s = A_r v_r = A_g v_g$ | Mass flow rate conservation |
| 17 | Pouring Rate | $Q = \dfrac{W}{t_p}$ | $Q$ = volumetric pouring rate (kg/s) |
| 18 | Sprue Velocity (top pouring) | $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | Free fall velocity from height $h$ |
| 19 | Yield of Casting | $Y = \dfrac{W_c}{W_c + W_r + W_s} \times 100$ | $W_c$ = casting, $W_r$ = riser, $W_s$ = sprue |
| 20 | Heat Capacity Multiplier (pouring temp) | $T_{pour} = T_m + \text{superheat}$ | Pouring temperature = melting + superheat (typically 50–100 °C) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These numericals are **not academic exercises** — they form the backbone of:
- **Foundry floor practice** in automotive, aerospace, and heavy engineering industries.
- **Investment casting** of turbine blades (where Chvorinov's rule determines cooling cycle).
- **Sand casting of engine blocks, pump housings, and gear blanks** (where riser design prevents shrinkage porosity).
- **Defect analysis** in failure investigations (radiographic inspection rejects are often due to poor modulus design).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Chvorinov's Rule — Why $(V/A)^2$?

### Logical Basis
Chvorinov (1940) observed that the time for a casting to solidify is governed by the heat that must be removed per unit time. Since:
- Heat stored is proportional to **Volume ($V$)**.
- Heat dissipated is proportional to **Surface Area ($A$)**.

The ratio $V/A$ is called the **Modulus ($M$)** — it represents the "thermal mass per unit cooling surface." Larger the modulus, longer the solidification time.

Empirically (Newton's cooling approximation): $t \propto (V/A)^2$

$$
t_s = B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^2
$$

### Worked Example 1 — Chvorinov's Rule (Cube Casting)
**Problem:** A cube casting of side $a = 10$ cm is poured in a sand mould. Mould constant $B = 3.5$ min/cm². Calculate the solidification time. Assume the bottom face sits on the mould (no heat loss from top free surface to mould).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify geometry:
A cube of side $a = 10$ cm.

Step 2 — Compute volume:
$$
V = a^3 = (10)^3 = 1000 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Step 3 — Compute effective cooling area (5 faces, since top is free/air-exposed):
$$
A = 5 \times a^2 = 5 \times (10)^2 = 500 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Step 4 — Apply Chvorinov's rule:
$$
t_s = B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^2 = 3.5 \times \left( \frac{1000}{500} \right)^2
$$

$$
t_s = 3.5 \times (2)^2 = 3.5 \times 4 = 14 \text{ min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If the problem says "all faces are in contact with mould," use $A = 6a^2$. Default KTU convention is **5 faces** for cube unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Riser Design — Caine's Method

### Logical Basis
A riser is a reservoir of molten metal that feeds the casting during solidification. For the riser to function, it **must solidify AFTER** the casting. Using Chvorinov's rule:

$$
\frac{V_r}{A_r} \ge \frac{V_c}{A_c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_r \ge M_c
$$

Caine added a **safety factor of 1.2** to account for:
- Insulating mould coating on riser
- Practical variations in metal temperature
- Side riser inefficiencies

$$
M_r \ge 1.2 \, M_c
$$

### Worked Example 2 — Cylindrical Riser Design
**Problem:** A steel casting has the shape of a rectangular plate of dimensions 20 cm × 15 cm × 5 cm. Design a top cylindrical riser with $H/D = 1.5$ such that it solidifies after the casting. Apply Caine's criterion with safety factor 1.2.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute casting volume:
$$
V_c = 20 \times 15 \times 5 = 1500 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Step 2 — Compute casting cooling area (assume 5 faces — top open to riser):
$$
A_c = (20 \times 15) + 2(20 \times 5) + 2(15 \times 5) = 300 + 200 + 150 = 650 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Step 3 — Compute casting modulus:
$$
M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c} = \frac{1500}{650} = 2.308 \text{ cm}
$$

Step 4 — Apply Caine's criterion:
$$
M_r = 1.2 \times M_c = 1.2 \times 2.308 = 2.769 \text{ cm}
$$

Step 5 — Cylindrical riser formulae (top-fed, open at top):
$$
V_r = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H, \quad A_r = \pi D H + \frac{\pi}{4} D^2
$$

Step 6 — Substituting $H = 1.5 D$:
$$
V_r = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 (1.5 D) = \frac{1.5 \pi}{4} D^3 = 1.178 \, D^3
$$

$$
A_r = \pi D (1.5 D) + \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = 1.5 \pi D^2 + 0.7854 D^2 = 5.498 \, D^2
$$

Step 7 — Compute riser modulus:
$$
M_r = \frac{V_r}{A_r} = \frac{1.178 \, D^3}{5.498 \, D^2} = 0.2143 \, D
$$

Step 8 — Equate to required modulus:
$$
0.2143 \, D = 2.769
$$

$$
D = \frac{2.769}{0.2143} = 12.92 \text{ cm} \approx 13 \text{ cm}
$$

Step 9 — Compute riser height:
$$
H = 1.5 \times D = 1.5 \times 13 = 19.5 \text{ cm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Riser Dimensions:** $D \approx 13$ cm, $H \approx 19.5$ cm.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Shrinkage Allowance
**Problem:** A cast iron pattern is to be made for a casting of diameter 200 mm. If the linear shrinkage of cast iron is 1.0%, calculate the pattern diameter.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify formula:
$$
L_m = L_c (1 + S)
$$

Step 2 — Substitute:
$$
L_m = 200 \times (1 + 0.01) = 200 \times 1.01 = 202 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 3 — Pattern is **2 mm larger** than final casting dimension.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Pouring Time Calculation
**Problem:** A cast iron casting weighs 100 kg. Calculate the pouring time using the empirical formula for medium castings. Also determine the pouring rate.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify formula (medium castings):
$$
t_p = 1.0 \sqrt{W} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ seconds}
$$

Step 2 — Pouring rate:
$$
Q = \frac{W}{t_p} = \frac{100}{10} = 10 \text{ kg/s}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Gating System Ratio
**Problem:** A top-gated gating system uses a sprue of area 4 cm². If the gating ratio is $A_s : A_r : A_g = 1 : 2 : 4$ (unpressurised), calculate the runner and gate areas.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Use the ratio $1 : 2 : 4$:
$$
A_s : A_r : A_g = 1 : 2 : 4
$$

Step 2 — Sprue area:
$$
A_s = 4 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Step 3 — Runner area:
$$
A_r = 2 \times A_s = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Step 4 — Gate area:
$$
A_g = 4 \times A_s = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ cm}^2
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Chart for KTU Problems

| **Given in Problem** | **Use This Formula** | **Final Output** |
|---|---|---|
| Side of cube / cylinder / sphere | Chvorinov's rule: $t_s = B(V/A)^2$ | Solidification time in min |
| Casting dimensions + riser $H/D$ | Caine's criterion: $M_r \ge 1.2 M_c$ | Riser diameter and height |
| Casting diameter + alloy | $L_m = L_c(1 + S)$ | Pattern dimension |
| Mass of casting only | $t_p = K \sqrt{W}$ | Pouring time and rate |
| Sprue area + ratio $A_s : A_r : A_g$ | Direct proportional scaling | Runner and gate area |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Casting Numerical Problem

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **logical flow** of solving any KTU-level numerical problem in casting design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read the Problem] --> B[Identify Casting Geometry]
    B --> C[Identify Material and Alloy]
    C --> D{Determine Required Output}
    D -->|Solidification Time| E[Apply Chvorinov's Rule]
    D -->|Riser Dimensions| F[Compute M_c and apply Caine's Criterion]
    D -->|Pattern Dimension| G[Apply Shrinkage Allowance Formula]
    D -->|Pouring Time| H[Apply t_p = K * sqrt of W]
    D -->|Gating Dimensions| I[Use A_s : A_r : A_g Ratio]
    E --> J[Compute V and A from geometry]
    F --> J
    G --> K[Lookup Shrinkage from standard table]
    H --> L[Classify casting mass category]
    I --> M[Apply proportionality with given A_s]
    J --> N[Substitute values in SI or CGS units]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Verify Units and Validity]
    O --> P[Present Final Answer with Units]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Relationship Between Modulus and Solidification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Casting with Modulus M_c] -->|Feeds via| B[Riser with Modulus M_r]
    B -->|M_r is greater than 1.2 times M_c| C[Solidification Time: t_s]
    A --> C
    C -->|Largest solidifies last| D[Riser supplies molten metal]
    D -->|Compensates for| E[Shrinkage Porosity]
    E -->|If inadequate| F[Defect: Pipe or Cavity]
    E -->|If adequate| G[Sound Casting]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block — Gating System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pouring Cup] --> B[Sprue]
    B --> C[Sprue Well]
    C --> D[Runner]
    D --> E[Gate]
    E --> F[Casting Cavity]
    F --> G[Riser]
    G --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for Students:** The sprue carries metal downward; the runner distributes it horizontally; the gate injects it into the mould cavity. In a properly designed **unpressurised system**, the gate is the smallest cross-section (ratio $1:2:4$), so that any choke occurs at the gate — preventing mould erosion and splashing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State Chvorinov's rule for solidification of castings. Define mould constant and explain how it affects solidification time.**

**Model Answer:**
Chvorinov's rule states that the solidification time of a casting is proportional to the square of its volume-to-surface-area ratio:

$$
t_s = B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^2
$$

The **mould constant $B$** is a material-dependent parameter that captures the thermal properties of the mould material, the casting alloy, and the superheat. It is the time required for a casting of unit modulus ($V/A = 1$) to solidify.

**Effect of $B$ on solidification time:**
- A **higher $B$** (e.g., insulating sand mould) → **slower heat removal** → **longer solidification time**.
- A **lower $B$** (e.g., conductive metal/chill mould) → **faster heat removal** → **shorter solidification time**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $B$ in mixed units (e.g., min for $B$ and cm² for $A$) without conversion. Always express $B$ in $\text{min/cm}^2$ when $V$ is in cm³ and $A$ in cm².

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain Caine's criterion for riser design. Why is a safety factor of 1.2 used?**

**Model Answer:**
Caine's criterion states that for a top-fed cylindrical riser to effectively feed a casting during solidification, the modulus of the riser must be at least 1.2 times the modulus of the casting:

$$
M_r \ge 1.2 \, M_c
$$

where $M = V/A$ is the modulus.

**Reason for the safety factor of 1.2:**
- Accounts for the **insulating mould coating** applied to the riser (which reduces its actual cooling rate).
- Compensates for **practical variations in pouring temperature** and metal chemistry.
- Ensures the riser remains **molten until the casting is fully fed**, avoiding premature freezing and shrinkage porosity.
- Empirically validated by Caine from extensive foundry experiments.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
A steel casting in the form of a sphere of diameter 25 cm is to be cast. The mould constant is $B = 4$ min/cm². Calculate the solidification time of the casting.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Design a top cylindrical riser with $H/D = 1.5$ for the casting in part (a), assuming all 5 faces of an equivalent solid cube are in contact with the mould and the riser top is exposed. Use Caine's criterion with safety factor 1.2.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution

Step 1 — Identify geometry (sphere of diameter 25 cm):
$$
V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (12.5)^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (1953.125)
$$

$$
V = 8181.23 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Step 2 — Surface area of sphere:
$$
A = 4 \pi r^2 = 4 \pi (12.5)^2 = 4 \pi (156.25) = 1963.5 \text{ cm}^2
$$

Step 3 — Apply Chvorinov's rule:
$$
t_s = B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^2 = 4 \times \left( \frac{8181.23}{1963.5} \right)^2
$$

$$
t_s = 4 \times (4.167)^2 = 4 \times 17.36 = 69.44 \text{ min}
$$

> **Answer (a):** Solidification time of the sphere casting = **69.44 minutes**.

#### Part (b) — Solution

Step 1 — Compute casting modulus (sphere):
$$
M_c = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{8181.23}{1963.5} = 4.167 \text{ cm}
$$

Step 2 — Apply Caine's criterion:
$$
M_r = 1.2 \times 4.167 = 5.0 \text{ cm}
$$

Step 3 — Riser formulae with $H = 1.5 D$:
$$
V_r = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 (1.5 D) = 1.178 \, D^3
$$

$$
A_r = \pi D H + \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \pi D (1.5 D) + \frac{\pi}{4} D^2
$$

$$
A_r = 4.7124 \, D^2 + 0.7854 \, D^2 = 5.4978 \, D^2
$$

Step 4 — Riser modulus:
$$
M_r = \frac{V_r}{A_r} = \frac{1.178 \, D^3}{5.4978 \, D^2} = 0.2143 \, D
$$

Step 5 — Equate:
$$
0.2143 \, D = 5.0
$$

$$
D = 23.33 \text{ cm}
$$

Step 6 — Riser height:
$$
H = 1.5 \times 23.33 = 35.0 \text{ cm}
$$

> **Answer (b):** Riser dimensions: $D \approx 23.3$ cm, $H \approx 35.0$ cm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
A cylindrical casting of diameter 15 cm and height 30 cm is made in a sand mould. The linear shrinkage of the alloy is 1.5%. Calculate:
(i) The pattern dimensions.
(ii) The volume of the pattern (assuming pattern is a solid cylinder).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
A cast iron casting weighs 81 kg. Using the empirical formula for medium castings, calculate the pouring time. If a top-poured sprue of cross-sectional area 6 cm² is used with a pouring head of 20 cm, find the velocity of molten metal at the bottom of the sprue.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution

Step 1 — Shrinkage allowance:
$$
S = 1.5\% = 0.015
$$

Step 2 — Pattern diameter:
$$
D_m = D_c (1 + S) = 15 \times 1.015 = 15.225 \text{ cm}
$$

Step 3 — Pattern height:
$$
H_m = H_c (1 + S) = 30 \times 1.015 = 30.45 \text{ cm}
$$

Step 4 — Volume of pattern:
$$
V_m = \frac{\pi}{4} (D_m)^2 H_m = \frac{\pi}{4} (15.225)^2 (30.45)
$$

$$
V_m = 0.7854 \times 231.8 \times 30.45 = 5543.5 \text{ cm}^3
$$

> **Answer (a):** $D_m = 15.225$ cm, $H_m = 30.45$ cm, $V_m = 5543.5$ cm³.

#### Part (b) — Solution

Step 1 — Pouring time (medium castings):
$$
t_p = 1.0 \sqrt{W} = \sqrt{81} = 9 \text{ seconds}
$$

Step 2 — Velocity at bottom of sprue (Torricelli's equation with $g = 981$ cm/s²):
$$
v = \sqrt{2 g h} = \sqrt{2 \times 981 \times 20}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{39240} = 198.1 \text{ cm/s}
$$

Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate:
$$
Q = A_s \times v = 6 \times 198.1 = 1188.6 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}
$$

> **Answer (b):** Pouring time = **9 s**; sprue velocity = **198.1 cm/s**; flow rate = **1188.6 cm³/s**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** (mixing cm and m for $g$ in Torricelli's equation) — KTU deducts up to **2 marks** for wrong $g$ value.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply shrinkage** to both diameter AND height — only diameter alone gets **3 out of 7 marks**.
> 3. **Using the wrong empirical constant $K$** — light castings use $K = 1.6$ to $2.0$, medium uses $K = 1.0$, heavy uses $K = 0.8$. Wrong choice = **–1 mark**.
> 4. **Caine's safety factor 1.2 missing** — partial marking allowed (3/7 marks) if the student derives $M_r = M_c$ but forgets the factor.
> 5. **Surface area of sphere formula** — using $A = 2\pi r^2$ (cylinder) instead of $A = 4\pi r^2$ loses all marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B(V/A)^2$ — solidification time is governed by the **modulus squared** ($V/A$).
- **Modulus $M = V/A$** is the master parameter in all casting design — bigger the modulus, longer the solidification.
- **Caine's Criterion:** For a top-fed riser to be effective, $M_r \ge 1.2 \, M_c$.
- **Spherical risers** are the most efficient ($M = D/6$); cylindrical risers with $H/D \approx 1.5$ are practical.
- **Shrinkage allowance** must be **added** to pattern dimensions; values range from 1.0% (cast iron) to 2.0% (steel).
- **Pouring time** depends on casting mass: use $t_p = K\sqrt{W}$ with $K = 1.6$ to $2.0$ (light), $1.0$ (medium), $0.8$ (heavy).
- **Gating ratio** $A_s : A_r : A_g$ — unpressurised = $1:2:4$; pressurised = $4:2:1$ (or $1:2:1$ for neutral systems).
- **Always check** that the riser modulus is **greater than** the casting modulus before finalising dimensions.
- **Mould constant $B$** is a property of the mould-casting system, not just the mould; sand moulds have $B \approx 2$ to $5$ min/cm², metal moulds have $B \approx 0.5$ to $1$ min/cm².
- **In KTU exams**, always show the **derivation step** (computing $V$, $A$, then $M$, then $M_r$) to score full marks — partial marking is liberal but not for missing steps.
- **Unit discipline** is paramount — mixing SI and CGS is the #1 reason for mark loss in numerical problems.
- **Verify final riser dimensions** are realistic (e.g., $D$ of 50 cm for a small casting is an indication of a calculation error).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defects in Castings. Special Casting Process - Shell Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Defects in Castings. Special Casting Process - Shell Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Defects in Castings — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Casting Defect:** *An irregularity in the chemical composition, structure, or physical form of a cast component that deviates from the intended design specification, rendering the casting unsuitable for its intended service application.*

A casting defect is any undesirable imperfection introduced during the **melting**, **pouring**, **solidification**, or **cooling** stages of a casting operation. These defects compromise the **mechanical strength**, **dimensional accuracy**, **surface finish**, or **service life** of the cast product.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to *identify, classify, state the cause, and prescribe the remedy* for the most common casting defects as this forms a recurring short-answer (3-mark) and a part (a) of long-answer (7-mark) questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

### 1.2 Shell Moulding (Croning Process) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shell Moulding:** *A revolutionary expendable-mould sand casting process in which a thin, hardened shell (10–20 mm thick) of resin-bonded sand is formed around a heated metal pattern, and two such shells are clamped together to form the final mould cavity for pouring molten metal.*

Also known as the **Croning Process**, it was invented by **Dr. Johannes Croning** of Germany in **1943**. It is a hybrid of sand casting and permanent-mould casting — giving better surface finish and dimensional accuracy than green-sand moulds.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Defects in Castings — Analogy:**
Think of pouring molten metal into a mould exactly like **baking a cake in a baking pan**. If your batter (molten metal) has trapped air bubbles, the cake will have holes. If the pan isn't vented, steam blows out holes. If the pan is too cold, the surface wrinkles (cold shut). If the batter shrinks while cooling, the cake cracks. **Casting defects are the "baking flaws" of metallurgy** — every imperfection has a specific physical/metallurgical root cause.

**Shell Moulding — Analogy:**
Imagine making a **chocolate Easter egg**. You don't make a thick solid mould — you make a thin, hard, hollow chocolate shell by coating a hot egg-shaped mould with liquid chocolate that hardens instantly on contact. Then you peel off the shell, fill it, and seal. Shell moulding works on the same principle: a **hot metal pattern** is dipped into **resin-coated sand**, which cures into a hard, thin, hollow shell that becomes the mould.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants / Standard Metrics for Shell Moulding (in bold):**
> - Pattern temperature: **200 °C – 300 °C**
> - Resin (phenolic) content in sand: **3 % – 6 % by weight**
> - Shell curing time on pattern: **30 s – 120 s**
> - Typical shell thickness: **10 mm – 20 mm**
> - Dimensional tolerance achievable: **± 0.25 mm to ± 0.40 mm**
> - Surface finish (Ra): **2.5 µm – 5.0 µm**

---

### 1.4 Why Study These Topics?

| Aspect | Defects | Shell Moulding |
|---|---|---|
| **Industrial Relevance** | ~15–20 % of all castings are rejected due to defects — costs the foundry industry billions annually. | Used in mass production of automotive cylinder heads, connecting rods, gear housings, pipe fittings. |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | Frequently asked in ESE as 3-mark & 7-mark questions. | Frequently asked as a 14-mark ESE long answer. |
| **Engineering Skill** | Develops failure-analysis thinking. | Develops process-parameter optimization thinking. |

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Casting Defects

A systematic KTU-friendly classification divides defects by their **root cause**:

### A. Gas Defects (Caused by entrapped gases)

1. **Blow Holes** — Large, rounded, smooth-walled cavities just below the casting surface.
   - **Cause:** Moisture in sand, low permeability, inadequate venting.
   - **Remedy:** Dry moulds, increase permeability, add vents.

2. **Pin Holes** — Numerous small, pin-prick sized holes distributed across the casting.
   - **Cause:** Excessive gas evolution at the metal–mould interface; high hydrogen content in melt.
   - **Remedy:** De-gas the melt, use dry sand, control pour temperature.

3. **Subsurface Porosity** — Fine, dispersed micro-voids within the casting.
   - **Cause:** Dissolved gases rejected by solidifying metal.
   - **Remedy:** Degassing (e.g., chlorine treatment of aluminium).

4. **Blister** — A subsurface gas pocket that causes the surface to bulge like a bubble.
   - **Cause:** Gas pressure exceeds the solidified skin's strength.

### B. Shrinkage Defects (Caused by volumetric contraction during solidification)

5. **Shrinkage Cavity (Pipe)** — A large, irregular void, usually in the last-to-solidify region (hot spot).
   - **Cause:** Insufficient feed metal, improper riser design.
   - **Remedy:** Add risers, use chills, follow Chvorinov's rule.

6. **Micro-shrinkage (Centre-line shrinkage)** — Fine interconnected porosity along the thermal centre.
   - **Cause:** Wide freezing range alloy, no directional solidification.

7. **Warping / Distortion** — Casting bends or twists as it cools.
   - **Cause:** Uneven cooling, residual stresses.

### C. Mould Material Defects

8. **Sand Inclusion** — Embedded sand grains in the casting surface.
   - **Cause:** Weak mould surface, sand erosion from flowing metal.
   - **Remedy:** Strengthen mould, use mould washes, control pour rate.

9. **Sand Drop / Scab** — A mass of sand falls from mould wall and is embedded in casting.
   - **Cause:** Mould surface buckling due to high thermal stress.

10. **Rash (Penetrations)** — Metal penetrates into sand pores → rough surface.
    - **Cause:** High pouring temperature, low permeability sand.

### D. Pouring Metal Defects

11. **Cold Shut / Cold Lap** — A discontinuity where two metal streams meet but fail to fuse.
    - **Cause:** Low pouring temperature, poor gating, thin sections.

12. **Misrun** — Casting is incomplete; metal solidifies before filling the mould.
    - **Cause:** Same as cold shut, but more severe.

13. **Runout** — Molten metal leaks out of the mould.

### E. Metallurgical / Solidification Defects

14. **Hot Tear (Hot Crack)** — An irregular, branched crack formed while metal is still hot but solid.
    - **Cause:** Restrained contraction during solidification.
    - **Remedy:** Add fillets, use proper composition, uniform cooling.

15. **Hot Crack** — Similar to hot tear, formed at high temperatures.

16. **Cold Crack** — Sharp, clean-edged crack formed after complete solidification.
    - **Cause:** Excess residual stress from uneven cooling.

17. **Hot Spot Cracks** — Cracks at hot spots where thick sections meet thin sections.

### F. Other Common Defects

18. **Flash / Fin** — Thin projection of metal at parting line.
    - **Cause:** Improper mould clamping.

19. **Sponginess / Sponge Iron Effect** — Honeycomb-like structure, common in ductile iron if Mg treatment fails.

20. **Buckle** — Deformation of mould surface due to thermal expansion.

---

### 2.2 Shell Moulding — Process Theory

> [!NOTE]
> The **phenolic resin** used is a **thermosetting Novolac** resin (no volatile by-products, so dimensional stability is high) and is pre-coated on dry silica sand grains by hot-coating process. The resin melts at ~**95 °C**, then cross-links and cures at ~**150 °C – 200 °C**, hardening the shell irreversibly.

#### Step-by-Step Process Logic:

1. **Pattern Preparation:** A metal (cast iron or steel) pattern is mounted on a dump-box plate and pre-heated to **200 °C – 300 °C**.
2. **Resin-Sand Coating:** A thin layer of mould-release agent (silicone) is sprayed on the pattern to prevent sticking.
3. **Sand Dumping:** The heated pattern is inverted over a **dump box** containing **resin-coated sand**. The hot pattern melts the resin film on contact.
4. **Dwell Time:** The pattern remains in the sand for **30 s – 120 s**. The thickness of the shell formed is determined by the dwell time.
5. **Curing (inverting):** The pattern is rotated back upright. The cured shell remains on the pattern.
6. **Stripping:** Stripping pins push the shell off the pattern.
7. **Shell Assembly:** Two shells (cope & drag) are clamped / glued / bolted together.
8. **Pouring:** Molten metal is poured into the shell.
9. **Solidification & Shakeout:** The shell is brittle and breaks away easily from the casting.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning | Units / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t = K \cdot (V/A)^{n}$ | **Chvorinov's Rule** — solidification time $t$, Volume $V$, Surface Area $A$, $n \approx 2$, $K$ = mould constant. Used to design risers. | $t$ in seconds |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | **Modulus** of casting — key to Chvorinov's rule application. | m or mm |
| 3 | $t_{riser} \geq t_{casting}$ | Riser solidification time ≥ casting solidification time → Riser modulus ≥ Casting modulus. | — |
| 4 | $\Delta V = V_{liquid} - V_{solid} = \beta \cdot V_{solid} \cdot \Delta T$ | Volumetric shrinkage of metal. $\beta$ = volumetric shrinkage coefficient, $\Delta T$ = temp. range. | $\beta \approx 0.06$ for steel, $0.035$ for grey CI, $0.05$ for Al. |
| 5 | $V_{riser} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{casting}}{\eta_{riser}}$ | Riser volume must feed shrinkage. $\eta$ = riser efficiency (typically $0.20 - 0.40$). | m³ or cm³ |
| 6 | $\sigma_{riser} = P_{atm} + \rho g h - P_{shrinkage}$ | Pressure head at riser top must overcome metallostatic head + shrinkage pressure. | Pa |
| 7 | $h_{c} = \dfrac{P_{mould} \cdot d}{2 \tau_{sand}}$ | Critical height $h_{c}$ before mould wall collapse (against sand crushing shear stress $\tau$). | m |
| 8 | $L_{shell} = k \sqrt{t_{dwell}}$ | Shell thickness $L$ varies with square root of dwell time $t_{dwell}$ on hot pattern; $k$ = process constant. | mm |
| 9 | $h = \dfrac{\pi r^{2} H \rho g}{A_{mould}}$ | Ferostatic head causing metal penetration into sand; must be controlled. | m |
| 10 | $S_{v} = \dfrac{6}{\rho_{sand} \cdot D_{grain}}$ | Specific surface area of sand, controlling permeability. | m²/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Chvorinov's Rule is **THE** most-asked numerical from this module in KTU exams. Memorize $t = K (V/A)^2$ and its implications on riser placement.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Defect analysis** is the foundation of **Quality Control (QC)** and **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** — radiography, ultrasonic, dye-penetrant testing are all used to detect these defects.
- **Shell moulding** is the workhorse of **automotive mass production** — e.g., engine cylinder heads, manifolds, gear blanks, and small-to-medium castings for tractors, pumps, and electrical fittings.
- Chvorinov's rule is applied in **investment casting** and **die casting** riser design as well, not just sand casting.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Chvorinov's Rule — Exhaustive Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> Chvorinov's Rule is the most important KTU numerical from this module. The full derivation logic is shown below.

#### Statement
A casting with a higher **modulus** ($V/A$) takes **longer to solidify** than one with a lower modulus.

#### Derivation Logic

**Step 1: Energy balance at the casting–mould interface.**
The rate of heat extraction from the casting into the mould is governed by the **Fourier conduction law** through the mould wall. For a thin mould wall of thickness $L$ and thermal conductivity $k$:

$$
q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_{c} - T_{m})}{L}
$$

where $q$ is the rate of heat flow (W), $A$ is the surface area through which heat flows, $T_c$ is the casting surface temperature, and $T_m$ is the mould temperature.

**Step 2: Total heat to be removed.**
The total heat to be extracted from the casting as it solidifies and cools to mould temperature is the **latent heat of fusion** plus sensible heat:

$$
Q = V \cdot \rho \cdot [L_{f} + c_{p} (T_{pour} - T_{solidus})]
$$

where $V$ is the volume of the casting, $\rho$ is the density, $L_f$ is the latent heat of fusion, $c_p$ is the specific heat, $T_{pour}$ is the pouring temperature, and $T_{solidus}$ is the solidus temperature.

**Step 3: Solidification time is total heat / rate of heat removal.**

$$
t = \dfrac{Q}{q} = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho \cdot [L_{f} + c_{p} (T_{pour} - T_{solidus})] \cdot L}{A \cdot k \cdot (T_{c} - T_{m})}
$$

**Step 4: Define the mould constant.**
For a given metal and mould material, the quantities inside the brackets and $L$, $k$, $T_c$, $T_m$ can be lumped into a single constant $K$:

$$
K = \dfrac{\rho \cdot [L_{f} + c_{p} (T_{pour} - T_{solidus})] \cdot L}{k \cdot (T_{c} - T_{m})}
$$

**Step 5: Final Chvorinov's Rule.**

$$
\boxed{\,t = K \cdot \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^{2}\,}
$$

The exponent $n = 2$ is empirical; in real castings $1.5 \leq n \leq 2.5$.

**Step 6: Riser Design Implication.**
A riser is a reservoir of molten metal that feeds the shrinkage of the casting. The riser must **freeze after** the casting finishes freezing, so that liquid metal is available till the end. This requires:

$$
t_{riser} \geq t_{casting}
$$

Applying Chvorinov's rule:

$$
K \left( \dfrac{V_{riser}}{A_{riser}} \right)^{2} \geq K \left( \dfrac{V_{casting}}{A_{casting}} \right)^{2}
$$

Cancelling $K$ and taking square root:

$$
\boxed{\,\left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)_{riser} \geq \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)_{casting}\,}
$$

The riser modulus must be larger than the casting modulus.

---

### 3.2 Riser Volume — Worked Analytical Derivation

**Given:** A steel casting of volume $V_c = 1500 \text{ cm}^3$ with surface area $A_c = 1100 \text{ cm}^2$. Volumetric shrinkage of steel $\beta = 0.06$ (6 %). Riser efficiency $\eta = 0.25$.

**Step 1: Calculate shrinkage volume that must be fed.**

$$
V_{shrinkage} = \beta \cdot V_{c} = 0.06 \times 1500 = 90 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 2: Calculate riser volume to feed the shrinkage.**

$$
V_{riser} = \dfrac{V_{shrinkage}}{\eta_{riser}} = \dfrac{90}{0.25} = 360 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 3: Verify the modulus condition (assume cylindrical riser with $H = 1.5 D$).**
Riser surface area: $A_r = \pi D H + \dfrac{\pi D^2}{2} = \pi D (1.5 D) + \dfrac{\pi D^2}{2}$

$$
A_r = 1.5 \pi D^2 + 0.5 \pi D^2 = 2.0 \pi D^2
$$

Riser volume: $V_r = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot H = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot 1.5 D = 0.375 \pi D^3$

Setting $V_r = 360 \text{ cm}^3$:

$$
0.375 \pi D^3 = 360 \implies D^3 = \dfrac{360}{0.375 \times 3.1416} = 305.6 \implies D = 6.74 \text{ cm}
$$

Riser modulus:

$$
M_r = \dfrac{V_r}{A_r} = \dfrac{0.375 \pi (6.74)^3}{2.0 \pi (6.74)^2} = \dfrac{0.375 \times 6.74}{2.0} = 1.26 \text{ cm}
$$

Casting modulus:

$$
M_c = \dfrac{V_c}{A_c} = \dfrac{1500}{1100} = 1.36 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 4: Check — Riser modulus (1.26) < Casting modulus (1.36) ❌ Riser will freeze first!**

**Step 5: Iteration — increase riser diameter to 7.5 cm.**

$$
V_r = 0.375 \pi (7.5)^3 = 0.375 \times 3.1416 \times 421.88 = 497.0 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
M_r = \dfrac{0.375 \times 7.5}{2.0} = 1.406 \text{ cm}
$$

Now $M_r = 1.406 > M_c = 1.36$ ✅ **Riser will remain molten until casting freezes.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU students must learn this iterative modulus-balancing technique.** This type of problem is a 14-mark ESE favorite.

---

### 3.3 Shell Thickness vs Dwell Time — Analytical Model

The shell thickness $L$ formed on the pattern is governed by transient heat conduction from the hot pattern into the sand. Using a 1-D semi-infinite solid model:

$$
L = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k_{sand} t_{dwell}}{\rho_{sand} c_{p,sand}}}
$$

or empirically:

$$
L = k \cdot \sqrt{t_{dwell}}
$$

where $k$ is a process constant depending on resin type, pattern temperature, and sand grain size.

**Worked Example:** If $k = 4 \text{ mm/s}^{0.5}$ and $t_{dwell} = 25$ s:

$$
L = 4 \times \sqrt{25} = 4 \times 5 = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

This matches the standard KTU value of shell thickness.

---

### 3.4 Permeability & Gas Defect — Analytical Link

The permeability number $P$ of mould sand controls how fast gases can escape:

$$
P = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}
$$

where $Q$ is gas flow rate (m³/s), $L$ is sand mould thickness, $A$ is cross-sectional area, $\Delta P$ is pressure drop.

If $P$ is **too low** → gas cannot escape → **blow holes** form.
If $P$ is **too high** → metal penetrates sand → **rashes** form.

**Optimum permeability for steel castings:** $P \approx 100 - 300$ units (AFS scale).

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What are **blow holes** in castings? State any **two causes** and **two remedies**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** Blow holes are large, rounded, smooth-walled cavities formed just below the casting surface, caused by entrapped gases that fail to escape from the molten metal before solidification.
>
> **Causes (1 mark):**
> 1. Excessive moisture in the moulding sand.
> 2. Low permeability of the sand mould leading to poor gas venting.
>
> **Remedies (1 mark):**
> 1. Use dry, properly tempered moulding sand (moisture < 6 %).
> 2. Provide adequate vents in the mould and add coal dust / sea coal to green sand to create a burnt-out gas-permeable layer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** Define **Shell Moulding**. Name the **resin** used and state the **pattern temperature** range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** Shell moulding is an expendable mould casting process in which a thin, hard shell (10–20 mm) of resin-bonded sand is formed on a heated metal pattern; two such shells are clamped to form the mould.
>
> **Resin used (½ mark):** **Thermosetting phenolic resin (Novolac type)** coated on dry silica sand.
>
> **Pattern temperature (½ mark):** **200 °C – 300 °C**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Classify the various **casting defects** with at least **two examples under each category**. State the **cause and remedy** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Shell Moulding (Croning) process** in detail. List its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Introduction (½ mark):** Casting defects are irregularities that make a casting unfit for use. They are classified by their root cause into six major categories.
>
> **1. Gas Defects (1 mark)**
> - *Blow holes:* Large smooth-walled cavities below surface. **Cause:** moisture in sand, poor venting. **Remedy:** dry sand, add vents, use coal dust.
> - *Pin holes:* Small, distributed pin-pricks. **Cause:** dissolved gases (e.g., hydrogen) in melt. **Remedy:** degas the melt, use fluxes.
>
> **2. Shrinkage Defects (1 mark)**
> - *Shrinkage cavity (pipe):* Large irregular void at hot spot. **Cause:** insufficient feed metal, no riser. **Remedy:** proper riser design using Chvorinov's rule.
> - *Centreline porosity:* Fine voids along thermal centre. **Cause:** wide freezing range alloy. **Remedy:** directional solidification, use chills.
>
> **3. Mould Material Defects (1 mark)**
> - *Sand inclusion:* Sand grains embedded in casting. **Cause:** weak mould surface, sand erosion. **Remedy:** strengthen mould, use mould wash, control pour rate.
> - *Sand drop / scab:* A mass of sand falls into cavity. **Cause:** thermal buckling of mould surface. **Remedy:** add seacoal to improve collapsibility.
>
> **4. Pouring Metal Defects (1 mark)**
> - *Cold shut:* Two metal streams fail to fuse. **Cause:** low pour temperature, thin sections. **Remedy:** increase pour temperature, improve gating.
> - *Misrun:* Incomplete filling. **Cause:** insufficient metal or excessive chills. **Remedy:** larger pouring basin, better gating system.
>
> **5. Metallurgical Defects (1 mark)**
> - *Hot tear:* Branched irregular crack in hot state. **Cause:** restrained contraction. **Remedy:** add fillets, uniform cooling.
> - *Cold crack:* Sharp, clean-edged crack after solidification. **Cause:** residual stress. **Remedy:** stress-relief annealing.
>
> **6. Other Defects (½ mark)**
> - *Flash/fin:* Thin metal projection at parting line. **Cause:** poor mould clamping. **Remedy:** use proper clamping weight.
> - *Sponginess:* Honeycomb in ductile iron. **Cause:** Mg treatment failure. **Remedy:** correct inoculation practice.
>
> **Conclusion (½ mark):** A sound understanding of defect classification is essential for foundrymen to minimize rejection rates and improve yield.

---

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Introduction (½ mark):** Shell moulding was invented by Dr. Johannes Croning (Germany, 1943). It is also called the **Croning process**.
>
> **Process Steps (4 marks):**
> 1. **Pattern preparation (½ mark):** A metal (cast iron/steel) pattern mounted on a dump-box plate, pre-heated to **200–300 °C**, coated with silicone release agent.
> 2. **Sand dumping (½ mark):** The hot pattern is inverted over a dump box of **resin-coated sand (3–6 % phenolic resin + 94–97 % silica sand)**. The heat melts the resin film on contact.
> 3. **Dwell / curing (½ mark):** Pattern remains in sand for **30–120 s**, allowing a shell of **10–20 mm** to cure.
> 4. **Inverting & stripping (½ mark):** The pattern is rotated upright; stripping pins push the hardened shell off the pattern.
> 5. **Assembly (½ mark):** Two shells (cope & drag) are clamped or glued together and supported with backing sand/metal shot.
> 6. **Pouring (½ mark):** Molten metal is poured; the shell is self-supporting.
> 7. **Shakeout (½ mark):** The brittle shell breaks away easily from the casting.
>
> **Advantages (1½ marks):**
> 1. Excellent surface finish (Ra 2.5–5 µm) — minimal machining.
> 2. High dimensional accuracy (± 0.25 mm to ± 0.40 mm).
> 3. Faster production rate than green-sand moulds.
> 4. Less skilled labour required.
> 5. Thin walls (down to 1.5 mm) can be cast.
>
> **Limitations (1 mark):**
> 1. High cost of metal pattern and phenolic resin.
> 2. Limited to small-to-medium castings (typically < 50 kg).
> 3. Resin fumes are hazardous; proper ventilation required.
> 4. Long curing time per cycle compared to die casting.
>
> **Conclusion (½ mark):** Shell moulding is a high-precision, moderate-volume process ideally suited for automotive components.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam — June 2022] — ALTERNATIVE**

**(a)** Define **Chvorinov's Rule**. A steel casting has a modulus of **2.5 cm**. If the mould constant $K = 80$ s/cm², calculate the **solidification time** of the casting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **causes and remedies** of the following defects: (i) **Hot tear**, (ii) **Sand inclusion**, (iii) **Cold shut**, (iv) **Blister**. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** Chvorinov's Rule states that the solidification time of a casting is directly proportional to the square of the ratio of its volume to surface area (the modulus squared), i.e., $t = K (V/A)^2$. The rule is used to design risers by ensuring the riser modulus is greater than the casting modulus.
>
> **Given (1 mark):** $M = V/A = 2.5 \text{ cm}$, $K = 80 \text{ s/cm}^2$.
>
> **Formula (1 mark):**
>
> $$
> t = K \cdot \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^2 = K \cdot M^2
> $$
>
> **Substitution (1 mark):**
>
> $$
> t = 80 \times (2.5)^2 = 80 \times 6.25 = 500 \text{ s}
> $$
>
> **Result (1 mark):** Solidification time $t = 500$ seconds = **8 minutes 20 seconds**.
>
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** Chvorinov's rule can also be used to design a riser such that $M_{riser} \geq 1.2 M_{casting}$ for safety margin.

---

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **(i) Hot tear (1½ marks):**
> - **Cause:** Restrained contraction of the casting during solidification when the metal is in the mushy (semi-solid) state. Occurs in alloys with long freezing range or at junctions where uniform contraction is hindered.
> - **Remedy:** Provide adequate **fillets and generous radii** at section changes; ensure **uniform cooling**; adjust alloy composition to reduce hot-shortness; design the casting with balanced wall thickness.
>
> **(ii) Sand inclusion (1½ marks):**
> - **Cause:** Erosion of mould surface by flowing molten metal; weak mould surface; insufficient binder content; mould moisture too high.
> - **Remedy:** Increase **clay/binder content**; control moisture; use **mould washes** (zircon, graphite); reduce pouring rate; improve gating design.
>
> **(iii) Cold shut (1½ marks):**
> - **Cause:** Two streams of metal meet at low temperature and fail to fuse completely. Occurs in thin sections, low pouring temperature, poor gating.
> - **Remedy:** Increase **pouring temperature**; improve **gating system** for smooth flow; use **chills** to direct flow; thicken sections; preheat pouring basin.
>
> **(iv) Blister (1½ marks):**
> - **Cause:** Subsurface gas pocket forms and the surface bulges when gas pressure exceeds the strength of the solidified skin. Common in steel castings due to dissolved hydrogen or moisture reaction.
> - **Remedy:** **Degass the melt** (e.g., with aluminium-killed steel practice); use **dry, well-vented moulds**; control mould coatings.
>
> **Conclusion (½ mark):** A combination of metallurgical and foundry controls is essential to prevent these defects.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **In Chvorinov's rule problems,** students often write $V \times A$ instead of $(V/A)^2$ — **lose 2–3 marks** for this.
> 2. **In shell moulding questions,** students forget to mention **pattern material (metal)**, **resin type (phenolic Novolac)**, and **dwell time (30–120 s)** — these are mandatory keywords the examiner looks for.
> 3. **In defect questions,** listing defects without cause AND remedy loses 1 mark per defect.
> 4. **Riser modulus comparison** must be written as a *ratio* — merely saying "riser is bigger" earns 0 marks.
> 5. **Drawings carry 1–2 marks** in long answers — always include a labelled diagram (e.g., shell moulding process line diagram) even if the question doesn't explicitly say "with sketch."

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [x] **Casting defects** are classified into 6 broad categories: **Gas, Shrinkage, Mould material, Pouring metal, Metallurgical, and Others**.
- [x] **Blow holes** → gas; **Hot tear** → restrained contraction; **Cold shut** → low pour temperature; **Sand inclusion** → weak mould.
- [x] **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t = K (V/A)^2$ — the **single most-asked** numerical from this module.
- [x] **Riser design rule:** $M_{riser} \geq M_{casting}$ (riser must freeze LAST).
- [x] **Volumetric shrinkage of steel** ≈ **6 %**; of **grey cast iron** ≈ **3.5 %**; of **aluminium** ≈ **5 %**.
- [x] **Shell moulding** = **Croning process**, invented in **1943**, uses **phenolic resin-coated sand** (3–6 %) on a heated metal pattern (**200–300 °C**).
- [x] **Shell thickness** = **10–20 mm**, governed by **dwell time** = **30–120 s**.
- [x] **Shell moulding advantages:** better surface finish (Ra 2.5–5 µm), better dimensional accuracy (± 0.25 mm), thin walls (1.5 mm), no parting line flash.
- [x] **Shell moulding limitations:** pattern cost, size limit (~50 kg), resin fume hazard.
- [x] **Pattern material** for shell moulding is **metal (cast iron/steel)**, NOT wood or plastic (unlike green-sand).
- [x] **Riser efficiency $\eta$** typically 0.20–0.40; common assumptions: $\eta = 0.25$ for cylindrical top risers.
- [x] **Chvorinov's rule exponent** $n = 2$ is empirical — real castings show $1.5 \leq n \leq 2.5$.
- [x] **Always state "cause + remedy"** when describing a defect — examiners look for both.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precision Investment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Precision Investment Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Precision Investment Casting (PIC)**, also known as the **Lost-Wax Process**, is a near-net-shape metal forming process in which a disposable **wax pattern** (replicating the desired geometry with very high fidelity) is repeatedly dipped into a refractory ceramic slurry, stuccoed with graded sand, dried, and finally de-waxed to yield a one-piece, thin-walled ceramic shell mold. Molten metal is poured into this pre-heated shell, solidifies, and the ceramic is mechanically knocked out to recover a finished component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Investment casting is a precision casting process that uses a one-piece ceramic shell built around a consumable wax pattern; it is the preferred route for complex geometries, thin sections, super-alloys, and aerospace/medical implants where tolerances of **± 0.1 mm** and surface roughness **Ra 1.6 – 6.3 µm** are mandatory."*

| Formal Parameter | Standard Value (per ASTM 732) |
|---|---|
| Dimensional Tolerance | **± 0.1 mm to ± 0.5 mm** (small parts) |
| Surface Roughness (Ra) | **1.6 – 6.3 µm** |
| Minimum Section Thickness | **0.4 – 0.8 mm** (for non-ferrous) |
| Maximum Practical Mass | **≈ 25 kg** (typical) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
