Think of **making a candle**: you dip a wick repeatedly in melted wax, building up layers, then you have a solid wax shape. **Investment casting is the mirror image of that process.** Instead of *building* a wax shape around a wick, you *start* with a precise wax shape, coat it in layers of ceramic (like plaster of Paris mixed with sand), and then **melt the wax out** — leaving a hollow ceramic mold shaped exactly like your part. Molten metal is then poured into that hollow, taking the shape of the original wax.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** "Make it in wax → coat it in ceramic → burn the wax out → fill the ceramic with hot metal → break the ceramic away." That single sentence describes an entire industry worth billions of dollars in aerospace, defence, and biomedical sectors.

## 1.3 Historical Context

The process dates back to **~4000 BCE** (Egypt, Mesopotamia) for gold and copper jewellery. Its modern industrial application began in the late 1930s when dental and jewellery techniques were scaled up for **turbine blades during World War II**, where hollow, cooled blades could not be machined from solid super-alloys.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Sequential Process Steps

1. **Wax Pattern Injection** – Wax (or polystyrene) is injected into an aluminium die at **~70 °C** under **0.3 – 1.0 MPa** pressure.
2. **Pattern Assembly (Tree Building)** – Multiple wax patterns are welded onto a central wax sprue to form a "tree" — maximising melt-pour yield.
3. **Shell Building (Primary Coat)** – The tree is dipped into a **zircon-based (ZrSiO₄) primary slurry** (finer, denser) and stuccoed with fine ceramic sand.
4. **Shell Building (Secondary Coats)** – Repeated dipping in cheaper **alumino-silicate (mullite) slurry** + coarser stucco, with **drying cycles of 4–8 hours** between coats (typically **6–8 layers** total).
5. **De-Waxing (Pattern Removal)** – The shell is placed in a **flash-fire dewaxing autoclave at 150–200 °C** for **5–15 minutes**; the wax runs out rapidly and is recovered for recycling.
6. **Mold Firing / Sintering** – The shell is fired at **900 – 1100 °C** to burn residual wax, sinter the ceramic, and develop final strength.
7. **Pouring, Cooling, Knockout, Finishing** – Molten metal is poured under gravity, vacuum, or centrifugal force; the shell is broken by vibration or water-blast; gates are cut off and the part is finished.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook — "W-A-S-D-F-P-K":** **W**ax → **A**ssemble → **S**hell build → **D**ewax → **F**ire → **P**our → **K**nockout.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta L_{total} = \Delta L_{wax} + \Delta L_{shell} + \Delta L_{metal}$ | Total linear shrinkage compensation | mm | Master die / pattern sizing |
| 2 | $S_{wax} = \alpha_{w} \cdot \Delta T_{w} \cdot 100$ | Wax linear shrinkage | % | Pattern shrink rule |
| 3 | $S_{shell} = \alpha_{s} \cdot \Delta T_{s} \cdot 100$ | Ceramic shell shrinkage | % | Shell-room vs firing correction |
| 4 | $S_{metal} = \alpha_{m} \cdot \Delta T_{m} \cdot 100$ | Metal solidification shrinkage | % | Pouring-shrinkage rule |
| 5 | $D_{die} = D_{part} \cdot (1 + S_{total})$ | Die dimension for desired part | mm | Die design |
| 6 | $A_{shell} = A_{primary} + (n-1) \cdot A_{secondary}$ | Cumulative shell thickness | mm | Shell-build planning |
| 7 | $t_{pour} = \dfrac{V_{part} \cdot n_{tree}}{Q_{pour}}$ | Pouring time per tree | s | Foundry scheduling |
| 8 | $Y_{cast} = \dfrac{m_{usable}}{m_{poured}} \times 100\%$ | Casting yield | % | Costing |
| 9 | $Ra_{cast} \approx 0.6 \cdot Ra_{die}$ | Surface finish rule of thumb | µm | Finish prediction |
| 10 | $N_{Re,\,pour} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (pouring flow) | dimensionless | Gating design |

### 2.3 Typical Material Constants (Bold per protocol)

- **Wax linear coefficient of thermal expansion:** $\alpha_{w} \approx 5 \times 10^{-5} \, /^{\circ}\!C$
- **Ceramic shell CTE:** $\alpha_{s} \approx 6 \times 10^{-6} \, /^{\circ}\!C$
- **Carbon-steel solidification shrinkage:** $\alpha_{m} \approx 1.6$ – **2.0 %**
- **Aluminium alloy solidification shrinkage:** $\alpha_{m} \approx$ **1.25 – 1.5 %**
- **Stainless steel 304 shrinkage:** $\alpha_{m} \approx$ **2.0 – 2.2 %**
- **Inconel 718 shrinkage:** $\alpha_{m} \approx$ **2.0 %**

## 2.4 Why This Process Is Engineering-Critical

Investment casting is the **only economically viable route** for:
- **Single-crystal (SX) and directionally-solidified (DS) turbine blades** in jet engines.
- **Biomedical implants** (knee, hip, dental) requiring **Ra < 0.8 µm** without post-machining.
- **Complex internal cavities** (e.g., turbocharger housings, impellers) with curved flow paths that are impossible to machine or forge.
- **Super-alloys** (Inconel, Waspaloy, Haynes) which are too hard to machine economically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Without investment casting, modern **gas-turbine engines** (used in 90% of civil aviation) could not exist — the hollow, film-cooled blades with internal serpentine channels **cannot be made by any other process**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Shrinkage Allowance for the Master Die

The KTU board expects you to derive the **die-correction factor** for a given final part dimension when the wax, the shell, and the cast metal all undergo thermally-induced dimensional changes. This is a high-yield question, often asked for **7 marks** in Part B.

### 3.1.1 Given (Typical Board Problem)

A carbon-steel gear blank is to be investment cast. The required final outside diameter is **$D_{part} = 100.00$ mm**. The process is run at the following operating points:

- Wax is injected at $T_{inject} = 70\,^{\circ}\!C$ and the pattern is measured at $T_{room} = 25\,^{\circ}\!C$.
- The ceramic shell is built at $T_{room} = 25\,^{\circ}\!C$ and fired at $T_{fire} = 1000\,^{\circ}\!C$.
- Molten steel is poured at $T_{pour} = 1550\,^{\circ}\!C$ and solidifies near $T_{solidus} = 1450\,^{\circ}\!C$; the part is measured at $T_{room} = 25\,^{\circ}\!C$.

Material constants (all linear):

$$
\alpha_{w} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, /^{\circ}\!C, \quad
\alpha_{s} = 6 \times 10^{-6} \, /^{\circ}\!C, \quad
\alpha_{m} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, /^{\circ}\!C
$$

**Find:** the diameter $D_{die}$ that the aluminium master die must be machined to.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Temperature differences for each stage:**

$$
\Delta T_{w} = T_{inject} - T_{room} = 70 - 25 = 45\,^{\circ}\!C
$$

$$
\Delta T_{s} = T_{fire} - T_{room} = 1000 - 25 = 975\,^{\circ}\!C
$$

$$
\Delta T_{m} = T_{solidus} - T_{room} = 1450 - 25 = 1425\,^{\circ}\!C
$$

**Step 2 — Strain (linear shrinkage fraction) of each stage:**

The linear thermal strain is given by $\varepsilon = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$. Convert to a percentage to match KTU convention:

$$
S_{w} = \alpha_{w} \cdot \Delta T_{w} \times 100 = (5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 45 \times 100 = 0.225\,\%
$$

$$
S_{s} = \alpha_{s} \cdot \Delta T_{s} \times 100 = (6 \times 10^{-6}) \times 975 \times 100 = 0.585\,\%
$$

$$
S_{m} = \alpha_{m} \cdot \Delta T_{m} \times 100 = (1.8 \times 10^{-5}) \times 1425 \times 100 = 2.565\,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Total compounded shrinkage fraction:**

Because the three shrinkages occur sequentially on the *same* linear dimension, they multiply, not add. Using the small-strain approximation $(1 + a)(1 + b) \approx 1 + a + b$:

$$
S_{total} = S_{w} + S_{s} + S_{m} = 0.225 + 0.585 + 2.565 = 3.375\,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the master-die diameter:**

The part we want at the end has dimension $D_{part}$. The pattern must therefore be *larger* by the factor $(1 + S_{total})$; the die cavity must in turn be *larger* than the pattern by the *same* logic. Hence:

$$
D_{die} = D_{part} \cdot (1 + S_{total}/100)
$$

$$
D_{die} = 100.00 \times \left(1 + \frac{3.375}{100}\right) = 100.00 \times 1.03375
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{die} = 103.375\ \text{mm}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Why the result makes physical sense

- **0.225 %** comes from wax cooling from injection to room temperature — a real but small effect.
- **0.585 %** comes from the shell expanding when it is fired at 1000 °C; because the part is measured *cold* and the mold was hot when the metal filled it, the cold shell is *smaller* than its fired size, leaving the cast *larger* — so we add shrinkage on the die.
- **2.565 %** is the dominant term — almost the entire correction comes from the metal itself contracting as it solidifies and cools.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** If the question gives you an *allowance per side*, you must divide by 2. Always state whether your shrinkage is **linear (%)** or **per-side (mm)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shell Thickness from Layered Dipping

### 3.2.1 Given

A wax tree is dipped in a primary slurry giving a coat of **$t_p = 0.5$ mm** (wet), followed by **$n = 7$ secondary coats**, each adding **$t_s = 0.8$ mm** (wet). The wet-to-dry shrinkage of the ceramic is **$\eta = 15\%$** (i.e., the dried coat is 15 % thinner than the wet coat). Find the final dry shell thickness $T_{dry}$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total wet thickness:**

$$
T_{wet} = t_p + (n-1) \cdot t_s = 0.5 + 6 \times 0.8 = 5.3\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the dry/wet ratio $(1 - \eta)$:**

$$
T_{dry} = T_{wet} \times (1 - \eta) = 5.3 \times 0.85
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{dry} = 4.505\ \text{mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering check:** Typical industrial shells are 6–15 mm thick; a 4.5 mm shell is on the thin side, suitable for **small aluminium parts** but **NOT** for heavy steel pours — would need reinforcement.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Process vs. Competing Routes)

| Attribute | Investment Casting | Sand Casting | Die Casting | CNC Machining |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dimensional Tolerance | **± 0.1 – 0.4 mm** (Excellent) | ± 1.0 – 2.0 mm | ± 0.05 – 0.2 mm | ± 0.01 mm (Best) |
| Surface Finish Ra | **1.6 – 6.3 µm** (Excellent) | 12.5 – 25 µm | 0.8 – 3.2 µm | 0.4 – 1.6 µm |
| Min Section Thickness | **0.4 – 0.8 mm** | 3 – 6 mm | 0.8 – 1.5 mm | 0.2 – 0.5 mm |
| Max Part Size | ≈ 1 m, 25 kg | Unlimited | 0.6 m, 5 kg | Bed-limited |
| Cost per Piece (low volume) | **Medium** | Low | Very High (tooling) | Very High |
| Cost per Piece (high volume) | Medium | Low | **Lowest** | High |
| Material Range | **Almost all** (incl. super-alloys) | All | Non-ferrous only | All machinable |
| Internal Complexity | **Excellent** | Poor | Moderate | Poor (drilling only) |
| Lead Time (tooling) | 4 – 8 weeks | 1 – 2 weeks | 12 – 16 weeks | 1 – 4 weeks |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** When asked *"why investment casting?"*, always cite **three reasons together**: (1) complex internal geometry, (2) super-alloy workability, (3) near-net shape eliminating machining.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow Diagram (Investment Casting Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Wax Injection into Aluminium Die at 70 C] --> B[Step 2: Pattern Inspection and Trimming]
    B --> C[Step 3: Tree Assembly - Wax Patterns Welded to Central Sprue]
    C --> D[Step 4: Primary Dip in Zircon Slurry plus Fine Stucco]
    D --> E[Step 5: Drying 4 to 6 hours in Controlled Humidity Room]
    E --> F{Step 6: More Coats Required?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Step 7: Secondary Dip in Alumino-Silicate Slurry plus Coarse Stucco]
    G --> E
    F -- No --> H[Step 8: Dewaxing in Flash-Fire Autoclave at 150 to 200 C]
    H --> I[Step 9: Wax Recovery for Recycling]
    I --> J[Step 10: Shell Firing in Furnace at 900 to 1100 C]
    J --> K[Step 11: Pre-Heated Shell Transferred to Pouring Station]
    K --> L[Step 12: Pouring of Molten Metal by Gravity or Vacuum or Centrifugal]
    L --> M[Step 13: Controlled Solidification and Cooling]
    M --> N[Step 14: Knockout by Vibration or Water Blast]
    N --> O[Step 15: Cut-off, Grinding, and Final Inspection]
    O --> P[Step 16: Finished Investment Cast Component]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Investment Casting Foundry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAW[Raw Material Bay]
        R1[Wax Pellets]
        R2[Ceramic Slurry Tanks]
        R3[Stucco Sand Silos]
        R4[Alloy Ingots]
    end

    subgraph MFG[Manufacturing Floor]
        M1[Die Shop and Wax Injection Presses]
        M2[Pattern Assembly Room]
        M3[Shell Building Robot Line]
        M4[Dewax Autoclave]
        M5[Shell Firing Furnace]
        M6[Pouring Station with Vacuum Chamber]
        M7[Knockout and Cut-off Cell]
        M8[Finishing and Inspection Cell]
    end

    subgraph QC[Quality Control]
        Q1[CMM and 3D Scanner]
        Q2[Radiographic and Dye Penetrant Test]
        Q3[Metallographic Lab]
    end

    R1 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    R2 --> M3
    R3 --> M3
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M5
    M5 --> M6
    R4 --> M6
    M6 --> M7
    M7 --> M8
    M8 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> SHIP[Shipping to Customer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Each block represents an **isolated cleanroom-grade workstation** with its own HVAC, dust extraction, and temperature/humidity control — contamination control is critical in investment casting, unlike sand casting where a dusty shop is normal.

## 4.3 Schematic of a Single Wax Pattern + Shell (cross-section)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W[Wax Pattern Body] --> SP[Sprue Wax]
    SP --> CE[Primary Ceramic Coat 0.3 to 0.5 mm]
    CE --> SE[Secondary Ceramic Coats 4 to 12 mm]
    SE --> OUT[Outer Stucco Grains for Surface Grip]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q:** List any **six advantages** of investment casting over sand casting.

**Model Answer (6 points, 3 marks):**

1. **Higher dimensional accuracy** (tolerances of **± 0.1 mm** vs. **± 1.0 mm** in sand).
2. **Superior surface finish** (Ra **1.6 – 6.3 µm** vs. Ra **12.5 – 25 µm** in sand).
3. **Capability to cast intricate shapes and thin sections** down to **0.4 mm**, which is impossible in sand.
4. **Wider alloy range** — can cast reactive metals (titanium, super-alloys) because ceramic shell is chemically inert.
5. **Minimal or no machining** — near-net shape reduces material wastage by **60 – 90 %**.
6. **Reproducible, repeatable geometry** — wax dies ensure every part is identical, ideal for serial production of critical aerospace parts.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** What is the function of the **primary coat** in shell building?

**Model Answer:**

The primary (first) coat is prepared from a **fine refractory flour (zircon ZrSiO₄ or fused silica)** mixed with a silica-based binder. Its functions are:

1. To **reproduce the surface finish** of the wax pattern accurately on the inner mold surface — this directly determines the **as-cast Ra** of the component.
2. To provide a **dense, low-permeability layer** that resists metal penetration and minimizes surface reactions with reactive alloys (titanium, Inconel).
3. To form a strong **ceramic–wax interface** so the shell does not delaminate during dewaxing.

Subsequent secondary coats use **coarser, cheaper mullite or alumino-silicate** to build bulk strength.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**
**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, explain the **step-by-step process of investment casting**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bronze impeller is to be investment cast. The required finished diameter is **$D = 80$ mm**. Given: $\alpha_{wax} = 4 \times 10^{-5} /^{\circ}\!C$, $\alpha_{shell} = 5 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}\!C$, $\alpha_{bronze} = 1.9 \times 10^{-5} /^{\circ}\!C$, wax cooled from 75 °C to 25 °C, shell fired from 25 °C to 1050 °C, bronze poured at 1100 °C, solidified at 980 °C. **Calculate the master-die diameter** to be used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Flow Diagram (Already covered in Section 4.1) — Verbal Explanation:**

1. **Wax Injection** — wax pellets melted at 70–80 °C, injected into an aluminium die under 0.3–1.0 MPa. Die is then cooled and the wax pattern is ejected.
2. **Pattern Inspection & Trimming** — parting-line flash removed with a hot blade.
3. **Tree Assembly** — multiple patterns are welded to a wax sprue to form a cluster.
4. **Primary Slurry Dip + Stucco** — tree dipped in zircon slurry, then rain-sand stuccoed with fine ceramic sand (e.g., 100 mesh). Dries in 4–6 h.
5. **Secondary Coat Building** — repeated 4–7 times using cheaper alumino-silicate slurry + coarser stucco.
6. **Dewaxing** — autoclave at 150–200 °C for 5–15 min; wax runs out → recovered.
7. **Shell Firing** — 900–1100 °C for 1–2 h; wax residues burn off, ceramic sinters, shell becomes rigid.
8. **Pouring** — molten metal poured by gravity, vacuum, or centrifugal force into the pre-heated shell.
9. **Cooling & Knockout** — shell is vibrated or water-blasted away.
10. **Cut-off & Finishing** — gates cut, surfaces ground, part inspected.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Temperature differences:**

$$
\Delta T_w = 75 - 25 = 50\,^{\circ}\!C
$$

$$
\Delta T_s = 1050 - 25 = 1025\,^{\circ}\!C
$$

$$
\Delta T_m = 980 - 25 = 955\,^{\circ}\!C
$$

**Step 2 — Percentage shrinkages:**

$$
S_w = (4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 50 \times 100 = 0.20\,\%
$$

$$
S_s = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1025 \times 100 = 0.5125\,\%
$$

$$
S_m = (1.9 \times 10^{-5}) \times 955 \times 100 = 1.8145\,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Total shrinkage:**

$$
S_{total} = 0.20 + 0.5125 + 1.8145 = 2.527\,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Die diameter:**

$$
D_{die} = 80 \times \left(1 + \frac{2.527}{100}\right) = 80 \times 1.02527
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{die} = 82.022\ \text{mm}}
$$

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**
**(a)** Compare **investment casting, sand casting, and die casting** based on tolerance, surface finish, section thickness, cost for small batch, and applicable alloys. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **defects commonly encountered in investment castings** and their remedies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table** — already prepared in **Section 3.3**. Reproduce the matrix in the exam. *(Allocate ~5 cells × 1.4 marks each.)*

**(b) Defects and Remedies:**

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Misrun / Cold shut** | Pouring temperature too low, slow fill | Increase pour temp by 30–50 °C; use vacuum or centrifugal pouring |
| **Porosity (gas)** | Entrapped air in shell, moisture in wax | Pre-heat shell; vacuum pour; dry wax |
| **Shrinkage cavity** | Inadequate riser design | Add directional risers; use chills |
| **Ceramic inclusions** | Shell flaking during pour | Strengthen primary coat; avoid rough handling |
| **Surface裂纹 (cracks)** | Hot tearing due to shell restraint | Adjust alloy composition; control cooling rate |
| **Wax pattern distortion** | Uneven cooling, wrong wax | Use proper wax; control die temperature |
| **Metal-mold reaction** | Reactive alloys (Ti) attacking silica | Use yttria-based face coats for reactive metals |
| **Dimensional out-of-tolerance** | Wrong shrinkage allowance | Recalculate $S_{total}$ precisely; verify die temp |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Many students write $D_{die} = 80 \times 1.025 = 81.998$ mm, forgetting that $S$ is in **%** and must be divided by 100 before being added. **Always show the $(1 + S/100)$ form explicitly.**
> 2. **Sign error in shell shrinkage:** The shell *expands* on heating but the *part is measured cold*, so the cold shell is *smaller*. The final part is *larger* than the cold shell — but the part has also shrunk relative to the *hot* shell, so the **net effect still adds to total shrinkage** (it is a positive contribution to $S_{total}$). Do NOT subtract it.
> 3. **Wrong CTEs:** Students often confuse *linear* and *volumetric* coefficients. Volumetric = **3 × linear**. Investment-casting boards want the **linear** form.
> 4. **Skipping the units of $\Delta T$:** Always write $°C$ or K — both work because it's a *difference*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Precision Investment Casting**

- **Full name:** Investment casting = Lost-wax casting = Precision casting.
- **Key advantages:** ± 0.1 mm tolerance, Ra 1.6–6.3 µm, min section 0.4 mm, complex internal passages, super-alloy capable.
- **Master formula to memorize:** $D_{die} = D_{part} \cdot (1 + S_{total}/100)$.
- **Three shrinkages to sum:** Wax (cooling) + Shell (firing expansion reversed) + Metal (solidification + cooling).
- **Primary coat purpose:** Surface reproduction + chemical barrier.
- **Secondary coat purpose:** Bulk structural strength.
- **Dewaxing is rapid, not slow** — slow heating causes shell cracking from wax pressure; flash-fire dewaxing is the industrial standard.
- **Shell firing temperature:** 900 – 1100 °C.
- **Wax injection pressure:** 0.3 – 1.0 MPa at 70 °C.
- **Typical layer count:** 6 – 8 dips with drying in between.
- **Most common defect in KTU questions:** Misrun and shrinkage porosity.
- **Industries served:** Aerospace (turbine blades), biomedical (implants), defence, jewellery, automotive (turbochargers).
- **Material range:** Almost unlimited — ferrous, non-ferrous, super-alloys, titanium, even precious metals.
- **Cost driver:** Wax tooling is cheaper than die-casting dies, so investment casting wins at **low-to-medium volumes** (50 – 50,000 parts/year).
- **The 16-step process (W-A-S-D-F-P-K + extras):** Wax → Assemble → Shell → Dewax → Fire → Pour → Knockout.
- **Always re-check:** $S_{total}$ is a *sum of percentages*, then converted to a *fraction* by dividing by 100 before being used in the $(1 + x)$ term.

---

**End of Notes — Module 1 / Precision Investment Casting (PBMET304)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Die Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Die Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Die Casting** is a precision metal casting process in which molten metal is forced under high pressure (typically **10 MPa to 175 MPa**) into a reusable steel mold (called a **die**) having the cavity of the desired shape. The metal solidifies rapidly under sustained pressure, the die opens, and the casting is ejected. The process is highly suited for **non-ferrous alloys** (Zinc, Aluminum, Magnesium, and Copper alloys) and is governed by the principle of **permanent mold casting under pressure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expected Answer):**  
> Die casting is a manufacturing process that produces accurately dimensioned, sharply defined, smooth or textured-surface metal parts by forcing molten metal under high pressure into reusable steel dies (molds) called dies.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (Board Examiner Expectation)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Die** | Reusable hardened steel mold (typically H13 tool steel) |
| **Shot** | The volume of molten metal injected per cycle |
| **Plunger / Gooseneck** | The piston that injects molten metal into the die |
| **Biscuit** | Excess metal remaining in the shot sleeve after injection |
| **Die Cavity** | The negative space inside the die that gives the part its shape |
| **Overflow Well** | Reservoir that traps contaminants, air, and cold metal |
| **Flash** | Thin excess metal escape at die parting line |
| **Ejector Pins** | Pins that push the solidified casting out of the die |
| **Cooling Channels** | Water/oil lines that regulate die temperature |
| **Cycle Time** | Total time from die close to die open for ejection |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Syringe & Jelly Mold" Concept

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy:**  
> Imagine a **giant medical syringe** filled with hot molten chocolate (your molten aluminum). When you push the plunger with great force, the liquid chocolate rushes into a **sturdy metal cookie-cutter shape (the die)**. You hold the pressure for a few seconds — the chocolate hardens, you pull the plunger back, open the mold, and pop out a perfectly shaped chocolate figure. That cookie-cutter is the **die**, the syringe is the **plunger-sleeve assembly**, and the resulting chocolate figure is your **die casting**. Reusing the same cookie-cutter shape is what makes die casting "permanent mold" and economical for mass production.

## 1.4 Classification of Die Casting Processes (KTU High-Yield)

Die casting is broadly classified into **two main categories**, each with distinct KTU exam relevance:

1. **Hot-Chamber Die Casting (also called "Gooseneck" Process)**
2. **Cold-Chamber Die Casting**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Board Question Frequency:** A direct question on the difference between hot-chamber and cold-chamber die casting has appeared in **3 out of the last 5 KTU University Exams** for Manufacturing Processes (PBMET304). This is a guaranteed high-weightage topic.

### 1.4.1 Hot-Chamber Die Casting

- The **melting pot / furnace is integral** to the machine.
- The gooseneck is **submerged in the molten metal bath**.
- Molten metal flows into the shot sleeve by **gravity/pressure differential**.
- Best suited for **low melting-point alloys**: Zinc, Tin, Lead, and Magnesium alloys.
- **Melting point < 420°C** is the practical operating limit.
- Cycle times are very fast (**typically 200 to 500 cycles per hour**).

### 1.4.2 Cold-Chamber Die Casting

- The melting furnace is **separate** from the die casting machine.
- Molten metal is **poured manually or by ladle** into the shot sleeve.
- The plunger then injects the metal into the closed die.
- Best suited for **high melting-point alloys**: Aluminum, Brass, Magnesium with high Al content, and Copper alloys.
- **Melting point > 420°C** to avoid overheating the plunger and sleeve.
- Cycle times are slightly slower due to the pouring step.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Operational Step-by-Step Logic (Hot-Chamber Die Casting)

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always draw or describe the **cycle sequence** for full marks in a "describe the process" question.

1. **Die Closing:** The two die halves (cover die and ejector die) close under high **clamping force** (typically 50 to 4000 tonnes).
2. **Molten Metal Charging:** The gooseneck descends, submerges its nozzle in the bath, and molten metal fills the shot sleeve through the intake port.
3. **Injection:** Hydraulic pressure (typically **7 to 14 MPa**) is applied to the plunger. Through a **pressure intensification system**, this is amplified to **14 to 35 MPa** at the die cavity.
4. **Cooling/Solidification:** Water-cooled channels extract heat. Solidification time is governed by **Chvorinov's Rule**.
5. **Die Opening:** The clamping unit releases and the die opens along the **parting line**.
6. **Ejection:** Ejector pins push the casting (along with biscuit and overflow metal) out of the die cavity.
7. **Die Lubrication & Reset:** Spray lubricant is applied; die closes; cycle repeats.

## 2.2 Operational Step-by-Step Logic (Cold-Chamber Die Casting)

1. Die closes; clamping force is applied.
2. A measured **shot of molten metal is ladled** into the shot sleeve (horizontal cold-chamber machines are most common).
3. The plunger injects the metal into the die cavity under high pressure (**30 to 175 MPa**).
4. Solidification occurs with continued pressure (maintained for **5 to 15 seconds**).
5. Die opens; casting is ejected; shot sleeve is cleaned of biscuit.
6. Die is lubricated, closed, and the next ladle is poured.

## 2.3 The Pressure Intensification Principle (Critical for KTU Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> This is the **single most important numerical concept** in die casting. The hydraulic system uses a differential area principle to multiply the applied force.

The hydraulic pressure acts on a large piston, which in turn drives a smaller plunger. By Pascal's Law, the pressure is conserved, but the force on the smaller plunger tip is amplified.

**Pressure Intensification Ratio (PIR):**

$$
PIR \;=\; \frac{A_{\text{ram}}}{A_{\text{plunger}}}
$$

Where:
- $A_{\text{ram}}$ = Cross-sectional area of the hydraulic (booster) ram
- $A_{\text{plunger}}$ = Cross-sectional area of the injection plunger

The **intensified injection pressure** delivered at the metal:

$$
P_{\text{metal}} \;=\; P_{\text{hydraulic}} \times PIR \;=\; P_{\text{hyd}} \times \frac{A_{\text{ram}}}{A_{\text{plunger}}}
$$

## 2.4 Clamping Force Calculation (Tonnage of Die Casting Machine)

The **clamping force** must resist the **mould opening force** generated by injection pressure on the projected area of the casting (plus runner and overflow).

**Mould Opening Force (M):**

$$
F_{\text{mould opening}} \;=\; P_{\text{metal}} \times A_{\text{projected}}
$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{metal}}$ = Intensified metal injection pressure (Pa or MPa)
- $A_{\text{projected}}$ = Total projected area normal to the parting line ($\text{m}^2$ or $\text{mm}^2$)

**Required Clamping Tonnage (T):**

$$
T_{\text{clamp}} \;\geq\; F_{\text{mould opening}} \times SF
$$

Where:
- $SF$ = **Safety Factor**, typically between **1.15 to 1.5** to account for dynamic pressure spikes

## 2.5 Solidification Time (Chvorinov's Rule Applied to Die Casting)

Because the die is a high-thermal-conductivity steel mold, the cast surface temperature approximates the die temperature. Chvorinov's rule still applies:

$$
t_{s} \;=\; B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^{n}
$$

Where:
- $t_{s}$ = Solidification time (seconds)
- $V$ = Volume of the casting ($\text{m}^3$)
- $A$ = Surface area of the casting exposed to the die ($\text{m}^2$)
- $B$ = Die constant (depends on metal and die material) — typically **2.5 to 4.0 for steel dies**
- $n$ = Exponent, conventionally **n = 2** for most casting situations

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pressure Intensification Ratio** | $PIR = A_{\text{ram}} / A_{\text{plunger}}$ | dimensionless | Step-up of hydraulic to injection pressure |
| 2 | **Intensified Metal Pressure** | $P_{\text{metal}} = P_{\text{hyd}} \times PIR$ | MPa | Pressure at die cavity |
| 3 | **Mould Opening Force** | $F_{\text{open}} = P_{\text{metal}} \times A_{\text{projected}}$ | N or kN | Force trying to open the die |
| 4 | **Clamping Tonnage** | $T = F_{\text{open}} \times SF$ | Tonnes (1 T = 9.81 kN) | Machine selection |
| 5 | **Solidification Time** | $t_{s} = B \left( V/A \right)^{2}$ | seconds | Cooling time per cycle |
| 6 | **Cycle Time** | $t_{c} = t_{\text{close}} + t_{\text{inject}} + t_{\text{cool}} + t_{\text{eject}}$ | seconds | Production rate calculation |
| 7 | **Production Rate** | $R = (3600 \times 60 / t_{c}) \times \eta$ | pieces/hr | Where $\eta$ = efficiency (0.7 to 0.9) |
| 8 | **Mass of Casting** | $m = \rho \times V$ | kg | Shot weight |
| 9 | **Volumetric Shrinkage** | $\Delta V = V_{0} \times S_v$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Feeder/runner design |
| 10 | **Gate Velocity** | $v_{g} = Q / A_{g}$ | m/s | $Q$ = volumetric flow; $A_{g}$ = gate area |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOR KTU:** When writing absolute value in any formula in your answer book, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never use the vertical bar character which can be confused with set notation.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

Die casting is the backbone of:
- **Automotive industry** (engine blocks, gearbox housings, brackets) — typically cold-chamber Al die casting
- **Consumer electronics** (laptop chassis, smartphone bodies) — hot-chamber Zn or Mg die casting
- **Hardware industry** (door locks, hinges, handles)
- **Toys and decorative items** (Zamak alloys)
- **Aerospace** (magnesium alloy housings for instruments)

The **modern electric vehicle revolution** has made **high-pressure cold-chamber aluminum die casting** (e.g., Tesla's "Giga Press" at **6000 to 9000 tonnes clamping force**) one of the most talked-about manufacturing advancements of the 2020s.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Deriving the Clamping Force Equation from First Principles

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** A 7-mark question often asks to "derive an expression for the clamping force required for a die casting machine." Students who write only the final formula without derivation lose 3-4 marks.

### Step 1 — Define the Physical System

Consider a die casting machine injecting molten metal into a closed steel die. The hydraulic actuator applies a force $F_{\text{hyd}}$ on the booster ram of cross-sectional area $A_{\text{ram}}$. This drives the injection plunger of area $A_{\text{plunger}}$, which in turn pushes molten metal into the die cavity.

### Step 2 — Apply Pascal's Law at the Hydraulic Ram

The hydraulic pressure (assumed incompressible, no losses):

$$
P_{\text{hyd}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\text{hyd}}}{A_{\text{ram}}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
F_{\text{hyd}} \;=\; P_{\text{hyd}} \times A_{\text{ram}}
$$

### Step 3 — Force Transmission to the Injection Plunger

Assuming a **rigid mechanical linkage** between the booster ram and the injection plunger (in actual machines, a differential piston arrangement is used), the force on the plunger is:

$$
F_{\text{plunger}} \;=\; F_{\text{hyd}} \;=\; P_{\text{hyd}} \times A_{\text{ram}}
$$

### Step 4 — Pressure of Molten Metal at the Plunger Tip

The molten metal pressure is the plunger force divided by the plunger's cross-sectional area:

$$
P_{\text{metal}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\text{plunger}}}{A_{\text{plunger}}}
$$

Substituting $F_{\text{plunger}}$:

$$
P_{\text{metal}} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{hyd}} \times A_{\text{ram}}}{A_{\text{plunger}}}
$$

### Step 5 — Define the Pressure Intensification Ratio

By definition:

$$
PIR \;=\; \frac{A_{\text{ram}}}{A_{\text{plunger}}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{\text{metal}} \;=\; P_{\text{hyd}} \times PIR
$$

This completes the **intensification derivation**.

### Step 6 — Force Acting on the Die Cavity Walls

The molten metal at pressure $P_{\text{metal}}$ acts on every internal wall of the die. The **net mould-opening force** is the pressure multiplied by the **projected area** of the casting (plus runner, gate, and overflow) on a plane normal to the die-opening direction:

$$
F_{\text{open}} \;=\; P_{\text{metal}} \times A_{\text{projected}}
$$

### Step 7 — Apply Equilibrium to the Clamping System

For the die to remain closed, the clamping force $F_{\text{clamp}}$ must balance the mould-opening force with a safety margin:

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} \;\geq\; F_{\text{open}}
$$

Including a safety factor $SF$ (typically 1.2):

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} \;=\; F_{\text{open}} \times SF
$$

### Step 8 — Convert Force to Machine Tonnage

Since die casting machines are rated in **tonnes of clamping force** and 1 tonne-force = $9.81 \times 10^{3}$ N:

$$
T_{\text{clamp}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\text{clamp}}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} \quad \text{(in tonnes-force)}
$$

### Final Consolidated Equation

$$
\boxed{\,T_{\text{clamp}} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{hyd}} \times PIR \times A_{\text{projected}} \times SF}{9.81 \times 10^{3}}\,}
$$

## 3.2 Fully Solved KTU-Style Numerical Problem (Worked Example)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Board-Standard Problem:** This is the **exact format** of KTU numerical questions. Practice it until you can solve it in under 6 minutes.

**Problem:**  
A die casting machine has a hydraulic pressure of 12 MPa acting on a booster ram of diameter 200 mm. The injection plunger has a diameter of 80 mm. The casting's projected area on the parting plane is 25,000 $\text{mm}^2$. Calculate:
1. The pressure intensification ratio.
2. The pressure of the molten metal entering the die.
3. The mould-opening force.
4. The required machine tonnage (use SF = 1.25).

**Given:**
- $P_{\text{hyd}} = 12 \text{ MPa} = 12 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- $D_{\text{ram}} = 200 \text{ mm}$
- $D_{\text{plunger}} = 80 \text{ mm}$
- $A_{\text{projected}} = 25{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$
- $SF = 1.25$

### Step 1 — Areas of Ram and Plunger

$$
A_{\text{ram}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} D_{\text{ram}}^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \times (200)^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \times 40{,}000
$$

$$
A_{\text{ram}} \;=\; 31{,}415.93 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_{\text{plunger}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} D_{\text{plunger}}^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \times (80)^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6{,}400
$$

$$
A_{\text{plunger}} \;=\; 5{,}026.55 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Pressure Intensification Ratio

$$
PIR \;=\; \frac{A_{\text{ram}}}{A_{\text{plunger}}} \;=\; \frac{31{,}415.93}{5{,}026.55} \;=\; 6.25
$$

**Answer (1):** $PIR = 6.25$ (dimensionless)

### Step 3 — Molten Metal Pressure

$$
P_{\text{metal}} \;=\; P_{\text{hyd}} \times PIR \;=\; 12 \text{ MPa} \times 6.25
$$

$$
P_{\text{metal}} \;=\; 75 \text{ MPa} \;=\; 75 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Answer (2):** $P_{\text{metal}} = 75$ MPa

### Step 4 — Mould Opening Force

$$
F_{\text{open}} \;=\; P_{\text{metal}} \times A_{\text{projected}} \;=\; 75 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 25{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
F_{\text{open}} \;=\; 1{,}875{,}000 \text{ N} \;=\; 1875 \text{ kN}
$$

**Answer (3):** $F_{\text{open}} = 1875$ kN

### Step 5 — Required Clamping Tonnage

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} \;=\; F_{\text{open}} \times SF \;=\; 1875 \text{ kN} \times 1.25 \;=\; 2343.75 \text{ kN}
$$

Converting to tonnes-force:

$$
T_{\text{clamp}} \;=\; \frac{2343.75 \times 10^{3} \text{ N}}{9.81 \times 10^{3} \text{ N/tonne}} \;=\; 238.91 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Answer (4):** $T_{\text{clamp}} \approx 239$ tonnes (commercial machine selection: **250 tonne** machine)

## 3.3 KTU Analytical Matrix: Hot-Chamber vs Cold-Chamber

| Parameter | Hot-Chamber | Cold-Chamber |
|---|---|---|
| **Melting Furnace Position** | Integral with machine | Separate ladling furnace |
| **Alloy Suitability** | Zn, Pb, Sn, low-Mg (Tm < 420°C) | Al, Cu, Mg, Brass (Tm > 420°C) |
| **Injection Pressure** | 7 to 35 MPa | 30 to 175 MPa |
| **Cycle Time** | Faster (no ladling) | Slower (ladling step) |
| **Plunger Life** | Shorter (immersed in metal) | Longer |
| **Metal Contamination** | Higher (iron pickup from gooseneck) | Lower |
| **Typical Shot Weight** | < 5 kg | Up to 50+ kg |
| **Capital Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Die Temperature Control** | Tighter | Looser |
| **Common Products** | Zamak toy parts, locks | Engine blocks, transmission housings |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Hot-Chamber Die Casting — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Melting Furnace with Molten Zn Alloy] --> B1[Gooseneck Nozzle Submerges]
    B1 --> C1[Intake Port Opens]
    C1 --> D1[Molten Metal Fills Shot Sleeve]
    D1 --> E1[Hydraulic Pressure Applied 7-14 MPa]
    E1 --> F1[Pressure Intensifier Multiplies Force]
    F1 --> G1[Plunger Pushes Metal at 14-35 MPa]
    G1 --> H1[Closed Steel Die with Cavity]
    H1 --> I1[Solidification with Cooling Channels]
    I1 --> J1[Die Opens at Parting Line]
    J1 --> K1[Ejector Pins Push Casting Out]
    K1 --> L1[Trim and Finish Casting]
    L1 --> M1[Cycle Restarts]
```

## 4.2 Cold-Chamber Die Casting — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A2[Separate Melting Furnace] --> B2[Operator Ladles Measured Shot]
    B2 --> C2[Pour into Horizontal Shot Sleeve]
    C2 --> D2[Plunger Advances Horizontally]
    D2 --> E2[High Pressure Injection 30-175 MPa]
    E2 --> F2[Closed Die Cavity Fills Rapidly]
    F2 --> G2[Pressure Held 5-15 seconds]
    G2 --> H2[Metal Solidifies]
    H2 --> I2[Die Opens]
    I2 --> J2[Ejector System Activated]
    J2 --> K2[Shot Sleeve Cleaned of Biscuit]
    K2 --> L2[Next Ladle Poured]
```

## 4.3 Pressure Intensification System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Hydraulic Pump 7-14 MPa] --> P2[Booster Ram Area Aram]
    P2 --> P3[Intensification Chamber]
    P3 --> P4[Injection Plunger Area Aplunger]
    P4 --> P5[High Pressure Molten Metal 14-175 MPa]
    P5 --> P6[Die Cavity]
    P6 --> P7[Solidified Casting]
```

## 4.4 Complete Die Casting Cycle — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ST1[Step 1 Die Close and Lock] --> ST2[Step 2 Lubricant Spray]
    ST2 --> ST3[Step 3 Metal Charge]
    ST3 --> ST4[Step 4 Plunger Injection]
    ST4 --> ST5[Step 5 Hold Pressure and Cool]
    ST5 --> ST6[Step 6 Die Open]
    ST6 --> ST7[Step 7 Eject Casting]
    ST7 --> ST8[Step 8 Trim Overflow]
    ST8 --> ST1
```

## 4.5 Die Components and Their Functions — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DC1[Cover Die Half] --> DCF1[Contains Cavity]
    DCF1 --> DCF2[Forms External Shape]
    DC3[Ejector Die Half] --> DCF3[Contains Ejector Pins]
    DCF3 --> DCF4[Contains Cooling Channels]
    DC5[Shot Sleeve] --> DCF5[Receives Molten Metal]
    DCF5 --> DCF6[Houses Plunger]
    DC7[Overflow Wells] --> DCF7[Trap Air and Slag]
    DC9[Parting Line] --> DCF9[Die Separation Plane]
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define die casting. List any four advantages of die casting over sand casting.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Die casting is a permanent mold casting process in which molten metal is forced into a steel die cavity under high pressure, where it solidifies rapidly to form the desired shape.

**Four Advantages:**

1. **Excellent Dimensional Accuracy** — Tolerances of $\pm 0.1$ mm are routinely achieved without machining.
2. **Superior Surface Finish** — Surface roughness as low as 1 to 2 $\mu$m Ra is typical, eliminating the need for secondary finishing.
3. **High Production Rate** — Cycle times of 5 to 60 seconds allow thousands of castings per shift.
4. **Reduced Machining** — Near-net-shape production reduces material wastage and machining cost.
5. **Higher Mechanical Strength** — Rapid solidification produces fine grain structure, improving tensile strength.

*(Any four correct points — 0.5 marks each for advantages + 1 mark for definition)*

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between hot-chamber and cold-chamber die casting processes (any 4 points).**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Hot-Chamber Die Casting | Cold-Chamber Die Casting |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Melting pot is integral with the machine | Melting furnace is separate |
| 2 | Gooseneck is submerged in molten metal | Ladle pours metal into shot sleeve |
| 3 | Used for low-melting alloys (Zn, Pb) | Used for high-melting alloys (Al, Cu, Mg) |
| 4 | Lower injection pressure (7–35 MPa) | Higher injection pressure (30–175 MPa) |
| 5 | Faster cycle time | Slower cycle time due to ladling |
| 6 | Iron contamination is higher | Iron contamination is lower |

*(Any 4 correct pairs — 0.75 marks per pair)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Internal Choice 1):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **hot-chamber die casting process** in detail. List its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A die casting machine has a hydraulic cylinder of **150 mm diameter** and a plunger of **60 mm diameter**. The hydraulic pressure is **10 MPa**. The projected area of the casting including runners is **30,000 mm²**. Determine: **(i)** the metal injection pressure, **(ii)** the mould-opening force, and **(iii)** the required clamping tonnage (use safety factor = 1.2). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Hot-Chamber Die Casting Process:**

In hot-chamber (gooseneck) die casting, the melting furnace is built into the die casting machine itself. The gooseneck-shaped metal conduit remains permanently immersed in the molten metal bath. The working sequence is:

1. **Initial Position:** The die is closed and locked. The gooseneck nozzle is submerged in the molten metal bath.
2. **Charging:** A small hydraulic cylinder raises the plunger, creating a partial vacuum. Molten metal rushes up through the intake port into the shot sleeve.
3. **Injection:** A second, larger hydraulic cylinder applies pressure to the plunger, which forces the molten metal through the nozzle and into the die cavity at high pressure.
4. **Cooling:** Water circulated through the die cooling channels extracts heat; the metal solidifies under sustained pressure.
5. **Ejection:** The die opens; ejector pins push the casting (with biscuit attached) out of the die.
6. **Reset:** Lubricant is sprayed, die closes, and the cycle repeats.

**Advantages:**
- High production rate (500+ cycles/hour for zinc)
- Lower injection pressure required
- Compact machine footprint
- Excellent for thin-walled zinc castings
- Energy efficient (no ladling)

**Limitations:**
- Cannot be used for high-melting-point alloys (Al, Cu)
- Iron pickup from the gooseneck reduces alloy purity
- Plunger and gooseneck wear out faster
- Limited shot weight (usually < 5 kg)

**Sketch Description (Board Standard):**  
*(Since KTU allows schematic representation)* — Draw a vertical die casting machine with: (1) Melting pot at the bottom, (2) Gooseneck rising from the pot, (3) Shot sleeve and plunger, (4) Booster cylinder, (5) Two die halves (cover and ejector), (6) Cooling water inlet/outlet, (7) Ejector pins, (8) Hydraulic clamping unit.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given Data:**
- $D_{\text{ram}} = 150 \text{ mm}$
- $D_{\text{plunger}} = 60 \text{ mm}$
- $P_{\text{hyd}} = 10 \text{ MPa} = 10 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- $A_{\text{projected}} = 30{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$
- $SF = 1.2$

**Calculations:**

Areas:

$$
A_{\text{ram}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \times (150)^{2} \;=\; 17{,}671.46 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_{\text{plunger}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \times (60)^{2} \;=\; 2827.43 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**(i) Metal Injection Pressure:**

$$
PIR \;=\; \frac{A_{\text{ram}}}{A_{\text{plunger}}} \;=\; \frac{17{,}671.46}{2827.43} \;=\; 6.25
$$

$$
P_{\text{metal}} \;=\; P_{\text{hyd}} \times PIR \;=\; 10 \times 6.25 \;=\; 62.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Mould-Opening Force:**

$$
F_{\text{open}} \;=\; P_{\text{metal}} \times A_{\text{projected}} \;=\; 62.5 \times 30{,}000
$$

$$
F_{\text{open}} \;=\; 1{,}875{,}000 \text{ N} \;=\; 1875 \text{ kN}
$$

**(iii) Required Clamping Tonnage:**

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} \;=\; F_{\text{open}} \times SF \;=\; 1875 \times 1.2 \;=\; 2250 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
T_{\text{clamp}} \;=\; \frac{2250 \times 10^{3}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} \;=\; 229.36 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Result:** A **250-tonne** commercial die casting machine should be selected.

---

### **Question B (Internal Choice 2):** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **cold-chamber die casting process** with a schematic diagram. State the **applications of die casting** in the automotive and consumer electronics industries. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **defects encountered in die castings** (any 7 defects with their causes and remedies). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Cold-Chamber Die Casting Process:**

In cold-chamber die casting, the melting furnace is **separate** from the die casting machine. A measured quantity of molten metal is **ladled** into the horizontal shot sleeve, after which a hydraulically driven plunger forces the metal into the closed die cavity.

**Working Sequence:**

1. The die is closed and locked with high clamping force.
2. An operator (or auto-ladle) pours a precisely measured **shot** of molten aluminum (or copper alloy) into the shot sleeve.
3. The plunger is actuated, injecting the metal at **30 to 175 MPa** into the die cavity.
4. Pressure is maintained for **5–15 seconds** to compensate for shrinkage.
5. The die opens, and ejector pins push the casting (with biscuit) out.
6. The biscuit is sheared off, and the casting proceeds to trimming.

**Why "Cold Chamber"?** The shot sleeve is not immersed in molten metal, so it remains comparatively cool, allowing the use of higher-temperature alloys without damaging the equipment.

**Applications:**

*Automotive Industry:*
- Engine blocks and cylinder heads (Al alloys)
- Transmission housings and gearbox covers
- Piston housings and alternator frames
- Steering column components
- Electric vehicle structural components (Tesla-style "gigacastings")

*Consumer Electronics:*
- Laptop chassis (Mg alloys)
- Smartphone frames and bodies
- Heat sinks for CPUs and GPUs
- Camera housings
- Connector housings and RF shields

#### Model Solution to (b): Die Casting Defects

| S.No. | Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Porosity (Gas holes)** | Air trapped in cavity, steam from lubricant | Improve venting, reduce injection speed, degas melt |
| 2 | **Cold Shuts** | Metal freezes before fusing due to low temperature | Increase pouring/injection temperature, faster injection |
| 3 | **Flow Lines** | Uneven metal flow fronts meeting | Increase die temperature, optimize gate location |
| 4 | **Flash (Burrs)** | Excessive injection pressure or worn dies | Use proper clamping force, polish die surfaces |
| 5 | **Hot Cracks** | High residual stress from uneven cooling | Uniform cooling channel design, lower die temp gradient |
| 6 | **Soldering (Die Sticking)** | Metal sticks to die surface | Use proper die lubricants, die coating, lower die temp |
| 7 | **Misruns** | Insufficient metal reaches cavity | Increase shot size, increase injection pressure |
| 8 | **Blowholes** | Air entrapment in shot sleeve | Use proper plunger design, evacuate air |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Die Casting Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor (SF)** in clamping tonnage — examiners specifically test this. Always multiply by SF = 1.15 to 1.5.
> 2. **Unit errors in numerical problems** — Mixing mm² with m², or MPa with Pa. **Always standardize units first** (N/mm² and mm² are easiest).
> 3. **Confusing hot and cold chamber limits** — Writing "Aluminum is used in hot-chamber" loses 2-3 marks instantly. Memorize: **Zn → Hot, Al → Cold**.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation steps** — A 7-mark derivation question is graded on the **intermediate steps**, not just the final formula. Show every algebraic transition.
> 5. **No sketch in descriptive questions** — A neat labeled diagram is worth **1.5 to 2 marks** even if your text is brief. Always include one.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert kN to tonnes** at the end — Final answer must be in **tonnes-force**, not kN.
> 7. **Using "Aluminum die castings" without specifying cold-chamber** — Be specific in applications.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Die casting = **pressure-assisted permanent mold casting** using a steel die.
- **Two main types:** **Hot-chamber** (Zn, Pb, Sn — low Tm) and **Cold-chamber** (Al, Cu, Mg — high Tm).
- **Hot-chamber:** Gooseneck submerged in metal; 7–35 MPa injection; fast cycles.
- **Cold-chamber:** Ladle pours into horizontal sleeve; 30–175 MPa injection; slower but handles high Tm alloys.
- **Pressure Intensification Ratio (PIR)** = $A_{\text{ram}} / A_{\text{plunger}}$ — the **multiplication factor** for hydraulic pressure.
- **Metal Pressure:** $P_{\text{metal}} = P_{\text{hyd}} \times PIR$.
- **Mould-opening force:** $F_{\text{open}} = P_{\text{metal}} \times A_{\text{projected}}$.
- **Clamping tonnage:** $T_{\text{clamp}} = (F_{\text{open}} \times SF) / 9810$ — answer in **tonnes**.
- **Chvorinov's Rule** applies to solidification time: $t_{s} = B (V/A)^2$.
- **Cycle time** = closing + injection + cooling + ejection.
- **Production rate** = (3600 / cycle time in seconds) × efficiency $\eta$.
- **Common defects:** Porosity, cold shuts, flash, soldering, hot cracks, misruns, blowholes.
- **Modern milestone:** Tesla Giga Press uses **6000–9000 tonne** cold-chamber aluminum die casting for entire car underbodies.
- **Always include a labeled sketch** in descriptive questions.
- **Always apply safety factor** in numerical problems.
- **Use mm² and N/mm²** to avoid unit conversion errors.
- **Memorize alloy-process mapping** (Zn→Hot, Al→Cold) for instant marks.
- **Distinguish between die, mould, and pattern** clearly in definitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centrifugal Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Centrifugal Casting** is a permanent mould casting process in which the molten metal is poured into a rotating mould (die) and the centrifugal force generated by the rotation distributes the metal along the inner surface of the mould cavity, thereby producing a casting of definite shape. The process exploits the principle of **centrifugal force** — a pseudo-force experienced by a body moving in a circular path — to shape, feed, and densify the molten metal without the use of external cores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Centrifugal casting is classified under *permanent mould casting processes* in the KTU 2024 Manufacturing Processes syllabus. Students must clearly distinguish it from die casting, investment casting, and shell moulding.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine spinning a bucket half-filled with water in a vertical circle. The water does not fall out, even when the bucket is upside down. Instead, it is pressed firmly against the inner wall of the bucket by a force that we *call* the centrifugal force. **Centrifugal casting works on exactly the same idea**, except we replace the bucket with a hollow cylindrical steel mould, and the water with molten metal.

- The **rotating mould** is the bucket.
- The **molten metal** is the water.
- The **centrifugal force** is what we are harnessing to throw the metal against the mould wall.
- Once the metal hits the wall, it begins to **solidify from the outside inward** because the wall is cooler, giving a clean, dense, fine-grained outer surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike gravity casting, the metal is not "poured" to fill a static cavity. Instead, the cavity is *created by rotation* itself. The mould's rotation determines the casting's shape.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Gravitational acceleration, g = 9.81 m/s² (or 32.2 ft/s²)**
- **Conversion constant, g_c = 1 kg·m/(N·s²) or 32.2 lbm·ft/(lbf·s²)**
- **Recommended centrifugal force factor, C = 60 to 80** for ferrous metals, **C = 30 to 90** for non-ferrous metals (unitless multiplier on gravity).
- **Mould speed, N**, is expressed in **revolutions per minute (rpm)**.
- **Metals commonly cast:** cast iron, steel, copper alloys (bronze, brass), aluminium alloys, and magnesium alloys.

### 1.4 Classification of Centrifugal Casting

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorises centrifugal casting into **three distinct sub-processes**:

| Sub-Process | Mould Axis | Core Required? | Typical Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| **True Centrifugal Casting** | Horizontal (or vertical) | No external core | Pipes, tubes, cylinders, bushings |
| **Semi-Centrifugal Casting** | Vertical | Yes (central core) | Wheels, gear blanks, pulleys |
| **Centrifuging (Pressure Casting)** | Vertical | Gating only | Small intricate parts, complex shapes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics Behind Centrifugal Casting — The 'Why' and 'How'

The process can be broken down into **five sequential logic steps**:

1. **Step 1 — Mould Acceleration:** The mould is mounted on a spindle and rotated by an electric motor through a pulley/gear drive. The mould is accelerated up to a pre-calculated **critical mould speed N**.

2. **Step 2 — Metal Pouring:** Molten metal is poured into the mould either through a pouring ladle (axis vertical) or via a long spout/feeder trough (axis horizontal). The moment the metal contacts the rotating surface, it is immediately thrown outward.

3. **Step 3 — Centrifugal Force Action:** The centripetal acceleration $a = \omega^2 r$ acting inward is balanced by the centrifugal force pushing the metal outward. The metal is pressed uniformly against the inner mould wall.

4. **Step 4 — Solidification:** Heat is extracted rapidly through the metal mould wall. Solidification starts at the **outer periphery** and progresses inward. The last-to-solidify region is the **inner bore (free surface)** — this is where shrinkage porosity tends to concentrate, so an **effective pouring rate** is maintained to feed shrinkage.

5. **Step 5 — Ejection:** After complete solidification, the mould is stopped, opened, and the casting is withdrawn. In **horizontal axis**, the casting slides out; in **vertical axis**, it is lifted out.

### 2.2 Why Mould Speed is the Most Critical Parameter

- **Too low a speed:** Metal will not be thrown to the wall — the casting will be mis-shapen with rain drops / splashes leading to cold-shuts and slag inclusions.
- **Too high a speed:** Excessive metal wash, vibration, segregation of alloying elements, and cracking due to high residual stress.
- **Optimum speed:** Determined empirically using the **Chworinoff rule**, which links speed to a dimensionless constant *C*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** The "**C**" in Chworinoff's formula is **NOT a constant of nature** — it is an **empirical factor** that depends on the metal's density, surface tension, and pouring temperature. Always quote its range, never a single value.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Validity / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal force per unit mass | $F = \omega^2 r = \dfrac{v^2}{r}$ | $\omega$ in rad/s, $r$ in m, $v$ in m/s | Universal |
| 2 | Centrifugal pressure head | $h_c = \dfrac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g}$ | $h_c$ in m of metal | Universal |
| 3 | Chworinoff's empirical rule | $\dfrac{v^2}{g r} = C$ | $C$ = dimensionless factor, $v$ = tangential velocity (m/s) | $C = 60$–$80$ (ferrous); $30$–$90$ (non-ferrous) |
| 4 | Mould speed (Chworinoff-derived) | $N = \dfrac{42.3\sqrt{C}}{\sqrt{r}}$ | $N$ in rpm, $r$ in metres | True centrifugal casting |
| 5 | Mould speed (US Customary form) | $N = \dfrac{0.59}{\sqrt{r}}\sqrt{C}$ | $r$ in feet, $N$ in rpm | US units |
| 6 | Linear / tangential velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \omega r$ | $D = 2r$ in m, $N$ in rpm | Universal |
| 7 | Centrifugal force on a particle | $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ | $m$ = mass (kg), $F_c$ in N | Universal |
| 8 | G-factor (effective gravity ratio) | $G = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$ | Dimensionless | True centrifugal casting |
| 9 | Specific speed (groth) | $N_s = N \sqrt{V}$ | $V$ = volume of metal | Empirical design check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical-axis True Centrifugal Casting:** A modified Chworinoff formula applies because gravity assists or opposes the centrifugal field depending on the mould position: $v^2 = (g \pm \omega^2 r) r$. Use **(+)** for top pouring (mould axis vertical, pour from top) and **(−)** for bottom pouring.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipe industry:** Cast iron, ductile iron, and steel pipes up to 6 m length and 500 mm diameter are produced by horizontal-axis centrifugal casting (e.g., **spun pipes** for water mains).
- **Bushing / sleeve manufacturing:** Bronze and brass bushings for bearings are produced in mass volumes.
- **Railway wheels:** Semi-centrifugal casting is used to feed large wheel castings where a central core is required.
- **Bi-metallic components:** Centrifuging allows embedding a second alloy layer (e.g., steel-bronze composite liners).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Chworinoff's Rule for Mould Speed

**Given:** A horizontal-axis cylindrical mould of inner radius $r$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ contains molten metal thrown to the wall.

**Objective:** Derive the optimum mould speed $N$ in rpm.

**Step 1 — Condition for shape retention.**
A metal particle of mass $m$ on the inner wall experiences:
- Centrifugal force (outward): $F_c = m \omega^2 r$
- Gravitational force (downward): $F_g = mg$

For the casting to retain its cylindrical shape without rain/splashing, the centrifugal force must dominate gravity by an empirical factor $C$:

$$
\frac{F_c}{F_g} = C \quad \text{(Chworinoff constant)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the forces.**

$$
\frac{m \omega^2 r}{m g} = C
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $m$ and simplify.**

$$
\frac{\omega^2 r}{g} = C
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce tangential velocity** $v = \omega r$, so $\omega = v / r$.

$$
\frac{(v/r)^2 \, r}{g} = C \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{v^2}{g r} = C
$$

This is **Chworinoff's rule**.

**Step 5 — Solve for tangential velocity $v$.**

$$
v^2 = C g r \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; v = \sqrt{C g r}
$$

**Step 6 — Relate $v$ to rotational speed $N$ (rpm).**
The tangential velocity is $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ with $D = 2r$:

$$
v = \frac{\pi \, (2r) \, N}{60} = \frac{\pi r N}{30}
$$

**Step 7 — Equate and solve for $N$.**

$$
\frac{\pi r N}{30} = \sqrt{C g r}
$$

$$
N = \frac{30 \sqrt{C g r}}{\pi r} = \frac{30}{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{C g}{r}}
$$

**Step 8 — Plug in numerical values** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\pi = 3.1416$:

$$
N = \frac{30}{3.1416} \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \, C}{r}} = 9.5493 \, \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \, C}{r}}
$$

$$
N = 9.5493 \times 3.1310 \times \sqrt{\frac{C}{r}} = 29.90 \sqrt{\frac{C}{r}}
$$

Using the more precise constant $g = 9.806$ and $\pi$ value, the **standard textbook form** is:

$$
\boxed{\,N \;=\; 42.3 \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{r}}\,}
$$

where $r$ is in **metres** and $N$ is in **rpm**.

**Step 9 — Validate the constant.**
Insert $g = 9.80665$, $\pi = 3.14159$:

$$
N = \frac{30}{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{9.80665 \, C}{r}} = 9.5493 \times 3.1310 \sqrt{\frac{C}{r}} = 29.901 \sqrt{\frac{C}{r}}
$$

The factor $42.3$ appears when $r$ is in **inches** (US units): $N = 0.59 / \sqrt{r} \times \sqrt{C}$ with $r$ in feet; the conversion $0.59 \times 71.66 \approx 42.3$ confirms the relationship.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (Model Question):** A horizontal-axis centrifugal casting mould has an inner diameter of **300 mm**. Calculate the **optimum mould speed** to cast a **copper alloy** pipe. Use $C = 60$.

**Step 1 — Convert units.**
Inner radius $r = 300/2 = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m.

**Step 2 — Apply the formula.**

$$
N = 42.3 \sqrt{\frac{C}{r}} = 42.3 \sqrt{\frac{60}{0.150}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inside of the square root.**

$$
\frac{60}{0.150} = 400
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root.**

$$
\sqrt{400} = 20
$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication.**

$$
N = 42.3 \times 20 = 846 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Result:** $N \approx 846$ rpm.

**Verification by tangential velocity method:**

$$
v = \sqrt{C g r} = \sqrt{60 \times 9.81 \times 0.150} = \sqrt{88.29} = 9.396 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
N = \frac{60 v}{\pi D} = \frac{60 \times 9.396}{\pi \times 0.300} = \frac{563.76}{0.9425} = 598.3 \text{ rpm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Pitfall (Valuation):** The discrepancy ($846$ vs $598$ rpm) is because the $42.3$ constant assumes $r$ in **inches**, not metres. KTU examiners expect students to use the **exact derivation** rather than blindly plug into a formula card. The correct SI form is $N = 29.9 \sqrt{C/r}$ which gives $29.9 \times 20 = 598$ rpm. Always carry units through the derivation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Three Sub-Processes Compared

| Feature | True Centrifugal | Semi-Crifugal | Centrifuging |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mould axis** | Horizontal (commonly) | Vertical | Vertical |
| **Core** | None | Yes (central) | Gating only |
| **Centrifugal force role** | Forms the bore | Feeds solidification | Distributes metal in cavities |
| **Shape range** | Hollow cylinders only | Axisymmetric with hub | Most complex shapes |
| **Production rate** | High (continuous) | Medium | High (batch) |
| **Tolerance on bore** | Fair (some machining) | Good | Excellent (gravity-fed where possible) |
| **Examples** | Pipes, tubes | Wheels, pulleys | Impellers, gear blanks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Horizontal-Axis True Centrifugal Casting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drive Motor] --> B[Variable Speed Pulley]
    B --> C[Mandrel Spindle Bearings]
    C --> D[Rotating Mould]
    D --> E[Pouring Ladle or Spout]
    E --> D
    D --> F[Solidification Zone]
    F --> G[Cooling Water Spray]
    G --> D
    D --> H[Extraction Station]
    I[Control Panel RPM] --> B
    J[Cooling Tower] --> G
    H --> K[Finished Cast Pipe]
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style D fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#bf8f00,color:#fff
    style F fill:#548235,color:#fff
    style G fill:#00b0f0,color:#fff
    style H fill:#7030a0,color:#fff
    style K fill:#00b050,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Centrifugal Casting Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Pre-heat mould] --> P2[Accelerate mould to N rpm]
    P2 --> P3[Pour molten metal via spout]
    P3 --> P4[Centrifugal force throws metal to wall]
    P4 --> P5[Solidification outside-in]
    P5 --> P6[Maintain pour rate to feed shrinkage]
    P6 --> P7[Reduce speed after solidification]
    P7 --> P8[Extract casting]
    P8 --> P9[Trimming and inspection]
    P9 --> P1
    style P1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#bf8f00,color:#fff
    style P4 fill:#bf8f00,color:#fff
    style P5 fill:#548235,color:#fff
    style P6 fill:#bf8f00,color:#fff
    style P7 fill:#7030a0,color:#fff
    style P8 fill:#00b0f0,color:#fff
    style P9 fill:#00b050,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Functional Comparison Diagram — Sub-Process Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the casting axisymmetric with a hole?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Can it self-form the bore?}
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3[Use SEMI-CENTRIFUGAL with central core]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q4[Use TRUE CENTRIFUGAL Casting]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q5[Use TRUE CENTRIFUGAL with top/bottom gating]
    Q3 --> R[Vertical axis mould]
    Q4 --> S[Horizontal axis mould preferred]
    Q5 --> T[Vertical axis mould]
    style Q1 fill:#7030a0,color:#fff
    style Q2 fill:#7030a0,color:#fff
    style Q3 fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style Q4 fill:#548235,color:#fff
    style Q5 fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Force Diagram — Centrifugal Force on a Metal Particle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Metal particle mass m] -->|Weight mg downward| B[Mould inner wall]
    A -->|Centrifugal F=omega squared r outward| B
    B -->|Normal reaction N inward| A
    style A fill:#bf8f00,color:#fff
    style B fill:#c00000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** The wall pushes *inward* on the particle (centripetal) while the pseudo-force of rotation pushes the particle *outward*. In the rotating reference frame, these are perfectly balanced for a particle moving with the wall.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]** Define centrifugal casting. Name its three variants.

**Model Answer:**
Centrifugal casting is a permanent mould casting process in which molten metal is poured into a rotating mould and the centrifugal force generated distributes the metal along the mould's inner surface to form a casting. The three variants are:
1. **True centrifugal casting** — produces hollow cylindrical parts without using a core.
2. **Semi-centrifugal casting** — uses a central core; employed for parts like wheels.
3. **Centrifuging** — centrifugal force aids in filling complex mould cavities, used for irregular shapes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]** What is the role of the factor *C* in Chworinoff's rule? What are its typical values?

**Model Answer:**
The factor *C* in Chworinoff's rule is a dimensionless empirical constant that represents the ratio of centrifugal force to gravitational force acting on the molten metal. It ensures that the metal is held firmly against the mould wall during solidification, preventing rain/splashing. Typical values:
- **For ferrous metals: C = 60 to 80**
- **For non-ferrous metals: C = 30 to 90**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of **true centrifugal casting** for the production of cast iron pipes. List its **advantages and limitations**.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
- A horizontal cylindrical steel mould is mounted on rollers and rotated by an electric motor.
- Molten cast iron is poured through a long spout (or ladle) running along the axis of the mould.
- The centrifugal force throws the metal against the inner cylindrical surface where it solidifies.
- The metal is fed continuously to compensate for shrinkage, and the inner bore forms by itself (no core is required).
- Water spray cooling accelerates solidification; the mould is decelerated and the pipe is withdrawn.

**Diagram Description (1 mark):** Mould on rollers, motor, spout, water spray ring, extraction end, finished pipe output.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- No core or sand mould required for the bore — saves material and time.
- Dense, fine-grained casting with superior mechanical properties.
- High production rate suitable for pipe manufacture.
- Low material wastage; risers not required since shrinkage is fed by the rotating pool.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Limited to axisymmetric (rotationally symmetric) shapes.
- High initial equipment cost.
- Difficult to cast alloys with a wide freezing range — segregation can occur.
- Skilled operators needed for speed control.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive **Chworinoff's formula** for calculating the optimum mould speed in centrifugal casting. State the assumptions made.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The mould axis is horizontal.
2. The molten metal is a uniform incompressible fluid.
3. The metal temperature is constant during rotation.
4. The factor *C* adequately captures the ratio of centrifugal to gravitational force needed for shape retention.
5. Steady-state rotation; vibration effects neglected.

**Derivation (6 marks):**
Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the exhaustive step-by-step derivation. Key result:

$$
\boxed{\;N \;=\; 42.3 \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{r}}\;}
$$

where $N$ is in rpm and $r$ is in **inches**. (The corresponding SI form is $N = 29.9 \sqrt{C/r}$ with $r$ in metres.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **true centrifugal casting**, **semi-centrifugal casting**, and **centrifuging** with respect to mould axis, core usage, products, and centrifugal force role.

**Model Solution:** Refer to the **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.3** of these notes for a complete comparison. Summary (3 marks for matrix):

| Aspect | True | Semi | Centrifuging |
|---|---|---|---|
| Axis | Horizontal | Vertical | Vertical |
| Core | None | Yes | No |
| Products | Pipes, tubes | Wheels, pulleys | Complex parts |
| Force role | Forms bore | Feeds shrinkage | Distributes metal |

**Explanations (4 marks):**
- **True centrifugal casting:** Centrifugal force is the primary shaping force; the bore is formed by gravity and the inner free surface. Used for hollow cylinders.
- **Semi-centrifugal casting:** A central core is placed; centrifugal force feeds the casting from the periphery toward the centre as solidification proceeds. Useful for axisymmetric parts with hubs.
- **Centrifuging:** Mould is rotated about a vertical axis; centrifugal force pushes metal into the cavity moulds arranged around the periphery. After solidification, the gating system is cut off and individual parts are recovered.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal-axis centrifugal casting mould has an internal diameter of **250 mm**. The metal is **aluminium alloy** (use $C = 50$). Calculate: **(i)** the optimum mould speed, and **(ii)** the centrifugal force per kg of metal at the mould wall.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Optimum mould speed (4 marks):**

Step 1 — Convert radius: $r = 250/2 = 125$ mm $= 0.125$ m.

Step 2 — Apply SI formula:

$$
N = 29.9 \sqrt{\frac{C}{r}} = 29.9 \sqrt{\frac{50}{0.125}} = 29.9 \sqrt{400} = 29.9 \times 20 = 598 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Result: N ≈ 598 rpm**

Step 3 — Cross-check using tangential velocity:

$$
v = \sqrt{C g r} = \sqrt{50 \times 9.81 \times 0.125} = \sqrt{61.31} = 7.83 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
N = \frac{60 v}{\pi D} = \frac{60 \times 7.83}{\pi \times 0.250} = \frac{469.8}{0.7854} = 598.2 \text{ rpm} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**(ii) Centrifugal force per kg (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Angular velocity: $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 598}{60} = 62.62$ rad/s.

Step 2 — Force per unit mass: $F/m = \omega^2 r = (62.62)^2 \times 0.125 = 3921.3 \times 0.125 = 490.2$ N/kg.

Step 3 — Express as G-factor: $G = \omega^2 r / g = 490.2 / 9.81 = 49.97 \approx 50$.

**Result: F ≈ 490.2 N/kg (≈ 50 g)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Using the formula $N = 42.3 \sqrt{C/r}$ with $r$ in metres will give a wrong answer (1069 rpm instead of 598 rpm). Always confirm the unit of $r$ against the formula's derivation.
> 2. **C-factor confusion:** Do NOT use $C = 70$ (typical ferrous value) for aluminium — use the lower range 30–50.
> 3. **G-factor vs absolute force:** Examiners may ask for either. Be ready to compute both.
> 4. **Vertical-axis caveat:** The Chworinoff derivation here is for *horizontal* axis. For *vertical* axis, gravity adds/subtracts from the centripetal field — a different formula applies.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Centrifugal casting = rotating permanent mould + centrifugal force = shaped casting without sand core (true type).**
- **Three variants:** True, Semi, and Centrifuging — distinguish by axis, core use, and product type.
- **Key formula:** $N = 29.9 \sqrt{C/r}$ (SI, $r$ in m) or $N = 42.3 \sqrt{C/r}$ (US, $r$ in inches).
- **Chworinoff's rule:** $v^2 = C g r$, where $C = 60$–$80$ (ferrous) and $C = 30$–$90$ (non-ferrous).
- **Centrifugal force:** $F = m \omega^2 r$, pointing radially outward.
- **G-factor:** $\omega^2 r / g$ — measures effective gravity experienced by the melt.
- **Solidification direction:** Outside-inward; last-to-freeze region is the inner bore → feed shrinkage via continuous pour.
- **Process advantages:** Dense casting, no core, less wastage, high production rate for pipes/sleeves.
- **Process limitations:** Axisymmetric shapes only (true type); high capital cost; segregation risk in wide-freezing-range alloys.
- **Critical design parameter:** Mould speed $N$ — too low → rain defects; too high → vibration, segregation.
- **Real-world products:** Cast iron spun pipes, bronze bushings, aluminium cylinder liners, railway wheel blanks.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always write down the **assumptions** of Chworinoff's rule and the **units** of every variable before substituting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Casting and Squeeze Casting. Powder Metallurgy - Powder Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Continuous Casting and Squeeze Casting. Powder Metallurgy - Powder Production ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Continuous Casting (CC) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** Continuous Casting is an advanced **primary metal forming process** in which molten metal is continuously poured into a water-cooled, open-ended mold, partially solidified into a shell, and withdrawn at a controlled rate to form a semi-finished product of constant cross-section (slab, bloom, billet, or strip) for subsequent secondary processing (rolling, forging, or extrusion). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Continuous casting is classified as a *primary forming process* and represents the dominant modern method for producing **~95% of the world's steel** in liquid steel form. It bridges *extractive metallurgy* and *mechanical working*. **Conceptual Analogy — "The Infinite Sausage Maker"** Imagine a machine that works like a sausage stuffer. Liquid batter (molten metal) is pushed into a cold tube (water-cooled copper mold). A thin skin forms on the inside wall (initial solidification shell). The sausage is continuously pulled out the other end while more batter flows in from the top. By the time the "sausage" exits the machine, it is solid enough to slice — except here, it becomes a steel slab that is cut into measured lengths by an **oxy-gas torch** traveling in sync with the strand. ### 1.2 Squeeze Casting **Formal Definition:** Squeeze Casting (also called *Liquid Metal Forging* or *Pressure Crystallization*) is a **hybrid casting-forging process** in which molten metal is poured into a re-usable die and **solidified under sustained high pressure (typically 50 to 150 MPa)** applied through a direct punch. The applied pressure remains in effect throughout solidification, eliminating shrinkage porosity and producing components with mechanical properties approaching those of forged parts. > [!NOTE] > **Principle Identifier:** Squeeze casting is a *near-net-shape* process that uses the combination of (a) gravity or low-pressure die filling and (b) **pressurized solidification** to obtain a part with wrought-quality mechanical properties but cast-quality dimensional accuracy. **Conceptual Analogy — "The Waffle Iron with a Press"** Think of a waffle iron: liquid batter is poured, the lid closes, and heat cooks it. Now imagine the lid also pushes down with tremendous force while the batter solidifies. The waffle comes out dense, with no bubbles, and with crispy edges — much stronger than one made by simply pouring batter and waiting. Squeeze casting works on the same principle, except pressures reach **hundreds of atmospheres** rather than the gentle closure of a waffle iron. ### 1.3 Powder Metallurgy (PM) — Powder Production Stage **Formal Definition:** Powder Metallurgy is the science and technology of producing metal powders and using them to manufacture finished or semi-finished engineering components. The **first stage** of any PM process is the production of metal powders, which are subsequently compacted in a die and sintered to achieve metallurgical bonding. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Focus:** The 2024 Scheme places emphasis on the *characterization* and *production methods* of metal powders, which is the most important first step in PM.
