# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of the Arc — Why It Melts Metal

When a potential difference (typically **18–40 V** open-circuit) is applied and the electrode is brought near the workpiece:

1. **Field emission** of electrons from the cathode (cold or thermionic).
2. Electrons accelerate across the **arc gap** and collide with gas molecules → **ionization**.
3. Positive ions strike the cathode, releasing more electrons and heating it (**cathode heating**).
4. The resistive heating of the ionized gas column (plasma) sustains temperatures of **6000–20 000 K**.
5. The anode is heated by electron bombardment (**anode heating** — typically hotter than cathode).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage (V)** governs the **arc length** and **heat spread** (longer arc = wider, cooler pool).
> **Current (I)** governs the **heat input** and **deposition rate** (more I = more metal melted per second).

---

## 2.2 Process Parameters in Arc Welding

The four **master parameters** that control weld quality, penetration, and heat input are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arc Voltage** | V | 18–32 V | Pool width, bead profile |
| **Welding Current** | I | 60–500 A | Penetration, deposition |
| **Travel Speed** | S | 2–20 mm/s | Bead size, heat input |
| **Electrode Diameter** | d | 1.6–6.4 mm | Current capacity |

### Supporting Parameters
- **Electrode angle / work angle** (push vs drag — 0° to 45°)
- **Arc length** (short, normal, long)
- **Polarity** (DCEP, DCEN, AC)
- **Pre-heat / interpass temperature**
- **Joint geometry** (V, U, J, square groove; root gap; root face)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Arc Welding Numericals)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **the most frequently tested** in KTU ESE numericals. Memorize these and their units.

### 2.3.1 Heat Input (HI)

The **Heat Input** is the energy delivered by the arc per unit length of weld. It directly controls penetration, HAZ width, and distortion.

$$\text{Heat Input (HI)} = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ / mm]}$$

where:
- $V$ = arc voltage in **volts (V)**
- $I$ = welding current in **amperes (A)**
- $S$ = travel speed in **mm/min**
- 60 converts seconds → minutes; 1000 converts J → kJ

**Equivalent form** if travel speed is in mm/s:
$$\text{HI} = \frac{V \times I}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm, with } S \text{ in mm/s]}$$

**Thermal Efficiency Factor (η):** Not all arc heat goes into the workpiece. A **heat transfer efficiency** is applied:
$$\text{Actual HI delivered to plate} = \eta \times \text{HI}$$

Typical values of $\eta$:
- SMAW: $\eta \approx 0.75$ to $0.85$
- GMAW: $\eta \approx 0.70$ to $0.85$
- GTAW: $\eta \approx 0.40$ to $0.60$
- SAW: $\eta \approx 0.90$ to $0.99$
- PAW: $\eta \approx 0.45$ to $0.55$

### 2.3.2 Power Consumed

$$P = V \times I \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

### 2.3.3 Deposition Rate (Mass of Filler Deposited per Minute)

$$R_d = \frac{I \times \text{mass factor}}{1000} \quad \text{[kg/min]}$$

For SMAW, an empirical **mass deposition rate** per ampere can be used:
$$R_d \approx k \times I \quad \text{[g/min]}$$

where $k$ (the **melting rate constant**) depends on electrode type — typically **0.06–0.10 g/A·min** for rutile-coated mild steel electrodes.

### 2.3.4 Melting Rate of Electrode

$$M_r = \frac{I \times \rho_{\text{wire}} \times A_{\text{wire}} \times 60}{1000} \quad \text{[kg/h]}$$

where $A_{\text{wire}} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$, $\rho$ = density of electrode material.

### 2.3.5 Energy per Unit Length (alternative form)

$$E_L = \frac{\eta \times V \times I}{S} \quad \text{[J/mm]}$$

### 2.3.6 Oxyfuel Welding — Specific Fuel Consumption

For **neutral flame**, volume of O₂ required per volume of C₂H₂:
$$\frac{V_{O_2}}{V_{C_2H_2}} = 2.5 \quad \text{(stoichiometric)}$$

Actual practice uses **slightly more O₂** for safety (≈ 2.6 : 1).

---

## 2.4 Master Equation Summary Table

| Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Input | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{V I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Most-tested numerical |
| Actual heat into plate | $\text{HI}_{\text{actual}} = \eta \times \text{HI}$ | kJ/mm | With efficiency |
| Arc power | $P = V I$ | W | Power source rating |
| Energy per unit length | $E_L = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S}$ | J/mm | If $S$ in mm/s |
| Deposition rate | $R_d = k I$ | g/min | Filler weight per minute |
| Stoichiometric O₂/C₂H₂ | ratio = 2.5 | — | Neutral flame reference |
| Electrode area | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | mm² | Current capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions / Validity:**
> - HI formula is valid when **arc is stable** and travel speed is **constant** (typical assumption in KTU problems).
> - $\eta$ is empirical — varies with process, current, and travel speed. Use the value **given in the question**; do not invent one.
> - HI > 4 kJ/mm is generally considered **excessive** (risk of HAZ softening in steels).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Where It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Heat Input control** | PWHT requirement (ASME Sec IX), HAZ toughness in low-alloy steels |
| **η values** | Cost estimation of power bills; choosing energy-efficient processes |
| **Deposition rate** | Productivity planning; weld pass count; man-hour calculation |
| **Travel speed** | Distortion control in thin-plate fabrication (e.g., ship hull panels) |
| **O₂/C₂H₂ ratio** | Field quality control in OAW — flame adjustment by ear/hiss |
| **SMAW flux chemistry** | AWS electrode selection for sour-service, low-temperature, creep-resistant steels |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heat Input Formula

The objective: Express the energy deposited per unit length of weld in standard KTU-preferred units.

**Starting Point — Arc Power:**

The arc is essentially an electrical load. The **electrical power** consumed by the arc is given by the product of arc voltage and current.

$$P = V \times I \quad \text{[Watts]} = \text{[Joules / second]}$$

**Step 1 — Energy delivered per second of arc time.**

Every second the arc burns, the source supplies $V \times I$ joules of energy.

$$E_{1s} = V \times I \quad \text{[J / s]}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to energy per minute (since travel speed is often given in mm/min).**

Multiply both sides by **60 seconds** to obtain the energy delivered in one minute of arc time.

$$E_{1\min} = V \times I \times 60 \quad \text{[J / min]}$$

**Step 3 — Express energy per unit length of weld.**

If the travel speed is $S$ mm/min, then in one minute the arc travels $S$ mm of weld length. The energy deposited over that length is therefore:

$$E_L = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S} \quad \text{[J / mm]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kilojoules per millimetre (kJ/mm).**

Divide by 1000 to switch from joules to kilojoules.

$$\boxed{\text{HI} = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ / mm]}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the thermal efficiency $\eta$** (when required by the problem).

Not all of the arc's electrical energy enters the workpiece — some is lost as convection, radiation, and spatter. Multiply by the efficiency factor $\eta$ to obtain the energy actually delivered to the plate.

$$\text{HI}_{\text{actual}} = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Mass Deposition Rate (For Consumable Arc Welding)

**Given:**
- Electrode diameter $d$ in mm
- Density of steel electrode $\rho \approx 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Wire feed speed $F$ in m/min

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of electrode.**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \quad \text{[mm}^2\text{]}$$

**Step 2 — Volume deposited per minute** (length $F$ metres per minute, area $A$).

$$V = A \times F \times 10^{-6} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{ / min]}$$

(We multiply by $10^{-6}$ to convert mm²·m → m³.)

**Step 3 — Mass per minute** (density × volume).

$$R_d = \rho \times A \times F \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 \quad \text{[kg / min]}$$

(Since $\rho$ is in kg/m³, multiply by 1000 if we want g/min.)

**Simplified KTU form for SMAW** (using the empirical constant $k$ in g/A·min):

$$R_d = k \times I \quad \text{[g / min]}$$

A typical value: $k = 0.08$ g/A·min for a 4 mm mild-steel rutile electrode.

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Problems (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> The following four problems are representative of the **numerical questions** asked in KTU ESE for this module. Each is solved step-by-step with units.

---

### **Numerical 1 — Heat Input Calculation (Basic)**

> **Problem:** In a SMAW operation, the arc voltage is **25 V** and welding current is **200 A**. The travel speed is **5 mm/s**. Calculate the heat input in **kJ/mm**. Take $\eta = 0.80$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify units.** Travel speed is in **mm/s**, so we will use the alternative form of the heat-input formula.

$$\text{HI} = \frac{V \times I}{S \times 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$\text{HI} = \frac{25 \times 200}{5 \times 1000} = \frac{5000}{5000} = 1.0 \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency.**

$$\text{HI}_{\text{actual}} = 0.80 \times 1.0 = 0.80 \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{HI} = 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm}; \quad \text{HI}_{\text{actual}} = 0.80 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

---

### **Numerical 2 — Travel Speed from Heat Input**

> **Problem:** A fabricator wants to limit the heat input to **1.5 kJ/mm** in a GMAW process. Voltage is **28 V**, current is **240 A**, and thermal efficiency is **0.85**. Calculate the **maximum permissible travel speed** in mm/min.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the heat input formula and solve for $S$.**

$$\text{HI}_{\text{actual}} = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$$

$$1.5 = 0.85 \times \frac{28 \times 240 \times 60}{S \times 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator.**

$$0.85 \times \frac{28 \times 240 \times 60}{1000} = 0.85 \times \frac{403200}{1000} = 0.85 \times 403.2 = 342.72$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $S$.**

$$1.5 = \frac{342.72}{S} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S = \frac{342.72}{1.5} = 228.48 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 228.5 \text{ mm/min} \;(\approx 3.81 \text{ mm/s})}$$

---

### **Numerical 3 — Welding Current from Heat Input (Reverse Problem)**

> **Problem:** The required heat input for a SMAW joint is **2.0 kJ/mm**. Voltage is **22 V**, travel speed is **6 mm/s**, and $\eta = 0.75$. Find the **welding current**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Heat input with $S$ in mm/s.**

$$\text{HI}_{\text{actual}} = \eta \times \frac{V \times I}{S \times 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and solve for $I$.**

$$2.0 = 0.75 \times \frac{22 \times I}{6 \times 1000}$$

$$2.0 = \frac{0.75 \times 22 \times I}{6000}$$

$$2.0 = \frac{16.5 \times I}{6000}$$

$$I = \frac{2.0 \times 6000}{16.5} = \frac{12000}{16.5} \approx 727.27 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check.** A current of ~727 A is **very high** for SMAW (typical 100–300 A). This suggests an **inconsistent problem** — in KTU exams, when this happens, **state the assumption** and proceed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner pitfall:** Always **check the physical plausibility** of your answer. Welding current > 500 A is uncommon for SMAW; > 1000 A is for **submerged arc welding (SAW)** or **electroslag welding (ESW)**. If the answer is unrealistic, the data in the question may be inconsistent — note this in your solution.

---

### **Numerical 4 — Deposition Rate and Time to Deposit a Fillet Weld**

> **Problem:** A 4 mm diameter mild-steel electrode (density 7.85 g/cm³) is used in SMAW with a current of **180 A** and arc-on time factor (duty cycle) of **0.40**. The empirical deposition constant is **$k = 0.08$ g/A·min**. A single-pass fillet weld of length **2.5 m** requires a deposited weight of **300 g**. Calculate:
> (a) The deposition rate in g/min.
> (b) The total arc time required.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Deposition Rate:**

$$R_d = k \times I = 0.08 \times 180 = 14.4 \quad \text{g/min (gross)}$$

Apply duty cycle:
$$R_{d,\text{effective}} = 0.40 \times 14.4 = 5.76 \quad \text{g/min}$$

**Part (b) — Arc time required:**

$$t = \frac{W}{R_{d,\text{effective}}} = \frac{300 \text{ g}}{5.76 \text{ g/min}} \approx 52.08 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{R_{d,\text{effective}} = 5.76 \text{ g/min}; \quad t \approx 52 \text{ min}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — OAW Flame Selection

| Base Metal | Recommended Flame | Reason | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild steel (low-C) | **Neutral** | No chemical attack; clean weld | General fabrication |
| High-carbon steel | **Carburizing (slight)** | Adds C to compensate for decarburization | Tool steel, springs |
| Cast iron | **Carburizing (slight)** | Reduces oxidation of C; reduces brittleness | Repair of castings |
| Copper | **Neutral** (with flux) | Cu has high thermal conductivity → preheat | Copper pipe, bus bars |
| Brass | **Oxidizing (slight)** | Oxide film prevents Zn vaporization | Brass plumbing |
| Aluminum / Mg alloys | **Neutral** (special flux) | Avoids oxide formation | Sheet metal work |
| Stainless steel | **Neutral** | Prevents carbide formation | Food, chemical equipment |
| Monel / Nickel | **Slightly Carburizing** | Reduces oxide | Chemical plant |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Electrode Selection in SMAW

| AWS Class | Coating | Polarity | Position | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E6010** | High-cellulose (sodium) | DCEP | All (esp. vertical) | Pipeline, structural (deep penetration) |
| **E6011** | High-cellulose (potassium) | AC / DCEP | All | Same as E6010, AC-friendly |
| **E6013** | High-titania (rutile) | AC / DCEN | All (smooth arc) | Light fabrication, sheet metal |
| **E7018** | Low-hydrogen iron powder | DCEP | All | Pressure vessels, low-alloy, critical welds |
| **E7024** | Iron powder (high deposition) | AC / DCEP | Flat / horizontal | Heavy plate, high productivity |
| **E308L-16** | Lime-coated stainless | DCEP | All | Stainless steel 304L |
| **E9018-B3** | Low-H creep-resistant | DCEP | All | Cr-Mo steel (boiler) |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU:** The "**last digit**" in AWS classification tells the coating/current/position family. **8 = low-hydrogen, DCEP, all-position** — the most-tested in exams.

## 4.2 Oxyacetylene Flame Structure (Neutral Flame)

```mermaid
graph TD
    flameRoot[OXYACETYLENE FLAME STRUCTURE] --> innerC[INNER CONE White Bright]
    flameRoot --> feather[FEATHER ZONE Intermediate]
    flameRoot --> envelope[OUTER ENVELOPE Blue]
    flameRoot --> outerFlame[OUTER FLAME Final Combustion]

    innerC --> innerChem[Primary Combustion: C2H2 plus O2 gives 2CO plus H2]
    innerChem --> innerTemp[Temp approx 3200 deg C]

    feather --> featherChem[Excess Acetylene: C plus H decomposing]

    envelope --> envChem[Secondary Combustion: CO plus O2 and H2 plus O2]
    envChem --> envTemp[Temp approx 2500 deg C]

    outerFlame --> outChem[Complete Oxidation to CO2 and H2O]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of an SMAW Setup

```mermaid
graph LR
    PSU[POWER SOURCE Transformer or Rectifier or Inverter] --> cableA[Heavy Welding Cable]
    cableA --> holder[ELECTRODE HOLDER Stinger]
    holder --> electrode[FLUX COATED ELECTRODE Consumable]
    electrode --> arc[ELECTRIC ARC 18 to 32 V]
    arc --> workpiece[WORKPIECE Ground]

    workpiece --> cableB[Return Cable]
    cableB --> PSU

    arc --> pool[WELD POOL]
    arc --> shieldG[SHIELDING GAS From Flux Decomposition]
    arc --> slag[MOLTEN SLAG Flux Coating Melts]

    slag --> slagS[SOLID SLAG LAYER Protects Weld from Atmosphere]

    holder --> welder[HUMAN OPERATOR Manual Process]

    PSU -.AC or DC.-> PSU
    PSU -.DCEP most common.-> PSU
```

## 4.4 Process Flow Chart of an Arc Welding Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    pStart[START: Set up joint and position] --> p1[Select electrode type and size]
    p1 --> p2[Set voltage V and current I on power source]
    p2 --> p3[Strike the arc by scratching or tapping]
    p3 --> p4[Establish stable arc length 2 to 4 mm]
    p4 --> p5[Maintain travel speed S constant]
    p5 --> p6[Monitor pool and slag behaviour]
    p6 --> p7{Defects visible?}
    p7 -- Yes --> p8[Adjust parameters or grind out]
    p8 --> p5
    p7 -- No --> p9[Complete the pass]
    p9 --> p10[Remove slag with chipping hammer and wire brush]
    p10 --> p11[Inspect: visual, NDT if required]
    p11 --> pStop[END]
```


# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Modeled after KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Dec 2023 pattern**

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between fusion welding and solid-state welding. Give two examples of each.**

### Model Answer (3 marks)

| Aspect | Fusion Welding | Solid-State Welding |
|---|---|---|
| State at interface | **Molten** | **Solid** (no melting) |
| Heat required | Up to and above melting point | **Below** melting point |
| HAZ | **Present** (significant) | **Negligible / absent** |
| Mechanism | Solidification of weld pool | Plastic deformation, diffusion, recrystallization |
| Filler required | Often yes (consumable electrode) | Rarely |

**Examples:**
- **Fusion:** Arc welding (SMAW), Oxyacetylene welding, Laser welding
- **Solid-state:** Friction welding, Friction stir welding, Diffusion bonding, Cold welding

*(Allocate ~1 mark each for the difference table, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for HAZ/heat distinction — distribution depends on KTU key.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Explain the chemistry of combustion in oxyacetylene welding. How is the neutral flame achieved?**

### Model Answer (3 marks)

**Chemistry:**
- **Primary combustion (inner cone, 3200 °C):** Limited O₂ supplied
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2$$
- **Secondary combustion (outer envelope, 2500 °C):** Additional O₂ supplied
$$2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO}_2; \quad \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- **Overall stoichiometric equation:**
$$2\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 5\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Neutral flame condition:** O₂ : C₂H₂ = **2.5 : 1** by volume (exactly stoichiometric). In practice, welder adjusts the torch valves until the inner cone is sharp white with a barely visible transition zone — neither feather (carburizing) nor hiss (oxidizing).

*(1 mark for each combustion step + 1 mark for neutral ratio condition.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU ESE Format: Each 14-mark question has TWO sub-parts of 7 marks each.**

---

### **OPTION A — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** process. Discuss **any six functions** of the flux coating. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A fabricator is using SMAW to weld a **10 mm thick** mild-steel plate. The process parameters are: arc voltage = **24 V**, welding current = **220 A**, travel speed = **4 mm/s**, and thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.80$.
>   (i) Calculate the **net heat input** in kJ/mm.
>   (ii) If the weld length is **3 m**, calculate the **total energy consumed** during the weld in kJ.
>   (iii) Comment on whether this heat input is suitable for mild steel. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to A(a) — 7 marks**

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

In SMAW, an electric arc is struck between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the workpiece. The arc melts the electrode tip and the base metal, forming a **weld pool**. As the electrode is consumed, it is progressively fed by the welder. The arc heat, the molten metal transfer, and the protective functions of the decomposed flux coating all occur simultaneously.

**Sketch description (2 marks):** The setup includes the **power source**, **electrode holder (stinger)**, **electrode with flux coating**, **arc**, **weld pool**, **solidifying slag layer**, and the **workpiece** connected through the **ground cable** back to the source.

**Six Functions of Flux Coating (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Shielding gas generation** — Flux decomposes to form CO₂, H₂, and H₂O vapour that displaces atmospheric N₂ and O₂ from the arc zone.
2. **Slag formation** — A molten silicate/oxide slag floats on the weld pool, slows cooling, prevents oxide entrapment, and is chipped off after solidification.
3. **Arc stabilization** — Ionizing elements (K, Na, Ca oxides) lower the ionization potential, allowing stable arc at low OCV.
4. **Deoxidation / scavenging** — Si, Mn, Al, and Ti in the flux react with dissolved oxygen in the pool and float out as slag.
5. **Alloying addition** — Elements like Cr, Ni, Mo, V are transferred into the weld metal through the flux.
6. **Mechanical rigidity** — Provides extrusion strength for the electrode and supports the welding current uniformly.

---

#### **Solution to A(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $V = 24$ V
- $I = 220$ A
- $S = 4$ mm/s
- $\eta = 0.80$
- L = 3 m

**Part (i) — Net Heat Input (3 marks):**

Using the form with $S$ in mm/s:
$$\text{HI} = \frac{V \times I}{S \times 1000} = \frac{24 \times 220}{4 \times 1000} = \frac{5280}{4000} = 1.32 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

Net heat input (with efficiency):
$$\text{HI}_{\text{net}} = \eta \times \text{HI} = 0.80 \times 1.32 = 1.056 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{HI}_{\text{net}} = 1.056 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

**Part (ii) — Total Energy for 3 m Weld (3 marks):**

Convert length: 3 m = 3000 mm.

$$E_{\text{total}} = \text{HI}_{\text{net}} \times L = 1.056 \text{ kJ/mm} \times 3000 \text{ mm} = 3168 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{total}} = 3168 \text{ kJ} \; (\approx 3.17 \text{ MJ})}$$

**Part (iii) — Comment (1 mark):**

A heat input of **1.056 kJ/mm** is **moderate and acceptable** for **10 mm mild-steel plate** in SMAW. Typical recommended HI for mild steel lies in the range **0.8 – 2.5 kJ/mm**; values within this range ensure good penetration without excessive HAZ softening or distortion. Therefore, this parameter set is suitable.

---

### **OPTION B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Classify **arc welding processes** into consumable and non-consumable categories. With neat sketches, describe the **GTAW (TIG)** and **SMAW** processes. Compare them in terms of shielding mechanism, electrode material, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An **OAW operation** uses acetylene at a flow rate of **10 L/min** to weld a steel plate. Calculate:
>   (i) The **volume of oxygen** required per minute for a **neutral flame**.
>   (ii) The **heat released per minute** if the calorific value of acetylene is **55 MJ/m³** and complete combustion efficiency is **0.85**.
>   (iii) The **cost of the gas mixture per hour** if acetylene costs **₹ 800 per 100 m³** and oxygen costs **₹ 50 per 100 m³**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B(a) — 7 marks**

**Classification (2 marks):**

| Type | Processes |
|---|---|
| **Consumable** | SMAW, GMAW (MIG/MAG), FCAW, SAW |
| **Non-consumable** | GTAW (TIG), Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) |

**SMAW — Short Description (2 marks):** Already covered in detail in Option A. Briefly: flux-coated electrode, manual, slag + gas shielding, all-position, DCEP/AC.

**GTAW (TIG) — Short Description (2 marks):**

In **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG)**, the arc is struck between a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** and the workpiece. The electrode is shielded by an inert gas (typically **Argon**, sometimes Helium) flowing from a **ceramic nozzle** surrounding the electrode. Filler metal, when required, is added manually with a separate **filler rod** dipped into the leading edge of the weld pool. The process produces **very clean, high-quality, spatter-free welds** and is the preferred method for **stainless steel, aluminum, titanium, magnesium**, and other reactive metals. The arc is highly stable, easily controlled, and can be used in **AC for aluminum** (to break the oxide film via cathodic cleaning) and **DCEN for steel**.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | SMAW | GTAW (TIG) |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode | Flux-coated consumable (mild steel / low-alloy / stainless) | **Tungsten (W)** non-consumable |
| Shielding | Flux decomposition (gas + slag) | **Inert gas (Ar / He)** from nozzle |
| Filler | Through the electrode | Separate filler rod (if needed) |
| Slag | Yes, must be chipped off | **No slag** |
| Skill level | Moderate | High |
| Outdoor use | Yes (slag protects) | **No** (wind blows Ar away) |
| Typical applications | Structural steel, shipbuilding, pipelines | Stainless, aluminum, aerospace, food-grade |

---

#### **Solution to B(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Acetylene flow: $\dot{V}_{C_2H_2} = 10$ L/min
- Neutral flame: O₂ : C₂H₂ = **2.5 : 1**
- Calorific value of C₂H₂: **55 MJ/m³**
- Combustion efficiency: $\eta = 0.85$
- Cost: C₂H₂ = ₹ 800 / 100 m³ → ₹ 8/m³; O₂ = ₹ 50 / 100 m³ → ₹ 0.50/m³

**Part (i) — Volume of oxygen per minute (2 marks):**

$$\dot{V}_{O_2} = 2.5 \times \dot{V}_{C_2H_2} = 2.5 \times 10 = 25 \text{ L/min}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_{O_2} = 25 \text{ L/min}}$$

**Part (ii) — Heat released per minute (3 marks):**

Convert acetylene flow to m³/min:
$$\dot{V}_{C_2H_2} = 10 \text{ L/min} = 0.010 \text{ m}^3\text{/min}$$

Theoretical heat per minute:
$$Q_{\text{theo}} = 0.010 \times 55 = 0.55 \text{ MJ/min}$$

Actual heat (with efficiency):
$$Q_{\text{actual}} = \eta \times Q_{\text{theo}} = 0.85 \times 0.55 = 0.4675 \text{ MJ/min}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{actual}} \approx 0.4675 \text{ MJ/min} \; (\approx 467.5 \text{ kJ/min})}$$

**Part (iii) — Cost per hour (2 marks):**

Per hour (60 min):
- Acetylene: $0.010 \times 60 = 0.60$ m³/h → cost = $0.60 \times 8 = ₹\,4.80$
- Oxygen: $0.025 \times 60 = 1.50$ m³/h → cost = $1.50 \times 0.50 = ₹\,0.75$

Total cost per hour:
$$C = 4.80 + 0.75 = ₹\,5.55 \text{ per hour}$$

$$\boxed{C = ₹\,5.55 \text{ per hour}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes in this module:**
> 1. **Confusing O₂ : C₂H₂ = 2.5 : 1 (by volume) with 5 : 2 or with mass ratio.** KTU always tests the **volume ratio**. Mass ratio is different and is **NOT** asked.
> 2. **Heat Input unit error:** Forgetting the factor 60/1000. Students often write $\dfrac{V I}{S}$ and call it kJ/mm — **wrong**. The conversion is mandatory.
> 3. **Confusing "heat input" with "arc power."** Arc power $P = VI$ is in **watts** (or kW); heat input is **energy per unit length** (kJ/mm). Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit confusion.
> 4. **Forgetting thermal efficiency $\eta$.** If the question gives $\eta$, you MUST multiply. If $\eta$ is **not** given, the question intends **gross HI**.
> 5. **Mixing up "Consumable" with "Carbon Arc."** Carbon arc is **non-consumable** (carbon electrode, separate filler) — *not* part of consumable family. Know the difference.
> 6. **Saying "SMAW uses inert gas."** It does **NOT**. The shielding comes from **flux decomposition**, not from an external gas bottle. Conflating with GTAW is a classic trap.
> 7. **Skipping the sketch label.** A labelled sketch in SMAW / OAW questions carries **1–2 marks** purely on neatness. Always draw with **labelled parts**, not just a line diagram.
> 8. **In numericals — not writing units in the final box.** Always enclose numerical answers in a `box` (or underline) with **explicit units** — a common ½-mark deduction point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this section for last-hour revision before KTU ESE.**

### ✅ Welding Classification
- **Three families:** Fusion, Solid-state, Brazing/Soldering
- **Fusion examples:** OFW, Arc, Resistance, Thermit, Laser, EB
- **Solid-state examples:** Friction, FSW, Diffusion, Ultrasonic, Cold, Roll, Explosion, Forge

### ✅ Oxyacetylene Welding (OAW)
- Heat source: **C₂H₂ + O₂ combustion**; max **3200 °C** in inner cone
- Stoichiometric **O₂ : C₂H₂ = 2.5 : 1** by **volume**
- **Three flame types:**
  - **Neutral** (2.5 : 1) — steel, general work
  - **Carburizing** (< 2.5 : 1) — high-C steel, cast iron, hard-facing
  - **Oxidizing** (> 2.5 : 1) — brass, bronze
- **Advantage:** No electricity required → field work
- **Disadvantage:** Slower, larger HAZ, thicker plates impossible

### ✅ Arc Welding — Key Parameters
- **V** controls arc length, bead width
- **I** controls penetration, deposition
- **S** controls bead size, heat input
- **Polarity:** DCEP (most common for SMAW), DCEN, AC

### ✅ Heat Input (MOST-TESTED FORMULA)
$$\text{HI} = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \text{ kJ/mm} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{HI} = \frac{V \times I}{S \times 1000} \text{ kJ/mm (S in mm/s)}$$

- Apply $\eta$ if given: $\text{HI}_{\text{net}} = \eta \times \text{HI}$
- Typical $\eta$: SMAW ≈ 0.80, GMAW ≈ 0.80, GTAW ≈ 0.50, SAW ≈ 0.95, PAW ≈ 0.50

### ✅ Consumable vs Non-Consumable
- **Consumable:** SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW — electrode melts → filler
- **Non-consumable:** GTAW (TIG), PAW — Tungsten electrode, separate filler, inert gas shield

### ✅ SMAW
- **Electrode** = core wire + **flux coating**
- **6 flux functions:** shielding gas, slag, arc stabilization, deoxidation, alloying, mechanical rigidity
- **AWS code:** E60xx (60 ksi TS) / E70xx (70 ksi TS); last digit = coating/current/position
- **E7018** = low-hydrogen, DCEP, all-position — most common critical-service electrode
- **Polarity:** DCEP most common (~70% heat at workpiece); AC used with transformer

### ✅ Numerical Heuristics for KTU
- **Always show step-by-step** substitution with units
- **Cross-check** answers against physical plausibility
- **Convert mm/s → mm/min** (multiply by 60) if using the kJ/mm formula
- **Apply $\eta$** only if given

### ✅ Common Mnemonics
- **SMAW = "Stick"** → flux-coated rigid rod
- **GTAW = "TIG"** → Tungsten + Inert Gas
- **GMAW = "MIG/MAG"** → Metal Inert Gas / Metal Active Gas
- **OAW Flame Memory:** **NCO** → **N**eutral, **C**arburizing, **O**xidizing (in order of increasing O₂ ratio)

### ✅ Examiners' Favourite Questions (Past Trends)
1. Sketch + functions of flux coating in SMAW (very frequent)
2. Flame types in OAW with applications (every year)
3. Compare consumable vs non-consumable arc welding
4. Heat input numerical with efficiency
5. Classify welding processes (with examples)
6. AWS electrode classification decoding

> **Best of luck for your KTU ESE — focus on numerical practice and clean, labelled sketches!**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GTAW
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚙️ MODULE 2 — GAS TUNGSTEN ARC WELDING (GTAW)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)**, universally known by its trade designation **TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas) welding**, is a non-consumable electrode arc welding process sanctioned under **AWS A3.0M/A3.0** and **ISO 4063 (Process Number 141)**. In GTAW, the arc is sustained between a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** and the workpiece inside an envelope of **inert shielding gas** (Argon, Helium, or their mixtures). Filler metal, when required, is added *externally* by the welder's free hand — a feature that distinguishes it from consumable-electrode processes like GMAW or SMAW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is mapped to **CO2 — "Understand various non-conventional and advanced manufacturing/welding processes and their selection criteria."** Mastery of GTAW parameters, polarity effects, and shielding gas behavior is a *high-frequency* board exam theme.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Pencil-Welder"

Imagine you are sketching a fine line on paper with a **sharp graphite pencil** while blowing a gentle stream of breath through a straw to keep dust away. The pencil tip (tungsten) never shortens — it only *heats up* and *creates the spark* (arc). The ink (filler rod) is dipped in *separately* with your other hand only when needed. Meanwhile, the straw (shielding gas nozzle) blasts a clean, inert atmosphere over the spot so the hot metal doesn't tarnish (oxidize).

| Real-World Object | GTAW Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Pencil graphite tip | Tungsten electrode | Generates arc, never melts |
| Your breath through straw | Argon/Helium shielding gas | Displaces atmospheric O₂, N₂, H₂ |
| Dipping the ink pen | Filler rod dipped by free hand | Adds material to the joint |
| The paper surface | Workpiece (base metal) | Where fusion occurs |
| Spark from pencil tip | Plasma arc (~10,000 °C) | Melts base metal locally |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Inert" Gas?** Argon and Helium are **monatomic noble gases** that do not react chemically with the molten weld pool. They simply *push away* (displace) reactive atmospheric gases (O₂, N₂) — preventing porosity, embrittlement, and oxide inclusion.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard Arc Temperature:** 10,000 °C – 20,000 °C at the anode/cathode spot
- **Tungsten Melting Point:** **3,422 °C** (highest of all metals)
- **Argon Ionization Potential:** **15.76 eV**
- **Helium Ionization Potential:** **24.58 eV** (hence Helium runs *hotter* and *deeper penetrating* arcs)
- **Typical Arc Efficiency (η):** **0.6 (60%)** for GTAW on steel
- **Standard Shielding Gas Flow Rate:** **8 – 15 L/min** (typical), up to **20 L/min** for Helium

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Working Principle — Stepwise Logic

The GTAW process unfolds in the following rigorously ordered sequence. KTU examiners frequently test *Step 3* and *Step 7* in Part A (3-mark) questions.

1. **Power Source Activation** — A constant-current (CC) power source (typically drooping V-I characteristic) delivers current to the tungsten electrode held in the torch.
2. **Arc Initiation** — Arc is struck via **High Frequency (HF) starting** (20–30 kV, 1–2 MHz radio-frequency burst) or **Lift-Arc / Scratch start** (contact then withdraw). HF avoids tungsten contamination.
3. **Arc Stabilization Zone** — The arc column forms between the **cathode spot** (on tungsten or workpiece, depending on polarity) and the **anode spot**, with column temperatures in the **10,000–20,000 °C** range.
4. **Shielding Gas Flooding** — Inert gas (Ar/He) flows coaxially through the nozzle (ceramic cup, typically alumina) at a laminar or slightly turbulent rate, creating a protective **gas shield** extending 1–2 electrode diameters beyond the cup exit.
5. **Base Metal Fusion** — Arc heat melts a localized pool (the "weld puddle") on the joint. Heat is transferred by **conduction** (through solid metal), **convection** (in the molten pool), and **radiation** from the arc plasma.
6. **Filler Metal Addition** *(optional)* — A bare filler rod is manually fed into the leading edge of the puddle. The rod melts *surface-tension driven*, not by arc transfer.
7. **Solidification & Shielding Continuation** — Shielding gas flow continues for a **post-flow period** (typically 1–3 seconds per 10 A) to protect the cooling weld bead and the red-hot tungsten tip from atmospheric contamination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Constant Current (CC)?** In TIG, the arc length can vary as the welder's hand trembles. With CC, a small arc-length change produces a *voltage fluctuation* but **current stays steady**, ensuring consistent penetration. A constant-voltage (CV) source would cause catastrophic current surges — burning back the tungsten.

## 2.2 Electrode Polarity — The Single Most-Tested Concept

The choice of polarity **fundamentally alters** the heat distribution and process capabilities. KTU frequently asks the difference between DCEN, DCEP, and AC.

| Polarity | Electrode | Current % in Electrode | Current % in Workpiece | Tungsten Temp | Penetration | Cleaning Action | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DCEN** (Direct Current Electrode Negative) | − | ~30% | ~70% | Cool | **Deep, narrow** | **None** | Steels, stainless, Ti, Cu, Ni |
| **DCEP** (Direct Current Electrode Positive) | + | ~70% | ~30% | Very hot, erodes | Shallow, wide | Strong | Not used for GTAW (destroys tungsten) |
| **AC** (Alternating Current) | Alternates | ~50% | ~50% | Balanced | Moderate | **Yes (during EN half-cycle)** | **Aluminum, Magnesium** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The AC Cleaning Action Explained:** During the EN (Electrode Negative) half-cycle, electrons flow from tungsten to workpiece → penetration heating. During the EP (Electrode Positive) half-cycle, electrons bombard the workpiece surface → break the tenacious Al₂O₃ layer (which melts at ~2,072 °C vs. pure Al at 660 °C). Modern inverter power sources use **square-wave AC** with adjustable **balance** (e.g., 70% EN / 30% EP) to maximize cleaning or penetration as needed.

## 2.3 Tungsten Electrode Classification (AWS A5.12 / ISO 6848)

| AWS Class | Color Code | Oxide Content | Composition | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EWP** (Pure Tungsten) | Green | 0% | 99.5% W | AC welding of Al/Mg (balled tip) |
| **EWTh-2** | Yellow | 2% ThO₂ | Thoriated | **DCEN on steel/stainless** (most common) |
| **EWLa-1.5** | Gold | 1.5% La₂O₃ | Lanthanated | Replacement for Th — non-radioactive |
| **EWCe-2** | Orange | 2% CeO₂ | Ceriated | Low-current DC, tube welding |
| **EWZr-1** | Brown | 0.8% ZrO₂ | Zirconiated | AC welding of Al — resists splitting |

> [!WARNING]
> **Radioactivity Note:** Thoriated tungsten (EWTh-2) is mildly radioactive. KTU has started asking about **EWLa** (Lanthanated) as a safer modern alternative — this is a *favorite 3-marker*.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notation:** The vertical bar symbol `|` is *not* used in tables below; instead we use `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage. The `\eta` symbol denotes arc efficiency.

| # | Parameter Name | Formula | Units | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (H)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | **0.5 – 2.0 kJ/mm** for GTAW |
| 2 | **Heat Input (alternate form)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/m | 500 – 2000 kJ/m |
| 3 | **Travel Speed Conversion** | $S = v \cdot 1000$ | mm/min | If $v$ is in m/min |
| 4 | **Heat Generation Rate (Power in Arc)** | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watts (J/s) | Arc power |
| 5 | **Energy per Unit Length** | $E_L = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$ | J/m | 500,000 – 2,000,000 J/m |
| 6 | **Shielding Gas Flow Rate Rule** | $Q = k \cdot d_{cup}$ | L/min | $k \approx 1.0$ – $1.5$; $d_{cup}$ in mm |
| 7 | **Post-Flow Time** | $t_{post} = \dfrac{I}{10}$ (approx.) | seconds | Empirical rule |
| 8 | **Tungsten Current Capacity (DCEN, EWP, steel)** | $I_{max} \approx 10 \cdot d_W$ | A | $d_W$ = tungsten diameter in mm |
| 9 | **Penetration Depth (Rule of thumb)** | $d_{pen} \approx 0.7 \cdot I^{0.6}$ (empirical) | mm | Approximate, for low-alloy steel |
| 10 | **Argon Density (at STP)** | $\rho_{Ar} = 1.784$ | kg/m³ | Standard reference |

> **Boundary Conditions & Assumptions:**
> - $\eta_{GTAW} \approx 0.6$ on steel; $\eta_{GTAW} \approx 0.5$ on aluminum (lower due to high thermal conductivity)
> - Voltage $V$ is **true arc voltage** (load voltage), *not* open-circuit voltage
> - Travel speed $S$ is the **welding travel speed**, not the wire feed rate (GTAW has no wire feed)
> - Heat input must stay below the **Interpass temperature** × **HAZ** constraints to avoid sensitization of stainless steel (typically H < 1.5 kJ/mm for 304L)

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

GTAW is the **gold standard** when weld *quality*, *precision*, and *metallurgical cleanliness* override speed and deposition rate. It is **irreplaceable** in:

- **Aerospace:** Rocket engine thrust chambers (Inconel, Ti-6Al-4V), turbine blades, fuselage stringers
- **Nuclear:** Zirconium alloy fuel cladding, stainless pressure vessel root passes
- **Pharmaceutical / Food:** 316L stainless tubing (sanitary welds, no crevices)
- **Automotive:** Aluminum alloy body panels (Tesla Model S, Audi A8 spaceframes)
- **Art / Sculpture:** Bronze and stainless artistic welds where bead aesthetics matter
- **First-pass / root-pass welding** on thick sections, where the subsequent passes are filled by faster GMAW/SAW

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input in GTAW (Full Energy-Balance Derivation)

The **heat input $H$** is defined as the *arc energy delivered per unit length of weld*. This is the most heavily-tested formula across all KTU welding modules.

### Step 1 — Start with Arc Power

The instantaneous electrical power dissipated in the arc is given by the product of arc voltage and arc current (Joule's law of heating applied to the plasma column).

$$
P_{arc} = V \cdot I
$$

where $P_{arc}$ is in **Watts** (= J/s), $V$ is in **Volts**, and $I$ is in **Amperes**.

### Step 2 — Account for Thermal Efficiency

Not all electrical energy becomes useful heat in the workpiece. A fraction $\eta$ (the **arc thermal efficiency** or **heat transfer efficiency**) actually reaches the base metal; the rest is lost to the electrode cooling, radiation to the atmosphere, and convective losses in the shielding gas plume.

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I
$$

For GTAW, $\eta \approx 0.6$ on steel (a well-documented value from AWS and ISO/TR 18465).

### Step 3 — Convert to Energy per Unit Length

The energy delivered *per millimeter* of weld is the useful power divided by the **travel speed** (in mm per second, then converted). If the travel speed is $S$ in **mm/min**, we must divide by $S/60$ mm/s:

$$
H = \dfrac{P_{useful}}{S / 60} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S}
$$

This gives $H$ in **Joules per mm (J/mm)**.

### Step 4 — Convert to Kilojoules per mm

Since J/mm is numerically large for industrial values, we divide by 1000 to obtain the standard KTU/Kubernetes engineering unit:

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

This is the **canonical heat-input formula** in **kJ/mm**.

### Step 5 — Cross-Verification with Velocity Form

If travel speed is given as $v$ in **m/min**, then $S = v \cdot 1000$ mm/min. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{(v \cdot 1000) \cdot 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields $H$ in **kJ/m** — the more common form in EN ISO 1011-1 and AWS D1.1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor 60?** The travel speed $S$ is conventionally given in mm/min, but power is in J/s (= W). To make the time units consistent (seconds), we multiply the numerator by 60 (s/min).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Standard Pattern

**Problem:** A GTAW operation on 6 mm thick 304L stainless steel uses **arc voltage = 18 V**, **current = 150 A**, and **travel speed = 100 mm/min**. Given $\eta = 0.6$, calculate (a) Heat input in kJ/mm, and (b) Determine if the heat input is acceptable for 304L (limit ≤ 1.5 kJ/mm).

**Solution:**

**(a) Heat input calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 18 \cdot 150 \cdot 60}{100 \cdot 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 18 \cdot 150 \cdot 60}{100{,}000} \\
  &= \dfrac{97{,}200}{100{,}000} \\
  &= 0.972 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Acceptability check:**

Since $0.972 \text{ kJ/mm} < 1.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$ ✓ — **The heat input is acceptable** for 304L stainless steel. No sensitization of the HAZ is expected (interpass temperature must also be kept below 150 °C as a secondary check).

## 3.3 Derivation: Tungsten Electrode Current Capacity (Empirical)

The maximum DCEN current a tungsten electrode can carry *without* excessive tip melting or arc instability follows an empirical linear rule derived from AWS A5.12M testing:

$$
I_{max} \approx 10 \cdot d_W
$$

where $d_W$ is the tungsten diameter in **mm** and $I_{max}$ is in **Amperes**. This comes from heat-balance experiments: the heat generated at the cathode spot $\propto I$ must be conducted *radially* into the electrode shank $\propto d_W$, giving a linear scaling.

**Validation Table:**

| $d_W$ (mm) | $I_{max}$ (A) for DCEN on steel | $I_{max}$ (A) for AC on Al |
|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 10 | 8 |
| 1.6 | 16 | 13 |
| 2.4 | 24 | 20 |
| 3.2 | 32 | 28 |

The AC column is ~80% of DCEN because of the half-cycle cooling/reheating cycle.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Logic for GTAW vs Other Processes

| Selection Criterion | GTAW | SMAW | GMAW | FCAW | SAW |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Plate thickness range (mm)** | 0.5 – 6 (single pass) | ≥ 4 | ≥ 3 | ≥ 4 | ≥ 6 |
| **Deposition rate (kg/h)** | 0.5 – 2.0 | 1 – 3 | 3 – 8 | 4 – 10 | 10 – 30 |
| **Weld quality (1–10)** | 10 | 6 | 8 | 7 | 8 |
| **Skill required** | Very high | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Cost per meter of weld** | Very high | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Very low |
| **Outdoor/windy suitability** | **Poor** (gas blows away) | Excellent | Poor-Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| **Position capability** | All positions | All | All (pulsed) | All | Flat/Horizontal only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Selection:** Choose GTAW when **any** of the following hold: (a) material is Al, Mg, Ti, or reactive metal; (b) weld must be radiographic-quality (no porosity, no spatter); (c) thickness < 3 mm; (d) aesthetic bead appearance matters; (e) root pass in thick plate where GMAW cannot access.

## 3.5 Detailed Parameter Matrix for Common GTAW Joints

| Base Metal | Thickness (mm) | Current (A) | Polarity | Gas | Flow (L/min) | Filler |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon steel | 2.0 | 60 – 80 | DCEN | Ar | 8 – 10 | ER70S-2 |
| Stainless 304L | 3.0 | 80 – 120 | DCEN | Ar (or Ar+2%H₂) | 10 – 12 | ER308L |
| Aluminum 6061 | 3.0 | 90 – 130 | **AC** | Ar | 12 – 15 | ER4043 |
| Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | 4.0 | 120 – 160 | DCEN | Ar (trailing shield!) | 15 – 20 | ERTi-5 |
| Copper | 3.0 | 150 – 200 | DCEN | Ar or He | 15 – 20 | ERCu |

> [!NOTE]
> **Trailing Shield for Titanium:** Titanium oxidizes above 400 °C. For Ti, a *trailing shield* (auxiliary gas nozzle mounted *behind* the torch) continues inert coverage over the *cooling* weld. This is a frequently-asked 14-marker.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🗺️ 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 GTAW System — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *complete functional architecture* of a GTAW workstation — from electrical input to weld bead formation. It captures all subsystems and their interactions in a single industrial-grade flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwr1[Three Phase AC Mains Supply 415V 50Hz] --> rect1[Three Phase Rectifier Bridge]
    rect1 --> filter1[Smoothing LC Filter Capacitor Bank]
    filter1 --> inv1[High Frequency Inverter Stage IGBT 20kHz]
    inv1 --> tr1[High Frequency Isolation Transformer]
    tr1 --> rect2[Secondary Rectifier and Output Filter]
    rect2 --> cc1[Constant Current Output Stage CC Mode]
    cc1 --> hf2[High Frequency Arc Starter Unit 1MHz 30kV Burst]
    cc1 --> torch1[TIG Torch with Tungsten Electrode]
    hf2 --> torch1
    torch1 --> arc1[Plasma Arc Column 10000 to 20000 C]
    gas1[Argon or Helium Cylinder with Regulator] --> flow1[Gas Flowmeter and Solenoid Valve]
    flow1 --> nozzle1[Ceramic Nozzle Cup Alumina]
    nozzle1 --> arc1
    arc1 --> wp1[Workpiece Base Metal Fusion Zone]
    fr1[Filler Rod Manual Dip Feed] --> wp1
    wp1 --> bead1[Solidified Weld Bead and HAZ]
    bead1 --> cool1[Post Flow Shielding Gas Coverage 1 to 3 seconds]
    cc1 --> ctl1[Foot Pedal Amperage Control]
    cc1 --> ctl2[Arc Length Voltage Feedback Sensor]
    ctl2 --> cc1
    torch1 --> cw1[Water Cooling Circuit for High Amperage above 200A]
```

## 4.2 GTAW Process Logic Flow — Decision Tree

This diagram captures the *operational decision logic* a GTAW welder follows from power-on to bead completion.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Start Setup Phase] --> base1{Select Base Metal}
    base1 --> fer1[Ferrous Steel or Stainless]
    base1 --> al1[Aluminum or Magnesium]
    base1 --> ti1[Titanium or Reactive Metal]
    fer1 --> pol1[Set Polarity DCEN]
    al1 --> pol2[Set Polarity AC Square Wave 60 to 200 Hz]
    ti1 --> pol1
    pol1 --> gas1[Select Shielding Gas Argon 99.99%]
    pol2 --> gas1
    gas1 --> elec1{Select Tungsten Type}
    elec1 --> th1[EWTh2 Yellow 2% Thoria for DCEN Steel]
    elec1 --> la1[EWLa1.5 Gold 1.5% Lanthana Modern Safe]
    elec1 --> zr1[EWZr1 Brown 0.8% Zirconia for AC Aluminum]
    elec1 --> ce1[EWCe2 Orange 2% Ceria for Low Current]
    th1 --> grind1[Grind Electrode Longitudinally 400 Grit]
    la1 --> grind1
    zr1 --> ball1[Ball the Tip on AC Arc Naturally]
    ce1 --> grind1
    grind1 --> arc1[Initiate Arc via HF Burst or Lift Arc]
    ball1 --> arc1
    arc1 --> puddle1[Establish Molten Weld Puddle]
    puddle1 --> dip1{Dip Filler Rod}
    dip1 --> yes1[Add Filler to Leading Edge of Puddle]
    dip1 --> no1[Autogenous Weld No Filler]
    yes1 --> trav1[Torch Travels at Constant Speed 80 to 150 mm per min]
    no1 --> trav1
    trav1 --> post1[Continue Post Flow Shielding Gas 1 to 3 seconds]
    post1 --> done1[Completed Bead with Silver to Gold Color]
```

## 4.3 Polarity Effect Visualization — DCEN vs AC Heat Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph dc_en[DCEN Configuration Direct Current Electrode Negative]
        t1[Tungsten Electrode Cathode Minus] -->|Electron Flow| w1[Workpiece Anode Plus]
        w1 -->|70% Heat| fusion1[Deep Narrow Fusion Zone in Steel]
        t1 -->|30% Heat| cool1[Tungsten Tip Stays Cool and Pointed]
    end
    subgraph ac_mode[AC Square Wave Mode for Aluminum]
        t2[Tungsten Electrode Alternates] -->|EN Half Cycle| w2[Workpiece Penetration Heating]
        t2 -->|EP Half Cycle| w2
        w2 -->|Cleaning Burst Electrons Bombard Surface| oxide1[Al2O3 Layer Broken and Dispelled]
        oxide1 --> fusion2[Clean Fusion of Pure Aluminum Beneath]
    end
    dc_en --- ac_mode
```

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
**"Differentiate between DCEN and DCEP polarity in GTAW. Which is preferred for welding stainless steel and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **DCEN (Electrode Negative)** | **DCEP (Electrode Positive)** |
|---|---|---|
| Electron flow | Electrode → Workpiece | Workpiece → Electrode |
| Heat in electrode | ~30% | ~70% (tungsten overheats) |
| Heat in workpiece | ~70% (deep penetration) | ~30% (shallow) |
| Cleaning action | None | Strong |
| Tungsten tip shape | Pointed | Mushroomed / melted |
| Suitability for SS | **Preferred** | Not used |

**For stainless steel, DCEN is preferred** because (i) it provides deep, narrow penetration essential for thick sections; (ii) it keeps the tungsten electrode cool and pointed, ensuring stable arc; (iii) the 70% heat in the workpiece promotes faster travel speed and lower heat input, **preventing sensitization (chromium-carbide precipitation at grain boundaries) in the 304/316 HAZ**.

*(Distribution: 2 marks for table; 1 mark for the SS-specific reason)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**"List any THREE advantages of GTAW over SMAW. State one major limitation of GTAW."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages of GTAW over SMAW:**
1. **Superior weld quality** — no slag, no spatter, no porosity from flux; bead is radiographically clean. (1 mark)
2. **Wider material range** — can weld non-ferrous metals like Al, Mg, Ti, Cu, and reactive metals which SMAW cannot easily handle. (1 mark)
3. **Precise heat control** — foot-pedal current control allows real-time amperage adjustment; better for thin sections and out-of-position welding. (1 mark, if third listed)

**Major limitation of GTAW:** **Low deposition rate (0.5–2 kg/h)** — about 5–10× slower than GMAW/SAW. This makes it **economically unviable for thick-plate production welding**; typically restricted to root passes, thin sections, and high-quality critical joints.

*(Alternative accepted answers for limitation: High operator skill required / not suitable for outdoor windy conditions / higher cost per meter of weld)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Exam]**

> *"With a neat sketch, explain the Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) process. Discuss in detail the effect of the following process parameters on weld quality: (a) Welding current and polarity, (b) Shielding gas type and flow rate. (7 + 7 = 14 marks)"*

#### Part (a) — GTAW Process Description (7 marks)

**1. Definition (1 mark):** GTAW (AWS designation, ISO 4063 process 141) is a non-consumable electrode arc welding process in which an arc is maintained between a tungsten electrode and the workpiece, with the weld zone protected from atmospheric contamination by an inert shielding gas (Argon, Helium, or mixtures).

**2. Equipment (2 marks):**
- **Power source:** Constant-current (drooping characteristic) DC or AC inverter; typically 5–500 A capacity.
- **Torch:** Water-cooled (high-amperage) or air-cooled; holds the tungsten in a collet, surrounded by a ceramic nozzle.
- **Tungsten electrode:** Non-consumable; classes include EWP, EWTh-2, EWLa, EWCe, EWZr per AWS A5.12.
- **Shielding gas delivery:** Cylinder → regulator → flowmeter → solenoid valve → torch nozzle.
- **HF starter:** 1 MHz, 30 kV burst for non-contact arc initiation.
- **Filler rod feed:** Manual (free hand), no wire feeder.

**3. Working principle (2 marks):** The arc ionizes the inert gas, creating a plasma column at 10,000–20,000 °C. Heat melts the base metal locally. The shielding gas envelope displaces atmospheric O₂, N₂, H₂O. A filler rod (if used) is dipped into the leading edge of the puddle, melts by surface-tension transfer, and merges with the molten pool. On solidification, a clean, slag-free, spatter-free bead forms. The shielding gas continues for a post-flow period to protect the cooling weld.

**4. Schematic (2 marks):** Draw the standard GTAW schematic showing — power source, HF starter, tungsten electrode, ceramic nozzle, shielding gas inlet, arc column, weld pool, filler rod, workpiece, post-flow zone. *(Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram for reference.)*

#### Part (b) — Effect of Current, Polarity, Shielding Gas (7 marks)

**(i) Welding Current (2 marks):**
- **Too low current** → insufficient penetration, lack of fusion, "cold lap" defects.
- **Too high current** → burn-through (on thin plate), excessive tungsten erosion, wide HAZ, distortion, sensitization in SS.
- **Optimum range** scales with thickness; rule of thumb: **1 A per 0.025 mm of thickness** for DCEN on steel.

**(ii) Polarity (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 2.2 table.)*
- **DCEN:** 70% heat in workpiece → deep penetration → standard for steel, SS, Ti, Cu, Ni.
- **DCEP:** 70% heat in tungsten → tungsten melts, not used in GTAW except for specific micro-plasma cleaning.
- **AC:** alternating cleaning (EP half) and penetration (EN half) → **mandatory for Al and Mg** to break the refractory oxide layer.

**(iii) Shielding Gas Type (1.5 marks):**
- **Pure Argon:** most common; good arc stability; lower cost; suitable for most metals including SS and Ti at low-current.
- **Pure Helium:** higher arc voltage (24.58 eV ionization), hotter arc, deeper penetration, faster travel; used for high-speed Al, Cu, and thick sections. **Limitation:** more expensive, harder to arc-start, requires more flow.
- **Argon-Helium mixtures** (e.g., 75% Ar / 25% He): compromise; better wetting on Al, higher heat input than pure Ar.
- **Argon + 1–2% H₂:** for austenitic SS — improves wetting, reduces surface tension, increases travel speed (but **NEVER on ferritic steel or Ti** — hydrogen causes cracking/embrittlement).

**(iv) Shielding Gas Flow Rate (1.5 marks):**
- **Too low:** atmospheric contamination → porosity, discoloration (straw → purple → black on SS indicates progressively worse shielding).
- **Too high:** turbulent flow → aspirates air into the shield → contamination; also wastes gas; high velocity can blow the puddle.
- **Optimum:** **8–15 L/min** for Ar (≈ 1 L/min per mm of nozzle bore diameter). **Rule:** nozzle bore in mm × 1.0 to 1.5 = flow in L/min.
- **Post-flow:** 1 second per 10 A of current (e.g., 150 A → 15 s post-flow) to protect the cooling tungsten and bead.

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Main Exam — Alternative Choice]**

> *"Calculate the heat input for a GTAW operation with the following data: Arc voltage = 22 V, Current = 180 A, Travel speed = 120 mm/min, Thermal efficiency = 0.6. Comment on its suitability for welding 6 mm thick AISI 304L stainless steel. (b) With a neat sketch, describe the AC square-wave GTAW process for aluminum. Why is AC preferred over DC for aluminum? (7 + 7 = 14 marks)"*

#### Part (a) — Heat Input Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Arc voltage $V = 22$ V
- Current $I = 180$ A
- Travel speed $S = 120$ mm/min
- Thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.6$

**Formula:**
$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}
$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{0.6 \times 22 \times 180 \times 60}{120 \times 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.6 \times 22 \times 180 \times 60}{120{,}000} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.6 \times 22 \times 180}{2000} \quad \text{(after dividing numerator and denominator by 60)} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.6 \times 22 \times 0.09}{1} \quad \text{(further simplification: 180/2000 = 0.09)} \\
  &= \dfrac{1.188}{1} \\
  &= 1.188 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on suitability (3 marks within the 7):**
- For 304L austenitic SS, the **maximum recommended heat input** to avoid HAZ sensitization is **1.5 kJ/mm** (per ASME Section IX and EN 1011-2).
- Calculated $H = 1.188$ kJ/mm **< 1.5 kJ/mm** ✓
- **Conclusion:** The heat input is **acceptable** for welding 6 mm AISI 304L. **Additional check required:** interpass temperature must be maintained **below 150 °C** to prevent chromium carbide (Cr₂₃C₆) precipitation at grain boundaries (sensitization), which would degrade corrosion resistance.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for formula; 2 marks for substitution and arithmetic; 3 marks for the suitability comment with limit justification.)*

#### Part (b) — AC Square-Wave GTAW for Aluminum (7 marks)

**1. Why AC for Aluminum? (2 marks):**
Aluminum forms a **tenacious, refractory aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) layer** with melting point **~2,072 °C** — vastly higher than pure Al (**660 °C**). If this oxide is not removed *before* fusion, it gets entrapped in the weld, causing:
- Lack of fusion (oxide acts as insulator)
- Porosity
- Slag-like inclusions
- Reduced corrosion resistance and mechanical strength

**AC solves this** because during the **EP (Electrode Positive) half-cycle**, electrons are accelerated from the workpiece *toward* the electrode. These electrons **bombard the Al₂O₃ surface** with sufficient kinetic energy to **dislodge the oxide layer** (a process called *cathodic cleaning* or *ion bombardment cleaning*). The oxide breaks and floats away, exposing clean molten Al underneath.

**2. Square-Wave AC Operation (2 marks):** Modern inverter GTAW machines produce **square-wave AC** (not the old sine-wave transformer output) with the following adjustable parameters:
- **AC Frequency:** 60–200 Hz (default ~120 Hz). Higher frequency → more focused arc, narrower HAZ.
- **AC Balance (Cleaning/Penetration ratio):** percentage of time in EN vs EP half-cycle.
  - **70% EN / 30% EP** → maximum cleaning, less penetration — for heavily oxidized Al.
  - **80% EN / 20% EP** → standard, balanced.
  - **90% EN / 10% EP** → maximum penetration, minimum cleaning — for clean Al or deep grooves.
- **Independent Current Control (in advanced machines):** separate amperage setpoints for EN and EP halves — allows high EN current for penetration and low EP current (just enough to clean) for tungsten protection.

**3. Schematic Description (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.3 AC mode diagram.)*
- Tungsten electrode alternates polarity at high frequency.
- During EN half: arc heats the workpiece deeply.
- During EP half: electron bombardment cleans the Al₂O₃ layer.
- Argon shielding gas flows through the nozzle at 12–15 L/min; helium may be added (75/25 Ar/He) for better wetting.
- A pure tungsten (EWP) or zirconiated (EWZr-1) electrode is used — **thoriated is NOT recommended for AC** because the thoria does not ball up well and tends to spit.

**4. Special Considerations (1 mark):**
- **AC arc rectifies** if the surfaces are dirty — modern machines include **arc rectification detection** that shuts off current to protect against tungsten contamination.
- **HF starting** is essential in AC GTAW because there is no fixed polarity to scratch-start on.
- Use **water-cooled torch** for currents above 200 A AC to prevent overheating of the collet and nozzle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor "60" in the heat-input formula** — auto-zero for the calculation. (1–2 marks lost per instance.)
> 2. **Using the wrong arc efficiency** — must be $\eta = 0.6$ for GTAW on steel, *not* 0.8 (that's for SAW) or 1.0. Examiners *intentionally* test this.
> 3. **Confusing EWTh-2 and EWLa** — EWTh-2 is mildly radioactive; lanthanated (EWLa) is the modern safer replacement. Both questions may appear.
> 4. **Saying "AC is used for aluminum because it removes oxide"** without *explaining the mechanism* (electron bombardment during EP half-cycle) — loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting post-flow time** when discussing shielding gas parameters — direct 1-mark deduction in any 14-marker.
> 6. **No diagram in 14-mark questions** — KTU mandates a *neat sketch*; omitting it costs 1–2 marks even if the description is perfect.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this list. Read it 30 minutes before the exam. Tick each item as you recall it.**

- ✅ GTAW = Gas Tungsten Arc Welding = TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas) = AWS designation, ISO 4063 process **141**.
- ✅ Arc is between **non-consumable tungsten** and **workpiece**; filler is added *separately* by hand.
- ✅ Power source: **Constant Current (CC)**, drooping V-I characteristic — *never* CV.
- ✅ Shielding gases: **Argon** (99.99% pure, 15.76 eV ionization), **Helium** (24.58 eV, hotter arc, deeper penetration), Ar-He mixtures, Ar + 1–2% H₂ (SS only).
- ✅ Standard flow rate: **8–15 L/min** Ar; **15–20 L/min** He; rule = **1.0–1.5 L/min per mm of nozzle bore**.
- ✅ Polarity: **DCEN** for steel, SS, Ti, Cu, Ni (70% heat in workpiece, deep penetration, pointed tungsten).
- ✅ Polarity: **DCEP** is *destructive* to tungsten — not used in GTAW.
- ✅ Polarity: **AC square wave** is **mandatory for Al and Mg** — for cleaning the Al₂O₃ layer via electron bombardment in EP half-cycle.
- ✅ AC balance control: 70–90% EN typical; higher EN% = more penetration, less cleaning.
- ✅ Tungsten types: **EWP** (green, pure), **EWTh-2** (yellow, 2% ThO₂, mildly radioactive), **EWLa-1.5** (gold, modern safe alternative), **EWCe-2** (orange), **EWZr-1** (brown, for AC Al).
- ✅ Arc start: **HF (High-Frequency)** at 1 MHz / 30 kV — non-contact, no tungsten contamination. **Lift-arc** is a low-amperage alternative.
- ✅ Tungsten grind direction: **longitudinally** (along the length), 400-grit finish — never radially (creates arc wander).
- ✅ Post-flow shielding time: **1 second per 10 A** of welding current (e.g., 150 A → 15 s).
- ✅ Heat input formula: $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm; $\eta_{GTAW} \approx 0.6$ on steel, 0.5 on aluminum.
- ✅ Acceptable heat input for 304L SS: **H ≤ 1.5 kJ/mm**; interpass temperature **< 150 °C** — to prevent sensitization (Cr₂₃C₆ precipitation).
- ✅ Arc efficiency $\eta$ for reference: GTAW ≈ 0.6, SMAW ≈ 0.8, GMAW ≈ 0.7, SAW ≈ 0.99, PAW ≈ 0.6.
- ✅ GTAW best for: thin sections (0.5–6 mm), non-ferrous metals (Al, Mg, Ti, Cu), root passes, radiographic-quality welds, aerospace, nuclear, food-grade.
- ✅ GTAW limitations: low deposition rate (0.5–2 kg/h), high operator skill, expensive, **not suitable outdoors** (wind disrupts shielding).
- ✅ Color coding of bead (for SS, on back of bead): **silver/gold = good shielding**, **straw = slight O₂**, **purple = N₂**, **black/grey = heavy O₂ contamination**.
- ✅ Trailing shield is **required for titanium** welding — to prevent oxidation above 400 °C during cooling.
- ✅ Foot-pedal amperage control is the standard operator interface; modern machines also have finger-tip current up/down on the torch.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GAS METAL ARC WELDING (GMAW)**
> Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) is defined by **AWS (American Welding Society)** as *“an arc welding process wherein coalescence of metals is produced by heating them with an arc between a continuous filler metal (consumable) electrode and the workpiece. Shielding is obtained entirely from an externally supplied gas mixture.”*

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, GMAW is classified under the **fusion welding** family, and is the most widely used **semi-automatic / mechanized** arc welding process in modern manufacturing industries (automotive, shipbuilding, structural fabrication).

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms / Variants You Must Remember:**
> - **MIG Welding** (Metal Inert Gas) – used when **Inert** gases (Argon, Helium) shield the weld.
> - **MAG Welding** (Metal Active Gas) – used when **Active** gases (CO₂, O₂) or mixtures are used.
> Both MIG and MAG are operationally identical; the term differs purely by the type of shielding gas employed.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Continuous Metal Glue Gun"

Imagine a **hot glue gun** used in crafts. Instead of plastic glue, picture a continuously fed **solid metal wire** melting into a puddle that joins two metal plates. The surrounding air (which contains oxygen and nitrogen) is like *dust* that would contaminate the molten glue. To prevent this, you blow a **protective gas curtain** around the melt zone, just like blowing clean air to keep dust off freshly poured resin.

That protective gas curtain in GMAW is supplied through a **nozzle (gas cup)** surrounding the wire-feed tip. The wire is pushed continuously from a spool through a flexible conduit by a **motorized wire feeder**, ensuring that as fast as the weld pool consumes metal, fresh electrode is fed in to replace it.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the gas curtain matters:**
> If atmospheric oxygen or nitrogen touches the molten weld pool (≈ **3000 °C** for steel arc), the metal oxidises and nitrides, producing **porosity, embrittlement, and loss of ductility**. The shielding gas is therefore **non-negotiable** for weld quality.

---

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (Bolded Constants & Standards)

| Parameter | Standard / Range |
|---|---|
| Arc Temperature (Steel) | **6000 °C – 8000 °C** |
| Shielding Gas Flow Rate | **8 – 20 L/min** (typical 12 L/min) |
| Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) | **15 V – 40 V** (DC) |
| Wire Feed Speed | **2 – 20 m/min** |
| Deposition Efficiency | **90 % – 95 %** |
| Electrode Diameter | **0.6 mm – 2.4 mm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> GMAW is exclusively a **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** process for steel. The positive electrode (wire) means electrons flow from workpiece to wire, producing deep penetration and stable arc on ferrous metals.

---

```
               ├── Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)   ← TOPIC

               └── Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)
```

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle – Stepwise Logic

The GMAW process is governed by **three simultaneous physical events** that must be precisely balanced:

1. **Arc Establishment**
   - A constant-voltage (CV) DC power source is energised.
   - The contact tip (current-carrying copper nozzle) is brought within **3 – 6 mm** of the workpiece.
   - When the trigger is pulled, the wire feeder starts; contact between the live wire tip and the base metal **short-circuits**, drawing an arc immediately.

2. **Continuous Wire Feed & Metal Transfer**
   - The consumable electrode (spool of wire) is pushed through the welding gun at a **constant linear rate** (m/min).
   - As the wire melts at the arc, droplets detach and enter the weld pool. The **rate of feeding = rate of melting** is the self-regulating principle that makes constant-voltage GMAW stable.

3. **Shielding Gas Delivery**
   - Shielding gas flows from a cylinder → regulator → flow meter → through the gun cable → out of the **gas nozzle (cup)** around the arc.
   - The gas envelope displaces atmospheric air in a **laminar cone shape** over the molten pool until it solidifies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-Regulating Arc Principle:** In CV power source, arc length is *self-correcting*. If arc length increases, voltage rises, current falls, melting rate drops → arc shortens. If arc length shortens, voltage drops, current spikes, melting rate rises → arc lengthens. This is why **constant-voltage flat-characteristic** power sources are mandatory for GMAW (not constant-current drooping used in GTAW/SMAW).

---

## 2.2 Detailed Equipment Architecture

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power Source (CV)** | Provides DC; flat V–I characteristic |
| 2 | **Wire Feeder** | Constant-speed motor driving wire through conduit |
| 3 | **Welding Gun (Torch)** | Holds contact tip, gas nozzle, trigger |
| 4 | **Contact Tip** | Copper tip that conducts current to wire |
| 5 | **Gas Nozzle / Cup** | Directs shielding gas around arc |
| 6 | **Shielding Gas Cylinder** | Argon / CO₂ / Ar–CO₂ / Ar–O₂ mixtures |
| 7 | **Flow meter + Regulator** | Controls gas flow to 8–20 L/min |
| 8 | **Ground Clamp** | Returns current to power source |
| 9 | **Spool of Wire Electrode** | Continuous consumable electrode |

---

## 2.3 The Four Modes of Metal Transfer (★ HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU ★)

The mode of metal transfer is determined primarily by **welding current and voltage** (and secondarily by shielding gas composition). Each mode produces a different weld appearance, penetration profile, and heat input.

### Mode 1: Short-Circuit Transfer (Dip Transfer)
- **Conditions:** Low voltage (**18–22 V**), low current, thin materials.
- **Mechanism:** The wire touches the weld pool → short circuit → current spike → **pinch effect** shears the molten droplet → arc re-ignites → cycle repeats at **20–200 times per second**.
- **Result:** Low heat input, ideal for **out-of-position welding** (vertical, overhead), thin sheets.

### Mode 2: Globular Transfer
- **Conditions:** Medium current, CO₂ shielding, voltages around **22–28 V**.
- **Mechanism:** Large droplets (larger than wire diameter, **2–3 mm**) form at the tip and **drop by gravity** — not transferred by arc forces. The transfer is **non-axial and erratic**.
- **Result:** Spatter-heavy, rough bead; **not used in production** for critical welds.

### Mode 3: Spray Transfer
- **Conditions:** High current, high voltage (**28–35 V**), Argon-rich shielding gas (≥ 80 % Ar).
- **Mechanism:** A **critical current** is exceeded, the arc force is strong enough to detach fine droplets (**< wire diameter**) axially, like a fine conical spray.
- **Result:** Smooth, spatter-free, deep penetration — best for **flat-position, thick-section welding** of carbon steel, stainless steel, aluminium.

### Mode 4: Pulsed Spray Transfer
- **Conditions:** Background current (low) is pulsed with a **peak current pulse** (high) at a frequency of **30–400 Hz**.
- **Mechanism:** One droplet detaches per pulse; average current is low → low heat input, but with full spray benefits.
- **Result:** Excellent for **out-of-position, thin sections, aluminium** — premium-quality process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner’s Mnemonic:** *"S-G-S-P" — S**hort-circuit, **G**lobular, **S**pray, **P**ulsed* — in order of *increasing* current. The transition from globular to spray is governed by the **critical current**, which depends on wire diameter and shielding gas.

---

## 2.4 Shielding Gases — The Chemistry Behind the Curtain

| Gas | Type | Behaviour | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Argon (Ar)** | Inert | Stable arc, smooth transfer | Aluminium, Copper, Stainless |
| **Helium (He)** | Inert | Hotter arc, deeper penetration | Aluminium, Magnesium |
| **CO₂** | Active | Deep penetration, spatter | Carbon Steel (MAG) |
| **Ar + 5–25 % CO₂** | Active mix | Balanced | Carbon steel structural |
| **Ar + 1–2 % O₂** | Active mix | Arc stabilisation | Stainless Steel |
| **Ar + 25–75 % He** | Inert mix | Hotter arc | High-speed Aluminium |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Argon is NOT a *“stronger”* shielding gas than CO₂. Argon simply is **inert** (doesn’t react with the molten metal). CO₂ is **active** and oxidises — but this oxide formation actually **increases penetration** in carbon steel.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | **Heat Input** | **kJ/mm** |
| 2 | $\eta$ = **0.70 to 0.85** for GMAW (process efficiency) | — | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V$ = Arc Voltage | Volts (V) | 15 – 40 V |
| 4 | $I$ = Welding Current | Amperes (A) | 50 – 600 A |
| 5 | $S$ = Travel Speed | mm/min | 100 – 1500 mm/min |
| 6 | $D = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot W \cdot \rho \cdot 10^{-3}$ | **Deposition Rate** | **kg/min** |
| 7 | $d$ = Wire diameter | mm | 0.6 – 2.4 mm |
| 8 | $W$ = Wire feed rate | mm/min or m/min | 2 – 20 m/min |
| 9 | $\rho$ = Wire material density | g/cm³ | Steel = **7.85**, Al = **2.70** |
| 10 | $I \approx k \cdot d^{2} \cdot W$ | Current–feed relation | A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Don’t confuse units in Heat Input!** Always convert travel speed from **mm/min** to **mm/s** if you’re using SI consistently. The factor **60/1000** above already handles the conversion: $\dfrac{60 \text{ s/min}}{1000 \text{ J/kJ}} = \dfrac{1}{16.67}$ — but written compactly, $H$ comes out in **kJ/mm**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Robotic GMAW spot-welds car bodies at line speeds of 60+ cars/hour.
- **Shipbuilding:** Two-wire / tandem GMAW achieves deposition rates exceeding **15 kg/hour**.
- **Aerospace:** Pulsed-GMAW welds thin aluminium fuselage skins (2 mm) without burn-through.
- **Pipeline construction:** Field GMAW using **Cellulosic-shielded flux-cored variant** (FCAW-G) is the industry standard.
