# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input in GMAW (KTU Favourite)

**Statement:** The heat input $H$ is the amount of energy supplied per unit length of weld.

**Starting Concept:** Power supplied to the arc is the product of arc voltage and welding current:

$$
P_{\text{arc}} = V \cdot I \quad [\text{Watts} = \text{J/s}]
$$

**Step 1:** Energy supplied per unit length of weld (per millimetre of travel):

Energy per unit length is the power divided by travel speed (with unit conversion):

$$
H_{\text{raw}} = \frac{V \cdot I}{S} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{J/s}}{\text{mm/s}} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}} \right]
$$

**Step 2:** Convert travel speed from mm/min to mm/s by multiplying by a factor of $\dfrac{1}{60}$:

$$
H_{\text{raw}} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\left( \dfrac{S}{60} \right)} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}} \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Apply process efficiency $\eta$ (only $\approx 70-85\,\%$ of electrical energy reaches the weld pool, the rest is lost to spatter, radiation, convection):

$$
H = \eta \cdot \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert Joules to kiloJoules (divide by 1000):

$$
\boxed{\,H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}\,}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):

A GMAW operation is performed on **6 mm mild steel** plate with the following parameters:
- Voltage $V = 28$ V
- Current $I = 250$ A
- Travel speed $S = 350$ mm/min
- Efficiency $\eta = 0.80$

Compute the heat input.

**Substitution:**

$$
H = \frac{0.80 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60}{350 \times 1000}
$$

**Simplify numerator:**

$$
0.80 \times 28 = 22.4
$$

$$
22.4 \times 250 = 5600
$$

$$
5600 \times 60 = 336000
$$

**Simplify denominator:**

$$
350 \times 1000 = 350000
$$

**Final division:**

$$
H = \frac{336000}{350000} = 0.96 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** 0.96 kJ/mm = 960 J/mm. For comparison, a 6 mm plate requires roughly **0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm** for proper fusion. The value lies in the acceptable range — heat input is **adequate** for full penetration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Deposition Rate

**Statement:** The mass of weld metal deposited per minute is the volume swept out by the wire (per minute) times its density.

**Step 1:** Volume of wire consumed per minute = cross-sectional area × length per minute:

$$
V_{\text{wire}} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot W \quad \left[ \text{mm}^{3}/\text{min} \right]
$$

where $W$ is the wire feed rate in mm/min.

**Step 2:** Mass per minute = volume × density (note $\rho$ in g/cm³ = 10⁻³ g/mm³ = 10⁻⁶ kg/mm³... careful):

$$
\text{Mass} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot W \cdot \rho \quad \left[ \text{g/min} \right]
$$

with $\rho$ in g/cm³. Converting to kg/min by multiplying by $10^{-3}$:

$$
D = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot W \cdot \rho \cdot 10^{-3} \quad \left[ \text{kg/min} \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Numerical Example:

A 1.2 mm steel wire ($\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³) is fed at $W = 6$ m/min = 6000 mm/min.

$$
D = \frac{\pi (1.2)^{2}}{4} \cdot 6000 \cdot 7.85 \cdot 10^{-3}
$$

**Compute the cross-section:**

$$
\frac{\pi (1.44)}{4} = 1.131 \;\; \text{mm}^{2}
$$

**Compute volume per minute:**

$$
1.131 \times 6000 = 6786 \;\; \text{mm}^{3}/\text{min} = 6.786 \;\; \text{cm}^{3}/\text{min}
$$

**Compute mass per minute:**

$$
D = 6.786 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0533 \;\; \text{kg/min} = 3.2 \;\; \text{kg/hr}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Deposition rate is the **rate-limiting metric** for industrial productivity. Tandem-GMAW (two wires) doubles this figure.

---

## 3.3 The Current–Wire Feed–Diameter Relationship (Empirical)

The self-regulating characteristic produces a near-linear empirical relationship:

$$
I = a + b \cdot d^{2} \cdot W
$$

For **steel with Ar/CO₂ shielding**, a reasonable rule of thumb is:

$$
I \approx 60 \cdot d^{2} \cdot W \quad \text{(approximate, $d$ in mm, $W$ in m/min)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU problems may give you a target current and ask you to find wire feed speed — use this empirical form when no table is provided.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Metal Transfer Modes

| Parameter | Short-Circuit | Globular | Spray | Pulsed Spray |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Current** | Low (50–150 A) | Medium (150–200 A) | High (200–500 A) | Pulsed peak |
| **Voltage** | 18–22 V | 22–28 V | 28–35 V | 22–30 V (avg) |
| **Droplet Size** | > wire diameter | 2–3× wire diameter | < wire diameter | One droplet / pulse |
| **Heat Input** | Low | Medium | High | Low (but deep pen.) |
| **Spatter** | Moderate | **High** | Negligible | Low |
| **Position** | All positions | Flat only | Flat / Horizontal | All positions |
| **Material Thickness** | Thin (<3 mm) | Medium (3–6 mm) | Thick (>3 mm) | Thin to medium |
| **Preferred Gas** | Ar + CO₂ | 100 % CO₂ | Ar-rich (≥ 80 %) | Ar-rich |
| **Industrial Use** | Auto body sheet | Bridge fabrication | Pressure vessels | Aerospace Al |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Students often write that **“higher current = more heat = more penetration, therefore spray is best.”** This is *only true for flat position*. In **vertical/overhead**, spray is unusable because gravity carries molten metal out of the joint — that’s when **pulsed or short-circuit** modes are mandatory.

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Green path** → Welding process (arc melts metal, weld pool forms, solidifies as bead).

## 4.3 The Self-Regulating Arc Mechanism (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> WireAdvances
    WireAdvances --> ContactWithPool: Wire touches weld pool
    ContactWithPool --> ShortCircuit: Current spikes to high value
    ShortCircuit --> PinchForceBuilds: Pinch effect shears droplet
    PinchForceBuilds --> ArcReignited: Bridge breaks, arc re-strikes
    ArcReignited --> WireAdvances: Wire feeds again, cycle restarts
    ArcReignited --> VoltageRises: If arc gets long
    VoltageRises --> CurrentDrops: CV source responds
    CurrentDrops --> MeltingRateFalls: Wire melts less
    MeltingRateFalls --> ArcShortens: Arc returns to setpoint
    ArcShortens --> WireAdvances
```

> [!NOTE]
> This state diagram is the **cyclic physical model** of short-circuit transfer mode, occurring at **20 – 200 Hz** during real welding.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between MIG and MAG welding. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer (Concise, 3-mark format):**

| Feature | MIG | MAG |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | Metal **Inert** Gas | Metal **Active** Gas |
| Shielding Gas | Inert (Argon, Helium) | Active (CO₂, O₂) or Ar mixtures |
| Arc Stability | Very stable | Slightly less stable |
| Spatter | Low | Higher |
| Penetration | Moderate | Deep (CO₂) |
| Application | Aluminium vessel fabrication in aerospace | Automobile chassis welding (structural steel) |

> [!TIP]
> **Awarding Rule:** A clear distinction between **inert** vs **active** gas usage, plus *one specific industrial application* each, fetches full 3 marks.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List the four modes of metal transfer in GMAW. Identify the mode that produces the highest heat input and the mode best suited for vertical-up welding.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Short-circuit (Dip) transfer
2. Globular transfer
3. Spray transfer
4. Pulsed-spray transfer

- **Highest heat input:** **Spray transfer** (highest current, 200–500 A).
- **Best for vertical-up welding:** **Short-circuit (dip) transfer** — low heat input prevents the molten pool from sagging under gravity.

> [!TIP]
> Always write the *reason* (low heat input / gravity resistance) — naming the mode alone gets 2 of 3 marks; the justification gets the third.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with KTU Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of Gas Metal Arc Welding. Discuss the various modes of metal transfer and the role of shielding gases. List at least four advantages and two limitations of GMAW.**

#### **Part (a) — Working Principle, Equipment & Schematic (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Process Definition (1 mark):**
GMAW is a fusion welding process in which the heat of an electric arc between a continuously fed consumable wire electrode and the workpiece melts the base metal, producing coalescence. Shielding is provided exclusively by an externally supplied gas (no flux).

**Step 2 — Equipment (2 marks):**
- Constant-voltage (CV) DC rectifier power source
- Wire-feed unit with drive rolls (constant speed motor)
- Welding torch with contact tip + gas nozzle
- Shielding gas cylinder + regulator + flow meter
- Ground clamp and cable return

**Step 3 — Working Sequence (2 marks):**
1. Trigger pulled → wire feed starts, shielding gas begins to flow.
2. Wire (live) contacts workpiece → short circuit → arc strikes.
3. Arc melts wire tip; molten droplets transfer across arc into weld pool.
4. CV source self-regulates arc length: long arc → I drops → wire melts slower → arc shortens.
5. Travelling torch leaves a solidified weld bead behind.

**Step 4 — Schematic Description (2 marks):** *(Refer to Block Diagram 4.1.)*
Show functional block diagram with: power source → torch → arc → workpiece → ground return; cylinder → regulator → flow meter → nozzle; spool → feeder → torch.

#### **Part (b) — Metal Transfer Modes, Shielding Gases, Advantages, Limitations (7 Marks)**

**Modes of Metal Transfer (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.4 matrix.)*
- **Short-circuit:** low voltage, low current, low heat — for thin sheets, all positions.
- **Globular:** medium current, CO₂ — spattery, **not preferred in production**.
- **Spray:** high current, Argon-rich — deep pen., flat position, spatter-free.
- **Pulsed spray:** pulsing peak current — combines low heat input with deep pen., all positions.

**Shielding Gases (2 marks):**
- **Inert gases (Ar, He):** stable arc, no chemical reaction, used for Al, SS, Cu.
- **Active gases (CO₂, O₂, Ar–CO₂ mixes):** promote wetting and deeper penetration in carbon steel; oxidising.
- *Gas selection rule:* Aluminium → pure Ar; Carbon steel → Ar+CO₂; Stainless → Ar+1–2% O₂.

**Advantages (1 mark — list 4):**
1. Continuous welding — no stops to change electrode.
2. High deposition rate (3–5 kg/hr single wire).
3. Spatter-free (in spray mode) → less post-weld cleaning.
4. Adaptable to robotics / automation.
5. Welds a wide range of metals and thicknesses.
6. No slag (compared to SMAW).

**Limitations (1 mark — list 2):**
1. Cannot be used in windy / outdoor conditions — shielding gas disperses.
2. Equipment cost is high; requires trained operator.
3. Sensitive to gun manipulation and stickout variation.
4. Gas contamination causes porosity.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**In a GMAW operation on a 6 mm mild-steel plate, the following data are given: arc voltage = 28 V, welding current = 250 A, travel speed = 350 mm/min. Assuming a process efficiency of 0.80, compute the heat input. Comment on whether this heat input is sufficient for full penetration of the 6 mm plate. Also explain the effect of shielding gas composition on weld penetration.**

#### **Part (a) — Heat Input Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Formula (2 marks):**

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$
H = \frac{0.80 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60}{350 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Step-by-step arithmetic (2 marks):**

$$
= \frac{0.80 \times 28 \times 15000}{350000}
$$

$$
= \frac{336000}{350000}
$$

$$
\boxed{H = 0.96 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
For 6 mm mild steel, the recommended heat input range is approximately **0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm** for single-pass full penetration. Since 0.96 kJ/mm lies in this range, **the heat input is sufficient** for full penetration of the 6 mm plate.

#### **Part (b) — Effect of Shielding Gas on Weld Penetration (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Inert vs Active (2 marks):**
- **Inert gases (Ar, He):** Provide a stable, constricted arc column; Helium’s higher thermal conductivity produces a **wider, deeper** penetration profile than Argon.
- **Active gases (CO₂):** Dissociate in the arc, absorbing large amounts of heat; the **recombination of CO₂ at the molten pool front** releases this energy into the workpiece, producing **deeper penetration** in carbon steel.

**Step 2 — Gas mixtures (2 marks):**
- **Ar + 25 % CO₂:** Industry-standard for carbon steel — combines stability of Ar with deeper pen. of CO₂.
- **Ar + 1–2 % O₂:** Used for stainless steel — improves wetting and bead shape without excessive oxidation.
- **Ar + 25–75 % He:** Used for high-speed aluminium welding — high thermal conductivity → deep pen.

**Step 3 — Mechanism of penetration change (2 marks):**
- Higher CO₂ content → higher arc energy density at the workpiece → deeper finger-like penetration.
- Pure Argon → broad, shallow, “wine-glass” penetration profile in steel.
- For aluminium, pure Ar is mandatory because active gases cause **oxide entrapment**.

**Step 4 — Practical rule (1 mark):**
*“For deeper penetration in carbon steel, increase CO₂ fraction; for smoother bead and broader profile, increase Ar fraction.”*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on GMAW Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit mix-up in Heat Input** — Forgetting the **60/1000** factor is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. Always state the formula *first*, then plug values.
> 2. **Confusing MIG and MAG** — Examiners *expect* a clear statement: “MIG = Inert gas; MAG = Active gas.” Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Naming metal transfer modes without explaining current/voltage ranges** — A bare list fetches only 1 of 3 marks. Always mention the *enabling condition* (e.g., “spray — above critical current with Argon-rich gas”).
> 4. **Wrong polarity** — Many students write GMAW uses DCEN. **It is DCEP** for steel. Polarity reversal is a 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **Forgetting that the efficiency η in heat-input formula is 70–85 %, not 100 %** — Using η = 1.0 inflates the answer.
> 6. **No schematic in 14-mark answers** — Even a hand-drawn block diagram of the GMAW setup is mandatory; absence = 2-mark deduction.
> 7. **Confusing “deposition rate” (kg/min) with “wire feed rate” (m/min)** — They are *related* but *not the same*. Always carry units in your answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this list. Memorise it. You will not regret it for KTU ESE.**

- ✅ GMAW = **Gas Metal Arc Welding**; same as MIG/MAG depending on gas.
- ✅ Electrode is **continuous consumable wire** (no flux, no slag).
- ✅ Polarity for steel: **DCEP (Electrode Positive)**.
- ✅ Power source: **Constant Voltage (CV)**, flat characteristic — *not* constant current.
- ✅ Shielding gas is **mandatory**; gas flow typically **8–20 L/min**.
- ✅ **Four metal transfer modes** in order of increasing current:
  1. **Short-circuit (dip)** — 18–22 V, low I, all positions, thin sheet.
  2. **Globular** — CO₂, spattery, flat only.
  3. **Spray** — Ar-rich, high I/V, deep pen., flat position, spatter-free.
  4. **Pulsed spray** — pulsing peak current, all positions, premium aluminium/SS.
- ✅ **MIG** uses inert gases (Ar, He) for Al, SS, Cu.
- ✅ **MAG** uses active gases (CO₂, Ar–CO₂) for carbon steel.
- ✅ **Heat Input formula:** $\;H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}\;$ kJ/mm, with $\eta = 0.70 - 0.85$.
- ✅ **Deposition rate formula:** $\;D = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot W \cdot \rho \cdot 10^{-3}\;$ kg/min.
- ✅ **Deposition efficiency of GMAW:** ~ **90–95 %** (higher than SMAW’s 60–70 %).
- ✅ **Self-regulating arc principle** is what makes CV source mandatory.
- ✅ **Critical current** is the threshold above which spray transfer begins.
- ✅ **Cannot weld outdoors in wind** — gas shield disperses.
- ✅ **GMAW is fully automatable** — backbone of robotic welding in automotive.
- ✅ **Compare to SMAW:** GMAW has higher deposition, less slag, no electrode change; SMAW is portable and cheaper.
- ✅ **Heat input range for 6 mm mild steel** ≈ 0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm single pass.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Brazing - Soldering - Adhesive Bonding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) is an advanced arc welding process in which a constricted arc is generated by forcing the arc plasma through a copper nozzle with a small orifice. A separate shielding gas (typically Argon) is used to protect the weld pool. The high-velocity ionized gas (plasma) reaches temperatures of **20,000 K to 50,000 K**, enabling deep penetration welds at high travel speeds.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a garden hose with a thumb pressed against the opening — the water shoots out faster and more concentrated. PAW works the same way: by squeezing the electric arc through a tiny hole, we get a super-hot, focused jet of plasma that melts metal like a laser knife.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from TIG:** In TIG, the arc is "free" between tungsten and workpiece. In PAW, the arc is *constricted* through a nozzle orifice, making it more energetic, stable, and deeper penetrating.

### Two Modes of PAW Operation

| Mode | Description | Penetration |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Melt-in Mode (Non-keyhole)** | Low current (~1–150 A), similar to TIG, used for thin sheets | Shallow |
| **Keyhole Mode** | High current (>150 A), arc pierces through the plate, metal flows around | Full penetration, square butt joints possible |

---

## 1.2 Thermit Welding (Thermite Welding)

**Formal Definition:**
A fusion welding process in which heat is produced by the **exothermic chemical reaction** between a mixture of metallic oxide (typically **Fe₂O₃**) and a reducing agent (aluminum powder). The reaction temperature reaches approximately **2500 °C**, enough to melt the steel filler.

$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Heat} \; (\approx 850 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine pouring molten iron from a crucible into a carefully built sand mould around two rail ends — the super-hot liquid iron fills the gap and fuses the two pieces. It is essentially *pouring a weld* rather than depositing it with an arc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic Application:** Joining railway tracks, large steel shafts, and repairing broken machinery in the field (e.g., crankshaft repair). Discovered by **Hans Goldschmidt (1895)**.

---

## 1.3 Friction Welding (FRW)

**Formal Definition:**
A solid-state welding process in which heat is generated by **mechanically induced frictional rubbing** between two workpieces, one held stationary and the other rotated under axial pressure. When the interface reaches a plastic state, the rotation is stopped and forge pressure is applied to complete the bond.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Rub your hands together very fast — they get hot! Now press them together tightly while still warm. They "weld" together. Industrial friction welding uses this same principle but with controlled pressure, rotation, and time.

### Types of Friction Welding
- **Continuous Drive Friction Welding** — one part rotated by a motor
- **Inertia (Flywheel) Friction Welding** — kinetic energy of a flywheel is converted to heat
- **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** — non-consumable rotating tool traverses the joint

---

## 1.4 Electroslag Welding (ESW)

**Formal Definition:**
A vertical-up welding process in which the weld pool is kept molten by the **resistance heat** generated as electric current passes through a molten slag pool (typically CaF₂-based flux). Consumable wire electrodes are fed into the slag. No arc exists in steady-state operation — the slag itself conducts current.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a Jacuzzi of molten slag that conducts electricity. As the wire dips in, it melts. The heat from the current keeps everything molten, and the weld pool rises upward between two water-cooled copper shoes that contain the molten metal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Use:** Welding very thick plates (25 mm to 450 mm) in a single pass — used for ship hulls, pressure vessels, and thick-walled cylinders. Vertical orientation only.

---

## 1.5 Ultrasonic Welding (USW)

**Formal Definition:**
A solid-state welding process that uses **high-frequency vibratory energy (20–40 kHz)** under static pressure to produce a weld. Heat is generated by combined interfacial friction and plastic deformation — no melting occurs.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine vibrating a stack of papers while pressing down — at the right frequency they stick together. Now replace paper with thin metal foils, and the atomic bonds form. The vibration literally shakes atoms across the interface into a shared bond.

**Application:** Joining dissimilar metals (Al–Cu, Al–steel), foil-to-foil, wire-to-terminal in electronics, packaging industry.

---

## 1.6 Electron Beam Welding (EBW)

**Formal Definition:**
A fusion welding process in which a **high-velocity, focused beam of electrons** is accelerated by voltages of **30–200 kV** and directed at the workpiece. Kinetic energy is converted to heat upon impact. The process must be performed in a **vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr)** to prevent electron scattering.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Like a magnifying glass focusing sunlight to burn a leaf — but using a beam of electrons instead of light, and in a vacuum chamber to keep the beam sharp.

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Density:** EBW delivers 10⁶ to 10⁷ W/mm² — orders of magnitude higher than arc welding. Produces very narrow, deep, parallel-sided welds with minimal HAZ.

---

## 1.7 Laser Beam Welding (LBW)

**Formal Definition:**
A fusion welding process in which a **coherent, monochromatic, collimated beam of photons** (typically from CO₂ or Nd:YAG fiber lasers) is focused to a small spot, producing localized heating. Can be performed in air for high-power CO₂ lasers or with shielding gas.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A surgical laser that cuts tissue — replace tissue with metal, and you have a high-precision welding/cutting tool. The beam doesn't need vacuum (unlike EBW) so it can be used in continuous production lines.

---

## 1.8 Resistance Welding (RW)

**Formal Definition:**
A group of welding processes in which coalescence is produced by the **heat obtained from resistance of the workpiece to the flow of electric current**, with pressure simultaneously applied. The heat is governed by **Joule's Law**:

$$Q = I^2 R t$$

where **I = current (A)**, **R = resistance (Ω)**, **t = time (s)**, **Q = heat (J)**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Like the heating element in a toaster — pass large current through a high-resistance point, and it gets hot enough to glow. In resistance welding, the interface between two pieces is the "high-resistance heating element."

### Types of Resistance Welding
- **Spot Welding (RSW)** — most common; nugget weld between overlapping sheets
- **Projection Welding** — current concentrated at pre-formed dimples
- **Seam Welding** — continuous roller electrodes produce leak-tight joints
- **Butt Welding** — end-to-end joining of wires, bars, tubes
- **Flash Welding** — arc + resistance; used for rails and large sections

---

## 1.9 Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)

**Formal Definition:**
The region of the base metal adjacent to the weld whose **microstructure and mechanical properties have been altered by the heat of welding** but which has not been melted. Three sub-zones exist:
- **Coarse Grain HAZ** (nearest fusion line, >1100 °C peak)
- **Fine Grain HAZ** (recrystallized, ~900–1100 °C)
- **Intercritical HAZ** (~700–900 °C, partially transformed)

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a campfire with three zones — the embers (HAZ), the flames (weld pool), and the untouched wood (parent metal). The embers are altered but not consumed.

---

## 1.10 Weldability

**Formal Definition:**
Weldability is the **capacity of a material to be welded under the imposed fabrication conditions** into a specific, suitable structure that performs satisfactorily in the intended service (ISO 581:1980).

Carbon equivalent (CE) formula (IIW):
$$\text{CE} = \text{C} + \frac{\text{Mn}}{6} + \frac{\text{Cr+Mo+V}}{5} + \frac{\text{Ni+Cu}}{15}$$

| CE Value | Weldability |
|----------|-------------|
| < 0.35 | Excellent |
| 0.35–0.55 | Good (preheat may help) |
| > 0.55 | Poor (preheat + PWHT essential) |

---

## 1.11 Residual Stresses and Distortion

**Formal Definition:**
- **Residual Stress:** Internal stresses locked in a structure after welding due to **non-uniform heating and cooling**.
- **Distortion:** Visible shape change (warping, bending, twisting, angular, buckling) resulting from the same thermal cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation (Transverse Shrinkage for single-pass butt):**
> $$\delta = \frac{0.2 \cdot A}{t} \quad \text{(mm)}$$
> where A = cross-sectional area of weld (mm²), t = plate thickness (mm).

---

## 1.12 Defects in Welding

**Common Defects:** Cracks (hot, cold, crater, HAZ), porosity, slag inclusion, lack of fusion, lack of penetration, undercut, spatter, distortion, lamellar tearing.

---

## 1.13 Brazing

**Formal Definition:**
A joining process in which a **filler metal (with melting point > 450 °C but below the solidus of the base metal)** is heated to its melting temperature and distributed by capillary action between closely fitted faying surfaces. The base metal does **not melt**.

---

## 1.14 Soldering

**Formal Definition:**
A joining process similar to brazing, but the filler metal (solder) has a **melting point below 450 °C**. Common solders: Sn-Pb (60-40, 63-37 eutectic), lead-free (Sn-Ag-Cu, SAC).

---

## 1.15 Adhesive Bonding

**Formal Definition:**
A joining process in which an **adhesive** (polymer — epoxy, phenolic, acrylic, polyurethane) is applied between two adherends, and adhesion is achieved through chemical bonding, mechanical interlocking, and/or van der Waals forces. Curing may be at room temperature or elevated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Plasma Arc Welding — Process Mechanism

**Operating Principle — Step-by-step Logic:**
1. A non-consumable tungsten electrode is recessed inside a copper nozzle.
2. Pilot arc is struck between electrode and nozzle using high-frequency discharge.
3. Plasma gas (Ar) flows through the nozzle, gets ionized, and the arc is constricted through the orifice.
4. Constricted plasma jet (velocity ~Mach 1) impinges on the workpiece.
5. Separate shielding gas (Ar/Ar + H₂) flows through an outer nozzle to protect the weld.

**Why the arc is constricted:** A small orifice (≈2–4 mm) produces a **thermal pinch effect** — radial heat is trapped, increasing current density and arc temperature.

### Process Parameters of PAW
- Pilot arc current: 5–20 A
- Main arc current: 100–400 A
- Plasma gas flow: 1–8 L/min
- Orifice diameter: 2.8–4.8 mm
- Travel speed: 0.1–1.5 m/min
- Stand-off distance: 5–12 mm

---

## 2.2 Thermit Welding — Reaction Analysis

**Reaction Products and Energy:**
- Reaction enthalpy: **ΔH ≈ −850 kJ/mol**
- Steel temperature produced: **~2500 °C**
- Slag (Al₂O₃) floats on top, tapped off; molten steel fills the joint.

**Process Steps:**
1. Ends to be joined are aligned with a gap of 25–50 mm.
2. Sand or ceramic mould is built around the joint.
3. Thermit mixture (Fe₂O₃ + Al + alloying elements) is placed in a crucible above.
4. Reaction is ignited (magnesium ribbon + barium peroxide).
5. Molten steel flows down and fills the mould, fuses with parent metal.
6. After solidification, riser and excess metal are ground off.

---

## 2.3 Friction Welding — Thermal Cycle

**Phases of Friction Welding:**
1. **Phase I — Initial Rubbing:** Surfaces rub, asperities deform, heat begins.
2. **Phase II — Steady State:** Softening rate = extrusion rate; flash forms.
3. **Phase III — Forging (Upset):** Rotation stops, axial pressure increases → strong bond.

**Heat Generation Equation:**
$$Q = \mu \cdot F \cdot v \cdot t$$
where μ = coefficient of friction, F = axial force, v = rubbing velocity, t = time.

---

## 2.4 Electroslag Welding — Process Theory

**Why the arc disappears:** Once a thin slag pool forms, the current density through the molten CaF₂-based flux is insufficient to sustain an arc — the current flows by **ionic conduction through the slag**. Heat = I²R_ slag.

**Process Steps:**
1. Vertical gap (≈25–40 mm) between plates; water-cooled copper shoes on each side.
2. Wire electrode (or multiple) is fed into the gap.
3. Arc initially ignites; arc melts the flux and forms a slag pool (~1800–2000 °C).
4. Wire dips into slag, melts, droplets fall into weld pool below.
5. Weld pool solidifies against copper shoes; process moves upward.
6. Final weld is **large-grained**, requires **normalizing** to refine.

---

## 2.5 Ultrasonic Welding — Bonding Theory

**Mechanism:** At the interface, relative motion (vibration amplitude ~20–50 μm) combined with static clamping force breaks down surface oxides and contaminants. Clean metal-to-metal contact allows solid-state diffusion bonding.

**Process Parameters:**
- Frequency: 20–40 kHz (typically 20 kHz)
- Amplitude: 20–50 μm
- Clamping pressure: 0.5–10 MPa
- Weld time: 0.1–2 s

---

## 2.6 Electron Beam Welding — Beam Theory

**Electron Generation & Acceleration:**
- Filament (W or LaB₆) heated → thermionic emission of electrons.
- Electrons accelerated by **30–200 kV** potential difference.
- Magnetic lens focuses beam; spot size ~0.1–0.5 mm.
- Power density: **10⁶–10⁷ W/mm²**.
- Vacuum: 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr.

**Depth-to-Width Ratio:** EBW welds can achieve **30:1** (vs 1:1 for arc welding).

**Joint Types in EBW:** Square butt joints without edge preparation even in 50 mm thick plates.

---

## 2.7 Laser Beam Welding — Beam Characteristics

**Laser Types Used:**
- **CO₂ laser:** λ = 10.6 μm, power up to 25 kW, requires gas shielding
- **Nd:YAG laser:** λ = 1.06 μm, fiber-deliverable, good for metals
- **Fiber / Disc lasers:** modern high-efficiency alternatives

**Key Parameters:** Power (W), travel speed, focus spot size, focal position, beam mode (TEM₀₀ vs TEM₀₁).

---

## 2.8 Resistance Welding — Deep Theory

### 2.8.1 Generation of Heat at the Joint

Heat is generated at **three resistance locations**:
- **R₁**: Contact resistance between electrodes and top sheet
- **R₂**: Resistance of the top sheet
- **R₃**: Contact resistance between faying surfaces (highest)
- **R₄**: Resistance of the bottom sheet
- **R₅**: Contact resistance between electrode and bottom sheet

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_5$$

The bulk of the useful heat is generated at **R₃ (faying interface)**.

### 2.8.2 Heat Balance Equation (Most Important for Numericals)

$$\boxed{Q = I^2 R t = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T + Q_{loss}}$$

For spot welding, simplified as:

$$I^2 R t = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot t_w \cdot \rho \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

where:
- d = nugget diameter (m)
- t_w = sheet thickness (m)
- ρ = density of metal (kg/m³)
- C_p = specific heat (J/kg·K)
- T_m = melting point (K)
- T_0 = ambient temperature (K)

### 2.8.3 Process Parameters in Spot Welding
- Welding current: 5000–20,000 A
- Electrode force: 2–8 kN
- Weld time: 0.1–1.5 s (60–600 cycles)
- Hold time: 0.1–0.5 s
- Electrode material: Class 2 (Cu-Cr-Zr), Class 11 (Cu-W)

---

## 2.9 Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) — Detailed Sub-zones

| Sub-zone | Peak Temp (°C) | Microstructure Change | Risk |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|------|
| Coarse Grain | 1100–1450 | Grain growth, brittle | Hot cracking |
| Fine Grain | 900–1100 | Recrystallized refined grains | Toughness drop |
| Intercritical | 700–900 | Partial austenitization | Soft zones |
| Subcritical | 600–700 | Tempering only | Tempered martensite embrittlement |

---

## 2.10 Weldability of Metals — Analytical View

**Weldability Index:** Carbon content is the single most important factor for steels.

- **Low C steel (< 0.25% C):** Easily welded, no preheat.
- **Medium C steel (0.25–0.5% C):** Preheating at 150–300 °C needed.
- **High C steel (> 0.5% C):** Poor weldability, post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) mandatory.

**Non-ferrous Weldability:**
- **Aluminum:** High thermal conductivity → requires high heat input; oxide layer (Al₂O₃, MP 2050 °C) needs AC TIG or flux.
- **Copper:** High thermal conductivity, low resistivity → difficult in resistance welding; laser/EBW preferred.
- **Stainless Steel (Austenitic):** Good weldability; risk of sensitization (Cr carbide precipitation) at 450–850 °C → use 321/347 stabilized grades or L grades (low C).
- **Titanium:** Excellent with EBW/GTAW; reactive at high T → inert atmosphere essential.

---

## 2.11 Residual Stresses and Distortion — Mathematical Treatment

**Longitudinal Shrinkage Force:**
$$F = E \cdot A \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$
where E = Young's modulus, A = cross-section, α = thermal expansion coefficient, ΔT = temperature drop.

**Types of Distortion:**
1. **Transverse shrinkage** — perpendicular to weld axis
2. **Longitudinal shrinkage** — along weld axis
3. **Angular distortion** — V-groove butt joints
4. **Bowing distortion** — longitudinal bending
5. **Buckling distortion** — thin sheets
6. **Rotational distortion** — in pipe circumferential welds

**Control Methods:**
- Proper joint design (low restraint)
- Balanced welding (alternating sides, backstep sequence)
- Preheating
- Peening
- Use of strongbacks / fixturing
- PWHT (stress relief at 600–650 °C for steels)

---

## 2.12 Welding Defects — Classification

| Defect | Cause | Prevention |
|--------|-------|------------|
| Porosity | Trapped gas (H, N, CO) | Clean base metal, proper shielding gas |
| Slag inclusion | Incomplete slag removal | Proper cleaning between passes |
| Lack of fusion | Insufficient heat input | Higher current, slower travel |
| Lack of penetration | Joint design / heat | Larger root gap, higher current |
| Undercut | Excessive current, fast travel | Reduce current, correct technique |
| Crater crack | Improper crater filling | Use crater-fill function |
| HAZ cracking | Hydrogen, restraint | Preheat, low H consumables |
| Lamellar tearing | Through-thickness weakness in rolled plate | Z-grade plate, buttering |
| Spatter | High current, long arc | Lower current, shorter arc |

---

## 2.13 Brazing — Capillary Action Theory

**Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law):**
$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

where γ = surface tension, θ = contact angle, r = capillary gap radius.

**Joint Clearance:** 0.025–0.1 mm for capillary brazing; 0.5–1 mm for large-fill (paste braze).

**Filler Metal Examples:** BCuP (Cu-P), BAg (Ag-based), BAlSi (for Al), BNi (Ni-based for superalloys).

---

## 2.14 Soldering — Key Concepts

- **Rosin-core solder:** Self-fluxing for electronics
- **Liquidus of 60-40 Sn-Pb:** 238 °C
- **Eutectic 63-37:** 183 °C (sharp melting)
- **Lead-free SAC 305:** Liquidus 217 °C, solidus 217 °C
- **Wave soldering, reflow soldering** — common mass-production methods

---

## 2.15 Adhesive Bonding — Engineering View

**Adhesion Theories:**
1. **Mechanical interlocking** — adhesive flows into surface pores
2. **Adsorption (wetting) theory** — molecular contact
3. **Chemical bonding** — covalent/ionic at interface
4. **Electrostatic** — charge transfer
5. **Diffusion** — polymer chain interpenetration

**Common Adhesives:**
- **Epoxy:** Highest strength, room-temp or heat cure
- **Phenolic:** High-temp, structural
- **Polyurethane:** Flexible, good for plastics
- **Acrylic:** Fast curing, methyl methacrylate based
- **Silicone:** Heat resistant, flexible
- **Anaerobic:** Cures in absence of air (threadlockers)

---

## 2.16 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = I^2 R t$ | Joule's heat (resistance welding) | J |
| 2 | $Q = 0.24 I^2 R t$ | Heat in calories | cal |
| 3 | $H = I^2 R$ | Heat rate (power) | W |
| 4 | $T_w = m C_p \Delta T$ | Energy to heat metal | J |
| 5 | $\delta = 0.2 A/t$ | Transverse shrinkage | mm |
| 6 | $\text{CE} = \text{C} + \text{Mn}/6 + (\text{Cr+Mo+V})/5 + (\text{Ni+Cu})/15$ | Carbon equivalent | % |
| 7 | $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta / (\rho g r)$ | Capillary rise (brazing) | m |
| 8 | $\Delta T = 0.7\,T_m$ | Approx cooling range (HAZ) | K |
| 9 | $P = \eta V I$ | Arc power (η = 0.7–0.85) | W |
| 10 | $Q = \mu F v t$ | Friction heat | J |
| 11 | $d_n = f(I, t, F, \text{material})$ | Nugget diameter | mm |
| 12 | $E_b = 0.827\, V$ | Butt-weld electrical efficiency | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for spot welding current:** Approximately **10,000 A per 1 mm of sheet thickness** for mild steel. For aluminum use ~1.5× this value due to higher thermal conductivity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Generation in Resistance Spot Welding

We want to derive the relationship between welding current, time, and the resulting nugget size. We apply **Joule's Law** and equate it to the **sensible heat** required to melt a cylindrical nugget.

### Step 1 — Joule's Heat Generated

The heat generated during the weld time is governed by Joule's Law:

$$Q_{\text{gen}} = I^2 R t$$

### Step 2 — Heat Required to Form the Nugget

The nugget is a cylinder of diameter **d** and thickness approximately equal to the sheet thickness **t_w** (we consider the bottom sheet for simplicity).

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot \left(\frac{\pi d^2}{4}\right) t_w$$

Heat required to raise this mass from room temperature to melting point:

$$Q_{\text{req}} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

Substituting m:

$$Q_{\text{req}} = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot t_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

### Step 3 — Equating Heat Generated to Heat Required

Assuming all generated heat contributes to nugget formation (ignoring losses for ideal case):

$$I^2 R t = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot t_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

### Step 4 — Solving for Nugget Diameter d

$$d^2 = \frac{4 I^2 R t}{\pi \, t_w \, \rho \, C_p \, (T_m - T_0)}$$

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 I^2 R t}{\pi \, t_w \, \rho \, C_p \, (T_m - T_0)}}$$

$$\boxed{d = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{I^2 R t}{\pi \, t_w \, \rho \, C_p \, (T_m - T_0)}}}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Implications

Differentiating with respect to t and I, we see:

$$\frac{\partial d}{\partial I} > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial d}{\partial t} > 0$$

> Both **current** and **weld time** increase the nugget size. In practice, **current has a much stronger effect** because d ∝ I, but d ∝ √t.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Style (Resistance Spot Welding)

> **Problem:**
> Two 1.5 mm thick low-carbon steel sheets are to be spot welded. The current is 10,000 A and the weld time is 0.2 s. The total resistance at the interface is **120 μΩ**. Density = 7850 kg/m³, C_p = 660 J/(kg·K), T_m = 1500 °C, T_0 = 30 °C. Calculate:
> 1. The heat generated in Joules.
> 2. The minimum nugget diameter that can be formed.

### Solution — Step 1: Heat Generated

$$Q = I^2 R t$$
$$Q = (10{,}000)^2 \times (120 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.2$$
$$Q = 10^8 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.2$$
$$\boxed{Q = 2400 \text{ J}}$$

### Solution — Step 2: Nugget Diameter

Using:
$$I^2 R t = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot t_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

$$2400 = 7850 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 660 \times (1500 - 30)$$

$$2400 = 7850 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \times 0.0015 \times 660 \times 1470$$

Compute the constant term:
$$7850 \times 0.0015 \times 660 \times 1470 = 11{,}428{,}297.5 \approx 1.143 \times 10^7$$

$$\frac{\pi d^2}{4} \times 1.143 \times 10^7 = 2400$$

$$\frac{\pi d^2}{4} = 2.1 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$d^2 = \frac{4 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi} = 2.673 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 16.35 \text{ mm}}$$

> This is greater than the standard $d_{\min} = 5 \sqrt{t_w} \approx 6.12$ mm rule (good weld) — the weld is well above acceptable.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Heat Distribution and Losses

> **Problem:**
> In a spot weld, 2400 J is generated. The nugget absorbs 1800 J, electrode cooling losses are 360 J, and radiation/convection losses are 240 J. Determine the thermal efficiency.

### Solution

$$\eta = \frac{Q_{\text{nugget}}}{Q_{\text{total}}} \times 100 = \frac{1800}{2400} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 75\%}$$

> Typical efficiency in RSW = 60–85%.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Advanced Welding Processes

| Parameter | PAW | Thermit | Friction | ESW | USW | EBW | LBW |
|-----------|-----|---------|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Heat Source | Constricted arc | Chemical reaction | Friction | Slag resistance | Ultrasonic vibration | Electron beam | Photon beam |
| Temp (°C) | 20,000+ | 2500 | 1100–1300 | 1800–2000 | < Melting pt | Surface 2500+ | Surface 2500+ |
| Vacuum | No | No | No | No | No | **Yes** | No |
| Atmosphere | Shielding gas | Open | Open | Open | Open | Vacuum | Air or shield |
| Max thickness (single pass) | 12 mm | Any | 100 mm | 450 mm | 3 mm | 200+ mm | 25 mm |
| HAZ | Small | Large | **None (solid state)** | Very large | **None** | Very small | Small |
| Joint type | Butt, lap | Butt only | Butt (round) | Butt (vertical) | Lap, spot | Butt, lap | Butt, lap |
| Material range | Stainless, Ti, Ni | Steel only | Most metals, dissimilar | Carbon steel | Foils, thin sheets | Refractory metals | Most metals |
| Distortion | Low | Moderate | None | High | None | Low | Low |
| Power density (W/mm²) | 10⁴ | — | — | — | 10² | 10⁶–10⁷ | 10⁵–10⁶ |

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Capillary Pressure in Brazing (Lap Joint)

For a brazed lap joint with a fillet of radius r, capillary pressure pulling the filler in is given by:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{h}$$

where h is the joint gap, γ the surface tension of molten filler, θ the contact angle.

### Step-by-step:
1. A liquid column in a thin gap forms a meniscus with contact angle θ.
2. The Laplace pressure across a curved interface is:
   $$\Delta P = \gamma \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$$
3. For a thin parallel gap, R₁ = h/2 (vertical) and R₂ → ∞ (along the joint).
4. Substituting: $\Delta P = 2\gamma \cos\theta / h$
5. Capillary force pulls filler into the joint, hence the **0.025–0.1 mm clearance** is critical.

---

## 3.6 Heat Flow in Welding — Rosenthal's Solution (Conceptual)

Rosenthal (1941) solved the 2D heat flow equation for a moving point source on a thick plate:

$$T - T_0 = \frac{q}{2\pi k} \cdot e^{-v(x+v\tau)/2a} \cdot K_0\left(\frac{vR}{2a}\right)$$

where q = heat input rate, k = thermal conductivity, v = travel speed, a = diffusivity, R = distance, K₀ = modified Bessel function of second kind.

**Key insight (Cited as standard in welding metallurgy texts):** Increasing travel speed v reduces HAZ width but increases cooling rate → risk of martensite and hydrogen cracking.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

flowchart TD

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define Plasma Arc Welding. How does it differ from TIG welding in terms of arc constriction and penetration?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) is an arc welding process in which the arc is **constricted by a copper nozzle with a small orifice**, increasing the arc's energy density and temperature. The plasma gas (Ar) is forced through the orifice, producing a high-velocity ionized jet at **20,000–50,000 K**.

In **TIG welding**, the arc is "free" — it spreads between the tungsten electrode and the workpiece, producing a wider, less focused heat source. In **PAW**, the orifice constricts the arc, producing a **higher power density**, deeper penetration, and the ability to weld thicker sections in a single pass without edge preparation. Additionally, PAW uses a separate **shielding gas** in addition to the plasma gas, while TIG typically uses one combined gas.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**List any three differences between Brazing and Soldering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — table format expected):**

| Feature | Brazing | Soldering |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| Filler melting point | **Above 450 °C** | **Below 450 °C** |
| Joint strength | Higher (structural) | Lower (electrical / light mechanical) |
| Typical filler | Ag-based, Cu-P, BCu | Sn-Pb, Sn-Ag-Cu (SAC) |
| Heat source | Torch, furnace, induction | Soldering iron, hot plate |
| Base metal melting | Does not melt | Does not melt |
| Application examples | Refrigeration coils, plumbing | Electronics, electrical joints |

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]

**Q. (a) Explain with neat sketches the process of Resistance Spot Welding. List the important process parameters. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Resistance Spot Welding (RSW) Definition:**
A resistance welding process in which the fusion of two overlapping sheets is achieved by **resistance heating** from a large current (5000–20,000 A) for a short duration, with **simultaneous application of pressure** through water-cooled copper electrodes.

**Working Principle:**
- Two copper electrodes clamp the workpieces.
- A high current passes through the stack.
- Heat (Q = I²Rt) is generated primarily at the **faying interface** (highest contact resistance).
- A **castellated or mushroom-shaped nugget** is formed.
- Current is cut off; **hold time** allows nugget to solidify under pressure.

**Sketch (textual):**

```
  ELECTRODE (Upper) — moves vertically
        |
   ---+---+---        Top Sheet
       ↓ ↑
   ---+---+---        Bottom Sheet  
  ELECTRODE (Lower) — fixed
        |
   [Force applied]
```

**Process Parameters:**
1. **Welding current** (5–20 kA) — primary control
2. **Weld time** (0.1–1.5 s, 5–60 cycles)
3. **Electrode force** (2–8 kN)
4. **Hold time / forge time** (0.1–0.5 s)
5. **Electrode material and tip geometry**
6. **Sheet surface condition** (clean, oxide-free)
7. **Cooling water flow** through electrodes

**Nugget Quality Checks:**
- Minimum nugget diameter $d = 5\sqrt{t_w}$ (rule of thumb)
- Tensile-shear and cross-tension tests for strength

---

**Q. (b) In a spot welding operation on two 2 mm thick low-carbon steel plates, the following data are given:
Welding current = 9000 A
Weld time = 0.3 s
Total contact resistance = 150 μΩ
Density of steel = 7860 kg/m³
Specific heat = 660 J/(kg·K)
Melting point = 1500 °C
Initial temperature = 30 °C

Calculate:
(i) The total heat generated in joules.
(ii) The minimum diameter of the weld nugget.
(iii) The thermal efficiency if the nugget absorbs 75% of total heat. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Heat generated:**
$$Q = I^2 R t = (9000)^2 \times (150 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.3$$
$$= 81 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3$$
$$= 3645 \text{ J}$$
$$\boxed{Q \approx 3645 \text{ J}}$$

**(ii) Nugget diameter:**
Using the heat balance equation:
$$I^2 R t = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot t_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

$$3645 = 7860 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 660 \times (1500 - 30)$$

Compute the constant:
$$7860 \times 0.002 \times 660 \times 1470 = 1.5245 \times 10^7$$

$$\frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{3645}{1.5245 \times 10^7} = 2.391 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$d^2 = \frac{4 \times 2.391 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi} = 3.044 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$d = \sqrt{3.044 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01745 \text{ m} = 17.45 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 17.45 \text{ mm}}$$

> Rule of thumb: $d_{min} = 5\sqrt{t_w} = 5\sqrt{2} = 7.07$ mm. Since 17.45 ≫ 7.07, weld is well above acceptable. ✓

**(iii) Thermal efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{0.75 \times 3645}{3645} \times 100$$
$$\boxed{\eta = 75\%}$$

This is within the typical RSW efficiency range of **60–85%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**Q. (a) Explain the principle and working of Plasma Arc Welding (PAW). Differentiate between melt-in mode and keyhole mode. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
PAW uses a **constricted arc** between a non-consumable tungsten electrode (placed inside a copper nozzle) and the workpiece. The constriction is achieved by a small-diameter orifice in the nozzle, which produces a **thermal pinch effect** — radial heat loss is restricted, current density rises, and arc temperature increases to **20,000–50,000 K**.

**Working:**
1. **Plasma gas** (typically Argon, sometimes with H₂ or He) is introduced through the nozzle.
2. A **pilot arc** is struck between the tungsten electrode and the nozzle using a high-frequency unit.
3. The pilot arc ionizes the plasma gas.
4. The main arc is transferred to the workpiece — now the plasma gas is forced through the constricting orifice.
5. The high-velocity, high-energy plasma jet (Mach ~1) impinges on the workpiece.
6. A **shielding gas** (Ar/Ar-He) from an outer nozzle protects the molten pool.

**Two Operating Modes:**

| Feature | Melt-in Mode | Keyhole Mode |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Current range | 1–150 A | 150–500 A |
| Penetration | Shallow (conduction) | Full penetration |
| Joint type | Lap, thin butt | Square butt, thick sections |
| Mechanism | Heat transferred by conduction | Arc pierces through, metal flows around |
| Pool shape | Concave/convex | Tall, narrow keyhole |

**Keyhole Mode Specifics:**
- Power density exceeds **10⁵ W/mm²**.
- As arc pierces the plate, molten metal flows around it to the back.
- Travel speeds 5–10× higher than TIG.
- Used for stainless steel 5–10 mm, titanium 5–12 mm.

**Advantages of PAW:**
- Higher travel speed, less distortion.
- Stable arc even at low currents.
- Single-pass thick section welding.
- Excellent for reactive metals (Ti, Zr) in keyhole mode.

---

**Q. (b) What is Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)? With the help of a schematic temperature profile, explain the different sub-zones of HAZ. List the factors affecting HAZ width. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
The HAZ is the region of base metal adjacent to the weld whose **microstructure and mechanical properties have been altered** by the welding heat cycle, but which has **not melted**.

**Temperature Profile (from fusion line outward):**

```
   Temperature
       ^
   1500°C -------- FUSION LINE (Melting)
       |
   1100-1450°C --- Coarse Grain HAZ
       |
    900-1100°C ---- Fine Grain HAZ
       |
    700-900°C ----- Intercritical HAZ
       |
    600-700°C ----- Subcritical HAZ
       |
   Ambient --------- BASE METAL
       |
       +----------> Distance from weld center
```

**Sub-zones Description:**

1. **Coarse Grain HAZ** (1100–1450 °C)
   - Grain growth due to high temperature.
   - Risk of **hot cracking** in this zone.

2. **Fine Grain HAZ** (900–1100 °C)
   - Recrystallization refines grains.
   - Toughness improves; strength good.

3. **Intercritical HAZ** (700–900 °C)
   - Partially transforms to austenite, re-transforms on cooling.
   - **Soft zone** risk in some steels.

4. **Subcritical HAZ** (600–700 °C)
   - Only tempering occurs.
   - **Tempered martensite embrittlement (TME)** risk in alloy steels.

**Factors Affecting HAZ Width:**
1. **Heat input (H)** — H = V·I/S (V = voltage, I = current, S = travel speed); higher H → wider HAZ.
2. **Preheating** — reduces cooling rate, narrows hardening zones.
3. **Interpass temperature** — high interpass → coarser HAZ.
4. **Base metal thermal conductivity** — higher k → narrower HAZ.
5. **Plate thickness** — thicker plates dissipate heat differently.
6. **Welding process** — laser/EBW produce very narrow HAZ; SMAW/ESW wider.
7. **Joint design** — multi-pass welds can refine HAZ (next pass reheats previous HAZ).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **HAZ with Fusion Zone (FZ)**. Remember: FZ has **melted and re-solidified** (cast structure); HAZ has **NOT melted** (only microstructural change due to heat). Marks are deducted for this confusion.

---

### Additional 14-Mark Practice Question (Extra)

**Q. (a) Explain with a neat sketch the Electron Beam Welding (EBW) process. State its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Working Principle:**
- Filament (W or LaB₆) heated to ~2500 °C emits electrons (thermionic emission).
- High voltage (30–200 kV) accelerates electrons to ~50–70% of light speed.
- Magnetic lens focuses beam; spot diameter 0.1–0.5 mm.
- Workpiece in vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr).
- Kinetic energy → heat on impact; power density 10⁶–10⁷ W/mm².
- Very narrow, deep weld (depth/width = 30:1).

**Sketch (textual):**
```
        [Filament]
            |
       [Anode + Acc. Voltage 30-200 kV]
            |
        [Magnetic Lens]
            |
       [Beam focused → Workpiece in vacuum chamber]
```

**Advantages:**
- Very narrow HAZ, minimal distortion.
- Deep penetration — 50 mm in one pass without edge preparation.
- Welds refractory metals (W, Mo, Ta).
- Dissimilar metal joining possible.
- No filler required.

**Limitations:**
- Expensive equipment.
- Vacuum chamber size limits workpiece dimensions.
- X-ray generation (radiation hazard, shielding required).
- Sensitive to fit-up (small gap tolerance).

**Q. (b) Differentiate between Friction Welding and Friction Stir Welding. (4 marks)**
**Q. (c) Write a short note on defects in welding. (3 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Numerical Mistakes in Spot Welding:** Converting **μΩ to Ω** (×10⁻⁶) and **mm to m** (×10⁻³) is where most students lose 1–2 marks. Always write units explicitly.
> 2. **Conflating FZ and HAZ:** FZ = melted, HAZ = not melted. Examiners will deduct marks.
> 3. **Writing "Thermit" but skipping the chemical reaction equation** — at least one equation is mandatory in a 14-mark question.
> 4. **Forgetting Capillary Action explanation in Brazing** — it's the *key* mechanism, not just "filler melts and joins."
> 5. **Drawing ESW as a "welding with arc"** — In steady state, ESW has **no arc**; current conducts through molten slag. This is a very common conceptual error.
> 6. **Confusing LBW and EBW:** EBW needs vacuum, LBW does not. EBW uses electrons, LBW uses photons.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Welding Processes Summary

- **PAW:** Constricted arc, 20,000 K, keyhole mode for thick sections, two gas system.
- **Thermit:** Exothermic Fe₂O₃ + Al → Fe + Al₂O₃, 2500 °C, rail joining, **no electricity required**.
- **Friction:** Solid-state, three phases (rub → steady → forge), **no HAZ**, dissimilar metal joining easy.
- **ESW:** Vertical thick plates, **no arc in steady state**, slag conducts, requires normalizing.
- **USW:** 20–40 kHz vibration, no melting, foils & electronics, dissimilar thin sheets.
- **EBW:** Vacuum, 30–200 kV, power density 10⁶–10⁷ W/mm², deep narrow welds, refractory metals.
- **LBW:** CO₂ or Nd:YAG laser, no vacuum, high speed, good for automation.

### Resistance Welding — High-Yield Points

- $Q = I^2 R t$ — must be in SI units.
- $d_{min} = 5\sqrt{t_w}$ rule of thumb (mm).
- Sheet surface must be **clean and oxide-free** for low R₃ variability.
- Electrode cooling water: 4–6 L/min typical.
- **Three heat-affected resistances** are at the faying interface and the electrode-sheet contacts.

### HAZ & Weldability

- **HAZ has 4 sub-zones** (coarse, fine, intercritical, subcritical).
- **Carbon equivalent** predicts weldability of steels.
- **Sensitization** in austenitic SS: use 304L, 321, 347.
- **Aluminum welding** needs AC TIG (to break Al₂O₃ layer) or MIG with Ar.

### Residual Stresses & Distortion

- Caused by non-uniform heating.
- **PWHT (Post-Weld Heat Treatment)** at 600–650 °C for stress relief.
- **Peening** every pass reduces shrinkage stress.
- **Backstep / skip sequence** controls distortion.

### Defects in Welding

- **Hot cracks** → in crater and HAZ; control S, P content.
- **Cold (Hydrogen) cracks** → preheat + low-H electrodes.
- **Porosity** → shield gas coverage.
- **Lamellar tearing** → Z-direction tested plate (Z-grade).
- **Lack of fusion / penetration** → technique.

### Brazing, Soldering, Adhesive Bonding

- **Brazing** → filler > 450 °C; **Soldering** → filler < 450 °C.
- **Capillary action** drives brazing filler into joints.
- **Joint clearance** 0.025–0.1 mm for capillary brazing.
- **Soldering** mostly for electronics; SAC 305 is lead-free standard.
- **Adhesive bonding** uses epoxy / phenolic / PU / silicone; surface prep (degrease + abrade) is critical.
- **Adhesive advantages:** uniform stress distribution, dissimilar materials, vibration damping, thin sections.

### Numerical Problem-Solving Strategy (Spot Welding)

1. **Write given data with units** — convert μΩ → Ω and mm → m.
2. Compute **Q = I²Rt**.
3. Equate Q = ρ(πd²/4)·t_w·C_p·(T_m − T_0).
4. Solve for d.
5. Check against $d_{min} = 5\sqrt{t_w}$.
6. Compute thermal efficiency if asked: η = Q_absorbed / Q_total.

### Must-Memorize Constants for Numericals

- Density of steel: **7850 kg/m³**
- Specific heat of steel: **660 J/(kg·K)**
- Melting point of steel: **1500 °C**
- Aluminum density: **2700 kg/m³**, C_p = **900 J/(kg·K)**, T_m = **660 °C**

### Common KTU Question Patterns
- Define + differentiate (3 marks)
- Numerical on spot welding (7 marks)
- Process explanation with sketch (7 marks)
- HAZ sub-zones (3–7 marks)
- Weldability formula application (3–7 marks)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Deformation and Yield Criteria - Hot and Cold Working Processes. Rolling - Flat-Rolling Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Plastic Deformation and Yield Criteria - Hot and Cold Working Processes. Rolling - Flat-Rolling Process # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Plastic Deformation **Definition:** Plastic deformation is the permanent, irreversible change in the shape of a solid material when subjected to an applied stress that exceeds the material's **yield strength**, without any rupture or fracture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Plastic deformation is a *time-independent, permanent* shape change. Once the load is removed, the material **does NOT** return to its original dimensions. This is the foundational principle behind ALL bulk metal forming operations (rolling, forging, extrusion, drawing, etc.). **Conceptual Analogy — The Play-Doh / Clay Model:** Imagine squeezing a ball of soft clay (Play-Doh) between your palms. Initially it resists slightly (elastic region — it springs back if you gently tap it). But once you press hard enough, it permanently flattens into a disc. The clay has *flowed* — atoms have slid past each other along slip planes. This is plastic deformation. The "press hard enough" threshold is governed by the **Yield Criterion**. --- ## 1.2 Yield Criteria A yield criterion is a mathematical *failure hypothesis* that predicts the onset of plastic yielding in a material subjected to a complex 3D stress state. > [!NOTE] > The two principal KTU-mandated yield criteria are: > 1. **Tresca (Maximum Shear Stress) Criterion** — simpler, conservative > 2. **Von Mises (Distortion Energy) Criterion** — more accurate, matches experimental data for ductile metals **Intuitive Analogy — The Balloon Test:** Imagine inflating a balloon. It bursts when the **maximum principal stress difference** exceeds a critical value (Tresca), but actually bursts slightly later because *all* stress components contribute to distortion (Von Mises). Von Mises is the "smarter" prediction. --- ## 1.3 Hot Working vs. Cold Working | Aspect | Hot Working | Cold Working | |---|---|---| | **Temperature** | **Above recrystallization temperature** (T > 0.6 T_melt, in K) | **Below recrystallization temperature** | | **Analogy** | Working warm butter | Working cold chocolate bar | | **Effect on properties** | Refines grain, no strain hardening | Increases strength via strain hardening | | **Surface finish** | Poor (oxidation/scaling) | Excellent | **Conceptual Analogy — Hot vs Cold Working:** - **Hot working** is like bending warm taffy — soft, easy to deform, but the surface gets "sweaty" (oxide scale). - **Cold working** is like bending a metal ruler — harder, requires more force, but you get a clean, precise shape and the metal becomes *stronger* (work hardening). --- ## 1.4 Flat Rolling Process **Definition:** Rolling is a metal forming process in which the workpiece (stock) is passed between **two counter-rotating rolls** to reduce its thickness, change its cross-section, or impart desired mechanical properties. > [!IMPORTANT] > In **Flat Rolling**, the rolls have a constant cylindrical profile, and the workpiece cross-section is *rectangular* (plates, sheets, strips). This is the single most widely used bulk forming process — over **50% of all steel produced** passes through rolling mills. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Plastic Deformation When a metal is loaded, it progresses through three distinct regimes on the **Engineering Stress-Strain Diagram**: 1. **Elastic Region** — Stress is proportional to strain (Hooke's Law: σ = Eε). Deformation is *reversible*. Governed by atomic bond stretching. 2. **Yield Point (σ_y)** — The threshold where permanent (plastic) flow begins. Governed by dislocation movement along slip planes. 3. **Plastic Region** — Strain hardening occurs. Dislocations multiply, tangle, and impede further slip, raising the flow stress. **Key Microstructural Mechanism — Dislocation Glide:** Plastic deformation in crystalline metals occurs primarily by the motion of **line defects (dislocations)** along close-packed atomic planes (slip planes). The resolved shear stress on the slip system must exceed the **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)** for slip to occur — this is the *Schmid's Law* condition. > Schmid's Law: $\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \cos\lambda$ where φ = angle between tensile axis and slip plane normal, λ = angle between tensile axis and slip direction. --- ## 2.2 Yield Criteria — Mathematical Formulation ### 2.2.1 Tresca Yield Criterion (1864) **Statement:** Yielding begins when the **maximum shear stress** in the material reaches a critical value equal to the shear yield stress in pure tension. $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{Y}{2} = k}$$ where: - $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ are the principal stresses - $Y$ = yield strength in uniaxial tension - $k$ = shear yield strength ### 2.2.2 Von Mises Yield Criterion (1913) **Statement:** Yielding begins when the **distortion energy** per unit volume reaches the same value as in a uniaxial tension test at yield. $$\boxed{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2Y^2}$$ In terms of principal stresses or general stress state: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (\sigma_y - \sigma_z)^2 + (\sigma_z - \sigma_x)^2 + 6(\tau_{xy}^2 + \tau_{yz}^2 + \tau_{zx}^2)\right] = Y^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Von Mises is the **industry standard** used in FEA software (ANSYS, ABAQUS) for predicting plastic deformation in sheet metal forming, forging, and rolling simulations. Tresca gives more conservative (safe) results, useful for design codes. ### 2.2.3 Geometric Interpretation In 2D principal stress space (σ₁, σ₂), Tresca defines a **hexagonal yield surface** while Von Mises defines an **elliptical yield surface** that is inscribed/circumscribes the hexagon. Both pass through uniaxial tension points (Y, 0) and pure shear points. --- ## 2.3 Hot Working vs. Cold Working — Deep Comparison | Parameter | Hot Working | Cold Working | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Plastic deformation above recrystallization temperature | Plastic deformation below recrystallization temperature | | **Temperature Range** | T > 0.6 × T_m (Kelvin) | T < 0.3 × T_m (typically room temperature) | | **Recrystallization** | Occurs simultaneously with deformation | Does NOT occur during deformation | | **Strain Hardening** | Negligible (offset by softening) | Significant | | **Flow Stress** | Lower (high temperature softening) | Higher | | **Grain Structure** | Refined, equiaxed | Elongated, distorted (preferred orientation) | | **Mechanical Properties** | Improved ductility & toughness | Higher yield strength, hardness | | **Surface Finish** | Poor (oxide scale, decarburization) | Excellent (bright finish) | | **Dimensional Accuracy** | Low (thermal contraction on cooling) | High (close tolerances) | | **Force Required** | Low | High (1.5 to 3× hot working force) | | **Examples** | Hot rolling, hot forging, extrusion of steel | Cold rolling, cold drawing, deep drawing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Recrystallization Temperature (T_rec):** The temperature above which new strain-free grains nucleate and replace the deformed grains. **For steel: T_rec ≈ 0.4 × T_melt ≈ 450°C.** Hot working is performed **above** this temperature. --- ## 2.4 Flat Rolling Process — Theory and Geometry ### 2.4.1 Rolling Mill Components - **Two opposing cylindrical rolls** rotating in opposite directions - **Work rolls** (small dia) + **Backup rolls** (large dia) in 4-high mills - **Chocks and bearings** to support roll shafts - **Screws/hydraulic cylinders** for roll gap adjustment - **Guides, pinch rolls, coiler** for entry/exit ### 2.4.2 Key Geometric Parameters Let: - $R$ = roll radius - $t_0$ = initial thickness (entry side) - $t_f$ = final thickness (exit side) - $w$ = width of strip - $\Delta h = t_0 - t_f$ = draft (reduction in thickness) - $\alpha$ = contact angle / bite angle / angle of nip **Contact Length (L):** The arc length of contact between roll and workpiece. For small bite angles (sin α ≈ α ≈ √(Δh/R)): $$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$$ **True Strain in Rolling:** $$\varepsilon = \ln\left(\frac{t_0}{t_f}\right)$$ ### 2.4.3 Neutral Point (No-Slip Point) There exists a point on the contact arc where the **roll surface velocity equals the material velocity**. On the entry side, material moves slower than roll surface → friction pulls it in. On the exit side, material moves faster than roll surface → friction holds it back. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Draft (Reduction)** | $\Delta h = t_0 - t_f$ | mm | | 2 | **Contact Length** | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | mm (valid for small α) | | 3 | **True Strain** | $\varepsilon = \ln(t_0/t_f)$ | dimensionless | | 4 | **Bite Angle** | $\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$ | radians | | 5 | **Maximum Reduction** | $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$ | mm (friction-limited) | | 6 | **Projected Contact Area** | $A = L \cdot w$ | mm² | | 7 | **Average Rolling Pressure** | $p_{avg} = Y_f \cdot (1 + \mu L / (2h_f))$ | MPa (Friction Hill) | | 8 | **Rolling Force** | $F = p_{avg} \cdot A$ | N (Newton) | | 9 | **Roll Torque (per roll)** | $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$ | N·mm (frictionless lever arm) | | 10 | **Total Power (2 rolls)** | $P = 2 \pi N T / 60$ | Watts | | 11 | **Tresca Yield Criterion** | $\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \vert = Y$ | MPa | | 12 | **Von Mises Yield Criterion** | $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2Y^2$ | MPa² | | 13 | **Hot Working Condition** | $T_{work} > 0.6 \cdot T_{melt}$ | Temperature in **Kelvin** | | 14 | **Schmid's Law (CRSS)** | $\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ | MPa | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Note:** You are expected to **memorize the contact length, rolling force, and torque formulas** (Eqns 2, 7, 8, 9). Numerical problems on rolling force and power are **guaranteed** in the university exam. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Steel Industry:** Flat rolling produces over **1.8 billion tonnes** of hot-rolled and cold-rolled steel annually worldwide for automotive, construction, and appliances. 2. **Aluminum Sheet Production:** Used for beverage cans (3004 alloy), aircraft skins (2024/7075). 3. **Yield Criteria in FEA:** Every crash simulation in automotive design uses Von Mises to predict plastic collapse. 4. **Hot Forging:** Critical for manufacturing crankshafts, connecting rods, gears. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Von Mises Yield Criterion (Distortion Energy Theory) ### Starting Point — Total Strain Energy For a 3D elastic stress state, the total strain energy per unit volume is: $$u_{total} = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2\right] - \frac{\nu}{E}\left[\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1\right]$$ ### Step 1: Split Total Energy into Two Components By the principle of energy decomposition: $$u_{total} = u_{volumetric} + u_{distortion}$$ - **Volumetric energy** = energy due to hydrostatic (uniform) stress → causes volume change only - **Distortion energy** = energy due to shear / deviatoric stress → causes *shape change* (yielding) ### Step 2: Express in Terms of Mean Stress The mean (hydrostatic) stress is: $$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$$ The deviatoric stresses (responsible for distortion) are: $$\sigma_1' = \sigma_1 - \sigma_m, \quad \sigma_2' = \sigma_2 - \sigma_m, \quad \sigma_3' = \sigma_3 - \sigma_m$$ By definition, the deviatoric stresses sum to zero: $$\sigma_1' + \sigma_2' + \sigma_3' = 0$$ ### Step 3: Write Distortion Energy in Terms of Deviatoric Stresses The distortion energy per unit volume is: $$u_{dist} = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right]$$ ### Step 4: Calibrate Against Uniaxial Tension Test In uniaxial tension at yield: $\sigma_1 = Y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$. Substituting into the distortion energy equation: $$u_{dist,uniaxial} = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(Y-0)^2 + (0-0)^2 + (0-Y)^2\right]$$ $$u_{dist,uniaxial} = \frac{1+\nu}{6E} \cdot 2Y^2 = \frac{(1+\nu)Y^2}{3E}$$ ### Step 5: Equate Distortion Energies Setting $u_{dist,general} = u_{dist,uniaxial}$: $$\frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right] = \frac{(1+\nu)Y^2}{3E}$$ Cancelling the common factor $\frac{1+\nu}{6E}$ from both sides: $$\boxed{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2Y^2}$$ > This is the **Von Mises Yield Criterion** — the onset of yielding in ductile metals. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Tresca Yield Criterion **Starting Logic:** Yielding begins when the maximum shear stress equals the shear yield stress $k$. In 3D stress state, the maximum shear stress (in principal stress space) is: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$ where $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$. In a uniaxial tension test at yield ($\sigma_1 = Y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$), the shear yield stress is: $$k = \frac{Y}{2}$$ Equating the general $\tau_{max}$ to the uniaxial shear yield stress: $$\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{Y}{2}$$ $$\boxed{\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \vert = Y}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Rolling Force (Friction Hill Equation) ### Step 1: Free Body Diagram Setup Consider a thin element of the workpiece within the roll gap at vertical position $h(x)$ where $x$ is measured from the neutral point. Apply vertical force equilibrium. ### Step 2: Vertical Force Equilibrium Let $p$ = vertical pressure at position $x$, $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, and the element has length $dx$ and width $w$. The friction force on the roll surface acts at angle φ to the horizontal. Horizontal equilibrium of the element gives: $$(p + dp)(h + dh) - p \cdot h = -2 \mu p \cdot dx \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \tan\phi$$ For small bite angles, $\cos\phi \approx 1$ and $\tan\phi \approx \phi$: $$h \cdot dp + p \cdot dh = -2 \mu p \cdot dx \cdot \phi$$ But $\tan\phi \approx \frac{dh}{dx} \Rightarrow \phi \approx \frac{dh}{dx}$, and $dx \cdot \phi \approx dh$. So: $$h \cdot dp + p \cdot dh = -2 \mu p \cdot dh$$ Rearranging: $$h \cdot dp + p \cdot dh + 2 \mu p \cdot dh = 0$$ $$h \cdot dp = -p(1 + 2\mu) \, dh$$ ### Step 3: Separate Variables and Integrate $$\frac{dp}{p} = -\frac{(1 + 2\mu)}{h} \, dh$$ Integrating from the entry ($h_0$, $p_0$) to exit ($h_f$, $p_f$): $$\int_{p_0}^{p_f} \frac{dp}{p} = -(1 + 2\mu) \int_{h_0}^{h_f} \frac{dh}{h}$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{p_f}{p_0}\right) = (1 + 2\mu) \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$$ ### Step 4: Final Friction Hill Equation $$\boxed{p_{avg} = Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right)}$$ > where $L = \sqrt{R \Delta h}$ and $Y_f$ is the average flow stress. This equation shows that friction **increases the rolling pressure** significantly — the "friction hill." --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Roll Torque For a frictionless rolling operation, the resultant rolling force $F$ acts at the **midpoint of the contact arc**. The moment arm is $L/2$. **Per Roll Torque:** $$T = F \times \frac{L}{2} = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$$ **Total Torque (2 rolls):** $$T_{total} = F \cdot L$$ **Power Required (2 rolls):** $$P = T_{total} \cdot \omega = F \cdot L \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi N F L}{60}$$ where $N$ is the rotational speed of the rolls in RPM. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example **Problem (KTU-Style):** A steel strip with initial thickness $t_0 = 50$ mm and width $w = 200$ mm is reduced to $t_f = 30$ mm in a rolling mill with roll radius $R = 300$ mm. The average flow stress of the material is $Y_f = 280$ MPa, the coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.1$, and the rolls rotate at $N = 60$ RPM. Calculate: (a) Contact length (b) Rolling force (c) Total rolling torque (d) Power required ### Solution: **(a) Contact Length:** $$\Delta h = t_0 - t_f = 50 - 30 = 20 \text{ mm}$$ $$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{300 \times 20} = \sqrt{6000} = 77.46 \text{ mm}$$ **(b) Rolling Force:** $$p_{avg} = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right) = 280 \left(1 + \frac{0.1 \times 77.46}{2 \times 30}\right)$$ $$p_{avg} = 280 \left(1 + \frac{7.746}{60}\right) = 280 \times 1.1291 = 316.15 \text{ MPa}$$ Projected area: $$A = L \cdot w = 77.46 \times 200 = 15{,}492 \text{ mm}^2$$ Rolling force: $$F = p_{avg} \cdot A = 316.15 \times 15{,}492 = 4.897 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 4897 \text{ kN}$$ **(c) Total Torque:** $$T_{total} = F \cdot L = 4.897 \times 10^6 \times 77.46 = 3.793 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 379.3 \text{ kN·m}$$ **(d) Power Required:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi = 6.283 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$P = T_{total} \cdot \omega = 3.793 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} \times 6.283 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$P = 2.383 \times 10^9 \text{ N·mm/s} = 2.383 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \approx 2383 \text{ kW}$$ > [!NOTE] > The student must be careful to **convert all units consistently** before plugging into the formula — this is a frequent source of mark loss. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Engineering Stress-Strain Diagram (Conceptual Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Load Applied] --> B[Elastic Region
sigma = E x epsilon] B --> C{Yield Point Reached?} C -- No --> D[Remove Load
Returns to Original Shape] C -- Yes --> E[Plastic Deformation Begins
Dislocation Slip Activates] E --> F[Strain Hardening Region
Flow Stress Increases] F --> G[Ultimate Tensile Strength] G --> H[Necking Region] H --> I[Fracture / Rupture] I --> J[Plastic Deformation
Permanent] ``` ## 4.2 Von Mises vs Tresca Yield Surface (2D Principal Stress Space) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph YieldSurfaces["Principal Stress Space sigma1 vs sigma2"] A[Tresca Hexagon
6 Linear Segments] --> B[Von Mises Ellipse
Smooth Curve Inscribed in Hexagon] end B --> C[Both pass through uniaxial tension point Y,0] B --> D[Both pass through pure shear point] ``` ## 4.3 Flat Rolling Process Architecture (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Entry["Entry Side"] A1[Coiler / Pay-off Reel] --> A2[Pinch Roll] A2 --> A3[Entry Guide Table] end A3 --> B1{Roll Gap
Bite Angle alpha} B1 --> B2[Roll 1 Rotating CW] B1 --> B3[Roll 2 Rotating CCW] B2 --> C1[Contact Arc Length L] B3 --> C1 C1 --> C2[Neutral Point
Vroll = Vmaterial] C2 --> C3[Draft Zone
thickness reduces t0 to tf] subgraph Exit["Exit Side"] C3 --> D1[Exit Guide Table] D1 --> D2[Run-out Table with Cooling] D2 --> D3[Coiler / Down-coiler] end C3 --> E1[Backup Rolls 4-High Mill] C3 --> E2[Screw Down Mechanism] C3 --> E3[Lubrication System] ``` ## 4.4 Hot Working vs Cold Working Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Metal Forming Requirement] --> B{Operating Temperature?} B -- Above T_rec 0.6 Tm --> C[Hot Working] B -- Below T_rec --> D[Cold Working] B -- Slightly above room T --> E[Warm Working] C --> C1[Lower Force Required] C --> C2[Recrystallization Removes Strain Hardening] C --> C3[Refined Equiaxed Grains] C --> C4[Poor Surface Finish Oxide Scale] D --> D1[Higher Force Required] D --> D2[Significant Strain Hardening] D --> D3[Elongated Grains] D --> D4[Excellent Surface Finish] D --> D5[Higher Strength Hardness] E --> E1[Intermediate Properties] E --> E2[Used for Steel Forging] ``` ## 4.5 Rolling Mill Stand Architecture (Block-Level Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MillStand["4-High Rolling Mill Stand"] A1[Screw Down Hydraulic Cylinder] --> A2[Backup Roll Bearings] A2 --> A3[Backup Rolls Large Diameter] A3 --> A4[Work Rolls Small Diameter] A4 --> A5[Roll Gap Set by Screws] B1[Drive Motor] --> B2[Gearbox Reducer] B2 --> B3[Pinion Stand] B3 --> A4 C1[Cooling Water Nozzles] --> A4 C2[Lubricant Inlet] --> A5 end A5 --> D1[Workpiece Strip] D1 --> E1[Thickness Gauge X-ray] E1 --> F1[Automatic Gauge Control AGC] F1 --> A1 ``` > [!NOTE] > The Automatic Gauge Control (AGC) loop shown in the block schematic is the modern real-world implementation in hot strip mills. The thickness gauge feedback continuously adjusts the screw-down position to maintain tight thickness tolerances — typically ±0.025 mm in cold rolling. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between hot working and cold working processes. State the recrystallization temperature criterion for hot working.** **Model Answer:** | Sl. No. | Hot Working | Cold Working | |---|---|---| | 1 | Performed **above** recrystallization temperature | Performed **below** recrystallization temperature | | 2 | No strain hardening (recrystallization occurs simultaneously) | Significant strain hardening occurs | | 3 | Lower flow stress and force required | Higher flow stress and force required | | 4 | Poor surface finish due to oxide scale | Excellent surface finish | | 5 | Less dimensional accuracy | High dimensional accuracy | | 6 | Examples: Hot rolling, hot forging, hot extrusion | Examples: Cold rolling, cold drawing, deep drawing | **Recrystallization Criterion:** Hot working is performed at a temperature $T_{work} > 0.6 \times T_{melt}$ (in Kelvin), which is generally above the recrystallization temperature of the metal. **For steel**, $T_{melt} \approx 1538°C$ and $T_{rec} \approx 450°C$, so hot rolling of steel is performed at **1100°C to 1250°C**. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **State Von Mises yield criterion.
