How is it different from Tresca criterion?** **Model Answer:** **Von Mises Yield Criterion Statement:** A ductile material begins to yield when the second invariant of the deviatoric stress tensor reaches a critical value. Mathematically: $$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2Y^2$$ where $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ are principal stresses and $Y$ is the uniaxial yield strength. **Comparison with Tresca:** | Aspect | Tresca | Von Mises | |---|---|---| | Based on | Maximum shear stress | Distortion energy | | Yield surface (2D) | Hexagon | Ellipse (inscribed in hexagon) | | Pure shear yield | $k = 0.5Y$ | $k = Y/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577Y$ | | Conservatism | More conservative | Less conservative, more accurate | | Complexity | Simpler | More complex | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 3]` **(a) Explain the phenomenon of plastic deformation in metals. Discuss the role of dislocations and slip systems with a neat sketch of the engineering stress-strain diagram.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** Plastic deformation is the permanent, irreversible change in the shape of a metal when the applied stress exceeds the yield strength. It occurs without fracture and the deformation remains after load removal. **Mechanism — Dislocation Motion:** Plastic deformation in crystalline metals occurs primarily by: 1. **Slip** — sliding of atomic planes over each other along close-packed directions. 2. **Twinning** — mirror-image reorientation of a portion of the crystal lattice. **Slip occurs on:** - **Slip plane** — the plane of highest atomic density (e.g., {111} in FCC, {110} in BCC). - **Slip direction** — the close-packed direction within the slip plane. The combination is called a **slip system**. FCC metals have 12 slip systems (highly ductile). BCC has 48, HCP has only 3 (least ductile). **Schmid's Law:** The resolved shear stress on the slip system is: $$\tau = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$ Slip begins when $\tau \geq \tau_{CRSS}$ (Critical Resolved Shear Stress). **Engineering Stress-Strain Diagram (Key Regions):** - **OA** — Elastic region: σ = Eε (Hooke's Law valid). - **B** — Upper yield point: dislocations break free from Cottrell atmospheres. - **C** — Lower yield point: sustained plastic flow begins. - **CD** — Strain hardening region: dislocation density rises, flow stress increases. - **D** — Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS): max stress before necking. - **DE** — Necking region: localized reduction in cross-section. - **E** — Fracture point: material fails. **Neat Sketch (Mark allocation — 2 marks for diagram):** ``` Stress | D (UTS) | /\ | / \ | / \E (Fracture) | / \ | / C \ | / / \ \ | / / \ \ | /B \ \ |/ \___\ A(Origin) Strain ``` **(b) With a neat sketch, explain the flat rolling process. Derive the expression for rolling force in flat rolling.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Flat Rolling Process (2 marks for sketch):** In flat rolling, the workpiece (rectangular cross-section) is passed between two counter-rotating cylindrical rolls. The rolls rotate inward, and friction between the roll and workpiece pulls the material into the roll gap (bite), reducing its thickness from $t_0$ to $t_f$. **Key Geometric Parameters:** - Draft: $\Delta h = t_0 - t_f$ - Contact length: $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ - Bite angle: $\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$ - True strain: $\varepsilon = \ln(t_0/t_f)$ **Derivation of Rolling Force (5 marks for derivation):** Refer to **Section 3.3** of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. The final result (Friction Hill Equation) is: $$p_{avg} = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right)$$ **Rolling Force:** $$F = p_{avg} \times A = p_{avg} \times L \times w$$ Substituting the friction hill equation: $$\boxed{F = Y_f \cdot L \cdot w \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right)}$$ **Sketch (for 2 marks):** ``` Roll 1 (CW) ___________ / alpha \ / \____ <- Contact arc length L t0 → / \ | h(x) | | | → F (rolling force) tf → \ / \ / \_________/ ___________ Roll 2 (CCW) Entry Exit side side ``` --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 3]` **(a) State and derive the Von Mises yield criterion. Compare it with Tresca criterion in pure shear loading.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Statement (2 marks):** A ductile material yields when the **distortion energy** per unit volume equals the distortion energy at yield in a uniaxial tension test. **Mathematical form:** $$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2Y^2$$ **Derivation (4 marks):** Full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps: 1. Express total strain energy: $u_{total} = u_{volumetric} + u_{distortion}$ 2. Compute volumetric energy using mean stress $\sigma_m = (\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)/3$ 3. Compute distortion energy using deviatoric stresses 4. Equate to uniaxial tension yield case ($\sigma_1 = Y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$) 5. Simplify to obtain the final equation. **Comparison in Pure Shear (1 mark):** In pure shear: $\sigma_1 = \tau$, $\sigma_2 = 0$, $\sigma_3 = -\tau$ where $\tau$ is the shear yield stress. **Tresca:** $\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \vert = Y \Rightarrow \vert \tau - (-\tau) \vert = Y \Rightarrow 2\tau = Y \Rightarrow \tau = Y/2 = 0.5Y$ **Von Mises:** $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2Y^2$ $$(\tau)^2 + (\tau)^2 + (-2\tau)^2 = 2Y^2 \Rightarrow 6\tau^2 = 2Y^2 \Rightarrow \tau = Y/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577Y$$ **Result:** Von Mises predicts higher shear yield (≈15.5% higher) and matches experimental data better. --- **(b) A copper strip is cold-rolled from 10 mm to 6 mm thickness in a 2-high mill with roll radius 250 mm. The strip width is 300 mm. The average flow stress of copper is 350 MPa and the coefficient of friction is 0.08. The rolls rotate at 50 RPM. Calculate the rolling force, torque per roll, and power required.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - $t_0 = 10$ mm, $t_f = 6$ mm, $w = 300$ mm - $R = 250$ mm, $Y_f = 350$ MPa, $\mu = 0.08$, $N = 50$ RPM **Step 1: Contact Length** $$\Delta h = t_0 - t_f = 10 - 6 = 4 \text{ mm}$$ $$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{250 \times 4} = \sqrt{1000} = 31.62 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Average Rolling Pressure (Friction Hill)** $$p_{avg} = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right) = 350 \left(1 + \frac{0.08 \times 31.62}{2 \times 6}\right)$$ $$p_{avg} = 350 \left(1 + \frac{2.530}{12}\right) = 350 \times 1.2108 = 423.78 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3: Projected Contact Area** $$A = L \times w = 31.62 \times 300 = 9486.83 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 4: Rolling Force** $$F = p_{avg} \times A = 423.78 \times 9486.83 = 4.020 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 4020 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5: Torque per Roll** $$T = 0.5 \times F \times L = 0.5 \times 4.020 \times 10^6 \times 31.62$$ $$T = 6.356 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm} = 63.56 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 6: Power Required** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{60} = 5.236 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Total power (2 rolls):** $$P = 2 \times T \times \omega = 2 \times 6.356 \times 10^7 \times 5.236$$ $$P = 6.656 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm/s} = 6.656 \times 10^5 \text{ W} \approx 665.6 \text{ kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Points:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm with m. Always keep **mm throughout** and convert N·mm/s to Watts at the END. > 2. **Friction Hill Omission:** Forgetting the term $\left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right)$ gives just $F = Y_f \times L \times w$, which is the *frictionless* answer and loses 3 marks. > 3. **Torque Formula:** Some students write $T = F \times L$ (total torque). The question asks for **torque per roll**, which is $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$. > 4. **Power = 2 × T × ω:** Remember the **two rolls** each contribute torque. > 5. **Verifying Bite Condition:** Always check $\mu \geq \tan\alpha$ for the strip to be drawn into the rolls. Here, $\tan\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h/R} = \sqrt{4/250} = 0.1265$, and $\mu = 0.08 < 0.1265$. **This violates the bite condition!** In real practice, the student would need to reduce the draft or increase friction. *For KTU numericals, this is usually ignored and the formula applied as-is.* --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3** ### Plastic Deformation Essentials - Plastic deformation = **permanent, irreversible** shape change above yield. - Mechanism = **dislocation glide** along close-packed slip planes. - **Schmid's Law:** $\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ - FCC = 12 slip systems (most ductile) | BCC = 48 | HCP = 3 (least ductile) ### Yield Criteria — Must Memorize - **Tresca:** $\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \vert = Y$ → Hexagonal surface → Conservative - **Von Mises:** $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2Y^2$ → Elliptical surface → Accurate - Pure shear comparison: Tresca → $k = 0.5Y$ | Von Mises → $k = Y/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577Y$ ### Hot vs Cold Working - **Hot Working:** $T > 0.6 \cdot T_{melt}$ (Kelvin), $T > T_{rec}$ - **Cold Working:** $T < T_{rec}$, causes strain hardening, better finish - Hot working has **no work hardening** (recrystallization offsets it) ### Flat Rolling — Key Formulas (Memorize!) - $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ (contact length) - $L = R \alpha$ (general form, α in radians) - $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$ (maximum reduction limit) - $p_{avg} = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right)$ (Friction Hill) - $F = p_{avg} \times L \times w$ (Rolling force) - $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$ (Torque per roll) - $P = \frac{2\pi N T_{total}}{60}$ (Power for both rolls) ### Engineering Units Reminder - **Stress:** N/mm² = MPa - **Force:** N or kN (1 kN = 10³ N) - **Torque:** N·mm or N·m (1 N·m = 10³ N·mm) - **Power:** W or kW (1 kW = 10³ W) - **Always** keep mm in rolling formulas; convert at the end. ### Bite Condition (Frequently Asked) For successful rolling, the friction must be sufficient to drag the workpiece into the roll gap: $$\mu \geq \tan\alpha \approx \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}}$$ If violated → use **grooved rolls** or **push the workpiece in** with a guide. ### Common Engineering Applications - **Hot Rolling:** Structural beams, rails, plates, hot-rolled coils for automotive. - **Cold Rolling:** Automotive body panels, appliance casings, tin plate for food cans. - **Hot Forging:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, gears, turbine shafts. - **Cold Drawing:** Wires, tubes, precision shafts. > **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — PBMET304 (KTU 2024 SCHEME)**

topics.title: Rolling Force and Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Rolling Force and Power # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Rolling Force and Power? In metal forming by **flat rolling**, the work-piece is passed between two counter-rotating cylindrical rolls. The rolls exert a huge compressive force normal to the roll surfaces to plastically deform the metal and reduce its cross-section. The horizontal component of this contact pressure integrated over the arc of contact gives the **Roll Separating Force (P)**. The work required to drive the rolls against this force constitutes the **Rolling Power**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Roll Separating Force (P):** The total vertical (radial) force exerted by the rolls on the work-piece. It is one of the most critical parameters because it determines the roll strength, roll deflection, mill housing stiffness, and motor power rating. > [!NOTE] > **Rolling Power (N):** The mechanical power required at the roll spindle to overcome the torque due to the separating force. It governs the selection of the main drive motor, gearbox, and coupling. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Dough Roller Imagine you are rolling out **chapati dough** with a heavy wooden rolling pin. The pin pushes down on the dough with a certain downward force. The harder the dough, the more force you need. The longer the path of contact (because you are flattening it more), the more tired your arms get. The force you apply is analogous to the **roll separating force**, while the energy you expend (Force × distance per stroke × number of strokes) is analogous to the **rolling power**. In industrial rolling of steel: - The roll acts like the rolling pin. - The hot billet/slab acts like the dough. - The roll motor must supply enough torque to maintain the roll rotation against the resistance of the deforming metal. ## 1.3 Why is this Topic Critical for KTU 2024? > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners frequently test the derivations of the **projected contact length**, **neutral angle**, **forward slip**, and the application of **Hill's, Siebel's, and Ekelund's formulae** for average roll pressure. Any mistake in the lever-arm length or the friction factor directly costs full marks in numericals. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Mean flow stress (σ̄ₘ or 2k_f):** The average yield stress of the material under the rolling conditions (in MPa or N/mm²). - **Friction coefficient (μ):** Typically 0.05 to 0.5 depending on lubrication, temperature, and material. - **Projected contact length (L):** Measured in millimetres. - **Roll radius (R):** In millimetres (typically 100 mm to 800 mm in industrial mills). - **Roll speed (N):** In revolutions per minute (rpm). - **Width of strip (w or b):** In millimetres. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanics of the Rolling Process When a work-piece enters the roll gap, three distinct zones exist between the entry plane and the exit plane: | Zone | Location | Behaviour | Surface Velocity Comparison | |------|----------|-----------|----------------------------| | **Entry zone (Back tension zone)** | From entry to neutral point | Material moves **slower** than roll surface | $v_0 < v_r$ | | **Neutral point (No-slip point)** | Where material speed equals roll speed | No relative motion; friction reverses direction | $v_0 = v_r$ | | **Exit zone (Front tension zone)** | From neutral point to exit | Material moves **faster** than roll surface | $v_0 > v_r$ | > [!NOTE] > The point at which the material velocity equals the tangential roll velocity is called the **neutral point** or **no-slip point**. The angle subtended at the roll centre from the entry plane to the neutral point is the **angle of bite (α)**, and from the entry plane to the neutral point is the **neutral angle (φₙ)**. The angle from the neutral point to the exit is **exit angle** which equals (α - φₙ). ## 2.2 Key Geometric Relations The **draft (Δh)** per pass is the difference between the initial and final thickness: $$\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$$ The **projected contact length (L)** between the roll and work-piece (assuming no flattening) is given by the classic Ekelund approximation: $$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$$ For small drafts, the **contact angle (α)** in radians is: $$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units / Remarks | |---|---------------------|---------|-----------------| | 1 | Draft | $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$ | mm | | 2 | Projected contact length | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | mm | | 3 | Contact angle (radians) | $\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$ | rad (small angle) | | 4 | Neutral angle (radians) | $\phi_n = \dfrac{\alpha}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{2\mu}\right)$ | rad | | 5 | Forward slip | $s = \dfrac{v_1 - v_r}{v_r} = \dfrac{R^2}{h_1}\phi_n^2$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Roll separating force (Hill's) | $P = L \cdot w \cdot \bar{p} = L \cdot w \cdot 2k_{ave}$ | N | | 7 | Siebel's average pressure | $\bar{p} = 1.15 \cdot \sigma_{ys}\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{2h_0}\right)$ | N/mm² | | 8 | Ekelund's average pressure | $\bar{p} = 2k_f\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{h_0}\right)$ (approx) | N/mm² | | 9 | Bland & Ford average pressure | $\bar{p} = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{\mu L / (2h_0)}$ | N/mm² | | 10 | Rolling torque (per roll) | $T = P \cdot l$ | N·mm | | 11 | Lever arm length | $l = J_p \cdot L$ | mm | | 12 | Lever arm factor (Tropenas) | $J_p \approx 0.4$ to $0.5$ for no friction, drops with high μ | dimensionless | | 13 | Rolling power | $N_{power} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} \times 2$ (for two rolls) | W or kW | | 14 | Specific roll pressure | $K_{avg} = 1.15 \cdot \sigma_{ys}$ for hot rolling simplification | N/mm² | | 15 | Coefficient of friction in rolling | $\mu \approx \tan \alpha$ (limiting condition) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lever arm (l):** Because the pressure distribution along the contact arc is not uniform (peaks at neutral point), the resultant force does not act at the mid-point of the projected length L. It is offset from the roll centre line by a distance called the **lever arm** or **Pivotal line distance (a)**. This is the perpendicular distance from the resultant force vector to the roll centre. ## 2.4 Why these Formulas Matter in Engineering - **Roll Separating Force** dictates the mill housing design, the roll neck bearing capacity, and the screw-down load. - **Rolling Power** decides the rating of the main motor — under-rated motors cause speed drops and poor productivity. - **Neutral angle and forward slip** are essential for setting the proper tension in continuous mills and tandem rolling lines. - **Average pressure formulas** help engineers predict forces without expensive finite-element simulations. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Projected Contact Length (L) Consider two rolls of radius **R** rotating in opposite directions. The work-piece of initial thickness **h₀** enters and exits with thickness **h₁**. The geometry of the roll bite is shown in the schematic (Section 4). Let **O** be the centre of the roll, **A** be the entry point, and **B** be the exit point. Let **C** be the point where the centre lines of the roll and work-piece intersect, and let **D** be a point at the entry plane such that **OD = R** and **CD = R - h₀/2 - h₁/2** (since the centre of the work-piece lies at a height of (h₀ + h₁)/2 below the roll centre). From the geometry of the circle: $$OD^2 = OC^2 + CD^2$$ Expanding using the Pythagorean theorem: $$R^2 = \left(R - \frac{h_0 + h_1}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2$$ Let us expand the bracket: $$R^2 = R^2 - R(h_0 + h_1) + \left(\frac{h_0 + h_1}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{L^2}{4}$$ Cancel $R^2$ on both sides: $$0 = -R(h_0 + h_1) + \left(\frac{h_0 + h_1}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{L^2}{4}$$ The term $\left(\frac{h_0 + h_1}{2}\right)^2$ is very small compared to $R(h_0+h_1)$ for thin strips, so we neglect it as a higher-order small quantity: $$R(h_0 + h_1) \approx \frac{L^2}{4}$$ Therefore: $$L^2 = 4R\left(\frac{h_0 + h_1}{2}\right)$$ Since the **average thickness** is $\bar{h} = (h_0 + h_1)/2$, we get: $$L = \sqrt{R(h_0 + h_1)}$$ If we define the draft $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$, and assume that the average thickness is approximated as the initial thickness $h_0$ (a standard KTU simplification for small reductions), then: $$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$$ This is the celebrated **Ekelund approximation** for the projected contact length. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Neutral Angle (φₙ) The neutral point is the location where the horizontal velocity of the work-piece equals the horizontal component of the tangential roll velocity. Let us consider a free-body diagram of an elemental strip of thickness **h** at angle **φ** from the roll centre line. The forces acting on the elemental slice are: - Radial roll pressure $p$ acting normal to the roll surface. - Friction force $\mu p$ acting tangentially (its direction reverses across the neutral point). Taking equilibrium in the horizontal direction for the element: $$(p + dp) \cdot \frac{w(h + dh)}{2} - p \cdot \frac{w \cdot h}{2} - 2\mu p \cdot R \, d\phi \sin\phi = 0$$ After algebraic simplification and integration with the boundary condition that the back tension and front tension are negligible (or constant), we obtain the standard result: $$\phi_n = \sqrt{\frac{h_0 - h_1}{R}} \cdot \sqrt{1 - \frac{\mu^2}{2}}$$ Since the contact angle $\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$, we can also write: $$\phi_n = \alpha \sqrt{1 - \frac{\mu^2}{2}}$$ A simpler form often used by KTU for small angles: $$\phi_n = \frac{\alpha}{2}\left(1 - \frac{\alpha}{2\mu}\right)$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Forward Slip (s) Forward slip is the relative speed by which the exiting strip moves faster than the roll surface at the exit. Let $v_r$ be the tangential roll velocity at the exit and $v_1$ be the actual exit strip velocity. The forward slip is defined as: $$s = \frac{v_1 - v_r}{v_r}$$ By volume constancy of plastic deformation: $$v_0 \cdot h_0 = v_1 \cdot h_1$$ And by the kinematic condition at the neutral point: $$v_0 \cos\phi_n = v_r \cos 0 = v_r$$ Combining these and integrating along the contact arc, the standard KTU result is: $$s = \frac{R^2}{h_1} \phi_n^2$$ ## 3.4 Derivation of Average Roll Pressure — Siebel's Formula Siebel assumed that the work done by the rolls is equal to the ideal work of homogeneous compression plus the redundant work due to friction and shear: $$W_{total} = W_{ideal} + W_{redundant}$$ The mean pressure for plane-strain compression (with no external friction) is: $$p_{ideal} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_y \approx 1.15 \sigma_y$$ Adding the friction hill (analogous to forging) gives the **Siebel-Dahl formula**: $$\bar{p} = 1.15 \sigma_y \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2h_0}\right)$$ ## 3.5 Derivation of Average Roll Pressure — Ekelund's Formula Ekelund integrated the elemental stress over the contact arc assuming sticking friction throughout (no slip zone simplification): $$\bar{p} = 2k_f \left(\frac{e^{\mu L / h_0} - 1}{\mu L / h_0}\right)$$ A widely used simplified form for moderate friction is: $$\bar{p} = 2k_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{h_0}\right)$$ ## 3.6 Derivation of Bland & Ford Formula Bland and Ford assumed that the friction hill follows an exponential law and that the material strain-hardens. The resultant mean pressure is: $$\bar{p} = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{\mu L / (2h_0)}$$ where $\sigma_0$ is the plane-strain yield stress $2k_f$ at the entry strain. ## 3.7 Derivation of Rolling Torque and Power The resultant of the distributed roll pressure passes through a point called the **Pivotal Point**, offset horizontally from the roll centre. The perpendicular distance from the roll centre to the line of action of the resultant separating force is the **lever arm length (l)**. The torque required to drive **each roll** is: $$T = P \cdot l$$ The lever arm $l$ is related to the projected contact length $L$ by a dimensionless factor called the **lever-arm coefficient (J_p)**: $$l = J_p \cdot L$$ For dry rolling with high friction, $J_p \approx 0.4$ to $0.5$. The total rolling power is the sum of the power consumed at both rolls: $$N_{power} = 2 \times \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2\pi N P l}{30}$$ where $N$ is the rotational speed of each roll in rpm. If an efficiency factor $\eta$ for the mill drive is included: $$N_{motor} = \frac{N_{power}}{\eta}$$ ## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Pressure Formulas | Formula | Assumption | Best Used For | Limitation | |---------|------------|---------------|------------| | **Hill (P = 2kLw)** | Uniform pressure, simple slab analysis | First estimate, no friction | Ignores friction hill | | **Siebel** | Plane strain + friction hill | Cold rolling, lubricated | Neglects strain hardening | | **Ekelund** | Sticking friction, integrated | Hot rolling, no lub | Overestimates at high μ | | **Bland & Ford** | Exponential friction hill + strain hardening | Cold rolling, wide range | Requires accurate $\sigma_0$ | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Roll Bite Geometry and Force Distribution ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Entry Plane
Thickness h0"] --> B["Roll Bite Arc
Contact Length L"] B --> C["Exit Plane
Thickness h1"] B --> D["Neutral Point
No slip zone"] D --> E["Backward Slip Zone
Friction opposes motion"] D --> F["Forward Slip Zone
Friction aids motion"] G["Roll Centre O
Radius R"] --> H["Pivotal Point
Offset by lever arm l"] I["Roll Separating Force P
Vertical reaction"] --> H J["Friction Force μP
Tangential at roll surface"] --> H K["Resultant R_eq
Acts through pivotal point"] --> H L["Torque T = P x l
About roll centre"] --> G ``` ## 4.2 Pressure Distribution Along the Contact Arc ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Entry
h = h0"] --> B["Pressure rises
due to friction hill"] B --> C["Peak Pressure
at Neutral Point"] C --> D["Pressure falls
due to material acceleration"] D --> E["Exit
h = h1"] F["Resultant P
acts at Lever Arm l"] --> C G["Pivotal Line
a = 0.45 L approx"] --> F ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rolling Mill Power Train ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Main Electric Motor
Rated Power in kW"] --> B["Gearbox
Speed Reduction"] B --> C["Spindle and Coupling
Transmits Torque"] C --> D["Roll Necks and Bearings
Support Rotating Rolls"] D --> E["Work Rolls
Apply Separating Force P"] E --> F["Work Piece
Hot Slab or Billet"] G["Mill Housing and Scredown
Absorbs Separating Force P"] --> E H["Cooling and Lubrication System
Controls μ"] --> E I["PLC and Control System
Monitors Speed Torque Tension"] --> A I --> G ``` ## 4.4 Force Equilibrium in the Roll Bite ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Entry Tension σ_b
Acts on rear face"] --> B["Elemental Slice
at angle phi"] B --> C["Normal Pressure p
on arc ds = R dphi"] C --> D["Friction Force μp
tangential to roll"] D --> E["Exit Tension σ_f
Acts on front face"] B --> F["Internal Horizontal Stress
σ_x balances pressure"] F --> G["Equilibrium Equation
dSigma_x = 2p tan phi dphi - 2μp dphi"] ``` ## 4.5 Neutral Point and Velocity Profile ```mermaid flowchart LR A["v0 Inlet Velocity
less than v_r"] --> B["Velocity Increases
through deformation zone"] B --> C["Neutral Point
v_material = v_r"] C --> D["Velocity Exceeds v_r
v1 greater than v_r"] D --> E["Forward Slip s
equals v1 minus v_r over v_r"] C --> F["Friction Reverses
from opposing to aiding"] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define the terms: (a) Draft, (b) Neutral Point, and (c) Forward Slip in flat rolling.** **Model Answer:** **(a) Draft (Δh):** It is defined as the difference between the initial thickness of the work-piece before entering the rolls and the final thickness after the pass. $$\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$$ It is the primary measure of thickness reduction per pass. **(b) Neutral Point:** It is the point along the arc of contact where the horizontal velocity of the deforming material exactly equals the tangential velocity of the roll surface. At this point, the relative motion between the roll and material is zero, and the friction force reverses direction. **(c) Forward Slip (s):** It is the relative difference between the exit velocity of the strip and the tangential velocity of the roll at the exit plane. It is expressed as: $$s = \frac{v_1 - v_r}{v_r}$$ Forward slip is caused by the increase in velocity of the material as it is squeezed to a smaller thickness while conserving volume. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Distinguish between the contact angle (α) and the neutral angle (φₙ) in rolling. Why is φₙ always less than α?** **Model Answer:** The **contact angle (α)** is the angle subtended at the roll centre by the entire arc of contact between the entry and exit planes. It depends only on the geometry: $$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}}$$ The **neutral angle (φₙ)** is the angle subtended at the roll centre from the entry plane up to the neutral point. It is the point where the material velocity equals the roll surface velocity. The neutral angle is always less than the contact angle because the neutral point always lies between the entry and exit planes, never at the exit. Mathematically, since the friction has a finite value and reverses direction at the neutral point, the equilibrium is satisfied at some interior point, not at the exit. The relationship is: $$\phi_n = \alpha \sqrt{1 - \frac{\mu^2}{2}}$$ --- ## PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **Q. (a)** Derive the expression for the **projected contact length (L)** in flat rolling. State the assumptions used. (7 marks) **(b) A steel slab 100 mm thick is reduced to 75 mm in a single pass using rolls of 500 mm diameter. The mean yield stress of the steel at the rolling temperature is 180 MPa. The roll speed is 30 rpm and the friction coefficient is 0.3. Calculate:** (i) The roll separating force using Hill's formula. (ii) The rolling power assuming a lever-arm factor of 0.45. (iii) The forward slip using the neutral angle approximation. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution to (a):** **Assumptions:** 1. The rolls are perfectly rigid (no elastic flattening). 2. The work-piece is wide enough to neglect the spread in width (plane-strain condition). 3. The roll bite is small so that higher-order terms are neglected. 4. The contact length is much smaller than the roll radius. **Derivation:** Let the roll centre be O, entry plane at A, exit plane at B, and the projection of the contact length on the horizontal plane be CD = L. From the entry point, draw a horizontal line meeting the roll at point E. In the right triangle OCE, applying Pythagoras: $$OC^2 + CE^2 = OE^2$$ Where: - $OC = R - \dfrac{h_0 + h_1}{2}$ (vertical drop of roll centre to mid-thickness) - $CE = \dfrac{L}{2}$ (half of projected contact length) - $OE = R$ (roll radius) Substituting: $$\left(R - \frac{h_0 + h_1}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 = R^2$$ Expanding the square: $$R^2 - R(h_0 + h_1) + \left(\frac{h_0 + h_1}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{L^2}{4} = R^2$$ Cancelling $R^2$ and neglecting the second-order term $\left(\frac{h_0 + h_1}{2}\right)^2$: $$R(h_0 + h_1) = \frac{L^2}{4}$$ Therefore: $$L = \sqrt{R(h_0 + h_1)}$$ For small drafts where $h_0 \approx h_1$, this simplifies to the **Ekelund approximation**: $$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$$ --- **Model Solution to (b):** **Given data:** - $h_0 = 100$ mm, $h_1 = 75$ mm - Roll diameter $D = 500$ mm, so $R = 250$ mm - $\sigma_y = 180$ MPa $= 180$ N/mm² - $N = 30$ rpm - $\mu = 0.3$ - $J_p = 0.45$ **Step 1 — Draft and projected contact length:** $$\Delta h = h_0 - h_1 = 100 - 75 = 25 \text{ mm}$$ $$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{250 \times 25} = \sqrt{6250} = 79.06 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Roll separating force (Hill's formula):** Hill's formula assumes that the average pressure equals twice the shear yield stress (mean flow stress). For plane strain, the mean flow stress $2k_f$ is related to uniaxial yield stress by the von Mises factor: $$2k_f = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_y = 1.155 \times 180 = 207.9 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ The width is not explicitly given; we assume a standard width $w = 1$ m $= 1000$ mm (for force per metre width) or, since the problem does not specify, we compute force per unit width. For force per unit width: $$\bar{p} = 2k_f = 207.9 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $$P = L \cdot w \cdot \bar{p} = 79.06 \times 1000 \times 207.9 = 1.6436 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$ $$P \approx 16.44 \text{ MN per metre width}$$ (If the strip width is given, multiply by the actual width in mm.) **Step 3 — Rolling power:** The lever arm: $$l = J_p \cdot L = 0.45 \times 79.06 = 35.58 \text{ mm}$$ Torque per roll (per metre width): $$T = P \cdot l = 1.6436 \times 10^7 \times 35.58 = 5.847 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm} = 584.7 \text{ kN·m}$$ Total rolling power (two rolls): $$N_{power} = \frac{2 \times 2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \times 2\pi \times 30 \times 584.7 \times 10^3}{60}$$ $$N_{power} = \frac{2.205 \times 10^8}{60} \approx 3.674 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 3674 \text{ kW per metre width}$$ **Step 4 — Forward slip:** Contact angle: $$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}} = \sqrt{\frac{25}{250}} = \sqrt{0.1} = 0.3162 \text{ rad}$$ Neutral angle (Ekelund approximation): $$\phi_n = \frac{\alpha}{2}\left(1 - \frac{\alpha}{2\mu}\right) = \frac{0.3162}{2}\left(1 - \frac{0.3162}{2 \times 0.3}\right)$$ $$\phi_n = 0.1581 \times \left(1 - 0.5270\right) = 0.1581 \times 0.4730 = 0.0748 \text{ rad}$$ Forward slip: $$s = \frac{R^2}{h_1} \phi_n^2 = \frac{250^2}{75} \times 0.0748^2 = \frac{62500}{75} \times 0.005595$$ $$s = 833.33 \times 0.005595 = 4.66$$ This value is unrealistically high — the KTU examiner would accept the formula application as the test of knowledge. The discrepancy arises because the simplified Ekelund formula $\phi_n = (\alpha/2)(1 - \alpha/2\mu)$ is an approximation; the more accurate form $\phi_n = \alpha \sqrt{1 - \mu^2/2}$ gives: $$\phi_n = 0.3162 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.045} = 0.3162 \times 0.9772 = 0.3090 \text{ rad}$$ $$s = \frac{62500}{75} \times 0.3090^2 = 833.33 \times 0.0955 = 79.6$$ This is also too high because the simple formula $s = (R^2/h_1)\phi_n^2$ is dimensionally wrong. The proper expression in radians is: $$s = \phi_n^2 \cdot \frac{R}{h_1} \quad \text{(not used in many KTU texts)}$$ **Note for students:** Always use the formula exactly as prescribed in your KTU textbook. The forward slip is typically very small (a few percent). Use the simplified form: $$s = \frac{v_1 - v_r}{v_r}, \quad \text{where} \quad v_1 = \frac{v_0 h_0}{h_1} \quad \text{and} \quad v_r = \pi D N / 60$$ **Recalculation using volume constancy:** $$v_0 = \frac{\pi D N}{60} \text{ (if no slip) } = \frac{\pi \times 500 \times 30}{60} = 785.4 \text{ mm/s}$$ With friction, $v_0 = v_r \cos\phi_n \approx v_r$ (for small $\phi_n$): $$v_1 = \frac{v_0 h_0}{h_1} = \frac{785.4 \times 100}{75} = 1047.2 \text{ mm/s}$$ $$s = \frac{1047.2 - 785.4}{785.4} = 0.3333 = 33.33\%$$ (Values vary depending on the actual roll speed used; this is the standard KTU approach.) --- ### QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Q. (a)** Explain the concept of the **Neutral Point** and derive the expression for the **Neutral Angle (φₙ)** in flat rolling. (7 marks) **(b) Compare the average roll pressure formulas of **Siebel, Ekelund, and Bland & Ford**. A copper strip 50 mm wide and 25 mm thick is rolled to 20 mm in one pass using 300 mm diameter rolls. The mean yield stress is 250 MPa and the friction coefficient is 0.25. Calculate the rolling load and rolling power at 25 rpm using the **Ekelund formula** (take $J_p = 0.45$). (7 marks) --- **Model Solution to (a):** The **neutral point (no-slip point)** is the location along the arc of contact where the horizontal velocity of the deforming material exactly equals the horizontal component of the roll surface velocity. Friction is zero at this point, and it reverses direction across it. **Derivation of neutral angle:** Consider an elemental slice of work-piece of thickness h at an angle φ from the line of centres. The elemental arc length is $R \, d\phi$. Forces acting on the slice (per unit width): - Horizontal pressure on the right face: $(\sigma_x + d\sigma_x) \cdot (h + dh)$ - Horizontal pressure on the left face: $\sigma_x \cdot h$ - Vertical component of the normal roll pressure: $2p \cdot R \, d\phi \cdot \sin\phi$ - Horizontal component of friction (opposing motion of element): $2\mu p \cdot R \, d\phi \cdot \cos\phi$ Horizontal equilibrium (sum of horizontal forces = 0): $$(\sigma_x + d\sigma_x)(h + dh) - \sigma_x h - 2p R \sin\phi \, d\phi - 2\mu p R \cos\phi \, d\phi = 0$$ Neglecting the product $d\sigma_x \cdot dh$ as second-order small: $$\sigma_x \, dh + h \, d\sigma_x = 2p R (\sin\phi + \mu \cos\phi) \, d\phi$$ From geometry, $h = h_1 + R\phi^2$ (parabolic approximation of the roll profile): $$dh = 2R\phi \, d\phi$$ Substituting and simplifying: $$\sigma_x (2R\phi \, d\phi) + h \, d\sigma_x = 2p R (\sin\phi + \mu \cos\phi) \, d\phi$$ Dividing by $2R \, d\phi$: $$\phi \sigma_x + \frac{h}{2R} \frac{d\sigma_x}{d\phi} = p(\sin\phi + \mu \cos\phi)$$ For the case where the friction is at the limit $\mu = \tan\phi$ (sticking friction everywhere), and integrating with the boundary conditions at entry and exit (neglecting tensions), we obtain: $$\phi_n = \sqrt{\frac{h_0 - h_1}{R}} \sqrt{1 - \frac{\mu^2}{2}}$$ Since $\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$: $$\phi_n = \alpha \sqrt{1 - \frac{\mu^2}{2}}$$ For small $\mu$ (low friction), this approximates to: $$\phi_n \approx \frac{\alpha}{2}\left(1 - \frac{\alpha}{2\mu}\right)$$ The neutral point is always located such that $\phi_n < \alpha$, confirming that it is interior to the contact arc. --- **Model Solution to (b):** **Comparison of formulas:** | Aspect | Siebel | Ekelund | Bland & Ford | |--------|--------|---------|--------------| | **Friction Model** | Friction hill (linear) | Sticking friction, integrated | Exponential friction hill | | **Strain Hardening** | Neglected | Implicit | Explicit | | **Pressure expression** | $1.15\sigma_y(1 + \mu L / 2h_0)$ | $2k_f(e^{\mu L/h_0} - 1)/(\mu L/h_0)$ | $\sigma_0 e^{\mu L/2h_0}$ | | **Best for** | Cold rolling, lubricated | Hot rolling, dry | Cold rolling with hardening | | **Accuracy at high μ** | Low | High | Moderate | **Numerical Problem Solution:** **Given:** - $w = 50$ mm, $h_0 = 25$ mm, $h_1 = 20$ mm - $D = 300$ mm $\Rightarrow R = 150$ mm - $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa $= 250$ N/mm² - $\mu = 0.25$, $N = 25$ rpm, $J_p = 0.45$ **Step 1 — Projected contact length:** $$\Delta h = 25 - 20 = 5 \text{ mm}$$ $$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{150 \times 5} = \sqrt{750} = 27.39 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Mean flow stress (Ekelund uses $2k_f$):** $$2k_f = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sigma_y = 1.155 \times 250 = 288.7 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 3 — Average pressure (Ekelund):** $$\bar{p} = 2k_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{h_0}\right) = 288.7 \left(1 + \frac{0.25 \times 27.39}{25}\right)$$ $$\bar{p} = 288.7 \times (1 + 0.2739) = 288.7 \times 1.2739 = 367.8 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 4 — Rolling load:** $$P = L \cdot w \cdot \bar{p} = 27.39 \times 50 \times 367.8$$ $$P = 503{,}830 \text{ N} \approx 503.8 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — Lever arm and torque:** $$l = J_p \cdot L = 0.45 \times 27.39 = 12.33 \text{ mm}$$ $$T = P \cdot l = 503{,}830 \times 12.33 = 6.213 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 6.213 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 6 — Rolling power:** $$N_{power} = 2 \times \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \times 2\pi \times 25 \times 6213}{60}$$ $$N_{power} = \frac{1.951 \times 10^6}{60} = 32{,}520 \text{ W} \approx 32.5 \text{ kW}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in power:** Many students calculate power for only one roll. Remember that the rolling mill has **two rolls** rotating against the material; total power = $2 \times$ (power per roll). > 2. **Mixing up the flow stress:** $2k_f$ (mean flow stress) is **not** equal to $\sigma_y$. Use $2k_f = 1.155 \sigma_y$ for plane-strain rolling. > 3. **Using wrong unit for L:** L must be in **mm** if pressure is in **N/mm²** and force is in **N**. Mixing SI units (metres and MPa) is a common error. > 4. **Neglecting the lever arm factor:** Torque is $T = P \cdot l$, NOT $T = P \cdot L/2$. The resultant does not act at the geometric centre of the contact length. > 5. **Forward slip formula misuse:** Always use the formula as given in the prescribed KTU textbook; different sources use different approximations. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Roll Separating Force (P):** Vertical force on the work-piece; governs mill housing, screw-down, and roll strength. - **Projected contact length:** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ — the foundation of all force/pressure calculations. - **Contact angle (α):** Geometric; $\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$; maximum draft condition is $\mu \geq \tan\alpha$. - **Neutral angle (φₙ):** $\phi_n = \alpha\sqrt{1 - \mu^2/2}$; located inside the bite, always $\phi_n < \alpha$. - **Forward slip (s):** Strip exits faster than roll surface; $s = (v_1 - v_r)/v_r$; small in practice (2–10 %). - **Hill's formula:** $P = L \cdot w \cdot 2k_f$ — simplest slab analysis. - **Siebel's formula:** Includes friction hill linearly; best for cold rolling. - **Ekelund's formula:** Uses sticking friction integration; preferred for hot rolling. - **Bland & Ford formula:** Exponential friction hill with strain hardening; most accurate for cold rolling. - **Torque per roll:** $T = P \cdot l$, where $l = J_p \cdot L$ and $J_p \approx 0.4$ to $0.5$. - **Rolling power:** $N = 2 \times (2\pi N_{rpm} T)/60$; remember both rolls and unit conversion (1 kW = 1000 W). - **Friction requirement for entry:** $\mu \geq \tan\alpha$ (else the roll cannot "bite" the work-piece). - **Volume constancy:** $v_0 h_0 = v_1 h_1$ — the basis of forward slip. - **Plane-strain factor:** $2k_f = (2/\sqrt{3})\sigma_y = 1.155 \sigma_y$. - **Assumptions of slab analysis:** Uniform pressure across width, no spread, rigid rolls (no flattening). - **Units discipline:** Use mm, N, N/mm², mm², N·mm, rpm, kW consistently. ---

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulk Deformation (Metal Forming) Processes** are manufacturing processes in which a metallic workpiece is plastically deformed under the action of externally applied compressive, tensile, or shear forces to change its shape, size, and properties while retaining mass (volume constancy). Module 3 of PBMET304 covers **Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, and Drawing** — the four pillar processes whose analytical treatment centers on **flow stress (Yf)**, **friction (μ)**, **geometry (L, h, w, R)**, and the resulting **deformation forces, torque, and power**.

In bulk deformation analysis, the objective of any **numerical problem** is to convert the physical geometry of a forming operation into a force/power balance that a student can compute using empirical and analytical formulas derived from **slab method analysis**, **upper bound analysis**, or **energy methods**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Play-Doh Machine"**
> Imagine you are squeezing a tube of toothpaste or rolling clay with a pin. To push the clay thinner, you must push harder the larger the area. The harder the clay resists (flow stress), the more force you need. Now place a rough cloth under the clay — it resists sliding and needs even more force. This is exactly what happens in forging, rolling, extrusion, and drawing:
> - **Force = (How much the metal resists deforming) × (Contact Area) × (Friction Factor)**
> - The **numerical problems** in this module are nothing but translating this "squeezing" intuition into math.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted for KTU Board)

- **Coefficient of Friction (μ) for hot metal forming:** **0.1 – 0.5** (typical: **0.2**)
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ) for cold metal forming:** **0.05 – 0.15** (typical: **0.08**)
- **Flow Stress (Yf) for mild steel at room temperature:** **~300 – 600 MPa**
- **Flow Stress (Yf) for hot steel (1000 °C):** **~10 – 50 MPa**
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **π (pi):** **3.14159**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Every numerical problem in Module 3 hinges on three pillars — (1) **Yf** (material resistance), (2) **μ** (interface friction), and (3) **Geometry** (contact area, length, draft). Master these three and the rest is algebraic substitution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Slab Method (KTU High-Yield Concept)

The **slab method** is the workhorse analytical technique used in KTU 2024 problems. Its philosophy:

1. Slice the deforming body into thin **"slabs"** perpendicular to the direction of material flow.
2. Apply **force equilibrium** on each slab (sum of pressures + friction forces = 0).
3. Integrate along the deformation zone to obtain the **total forming force**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU problems?** Almost every "calculate the forging/rolling/extrusion force" question in the KTU 2024 paper is solved by setting up a **slab equilibrium** and integrating across the deformation zone. The integral collapse is what gives you the closed-form formulas in the table below.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Process | Key Formulas | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|--------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Open-Die Forging (Disc)** | $P_f = Y_f\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu\,d}{3\,h}\right)$ | $P_f$ = avg. forging pressure (Pa) |
| 2 | **Open-Die Forging Load (Disc)** | $F = Y_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu\,d}{3\,h}\right)$ | $F$ = forging force (N), $d$ = dia (m), $h$ = height (m) |
| 3 | **Open-Die Forging (Rectangular)** | $F = Y_f \cdot w \cdot t \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu\,w}{3\,t}\right)$ | $w$ = width, $t$ = thickness (m) |
| 4 | **Rolling — Contact Length** | $L = \sqrt{R\,\Delta h}$ | $R$ = roll radius (m), $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$ (m) |
| 5 | **Rolling — True Strain** | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | $h_0$, $h_f$ = entry & exit thickness (m) |
| 6 | **Rolling Force (No Friction)** | $F = Y_f \cdot L \cdot w$ | $w$ = strip width (m) |
| 7 | **Rolling Force (With Friction)** | $F = Y_f \cdot L \cdot w \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{h_{\text{avg}}}\right)$ | $h_{\text{avg}} = (h_0 + h_f)/2$ |
| 8 | **Rolling Torque** | $T \approx 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$ | $T$ = torque (N·m) |
| 9 | **Rolling Power** | $P = 2\pi N \cdot T$ | $N$ = roll speed (rev/s), $P$ (W) |
| 10 | **Maximum Draft (No Slip)** | $\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R$ | limiting draft (m) |
| 11 | **Direct Extrusion Force** | $F = Y_f A_0 \ln R_x + \pi D_b L_b Y_f$ | $R_x = A_0/A_f$, $D_b$ = billet dia, $L_b$ = billet length |
| 12 | **Indirect Extrusion Force** | $F = Y_f A_0 \ln R_x$ | (no billet-container friction term) |
| 13 | **Drawing Stress** | $\sigma_d = Y_f \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)(1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\tan\alpha})$ | $\alpha$ = die semi-angle (rad) |
| 14 | **Drawing Force** | $F_d = \sigma_d \cdot A_f$ | $A_f$ = final area (m²) |
| 15 | **Volume Constancy** | $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$ | $A_0, A_f$ = initial & final areas |
| 16 | **Friction Hill (Forging)** | $K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu\,d}{3\,h}$ (for disc) | $K_f$ = forging shape factor (dimensionless) |
| 17 | **Power in Deformation** | $P = F \cdot v$ | $v$ = ram/strip velocity (m/s) |
| 18 | **Ideal Work (Homogeneous)** | $W = Y_f \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon$ | $V$ = volume, $\varepsilon$ = true strain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In rolling, students often confuse **draft ($\Delta h$)** with **strain**. **Draft** is the linear reduction ($\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$); **strain** is the logarithmic ratio ($\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$). They are **not** the same. Marks are lost if the two are interchanged.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Crankshafts:** Open-die forging force calculations predict the **press tonnage** required.
- **Steel I-Beams:** Hot rolling force determines the **roll separating force** and motor rating.
- **Aluminium Extrusions (window frames):** Indirect extrusion force tells you the **ram capacity** needed.
- **Copper Wire Manufacturing:** Drawing stress dictates the **capstan power** and the number of dies in series.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Numerical Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problems in this section** are calibrated to the **typical 14-mark KTU University Exam pattern** for PBMET304. Each problem is solved with full algebraic transparency — no step is skipped.

---

### **Problem 1: Open-Die Forging of a Cylindrical Steel Billet [14-Mark Standard]**

**Question:** A steel cylindrical billet of initial diameter $d_0 = 100 \text{ mm}$ and height $h_0 = 150 \text{ mm}$ is forged in an open-die press. The final height after forging is $h_f = 75 \text{ mm}$. The flow stress of steel is $Y_f = 400 \text{ MPa}$ and the coefficient of friction at the die–workpiece interface is $\mu = 0.2$. Calculate:
(a) The **forging load (F)** and the **average forging pressure (Pf)**.
(b) The **ideal work** of deformation (assuming homogeneous deformation, no friction) and the **extra work done against friction**.

#### Given Data (Step 0 — Tabulation)

| Symbol | Quantity | Value | Unit |
|--------|----------|-------|------|
| $d_0$ | Initial diameter | 100 | mm |
| $h_0$ | Initial height | 150 | mm |
| $h_f$ | Final height | 75 | mm |
| $Y_f$ | Flow stress | 400 | MPa |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction | 0.2 | — |

#### Part (a) — Forging Load & Average Pressure

**Step 1 — Apply Volume Constancy to find final diameter.**

$$
A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f
$$

$$
\dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4} \cdot h_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot h_f
$$

$$
d_f^2 = d_0^2 \cdot \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} = (100)^2 \cdot \dfrac{150}{75} = 10000 \cdot 2 = 20000
$$

$$
d_f = \sqrt{20000} = 141.42 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Forging Shape Factor $K_f$ (Friction Hill) for a disc.**

$$
K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu\, d_f}{3\, h_f}
$$

$$
K_f = 1 + \dfrac{0.2 \times 141.42}{3 \times 75} = 1 + \dfrac{28.284}{225} = 1 + 0.1257 = 1.1257
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Final Cross-Sectional Area.**

$$
A_f = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.14142)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.02}{4} = 0.015708 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Forging Load F.**

$$
F = Y_f \cdot A_f \cdot K_f
$$

$$
F = 400 \times 10^6 \times 0.015708 \times 1.1257
$$

$$
F = 400 \times 10^6 \times 0.017679 = 7.072 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 7072 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Average Forging Pressure.**

$$
P_f = \dfrac{F}{A_f} = Y_f \cdot K_f = 400 \times 10^6 \times 1.1257 = 4.503 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 450.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $F = 7072 \text{ kN}$, $P_f = 450.3 \text{ MPa}$

#### Part (b) — Ideal Work and Frictional Extra Work

**Step 6 — Compute True Strain $\varepsilon$.**

$$
\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\dfrac{150}{75}\right) = \ln(2) = 0.6931
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Volume of the Billet (constant during deformation).**

$$
V = A_f \cdot h_f = 0.015708 \times 0.075 = 1.1781 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 8 — Compute Ideal Homogeneous Work (no friction).**

$$
W_{\text{ideal}} = Y_f \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon
$$

$$
W_{\text{ideal}} = 400 \times 10^6 \times 1.1781 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6931
$$

$$
W_{\text{ideal}} = 400 \times 10^6 \times 8.166 \times 10^{-4} = 3.266 \times 10^5 \text{ J} = 326.6 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute Actual Work from the Friction Hill Integral.**

The total forging work with friction is given by:

$$
W_{\text{actual}} = Y_f \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon \cdot K_f
$$

$$
W_{\text{actual}} = 326.6 \times 1.1257 = 367.6 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 10 — Extra Work Done Against Friction.**

$$
W_{\text{friction}} = W_{\text{actual}} - W_{\text{ideal}} = 367.6 - 326.6 = 41.0 \text{ kJ}
$$

> **Answer (b):** $W_{\text{ideal}} = 326.6 \text{ kJ}$, $W_{\text{friction}} = 41.0 \text{ kJ}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The **friction hill** factor $K_f > 1$ shows that friction inflates the required force by **12.57 %** in this case. Always quote the **shape factor** explicitly in your answer.

---

### **Problem 2: Hot Rolling of a Steel Strip [14-Mark Standard]**

**Question:** A steel strip of width $w = 250 \text{ mm}$ is hot-rolled from an initial thickness $h_0 = 20 \text{ mm}$ to a final thickness $h_f = 10 \text{ mm}$. The roll radius is $R = 300 \text{ mm}$ and the rolls rotate at $N = 20 \text{ rpm}$. The average flow stress of the steel at this temperature is $Y_f = 150 \text{ MPa}$, and the coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.3$. Calculate:
(a) The **roll separating force (F)**, **rolling torque (T)**, and **rolling power (P)**.
(b) Verify whether the **draft is allowable** (i.e., $\Delta h \le \mu^2 R$) and, if not, the **maximum possible reduction per pass**.

#### Given Data

| Symbol | Quantity | Value | Unit |
|--------|----------|-------|------|
| $w$ | Strip width | 250 | mm |
| $h_0$ | Initial thickness | 20 | mm |
| $h_f$ | Final thickness | 10 | mm |
| $R$ | Roll radius | 300 | mm |
| $N$ | Roll speed | 20 | rpm |
| $Y_f$ | Flow stress | 150 | MPa |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction | 0.3 | — |

#### Part (a) — Roll Separating Force, Torque, Power

**Step 1 — Compute the Draft $\Delta h$.**

$$
\Delta h = h_0 - h_f = 20 - 10 = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.010 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Contact Length L.**

$$
L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{300 \times 10} = \sqrt{3000} = 54.77 \text{ mm} = 0.05477 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Average Thickness $h_{\text{avg}}$.**

$$
h_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{h_0 + h_f}{2} = \dfrac{20 + 10}{2} = 15 \text{ mm} = 0.015 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Friction Hill Multiplier for Rolling.**

$$
1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{h_{\text{avg}}} = 1 + \dfrac{0.3 \times 0.05477}{0.015} = 1 + \dfrac{0.01643}{0.015} = 1 + 1.0954 = 2.0954
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Roll Separating Force F.**

$$
F = Y_f \cdot L \cdot w \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{h_{\text{avg}}}\right)
$$

$$
F = 150 \times 10^6 \times 0.05477 \times 0.250 \times 2.0954
$$

$$
F = 150 \times 10^6 \times 0.02869 = 4.303 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 4303 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Rolling Torque T.**

$$
T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 4.303 \times 10^6 \times 0.05477
$$

$$
T = 1.179 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m} = 117.9 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Rolling Power P.**

Convert $N$ to rev/s: $N = 20 \text{ rpm} = 20/60 = 0.3333 \text{ rev/s}$

$$
P = 2\pi N T = 2\pi \times 0.3333 \times 1.179 \times 10^5
$$

$$
P = 2.0944 \times 1.179 \times 10^5 = 2.469 \times 10^5 \text{ W} = 246.9 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $F = 4303 \text{ kN}$, $T = 117.9 \text{ kN·m}$, $P = 246.9 \text{ kW}$

#### Part (b) — Verify the Draft Limit

**Step 8 — Compute the Maximum Allowable Draft $\Delta h_{\max}$.**

$$
\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R = (0.3)^2 \times 300 = 0.09 \times 300 = 27 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 9 — Compare with Actual Draft.**

$$
\Delta h = 10 \text{ mm} \le 27 \text{ mm} = \Delta h_{\max} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Draft is allowable.)}
$$

**Step 10 — Maximum Reduction per Pass (if not allowable, the calculated $\Delta h_{\max} = 27$ mm = 0.027 m).**

> **Answer (b):** Since $10 \text{ mm} < 27 \text{ mm}$, the draft is allowable. The maximum possible reduction is $\Delta h_{\max} = 27 \text{ mm}$ per pass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The condition $\Delta h \le \mu^2 R$ is the **Siebel/Ford no-slip condition**. If it is violated, the rolls will **slip on the workpiece** and the strip will not be drawn into the rolls. Always check this in KTU numerical problems.

---

### **Problem 3: Direct Extrusion of an Aluminium Billet [14-Mark Standard]**

**Question:** A cylindrical aluminium billet of initial length $L_b = 200 \text{ mm}$ and diameter $D_b = 100 \text{ mm}$ is extruded in a direct extrusion press. The extrusion ratio is $R_x = 4$ (i.e., the final cross-sectional area is one-fourth of the billet area). The flow stress of aluminium is $Y_f = 80 \text{ MPa}$, and the coefficient of friction at the billet–container wall is $\mu = 0.1$. Calculate:
(a) The **direct extrusion force (F)**.
(b) The **ideal work of extrusion** and the **frictional work at the container wall**.

#### Part (a) — Direct Extrusion Force

**Step 1 — Compute Initial and Final Areas.**

$$
A_0 = \dfrac{\pi D_b^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_f = \dfrac{A_0}{R_x} = \dfrac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Homogeneous Deformation Work Term.**

$$
Y_f A_0 \ln R_x = 80 \times 10^6 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln(4)
$$

$$
= 80 \times 10^6 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.3863
$$

$$
= 80 \times 10^6 \times 0.01089 = 8.712 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 871.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Container Friction Term.**

$$
\pi D_b L_b Y_f = \pi \times 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 80 \times 10^6
$$

$$
= \pi \times 0.02 \times 80 \times 10^6 = 5.0265 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 5026.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Direct Extrusion Force.**

$$
F = 871.2 + 5026.5 = 5897.7 \text{ kN}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $F = 5897.7 \text{ kN}$

#### Part (b) — Ideal & Frictional Work

**Step 5 — Ideal Work (homogeneous deformation only, no friction).**

$$
W_{\text{ideal}} = 871.2 \text{ kN·m} \times \text{displacement} \dots
$$

For ideal work in terms of force × displacement, since ideal work is the work done by the **homogeneous deformation term only**, the equivalent "ideal" work per unit stroke:

$$
W_{\text{ideal}} = F_{\text{ideal}} \cdot L_b = 871.2 \times 10^3 \times 0.2 = 1.742 \times 10^5 \text{ J} = 174.2 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 6 — Frictional Work at the Container Wall.**

$$
W_{\text{friction}} = \pi D_b L_b Y_f \cdot L_b = 5026.5 \times 10^3 \times 0.2 = 1.005 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 1005.3 \text{ kJ}
$$

> **Answer (b):** $W_{\text{ideal}} = 174.2 \text{ kJ}$, $W_{\text{friction}} = 1005.3 \text{ kJ}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Note how the **friction term dominates** in direct extrusion (5026.5 kN vs 871.2 kN). This is why **indirect extrusion** is used industrially for long billets — it eliminates the container friction term and can reduce the ram force by **30 – 50 %**.

---

### **Problem 4: Wire Drawing [KTU 3-Mark Style Numerical]**

**Question:** A copper wire is drawn from an initial diameter $d_0 = 8 \text{ mm}$ to a final diameter $d_f = 6 \text{ mm}$ through a conical die with semi-angle $\alpha = 6°$. The average flow stress is $Y_f = 250 \text{ MPa}$ and $\mu = 0.08$. Calculate the **drawing stress ($\sigma_d$)** and the **drawing force ($F_d$)**.

**Step 1 — Areas.**

$$
A_0 = \dfrac{\pi (0.008)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_f = \dfrac{\pi (0.006)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Drawing Stress.**

$$
\sigma_d = Y_f \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\tan\alpha}\right)
$$

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{5.027}{2.827}\right) = \ln(1.778) = 0.5754
$$

$$
\tan(6°) = 0.1051
$$

$$
1 + \dfrac{0.08}{0.1051} = 1 + 0.7612 = 1.7612
$$

$$
\sigma_d = 250 \times 10^6 \times 0.5754 \times 1.7612 = 2.533 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 253.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Drawing Force.**

$$
F_d = \sigma_d \cdot A_f = 253.3 \times 10^6 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-5} = 7161 \text{ N} = 7.16 \text{ kN}
$$

> **Answer:** $\sigma_d = 253.3 \text{ MPa}$, $F_d = 7.16 \text{ kN}$

---

```mermaid
    E2 --> F2[Check Draft Limit mu squared R]
    E3 --> F3[Add Container Friction if Direct]
    E4 --> F4[Apply Die Angle Correction]
    F1 --> G1[Compute Forging Force F]
    F2 --> G2[Compute Rolling Force Torque Power]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define **flow stress ($Y_f$)** and explain how it varies with **strain**, **strain rate**, and **temperature**. **[3 Marks]**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Flow stress (Yf)** is the instantaneous **true stress** required to continue plastic deformation of a material at a given strain, strain rate, and temperature. It is expressed as:
> $$ Y_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^m $$
> - **Strain ($\varepsilon$):** Flow stress increases with strain due to **strain hardening** (higher $n$ ⇒ more hardening).
> - **Strain rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}$):** Higher strain rate increases Yf (strain rate sensitivity $m > 0$).
> - **Temperature (T):** Flow stress **decreases** with rising temperature due to increased atomic mobility and dynamic recovery/recrystallization.
> Thus, **hot working uses lower Yf** (less force) but risks oxidation, while **cold working uses higher Yf** but yields better surface finish and dimensional accuracy.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** What is the **friction hill** in metal forming? Write the friction hill expression for **open-die forging of a disc**. **[3 Marks]**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **friction hill** is the rise in pressure at the die–workpiece interface caused by interface friction. Because friction opposes metal flow radially outward, additional pressure is required to overcome it. For open-die forging of a disc:
> $$ P_f = Y_f \cdot K_f = Y_f \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu d}{3 h}\right) $$
> where $d$ and $h$ are the **current** diameter and height, $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction, and $Y_f$ is the flow stress. As $h$ decreases (during forging), $K_f$ grows — the pressure "hill" rises sharply as the workpiece gets thinner.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** A cylindrical steel billet of diameter 80 mm and height 120 mm is forged in open dies to a final height of 60 mm. The flow stress is $Y_f = 350$ MPa and $\mu = 0.15$. Calculate the **forging load** and **average forging pressure**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A copper rod of diameter 12 mm is drawn through a conical die of semi-angle $8°$ to a diameter of 8 mm. Given $Y_f = 280$ MPa and $\mu = 0.1$, compute the **drawing stress** and **drawing force**. Also find the **maximum reduction possible in one pass** (theoretical, ignoring buckling). **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Final diameter from volume constancy:**

$$
d_f = d_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}} = 80 \sqrt{\dfrac{120}{60}} = 80 \sqrt{2} = 113.14 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Friction hill factor:**

$$
K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu d_f}{3 h_f} = 1 + \dfrac{0.15 \times 113.14}{3 \times 60} = 1 + \dfrac{16.97}{180} = 1 + 0.0943 = 1.0943
$$

**Step 3 — Final area:**

$$
A_f = \dfrac{\pi (0.11314)^2}{4} = 0.01005 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Forging force:**

$$
F = Y_f A_f K_f = 350 \times 10^6 \times 0.01005 \times 1.0943 = 3.85 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 3850 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Average forging pressure:**

$$
P_f = Y_f K_f = 350 \times 1.0943 = 383.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $F = 3850 \text{ kN}$, $P_f = 383.0 \text{ MPa}$

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Areas:**

$$
A_0 = \dfrac{\pi (0.012)^2}{4} = 1.131 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \quad ; \quad A_f = \dfrac{\pi (0.008)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Drawing stress:**

$$
\sigma_d = Y_f \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\tan\alpha}\right)
$$

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{1.131 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = \ln(2.25) = 0.8109
$$

$$
\tan 8° = 0.1405 \quad ; \quad 1 + \dfrac{0.1}{0.1405} = 1.7117
$$

$$
\sigma_d = 280 \times 10^6 \times 0.8109 \times 1.7117 = 3.886 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 388.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Drawing force:**

$$
F_d = \sigma_d A_f = 388.6 \times 10^6 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-5} = 19534 \text{ N} = 19.53 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum reduction (theoretical, ideal work = yield stress × strain):**

For no-buckling, ideal maximum reduction is when $\sigma_d \le Y_f$ (very rough theoretical limit, considering only ideal work):

$$
\varepsilon_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \mu/\tan\alpha} = \dfrac{1}{1.7117} = 0.5842
$$

$$
\dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = e^{0.5842} = 1.7936 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{reduction} = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1.7936} = 0.4424 = 44.24\%
$$

> **Answer (b):** $\sigma_d = 388.6 \text{ MPa}$, $F_d = 19.53 \text{ kN}$, max reduction ≈ **44.24 %**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** A hot-rolling mill with roll radius $R = 250$ mm reduces a 200 mm wide steel plate from 15 mm to 8 mm in one pass. The roll speed is 25 rpm, flow stress $Y_f = 180$ MPa, and $\mu = 0.25$. Calculate the **roll separating force**, **torque**, and **rolling power**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A brass billet of diameter 60 mm and length 250 mm is direct-extruded with an extrusion ratio of 3.5. Given $Y_f = 100$ MPa and $\mu = 0.08$, calculate the **direct extrusion force** and compare it with the **indirect extrusion force**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Draft and contact length:**

$$
\Delta h = 15 - 8 = 7 \text{ mm} \quad ; \quad L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{250 \times 7} = \sqrt{1750} = 41.83 \text{ mm} = 0.04183 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify draft limit:**

$$
\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R = 0.0625 \times 250 = 15.625 \text{ mm} > 7 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Average thickness & friction multiplier:**

$$
h_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{15+8}{2} = 11.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0115 \text{ m}
$$

$$
1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{h_{\text{avg}}} = 1 + \dfrac{0.25 \times 0.04183}{0.0115} = 1 + 0.9093 = 1.9093
$$

**Step 4 — Roll separating force:**

$$
F = Y_f L w \cdot (1.9093) = 180 \times 10^6 \times 0.04183 \times 0.200 \times 1.9093 = 2.876 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 2876 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Torque and Power:**

$$
T = 0.5 F L = 0.5 \times 2.876 \times 10^6 \times 0.04183 = 6.014 \times 10^4 \text{ N·m} = 60.14 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
P = 2\pi N T = 2\pi \times \dfrac{25}{60} \times 6.014 \times 10^4 = 1.575 \times 10^5 \text{ W} = 157.5 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $F = 2876 \text{ kN}$, $T = 60.14 \text{ kN·m}$, $P = 157.5 \text{ kW}$

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Areas:**

$$
A_0 = \dfrac{\pi (0.06)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \quad ; \quad A_f = \dfrac{A_0}{3.5} = 8.077 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Direct extrusion force:**

$$
F_{\text{direct}} = Y_f A_0 \ln R_x + \pi D_b L_b Y_f
$$

$$
= 100 \times 10^6 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln(3.5) + \pi \times 0.06 \times 0.25 \times 100 \times 10^6
$$

$$
= 100 \times 10^6 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2528 + 4.712 \times 10^6
$$

$$
= 354.2 \times 10^3 + 4712.4 \times 10^3 = 5066.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Indirect extrusion force:**

$$
F_{\text{indirect}} = Y_f A_0 \ln R_x = 354.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

$$
\dfrac{F_{\text{direct}} - F_{\text{indirect}}}{F_{\text{direct}}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{5066.6 - 354.2}{5066.6} \times 100\% = 93.0\%
$$

> **Answer (b):** $F_{\text{direct}} = 5066.6 \text{ kN}$, $F_{\text{indirect}} = 354.2 \text{ kN}$. Indirect extrusion reduces the force by **~93 %** — a dramatic industrial advantage.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in PBMET304 Module 3 Numerical Problems (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing mm and m in the same formula. The pressure $Y_f$ is in **Pa**, so all lengths **must be in metres**. Convert mm → m at the START.
> 2. **Using Initial vs Final Dimensions in the Friction Hill (–2 marks):** The friction hill formula uses the **current** dimensions, not the initial ones. As forging progresses, $d$ grows and $h$ shrinks — use the **final** dimensions when computing peak load.
> 3. **Forgetting the Friction Term in Rolling (–2 marks):** Many students write $F = Y_f L w$ and stop. KTU problems **always** include the friction term. Add $(1 + \mu L / h_{\text{avg}})$.
> 4. **Forgetting to Check $\Delta h \le \mu^2 R$ (–1 mark):** KTU explicitly tests the **no-slip condition**. Mention it explicitly even if it is satisfied.
> 5. **Confusing $\ln$ with $\log_{10}$ (–1 mark):** The KTU board strictly uses **natural logarithm ($\ln$)** in strain and extrusion formulas. Scientific calculators are permitted in KTU exams — set to **LN mode**.
> 6. **Skipping the "Friction Hill" Derivation in Part (a) (–1 mark):** Always show the **substitution steps** of $K_f$ — partial-step answers are penalized.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Rapid-Fire Revision — Must Remember for KTU 2024 ESE**

- [x] **Volume constancy** $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$ is the FIRST equation to apply in any forming problem.
- [x] **Flow stress ($Y_f$)** has units of **Pa** (or MPa). Use SI throughout.
- [x] **Friction hill (forging disc):** $K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu d}{3 h}$. Use **final** $d$ and $h$.
- [x] **Rolling contact length:** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$. **Maximum draft:** $\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R$.
- [x] **Rolling force (with friction):** $F = Y_f L w \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{h_{\text{avg}}}\right)$. **Torque:** $T = 0.5 F L$. **Power:** $P = 2\pi N T$.
- [x] **Direct extrusion:** $F = Y_f A_0 \ln R_x + \pi D_b L_b Y_f$ (includes billet-container friction).
- [x] **Indirect extrusion:** $F = Y_f A_0 \ln R_x$ (no friction term — ram force much lower).
- [x] **Drawing stress:** $\sigma_d = Y_f \ln(A_0/A_f) (1 + \mu/\tan\alpha)$. **Force:** $F_d = \sigma_d A_f$.
- [x] **Strain:** $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f) = \ln(A_0/A_f)$ (volume constancy).
- [x] **Ideal work (homogeneous):** $W = Y_f V \varepsilon$.
- [x] **Always** verify units, **always** check the no-slip condition for rolling, **always** show the friction hill multiplier in forging.
- [x] **Industrial Insight:** Indirect extrusion saves up to **30 – 50 %** of the ram force compared to direct extrusion — a frequent KTU "application-based" question.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rolling Mill (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **rolling mill** is a machine unit used in metal forming that performs the **plastic deformation** of a workpiece by passing it through one or more pairs of **counter-rotating rolls** under compressive forces, thereby reducing the cross-sectional area and/or altering the shape of the material.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBMET304 – Manufacturing Processes)**, the classification of rolling mills is based on:
1. **Number and arrangement of rolls**
2. **Direction of material flow** (continuous / reversing)
3. **Product geometry** (flat / section / tube)
4. **Mechanical configuration** (cluster, planetary, universal, etc.)

## 1.2 The Underlying Principle of Rolling

Rolling is a **compression-dominated forming process** governed by the equation:

$$\sigma_f = Y_f \cdot \varepsilon^n$$

where **Y_f** is the material flow strength, **ε** is the true strain, and **n** is the strain-hardening exponent. The roll applies a **normal pressure (p)** that exceeds the **flow stress** of the workpiece to induce plastic flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All rolling mill designs are essentially different mechanical answers to the same engineering problem — *applying sufficient compressive force on the workpiece while keeping the rolls themselves from deflecting or flattening.*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Explanation)

Think of a **rolling pin** used in your kitchen to flatten dough. The dough is squeezed thinner as it passes between the two rotating cylinders. Now imagine:
- The dough becomes **work-hardened** (tougher) → you need more force.
- The dough must come out **uniformly flat** → the cylinders must not bend.
- You want the dough to keep coming out continuously → automate the feeding.

A rolling mill is just a **super-sized, precision-engineered, motorized rolling pin** built for steel, aluminum, copper, and other metals. The different "types" of rolling mills are different **engineering solutions** to scaling this simple concept for industrial production.

### 1.4 Engineering Significance

| Application Sector | Typical Mill Type Used |
|---|---|
| Hot strip mills (steel) | Tandem (continuous) 4-high |
| Cold rolling of sheet | Tandem 4-high / Sendzimir |
| Heavy plate production | 4-high reversing / Universal |
| Rails & structural sections | 2-high reversing / 3-high |
| Seamless tubes | Mannesmann / Plug mill |
| Foil production | Sendzimir (cluster) Z-mill |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners expect students to know the **unique feature**, **typical application**, and **advantage/disadvantage** of each mill type. Memorize the *distinguishing characteristic* of each.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic

Rolling mills are classified along the following **KTU-recognized axes**:

1. **By number of rolls** — 2-high, 3-high, 4-high, 6-high, 12-high (cluster)
2. **By operational mode** — Reversing, Non-reversing, Continuous (Tandem)
3. **By product shape** — Flat-product mills, Section mills, Tube mills
4. **By specialized geometry** — Universal, Planetary, Cross-country, Mannesmann
5. **By temperature** — Hot rolling mill, Cold rolling mill

## 2.2 Detailed Operational Concept of Each Major Type

### 2.2.1 Two-High Rolling Mill
- **Configuration:** Two rolls arranged horizontally, one above the other.
- **Variants:**
  - **Reversing type:** Roll rotation direction is reversed after each pass. Used for **heavy ingot breakdown** (slabbing, blooming).
  - **Non-reversing type:** Rolls rotate in one direction; workpiece is fed, rolled, and ejected on the exit side.
- **Why it matters:** Simplest design, but the rolls are subjected to **high bending deflection** for wide workpieces.

### 2.2.2 Three-High Rolling Mill (Lauth Mill)
- **Configuration:** Three rolls stacked vertically — upper, middle, lower.
- **Mechanism:** The middle roll is fixed; the upper and lower rolls move vertically. Rolling is done in **one direction on one pass**, and the **opposite direction on the return pass**.
- **Advantage:** No reversing time loss → higher productivity than 2-high reversing.
- **Limitation:** The middle roll is heavily loaded and prone to wear.

### 2.2.3 Four-High Rolling Mill
- **Configuration:** Two **small working rolls** backed by two **large backup rolls**.
- **Mechanism:** The backup rolls prevent deflection of the small working rolls, enabling **precise thin-section rolling** with excellent flatness.
- **Why it matters:** Industry **workhorse** for both hot and cold strip rolling.

### 2.2.5 Cluster Rolling Mill (Sendzimir / Z-Mill)
- **Configuration:** A single small work roll is supported by **multiple backup rolls arranged in a cluster** (typically 2 intermediate rolls + 1 set of 8 backing bearing assemblies).
- **Advantage:** Extremely **stiff**, allows rolling of very thin foils (down to **0.0025 mm**).
- **Disadvantage:** Extremely expensive, complex setup.

### 2.2.6 Tandem (Continuous) Rolling Mill
- **Configuration:** A series of 2-high or 4-high stands arranged in sequence.
- **Mechanism:** Material passes continuously through multiple stands, with each stand reducing thickness progressively.
- **Application:** **Hot strip mills, cold strip mills** for mass production.

### 2.2.7 Universal Rolling Mill
- **Configuration:** Two **horizontal rolls** + two **vertical edging rolls**.
- **Application:** Producing wide-flange beams, H-sections with perfect flange edges.

### 2.2.8 Planetary Rolling Mill
- **Configuration:** Two large backup rolls surrounded by **~20 satellite working rolls** orbiting around them.
- **Mechanism:** Achieves **massive reduction (up to 95%) in a single pass** — typically used to convert a thick slab directly to a thin strip.

### 2.2.9 Mannesmann / Cross-Country / Plug Mills (Tube Mills)
- Specialized mills for **seamless tube production** using piercing, elongating, and sizing.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are testable in KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments for PBMET304.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Definition of Terms | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True Strain in Rolling | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | $h_0$ = entry thickness, $h_f$ = exit thickness | dimensionless |
| 2 | Draft (Absolute Reduction) | $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$ | Reduction per pass | mm |
| 3 | Coefficient of Friction (Ekelund) | $\mu = 0.8 - 0.01 \, T$ | $T$ = rolling temperature (°C) | dimensionless |
| 4 | Contact Length Approximation | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | $R$ = roll radius | mm |
| 5 | Projected Contact Area | $A_p = L \cdot w$ | $w$ = width of strip | mm² |
| 6 | Average Roll Pressure | $p_{avg} = Y_f \cdot Q_{pf}$ | $Y_f$ = flow stress, $Q_{pf} = 1.08$ (typical) | MPa |
| 7 | Roll Separating Force | $F = p_{avg} \cdot A_p = Y_f \cdot Q_{pf} \cdot w \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | The vertical force pushing the rolls apart | N (kN) |
| 8 | Rolling Torque | $T = F \cdot L \cdot 0.5$ (approx) or $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$ | Moment to drive each roll | N·m |
| 9 | Rolling Power | $P = 2 \pi N T$ | $N$ = roll rpm | W (kW) |
| 10 | Forward Slip | $s = \dfrac{v_f - v_r}{v_r}$ | $v_f$ = exit velocity, $v_r$ = roll velocity | dimensionless |
| 11 | Neutral Angle | $\tan \alpha_n = \dfrac{\mu}{2} - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2L}$ | Angle where material velocity = roll velocity | rad |
| 12 | Maximum Draft (No Slip) | $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$ | Limit before workpiece slips | mm |
| 13 | Roll Flattening (Hitchcock) | $R' = R \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{C \cdot p}{w \cdot \Delta h}\right]$ | $C$ = roll material constant (m) | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not confuse the contact length L with the roll length.** *Contact length* is the arc length of roll-workpiece engagement (~5–25 mm). *Roll length* is the barrel length (~1000–2500 mm).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive body panels:** Cold-rolled 4-high tandem mills produce deep-drawing-quality sheet steel.
- **Aluminum beverage cans:** Sendzimir mills roll AA5182 aluminum alloy to **0.25 mm** with ±0.005 mm tolerance.
- **Construction I-beams:** Universal mills produce **parallel-flange sections** faster than traditional 2-high section mills.
- **Tinplate for food cans:** Temper mills using 4-high configuration provide precise surface finish and hardness control.
