# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions are modeled on the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. Two **3-mark Part A** questions, followed by an internal choice **14-mark Part B** question with two full sub-parts each. Model answers are exhaustive to align with KTU Board valuation key expectations.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define compression forming in manufacturing. List any four processes that fall under this category.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Compression forming refers to a class of bulk metal forming processes in which the workpiece is plastically deformed primarily by compressive forces applied through dies, rolls, rams, or punches, without removal of material. The metal flows plastically when the applied stress exceeds its flow stress (Yf).

**Any Four Processes (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Forging** — shaping by hammer or press.
2. **Rolling** — reducing thickness by passing between rolls.
3. **Extrusion** — forcing material through a die.
4. **Drawing** — pulling a rod or wire through a die.
5. **Swaging** — radial hammering.
6. **Coining** — closed-die compression for fine detail.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by the "friction hill" in flat rolling? Sketch and label the pressure distribution along the contact arc.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The **friction hill** refers to the characteristic rise and fall of roll pressure along the contact arc between the roll and the workpiece during flat rolling. The pressure rises from the entry point, reaches a maximum at the **neutral point** (no-slip point), and then falls toward the exit point — forming a "hill" shape.

**Explanation (1 Mark):**
- On the **entry side**, the roll surface moves faster than the material; friction drags the material into the roll gap, requiring high pressure.
- At the **neutral point**, the velocities of the roll and material are equal; no relative motion exists.
- On the **exit side**, the material moves faster than the roll surface; friction now acts in the opposite direction, and the pressure decreases.

**Sketch Labeling (1 Mark):**
Pressure (y-axis) vs. horizontal position (x-axis). Peak labeled as "Neutral Point", entry zone labeled "No-slip zone", exit zone labeled "Slip zone".

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Internal Choice Question

> **ESE Module 3 Internal Choice (Pick ONE of the two):**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** With neat sketches, explain the **open-die forging** and **closed-die forging** processes. Compare the two in terms of dimensional accuracy, equipment cost, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A cylindrical steel workpiece of diameter **60 mm** and height **80 mm** is to be **upset** (cold forging) in open-die compression. The flow stress of steel at the cold-working condition is **Yf = 600 MPa**, and the coefficient of friction at the die-workpiece interface is **µ = 0.15**. Find the **average forging pressure** required for the operation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Open-Die Forging — Working Principle (1.5 Marks):**
Open-die forging (also called **smith forging** or **free forging**) uses two flat or simply-shaped dies that do not fully enclose the workpiece. The metal is deformed by repeated hammer blows or press strokes, with the operator controlling the shape change between blows.

**Sketch (1 Mark):** Two flat dies — top and bottom — with the workpiece being placed between them. A hammer is shown striking the top die.

**2. Closed-Die Forging — Working Principle (1.5 Marks):**
Closed-die (impression-die) forging uses dies with pre-machined impressions of the final shape. The heated workpiece is placed in the lower die cavity, and the upper die descends under high pressure, forcing the metal to flow into the cavity. Excess material escapes as a thin **flash** around the die parting line, which is later trimmed.

**Sketch (1 Mark):** Upper die with impression + lower die with matching impression + flash gutter at the parting line.

**3. Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Open-Die Forging** | **Closed-Die Forging** |
|---------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Dimensional Accuracy | Low (operator-dependent) | High (±0.25 mm typical) |
| Equipment Cost | Lower (simple presses/hammers) | Higher (precision dies, hammers) |
| Skill Requirement | High (operator) | Lower (machine-driven) |
| Production Rate | Low | High (mass production) |
| Typical Applications | Large shafts, rings, turbine rotors | Crankshafts, connecting rods, gears |
| Die Cost | Low | High |

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Initial diameter $D_0 = 60 \text{ mm}$, so initial radius $r = 30 \text{ mm}$
- Initial height $h = 80 \text{ mm}$
- Flow stress $Y_f = 600 \text{ MPa}$
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.15$

**Step 1 — Use the Cook-Larke Friction Correction Formula (2 Marks):**

$$p = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{2 \mu r}{3 h}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values (2 Marks):**

$$p = 600 \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 0.15 \times 30}{3 \times 80}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Friction Term (1 Mark):**

$$\frac{2 \mu r}{3 h} = \frac{2 \times 0.15 \times 30}{3 \times 80} = \frac{9}{240} = 0.0375$$

**Step 4 — Final Calculation (1 Mark):**

$$p = 600 \times (1 + 0.0375) = 600 \times 1.0375 = 622.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Express in SI Units (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{p = 622.5 \text{ MPa} = 622.5 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$

**Answer:** The average forging pressure required is **622.5 MPa**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** With the help of a clear diagram, describe the **direct (forward) extrusion** process. Explain the role of the **extrusion ratio** and **die angle** on the extrusion pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A billet of **diameter 100 mm** and **length 250 mm** is extruded to a final diameter of **40 mm** in **direct extrusion**. The flow stress at the extrusion temperature is **Yf = 120 MPa**. Calculate the **ram pressure** required. Compare it with the **indirect extrusion** pressure and comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Working Principle of Direct Extrusion (2 Marks):**
In direct (forward) extrusion, a heated cylindrical billet is placed in a container. A **ram** pushes the billet forward through a **die** of the desired cross-section. The extruded product emerges from the die as a continuous length of constant cross-section. A dummy block is placed between the ram and the billet to prevent direct contact.

**Diagram (1.5 Marks):**
- Container (stationary)
- Ram (with dummy block)
- Billet (inside container)
- Die (at the end of the container)
- Extruded product (emerging from die)
- Label: "BILLET → CONTAINER → DIE → PRODUCT"

**2. Extrusion Ratio and Its Effect (2 Marks):**
The **extrusion ratio** $r_x = A_0/A_f$ is the ratio of the original billet cross-section to the final product cross-section. A higher extrusion ratio means more plastic deformation, higher true strain ($\varepsilon = \ln r_x$), and consequently **higher ram pressure**. Practical extrusion ratios are limited to about **20:1 to 40:1** for steels and up to **400:1** for soft aluminum.

**3. Die Angle Effect (1.5 Marks):**
- **Small die angle** → excessive **redundant work** as material shears through a long deformation zone → high pressure.
- **Large die angle** → non-uniform flow, **dead metal zones** form at the corners → defects and high pressure.
- **Optimum die angle (45°–60°)** → minimum pressure requirement.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Billet diameter $D_0 = 100 \text{ mm}$, length $L_0 = 250 \text{ mm}$
- Final diameter $D_f = 40 \text{ mm}$
- Flow stress $Y_f = 120 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 1 — Extrusion Ratio (1 Mark):**

$$r_x = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \frac{D_0^2}{D_f^2} = \frac{100^2}{40^2} = \frac{10000}{1600} = 6.25$$

**Step 2 — True Strain (1 Mark):**

$$\varepsilon = \ln(r_x) = \ln(6.25) \approx 1.833$$

**Step 3 — Friction Term (1 Mark):**

$$\frac{2 L_0}{D_0} = \frac{2 \times 250}{100} = 5.0$$

**Step 4 — Direct Extrusion Pressure (2 Marks):**

$$p_{direct} = Y_f \left(\varepsilon + \frac{2 L_0}{D_0}\right) = 120 \left(1.833 + 5.0\right) = 120 \times 6.833$$

$$p_{direct} = 819.96 \text{ MPa} \approx 820 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Indirect Extrusion Pressure (1 Mark):**

$$p_{indirect} = Y_f \cdot \varepsilon = 120 \times 1.833 = 219.96 \text{ MPa} \approx 220 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison and Comment (1 Mark):**
- The direct extrusion pressure (820 MPa) is **3.73 times** the indirect extrusion pressure (220 MPa).
- This huge difference is entirely due to the **container friction term** ($2 L_0 / D_0 = 5.0$).
- **Comment:** Indirect extrusion is preferred for hard materials and high extrusion ratios because it eliminates friction losses. However, direct extrusion is more versatile and can handle larger billets.

**Final Answer:** $p_{direct} \approx 820 \text{ MPa}$, $p_{indirect} \approx 220 \text{ MPa}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing Hot Working and Cold Working Formulas**: Students often apply $Y_f$ as a single value. Remember: $Y_f$ depends on **temperature, strain, and strain rate**. Always state the conditions.
> 2. **Mixing Up True Strain and Engineering Strain**: In rolling, the formula is $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ — NOT $(h_0 - h_f)/h_0$. Using the wrong one will cost full marks.
> 3. **Units Confusion**: $Y_f$ must be in **MPa (N/mm²)** when lengths are in **mm** and forces in **N**. Mixing units is the #1 cause of numerical errors.
> 4. **Forgetting the Friction Term in Extrusion**: The term $2L_0/D_0$ applies **only to direct extrusion**, not indirect. Examiners test this distinction specifically.
> 5. **Roll Torque Factor**: Some textbooks use $T = 0.5 FL$ and some use $T = 0.55 FL$ (incorporating non-uniform pressure). Use $0.5 FL$ unless the problem specifies otherwise.
> 6. **Neutral Point vs. Friction Hill**: The neutral point is the location, the friction hill is the **pressure distribution shape** that peaks at the neutral point. Don't interchange the terms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 3 Compression Revision Sheet**

### ✅ Core Concepts to Lock In:
- [x] Compression forming is a **chipless** bulk deformation process; no material is removed.
- [x] The basic forging equation: **F = Yf × A** (force = flow stress × projected area).
- [x] **Cook-Larke correction** accounts for friction in upsetting: $p = Y_f(1 + 2\mu r / 3h)$.
- [x] **Open-die forging** = smith/forging with flat dies; **Closed-die forging** = impression-die with flash.
- [x] **Forging operations**: upsetting, cogging, drawing out, punching, bending, swaging, fullering, edging.

### ✅ Rolling Essentials:
- [x] **Contact length** (approximate): $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$.
- [x] **True strain** in rolling: $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$.
- [x] **Friction hill** peaks at the **neutral point** (no-slip point).
- [x] **Average roll pressure**: $\bar{p} = Y_f(1 + \mu L / h_f)$.
- [x] **Separating force**: $F = \bar{p} \cdot w \cdot L$.
- [x] **Torque per roll**: $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$; **Power per roll**: $P = 2\pi N T$.

### ✅ Extrusion Essentials:
- [x] **Direct extrusion**: ram pushes billet forward; high friction loss.
- [x] **Indirect extrusion**: die on ram moves backward; friction eliminated.
- [x] **Extrusion ratio**: $r_x = A_0 / A_f$; **True strain**: $\varepsilon = \ln(r_x)$.
- [x] **Direct extrusion pressure**: $p = Y_f(\varepsilon + 2L_0/D_0)$.
- [x] **Indirect extrusion pressure**: $p = Y_f \cdot \varepsilon$.
- [x] **Optimum die angle**: 45°–60° minimizes pressure.

### ✅ Drawing Essentials:
- [x] **Drawing stress**: $\sigma_d = Y_f(1 + \mu/\tan\alpha) \ln(A_0/A_f)$.
- [x] **Max reduction per pass**: ~20–30% for most metals.
- [x] **Typical die half-angle**: 6°–8° for wire drawing.

### ✅ Unit Vigilance (Most Lost Marks Zone):
- [x] Lengths in **mm**, stress in **N/mm² = MPa**, force in **N**, torque in **N·m**, power in **W**.
- [x] Roll speed **N in rev/s** (not rpm) when using $P = 2\pi N T$. If $N$ is in rpm, use $P = (2\pi N T)/60$.

### ✅ Common Numerical Traps in KTU Papers:
- [x] Always check whether the problem specifies **hot or cold working** — use the corresponding $Y_f$.
- [x] If asked to compare, **show both processes** even if the question implies one.
- [x] When asked to "comment," always interpret the numerical result qualitatively (e.g., "indirect extrusion saves 70% pressure").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tension and Compression Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tension and Compression Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tension and Compression Operations** refer to a broad class of **bulk and sheet metal forming processes** in which the workpiece is plastically deformed under the action of **uniaxial or multiaxial tensile**, **compressive**, or **combined tensile–compressive** stress states, without any intended removal of material. They are governed by the **plastic flow of metals** beyond the yield surface, as defined by the **von Mises** or **Tresca yield criteria**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, these operations are classified under **Mechanical Working / Forming Processes** and constitute a critical 14-mark weightage topic in Module 3.

### Classification Snapshot

| Stress State | Examples of Operations |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Pure Tension** | Stretch Forming, Wire Drawing, Tensile Testing |
| **Pure Compression** | Forging (Open/Closed Die), Extrusion, Rolling, Compression Moulding |
| **Combined** | Deep Drawing, Bending, Spinning, Hydroforming |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine a piece of **chewing gum** (or a soft clay bar) in your hands.
> - If you **pull both ends apart** → that is a **TENSION Operation** (the gum gets thinner and longer).
> - If you **push both ends together** → that is a **COMPRESSION Operation** (the gum gets shorter and fatter, or bulges outward in the middle).
> - If you **push on the middle while supporting the ends** → that is **BENDING** (top fibers stretch in tension, bottom fibers squash in compression).
> - If you **squeeze the gum between two flat plates** → that is **FORGING** (free compression).

Manufacturing simply industrializes these everyday actions using **hydraulic presses, hammers, rolls, and dies** capable of delivering forces from **few kilo-Newtons (kN)** in precision forming to **Mega-Newtons (MN)** in heavy forging.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Material Metrics (KTU Standard)

- **Flow Stress (σ_f or K):** Instantaneous resistance of metal to plastic deformation, measured in **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
- **True Strain (ε):** ε = ln(L_f / L_0); dimensionless.
- **Strain Hardening Exponent (n):** Typically **0.0 to 0.5**; higher n = better stretch formability.
- **Strength Coefficient (K):** MPa; obtained from σ = K·εⁿ.
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** Range **0.05 (lubricated) to 0.5 (dry)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the units (MPa, N, mm) explicitly. Examiners award 0.5 marks for correct unit conventions in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Plastic Deformation

When a metal is loaded beyond its **elastic limit**, permanent (plastic) deformation begins. The behavior is governed by:

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow
1. **Elastic Region (Hooke's Law):** σ = E·ε. Energy is stored, fully recoverable.
2. **Yield Point (σ_y):** Onset of plastic flow. Dislocations begin to move.
3. **Strain Hardening Region:** σ = K·εⁿ (Hollomon's Equation). Material gets stronger as it deforms.
4. **Necking & Failure (Tension):** Localized reduction in cross-section → fracture.
5. **Upsetting/Bulging (Compression):** Material flows sideways because volume is conserved.

### Volume Constancy Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> During plastic deformation, metal is assumed to be **incompressible**. This yields the foundational identity:
> **A₀·L₀ = A_f·L_f** (constant volume)

This simple identity is the seed from which **every** tension and compression formula in Module 3 is derived.

## 2.2 Tension-Based Operations — Theory

### A) Wire Drawing & Bar Drawing
A **tensile pulling force** is applied to pull a workpiece through a converging die. The reduction in area is restricted to **< 30% per pass** to avoid tensile failure.

### B) Stretch Forming
A sheet is clamped at its edges and stretched over a contoured die. Produces **aerospace panels and automotive body parts** with excellent surface finish.

### C) Deep Drawing (Combined Operation)
A blank is forced by a **punch** into a die cavity to produce a cup. Outer flange is in **compression** (causing wrinkling), while the wall over the punch radius is in **tension** (causing tearing).

## 2.3 Compression-Based Operations — Theory

### A) Forging
- **Open-Die Forging:** Workpiece is compressed between flat or simple dies; material flows freely laterally.
- **Closed-Die Forging:** Material is constrained within a die cavity (impression die), producing complex shapes like connecting rods.
- Forces range from **few MN (hammer forging)** to **hundreds of MN (hydraulic press forging)**.

### B) Extrusion
- **Direct (Forward) Extrusion:** Ram pushes billet through die; billet and ram move in same direction.
- **Indirect (Backward) Extrusion:** Ram is hollow; billet stays stationary; ram moves backward relative to die.
- **Hydrostatic Extrusion:** Billet is separated from ram by high-pressure fluid → reduces friction by **~30-40%**.

### C) Rolling
- **Flat Rolling:** Two opposing rolls reduce thickness.
- **Ring Rolling:** Used for seamless rings (bearings, flanges).
- **Shape Rolling:** Produces I-beams, rails, channels.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield, exam-ready formula sheet** for Tension and Compression Operations. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every Part B (14-mark) question.

| # | Operation | Key Formula | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Volume constancy | $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$ | A in mm², L in mm |
| 2 | True strain | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Reduction in area (R) | $R = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0}$ | dimensionless (%) |
| 4 | Average Flow Stress | $\sigma_{f,avg} = \dfrac{K \cdot \varepsilon^n}{n+1}$ | K in MPa, ε dimensionless |
| 5 | **Forging Force (Compression)** | $F = K_f \cdot A = \sigma_f \cdot A$ | F in N, A is instantaneous area |
| 6 | **Ideal Work in Forging** | $W = \sigma_f \cdot V \cdot \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | V = volume (mm³) |
| 7 | **Extrusion Pressure (Ideal)** | $p = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | p in MPa |
| 8 | Extrusion Force | $F = p \cdot A_0$ | F in N |
| 9 | **Drawing Stress (Tension)** | $\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | σ_d in MPa |
| 10 | **Max. Reduction per pass (Drawing)** | $\Delta A_{max} = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{n}{2}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Drawing Force | $F_d = \sigma_d \cdot A_f$ | F_d in N |
| 12 | **Bending Strain (Outer Fiber)** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2R}{t}}$ | R = bend radius, t = thickness |
| 13 | Roll Pressure (Fritz/Hitchcock) | $p = \sigma_f \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{2h_f}\right)$ | L = contact length |
| 14 | **Deep Drawing Ratio (DR)** | $DR = \dfrac{D_0}{D_p}$ | D_0 = blank dia., D_p = punch dia. |
| 15 | Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR) | $LDR \approx 2.0$ to $2.5$ (typical for steel) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Note that in the table above, the absolute value `|...|` and `L_0 / L_f` are intentionally typeset using `\dfrac` and `\ln\left(...\right)` to prevent any conflict with the markdown table pipe symbol `|`. Students copying formulas into KTU answer sheets must preserve this clarity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Forged turbine discs (Inconel) and stretch-formed aluminum wing skins.
- **Automotive:** Deep-drawn car body panels, forged crankshafts, extruded aluminum crash rails.
- **Energy:** Seamless rolled rings for wind turbine bearings, extruded heat sinks for solar inverters.
- **Construction:** Hot-rolled I-sections, cold-drawn prestressing wires for concrete.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Flow Stress (Foundation for All Force Calculations)

**Starting Point:** During plastic deformation, the instantaneous flow stress follows Hollomon's power-law hardening relation.

$$\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n$$

**Step 1:** Recognize that the work done per unit volume equals the area under the true stress–true strain curve.

$$W/v = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma_f \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the Hollomon equation into the integral.

$$W/v = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} K \cdot \varepsilon^n \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 3:** Apply the standard power rule of integration: $\int x^n dx = \dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}$.

$$W/v = K \left[ \dfrac{\varepsilon^{n+1}}{n+1} \right]_0^{\varepsilon_f}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the upper limit, noting the lower limit vanishes.

$$W/v = \dfrac{K \cdot \varepsilon_f^{\,n+1}}{n+1}$$

**Step 5:** Define the **Average Flow Stress** as the constant stress that would do the same work over the same strain range.

$$\sigma_{f,avg} = \dfrac{W/v}{\varepsilon_f} = \dfrac{K \cdot \varepsilon_f^{\,n+1}}{(n+1) \cdot \varepsilon_f}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the exponent.

$$\boxed{\sigma_{f,avg} = \dfrac{K \cdot \varepsilon_f^{\,n}}{n+1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** Average flow stress lets the engineer replace the *variable* resistance of a strain-hardening material with a *single equivalent constant*. This is why every forging, extrusion, and drawing problem in KTU uses this trick.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ideal Extrusion Pressure (Compression)

**Starting Point:** A cylindrical billet of initial area A₀ is extruded to final area A_f through a die. The material flows through the converging die under compressive ram pressure p.

**Step 1:** Apply volume constancy across an infinitesimal element. The material element entering with cross-section A and length dL leaves with cross-section (A + dA) and length (dL + d(−dL)).

**Step 2:** The incremental work done by the ram on an element equals pressure × volume change.

$$dW = p \cdot A \cdot dL$$

**Step 3:** The same work equals the force times the change in length of the element. Using flow stress σ_f and equilibrium on the element:

$$p \cdot A \cdot dL = \sigma_f \cdot A \cdot (-dL) + \sigma_f \cdot (A + dA) \cdot (dL - d(dL))$$

**Step 4:** A more elegant approach: The work done by the extrusion pressure equals the change in strain energy integrated through the deformation zone. For a block of volume V being reduced from height h₀ to h_f:

$$W = \int_{V} \sigma_f \, d\varepsilon \, dV$$

**Step 5:** Since dε = −dL/L (shortening strain), and V = A·L is constant, dV = 0:

$$W = \sigma_f \cdot V \cdot \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$$

**Step 6:** By volume constancy, h_0/h_f = A_f/A_0. So:

$$W = \sigma_f \cdot V \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**Step 7:** This work is supplied by the ram force F acting over a ram displacement = (L_0 − L_f) = L_0(1 − A_0/A_f).

$$F \cdot L_0 \left(1 - \frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 L_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**Step 8:** Cancel L_0 from both sides and solve for the pressure p = F/A_0.

$$p = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}{1 - \frac{A_0}{A_f}}$$

**Step 9:** For typical extrusion ratios (A_0/A_f > 4), the term (1 − A_0/A_f) ≈ 1 to a good engineering approximation. Thus the **ideal extrusion pressure** becomes:

$$\boxed{p = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Real extrusion includes friction. The **actual pressure** is p_actual = p_ideal + friction term. Always state whether your answer is "ideal" or "actual" in the KTU answer script to earn the distinction marks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Drawing Stress (Tension Operation)

**Starting Point:** A wire of initial area A_0 is drawn through a die to final area A_f. The drawing force F_d acts on the exit side, pulling the wire in tension.

**Step 1:** At the die exit, the wire must sustain a tensile stress σ_d to overcome the deformation resistance. By force equilibrium on the exit side:

$$F_d = \sigma_d \cdot A_f$$

**Step 2:** The work done by the drawing force equals the strain energy stored in the deformed material. Using volume constancy and the work formula derived earlier:

$$F_d \cdot \Delta L = \sigma_f \cdot V \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**Step 3:** The elongation ΔL of the wire between die entry and exit is:

$$\Delta L = L_0 - L_f = L_0 \left(1 - \frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute ΔL into the work equation:

$$F_d \cdot L_0 \left(1 - \frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 L_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for F_d:

$$F_d = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}{1 - \frac{A_0}{A_f}}$$

**Step 6:** Divide by the exit area A_f to get the **drawing stress**:

$$\sigma_d = \dfrac{F_d}{A_f} = \sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} \cdot \dfrac{\ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}{1 - \frac{A_0}{A_f}}$$

**Step 7:** For engineering approximations and consistent with standard textbook treatment, the simplified **drawing stress** is:

$$\boxed{\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}$$

**Step 8:** Constraint — the drawing stress must not exceed the failing strength of the drawn wire. This gives the **maximum permissible reduction per pass**:

$$\Delta A_{max} = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{n}{2}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison Insight:** Notice that the *ideal* extrusion pressure and *ideal* drawing stress share the same expression σ_f · ln(A_0/A_f). The **direction of force application** (compression vs. tension) is what differentiates them mechanically — a beautiful symmetry exploited by KTU examiners.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Reduction in Wire Drawing (Tension Limit)

**Starting Point:** Drawing will fail (wire snaps) if σ_d > σ_f. The maximum reduction occurs at σ_d = σ_f.

**Step 1:** Set the failure condition.

$$\sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \sigma_f$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the area ratio.

$$\ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{A_0}{A_f} = e \approx 2.718$$

**Step 3:** This is the **maximum possible reduction** if the material did NOT strain-harden (n = 0). But materials DO strain-harden. For a strain-hardening material (Hollomon's law, n > 0), the working allowable stress at the die exit is:

$$\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_f \cdot (1 + n)$$

**Step 4:** Set the drawing stress equal to this allowable limit.

$$\sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \sigma_f \cdot (1 + n)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the maximum area ratio.

$$\ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = 1 + n \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{A_0}{A_f} = e^{1+n}$$

**Step 6:** Express the maximum reduction.

$$\Delta A_{max} = 1 - \frac{A_f}{A_0} = 1 - e^{-(1+n)}$$

**Step 7:** For typical strain-hardening exponents n = 0 to 0.5, the **engineering approximation** widely used is:

$$\boxed{\Delta A_{max} \approx 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{n}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 8:** Numerical sanity check: For n = 0.2 (typical cold-drawn steel), ΔA_max ≈ 1 − e^(−0.1) ≈ **9.5% per pass**, justifying the empirical rule of "≤ 30% per pass."

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Model):** A cylindrical billet of 50 mm diameter and 80 mm height is compressed in a forging operation to a final height of 40 mm. The material has a flow curve given by σ_f = 600 · ε^0.2 (MPa). Assuming no friction and no barreling, calculate:
> (a) The true strain in the operation
> (b) The average flow stress
> (c) The forging force at the **final** instant of the stroke
> (d) The total ideal work done

### Solution

**Part (a) — True Strain:**
True strain in compression is computed as the natural log of the height ratio (initial / final) because the height decreases:

$$\varepsilon_f = \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{80}{40}\right) = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$$

**Part (b) — Average Flow Stress:**
Apply the derived average flow stress formula with n = 0.2, K = 600 MPa, ε_f = 0.693:

$$\sigma_{f,avg} = \dfrac{K \cdot \varepsilon_f^{\,n}}{n+1} = \dfrac{600 \cdot (0.693)^{0.2}}{0.2 + 1}$$

Calculate the intermediate power: (0.693)^0.2. Taking the natural log: 0.2 · ln(0.693) = 0.2 · (−0.3665) = −0.0733. Exponential: e^(−0.0733) ≈ 0.9293.

$$\sigma_{f,avg} = \dfrac{600 \cdot 0.9293}{1.2} = \dfrac{557.6}{1.2} \approx 464.7 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Part (c) — Final Forging Force:**
The final cross-sectional area (by volume constancy) is:

$$A_f = A_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h_f} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 50^2 \cdot \frac{80}{40} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 2500 \cdot 2 = 3927 \, \text{mm}^2$$

Instantaneous force = average flow stress × final area (engineering approximation used by KTU):

$$F = \sigma_{f,avg} \cdot A_f = 464.7 \cdot 3927 \approx 1,824,700 \, \text{N} \approx 1.82 \, \text{MN}$$

**Part (d) — Ideal Work Done:**
The volume of the billet: V = A_0 · h_0 = 1963.5 · 80 = 157,080 mm³.

$$W = \sigma_{f,avg} \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon_f = 464.7 \cdot 157{,}080 \cdot 0.693 \approx 5.06 \times 10^{10} \, \text{N·mm} = 50.6 \, \text{kJ}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer Box:**
> - ε_f = 0.693
> - σ_f,avg ≈ 464.7 MPa
> - F ≈ 1.82 MN
> - W ≈ 50.6 kJ

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Process Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forming Processes]:::root --> B[Tension Operations]:::ten
    A --> C[Compression Operations]:::com
    A --> D[Combined Operations]:::mix

    B --> B1[Wire Drawing]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between direct and indirect extrusion with a neat sketch.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | Direct Extrusion | Indirect Extrusion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ram motion** | Moves in same direction as billet flow | Ram is hollow; moves opposite to flow |
| **Friction** | Billet slides along container wall (high friction) | No relative motion at container wall (low friction) |
| **Ram force** | ~30% higher than indirect | Lower force required |
| **Product length** | Limited by billet length | Can be longer (continuous) |
| **Billet length** | Restricted | Can be larger |

*Sketch (verbal description):* In direct extrusion, the ram pushes the billet forward through a stationary die at the container's end. In indirect extrusion, the die is mounted on a hollow ram that moves backward into the stationary billet.

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
> **What is the Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR) in deep drawing? State its typical value for mild steel.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

- **Definition:** LDR is the **maximum ratio of blank diameter (D_0) to punch diameter (D_p)** that can be successfully drawn in a single operation without fracture of the cup wall.
- **Formula:** LDR = (D_0 / D_p)_max
- **Typical Value:** For **mild steel, LDR ≈ 2.0 to 2.5**.
- **Significance:** Determines whether a cup can be drawn in **one pass** or requires **multiple redraws** with intermediate annealing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> (a) Derive the expression for the **ideal extrusion pressure** starting from the principle of constancy of volume. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**
> (b) A billet of diameter 75 mm and length 150 mm is extruded into a round bar of diameter 25 mm. The material has a flow curve σ_f = 350·ε^0.15 MPa. Calculate **(i)** the extrusion ratio, **(ii)** the true strain, **(iii)** the average flow stress, and **(iv)** the ideal ram force. Neglect friction. **(7 Marks)**

### Full Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. Material is **incompressible** (volume constant).
2. **Homogeneous** deformation throughout the deformation zone.
3. **No friction** between billet and container walls (ideal case).
4. **Uniform flow stress** across the cross-section.

**Derivation Steps (each ~1 mark):**

**Step 1:** State the constancy of volume: A_0 · L_0 = A · L = A_f · L_f.

**Step 2:** For a differential element at the deformation zone, the incremental true strain is:

$$d\varepsilon = -\dfrac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 3:** The total strain when billet shortens from L_0 to L_f is:

$$\varepsilon = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} -\dfrac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L_0}{L_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Work done by the ram equals the plastic deformation work:

$$W = p \cdot A_0 \cdot \Delta L_{ram} = \int_V \sigma_f \, d\varepsilon \, dV$$

**Step 5:** Since V = A_0 · L_0 is constant, and σ_f is treated as the average flow stress:

$$p \cdot A_0 \cdot L_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 L_0 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Simplify by canceling A_0 · L_0 and solving:

$$p = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}{1 - \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}} \approx \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

The last approximation is valid for large extrusion ratios (A_0/A_f ≫ 1). **(1 mark)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- D_0 = 75 mm, L_0 = 150 mm, D_f = 25 mm
- Flow curve: σ_f = 350 · ε^0.15 MPa

**(i) Extrusion Ratio:**
$$r = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \left(\dfrac{D_0}{D_f}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{75}{25}\right)^2 = 3^2 = \mathbf{9}$$

**(ii) True Strain:**
$$\varepsilon_f = \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln(9) \approx \mathbf{2.197}$$

**(iii) Average Flow Stress:**
With K = 350 MPa, n = 0.15, ε_f = 2.197:

$$\sigma_{f,avg} = \dfrac{350 \cdot (2.197)^{0.15}}{1.15}$$

Compute (2.197)^0.15: ln(2.197) = 0.7875; 0.15 × 0.7875 = 0.1181; e^0.1181 ≈ 1.1253.

$$\sigma_{f,avg} = \dfrac{350 \cdot 1.1253}{1.15} = \dfrac{393.86}{1.15} \approx \mathbf{342.5 \, MPa}$$

**(iv) Ideal Ram Force:**
$$F = p \cdot A_0 = \sigma_{f,avg} \cdot \ln(r) \cdot A_0$$

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 75^2 = 4417.86 \, \text{mm}^2$$

$$F = 342.5 \cdot \ln(9) \cdot 4417.86 = 342.5 \cdot 2.197 \cdot 4417.86$$

$$F \approx 3{,}323{,}000 \, \text{N} \approx \mathbf{3.32 \, MN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mark Loss Point 1 (1 mark):** Forgetting the *assumption block* in part (a) — KTU examiners **explicitly allot marks** for stating assumptions. Always list at least 3.
> - **Mark Loss Point 2 (1 mark):** Confusing the **height ratio** with **area ratio** in compression strain. In forging, ε = ln(h_0/h_f) = ln(A_f/A_0). In extrusion/drawing, ε = ln(A_0/A_f). **Direction matters!**
> - **Mark Loss Point 3 (0.5 mark):** Using the wrong extrusion ratio formula (using D_0/D_f instead of its square). Area ratio is **squared** of diameter ratio.
> - **Mark Loss Point 4 (0.5 mark):** Writing "F = 3.32 MN" without units. KTU is strict about units.

> **Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Choice):**
> (a) With the help of a clear schematic, explain the **wire drawing process**. Derive the expression for **drawing stress** and state the condition for maximum reduction per pass. **(7 Marks)**
> (b) A steel wire of initial diameter 5.0 mm is drawn through a die in two passes. After the first pass, the diameter is 3.5 mm. After the second pass, it is 2.5 mm. The material has K = 500 MPa and n = 0.25. Determine the **drawing force** required for the **second pass** and the **maximum allowable reduction** per pass. **(7 Marks)**

### Full Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks)

**Schematic Description (2 marks):** Draw a conical die with a wire entering from the larger diameter side (A_0) and exiting from the smaller diameter side (A_f). Show the drawing force F_d acting on the exit side. Indicate the die semi-angle α, coefficient of friction μ, and the deformation zone.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Use the derivation from Section 3.3 of these notes:

$$\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

with the drawing force F_d = σ_d · A_f.

**Condition for Maximum Reduction (1 mark):**

$$\Delta A_{max} = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{n}{2}\right)$$

Above this, the wire will fracture at the die exit (σ_d exceeds the strain-hardened strength).

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- D_0 (pass 1) = 5.0 mm → D_f (pass 1) = 3.5 mm
- D_0 (pass 2) = 3.5 mm → D_f (pass 2) = 2.5 mm
- K = 500 MPa, n = 0.25

**Step 1 — Area Calculations:**
- A_0 (pass 2) = (π/4)·(3.5)² = 9.621 mm²
- A_f (pass 2) = (π/4)·(2.5)² = 4.909 mm²

**Step 2 — True Strain in Pass 2:**
$$\varepsilon_2 = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{9.621}{4.909}\right) = \ln(1.96) \approx 0.673$$

**Step 3 — Drawing Stress (Pass 2):**
$$\sigma_{f,2} = K \cdot \varepsilon_2^{\,n} = 500 \cdot (0.673)^{0.25}$$

Compute (0.673)^0.25: ln(0.673) = −0.396; 0.25 × (−0.396) = −0.099; e^(−0.099) ≈ 0.9057.

$$\sigma_{f,2} = 500 \cdot 0.9057 \approx 452.85 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{d,2} = \sigma_{f,2} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = 452.85 \cdot 0.673 \approx 304.77 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Drawing Force:**
$$F_{d,2} = \sigma_{d,2} \cdot A_f = 304.77 \cdot 4.909 \approx \mathbf{1496 \, N} \approx 1.5 \, \text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Allowable Reduction:**
$$\Delta A_{max} = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{0.25}{2}\right) = 1 - \exp(-0.125) = 1 - 0.8825 \approx 0.1175 \approx \mathbf{11.75\%}$$

**Verification:** Actual reduction in pass 2 = (9.621 − 4.909)/9.621 = 0.489 ≈ **48.9%** — which **EXCEEDS** the limit! This means the wire will likely break. **(Interpretation mark: 1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors in Wire Drawing Problems:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Forgetting to use the **strain-hardening n** in flow stress calculation (you must compute σ_f = K·εⁿ first, then use the average).
> - **Pitfall 2:** Using the **initial** diameter instead of the **pass-2 entry** diameter when computing strain.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Failing to **interpret** the numerical result (students compute F_d but never state whether the operation is safe).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tension and Compression Operations**

### Tension Operations
- ☐ **Wire Drawing** uses *tensile pull*; max reduction ΔA = 1 − exp(−n/2).
- ☐ **Stretch Forming** gives uniform thinning; ideal for large panels.
- ☐ **Drawing stress** σ_d = σ_f · ln(A_0/A_f) and force F_d = σ_d · A_f.
- ☐ Friction at die–workpiece interface *adds* to the required drawing force.

### Compression Operations
- ☐ **Forging Force** F = σ_{f,avg} · A (use *current* area, not original).
- ☐ **Extrusion Pressure** p = σ_f · ln(A_0/A_f) (ideal); friction adds ~30–40%.
- ☐ **Indirect extrusion** needs *less force* than direct.
- ☐ **Rolling Pressure** p = σ_f · (1 + μL/2h_f) — includes friction hill effect.

### Combined Operations
- ☐ **Deep Drawing** has compression in flange (wrinkling) + tension in wall (tearing).
- ☐ **LDR** for steel ≈ 2.0–2.5; LDR for aluminum ≈ 1.8–2.0.
- ☐ **Bending** strain on outer fiber: ε = t / (2R + t).
- ☐ **Hold-down (blank-holder) force** prevents wrinkles but excessive force causes tearing.

### Universal Foundation Principles
- ☐ **Volume Constancy:** A_0L_0 = A_fL_f — the most-cited equation in KTU papers.
- ☐ **True Strain:** ε = ln(L_f/L_0) = ln(A_0/A_f) (NOT engineering strain for plastic problems).
- ☐ **Average Flow Stress:** σ_{f,avg} = K·εⁿ/(n+1) — use this for *all* force calculations in strain-hardening materials.
- ☐ **Hollomon's Law:** σ_f = K·εⁿ — memorize K and n units (MPa and dimensionless).
- ☐ **Friction Direction:** Friction *opposes motion*; increases force in both tension and compression processes.

### High-Yield Mnemonic for KTU Viva
> **"TV-CV" → Tension = Volume, Compression = Volume** — volume is conserved in *both*. Then remember: **"T for Thin, C for Compact"** — tension thins, compression compacts.

> **Final Board Strategy:** In the 14-mark question, always structure the answer as **(i) Statement of governing principle, (ii) Step-by-step derivation, (iii) Numerical substitution, (iv) Final boxed answer with units.** This is the KTU marking scheme template and guarantees full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Module 3 Context — Welding & Joining Processes

Module 3 of KTU's PBMET304 (Manufacturing Processes) under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme covers **Welding and Allied Joining Processes**. The topic "Applications" consolidates the *industrial, structural, and engineering use-cases* of every welding family studied in this module — from traditional arc welding to advanced non-conventional joining methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Welding is a materials joining process in which two or more parts are coalesced at their contacting surfaces by suitable application of heat and/or pressure, with or without the use of filler metal, to produce a homogeneous joint with properties comparable to the base metal.* The **"Applications"** of welding refer to the systematic mapping of each process variant to specific industrial sectors, base-metal combinations, joint geometries, and production-volume scenarios.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Gluing Metal with Controlled Heat"**
> Imagine two pieces of chocolate. You want to fuse them together so the seam is invisible and as strong as the original bar. You can:
> - **Melt both edges** and let them mix (→ *Fusion Welding*).
> - **Press them together with friction** until they soften and bond (→ *Solid-State Welding*).
> - **Use a different, lower-melting "glue" chocolate** in between (→ *Brazing/Soldering*).
> 
> Each "method" of chocolate fusion has its *own best use-case* (e.g., you wouldn't use a microwave for a thin decoration). Welding applications follow the same logic — **the process is chosen based on material, thickness, strength requirement, and production environment.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Welding Applications

The following engineering quantities govern process selection in industrial applications:

- **Heat Input (H)** — measured in **kJ/mm**, the most critical selection parameter.
- **Welding Current (I)** — measured in **Amperes (A)**.
- **Arc Voltage (V)** — measured in **Volts (V)**.
- **Welding Speed (v)** — measured in **mm/min** or **m/min**.
- **Penetration Depth** — measured in **mm**.
- **Deposition Rate** — measured in **kg/h**.
- **Distortion** — measured in **mm** of angular/linear deviation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** When asked to "select a welding process for X application," always justify with **(a) base metal, (b) thickness, (c) production volume, and (d) joint quality required** — these are the four pillars of process selection in the KTU marking scheme.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Major Welding Families — Application Matrix Logic

The applications of welding processes are organized into **six broad families** in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Each family has distinct application niches based on the **heat-input mechanism, filler metal usage, and pressure application.**

### 2.1.1 Fusion Welding Processes (Heat + Filler ± Pressure)

1. **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** — "Stick electrode" welding.
2. **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG/MAG)** — Continuous wire + shielding gas.
3. **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)** — Non-consumable tungsten electrode.
4. **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** — Arc buried under granular flux.
5. **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** — Constricted plasma jet.
6. **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)** — Gas flame fusion.

### 2.1.2 Resistance Welding Processes (Heat from Resistance + Pressure)

1. **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** — Lap joints on sheet metal.
2. **Resistance Seam Welding (RSEW)** — Continuous leak-tight joints.
3. **Resistance Projection Welding (RPW)** — Pre-formed dimples.
4. **Flash Butt Welding (FBW)** — End-to-end bar joining.

### 2.1.3 Solid-State Welding (Pressure + Heat below melting point)

1. **Friction Welding (FW)** — Rotary or linear friction.
2. **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** — Rotating tool plasticizes material.
3. **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** — High-frequency vibration.
4. **Diffusion Bonding (DB)** — Atomic diffusion under heat + pressure.
5. **Explosion Welding (EXW)** — Controlled detonation bonds.

### 2.1.4 High-Energy Beam Welding

1. **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** — Coherent photon beam.
2. **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** — Focused electron stream in vacuum.

### 2.1.5 Thermochemical Welding

1. **Thermite Welding** — Exothermic oxide-reduction reaction (rail welding).

### 2.1.6 Brazing, Braze Welding & Soldering (Filler below parent metal MP)

- **Brazing** — Filler MP > **450 °C**.
- **Soldering** — Filler MP < **450 °C**.
- **Braze Welding** — Filler metal is melted and deposited in a V-groove (like fusion welding), but base metal is NOT melted.

## 2.2 Why Each Process Fits Specific Applications — The Selection Logic

### 2.2.1 Heat Input Equation (Universal Welding Formula)

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I}{v \times 1000} \;\; \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

Where:
- **H** = Heat input in **kJ/mm**
- **η (eta)** = Process thermal efficiency (SMAW ≈ **0.80**, GMAW ≈ **0.85**, GTAW ≈ **0.60**, SAW ≈ **1.00**)
- **V** = Arc voltage in **V**
- **I** = Welding current in **A**
- **v** = Travel speed in **mm/s**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Heat input directly controls the **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) width**, **residual stress**, and **distortion.** Low-heat-input processes (LBW, EBW, PAW) are selected for **heat-sensitive alloys** (titanium, thin stainless steel, aerospace alloys).

### 2.2.2 Penetration vs. Current Relationship (SMAW/GMAW)

For a constant arc voltage and travel speed, penetration depth is approximately proportional to current:

$$d_p \;\propto\; \frac{I}{v}$$

Where **d_p** is penetration depth. This is why **high-deposition-rate** applications (shipbuilding, structural fabrication) use **high-current GMAW/SAW** with thicker electrodes.

### 2.2.3 Resistance Welding Heat Equation

$$Q = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t \;\;\text{(Joules)}$$

Where:
- **Q** = Heat generated in **J**
- **I** = Current in **A**
- **R** = Electrical resistance of the interface in **Ω**
- **t** = Time of current flow in **seconds**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **Joule's First Law**, and it explains *why resistance welding uses very high currents (5,000–20,000 A) for very short durations (a few cycles of 0.02 s each).*

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Meaning | Typical Value or Unit | Application Link |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \eta V I / (v \cdot 1000)$ | Heat input | kJ/mm | Controls HAZ, distortion |
| 2 | $Q = I^2 R t$ | Joule heating | Joules | Resistance welding selection |
| 3 | Penetration $\propto I / v$ | Depth vs. current/speed | mm | Bead profile design |
| 4 | Thermal efficiency $\eta$ | Fraction of arc heat in workpiece | SMAW ≈ 0.80, SAW ≈ 1.00 | Process selection |
| 5 | Brazing filler MP > 450 °C | Phase boundary | °C | Process classification |
| 6 | Soldering filler MP < 450 °C | Phase boundary | °C | Process classification |
| 7 | EBW requires vacuum < 10⁻² Pa | Pressure threshold | Pascal | Chamber design |
| 8 | LBW power density ≈ 10⁶–10⁷ W/mm² | Power density | W/mm² | Deep penetration welding |
| 9 | FSW tool rotation speed | Typical 300–3000 rpm | rpm | Aluminum alloy joining |
| 10 | Spot weld nugget diameter | $d \approx 4\sqrt{t}$ for steel | mm | Sheet thickness t selection |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL Markdown Note for Tables:** All absolute-value expressions and conditional notations above use no vertical pipes (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ rather than $\vert x \vert$) to preserve KTU rendering integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Welding Applications

- **Aerospace**: LBW, EBW, FSW for titanium, Inconel, and aluminum-lithium fuselage panels — *minimum distortion, deep penetration, vacuum cleanliness.*
- **Automotive (mass production)**: RSW for car body-in-white (≈ **4,000–5,000 spot welds per vehicle**).
- **Shipbuilding**: SAW and GMAW for thick plate (up to **100 mm**) butt joints in double-hull vessels.
- **Pipeline (oil & gas)**: Flash Butt Welding (FBW) for longitudinal pipe seams; GMAW for girth welds.
- **Railway**: Thermite (alumino-thermic) welding for continuous welded rail (CWR) joints in-track.
- **Electronics**: USW for copper-to-copper wire bonding; soldering for PCB assembly.
- **Nuclear**: GTAW with autogenous (no filler) root pass for stainless steel pipes — *clean, slag-free, full penetration.*
- **Brazing**: Carbide tool tips brazed to steel shanks; refrigeration copper tubing joints.
- **Soldering**: RoHS-compliant lead-free solders (Sn-Ag-Cu) for consumer electronics at **~217 °C eutectic**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Heat Input Governs Application Selection

The **heat input equation** can be derived from first principles of energy conservation.

### Step 1: Define arc power

The arc is an electrical discharge between the electrode and the workpiece. The power dissipated in the arc is:

$$P_{arc} = V \times I \;\;\text{(Watts)}$$

Here, **V** is the arc voltage (potential difference across the arc gap) and **I** is the current flowing through the arc.

### Step 2: Account for thermal efficiency

Not all arc power reaches the workpiece. Some is lost to the electrode, radiation, spatter, and convection. A process-specific efficiency factor $\eta$ accounts for this:

$$P_{effective} = \eta \times P_{arc} = \eta \times V \times I \;\;\text{(Watts)}$$

### Step 3: Convert power to energy per unit length

Energy delivered to the workpiece per unit length of weld (per millimetre of travel) is power divided by travel speed. Converting units (W·s/mm = J/mm) and scaling to kJ/mm:

$$H = \frac{P_{effective}}{v} = \frac{\eta \times V \times I}{v} \;\;\text{(J/mm)}$$

Dividing by 1000 to express in kJ/mm:

$$H = \frac{\eta \times V \times I}{v \times 1000} \;\;\text{(kJ/mm)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** For a given current and voltage, *slower travel speed* means *more heat per millimetre* — causing wider HAZ, more grain growth, and higher distortion. This is why thin sheets or heat-sensitive alloys require **high-speed, low-heat-input** processes (LBW, PAW, EBW).

### Step 4: Worked numerical example

A fabricator uses **GMAW** to weld a **6 mm mild steel** plate.
- Arc voltage **V = 24 V**
- Current **I = 200 A**
- Travel speed **v = 5 mm/s**
- Process efficiency $\eta = 0.85$

Substituting:

$$H = \frac{0.85 \times 24 \times 200}{5 \times 1000} = \frac{4080}{5000} = 0.816 \;\text{kJ/mm}$$

Converting to kJ/m (multiply by 1000):

$$H = 816 \;\text{kJ/m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Acceptable range for 6 mm mild steel:** Typically **0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm**. So this weld is in the *low-heat-input* regime — acceptable for the application, with low distortion risk.

## 3.2 Derivation: Resistance Spot Welding (RSW) Heat Generation

### Step 1: Total contact resistance

When two sheets are pressed together, the **total resistance R** is the sum of:
- Two sheet bulk resistances: $2R_s$
- Two electrode-sheet contact resistances: $2R_c$
- One sheet-sheet (faying) interface resistance: $R_f$

$$R_{total} = 2R_s + 2R_c + R_f$$

### Step 2: Apply Joule's law

For a current **I** applied for time **t** (in seconds):

$$Q = I^2 \times R_{total} \times t \;\;\text{(Joules)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the sheets weld:** The faying interface $R_f$ is the highest resistance point in the circuit (oxide films, surface roughness), so it generates the most heat — and that's where the **nugget** forms.

### Step 3: Typical industrial values

For a **1.2 mm low-carbon steel** sheet:
- Current **I ≈ 8,000 A**
- Time **t ≈ 0.2 s** (≈ 10 cycles at 50 Hz)
- Resistance **R ≈ 100 μΩ = 100 × 10⁻⁶ Ω**

Substituting:

$$Q = (8000)^2 \times (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.2 = 64{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0001 \times 0.2 = 1280 \;\text{Joules}$$

This **1.28 kJ** of localized heat is enough to melt a nugget of **≈ 4–5 mm diameter** in ~0.2 seconds.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Application-to-Process Mapping

The following exhaustive matrix is what KTU examiners expect students to reproduce for full marks in "Compare-and-select-process" questions.

| Application Sector | Recommended Primary Process | Secondary Process | Justification (Why?) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Car body sheet steel** (0.7–2.0 mm) | **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** | RSW + adhesive bonding | High speed (≈ 2–3 s/weld), automatable, no filler |
| **Pressure vessel shell** (10–50 mm CS) | **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** | GMAW (for short seams) | High deposition rate, deep penetration, slag protects arc |
| **Stainless steel food-grade pipe** (sanitary) | **GTAW (TIG)** | PAW | Clean, slag-free, full-penetration autogenous root |
| **Aerospace titanium fuselage** | **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** | LBW, GTAW | Vacuum = no contamination, deep penetration, narrow HAZ |
| **Aluminum ship deck plates** (5–10 mm) | **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | GMAW (pulsed) | No melting → no porosity, no solidification cracks |
| **Aircraft aluminum-lithium panels** | **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** | FSW | Low distortion, autogenous, high speed |
| **Rail track in-field joint** | **Thermite (Aluminothermic) Welding** | Flash Butt (in plant) | Portable, no power supply needed, exothermic self-sustaining |
| **Carbide tip to steel shank** | **Brazing (induction / torch)** | — | Base metal NOT melted → no HAZ softening |
| **PCB component leads** | **Soldering (wave/reflow)** | — | Low MP, no thermal damage to components |
| **Oil pipeline girth weld** (API 5L) | **GMAW (pulsed) + FCAW** | SAW (in-plant section) | Field-portable, high-quality, radiographic-acceptable |
| **Thin foil (< 0.5 mm)** | **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** | Resistance microwelding | No arc → no burn-through, no spatter |
| **Reactor vessel internals** (austenitic SS, no sensitization) | **GTAW with low heat input** | PAW | Prevents chromium carbide precipitation at grain boundaries |
| **Hardsurfacing (excavator bucket lip)** | **SMAW with hardfacing electrode** | FCAW | High deposition of wear-resistant alloy (e.g., high-Cr iron) |
| **Heat exchanger fin-to-tube** | **High-frequency resistance welding** | — | Continuous seam, thin materials, mass production |
| **Aerospace turbine blade repair** | **Laser cladding / LBW** | EBW | Precision deposition, low dilution |
| **Bicycle frame (chromoly steel)** | **GTAW (TIG)** | GMAW | Aesthetics + control of thin-wall tubing (Ø 25–35 mm) |
| **Bridge girder (thick plate)** | **SAW (tandem-arc)** | Electroslag (ESW) | Single-pass deep penetration up to 400 mm |
| **Nuclear fuel cladding** (Zircaloy) | **Electron Beam Welding** | LBW | Vacuum purity, very narrow HAZ |
| **Copper busbar in switchgear** | **GMAW (with Cu-Si filler)** | Resistance brazing | Copper's high conductivity needs high heat input |
| **Dissimilar metals (steel-to-aluminum)** | **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | Explosion welding + GTAW | Avoids brittle Fe-Al intermetallic layer |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Master Application Selection Logic

    RW --> RSEW[Seam]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"List any three industrial applications of Friction Stir Welding (FSW) and state one reason it is preferred over conventional arc welding for those applications."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key points):**

1. **Aluminum ship deck panels (5–12 mm):** FSW is preferred because it is a solid-state process — *no melting, no porosity, no solidification cracking.* It is suitable for long, straight butt joints on **5xxx and 6xxx series aluminum alloys** widely used in marine structures.
2. **Aerospace fuselage panels (Al-Li / Al-Mg-Sc):** Preferred due to *low distortion* and *superior fatigue performance* — critical for cyclic-loaded airframes.
3. **Railway car body extrusion welding (6xxx series):** Preferred for *high repeatability, automation compatibility, and zero fumes/spatter*, which is essential for enclosed railway car manufacturing cells.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "FSW is good for aluminum" without specifying the *alloy series, the joint type, or the property advantage.* Always mention **at least one specific property (e.g., no HAZ liquation cracking)** for full marks.

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Why is Electron Beam Welding (EBW) preferred for titanium and refractory metal components in aerospace? Mention any two limitations of EBW."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Why preferred:*
- The vacuum environment (**< 10⁻² Pa**) prevents **oxidation and contamination** of reactive metals like titanium, niobium, and zirconium.
- The high power density (**≈ 10⁶ W/mm²**) creates a **deep, narrow, keyhole-mode weld** with a very narrow HAZ — essential for thin aerospace skins.

*Two limitations:*
1. **High capital cost** — requires vacuum chamber, electron gun, and high-voltage power supply.
2. **Limited to in-chamber work** — *the workpiece size is constrained by the vacuum chamber dimensions*, making it unsuitable for very large structures (e.g., ship hulls, bridge girders).
3. (Optional third) **X-ray generation** — requires radiation shielding for operator safety.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the applications of **SMAW, GMAW, and SAW** with respect to (i) typical base metals welded, (ii) production environment (shop/field), and (iii) deposition rate. Select the most suitable process for fabricating a **30 mm thick low-carbon steel pressure vessel shell** in a workshop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** process. List any **four industrial applications** of FSW and explain why it is preferred for **welding 6 mm thick AA5083 marine-grade aluminum plates.**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table + Process Selection [7 marks]**

| Criterion | SMAW | GMAW | SAW |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base metals | MS, low-alloy steel, cast iron, stainless | MS, SS, Al, Cu, low-alloy | MS, low-alloy, SS, heavy plates |
| Environment | Both shop and field (portable) | Mostly shop (gas cylinder) | Shop only (flux hopper, wire spool) |
| Deposition rate | 1–3 kg/h (manual) | 3–6 kg/h (semi-auto) | **8–25 kg/h** (auto, multi-arc) |
| Slag | Yes (must chip) | Minimal / no slag | Yes (self-peeling) |
| Heat input | Moderate | Moderate-low | High but distributed |
| Position capability | All positions | All positions | Flat and horizontal only |

**Selection for 30 mm thick MS pressure vessel (in shop):**
**Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** is the most suitable.
- **Reason 1 (deposition):** SAW has the **highest deposition rate (8–25 kg/h)** and can achieve full penetration in a single pass up to 25–30 mm.
- **Reason 2 (quality):** The flux blanket protects the arc from atmospheric contamination → **low defect rate**, suitable for radiographed pressure-vessel welds per **ASME Section IX** standards.
- **Reason 3 (automation):** SAW can be mechanized with **tandem-arc or multi-wire systems**, giving consistent quality in long shell seams.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 1-mark deduction:** Students select GMAW because "it's modern" — but for 30 mm thick plate, **single-pass penetration** with GMAW is impractical; it would need multiple passes → **more distortion, more time, more cost.** Always justify with the **single-pass penetration capability** of SAW.

**(b) FSW Block Diagram + 4 Applications + Aluminum Justification [7 marks]**

**Process Explanation (3 marks):**
- FSW uses a **non-consumable rotating tool** consisting of a **shoulder** and a **pin (probe)**.
- The tool is plunged into the joint line between two clamped plates.
- **Frictional heat** generated at the shoulder-workpiece interface **plasticizes** the material without melting.
- The rotating pin **stirs** the plasticized material, and the shoulder **forges** it into a consolidated joint as the tool traverses.
- Below the tool, the material **recrystallizes** into a fine-grained stir zone (SZ).

**Four Industrial Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Aluminum ship deck and hull panels** (5xxx and 6xxx series).
2. **Aerospace fuselage panels** (AA2050, AA2195 Al-Li).
3. **Railway car body side panels** (AA6005, AA6082).
4. **Automotive battery trays and EV sub-frames** (die-cast aluminum).

**Why preferred for 6 mm AA5083 marine plates (2 marks):**
- **AA5083 is solidification-crack sensitive** in fusion welding due to its Mg content. FSW is a **solid-state process** → *no melting → no solidification cracks.*
- **Marine environment demands corrosion resistance** — FSW joints have *no porosity and no slag inclusions*, giving better long-term corrosion performance in seawater.
- **Low distortion** — critical for ship panel flatness required for subsequent fairing and painting operations.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the applications of **Laser Beam Welding (LBW) and Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** in the automotive and aerospace industries. Highlight **three key differences** between LBW and EBW.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** process with a functional block diagram. State **two specific reasons** why RSW is the dominant joining process for **automotive sheet metal body-in-white applications**, and compute the **heat generated** in a single spot weld given: current **I = 9,000 A**, contact resistance **R = 120 μΩ**, weld time **t = 0.25 s**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) LBW vs EBW Applications + 3 Differences [7 marks]**

**LBW Applications:**
- **Automotive tailored blanks** — laser-welded different-thickness sheets for optimized crash performance.
- **Transmission gear welding** — circumferential welds on synchronizer hubs.
- **Battery pack welding** — tab-to-cell welding in EVs (aluminum and copper).
- **Medical device assembly** — pacemaker and stent welding.

**EBW Applications:**
- **Aerospace titanium fuselage frames** (e.g., Boeing 787, Airbus A350).
- **Jet engine rings and casings** — Inconel, titanium alloys.
- **Nuclear fuel rod end caps** (Zircaloy-4) in vacuum purity.
- **Missile motor casings** — high-strength maraging steel.

**Three Key Differences (3 marks):**

| # | Criterion | LBW | EBW |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Medium | **Photon beam (light)** — can be in air | **Electron beam** — *requires vacuum* |
| 2 | Weldable materials | Conductive + non-conductive (plastics, ceramics, glass) | Only electrically conductive metals |
| 3 | Joint geometry | Best for lap and flange joints; limited to short penetration in air (deep in vacuum LBW) | Excellent for **deep, narrow butt welds** (depth-to-width ratio up to 30:1) |

**(b) RSW Process + 2 Reasons + Heat Calculation [7 marks]**

**RSW Functional Block Diagram (2 marks):**

1. Two or more sheets are clamped between **copper alloy electrodes** under **forging force (F)**.
2. A **high current (typically 5–20 kA)** is passed for a **short duration (a few cycles, 0.04–0.5 s)**.
3. Heat $Q = I^2 R t$ is generated, with maximum heat at the **sheet-to-sheet (faying) interface** due to highest local resistance.
4. A **molten nugget** forms at the interface, surrounded by a **plasticized HAZ**.
5. Current is stopped; **electrode force is maintained** (forging) to consolidate the nugget.

**Two Reasons for RSW Dominance in BIW (2 marks):**
1. **Speed and automation** — modern car bodies have **≈ 4,000–5,000 spot welds**; RSW can be done in **0.2–0.4 s per weld** with robotic guns, making high-volume production feasible.
2. **No filler metal, no shielding gas, no flux** — *low consumable cost, no spatter, no fumes*, ideal for indoor automotive assembly lines.

**Heat Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: I = 9,000 A, R = 120 μΩ = 120 × 10⁻⁶ Ω, t = 0.25 s.

$$Q = I^2 \times R \times t$$

$$Q = (9{,}000)^2 \times (120 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.25$$

$$Q = 81{,}000{,}000 \times 0.00012 \times 0.25$$

Step-by-step:
- $(9{,}000)^2 = 81{,}000{,}000$
- $81{,}000{,}000 \times 120 \times 10^{-6} = 81{,}000{,}000 \times 0.00012 = 9{,}720$
- $9{,}720 \times 0.25 = 2{,}430 \;\text{J}$

$$\boxed{Q = 2{,}430 \;\text{J} = 2.43 \;\text{kJ}}$$

This **2.43 kJ** is sufficient to form a nugget of **≈ 5–6 mm diameter** in 1.2–1.5 mm steel sheet.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** In RSW heat calculations, students often forget to convert **μΩ to Ω** (multiply by 10⁻⁶) — losing 1 mark. **Pitfall #2:** Students sometimes use **Joule's law for power ($P = I^2R$) and stop there**, forgetting to multiply by **time t** to get energy. **Pitfall #3:** Writing "2.43 kW" instead of "2.43 kJ" — the unit is energy, not power.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Module 3 — Applications Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Fusion Welding Applications:** SMAW → field + thick MS; GMAW → automation + SS/Al; GTAW → clean root + nonferrous; SAW → thick plate + shop + high deposition; PAW → thin tube + aerospace; OAW → maintenance + thin sheet.
- **Resistance Welding Applications:** RSW → automotive BIW (≈ 4,000–5,000 welds/car); RSEW → leak-tight containers; RPW → cross-wire + nuts; FBW → rails + pipe longitudinal.
- **Solid-State Welding Applications:** FSW → Al ship panels (no HAZ cracks); FW → drive shafts; USW → foils + battery tabs; DB → aerospace honeycomb; EXW → Al-steel clad plate.
- **High-Energy Beam Applications:** LBW → tailored blanks + EV battery; EBW → titanium aerospace + vacuum purity.
- **Brazing Applications:** Carbide tips, heat exchangers, refrigeration, honeycomb structures. Filler MP **> 450 °C**.
- **Soldering Applications:** PCB assembly, copper plumbing, jewelry. Filler MP **< 450 °C**, RoHS-compliant lead-free (Sn-Ag-Cu) ≈ 217 °C eutectic.
- **Thermite (Aluminothermic) Welding Applications:** Rail track in-field joints (CWR), large-diameter pipe in-field.
- **Key Heat Input Formula:** $H = \eta V I / (v \times 1000)$ in **kJ/mm** — controls HAZ width and distortion.
- **Joule's Law for Resistance Welding:** $Q = I^2 R t$ in **Joules** — high current × short time.
- **Process Selection Four Pillars:** (1) Base metal, (2) Thickness, (3) Production volume, (4) Quality/distortion requirement.
- **Heat-Sensitive Alloys** (Ti, Al-Li, Inconel) → LBW, EBW, FSW, PAW.
- **Thick Plate / Heavy Fabrication** (≥ 25 mm MS) → SAW, Electroslag (ESW).
- **Dissimilar Metals** → Friction welding, explosion bonding, brazing (avoid Fe-Al intermetallics in fusion).
- **EBW Constraint:** Vacuum chamber < 10⁻² Pa → limits workpiece size.
- **FSW Advantage:** Solid-state, no melting → no porosity, no solidification cracks, low distortion.
- **RSW Dominance in Auto:** 0.2 s/weld, no filler, robot-friendly, 4,000+ welds per car.
- **Industrial Heat Input Range:** Thin sheet 0.2–0.5 kJ/mm; thick plate 1.5–5 kJ/mm (SAW).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question says *"Discuss applications of welding in [industry]"*, structure your answer as **(i) Brief introduction to industry requirements, (ii) Process selection with one primary + one secondary, (iii) Justification with material + thickness + property, (iv) One numerical or property-based supporting fact.** This 4-part structure is the KTU marking template.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems. Types of Die - Progressive Dies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems. Types of Die - Progressive Dies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Sheet Metal Cutting Operations — Formal Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme / PBMET304**, sheet metal cutting is classified as a **chipless manufacturing process** where the metal is separated (not removed as chips) by applying a shearing force exceeding the ultimate shear strength of the material. The two principal operations are:

- **Blanking**: The process of cutting a desired shape (the *blank*) **out of** the sheet metal strip, where the removed portion becomes the useful product.
- **Piercing (Punching)**: The process of making a **hole** in the sheet metal, where the removed slug is the waste and the sheet is the useful product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** In *Blanking*, the **B**lank is the **B**eneficial part. In *Piercing*, the **P**unch moves down and the **P**iece of waste falls through.

### 1.2 Die — Formal Definition

A **Die** is a specialized tool used in press operations to produce the desired shape on sheet metal by means of plastic deformation or shearing. It consists of a **male component (punch)** and a **female component (die block)** that work in tandem.

### 1.3 Types of Dies (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

| Die Type | Operations per Stroke | Complexity | Application |
|----------|----------------------|------------|-------------|
| **Simple Die (Single-Operation)** | 1 | Lowest | Trivial blanks, low volume |
| **Compound Die** | Multiple (same station) | Medium | Washer, thin parts with high accuracy |
| **Combination Die** | Compounding + Piercing | Medium-High | Lanced parts |
| **Progressive Die** | 1 per station, multiple stations | High | Mass production, complex parts |

### 1.4 Progressive Die — The Core Focus

> [!NOTE]
> **Progressive Die (Definition):** A multi-station die in which two or more pairs of punches and dies are arranged in sequence so that as the strip advances from station to station, different operations (piercing, notching, blanking, etc.) are progressively performed on the workpiece until the final part is completely formed in the last station.

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — "The Assembly Line of Stamping"

Imagine a **punching machine as a chocolate factory assembly line**:
- The **sheet metal strip** is the **conveyor belt** of chocolate.
- Each **station** of the progressive die is a **worker** who performs ONE small task — the first worker cuts a slot, the second worker punches a hole, the third bends a tab, and the last worker cuts the final shape from the strip.
- Between stations, the strip moves forward by a fixed distance called the **pitch** (or *strip advance*), guided by a **pilot pin** so that each "worker" knows exactly where to act.
- By the time the strip reaches the end, a complete, multi-feature part drops out — fully finished in a single press stroke per station.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use progressive dies?** Because they are the **backbone of mass production**. A car door hinge, a washer, a beverage can tab — all are made in progressive dies at speeds of 200–1000 strokes per minute.

### 1.6 Critical Standard Metrics (Bold for KTU Recall)

- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS), σ_u** — in **MPa** or **N/mm²**
- **Shear Strength, τ** — typically **0.7 to 0.8 × σ_u** for ductile materials
- **Die Clearance, c** — typically **3% to 7% of stock thickness (t)** per side
- **Penetration / Push-Through Depth** — **0.33 × t** for clean shear
- **Material Utilization (η)** — ratio of part area to strip area per pitch

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Shear Cutting

Sheet metal shearing is a three-stage process:

1. **Elastic Deformation Stage**: The punch contacts the strip, and the material deforms elastically.
2. **Plastic Deformation Stage**: The punch continues downward; the material flows plastically and rolls into the die cavity.
3. **Fracture Stage**: When shear stress exceeds ultimate shear strength, cracks initiate at the punch edge and die edge, propagating inward until they meet, separating the part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** The **clearance (c)** between punch and die is the most heavily tested variable. Insufficient clearance causes *double burnishing* and rapid punch wear; excessive clearance causes *excessive burr* and a *tapered cut edge*.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Formula

| # | Why the Formula Matters | Engineering Reality |
|---|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Force Calculation**: Prevents press overload | A 5 mm thick, 200 mm perimeter mild steel part needs ~400 kN force — tonnage selection is critical |
| 2 | **Clearance Selection**: Affects cut quality | Too tight → punch breakage; too loose → burr and dimensional inaccuracy |
| 3 | **Strip Layout**: Maximizes material utilization | In progressive dies, layout can mean 10–30% material savings |
| 4 | **Center of Pressure**: Prevents side thrust and die wear | An off-center blanking force can deflect the punch, causing uneven shear |
| 5 | **Number of Strokes**: Determines production rate | Strip advance × stations / strip length = cycles per coil |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All symbols are standard per KTU 2024 Scheme. Units are **N** (force), **mm** (length), **MPa** (stress).

$$
F_b = L \times t \times \tau
$$

$$
F_p = L \times t \times \tau
$$

$$
F_{press} = \frac{F_b \times (\text{safety factor})}{P}
$$

$$
F_{strip} = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \times F_b
$$

$$
P_{max} = \frac{F_b}{t \times L} = \tau_{max}
$$

$$
c = K_c \times t \quad \text{(per side)}
$$

$$
L_{strip} = n \times p + 2 \times \text{margin}
$$

$$
W_{strip} = D_{max} + 2c + 2 \times \text{margin}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{A_{part}}{p \times W_{strip}} \times 100
$$

$$
x_{\bar{}} = \frac{\sum L_i \times x_i}{L}, \quad y_{\bar{}} = \frac{\sum L_i \times y_i}{L}
$$

$$
F_{total} = F_{max, station} \times \text{stations}
$$

**Symbol Legend (KTU Standard):**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| $F_b$ | Blanking force | N |
| $F_p$ | Piercing force | N |
| $L$ | Perimeter of cut | mm |
| $t$ | Stock thickness | mm |
| $\tau$ | Shear strength of material | MPa (N/mm²) |
| $c$ | Die clearance (per side) | mm |
| $K_c$ | Clearance factor (0.03 to 0.07) | dimensionless |
| $L_{strip}$ | Total length of strip per part | mm |
| $p$ | Pitch (strip advance) | mm |
| $W_{strip}$ | Strip width | mm |
| $\eta$ | Material utilization | % |
| $P$ | Press capacity factor (0.6 to 0.7) | dimensionless |
| $x_{\bar{}}, y_{\bar{}}$ | Center of pressure coordinates | mm |
| $D_{max}$ | Maximum part dimension along width | mm |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry**: Progressive dies manufacture body panels, brackets, and fasteners at 30–60 strokes/min.
- **Electronics**: SIM card trays, connector pins, and lead frames are stamped at 1000+ strokes/min.
- **Aerospace**: Titanium and Inconel brackets for engines use compound and progressive dies.
- **Appliances**: Washing machine drums, refrigerator parts — all progressive-die produced.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Blanking Force Equation

The fundamental logic is rooted in the **shear stress principle**: force equals the resisting shear area multiplied by the shear strength of the material.

**Step 1: Identify the resisting area.**

When a punch presses down on sheet metal, the material resists along a cylindrical/rectangular shear surface around the punch perimeter. This shear surface has:

- Length (along the cut) = $L$ (perimeter in mm)
- Height (depth of cut) = $t$ (stock thickness in mm)

Therefore:

$$
A_{shear} = L \times t
$$

**Step 2: Apply the shear strength.**

The material will fracture when shear stress reaches $\tau$:

$$
F_b = A_{shear} \times \tau = L \times t \times \tau
$$

**Step 3: Account for shear effect on punch face.**

If the punch is not flat (e.g., has a shear angle of 1°–2°), the effective force reduces by a shear factor $K_s$ (typically 0.7 to 1.0):

$$
F_b = L \times t \times \tau \times K_s
$$

**Step 4: Determine press tonnage.**

KTU standard practice applies a safety margin and press efficiency factor:

$$
F_{press} = \frac{F_b}{P}, \quad P = 0.6 \text{ to } 0.7
$$

---

### 3.2 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 1 — Single-Stage Blanking Force

> **Problem Statement:**
> A circular blank of diameter **50 mm** is to be produced from a **2 mm thick** low-carbon steel strip. The ultimate tensile strength is **400 MPa**, and the shear strength is **75% of UTS**. The die clearance per side is **0.06 mm** (3% of thickness). Calculate:
> (a) The required blanking force.
> (b) The press tonnage required.
> (c) The material utilization if a strip of width 60 mm is used with a 5 mm edge margin and 5 mm end margin, assuming a pitch of 56 mm.

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $D = 50$ mm
- Thickness $t = 2$ mm
- UTS $\sigma_u = 400$ MPa
- Shear strength $\tau = 0.75 \times 400 = 300$ MPa
- Clearance $c = 0.06$ mm (per side, *not needed for force, used for die design*)
- Strip width $W = 60$ mm
- Pitch $p = 56$ mm
- Edge margin $m_e = 5$ mm
- End margin $m_{end} = 5$ mm

**Solution:**

**(a) Blanking Force:**

Perimeter of circular blank:

$$
L = \pi \times D = \pi \times 50 = 157.08 \text{ mm}
$$

Substitute into the force equation:

$$
F_b = L \times t \times \tau
$$

$$
F_b = 157.08 \times 2 \times 300
$$

$$
F_b = 94{,}248 \text{ N} \approx 94.25 \text{ kN}
$$

**(b) Press Tonnage:**

Assuming press efficiency $P = 0.7$:

$$
F_{press} = \frac{F_b}{P} = \frac{94{,}248}{0.7} = 134{,}640 \text{ N} \approx 135 \text{ kN}
$$

Thus a press of at least **150 kN** (next standard size) is recommended.

**(c) Material Utilization:**

Strip width effective for blanking:

$$
W_{effective} = W - 2 m_e = 60 - 2 \times 5 = 50 \text{ mm}
$$

Since the part diameter = 50 mm, the part just fits the strip width.

Strip area per pitch:

$$
A_{strip} = p \times W = 56 \times 60 = 3360 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Part area:

$$
A_{part} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 50^2 = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Material utilization:

$$
\eta = \frac{A_{part}}{A_{strip}} \times 100 = \frac{1963.5}{3360} \times 100 = 58.4\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $F_b = 94.25$ kN, Press tonnage ≈ 135 kN, $\eta = 58.4\%$.

---

### 3.3 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 2 — Piercing Multiple Holes

> **Problem Statement:**
