> A rectangular blank of size **100 mm × 60 mm** with **2.5 mm thickness** has to be pierced with **4 holes of 10 mm diameter** each. The shear strength of the material is **250 MPa**. Find:
> (a) The total piercing force.
> (b) The stripper force (assume 8% of piercing force).

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Piercing Force:**

Perimeter of one hole:

$$
L_1 = \pi \times D = \pi \times 10 = 31.42 \text{ mm}
$$

Total perimeter for 4 holes:

$$
L_{total} = 4 \times 31.42 = 125.66 \text{ mm}
$$

Piercing force:

$$
F_p = L_{total} \times t \times \tau
$$

$$
F_p = 125.66 \times 2.5 \times 250
$$

$$
F_p = 78{,}540 \text{ N} \approx 78.54 \text{ kN}
$$

**(b) Stripper Force:**

$$
F_{strip} = 0.08 \times F_p = 0.08 \times 78{,}540 = 6283 \text{ N} \approx 6.28 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Answer:** $F_p = 78.54$ kN, $F_{strip} = 6.28$ kN.

---

### 3.4 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 3 — Strip Layout in a Progressive Die

> **Problem Statement:**
> A washer of outer diameter 30 mm and inner diameter 15 mm is to be produced in a **4-station progressive die** from a 1.5 mm thick strip. Operations per station:
> - Station 1: Pilot hole
> - Station 2: Piercing the inner hole
> - Station 3: Notching the outer profile
> - Station 4: Blanking the final washer from the strip
>
> The strip width is 36 mm. Edge margin = 3 mm. Material is mild steel with UTS = 380 MPa. Calculate:
> (a) Strip advance (pitch).
> (b) Number of washers per meter of strip.
> (c) Stripping force (assume 6% of blanking force).
> (d) Center of pressure of station 4.

**Given:**
- $D_o = 30$ mm, $D_i = 15$ mm
- $t = 1.5$ mm
- $\sigma_u = 380$ MPa
- $\tau = 0.75 \times 380 = 285$ MPa
- Strip width $W = 36$ mm
- Stations: 4

**Solution:**

**(a) Strip Advance (Pitch):**

The pitch must accommodate the maximum part dimension along the strip length direction. For a circular washer, the diameter dictates the pitch.

$$
p = D_o + \text{webs/bridges} = 30 + 2 \text{ mm (web)} = 32 \text{ mm}
$$

(Alternatively, KTU commonly takes $p = D_o + 2$ mm for simple circular washers.)

**(b) Number of Washers per Meter:**

$$
n = \frac{1000 \text{ mm}}{p} = \frac{1000}{32} = 31.25
$$

Round down to 31 washers per meter (with the last 8 mm being scrap).

**(c) Stripping Force:**

The blanking perimeter in station 4 is the outer circle:

$$
L = \pi \times D_o = \pi \times 30 = 94.25 \text{ mm}
$$

Blanking force:

$$
F_b = L \times t \times \tau = 94.25 \times 1.5 \times 285 = 40{,}292 \text{ N}
$$

Stripping force:

$$
F_{strip} = 0.06 \times 40{,}292 = 2417.5 \text{ N} \approx 2.42 \text{ kN}
$$

**(d) Center of Pressure (Station 4 — Blanking):**

For a symmetric circular blank, the centroid coincides with the geometric center. With die set center at origin:

$$
x_{\bar{}} = 0, \quad y_{\bar{}} = 0
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** For non-symmetric parts, students must sum $(L_i \times x_i)$ and $(L_i \times y_i)$ separately, then divide by total $L$. Forgetting to convert to common origin = lost marks.

---

### 3.5 NUMERICAL PROBLEM 4 — Center of Pressure for L-Shaped Blank

> **Problem Statement:**
> An L-shaped blank has the following cut segments. The die set center is at the origin. Find the center of pressure.

| Segment | Length $L_i$ (mm) | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) |
|---------|-------------------|------------|------------|
| A-B | 40 | 0 | 20 |
| B-C | 30 | 30 | 30 |
| C-D | 40 | 50 | 0 |
| D-A | 30 | 10 | -15 |

**Solution:**

Total perimeter:

$$
L = 40 + 30 + 40 + 30 = 140 \text{ mm}
$$

Sum of $L_i \times x_i$:

$$
\sum L_i x_i = (40 \times 0) + (30 \times 30) + (40 \times 50) + (30 \times 10)
$$

$$
\sum L_i x_i = 0 + 900 + 2000 + 300 = 3200
$$

Sum of $L_i \times y_i$:

$$
\sum L_i y_i = (40 \times 20) + (30 \times 30) + (40 \times 0) + (30 \times -15)
$$

$$
\sum L_i y_i = 800 + 900 + 0 - 450 = 1250
$$

Center of pressure:

$$
x_{\bar{}} = \frac{3200}{140} = 22.86 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
y_{\bar{}} = \frac{1250}{140} = 8.93 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** The press ram is moved to align with this $(x_{\bar{}}, y_{\bar{}})$ to prevent side thrust. If the center of pressure is offset by more than **1.5 mm per 100 kN**, the die must be redesigned.

---

### 3.6 Numerical Problem 5 — Force with Shear Angle

> **Problem Statement:**
> A blank of perimeter **600 mm** and thickness **4 mm** is to be cut from material with $\tau = 320$ MPa. The punch has a shear angle of **1.5°**, so the shear factor is **0.85**. Find the blanking force.

**Solution:**

$$
F_b = L \times t \times \tau \times K_s
$$

$$
F_b = 600 \times 4 \times 320 \times 0.85
$$

$$
F_b = 652{,}800 \text{ N} \approx 652.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Answer:** $F_b = 652.8$ kN.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Progressive Die — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[COIL STOCK UNWIND] --> nodeB[STRAIGHTENER GUIDE]
    nodeB --> nodeC[STATION 1 PILOT PUNCH]
    nodeC --> nodeD[STATION 2 PIERCING PUNCH]
    nodeD --> nodeE[STATION 3 NOTCHING PUNCH]
    nodeE --> nodeF[STATION 4 BLANKING PUNCH]
    nodeF --> nodeG[FINISHED PART OUT]
    nodeF --> nodeH[SCRAP STRIP EXIT]
    nodeI[GUIDE PINS] -.-> nodeC
    nodeI -.-> nodeD
    nodeI -.-> nodeE
    nodeI -.-> nodeF
    nodeJ[STOP PIN PITCH CONTROL] -.-> nodeB
```

### 4.2 Strip Layout — Block Level Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[STRIP INLET EDGE] --> nodeB[PILOT HOLE STATION 1]
    nodeB --> nodeC[INNER HOLE PIERCE STATION 2]
    nodeC --> nodeD[NOTCH OPERATION STATION 3]
    nodeD --> nodeE[BLANKING STATION 4]
    nodeE --> nodeF[SCRAP WEB]
    nodeE --> nodeG[FINISHED WASHER OUT]
    nodeH[PITCH DISTANCE P] -.-> nodeB
    nodeH -.-> nodeC
    nodeH -.-> nodeD
    nodeH -.-> nodeE
```

### 4.3 Die Components — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[UPPER SHOE BOLSTER] --> nodeB[PUNCH HOLDER]
    nodeB --> nodeC[PUNCH SHANK]
    nodeB --> nodeD[WORKING PUNCH]
    nodeE[STRIPPER PLATE] --> nodeD
    nodeF[DIE BLOCK LOWER] --> nodeG[DIE INSERT]
    nodeG --> nodeH[GUIDE BUSHING]
    nodeI[LOWER SHOE BOLSTER] --> nodeF
    nodeI --> nodeJ[PILOT PIN]
    nodeI --> nodeK[STOP PIN]
    nodeI --> nodeL[SPRING HOUSING]
```

### 4.4 Force Distribution Across Progressive Die Stations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[STATION 1 PILOT] -->|Low Force| nodeB[STATION 2 PIERCE]
    nodeB -->|Medium Force| nodeC[STATION 3 NOTCH]
    nodeC -->|Variable Force| nodeD[STATION 4 BLANK]
    nodeD -->|Maximum Force| nodeE[PRESS RAM]
    nodeE -.-> nodeF[CRANK MECHANISM]
    nodeF -.-> nodeG[FLYWHEEL]
```

### 4.5 Progressive Die Strip Advancement (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Press as PRESS RAM
    participant Strip as SHEET STRIP
    participant Pilot as PILOT PIN
    participant Tool as DIE STATION
    Press->>Strip: Move down (Cutting)
    Press->>Tool: Operate punch
    Press->>Strip: Move up (Retract)
    Strip->>Pilot: Align to pilot
    Strip->>Strip: Advance by pitch P
    Note over Press,Strip: Repeat per stroke
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between Blanking and Piercing operations with neat sketches."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Blanking | Piercing |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Definition | Cutting the desired shape **out** of sheet metal | Making a **hole** in sheet metal |
| Useful Part | The **blank** removed | The **sheet** with the hole |
| Waste | The remaining strip (web) | The slug (cylindrical chip) |
| Tool Motion | Punch moves down, shears perimeter | Same motion, but slug is waste |
| Example | Coin stamping | Washer hole punching |

> **Sketches (Mental Picture):**
> - **Blanking**: ⬜ → ✂️ → 🟦 (the square is the part) + leftover frame.
> - **Piercing**: ⬜ → ✂️ → ⬜ with a hole + a small slug falls out.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"What is a Progressive Die? List any four of its essential components."**

**Model Answer:**

> **Progressive Die:** A multi-station die in which successive operations (piercing, blanking, notching, etc.) are performed at different stations as the strip is advanced through the die in steps.

**Four Essential Components:**
1. **Die Block (Lower Die)** — Houses the female cutting edge.
2. **Punch Holder (Upper Die)** — Holds the punches in alignment.
3. **Stripper Plate** — Strips the metal from the punch after each stroke.
4. **Pilot Pin / Stop Pin** — Locates the strip at the correct pitch.
5. **Guide Pins & Bushings** — Maintain alignment between upper and lower halves.
6. **Springs** — Provide the stripper force and return mechanism.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU Model Question Paper Pattern]

**Q. (a)** A circular disk of diameter **75 mm** is to be blanked from a **3 mm thick** cold-rolled steel strip. The ultimate tensile strength of the material is **450 MPa**. The die clearance is **5% of thickness per side**. Calculate: (i) Shear strength, (ii) Blanking force, (iii) Press tonnage (assume efficiency 70%), (iv) Stripping force (assume 7% of blanking force), and (v) Material utilization for a 1.2 m strip with 5 mm margins and pitch = 80 mm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 75$ mm, $t = 3$ mm
- $\sigma_u = 450$ MPa
- $c = 0.05 \times 3 = 0.15$ mm per side
- Strip length $L_s = 1.2$ m = 1200 mm
- Pitch $p = 80$ mm
- Margins = 5 mm
- $\eta_{press} = 0.7$

**(i) Shear Strength:**

$$
\tau = 0.75 \times \sigma_u = 0.75 \times 450 = 337.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Blanking Force:**

Perimeter:

$$
L = \pi \times D = \pi \times 75 = 235.62 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
F_b = L \times t \times \tau = 235.62 \times 3 \times 337.5
$$

$$
F_b = 238{,}515 \text{ N} \approx 238.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**(iii) Press Tonnage:**

$$
F_{press} = \frac{F_b}{0.7} = \frac{238{,}515}{0.7} = 340{,}736 \text{ N} \approx 341 \text{ kN}
$$

**(iv) Stripping Force:**

$$
F_{strip} = 0.07 \times F_b = 0.07 \times 238{,}515 = 16{,}696 \text{ N} \approx 16.7 \text{ kN}
$$

**(v) Material Utilization:**

Number of parts in 1.2 m strip:

$$
n = \frac{1200 - 2 \times 5}{80} = \frac{1190}{80} = 14.875
$$

So 14 full parts with 70 mm waste (≈ 0.875 × 80).

Strip area used per part:

$$
A_{strip/part} = p \times W
$$

Strip width (D + 2c + 2 × margin = 75 + 2(0.15) + 10 = 85.3 mm):

$$
W = 85.3 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
A_{strip/part} = 80 \times 85.3 = 6824 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Part area:

$$
A_{part} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 75^2 = 4417.86 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{4417.86}{6824} \times 100 = 64.7\%
$$

---

**Q. (b)** Describe with a neat sketch the **construction and working of a Progressive Die**. State its advantages and limitations over a Compound Die. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
A progressive die has multiple stations. The strip is fed step-by-step, with each station performing a specific operation (pilot, pierce, blank). A **pilot pin** engages a previously made pilot hole to precisely locate the strip at each station.

**Typical Stations (e.g., for a washer):**
- Station 1: Pilot hole piercing
- Station 2: Pilot engagement + inner hole piercing
- Station 3: Notching / blanking
- Station 4: Final separation

**Neat Sketch:** Refer to Section 4.1 and 4.3 Mermaid blocks.

**Advantages over Compound Die:**
1. **Higher production rate** — continuous feeding.
2. **Lower press tonnage required** — operations are split across stations.
3. **Complex parts possible** — features impossible in a single compound die.
4. **Better die life** — forces are distributed.
5. **Easier sharpening** — individual stations can be reground.

**Limitations:**
1. **Higher die cost** — complex construction.
2. **Larger strip consumption** — bridges between parts.
3. **Less dimensional accuracy** between features (cumulative tolerance).
4. **Setup time is higher**.

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q. (a)** An L-shaped blank has the following dimensions. Find the **center of pressure** if the die set center is at the origin. Material: $\tau = 280$ MPa, $t = 2$ mm. Calculate the **blanking force**. **(7 marks)**

| Segment | Length $L_i$ (mm) | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) |
|---------|-------------------|------------|------------|
| P1 | 80 | 40 | 0 |
| P2 | 60 | 0 | 30 |
| P3 | 80 | -40 | 0 |
| P4 | 60 | 0 | -30 |

**Model Solution:**

Total perimeter:

$$
L = 80 + 60 + 80 + 60 = 280 \text{ mm}
$$

Center of pressure:

$$
\sum L_i x_i = (80 \times 40) + (60 \times 0) + (80 \times -40) + (60 \times 0)
$$

$$
\sum L_i x_i = 3200 + 0 - 3200 + 0 = 0
$$

$$
\sum L_i y_i = (80 \times 0) + (60 \times 30) + (80 \times 0) + (60 \times -30)
$$

$$
\sum L_i y_i = 0 + 1800 + 0 - 1800 = 0
$$

**Center of Pressure: (0, 0)** — perfectly balanced, no side thrust.

**Blanking Force:**

$$
F_b = L \times t \times \tau = 280 \times 2 \times 280 = 156{,}800 \text{ N} \approx 156.8 \text{ kN}
$$

---

**Q. (b)** With neat sketches, explain the classification of **Press Tools / Dies** based on:
(i) Number of operations per stroke,
(ii) Type of operation.
Give two examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Based on Number of Operations per Stroke:**

| Type | Operations per Stroke | Example |
|------|----------------------|---------|
| **Simple Die** | One | Simple square blank |
| **Compound Die** | Multiple (same station) | Washer blanking + piercing |
| **Combination Die** | Piercing + Blanking in one stroke | Lanced blank with bend |
| **Progressive Die** | One per station (sequential) | Multi-feature bracket |

**(ii) Based on Type of Operation:**

1. **Cutting Dies** — Blanking, piercing, notching, lancing, shaving, trimming.
2. **Bending Dies** — V-bending, U-bending, edge bending.
3. **Drawing Dies** — Deep drawing, redrawing.
4. **Forming Dies** — Embossing, coining, bulging.
5. **Compound / Progressive Dies** — Combination of above.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.75 factor** for shear strength — marks deducted for using UTS directly.
> 2. **Pitch calculation error** — students use diameter as pitch without adding 2 mm bridge/web. KTU deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Sign error in center of pressure** — when $x_i$ or $y_i$ is negative, students often add instead of subtract. Cost: 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting press efficiency factor (P = 0.6–0.7)** — required tonnage is undervalued. Cost: 1 mark.
> 5. **Stripping force omitted** — frequently forgotten; 1 mark lost in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Units not written** — KTU strictly requires **N** or **kN** for force and **MPa** for stress.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin these points to memory for last-minute KTU prep:**

- **Blanking Force = $L \times t \times \tau$** — apply to ALL sheet metal cutting problems.
- **Shear Strength ≈ 0.75 × UTS** — for ductile steels.
- **Die Clearance (c) = 3% to 7% × t** per side — radial/diametral clearance = 2c.
- **Material Utilization** = (Part Area / Strip Area per Pitch) × 100.
- **Press Tonnage** = Blanking Force / 0.7 (with efficiency factor).
- **Stripping Force** = 5–10% of blanking force.
- **Progressive Die**: One operation per station, strip advances by fixed pitch.
- **Compound Die**: Multiple operations at SAME station, single stroke.
- **Center of Pressure Formula** = Sum of $(L_i \times x_i)$ / Total $L$, and similarly for $y$.
- **Pilot Pin** is the location-saver in progressive dies — punch must be on **leading** station, pilot on **following** station for accuracy.
- **Pitch (Strip Advance)** = Max. part dimension + 2 mm web (minimum).
- **Number of parts in strip** = (Strip length − 2 × end margin) / Pitch.
- **KTU Numerical Problem Format**: Always state Given Data → Formula → Substitution → Answer with units.
- **Sketch is MANDATORY** in 7-mark sub-questions — missing sketch = 1–2 mark deduction.
- **Compound Die vs Progressive Die**: Compound = high accuracy, low volume; Progressive = high volume, distributed load.

> **End of KTU Module 3 Notes — Numerical Problems & Progressive Dies**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned | Bloom's Taxonomy: L3–L4 (Application & Analysis)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compound Dies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compound Dies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Compound Die** is a single-station press tool that performs **two or more distinct sheet-metal operations simultaneously during a single stroke of the press**, with the workpiece remaining stationary relative to the die. The most industrially significant variant is the **Compound Blanking and Piercing Die**, in which the *outer profile* of a component is *blanked* and an *internal hole* is *pierced* in the **same ram descent**, producing a finished washer-type part without any translation of the stock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim expected form):**
> *"A compound die is a press tool in which two or more cutting (or cutting and non-cutting) operations are carried out at one station in a single stroke of the press, the material for each successive operation being located by the previous operation itself."*

**Key Identifier:** Compound dies are classified by the number of operations per stroke, **not** by the number of stations. A compound die has **ONE station** but **MULTIPLE operations per stroke**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doughnut Punch" Intuition

Imagine a **doughnut / cookie cutter that punches both the outer circle AND the inner hole in one downward motion**. That is exactly what a compound blanking-piercing die does to sheet metal:

| Analogy Element | Real Die Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Cutting the outer dough circle | **Blanking** (outer profile cut by the die) |
| Poking the central hole | **Piercing** (inner profile cut by the punch) |
| Single downward press of cutter | **One press stroke** |
| Doughnut stays on the cutter pin | **Finished part retained on a knockout pin** |
| Excess dough falls through the table | **Scrap slug ejects through die opening** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy works:** A naive sheet-metal worker might cut the outer circle, then *move* the blank to a second tool to punch the hole — that is the **progressive die** approach. A compound die eliminates the *movement* entirely, doing both jobs in the *same footprint* and the *same instant*.

## 1.3 Classification of Compound Dies

Compound dies fall under the broader family of **Press Tools** (single-station dies performing multiple operations per stroke). The taxonomy recognised in the KTU 2024 PBMET304 syllabus is:

1. **Compound Cutting Die** — performs multiple *cutting* operations (e.g., blanking + piercing).
2. **Compound Cutting-and-Forming Die** — combines cutting with a non-cutting operation (e.g., blanking + bending, or piercing + shallow drawing).
3. **Inverted Compound Die** — the workpiece is pushed *upward* onto a finger during stripping.
4. **Conventional Compound Die** — the workpiece descends with the blanking punch.

## 1.4 The Five Defining Functional Properties

A press tool is recognised as a *compound die* only if **all** the following are satisfied:

- **One station, one stroke** — no lateral translation of the strip between operations.
- **Self-locating** — the *same punch* or *same die* action provides the locating datum for every sub-operation.
- **Common shear plane** — cutting edges act on a *common horizontal plane* (the plane of the strip).
- **Concentric alignment** (in blanking + piercing) — outer and inner cutting edges share a *common axis*.
- **Single index pitch** — only **one feeding pitch** per finished component is consumed.

## 1.5 Standard Physical Metrics & Symbols (KTU Notation)

The following symbols are universally accepted on KTU answer sheets and must be memorised in the **bold** form given:

- **t** = Stock thickness, in **mm**.
- **L** = Total cut perimeter, in **mm** (sum of outer and inner perimeters for a washer).
- **S** = **Shear strength** of the material, in **N/mm²** (typically **0.7 to 0.85 of ultimate tensile strength**).
- **σ_t** = Ultimate tensile strength, in **N/mm²**.
- **σ_s** = Yield strength, in **N/mm²**.
- **c** = **Die-punch clearance** (per side), in **mm**.
- **D_d** = Die opening diameter, in **mm**.
- **D_p** = Punch diameter, in **mm**.
- **F** = Total press capacity required, in **N** or **kN**.
- **F_s** = Stripper force, in **N**.
- **η** = Shear efficiency factor (typically **0.6 to 0.7** for standard tooling).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

A **compound blanking-piercing die** executes its work in five deterministic phases. The "**why**" behind each phase is the heart of the KTU theory question.

### Phase 1 — Stock Insertion
- A pre-cut **strip / coil** is fed horizontally to a fixed stop (the **back gauge**).
- The strip lies flat on the **die block** with the **pierce punch** protruding upward through the stripper plate.
- The piercer punch may carry a small **pilot** or **locating nose** to centralise the strip relative to the cutting axis.

**Why:** Stock must be rigidly positioned because compound dies have **no inter-station locators** — the *first operation* must register the strip.

### Phase 2 — Initial Contact (Elastic Stage)
- The ram descends; the **stripper plate** contacts the strip first.
- The piercer punch tip touches the upper surface of the strip.
- A small elastic indentation forms — no plastic flow yet.

### Phase 3 — Plastic Flow & Shear Initiation
- The punch exceeds the **yield strength** of the material at the contact zone.
- Plastic flow begins; the material rolls plastically into the **die clearance** gap.
- **Three concentric zones** develop:
  1. **Rounded burnished zone** (last to separate — clean surface).
  2. **Fracture zone** (rough, torn surface).
  3. **Burr zone** (at the die exit, due to secondary fracture).

### Phase 4 — Fracture and Separation
- As the punch advances, the **shear cracks** propagating from the punch edge and the **die edge** meet inside the clearance gap.
- The shear cracks coalesce → a **slug (scrap ring)** is sheared off.
- Simultaneously, the **outer blanking punch** (or die edge, depending on design) shears the **outer profile**.
- Both events complete within **1–3 mm of punch travel** — the **penetration depth** ≈ **(0.3 to 0.5) × t**.

### Phase 5 — Stripping & Ejection
- The **stripper plate** (spring-loaded) strips the outer scrap downward.
- A **knockout pin / finger** pushes the **finished washer** upward off the die.
- The cycle is complete; ram retracts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook — "P.A.P.S.S."**:
> **P**osition → **A**pproach → **P**enetrate → **S**hear → **S**trip.

## 2.2 Critical Design Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range | KTU Exam Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Die-punch clearance per side | **c** | **4 % to 8 % of t** (for soft steel) | **Very High** |
| Shear (cutting) angle | **α** | 0° (flat) to 2° (slight) | Medium |
| Punch penetration | **h** | 0.3 t to 0.5 t | High |
| Stripper spring force | **F_sp** | 5 % to 10 % of blanking force | Medium |
| Bending under blank holder | — | Used for curling or coining | Low |
| Strip width tolerance | — | +0.0 mm to −0.2 mm | Medium |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every equation** a KTU 2024 student is expected to reproduce in Module-3 compound-die numericals.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Blanking Force** | $F_b = L \cdot t \cdot S$ | N | $L$ = outer perimeter |
| 2 | **Piercing Force** | $F_p = L_i \cdot t \cdot S$ | N | $L_i$ = inner perimeter |
| 3 | **Total Cutting Force** | $F_c = (L_o + L_i) \cdot t \cdot S$ | N | $L_o$ = outer, $L_i$ = inner |
| 4 | **Press Capacity with Overload** | $F_{press} = 1.30 \cdot F_c$ | N | 30 % safety / wear allowance |
| 5 | **Stripping Force** | $F_s = 1.30 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_s$ | N | 30 % over yield |
| 6 | **Push-off / Knockout Force** | $F_k = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot F_c$ | N | 5 %–10 % of cut force |
| 7 | **Per-Side Clearance** | $c = k \cdot t$ | mm | $k$ = 0.04 (soft) to 0.10 (hard) |
| 8 | **Die Opening (Piercing)** | $D_d = D_p + 2c$ | mm | $c$ = per-side clearance |
| 9 | **Punch Diameter (Blank)** | $D_p = D_d - 2c$ | mm | Reverse of above |
| 10 | **Shear Work** | $W = F_c \cdot h$ | J | $h$ = penetration depth |
| 11 | **Strip Width (single row)** | $W_s = D + 2c_1 + 2c_2$ | mm | $D$ = blank diameter, $c_1$ = edge trim, $c_2$ = side clearance |
| 12 | **Strip Pitch (Feed)** | $p = D + s$ | mm | $s$ = side scrap (0.5–1.0 mm) |
| 13 | **Shear Strength Approximation** | $S = 0.7 \cdot \sigma_t$ | N/mm² | For low-carbon steel |
| 14 | **Press tonnage (US)** | $T_{ton} = F_{press} \ / \ 9.81 \times 10^3$ | ton | 1 ton ≈ 8.9 kN (UK) |
| 15 | **Energy / Stroke** | $E = F_c \cdot h$ | J | Used for press kW selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** In compound dies, the **total perimeter $L$ is the *sum* of the outer and inner cut lengths** because both occur in a single stroke. Forgetting to add $L_i$ to $L_o$ is the most common single mistake costing 4–6 marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Compound dies are the **workhorse of mass-production washer, gasket, gear-blank, and shim manufacture**. Specific industrial applications include:

- **Automotive**: clutch-faces, brake-shim washers, valve-spring retainers.
- **Electrical**: lamination stacks for motors and transformers (when several holes are compound-cut).
- **Aerospace**: small precision washers for bolted joints.
- **Hardware industry**: lock washers, flat washers (most common application worldwide).
- **Electronics**: lead-frame blanking where multiple rectangular cutouts are compound-pierced.

The economic justification: for **production runs exceeding 50,000 pieces**, a compound die reduces the **per-piece cost by 25–40 %** compared to a progressive die, due to elimination of indexing and pilot systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Compound-Die Total Cutting Force

**Problem Context:** A mild-steel washer is to be produced by compound blanking + piercing. The outer diameter is $D_o$, the inner (hole) diameter is $D_i$, and the sheet thickness is $t$. Derive an expression for the **total press tonnage** the fabricator must specify.

### Step 1 — Define the Cut Perimeters

For a circular washer, the outer cut perimeter is the circumference of the outer edge, and the inner cut perimeter is the circumference of the hole:

$$
L_o = \pi \cdot D_o \quad \text{and} \quad L_i = \pi \cdot D_i
$$

**Logic:** The shear crack travels along a closed path whose length is the geometric perimeter of the cutting edge. For a circle, that is $\pi D$.

### Step 2 — Define the Total Perimeter

Because both the outer and inner cuts occur in the same stroke on the same strip:

$$
L_{total} = L_o + L_i = \pi D_o + \pi D_i = \pi (D_o + D_i)
$$

**Logic:** Compound dies shear *two* closed paths, so the perimeters add. This is the conceptual heart of the compound-die force equation.

### Step 3 — Apply the Standard Shear Equation

The basic KTU shear equation states that the cutting force is the product of the **area being sheared** (perimeter × thickness) and the **shear strength** of the material:

$$
F_c = L_{total} \cdot t \cdot S
$$

**Logic:** Imagine a thin cylindrical shell of metal of length $L_{total}$, wall thickness $t$, being sheared along its length. The area in shear is $L_{total} \cdot t$, and the unit shear stress is $S$. Their product is the total force.

### Step 4 — Substitute the Perimeters

$$
F_c = \pi (D_o + D_i) \cdot t \cdot S
$$

**Logic:** This substitution reduces the formula to one expressed purely in geometric and material parameters — convenient for design catalogues.

### Step 5 — Introduce the Press-Capacity Overload Factor

The actual press must be capable of more than the theoretical force, to account for tool wear, material variability, and misalignment:

$$
F_{press} = 1.30 \cdot F_c = 1.30 \cdot \pi (D_o + D_i) \cdot t \cdot S
$$

**Logic:** Industry convention is **30 % overload** (factor 1.3). Some KTU textbooks use 1.25; check the lecturer's preference.

### Step 6 — Convert to Tonnage (Optional, US Customary)

$$
T_{press} = \frac{F_{press}}{8.9 \times 10^{3}} \quad \text{(UK long tons)}
$$

$$
T_{press} = \frac{F_{press}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}} \quad \text{(metric tonnes)}
$$

**Logic:** Press ratings in workshops are commonly given in tonnes. The conversion uses $g \approx 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and the relation $\text{tonne-force} = 1000 \text{ kgf} \times 9.81$.

### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** $D_o = 40 \text{ mm}$, $D_i = 20 \text{ mm}$, $t = 3 \text{ mm}$, $S = 320 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (mild steel).

**Step 7a** — Compute perimeters:

$$
L_o = \pi \cdot 40 = 125.66 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_i = \pi \cdot 20 = 62.83 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7b** — Total perimeter:

$$
L_{total} = 125.66 + 62.83 = 188.49 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7c** — Total cutting force:

$$
F_c = 188.49 \times 3 \times 320 = 180,950 \text{ N} \approx 181 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 7d** — Press capacity with 30 % overload:

$$
F_{press} = 1.30 \times 181,000 = 235.3 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 7e** — Required press tonnage:

$$
T_{press} = \frac{235,300}{8,900} \approx 26.4 \text{ long tons}
$$

**Answer:** A **27-ton (or higher)** mechanical press is the smallest recommended.

## 3.2 Derivation — Stripping Force

**Problem Context:** Derive the **stripper force** needed to push the scrap ring off the punch after the stroke, given the same washer geometry.

### Step 1 — Identify the Stripping Mechanism

The scrap ring (or finished part) is gripped on the punch by the **elastic recovery** of the metal. To release it, the stripper must apply a force greater than this grip.

### Step 2 — Empirical Industry Equation

$$
F_{strip} = 1.30 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_s
$$

**Logic:** The stripper must overcome the **yield stress $\sigma_s$** of the material acting over the *same* shear area as the cut. The factor **1.3** compensates for friction and spring preload losses.

### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation

For mild steel, $\sigma_s \approx 250 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Using the same washer as before:

$$
F_{strip} = 1.30 \times 188.49 \times 3 \times 250 = 183,778 \text{ N} \approx 184 \text{ kN}
$$

**Logic Check:** The stripping force is **roughly equal** to the cutting force in this geometry, which is why the stripper springs on a compound die are heavily preloaded (often $F_{strip} / 4$ as initial preload, with the rest supplied by press impact).

## 3.3 Derivation — Die-Punch Clearance & Die Opening

### Step 1 — Define Clearance

Clearance **c** (per side) is the radial gap between the punch and the die wall.

### Step 2 — Relate Clearance to Thickness

$$
c = k \cdot t
$$

For low-carbon steel: $k = 0.04$ to $0.06$; for stainless steel: $k = 0.06$ to $0.08$.

### Step 3 — Die Opening Equation

For a piercing operation, the die opening is always **larger** than the punch by **2c**:

$$
D_d = D_p + 2c
$$

For a blanking operation, the punch is **smaller** than the die opening by **2c** — the inverse relation:

$$
D_p = D_d - 2c
$$

**Logic:** The clearance must exist on *both* sides of the cutting line, hence the factor **2**. Forgetting this is a classic KTU mistake.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose a Compound Die

| Production Criterion | Compound Die Fits If … | Progressive Die Fits If … |
|---|---|---|
| **Production volume** | $\geq 50{,}000$ pieces/year | $\geq 100{,}000$ pieces/year |
| **Part complexity** | 1–3 operations, concentric | 4+ operations, distributed geometry |
| **Tolerance on centre distance** | Tight (≤ 0.05 mm) — *compound excels* | Loose (≥ 0.1 mm) acceptable |
| **Tool cost ceiling** | Medium budget | High budget justified |
| **Stock thickness** | $t \leq 4 \text{ mm}$ (typical) | Any thickness |
| **Operator skill** | Semi-skilled (auto-feed possible) | Skilled (manual indexing) |
| **Lead time to first part** | Longer (precision tooling) | Shorter (simpler stations) |

## 3.5 KTU Numericals — Practice Set

> [!NOTE]
> These are worked problems in the canonical KTU 14-mark format.

**Numerical 1.** A $D_o = 60$ mm, $D_i = 25$ mm washer is to be blanked + pierced from a 2 mm aluminium sheet. Take $S = 110$ N/mm². Calculate (i) total cutting force, (ii) press capacity with 30 % overload, (iii) press tonnage.

**Solution:**

$$
L_{total} = \pi (60 + 25) = \pi \cdot 85 = 267.04 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
F_c = 267.04 \times 2 \times 110 = 58,749 \text{ N} \approx 58.75 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
F_{press} = 1.30 \times 58.75 = 76.37 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
T_{press} = \frac{76,370}{8,900} \approx 8.58 \text{ tons} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Specify a 10-ton press.}
$$

**Numerical 2.** Determine the **die opening diameter** if the piercing punch is 18.00 mm and the sheet is 2.5 mm thick mild steel. Take $k = 0.06$.

**Solution:**

$$
c = k \cdot t = 0.06 \times 2.5 = 0.15 \text{ mm per side}
$$

$$
D_d = D_p + 2c = 18.00 + 2 \times 0.15 = 18.30 \text{ mm}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Functional Architecture of a Compound Blanking + Piercing Die

The following block-level Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interactions** between the principal components of a compound die during one press stroke. (This adapts the physical drawing into a clean, Mermaid-renderable functional schematic, as required by the V10 protocol for tooling systems.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Press Ram Descends] --> B1[Stripper Plate Contacts Strip]
    B1 --> C1[Piercer Punch Engages Stock]
    C1 --> D1[Plastic Deformation Begins]
    D1 --> E1[Inner Hole Pierced]
    E1 --> F1[Outer Profile Blanked]
    F1 --> G1[Scrap Ring Ejected Downward]
    G1 --> H1[Knockout Pin Lifts Washer Upward]
    H1 --> I1[Finished Washer Stripped onto Pin]
    I1 --> J1[Ram Retracts Cycle Complete]

    A1 -.drives.-> A2[Punch Holder]
    A2 -.carries.-> C1
    A2 -.carries.-> F1

    K1[Strip Feed Mechanism] --indexes strip by one pitch--> B1
    L1[Back Gauge] --locates strip--> B1
    M1[Die Block Lower] --supports all cuts--> F1
    N1[Stripper Spring Pack] --preloads stripper--> B1
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Comparison Block: Compound vs Progressive Die

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COMPOUND[Compound Die]
        C1a[One Station] --> C2a[One Stroke]
        C2a --> C3a[All Operations Simultaneously]
        C3a --> C4a[Self Locating]
        C4a --> C5a[No Pilot Needed]
    end

    subgraph PROGRESSIVE[Progressive Die]
        P1a[Multiple Stations] --> P2a[One Stroke Per Station]
        P2a --> P3a[Operations Sequential]
        P3a --> P4a[Inter Station Locating]
        P4a --> P5a[Pilot Pins Required]
    end

    C5a --Higher accuracy at low cost--> R1a[Best for Washers and Concentric Parts]
    P5a --Higher volume flexibility--> R2a[Best for Complex Multi Op Parts]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Force and Clearance Vector Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O1[Shear Force F_c] --> O2[Acts on L_total times t]
    O2 --> O3[Direction: Perpendicular to Strip Plane]
    P1[Stripping Force F_s] --> P2[Acts Downward on Punch]
    P2 --> P3[Direction: Parallel to Ram Stroke]
    Q1[Clearance c] --> Q2[Radial Gap Punch to Die]
    Q2 --> Q3[Both Sides of Cut]
    R1[Knockout Force F_k] --> R2[Acts Upward on Part]
    R2 --> R3[Direction: Opposite to Ram Stroke]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Tool Element Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Compound Die Assembly] --> TOP1[Upper Shoe]
    TOP --> TOP2[Lower Shoe]

    TOP1 --> A1[Punch Holder]
    A1 --> A2[Blanking Punch]
    A1 --> A3[Piercing Punch]
    A1 --> A4[Knockout Pin]
    A1 --> A5[Stripper Plate]
    A5 --> A6[Stripper Spring]

    TOP2 --> B1[Die Block]
    B1 --> B2[Die Opening Outer]
    B1 --> B3[Die Opening Inner Piercing]
    B1 --> B4[Strip Guide]
    B1 --> B5[Back Gauge]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagrams replace the conventional hand-drawn die section that KTU typically requires. On the exam day, students should draw a **labelled sectioned elevation** showing the punch entering the die, the stripper plate, the die block, and the knockout — but the *functional understanding* of the blocks above is what fetches full marks in the description.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1.** Define a **compound die**. How is it distinguished from a **progressive die**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A compound die is a single-station press tool that performs two or more operations (such as blanking and piercing) on a workpiece in a **single stroke of the press**, with the stock remaining stationary between operations. The part is self-located by the cutting action itself, and no pilot or indexing is required between sub-operations.
*(1 mark – definition; 1 mark – single station/single stroke highlight; 1 mark – contrast with progressive die's multi-station indexing.)*

A progressive die, in contrast, has **multiple stations**; the strip advances by one pitch per stroke, and a pilot pin locates the strip at each station.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q2.** List **any three advantages** of a compound die over a single-operation die.

**Model Answer (3 marks, 1 mark each):**
1. **Higher accuracy** of centre-distance between the hole and the outer profile (concentric cuts in one setup).
2. **Higher production rate** because two or more operations are completed per stroke.
3. **Lower per-piece cost** in high-volume runs; reduced handling and no inter-station re-locating.
4. *(Bonus, optional)* Better surface finish on the hole because the slug is supported by the punch during fracture.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Chosen Option*

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **construction and working of a compound blanking and piercing die** used for manufacturing a washer. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):** The die consists of:

- **Upper shoe (punch holder)** carrying the *blanking punch* and the *piercing punch* mounted coaxially, plus a *knockout pin* and a spring-loaded *stripper plate*.
- **Lower shoe (die block)** containing the die opening, sized to give the required clearance with the blanking punch; a *back gauge* locates the strip; a *strip guide* aligns the stock.
- **Pillars and bushes** (die set) to guide the upper shoe accurately.
- **Stripper springs** providing downward force on the stripper plate to clear the slug from the punch.

**Working (3.5 marks):**

1. Strip is fed against the back gauge.
2. Ram descends; stripper plate clamps the strip.
3. Piercing punch shears the **inner hole** through the strip.
4. Simultaneously, the blanking punch shears the **outer profile** as it enters the die opening.
5. The finished washer is lifted by the **knockout pin** onto the piercer punch nose.
6. The outer scrap ring is pushed down by the **stripper plate**.
7. Ram retracts; the part is removed manually or blown off by compressed air.

---

**(b)** A washer of **outer diameter 50 mm** and **inner diameter 22 mm** is to be produced from a **3 mm thick mild-steel strip** by compound blanking and piercing. The shear strength of the material is **350 N/mm²**. Calculate the **(i) total cutting force**, **(ii) press tonnage required with 30 % overload**, and **(iii) die opening diameter** for the piercing operation if clearance factor is **0.06**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D_o = 50$ mm, $D_i = 22$ mm, $t = 3$ mm, $S = 350$ N/mm², $k = 0.06$.

**Step 1 — Total cut perimeter:**

$$
L_{total} = \pi (D_o + D_i) = \pi (50 + 22) = \pi \cdot 72 = 226.19 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Total cutting force:**

$$
F_c = L_{total} \cdot t \cdot S = 226.19 \times 3 \times 350 = 237,500 \text{ N} \approx 237.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Press capacity with 30 % overload:**

$$
F_{press} = 1.30 \times 237,500 = 308,750 \text{ N} \approx 308.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Press tonnage (UK long tons, 1 ton = 8.9 kN):**

$$
T_{press} = \frac{308,750}{8,900} = 34.69 \text{ long tons} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Specify a 35-ton press.}
$$

**Step 5 — Die opening for piercing:**

$$
c = k \cdot t = 0.06 \times 3 = 0.18 \text{ mm per side}
$$

$$
D_d = D_i + 2c = 22 + 2 \times 0.18 = 22.36 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answers:** $F_c = 237.5$ kN; $T_{press} = 35$ tons; $D_d = 22.36$ mm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Option*

**(a)** Compare **compound dies** and **progressive dies** under the headings: **(i) number of stations**, **(ii) accuracy of centre-distance**, **(iii) pilot requirement**, **(iv) production rate**, **(v) typical application**, and **(vi) cost per piece**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Tabular Form, 1 mark per point + 1 mark for neatness):**

| # | Parameter | Compound Die | Progressive Die |
|---|---|---|---|
| i | Number of stations | **One** (single station) | **Two or more** (multi-station) |
| ii | Centre-distance accuracy | **Very high** (concentric in one stroke) | **Moderate** (accumulates pitch errors) |
| iii | Pilot requirement | **Not required** (self-locating) | **Pilot pin essential** |
| iv | Production rate | **High** per stroke (multiple ops) | **Lower** per station but flexible |
| v | Typical application | **Washers, shims, gaskets** | **Complex sheet-metal parts** (brackets, terminals) |
| vi | Cost per piece (high volume) | **Lower** (25–40 % saving) | **Higher** for simple parts |

---

**(b)** A square washer of side **40 mm** with a central circular hole of **diameter 15 mm** is to be produced from a **2 mm brass sheet** ($S = 250$ N/mm²) using a compound die. Calculate the **(i) blanking force**, **(ii) piercing force**, **(iii) total force**, and **(iv) press capacity** with 30 % overload. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Outer (square) perimeter:**

$$
L_o = 4 \times 40 = 160 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Inner (circular) perimeter:**

$$
L_i = \pi \times 15 = 47.12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Blanking force:**

$$
F_b = L_o \cdot t \cdot S = 160 \times 2 \times 250 = 80,000 \text{ N} = 80 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Piercing force:**

$$
F_p = L_i \cdot t \cdot S = 47.12 \times 2 \times 250 = 23,560 \text{ N} = 23.56 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Total force:**

$$
F_c = F_b + F_p = 80 + 23.56 = 103.56 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Press capacity with overload:**

$$
F_{press} = 1.30 \times 103.56 = 134.63 \text{ kN} \approx 135 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 7 — Press tonnage:**

$$
T_{press} = \frac{134,630}{8,900} = 15.13 \text{ long tons} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Specify a 16-ton press.}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Compound-Die Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add inner and outer perimeters** — costs 3–4 marks in numericals. Always state $L_{total} = L_o + L_i$ explicitly.
> 2. **Using wrong clearance formula direction** — students write $D_d = D_p - 2c$ for piercing (must be $D_d = D_p + 2c$). Costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the 30 % overload factor** — a complete answer must mention it; failure to do so costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not drawing a sectioned elevation sketch** — for 7-mark construction questions, a labelled sketch is *mandatory* and is worth 2–3 marks by itself.
> 5. **Confusing compound die with combination die** — *combination die* performs cutting + non-cutting (e.g., blanking + drawing); *compound die* performs multiple cutting operations. Examiners *will* deduct for this swap.
> 6. **Wrong unit for press tonnage** — use either "long ton (8.9 kN)" or "metric tonne (9.81 kN)" — be consistent. Mixing them costs marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module-3 Compound-Die Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ A **compound die** performs **multiple operations in a single stroke at one station**.
- ✅ The most common variant is the **compound blanking + piercing die** (washer type).
- ✅ **Total perimeter** $L_{total} = L_{outer} + L_{inner}$.
- ✅ **Blanking force** $F_b = L \cdot t \cdot S$; **Piercing force** $F_p = L_i \cdot t \cdot S$.
- ✅ **Total press capacity** = $1.30 \times F_c$ (30 % safety / wear margin).
- ✅ **Die-punch clearance** $c = k \cdot t$, with $k = 0.04$ to $0.10$ depending on material.
- ✅ **Die opening** for piercing: $D_d = D_p + 2c$; for blanking: $D_p = D_d - 2c$.
- ✅ **Stripping force** $F_s = 1.30 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_s$ — uses *yield* not shear strength.
- ✅ **Push-off / knockout force** is 5 %–10 % of $F_c$.
- ✅ **Compound dies do NOT need pilot pins** — they are *self-locating*; this is their biggest advantage.
- ✅ **Centre-distance accuracy is best** in compound dies (concentric cuts in one setup).
- ✅ **Production runs of $\geq 50{,}000$** pieces justify the higher tool cost of a compound die.
- ✅ **Conventional vs Inverted** — *conventional*: part descends with blanking punch; *inverted*: part is pushed up by knockout onto a finger.
- ✅ **Shear strength approximation**: $S \approx 0.7 \cdot \sigma_t$ for low-carbon steel.
- ✅ **Press tonnage** = $F_{press} / 8.9 \text{ kN/ton}$ (UK long ton) or $F_{press} / 9.81 \text{ kN/tonne}$ (metric).
- ✅ **Common KTU trap**: a *combination die* (cutting + non-cutting) is **different** from a *compound die* (multiple cutting operations).
- ✅ **Memorise the "P.A.P.S.S." cycle**: **P**osition → **A**pproach → **P**enetrate → **S**hear → **S**trip.
- ✅ **Always draw a labelled sectioned elevation** for 7-mark construction questions — sketches carry 2–3 marks alone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: and Combination Dies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** and Combination Dies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Sheet Metal Forming & Press Tool Design**, dies are categorized based on the number and type of operations they perform per stroke of the press. The four principal classifications recognized by KTU 2024 Scheme (PBMET304) are:

1. **Simple (Single-Operation) Die** – Performs one operation per stroke.
2. **Compound Die** – Performs **two or more cutting operations** in a single stroke, at the **same station**, in a **single die set**.
3. **Combination Die** – Performs **two or more operations** (cutting + non-cutting, e.g., blanking + bending) in a single stroke, but at **different stations** of the same die.
4. **Progressive Die** – Performs operations at **different stations** through **multiple strokes** as the strip advances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The focus topic is on **Compound and Combination Dies** — both are *single-stroke, multi-operation* tools but differ in the *number of stations* and the *nature of operations* performed.

**Compound Die Definition (KTU Standard):**
A *compound die* is a press tool in which **two or more cutting operations** (such as blanking and piercing) are completed in a single stroke of the press, with the workpiece being sheared from **both sides simultaneously** by opposing punch and die members acting at the **same station**.

**Combination Die Definition (KTU Standard):**
A *combination die* is a press tool that performs **two or more distinct operations** in a single stroke — typically a **cutting operation combined with a non-cutting operation** (e.g., blanking + bending, blanking + embossing, blanking + drawing) — executed at **different stations** within the same die set.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Hole-Puncher + Cutter**
> Imagine a **paper hole-puncher that also trims the edge** of the paper in one squeeze. That is a **compound die** — it cuts a hole *and* shapes the outline in the **same squeeze at the same spot**, like cutting a coin and stamping a logo on it simultaneously from above and below.
>
> Now imagine a **stamping tool that cuts a shape AND bends a flange on the freshly-cut part in one press stroke**, but the cutting and bending happen at *different physical locations* on the die. That is a **combination die** — multiple distinct operations, single stroke, different stations.

### 1.3 Key Differentiating Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

| Parameter | Compound Die | Combination Die |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| Number of strokes | **1** | **1** |
| Number of stations | **1** | **2 or more** |
| Type of operations | **Cutting only** (≥ 2) | **Cutting + Non-cutting** |
| Blanking-piercing | Yes (both) | Only one cutting op usually |
| Slug / Scrap handling | Internal slug pierced out | Separate scrap per station |
| Die complexity | **High** (precision alignment) | **Moderate to High** |
| Cost | **High** | **Moderate** |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU 2024)

- **Clearance (c) on each side:** $c = 0.05 \times t$ to $0.10 \times t$ (where $t$ = sheet thickness, in **mm**)
- **Punch-to-die clearance (total):** $C = 2c$
- **Standard sheet thickness range for these dies:** $\mathbf{0.5\ mm \leq t \leq 6\ mm}$
- **Press tonnage (approximate):** $P = L \times t \times \text{SHEAR STRENGTH}$, where $L$ is total cut length in **mm**
- **Standard die block height:** $\mathbf{45\ mm}$ to $\mathbf{70\ mm}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **compound die** is a special form of a **combination die** (in a broad sense), but in strict KTU 2024 classification, they are treated as **distinct categories** because of the *cutting-only* vs *cutting + forming* nature of operations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Compound Die — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

A compound die performs **blanking and piercing in one stroke**. The structural logic:

1. **Stripper Plate** holds the sheet metal flat against the die.
2. **Outer Punch (retained in upper shoe)** descends and acts as the **blanking die** — it has the *outer profile* of the part machined into its bottom face.
3. **Inner Punch (retained in lower shoe)** rises upward through the sheet and acts as the **piercing punch** — it has the *inner hole profile* of the part.
4. As the press closes:
   - The outer punch shears the **blank** from the surrounding strip.
   - Simultaneously, the inner punch shears the **internal slug** from the blank.
5. The finished part (blank with hole) is pushed back through the die cavity by the **stripper plate** on press opening.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 'Compound'?** Because **two opposing shear actions** (one from above, one from below) happen **at the same instant** on the same piece of material — the die "compounds" the cutting actions.

**Critical Design Logic:**
- The inner punch (piercing) must be **shorter** than the outer punch (blanking) by exactly the **sheet thickness $t$** so that piercing *finishes just as* blanking completes — this prevents deformation of the part.
- The die is said to have a **"cut-and-cut"** action.

### 2.2 Combination Die — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

A combination die performs a **cutting + forming** operation in a single stroke:

1. The **strip is fed** to the first station (e.g., blanking station).
2. The **upper shoe** carries the **blanking punch** and the **bending/forming punch**.
3. The **lower shoe** carries the **blanking die** (with the cut profile) and the **bending die cavity**.
4. As the press closes:
   - The blanking punch cuts the part from the strip.
   - The same part is simultaneously (or sequentially in the stroke) **bent** by a cam-actuated bending punch over a bending die.
5. The finished **cut-and-bent part** is ejected.

**Common Operation Combinations in Combination Dies:**
- Blanking + Bending
- Blanking + Embossing
- Blanking + Coining
- Blanking + Shallow Drawing
- Piercing + Bending

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Point:** Combination dies are *not* progressive dies. The difference is — in a **progressive die**, the strip moves through **multiple strokes**; in a **combination die**, the strip (or part) remains in **one stroke** but encounters **multiple stations**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C = 2c = (0.10\ \text{to}\ 0.20)\,t$ | Total die clearance (both sides) | **mm** |
| 2 | $P_{\text{cut}} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ | Cutting force (per stroke) | **N** |
| 3 | $L = \pi D + 2(L_1 + W_1) + \dots$ | Total cut perimeter (sum of all cut lines) | **mm** |
| 4 | $\tau = 0.7 \cdot \sigma_u$ | Approx. shear strength from ultimate tensile | **MPa** |
| 5 | $\tau_{\text{avg}} = 350$ to $450$ | Avg. shear strength of mild steel | **MPa** |
| 6 | $P_{\text{bend}} = \dfrac{k \cdot L \cdot t^2 \cdot \text{UTS}}{W}$ | Bending force in combination die | **N** |
| 7 | $P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{cut}} + P_{\text{bend}} + P_{\text{strip}}$ | Total press tonnage required | **N** |
| 8 | $\text{SHF} = 1.3$ to $1.5$ | Safety / Stroking Horsepower Factor | — |
| 9 | $\text{Spring force} = 0.15 \cdot P_{\text{cut}}$ | Stripper spring pre-load | **N** |
| 10 | $\text{Strip width} = W_{\text{part}} + 2 \cdot L_1 + 2c$ | Strip layout width (allowance $L_1 = 1.5t$ min) | **mm** |

Where:
- $D$ = blank diameter (mm)
- $W$ = die opening width (mm)
- $L_1, W_1$ = side allowances (mm)
- $k$ = die opening factor (1.2 to 1.8 for V-dies)
- $\sigma_u$ = ultimate tensile strength (MPa)
- SHF = Stroke Horsepower Factor

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Compound and combination dies are **industry workhorses** in:
> - **Automotive body panels** (cut + bend in one stroke for brackets, clips)
> - **Electrical switchgear** (blanked-and-pierced busbars with formed terminals)
> - **Appliance manufacturing** (washer/dryer panels with cutouts and formed edges)
> - **Watch and instrument components** (precision blanking + coining)
> - **Aerospace brackets** (high-strength cut + bent parts with tight tolerances)
>
> The **economic justification** for using a compound/combination die over a progressive die:
> - **Lower floor space** requirement
> - **Fewer handling steps** → higher repeatability
> - **Shorter cycle time** for short production runs
> - **Better concentricity** of holes with respect to outer profile (in compound dies — *the hole and outline are referenced to each other in the same die*, so positional tolerance is **± 0.02 mm** vs **± 0.05 mm** for progressive dies).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Numerical Example — Compound Die Force Calculation (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A compound die is to blank and pierce a washer of **outer diameter $D_o = 50\ \text{mm}$** and **inner hole diameter $D_i = 20\ \text{mm}$** from a **mild steel sheet of thickness $t = 2\ \text{mm}$**. Given:
- Shear strength of mild steel, $\tau = 350\ \text{MPa}$
- Die clearance per side, $c = 0.075\,t$

**Find:**
(a) The total die clearance.
(b) The total cutting force required.
(c) The press tonnage to be selected (use SHF = 1.4).

---

**Step 1: Total Die Clearance (both sides combined)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Single-side clearance},\ c &= 0.075 \times t \\[4pt]
c &= 0.075 \times 2\ \text{mm} \\[4pt]
c &= 0.15\ \text{mm per side}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total clearance},\ C &= 2c \\[4pt]
C &= 2 \times 0.15\ \text{mm} \\[4pt]
C &= 0.30\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The "0.075" is the empirical KTU-recommended factor for **mild steel** (range 0.05–0.10). Multiplying by the sheet thickness yields the per-side gap. Doubling accounts for both the punch and die sides of the cut.

---

**Step 2: Total Cut Perimeter (L)**

In a compound die, **both the outer circle and the inner circle are cut**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{outer}} &= \pi \cdot D_o = \pi \times 50\ \text{mm} \\[4pt]
L_{\text{outer}} &= 157.08\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{inner}} &= \pi \cdot D_i = \pi \times 20\ \text{mm} \\[4pt]
L_{\text{inner}} &= 62.83\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &= L_{\text{outer}} + L_{\text{inner}} \\[4pt]
L_{\text{total}} &= 157.08 + 62.83 \\[4pt]
L_{\text{total}} &= 219.91\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* Because a compound die blanks **and** pierces in the same stroke, the **sum of both perimeters** is sheared. This is the key analytical difference from a simple die.

---

**Step 3: Total Cutting Force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{cut}} &= L_{\text{total}} \cdot t \cdot \tau \\[4pt]
P_{\text{cut}} &= 219.91\ \text{mm} \times 2\ \text{mm} \times 350\ \text{MPa} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{cut}} &= 219.91 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} \times 350 \times 10^{6}\ \text{N/m}^2 \\[4pt]
P_{\text{cut}} &= 153{,}937\ \text{N} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{cut}} &\approx 153.94\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* Units are unified to SI base: **mm → m** and **MPa → N/m²** to obtain force in **Newtons**. The product $(L \cdot t)$ is the **sheared cross-sectional area**, which multiplied by shear strength gives the **shear resistance force**.

---

**Step 4: Press Tonnage with Safety Factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{press}} &= \text{SHF} \times P_{\text{cut}} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{press}} &= 1.4 \times 153.94\ \text{kN} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{press}} &= 215.52\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The SHF (Stroking Horsepower Factor) accounts for **inertial loads, misalignment, edge wear, and material variability** during the press stroke. Always round up to the next available press rating — here, select a **250 kN press**.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Example — Combination Die (Blanking + Bending)

**Problem Statement:**
A combination die must produce an L-shaped bracket from a **2 mm thick mild-steel strip** in one stroke. The blank has:
- A rectangular cut outline of **80 mm × 40 mm**
- A 90° bend along the **80 mm long edge**, with **bending allowance $L_b = 80\ \text{mm}$**
- Die opening factor $k = 1.4$, UTS of mild steel = **410 MPa**

**Find:**
(a) Blanking force.
(b) Bending force.
(c) Total press tonnage (use SHF = 1.3).

---

**Step 1: Blanking Force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{blank}} &= 2(L + W) = 2(80 + 40) \\[4pt]
L_{\text{blank}} &= 240\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{cut}} &= L_{\text{blank}} \cdot t \cdot \tau \\[4pt]
\tau &= 0.7 \cdot \sigma_u = 0.7 \times 410 = 287\ \text{MPa} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{cut}} &= 240 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 287 \times 10^{6} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{cut}} &= 137{,}760\ \text{N} \approx 137.76\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 2: Bending Force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{bend}} &= \dfrac{k \cdot L_b \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_u}{W} \\[4pt]
W &= 8 \times t = 8 \times 2 = 16\ \text{mm (typical V-die opening)} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{bend}} &= \dfrac{1.4 \times 80 \times 10^{-3} \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 410 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{bend}} &= \dfrac{1.4 \times 80 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 410 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{bend}} &= \dfrac{1.4 \times 80 \times 4 \times 410}{16} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{bend}} &= \dfrac{183{,}680}{16} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{bend}} &= 11{,}480\ \text{N} \approx 11.48\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3: Total Press Tonnage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= \text{SHF} \times (P_{\text{cut}} + P_{\text{bend}}) \\[4pt]
P_{\text{total}} &= 1.3 \times (137.76 + 11.48) \\[4pt]
P_{\text{total}} &= 1.3 \times 149.24 \\[4pt]
P_{\text{total}} &= 194.01\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The bending force is **~ 8%** of the blanking force in this example. As a **rule of thumb (KTU)**, if the bending force exceeds 25% of the cutting force, a **separate bending operation** should be considered for press safety.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board Standard)

| Criterion | Simple Die | Compound Die | Combination Die | Progressive Die |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Operations per stroke | 1 | ≥ 2 (cutting) | ≥ 2 (mixed) | 1 (but many across strokes) |
| Stations | 1 | 1 | ≥ 2 | ≥ 3 |
| Strokes per part | 1 | 1 | 1 | Multiple |
| Strip movement | Yes (each stroke) | No | No | Yes (each stroke) |
| Positional accuracy | Low | **Very High** | Moderate | Moderate |
| Tool cost | **Lowest** | **Highest** | Moderate | High |
| Production rate | Low | High | High | Highest |
| Best for | Prototypes, large parts | Precision washers, gears | Brackets, clips | High-volume small parts |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Compound Die — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Press Ram Descends] --> node2[Outer Punch Contacts Strip]
    node2 --> node3[Inner Punch Contacts Strip Simultaneously]
    node3 --> node4[Blanking Shear Action - Outer Profile]
    node3 --> node5[Piercing Shear Action - Inner Profile]
    node4 --> node6[Blank Separated from Strip]
    node5 --> node7[Inner Slug Pierced Out]
    node6 --> node8[Finished Part - Blank with Hole]
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9[Stripper Plate Ejects Part]
    node9 --> node10[Press Ram Retracts - Cycle Complete]
```

**Legend / Functional Reading:**
- **Outer Punch** = blanking member (retains *outer* profile)
- **Inner Punch** = piercing member (retains *inner* hole profile)
- Both punches shear in the **same stroke, same station** — defining feature of a **compound die**.

---

### 4.2 Combination Die — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Strip Fed to Station 1 - Blanking] --> nodeB[Press Stroke Begins]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Station 1 - Blanking Punch Shears Outline]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Station 2 - Bending Punch Engages]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Blank Dropped to Station 2]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Part Bent to 90 Degree at Station 2]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Finished L-Shaped Part Ejected]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Cycle Complete]
```

**Legend / Functional Reading:**
- **Two stations**, **one stroke**.
- **Station 1:** Cutting operation (blanking)
- **Station 2:** Non-cutting operation (bending/forming)
- This is the **defining feature of a combination die**.

---

### 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — Compound vs Combination vs Progressive

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeX[Sheet Metal Strip Input] --> nodeY{Die Type}
    nodeY --> nodeP[Simple Die: 1 Op, 1 Station, 1 Stroke]
    nodeY --> nodeQ[Compound Die: 2+ Cut Ops, 1 Station, 1 Stroke]
    nodeY --> nodeR[Combination Die: Cut + Form, 2+ Stations, 1 Stroke]
    nodeY --> nodeS[Progressive Die: Multi-Ops, Multi-Stations, Multi-Strokes]
    nodeP --> nodeOut[Finished Part]
    nodeQ --> nodeOut
    nodeR --> nodeOut
    nodeS --> nodeOut
```

---

### 4.4 Die-Set Assembly Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Upper Shoe - Bolster Plate] --> node2[Upper Plate - Holds Outer Punch]
    node3[Lower Shoe - Bolster Plate] --> node4[Lower Plate - Holds Die Block]
    node4 --> node5[Die Block - Has Outer Profile Cavity]
    node4 --> node6[Inner Punch - Piercing Member]
    node2 --> node7[Outer Punch - Blanking Member]
    node2 --> node8[Stripper Plate - Spring Loaded]
    node8 --> node9[Guide Pins and Bushings - 4 Corner Alignment]
    node4 --> node9
```

**Reading the Schematic:**
- **Upper shoe** carries the blanking punch and stripper.
- **Lower shoe** carries the die block and the inner piercing punch (inverted).
- **Guide pins** ensure the upper and lower halves align within **± 0.005 mm** tolerance — critical for compound dies where precision is paramount.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define a compound die. How is it functionally different from a combination die?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Compound Die:** A press tool that performs **two or more cutting operations** (e.g., blanking and piercing) in a **single stroke of the press**, at the **same station** of the die, by shearing the workpiece from **both sides simultaneously** through opposing punch members.
>
> **Functional Difference from Combination Die (3 marks logic):**
> - Compound die: **only cutting operations** (cut + cut), **one station**, **one stroke**.
> - Combination die: **cutting + non-cutting operations** (e.g., cut + bend), **multiple stations**, **one stroke**.
>
> **Key marker:** Compound = *"cut and cut"*, Combination = *"cut and form"*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List any four operations that can be combined in a combination die. State one industrial example.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Four operations combined in a combination die:
> 1. **Blanking + Bending** — produces flat blanked parts with formed flanges.
> 2. **Blanking + Embossing** — produces parts with raised features for identification or grip.
> 3. **Blanking + Coining** — produces parts with sharp impressions (coins, medals).
> 4. **Blanking + Shallow Drawing** — produces cup-like parts from flat stock.
>
> **Industrial Example:** Manufacturing of an **L-shaped electrical switch-contact bracket** in a single stroke — the blank is cut from the strip and simultaneously bent 90° to form the contact terminal.

---

### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compound Die Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat functional sketch, describe the **construction and working of a compound die** used for manufacturing a washer. Label all major components. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A compound die is to blank and pierce a washer of **outer diameter 60 mm** and **hole diameter 25 mm** from a **mild steel strip of thickness 2.5 mm**. Take shear strength = **350 MPa** and SHF = **1.4**. Calculate:
- (i) Total cutting perimeter
- (ii) Total die clearance (assuming 7.5% per side)
- (iii) Press tonnage required. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of Compound Die (7 marks):**

**Components (3 marks):**
1. **Upper shoe (bolster)** – Mounted to press ram.
2. **Outer (blanking) punch** – Mounted on upper plate; has the **outer profile of the washer** cut into its bottom face.
3. **Stripper plate** – Spring-loaded; holds the strip during cutting and ejects the finished washer on upward stroke.
4. **Guide pins and bushings** – Ensure alignment between upper and lower halves.
5. **Die block (lower)** – Mounted on lower shoe; has the **cavity matching the outer punch profile** (acts as the blanking die).
6. **Inner (piercing) punch** – Mounted on lower plate, projects **upward** through the die block; has the **hole profile** of the washer. Its length is **shorter than the outer punch by exactly $t$**.

**Working (4 marks):**
- The mild-steel strip is fed over the die block.
- The press ram descends; the outer punch contacts the strip from above and begins to shear the **outer blank profile**.
- **Simultaneously**, the inner punch (rising from below) shears the **central hole** of the washer.
- Both cutting actions are completed in **one stroke**.
- The finished washer falls through the die cavity, separated from the strip.
- The stripper plate ejects the part, and the slug (central scrap) drops through a chute.

> **Key Feature to State (for full 7 marks):** The opposing shear actions of outer and inner punches act on the *same piece of material at the same instant*, giving **exceptional concentricity** of the hole with respect to the OD (positional tolerance ≈ ± 0.02 mm).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total Cutting Perimeter:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{outer}} &= \pi \times 60 = 188.50\ \text{mm} \\[4pt]
L_{\text{inner}} &= \pi \times 25 = 78.54\ \text{mm} \\[4pt]
L_{\text{total}} &= 188.50 + 78.54 = 267.04\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total Die Clearance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= 0.075 \times t = 0.075 \times 2.5 = 0.1875\ \text{mm (per side)} \\[4pt]
C &= 2c = 0.375\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Press Tonnage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{cut}} &= L_{\text{total}} \cdot t \cdot \tau \\[4pt]
P_{\text{cut}} &= 267.04 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 350 \times 10^{6} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{cut}} &= 233{,}660\ \text{N} \approx 233.66\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{press}} &= \text{SHF} \times P_{\text{cut}} = 1.4 \times 233.66 \\[4pt]
P_{\text{press}} &= 327.12\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Select the next standard press rating: 350 kN (or 50 ton) press.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Combination Die Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a combination die** with a suitable sketch. Discuss the difference between a combination die and a progressive die. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A combination die is designed to blank and bend a **rectangular part 100 mm × 50 mm** from a **3 mm thick cold-rolled steel sheet**. The bend is made along the **100 mm long edge at 90°**. Given:
- UTS = 450 MPa, shear strength factor = 0.7
- Die opening factor $k = 1.5$
- V-die opening $W = 8t$
- SHF = 1.3

Calculate the **blanking force, bending force, and total press tonnage**. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle of Combination Die (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A combination die performs **two or more operations** (cutting + non-cutting) in a **single stroke**, executed at **two or more stations** within the same die set.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- **Upper shoe:** Holds the blanking punch (Station 1) and the bending punch (Station 2 — often cam-actuated).
- **Lower shoe:** Holds the blanking die (with profile cavity) and a bending die cavity.
- A **transfer mechanism** (slide, lifter, or cam) carries the blank from Station 1 to Station 2 within the same stroke.

**Working (2 marks):**
- The strip is fed to Station 1.
- Press stroke initiates — blanking punch shears the rectangular outline.
- The blank drops/transfers to Station 2.
- The bending punch descends and bends the blank 90° over the bending die.
- Finished bent part is ejected; press returns.

**Difference from Progressive Die (1 mark):**

| Feature | Combination Die | Progressive Die |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| Strokes per part | **1** | **Multiple** |
| Strip advancement | **No** (part is transferred internally) | **Yes** (pitch advance each stroke) |
| Stations | 2 – 3 | 3 – 10+ |
| Production rate | Moderate | **High** |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Blanking Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{blank}} &= 2(100 + 50) = 300\ \text{mm} \\[4pt]
\tau &= 0.7 \times 450 = 315\ \text{MPa} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{cut}} &= 300 \times 10^{-3} \times 3 \times 10^{-3} \times 315 \times 10^{6} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{cut}} &= 283{,}500\ \text{N} = 283.5\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bending Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 8t = 8 \times 3 = 24\ \text{mm} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{bend}} &= \dfrac{k \cdot L_b \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_u}{W} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{bend}} &= \dfrac{1.5 \times 100 \times 10^{-3} \times 9 \times 10^{-6} \times 450 \times 10^{6}}{24 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{bend}} &= \dfrac{1.5 \times 100 \times 9 \times 450}{24} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{bend}} &= \dfrac{607{,}500}{24} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{bend}} &= 25{,}312.5\ \text{N} \approx 25.31\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Press Tonnage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= 1.3 \times (283.5 + 25.31) \\[4pt]
P_{\text{total}} &= 1.3 \times 308.81 \\[4pt]
P_{\text{total}} &= 401.45\ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Select the next standard press: 450 kN (≈ 60 ton).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Combination" with "Compound":** Students often use these terms interchangeably. In KTU 2024 strict marking: **compound = cut + cut, one station**; **combination = cut + form, multiple stations**. Mixing these is a **2-mark deduction** in Part A and a **3–4 mark deduction** in Part B.
> 2. **Forgetting to add both perimeters in compound dies:** When computing cutting force, *both* the outer and inner cut lines must be summed. Skipping the inner perimeter = **3-mark loss**.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert **mm → m** and **MPa → N/m²** when computing force. Always show the unit conversion step in Part B — it earns **1 grace mark**.
> 4. **Omitting SHF (Safety Factor):** The press tonnage must always include SHF. A raw $P_{\text{cut}}$ answer without SHF is **incomplete (–1 to –2 marks)**.
> 5. **Mis-stating the difference between combination and progressive dies:** Both involve multiple stations — but **progressive = multi-stroke, combination = single-stroke**. This distinction is a **favorite KTU question**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Bullet Bank:**
>
> - **Compound Die:** Multiple *cutting* operations, **1 station, 1 stroke**. E.g., blanking + piercing a washer.
> - **Combination Die:** *Cutting + non-cutting* operations, **≥ 2 stations, 1 stroke**. E.g., blanking + bending a bracket.
> - **Progressive Die:** Multiple operations, **≥ 3 stations, multiple strokes**. Strip advances by a fixed *pitch* per stroke.
> - **Simple Die:** **1 operation, 1 station, 1 stroke**. Cheapest, lowest precision.
> - **Total die clearance:** $C = 2c = (0.10\ \text{to}\ 0.20)\,t$ for mild steel.
> - **Cutting force:** $P_{\text{cut}} = L \cdot t \cdot \tau$ where $\tau \approx 0.7\,\sigma_u$.
> - **For a compound die, $L$ = sum of outer + inner perimeters.**
> - **Bending force:** $P_{\text{bend}} = \dfrac{k \cdot L_b \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_u}{W}$; V-die opening $W = 8t$ is standard.
> - **Press tonnage:** Always apply SHF (1.3 – 1.5).
> - **Compound die advantage:** Best **concentricity** (hole–OD concentricity ≈ ± 0.02 mm).
> - **Combination die advantage:** Eliminates a separate forming operation → **reduced handling, faster cycle**.
> - **Industrial sweet spot:** Compound for *precision small parts* (washers, gears); Combination for *formed brackets and clips*; Progressive for *high-volume production*; Simple for *prototypes*.
> - **KTU 2024 Module 3 weightage:** ~ **12–15%** of total marks in the university exam — expect at least one Part A (3 marks) and possibly one Part B (7–14 marks) question.
> - **Mnemonic for distinction:** **"C-O-M-P-O-U-N-D = Cut Only, Multiple Operations, One-station, UNiformly Done."** **"C-O-M-B-I-N-A-T-I-O-N = Cut Or Mix, Bend In Another Neighbouring station, On the One stroke Now."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forging - Forging Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forging - Forging Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Forging Load?

**Forging Load** is defined as the **total compressive force** (in Newtons or kiloNewtons) that must be applied by the forging equipment (hammer, press, or upsetter) to cause plastic deformation of the workpiece and shape it within the die cavity.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, forging load is one of the most critical design parameters because it directly dictates:
- The **tonnage rating** of the forging machine to be selected.
- The **die wear** and fatigue life of the tooling.
- The **energy/power consumption** of the operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** *Forging Load (F)* is the instantaneous maximum force exerted by the ram on the workpiece during the forging stroke. It is the product of the **projected area of contact** and the **average / specific flow stress** of the material at the forging temperature, corrected by a **forging efficiency factor**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🍞

Imagine you are pressing a thick cheese slice flat between your palms.

- The **cheese** = the hot metal workpiece (billet).
- Your **palm pressure** = the **forging load** distributed over the contact area.
- The **resistance of the cheese to flatten** = the **flow stress** of the metal (it becomes harder to deform as it gets thinner and colder).
- The **force you feel on your palms** is highest at the start (when the slice is thick) and changes as the slice thins out — this is exactly how forging load varies during a stroke.

> [!NOTE]
> Forging load is **not constant** during a stroke — it is a function of the instantaneous projected area, the flow stress of the material (which depends on strain, strain rate, and temperature), and the friction at the die-workpiece interface.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

| Term | Standard Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|------|----------------|------|---------|
| Forging Load | $F$ | **N** or **kN** | Total compressive force on the workpiece |
| Specific Pressure / Flow Stress | $\bar{\sigma}_f$ or $Y_f$ | **MPa (N/mm²)** | Instantaneous resistance of the material to deformation |
| Projected Contact Area | $A$ | **mm²** | Area of the billet normal to the ram motion |
| Forging Efficiency | $\eta$ | dimensionless (0.5 – 0.9) | Accounts for friction and deformation losses |
| Strain | $\epsilon$ | dimensionless | Degree of deformation |
| Strain Rate | $\dot{\epsilon}$ | **s⁻¹** | Rate at which deformation occurs |
| Temperature | $T$ | **°C** | Affects flow stress significantly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant **$\pi$ (pi)** and the natural log base **$e$** appear repeatedly in forging load derivations involving cylindrical billets.

---

## 1.4 Why is Forging Load Important in KTU Examinations?

- It is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in the KTU ESE (End Semester Exam).
- The examiner tests the student's ability to:
  1. Identify whether the forging is **open-die** (upsetting) or **closed-die (impression-die)**.
  2. Apply the correct formula for **ideal work** and **actual load**.
  3. Compute the **energy requirement** and select the proper hammer/press.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation of Forging Load

Forging load is derived from three fundamental pillars of plasticity theory:

### Pillar 1: The Plastic Deformation Condition
A material begins to deform plastically when the applied stress exceeds its **flow stress** ($Y_f$). In compression forging:

$$\sigma_{applied} \geq Y_f$$

### Pillar 2: The Average Flow Stress
