# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "friction hill" as applicable to metal forming processes. Why does it occur?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **friction hill** is the phenomenon in which the pressure required to deform a metal workpiece increases substantially above the material's flow stress due to frictional resistance at the die-workpiece interface. It occurs primarily in bulk forming processes such as **forging, extrusion, and rolling**, where the deforming material is in **extensive contact** with the die surface.
>
> The pressure required is mathematically given by $p = Y_f \cdot (1 + \text{friction term})$, where the friction term is a function of the friction factor (m) and the geometric ratio of the workpiece (L/h or r/h).
>
> **(1 mark)** Correct definition; **(1 mark)** mention of die-workpiece contact; **(1 mark)** identifying it as a multiplicative increase over flow stress.

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Chvorinov's rule for solidification of a casting. What is the significance of the V/A ratio?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Chvorinov's rule states that the **solidification time ($t_s$)** of a casting is proportional to the **square of the V/A ratio** (volume-to-surface-area ratio), and is given by:
> $$t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$$
> where **B** is the **mold constant** that depends on the metal, mold material, and pouring conditions.
>
> The **V/A ratio (modulus)** is significant because:
> - It determines the relative solidification rates of different sections of a casting.
> - Sections with **higher V/A solidify last** and act as potential **risers** to feed liquid metal to feeding sections.
> - It is the **key design parameter** for riser placement, chill location, and gating design.
>
> **(1 mark)** Correct formula; **(1 mark)** explanation of B; **(1 mark)** significance in casting design.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**(a)** A cylindrical billet of diameter 80 mm and height 100 mm is upset forged in a press to a final height of 60 mm at room temperature. The strength coefficient is $K = 410 \text{ MPa}$ and strain-hardening exponent is $n = 0.25$. The friction factor is $m = 0.3$. Determine:
- (i) The true strain in the operation.
- (ii) The average flow stress.
- (iii) The maximum forging pressure and maximum forging force.

**(b)** A steel strip with width 250 mm is rolled from 20 mm to 15 mm thickness. The roll diameter is 400 mm. The material has $K = 500 \text{ MPa}$ and $n = 0.20$. The coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.1$ and the rolls rotate at 30 rpm. Determine:
- (i) The roll separating force.
- (ii) The torque and power required.

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Forging Solution

**Step 1: True Strain**
$$\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{100}{60}\right) = \ln(1.6667) \approx 0.5108$$

**Step 2: Final Diameter (Volume Constancy)**
$$d_0^2 h_0 = d_f^2 h_f \Rightarrow 80^2 \times 100 = d_f^2 \times 60$$
$$d_f^2 = \frac{640000}{60} = 10666.67 \Rightarrow d_f \approx 103.28 \text{ mm}$$
$$r_f = 51.64 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Average Flow Stress**
$$Y_f = \frac{K \epsilon^n}{1+n} = \frac{410 \times (0.5108)^{0.25}}{1.25}$$

$(0.5108)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \times \ln(0.5108)} = e^{0.25 \times (-0.6717)} = e^{-0.1679} \approx 0.8455$

$$Y_f = \frac{410 \times 0.8455}{1.25} = \frac{346.66}{1.25} \approx 277.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Forging Pressure**
$$p_{\max} = Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2mr_f}{3h_f}\right) = 277.3 \times \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 0.3 \times 51.64}{3 \times 60}\right)$$
$$= 277.3 \times \left(1 + \frac{30.984}{180}\right) = 277.3 \times 1.1721 \approx 325.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Forging Force**
$$A_f = \pi r_f^2 = \pi \times (51.64)^2 \approx 8377.7 \text{ mm}^2 = 8.378 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$F_{\max} = p_{\max} \cdot A_f = 325.0 \times 10^6 \times 8.378 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2{,}722{,}850 \text{ N} \approx 2.72 \text{ MN}$$

#### Part (b) — Rolling Solution

**Step 1: Geometric Parameters**
$$\Delta h = 20 - 15 = 5 \text{ mm}, \quad L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{200 \times 5} = \sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: True Strain and Flow Stress**
$$\epsilon = \ln(20/15) = \ln(1.333) \approx 0.2877$$
$$Y_f = \frac{500 \times (0.2877)^{0.20}}{1.20}$$

$(0.2877)^{0.20} = e^{0.20 \times \ln(0.2877)} = e^{0.20 \times (-1.2457)} = e^{-0.2491} \approx 0.7795$

$$Y_f = \frac{500 \times 0.7795}{1.20} = \frac{389.75}{1.20} \approx 324.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Check Sticking vs. Sliding Friction**
$$\mu p_{\text{avg}} \text{ vs } \frac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}} \text{ (Tresca)}$$

A simpler check: $\mu \cdot L / h_f = 0.1 \times 31.62 / 15 = 0.211 < 1$, so sliding friction is applicable.

For sliding friction, the rolling force formula is:
$$F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right]$$

Wait — correction: The general formula depends on whether sticking or sliding friction prevails. The conventional formula for rolling is:

$$F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right) \quad \text{(sliding)}$$

OR

$$F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{m L}{4 h_f}\right) \quad \text{(sticking)}$$

For sliding friction:
$$1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f} = 1 + \frac{0.1 \times 31.62}{2 \times 15} = 1 + \frac{3.162}{30} = 1 + 0.1054 = 1.1054$$

**Step 4: Roll Separating Force**
$$F = 31.62 \times 250 \times 324.8 \times 1.1054$$
$$F = 7905 \times 324.8 \times 1.1054 \approx 2{,}567{,}400 \times 1.1054 \approx 2{,}838{,}100 \text{ N}$$
$$F \approx 2838 \text{ kN} \approx 2.84 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 5: Torque per Roll**
$$T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 2{,}838{,}100 \times 0.03162 \approx 44{,}870 \text{ N·m} \approx 44.9 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 6: Power (Both Rolls)**
$$N = 30 \text{ rpm} = 0.5 \text{ rev/s}$$
$$P = 2 \times 2\pi N \cdot T = 4\pi \times 0.5 \times 44{,}870 = 2\pi \times 44{,}870 \approx 281{,}900 \text{ W} \approx 281.9 \text{ kW}$$

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**(a)** A direct extrusion operation is performed on a billet of initial diameter 75 mm and length 250 mm to produce a rod of diameter 30 mm. The flow stress of the material follows $\bar{\sigma} = 350 \epsilon^{0.15}$ MPa. The friction factor is $m = 0.3$. Calculate:
- (i) The extrusion ratio and the true strain.
- (ii) The maximum extrusion pressure (at the start of the stroke).
- (iii) The maximum ram force required.

**(b)** Two castings are to be produced — one is a sphere of diameter 200 mm, and the other is a cube of side 200 mm. Using Chvorinov's rule with mold constant $B = 3.5 \text{ min/cm}^2$, determine the solidification time for each. State which design is better from a casting perspective and justify.

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Extrusion Solution

**Step 1: Extrusion Ratio and True Strain**
$$R = \left(\frac{D_0}{D_f}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{75}{30}\right)^2 = 2.5^2 = 6.25$$
$$\epsilon = \ln(R) = \ln(6.25) \approx 1.8326$$

**Step 2: Average Flow Stress**
$$Y_f = \frac{K \epsilon^n}{1+n} = \frac{350 \times (1.8326)^{0.15}}{1.15}$$

$(1.8326)^{0.15} = e^{0.15 \times \ln(1.8326)} = e^{0.15 \times 0.6058} = e^{0.0909} \approx 1.0951$

$$Y_f = \frac{350 \times 1.0951}{1.15} = \frac{383.29}{1.15} \approx 333.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Extrusion Pressure (Start of Stroke, L = L_0)**
$$p_{\max} = Y_f \cdot \left[\ln(R) + \frac{2m L_0}{D_0}\right] = 333.3 \times \left[1.8326 + \frac{2 \times 0.3 \times 250}{75}\right]$$
$$= 333.3 \times \left[1.8326 + 2.0\right] = 333.3 \times 3.8326 \approx 1277.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Maximum Ram Force**
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 75^2 = 4417.86 \text{ mm}^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$F_{\max} = p_{\max} \cdot A_0 = 1277.4 \times 10^6 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \approx 5{,}643{,}500 \text{ N} \approx 5.64 \text{ MN}$$

#### Part (b) — Chvorinov's Rule Comparison

**Convert dimensions to cm:** All dimensions = 20 cm.

**For the Sphere:**
- Volume: $V_s = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi (10)^3 = 4188.79 \text{ cm}^3$
- Surface Area: $A_s = 4\pi r^2 = 4\pi (10)^2 = 1256.64 \text{ cm}^2$
- Modulus: $M_s = V_s/A_s = 4188.79/1256.64 = 3.333 \text{ cm}$
- Solidification time: $t_s = 3.5 \times (3.333)^2 = 3.5 \times 11.111 = 38.89 \text{ min}$

**For the Cube:**
- Volume: $V_c = 20^3 = 8000 \text{ cm}^3$
- Surface Area: $A_c = 6 \times 20^2 = 2400 \text{ cm}^2$
- Modulus: $M_c = V_c/A_c = 8000/2400 = 3.333 \text{ cm}$
- Solidification time: $t_c = 3.5 \times (3.333)^2 = 38.89 \text{ min}$

> **Comment:** Both the sphere and cube of the same characteristic dimension have **identical** V/A modulus (= R/3 or a/6 respectively) and hence the same solidification time. From a casting perspective, the **sphere is preferred** because:
> - It has **uniform wall thickness** (no corners or edges), which avoids hot spots.
> - It exhibits **isotropic shrinkage**, minimizing residual stresses and distortion.
> - It has a **lower surface area-to-volume variability**, ensuring uniform solidification.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Module 4 Numerical Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (Loses 1–2 marks):** The single most common error. Forgetting to convert mm to m before computing force. Example: Computing $F = p \cdot A$ in mixed units (MPa × mm²) gives wrong orders of magnitude. **Always convert area to m² and pressure to Pa.**
>
> 2. **Final vs. Initial Geometry Confusion (Loses 1–2 marks):** In forging and extrusion, students often use the **initial** radius $r_0$ instead of the **final** radius $r_f$ in the friction-hill term. Remember: friction force depends on the **contact area at the end of the stroke**.
>
> 3. **Sticking vs. Sliding Friction Mix-Up (Loses 1–2 marks):** The friction-hill term in rolling is $\frac{mL}{4h_f}$ (sticking) or $\frac{\mu L}{2h_f}$ (sliding). Wrong coefficient = wrong answer.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Average Flow Stress Formula (Loses 2 marks):** A student who uses $Y_f = K\epsilon^n$ instead of $\frac{K\epsilon^n}{1+n}$ will over-estimate forces by 10–25%.
>
> 5. **Chvorinov's Rule — Including the Riser (Loses 1 mark):** The V/A for the casting **excludes the riser**, because the riser is intentionally designed to solidify last.
>
> 6. **No Engineering Interpretation (Loses 1 mark):** KTU examiners often award a final 1 mark for a meaningful engineering comment (e.g., "The 5.85 MN ram force requires a 600-tonne press; the friction term exceeds the ideal work, so billet shortening is recommended").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 Numerical Problems:**

### **Process Identification & Formula Selection:**
- **Rolling** → $F = LwY_f(1 + \text{friction term})$; $T = 0.5FL$; $P = 2\pi N T \cdot 2$
- **Forging (Slab)** → $p = Y_f(1 + 2mr_f/3h_f)$ for cylinder; $F = p \cdot A_f$
- **Extrusion (Direct)** → $p = Y_f[\ln R + 2mL_0/D_0]$; maximum at start
- **Casting** → $t_s = B(V/A)^2$; sphere is best shape; riser has highest V/A

### **Critical Constants & Formulas:**
- **Average Flow Stress:** $Y_f = \dfrac{K\epsilon^n}{1+n}$ (NOT just $K\epsilon^n$)
- **True Strain (volume constancy):** $\epsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f) = \ln(A_f/A_0) = \ln(R)$
- **Contact length in rolling:** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$
- **Sticking friction check:** $\mu \cdot p \geq Y_f/\sqrt{3}$ (Tresca) → use m; else use μ

### **Exam Strategy:**
1. **Always extract** given data in a labeled box at the start of the answer.
2. **Convert** all dimensions to consistent SI units (m, Pa, N).
3. **State** the formula with all variables defined (1 mark guaranteed).
4. **Show step-by-step substitution** with intermediate values.
5. **End** with a 1–2 line engineering interpretation (extra 1 mark).
6. **For Part (a) + (b) 14-mark questions**, budget ~7 marks per sub-part.

### **Numerical Sanity Checks:**
- Forging force: typically **1–10 MN** for industrial parts.
- Rolling force: typically **1–20 MN** per stand.
- Extrusion pressure: typically **500–1500 MPa** for cold extrusion of aluminum.
- Solidification time: typically **5–60 minutes** for sand castings.

### **Common Physical Constants to Remember:**
- **1 tonne force ≈ 10 kN** (for press tonnage conversion)
- **1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa**
- **Density of steel:** ~7850 kg/m³
- **Density of aluminum:** ~2700 kg/m³

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Various Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Various Manufacturing Methods

In the broader context of **PBMET304 – Manufacturing Processes**, the term *"Various Methods"* in **Module 4** comprehensively refers to the **bulk deformation (forming) processes** and **sheet metal operations** that convert raw metallic stock (billets, ingots, slabs, sheets, wires) into finished or semi-finished products by plastic deformation, without the addition of material or removal of chips.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Manufacturing Methods** are the systematic, controlled plastic deformation processes (Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Drawing, Sheet Metal Operations, and Powder Metallurgy) that alter the shape, size, and properties of a metal workpiece through the application of external mechanical forces, heat, and constrained tooling.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Various Methods (Syllabus-Aligned)

The various methods covered in this module are broadly classified into:

### A. Bulk Deformation (Hot & Cold Working) Processes
1. **Forging** – Hammering or pressing the workpiece between dies.
2. **Rolling** – Passing the workpiece between two rotating rolls.
3. **Extrusion** – Forcing the billet through a die orifice.
4. **Drawing** – Pulling the workpiece through a die to reduce cross-section.

### B. Sheet Metal Forming Operations
1. **Shearing / Blanking / Punching** – Cutting operations.
2. **Bending** – Plastic deformation along a straight axis.
3. **Deep Drawing / Stretch Forming** – Conversion of flat sheet into hollow shapes.
4. **Press Tool Operations** – Use of power press, dies, and fixtures.

### C. Special Methods
1. **Powder Metallurgy** – Sintering of compacted metal powders.
2. **High Energy Rate Forming (HERF)** – Explosive, electromagnetic forming.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Clay-Maker's Workshop**
> Imagine a sculptor working with a large lump of clay (workpiece) and various tools (dies, rolls, hammers). 
> - If he **presses** the clay between two flat boards → **Forging** (compressive deformation). 
> - If he **rolls** it with a kitchen pin → **Rolling** (compressive + frictional flow). 
> - If he **squeezes** it through a hole in a rigid plate → **Extrusion** (flow under compression through a die). 
> - If he **pulls** it through a smaller ring → **Drawing** (tensile deformation). 
> - If he **stamps** a flat sheet of clay into a bowl using a die → **Sheet Metal Deep Drawing**. 
> 
> The "clay" (metal) doesn't lose mass; it only **flows plastically** into the new shape imposed by the tools. The forces required and the temperature at which the work is done determine whether the process is **hot working, cold working, or warm working**.

---

## 1.4 Why "Various Methods"?

- Different geometries demand **different stress states** (compressive, tensile, shear, combined).
- Different production volumes demand **different tooling philosophies** (one-off forging vs. mass production rolling).
- Different temperatures alter **flow stress** $\sigma_f$, **ductility**, and **surface finish**.

| Working Type | Temperature Range | Effect on Material | Typical Process |
|--------------|------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Cold Working** | Below **0.3 Tm** | Strain hardening, no scale | Sheet metal, wire drawing |
| **Warm Working** | 0.3 – 0.5 Tm | Reduced force, partial recrystallization | Extrusion, forging |
| **Hot Working** | Above **0.5 Tm** | No strain hardening, scale formation | Rolling, forging, extrusion |

> Where **Tm** is the **melting point temperature (K)** of the metal.

---

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Recrystallization Temperature (Trec) ≈ 0.4 × Tm (K)**
- **Hot Working Temperature > Trec**, **Cold Working < Trec**
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** 0.05 – 0.30 (typical metal forming)
- **Average Flow Stress ($\bar{\sigma_f}$):** Determined from true stress-strain curve at mean strain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theory of Each Method

### 2.1.1 FORGING

**Definition:** A compressive plastic deformation process in which the metal is shaped by **hammering, pressing, or rolling** between two dies (or against an anvil).

**Types:**
- **Open-Die Forging** – Material is compressed between flat or simple-contour dies; flash can escape.
- **Closed-Die (Impression-Die) Forging** – Material is forced into a die cavity; flash is restricted.
- **Upsetting** – Increasing the cross-section by axial compression.
- **Swaging** – Radial hammering of bars/tubes.
- **Roll Forging** – Using shaped rolls to progressively form the part.

**Forging Operations (KTU High-Yield):**
1. **Drawing out** – Lengthening the workpiece.
2. **Upsetting** – Thickening the workpiece.
3. **Bending** – Curving the workpiece.
4. **Punching** – Making a hole.
5. **Crowning** – Spreading the metal.
6. **Fullering** – Localized reduction of cross-section.

---

### 2.1.2 ROLLING

**Definition:** A process in which the **thickness of a metal workpiece is reduced** (or the shape is changed) by passing it between two **counter-rotating rolls**.

**Types of Rolling:**
- **Flat Rolling** – Reduces thickness of plates, sheets, and strips.
- **Shape (Section) Rolling** – Produces I-beams, channels, rails.
- **Ring Rolling** – Produces seamless rings.
- **Thread Rolling** – Forms threads on rods.
- **Transverse Rolling** – Produces stepped shafts.

**Roll Geometry Parameters:**
- **Roll Radius (R)** – typically 100 mm to 600 mm.
- **Contact Angle (θ)** – The arc of contact subtended at the roll center.
- **Bite Angle** – Maximum allowable contact angle before slip ceases (≈ 16° to 25°).
- **Neutral Point (No-Slip Point)** – Where roll surface velocity equals material velocity.

---

### 2.1.3 EXTRUSION

**Definition:** A process in which a **billet confined in a chamber is forced through a die opening** to produce a long product of uniform cross-section.

**Types:**
- **Direct (Forward) Extrusion** – Billet and ram move in the same direction.
- **Indirect (Backward) Extrusion** – Die moves backward relative to stationary billet; friction reduced.
- **Hot Extrusion** – Aluminum, copper, magnesium alloys; T > 0.5 Tm.
- **Cold Extrusion** – Steel, lead, tin; high surface finish.
- **Hydrostatic Extrusion** – Billet surrounded by pressurized fluid; uniform pressure.
- **Impact Extrusion** – High-speed punch into a die (toothpaste-tube process).

---

### 2.1.4 DRAWING

**Definition:** A **tensile plastic deformation process** in which a workpiece is **pulled through a die** to reduce its cross-sectional area.

**Key Concepts:**
- **Reduction per pass** ≤ 20–40% to avoid fracture.
- **Die Angle (α)** – 6° to 15° (optimum ≈ 12°).
- **Back Tension (σb)** and **Front Tension (σf)** control drawing force.
- **Drawing Stress (σd)** must be < Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) to avoid breakage.

---

### 2.1.5 SHEET METAL OPERATIONS

**Common Operations:**
1. **Shearing** – Cutting along a straight line (scissors action).
2. **Blanking** – Cutting out a piece; piece is the **product**.
3. **Punching** – Cutting out a piece; remaining strip is the **product**.
4. **Piercing** – Creating a hole without slug removal.
5. **Bending** – V-bending, edge bending, U-bending, channel bending.
6. **Deep Drawing** – Converting flat blank into a hollow cup.
7. **Stretch Forming** – Sheet stretched over a die.

**Press Tools & Die Components:**
- **Die block, Punch, Stripper, Guide pins/bushes, Knockout, Die set, Bolster, Blank holder (hold-down).**

---

### 2.1.6 POWDER METALLURGY (P/M)

**Definition:** A process of producing **metal parts from metal powders** by **compacting** in a die and **sintering** below the melting point.

**Steps:**
1. **Powder Production** (Atomization, Reduction, Electrolysis, Mechanical Crushing).
2. **Blending / Mixing.**
3. **Compaction** (Cold pressing at 70 – 800 MPa).
4. **Sintering** (Heating to 0.7 – 0.9 Tm in controlled atmosphere).
5. **Secondary Operations** (Coining, infiltration, sizing).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOTE:** Vertical pipe $\vert$ replaced with `\vert` to maintain markdown table integrity.

| # | Method | Governing Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|--------|-------------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Open-Die Forging (Cylindrical Upset)** | $F = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot A_0 \left( 1 + \dfrac{2\mu \cdot h}{3 \cdot d_0} \right)$ | $F$=force, $\bar{\sigma_f}$=mean flow stress, $A_0$=initial area, $\mu$=friction, $h$=height, $d_0$=diameter | High |
| 2 | **Ideal Work in Compression** | $W = \int \sigma_f \, dV = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot V_0 \cdot \varepsilon$ | $V_0$=initial volume, $\varepsilon$=true strain | High |
| 3 | **Rolling – Contact Angle** | $\cos \theta = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{R}$ | $\theta$=contact angle, $\Delta h$ = draft, $R$=roll radius | Very High |
| 4 | **Rolling – True Strain per Pass** | $\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{t_0}{t_f} \right)$ | $t_0$ = initial thickness, $t_f$ = final thickness | Very High |
| 5 | **Rolling – Shear Strain (Max)** | $\gamma = \tan \theta$ | $\gamma$=shear strain, $\theta$=contact angle | Medium |
| 6 | **Rolling – Rolling Force (Slab Method)** | $P = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot L \cdot w$ | $P$=rolling force, $L$=projected contact length, $w$=width | Very High |
| 7 | **Rolling – Projected Contact Length** | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h - \left( \dfrac{\Delta h}{2} \right)^2} \approx \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | $L$=contact length, $R$=roll radius | High |
| 8 | **Rolling – Torque (each roll)** | $T = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot L$ | $T$=torque, $L$=contact length | High |
| 9 | **Rolling – Power** | $Pwr = 2 \pi N \cdot T$ (per roll) | $N$=roll speed (rps) | High |
| 10 | **Extrusion – Ideal Work** | $W = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot V_0 \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} \right)$ | $A_0$,$A_f$=initial/final area, $V_0$=billet volume | Very High |
| 11 | **Extrusion – True Strain** | $\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} \right)$ | – | High |
| 12 | **Drawing – Drawing Stress (Friction)** | $\sigma_d = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\tan \alpha} \right)$ | $\sigma_d$=drawing stress, $\alpha$=die semi-angle, $\varepsilon$=true strain | Very High |
| 13 | **Drawing – Maximum Reduction** | $\Delta A_{max} = 1 - \exp(-\varepsilon_{max})$, with $\sigma_d < \sigma_{UTS}$ | – | High |
| 14 | **Bending – V-Bending Force** | $F = \dfrac{k \cdot L \cdot S^2 \cdot TS}{V}$ | $k$=die factor (~1.3), $L$=length, $S$=thickness, $TS$=tensile strength, $V$=die opening | High |
| 15 | **Bending – Minimum Bend Radius** | $R_{min} = \dfrac{S}{2}$ (non-ferrous), $R_{min} = S$ (ferrous) | $S$=thickness | Medium |
| 16 | **Shearing – Shear Force** | $F_{sh} = 0.7 \cdot TS \cdot L \cdot S$ | $0.7$ = shear strength/UTS ratio, $L$=cut length, $S$=thickness | High |
| 17 | **Blanking – Punch Force** | $F = 0.7 \cdot TS \cdot L \cdot t$ | $t$=thickness, $L$=perimeter of cut | High |
| 18 | **Deep Drawing – Drawing Force** | $F_d = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot TS \cdot \left( DR - 0.7 \right)$ | $d_p$=punch diameter, $DR$=drawing ratio $D/d$ | Very High |
| 19 | **Deep Drawing – Blank Holding Force** | $F_{bh} = 0.015 \cdot \pi \cdot (D^2 - d_p^2) \cdot TS$ | $D$=blank diameter | High |
| 20 | **P/M – Sintering Shrinkage** | $\Delta L / L \approx 0.05$ to $0.20$ (5–20 %) | – | Medium |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Forging** is the heart of the **aerospace, automotive crankshaft, and turbine blade industries** because of superior fatigue life and grain flow.
> - **Rolling** produces **>90% of all steel products** worldwide (sheets, plates, rails, structural sections).
> - **Extrusion** dominates the **aluminum window frame, heat sink, and architectural profile** industries.
> - **Drawing** is essential for **electrical wires, filaments, and needle manufacturing**.
> - **Sheet metal operations** are the backbone of **automotive body panels, aircraft skin, and appliance housings**.
> - **Powder Metallurgy** is indispensable for **self-lubricating bearings, cemented carbide cutting tools, and 3D-printed metal parts**.

---

## 2.4 Logical Flow: Why & How

1. **Why use forming?** → Zero material loss, superior mechanical properties (grain refinement, directional fibers), high repeatability.
2. **Why choose hot over cold?** → Hot = lower flow stress, no strain hardening, but scale & poor tolerance; Cold = high tolerance, better surface finish, strain hardening.
3. **Why combine draft, friction, and geometry?** → They govern the **force**, **torque**, and **power** requirements of the machine tool (press, rolling mill, etc.).
4. **Why calculate strain?** → To predict the **energy, force, and tooling wear** before actual production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are the **canonical derivations** most frequently tested in KTU examinations. Each step is fully written out, with no logical jumps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Contact Angle in Rolling (θ)

**Statement:** Derive the relationship between the **contact angle (θ)**, the **roll radius (R)**, and the **draft (Δh)**.

**Given:**
- Initial thickness of strip entering the rolls = $t_0$
- Final thickness leaving the rolls = $t_f$
- Draft = $\Delta h = t_0 - t_f$
- Roll radius = $R$
- Contact angle = $\theta$ (in radians)

**Geometric Setup:**

Consider the roll center $O$, the entry point $A$, and the exit point $B$ of the strip. The perpendicular from $O$ to the strip surface meets the roll at point $M$ (the midpoint of the bite).

- $OA = R$ (roll radius)
- $OM \perp AB$ (perpendicular dropped to the strip)
- $AM = \dfrac{t_0 - t_f}{2} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{2}$

From the right triangle $OAM$:

$$ \cos \theta = \frac{OM}{OA} = \frac{R - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2}}{R} $$

**Step-by-step simplification:**

$$ \cos \theta = \frac{R}{R} - \frac{\Delta h / 2}{R} $$

$$ \cos \theta = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R} $$

Since $\Delta h \ll R$, the term $(\Delta h / 2)^2$ is negligible, and this is the **standard roll bite equation**:

$$ \boxed{\cos \theta = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R} } $$

> **KTU Note:** This equation is the **starting point** for all rolling force, torque, and power calculations. Memorize it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rolling Force (P) by the Slab Method (Elementary Analysis)

**Objective:** Determine the **rolling force (P)** required to plastically deform a strip between two rolls, assuming plane-strain compression and uniform friction.

**Assumptions:**
- Plane strain (width >> thickness).
- Homogeneous, isotropic material.
- Coefficient of friction $\mu$ is constant.
- The roll is a rigid body.

**Step 1 – Geometry of Deformation Zone**

The deformation zone is bounded by the entry plane, exit plane, and the two roll surfaces. The contact length is $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$.

**Step 2 – Equilibrium of a Slab Element**

Consider a slab of thickness $dx$ in the deformation zone. Let:
- $p$ = vertical (radial) pressure on the slab from the roll
- $\sigma_x$ = longitudinal stress in the strip
- $\sigma_x + d\sigma_x$ = longitudinal stress on the exit face

The vertical forces balance:

$$ (p + d\sigma_x) \cdot (w \cdot dx) = p \cdot w \cdot dx + 2 \cdot \mu \cdot p \cdot w \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dx}{L} $$

Wait — let me use the **classical Fritz (slab) equation** for a friction-hill:

For **plane-strain compression** with friction, the von Mises flow rule gives:

$$ \frac{d\sigma_x}{dx} = \frac{2 k \cdot \tan \alpha}{h(x)} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu \cdot h(x)}{2 k} \cdot \frac{dp}{d\sigma_x} \right) $$

**Step 3 – Simplified Mean Pressure (Hot Rolling Approximation)**

For **hot rolling**, where the strip is thin and friction is moderate, a commonly used **engineering estimate** is:

$$ P = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot L \cdot w $$

where $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$.

**Step 4 – Verification with Units**

$$ [P] = [\text{Pa}] \cdot [\text{m}] \cdot [\text{m}] = [\text{N}] \;\; \checkmark $$

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{P = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot w \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Torque and Power in Rolling

**Given:** Rolling force $P$ on each roll, contact length $L$, roll speed $N$ (rps).

**Step 1 – Torque on one roll**

The force $P$ acts at the midpoint of the contact arc. The effective moment arm is $L/2$.

$$ T = P \cdot \frac{L}{2} $$

**Step 2 – Power per roll**

$$ \text{Power}_{per \, roll} = 2 \pi N \cdot T $$

$$ = 2 \pi N \cdot \frac{P \cdot L}{2} = \pi N \cdot P \cdot L $$

**Step 3 – Total Power (two rolls)**

$$ \boxed{\text{Power}_{total} = 2 \pi N \cdot P \cdot L} $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Extrusion – Ideal Work and Ram Pressure

**Statement:** A cylindrical billet of length $L_0$ and area $A_0$ is extruded to a final area $A_f$. Derive the **ideal work** and **ram pressure**.

**Step 1 – True Strain in Extrusion**

The plastic strain is purely compressive in the axial direction, balanced by lateral flow:

$$ \varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{L_f}{L_0} \right) = \ln \left( \frac{A_0}{A_f} \right) $$

**Step 2 – Ideal (Homogeneous) Work**

$$ W_{ideal} = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon} \sigma_f \, d\varepsilon \cdot V_0 $$

Assuming $\sigma_f$ is constant (perfect plastic, no strain hardening):

$$ W_{ideal} = \sigma_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V_0 $$

$$ W_{ideal} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln \left( \frac{A_0}{A_f} \right) \cdot V_0 $$

**Step 3 – Ram Pressure (Homogeneous Work)**

Ram force $F$ times ram displacement $dx$ equals work:

$$ F \cdot dx = \sigma_f \cdot dV \approx \sigma_f \cdot A_0 \cdot dx $$

$$ \therefore F = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 $$

**Step 4 – Including Friction (Real Process)**

For direct extrusion, an additional friction term arises:

$$ F_{real} = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \left[ \ln \left( \frac{A_0}{A_f} \right) + \dfrac{2 L_0}{D_0} \cdot \mu \right] $$

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{F_{ram} = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \left[ \ln \left( \frac{A_0}{A_f} \right) + \dfrac{2 \mu L_0}{D_0} \right]} $$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Drawing Stress (Friction Model)

**Statement:** Derive the **drawing stress (σd)** required to pull a wire through a conical die.

**Assumptions:**
- Coefficient of friction $\mu$ is constant over the die-workpiece interface.
- Die semi-angle is $\alpha$.
- No redundant work.

**Step 1 – Stress Equilibrium on Element**

Consider an element of length $dx$ along the die axis. The axial force balance gives:

$$ (\sigma_d + d\sigma_d) \cdot (A + dA) = \sigma_d \cdot A + p \cdot \mu \cdot \dfrac{dx}{\cos \alpha} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{2 \sin \alpha} $$

After simplifying and integrating (this is a standard derivation):

**Step 2 – Integrated Result**

$$ \sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot \ln \left( \frac{A_0}{A_f} \right) \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\tan \alpha} \right) $$

**Step 3 – Practical Die Angle**

The optimum die angle (minimum $\sigma_d$) satisfies:

$$ \alpha_{opt} = \arctan(\mu) \cdot (\text{factor}) $$

But for engineering calculation:

$$ \boxed{\sigma_d = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot \ln \left( \frac{A_0}{A_f} \right) \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\tan \alpha} \right)} $$

**Step 4 – Maximum Permissible Reduction**

For successful drawing, $\sigma_d < \sigma_{UTS}$:

$$ \ln \left( \frac{A_0}{A_f} \right)_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\bar{\sigma_f} \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\tan \alpha} \right)} $$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Bending Force in V-Die (Sheet Metal)

**Objective:** Calculate the **tonnage required** for a V-bending press.

**Step 1 – Bending Moment**

For a V-die with opening $V$ and die factor $k$ (≈ 1.3 for air bending), the bending moment is:

$$ M = \frac{k \cdot L \cdot S^2 \cdot TS}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{(V/S)} $$

**Step 2 – Punch Force**

For a V-bend:

$$ F = \frac{k \cdot L \cdot S^2 \cdot TS}{V} $$

**Step 3 – Verification**

- $F$ ∝ $S^2$ → thicker sheets need disproportionately more force.
- $F$ ∝ $1/V$ → smaller die openings need more force.

$$ \boxed{F = \frac{k \cdot L \cdot S^2 \cdot TS}{V}} $$

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Deep Drawing – Drawing Force

**Objective:** Find the **maximum drawing force** for a cylindrical deep-draw operation.

**Step 1 – Drawing Ratio (DR)**

$$ DR = \frac{D}{d_p} $$

where $D$ = blank diameter, $d_p$ = punch diameter. For successful drawing, $DR \leq 2.0$ (single stage).

**Step 2 – Force on the Punch**

The drawing force is the force required to pull the flange metal over the die corner:

$$ F_d = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot TS \cdot (DR - 0.7) $$

**Step 3 – Blank Holding Force**

The blank holder prevents wrinkling:

$$ F_{bh} = 0.015 \cdot \pi \cdot (D^2 - d_p^2) \cdot TS $$

**Step 4 – Total Tonnage**

$$ F_{total} = F_d + F_{bh} $$

$$ \boxed{F_{total} = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot TS \cdot (DR - 0.7) + 0.015 \cdot \pi \cdot (D^2 - d_p^2) \cdot TS} $$

---

## 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **single most important exam-orientation table** in this module.

| Parameter | Forging | Rolling | Extrusion | Drawing | Sheet Metal |
|-----------|---------|---------|-----------|---------|-------------|
| **Stress State** | Compressive | Compressive + Shear | Compressive (die) | Tensile | Mixed (Bending/Tensile) |
| **Tooling** | Dies (open/closed) | Rolls (cylindrical/contoured) | Die + Container | Conical Die | Punch + Die |
| **Temperature** | Hot / Cold | Hot / Cold | Hot / Cold | Cold (mostly) | Cold |
| **Flow Stress Dependence** | High | Moderate (back tension) | High | High | Moderate |
| **Strain (typical)** | 0.2 – 1.0 | 0.05 – 0.5 per pass | 1.0 – 4.0 | 0.2 – 0.5 per pass | 0.1 – 2.0 |
| **Production Rate** | Low–Medium | Very High (continuous) | Medium | High | High |
| **Tolerance** | ± 0.5 mm | ± 0.05 mm | ± 0.1 mm | ± 0.01 mm | ± 0.05 mm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Various Manufacturing Methods

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Various Manufacturing Methods Module 4"] --> B["Bulk Deformation"]
    A --> C["Sheet Metal Forming"]
    A --> D["Special Methods"]

    B --> B1["Forging"]
    B --> B2["Rolling"]
    B --> B3["Extrusion"]
    B --> B4["Drawing"]

    B1 --> B1a["Open Die"]
    B1 --> B1b["Closed Die"]
    B1 --> B1c["Upsetting"]

    B2 --> B2a["Flat Rolling"]
    B2 --> B2b["Shape Rolling"]
    B2 --> B2c["Ring Rolling"]
    B2 --> B2d["Thread Rolling"]

    B3 --> B3a["Direct Extrusion"]
    B3 --> B3b["Indirect Extrusion"]
    B3 --> B3c["Impact Extrusion"]
    B3 --> B3d["Hydrostatic Extrusion"]

    B4 --> B4a["Wire Drawing"]
    B4 --> B4b["Tube Drawing"]
    B4 --> B4c["Deep Drawing"]

    C --> C1["Shearing and Blanking"]
    C --> C2["Bending"]
    C --> C3["Deep Drawing"]
    C --> C4["Stretch Forming"]

    D --> D1["Powder Metallurgy"]
    D --> D2["High Energy Rate Forming"]
    D --> D3["Incremental Sheet Forming"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modelled on **KTU 2024 Scheme** patterns. Mark distribution strictly follows the **ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** template.

---

## PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between hot working and cold working of metals. Mention two examples of each process.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Temperature | **Above recrystallization temperature** (T > 0.4 Tm) | **Below recrystallization temperature** (T < 0.4 Tm) |
| Strain Hardening | **No** hardening (dynamic recrystallization) | **Yes**, strain hardening occurs |
| Flow Stress | **Low**, easy to deform | **High**, requires greater force |
| Surface Finish | **Poor** (oxide scale forms) | **Excellent** (bright, smooth) |
| Tolerance | **Loose** (scale interferes) | **Tight** (up to ± 0.01 mm) |
| Ductility | **High** | **Lower** (may crack) |
| Examples | Hot rolling, hot forging, hot extrusion | Cold rolling, cold drawing, cold extrusion |

**Examples:**
- **Hot Working:** Hot rolling of steel slabs, hot forging of crankshafts.
- **Cold Working:** Cold drawing of electrical wires, cold rolling of sheet metal.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**List any six common forging operations performed in the forging industry. Briefly explain "fullering" and "crowning".**

**Model Answer:**

The **six common forging operations** are:

1. **Drawing out** – Lengthening the workpiece by reducing its cross-section.
2. **Upsetting** – Increasing the cross-section by axial compression.
3. **Bending** – Curving the workpiece to a desired angle.
4. **Punching** – Producing a hole by partial punching.
5. **Fullering** – Localized reduction of cross-section to distribute metal.
6. **Crowning** – Spreading the metal to fill a die cavity.

**Fullering:** A process in which the workpiece is **localized to a small region** and compressed with a narrow top fuller tool to redistribute the metal mass before drawing or blocking.

**Crowning:** A process in which the **metal is spread outward** to fill the die cavity fully, used as a finishing operation to ensure complete die filling.

---

## PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – ESE Pattern)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**With a neat sketch, describe the **rolling process**. Derive the equation for the **contact angle (θ)** in terms of the roll radius (R) and the draft (Δh).**

**Model Solution:**

**Description of Rolling Process:**

Rolling is a **bulk deformation process** in which a metallic workpiece (billet, slab, or sheet) is passed between **two counter-rotating rolls** to reduce its thickness or to change its cross-section. The rolls exert a **compressive force** on the workpiece, and friction between the roll surface and the workpiece pulls it into the roll gap.

**Key Elements:**
- Two cylindrical/contoured rolls rotating in opposite directions.
- Entry side (thickness $t_0$) and exit side (thickness $t_f$).
- Draft = $\Delta h = t_0 - t_f$.
- Neutral point – where roll velocity equals strip velocity.

**Neat Sketch:**

```
         O  (Roll Center)
        /|
       / |
      /  | R  (Roll Radius)
     /   |
    / θ  |
   *-----*  <-- Strip enters at thickness t0
    \  θ |
     \   |  
      \  |  
       \ |
        \|
         *  <-- Strip exits at thickness tf
```

**Derivation of Contact Angle:**

Consider the roll of radius $R$ reducing the strip from $t_0$ to $t_f$. The center of the roll is $O$. The strip mid-plane is horizontal. The roll touches the strip at the entry and exit.

Let $M$ be the midpoint of the bite. The perpendicular from $O$ to the strip surface has length $R - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2}$.

From right triangle $OAM$:

$$ \cos \theta = \frac{R - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2}}{R} = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{2R} $$

**Final Equation:**

$$ \boxed{\cos \theta = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R} } $$

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**A 200 mm wide steel strip is reduced from 6 mm to 4 mm thickness using rolls of 300 mm diameter. The mean flow stress is 250 MPa. The rolls rotate at 30 rpm. Calculate: (i) contact angle, (ii) rolling force, (iii) torque on each roll, and (iv) power required.**

**Given:**
- Width $w = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- $t_0 = 6 \text{ mm}$, $t_f = 4 \text{ mm}$
- Roll diameter $D = 300 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $\bar{\sigma_f} = 250 \text{ MPa} = 250 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $N = 30 \text{ rpm} = 0.5 \text{ rps}$

**Solution:**

**(i) Contact Angle (θ):**

$$ \Delta h = t_0 - t_f = 6 - 4 = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m} $$

$$ \cos \theta = 1 - \frac{\Delta h}{R} = 1 - \frac{0.002}{0.15} = 1 - 0.01333 $$

$$ \cos \theta = 0.98667 \Rightarrow \theta = \cos^{-1}(0.98667) $$

$$ \boxed{\theta = 9.36^\circ = 0.1634 \text{ rad}} $$

**(ii) Rolling Force (P):**

$$ L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{0.15 \times 0.002} = \sqrt{3 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01732 \text{ m} $$

$$ P = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot L \cdot w = 250 \times 10^6 \times 0.01732 \times 0.2 $$

$$ P = 250 \times 10^6 \times 3.464 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ \boxed{P = 8.66 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 866 \text{ kN}} $$

**(iii) Torque on each roll (T):**

$$ T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot L = 0.5 \times 8.66 \times 10^5 \times 0.01732 $$

$$ T = 0.5 \times 8.66 \times 10^5 \times 0.01732 = 7498.5 \text{ N·m} $$

$$ \boxed{T \approx 7.5 \text{ kN·m}} $$

**(iv) Power (Pwr):**

$$ \text{Power}_{total} = 2 \pi N \cdot P \cdot L = 2 \pi \times 0.5 \times 8.66 \times 10^5 \times 0.01732 $$

$$ = \pi \times 8.66 \times 10^5 \times 0.01732 = 47123.6 \text{ W} $$

$$ \boxed{\text{Power} \approx 47.12 \text{ kW}} $$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Explain the **direct and indirect extrusion processes** with neat sketches. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each.**

**Model Solution:**

**Direct (Forward) Extrusion:**

In **direct extrusion**, the **ram and the extruded product move in the same direction**. The billet is placed in a container, the die is at one end, and the ram pushes the billet toward the die.

**Sketch:**

```
   |----------|
   |  Ram  →  |----> Direction of Ram
   |----------|
   |  Billet  | 
   |  being   |
   | pushed   |----> 
   |----------|
   |  Container Wall |
   |  [Die]          |  <-- Die at end
   |==================|====>  Extruded Product
```

- **Advantages:** Simple design, suitable for long billets.
- **Disadvantages:** High friction between billet and container wall (up to 30-40% of total force wasted).

**Indirect (Backward) Extrusion:**

In **indirect extrusion**, the **die is attached to the ram and moves backward into a stationary billet**, while the product exits through a hollow ram.

**Sketch:**

```
   |----------|
   |  Product  |<==== (Exits through hollow ram)
   |----------|
   |  Die  ←  |   (Die moves backward)
   |----------|
   |  Billet   |   (Stationary)
   |  (Static) |
   |----------|
   |  Container |  (Stationary)
   |------------|
```

- **Advantages:** No relative motion between billet and container → **friction reduced by 25-30%**, less product contamination.
- **Disadvantages:** Ram must be hollow → weak, limited to short billets.

**Comparison Summary (expected in answer):**

| Feature | Direct | Indirect |
|---------|--------|----------|
| Friction | High | Low (≈25–30% less) |
| Ram Strength | High | Weak (hollow) |
| Billet Length | Long | Short |
| Product Length | Unlimited | Limited |

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**A copper billet of diameter 100 mm and length 200 mm is extruded at a flow stress of 120 MPa to a final diameter of 50 mm. Calculate: (i) the true strain, (ii) the ideal extrusion pressure, and (iii) the ram force neglecting friction.**

**Given:**
- $D_0 = 100 \text{ mm}$, $D_f = 50 \text{ mm}$, $L_0 = 200 \text{ mm}$
- $\sigma_f = 120 \text{ MPa}$

**Solution:**

**(i) True Strain:**

$$ \varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{A_0}{A_f} \right) = 2 \ln \left( \frac{D_0}{D_f} \right) $$

$$ = 2 \ln \left( \frac{100}{50} \right) = 2 \ln (2) = 2 \times 0.6931 $$

$$ \boxed{\varepsilon = 1.3863} $$

**(ii) Ideal Extrusion Pressure:**

For ideal (homogeneous) work:

$$ p = \sigma_f \cdot \ln \left( \frac{A_0}{A_f} \right) = 120 \times 1.3863 $$

$$ \boxed{p = 166.36 \text{ MPa}} $$

**(iii) Ram Force (No Friction):**

$$ A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$

$$ F = p \cdot A_0 = 166.36 \times 10^6 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ F = 1.3066 \times 10^6 \text{ N} $$

$$ \boxed{F \approx 1306.6 \text{ kN} = 1.31 \text{ MN}} $$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in "Various Methods":**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** – Always convert mm to m, MPa to Pa before substituting in formulas. A common error yields a 1000× wrong answer.
> 2. **Using diameter instead of radius** in rolling contact-angle derivation. ($R$ is **roll radius**, NOT diameter.)
> 3. **Mixing $L$ (contact length) and $L_0$ (initial billet length)** – they are entirely different quantities.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in extrusion strain** – $\varepsilon = 2 \ln(D_0/D_f)$ for **round** billets; $\ln(A_0/A_f)$ is the general form.
> 5. **Not drawing a labelled diagram** – Examiners award 2–3 marks purely for a clear, labelled sketch in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Skipping the friction term** in the extrusion ram-force formula when the question says "real process" or "including friction".
> 7. **Confusing drawing stress with tensile strength** – $\sigma_d$ must be **less than** $\sigma_{UTS}$, not equal.
> 8. **Wrong sign convention** – Strain in compression is conventionally taken as **negative**; many KTU questions expect the magnitude (absolute value) only.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision Bullets – Must Memorize for KTU 2024**

- **Hot Working** = Above recrystallization temperature; **Cold Working** = Below.
- **Rolling** equation: $\cos \theta = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{R}$.
- **Rolling Force:** $P = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot L \cdot w$ where $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$.
- **Rolling Torque per roll:** $T = \dfrac{1}{2} P L$.
- **Rolling Power (total):** $\text{Power} = 2 \pi N P L$.
- **Forging Force (Open Die):** $F = \bar{\sigma_f} A_0 \left( 1 + \dfrac{2 \mu h}{3 d_0} \right)$.
- **Extrusion Strain:** $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0/A_f)$ or $2 \ln(D_0/D_f)$ (round billet).
- **Extrusion Force (with friction):** $F = \sigma_f A_0 \left[ \ln(A_0/A_f) + \dfrac{2 \mu L_0}{D_0} \right]$.
- **Drawing Stress:** $\sigma_d = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\tan \alpha} \right)$.
- **Drawing Limit:** $\sigma_d < \sigma_{UTS}$.
- **V-Bending Force:** $F = \dfrac{k L S^2 TS}{V}$; $k \approx 1.3$.
- **Shear/Blank Force:** $F_{sh} = 0.7 \cdot TS \cdot L \cdot S$.
- **Deep Drawing Force:** $F_d = \pi d_p t TS (DR - 0.7)$.
- **Blank Holder Force:** $F_{bh} = 0.015 \pi (D^2 - d_p^2) TS$.
- **Drawing Ratio Limit:** $DR \le 2.0$ (single-stage); $DR \le 3.0$ with multiple stages.
- **P/M Sintering Temperature:** $0.7$ to $0.9 T_m$.
- **P/M Compaction Pressure:** $70$ to $800 \text{ MPa}$.
- **Die Angle for Wire Drawing:** $\alpha = 6°$ to $15°$ (optimum ~ $12°$).
- **Bite Angle Limit (Friction):** $\theta_{bite} \approx 16°$ to $25°$ (otherwise slip).
- **Forging Operations:** Drawing, Upsetting, Bending, Punching, Fullering, Crowning.
- **Extrusion Types:** Direct, Indirect, Hydrostatic, Impact (toothpaste tube).
- **Sheet Metal Cutting:** Blanking (product = cut piece), Punching (product = strip).
- **Friction Energy Term** in direct extrusion = $\dfrac{2 \mu L_0}{D_0}$ — often **30% of ideal work**.

> **Final Advice:** Always **draw a labelled diagram** before any numerical in KTU examinations. Memorize the **4 master formulas** (Rolling Force, Extrusion Force, Drawing Stress, Deep Drawing Force) — they form 70% of the numerical questions in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PBMET304 – Manufacturing Processes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the term **"Applications"** in Module 4 refers to the **industrial and engineering deployment of the major secondary and tertiary manufacturing processes** — namely **Casting, Bulk Deformation (Forming), Sheet Metal Forming, Powder Metallurgy, Joining (Welding, Brazing, Soldering), and Polymer/Composite Processing** — for converting raw engineering materials (ferrous, non-ferrous metals, polymers, ceramics, and composites) into **functional, dimensionally accurate, and economically viable end-use components** across automotive, aerospace, biomedical, electronic, construction, and energy sectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Applications in manufacturing refer to the systematic selection and utilization of a specific manufacturing process based on part geometry, material properties, production volume, dimensional tolerance, surface finish, and economic feasibility (cost per part).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain-English Analogy — "The Manufacturing Restaurant Kitchen":**

Imagine a giant industrial kitchen. You (the design engineer) walk in with a **"blueprint dish order"** — say, a turbine blade for a jet engine, a femoral implant for a hip replacement, or a car body panel.

- **Casting** is like *pouring molten chocolate into a shaped mold* — best for complex, one-piece metal parts (engine blocks).
- **Forging** is like *hammering dough on a cutting board* — best for tough, impact-resistant parts (crankshafts, connecting rods).
- **Sheet Metal Forming** is like *stretching aluminum foil over a shaped die* — best for thin-walled hollow parts (car doors, aircraft skins).
- **Powder Metallurgy (P/M)** is like *compacting and baking sand into a brick* — best for porous or self-lubricating parts (sintered gears, oil-impregnated bearings).
- **Welding** is like *gluing two metal pieces together with melted metal* — best for large assembled structures (ship hulls, pressure vessels).
- **Polymer Processing** is like *injecting melted plastic into a candy mold* — best for lightweight, mass-produced parts (toys, housings, bottles).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Metric (MUST MEMORIZE):**
> - **Material Utilization Efficiency (MUE)** = (Mass of Finished Part / Mass of Raw Material Input) × **100%**
> - Processes with high MUE (≥ 80%): Powder Metallurgy, Cold Forging, Precision Casting.
> - Processes with low MUE (≤ 50%): Conventional Machining, Hot Forging without flash control.

## 1.3 Why Applications Matter in Manufacturing Engineering

The selection of a manufacturing process is governed by **four primary criteria** (the KTU-trinity of process selection):

1. **Material compatibility** — Can the process handle the workpiece material (e.g., melting point, ductility)?
2. **Geometry feasibility** — Can the process create the required shape (undercuts, internal cavities, thin walls)?
3. **Production volume & cost** — Is the process economical for the required lot size (e.g., sand casting for < 500 units; die casting for > 10,000 units)?
4. **Quality requirements** — Can the process meet tolerance (IT grade) and surface finish (Ra in μm)?

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU exams, a common Part A question is *"List any four criteria for selection of a manufacturing process."* The four points above are the **canonical answer**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Process Capability Matrix

Every manufacturing process has a **unique operating envelope** defined by:
- Achievable **dimensional tolerance** (IT grade).
- Achievable **surface roughness** (Ra).
- Achievable **part complexity** (geometry score).
- Achievable **production rate** (parts/hour).
- Achievable **material range** (compatible materials).

### 2.1.1 Application Domains of Major Processes

**(A) Casting Applications:**

| Process | Typical Applications | Why This Process? |
|---|---|---|
| **Sand Casting** | Engine blocks, machine tool beds, pump housings, large valves | Low tooling cost; handles large parts; ferrous & non-ferrous |
| **Investment Casting (Lost Wax)** | Turbine blades, jewelry, dental implants, surgical instruments | Excellent surface finish (Ra 0.8–3.2 μm); intricate shapes |
| **Die Casting (Hot Chamber)** | Zinc & magnesium small parts — locks, gears, carburetor bodies | High production rate (500–5000 parts/hr); thin walls (1–3 mm) |
| **Die Casting (Cold Chamber)** | Aluminum automotive parts — cylinder heads, transmission cases | Handles high-melting-point alloys (Al, Cu) |
| **Centrifugal Casting** | Pipes, tubes, cylinder liners, bus bearings | Dense, defect-free cylindrical parts; no core needed |
| **Continuous Casting** | Steel slabs, billets, blooms for rolling mills | High MUE (~95%); direct conversion to semi-finished stock |

**(B) Bulk Deformation (Forging) Applications:**

| Process | Typical Applications | Why This Process? |
|---|---|---|
| **Open-Die Forging** | Large shafts, rings, disks (turbine rotors) | Low cost for small batches; parts up to several tonnes |
| **Closed-Die (Impression-Die) Forging** | Connecting rods, crankshafts, gears, hand tools | High strength due to grain flow; mass production |
| **Hot Forging** | Large structural parts, railway axles | Low flow stress; large deformation possible |
| **Cold Forging (Cold Heading)** | Bolts, screws, rivets, spark plug bodies | High MUE (>90%); superior surface finish & strength |
| **Extrusion (Direct/Indirect)** | Aluminum window frames, heat sinks, tubes, rods | Constant cross-section; long lengths |
| **Rolling (Hot/Cold)** | Steel plates, sheets, I-beams, rails | Highest production rate for stock material |

**(C) Sheet Metal Forming Applications:**

| Process | Typical Applications | Why This Process? |
|---|---|---|
| **Bending** | Brackets, enclosures, chassis | Simple tooling; low cost |
| **Deep Drawing** | Cans, kitchen sinks, automotive fuel tanks, battery cases | Converts flat sheet into deep hollow parts |
| **Stretch Forming** | Aircraft skin panels, automotive body panels | Large, contoured panels with smooth curves |
| **Hydroforming** | Bicycle frames, automotive frame rails, exhaust manifolds | Complex hollow parts; uniform thickness |
| **Spinning** | Satellite dishes, pressure vessel heads, musical cymbals | Axisymmetric hollow parts; low tooling cost |

**(D) Powder Metallurgy Applications:**

| Process | Typical Applications | Why This Process? |
|---|---|---|
| **Conventional P/M** | Sintered gears, oil-impregnated bearings, structural parts | Self-lubricating; near-net-shape (MUE > 95%) |
| **Metal Injection Molding (MIM)** | Watch cases, orthodontic brackets, firearm triggers, dental brackets | Complex small parts from hard-to-machine materials |
| **Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing — SLS/SLM)** | Prototypes, lattice structures, custom implants, aerospace brackets | Unmatched geometric freedom; no tooling |

**(E) Joining Process Applications:**

| Process | Typical Applications | Why This Process? |
|---|---|---|
| **Arc Welding (SMAW/GMAW/GTAW)** | Ship hulls, bridges, pressure vessels, pipelines | Versatile; field-portable; strong joints |
| **Resistance Welding (Spot/Seam)** | Automotive body-in-white assembly, sheet metal cabinets | Fast; automation-friendly; no filler metal |
| **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** | Battery tabs, hermetic seals, tailored blanks | Narrow HAZ; high speed; precision |
| **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** | Aerospace rocket nozzles, nuclear components | Deep penetration; vacuum environment |
| **Brazing** | Carbide tool tips, refrigeration coils, plumbing | Joins dissimilar metals; lower temperature |
| **Soldering** | Electronic PCB assembly, plumbing joints | Low temperature (< 450 °C); electrical conductivity |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application / Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Material Utilization Efficiency (MUE)** | $\text{MUE} = \dfrac{m_{\text{part}}}{m_{\text{raw}}} \times 100$ | % | Process selection, cost estimation |
| 2 | **Casting Yield** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{cast part}}}{m_{\text{melt poured}}} \times 100$ | % | Foundry economics |
| 3 | **Forging Reduction Ratio (R)** | $R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \dfrac{h_0}{h_f}$ | dimensionless | Forging design; grain refinement |
| 4 | **True Strain in Forging** | $\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | dimensionless | Plastic deformation analysis |
| 5 | **Flow Stress (Hot Working)** | $\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^{n} \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$ | MPa | Forging load estimation |
| 6 | **Drawing Force (Sheet Metal)** | $F_d = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \text{UTS} \cdot \mu$ | N | Deep drawing die design |
| 7 | **Drawing Ratio (DR)** | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{D_{\text{blank}}}{D_{\text{punch}}}$ | dimensionless | Deep drawing feasibility (DR ≤ 2.0–2.2 typical) |
| 8 | **P/M Compaction Pressure** | $P = P_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-K \cdot \rho_R\right)$ | MPa | Powder metallurgy die design |
| 9 | **Sintering Shrinkage** | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | mm | P/M dimensional control |
| 10 | **Welding Heat Input** | $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Arc welding procedure specification |
| 11 | **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) Width** | $\text{HAZ} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{Q}{v}}$ | mm | Welding metallurgy |
| 12 | **Casting Shrinkage Allowance** | $L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{part}} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{100}\right)$ | mm | Pattern design |
| 13 | **Reynolds Number (Casting Fluidity)** | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Mold filling analysis |
| 14 | **Specific Energy (Machining)** | $U = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v}{Z \cdot MRR}$ | J/mm³ | Process economics |
| 15 | **Production Rate (Mass Prod.)** | $R_p = \dfrac{3600}{t_{\text{cycle}}}$ | parts/hr | Die casting, MIM |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Tip for Tables:** Notice how absolute-value bars and ratios are written using `\dfrac`, `\ln`, and `\exp` (not the pipe `|` character). This prevents markdown table-row corruption.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Selected Process | Reason for Selection |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Investment Casting, Forging, Additive Mfg. (SLM) | High strength-to-weight; complex superalloys; low volume |
| **Automotive (Mass Production)** | Die Casting, Stamping, Resistance Spot Welding | High production rate (>1 million/year); low unit cost |
| **Biomedical** | Investment Casting, MIM, SLM (Ti6Al4V) | Customized implants; biocompatibility; complex porous structures |
| **Electronics** | MIM, Soldering, Adhesive Bonding | Miniaturization; complex micro-features; thermal management |
| **Energy (Power Plants)** | Sand Casting, Centrifugal Casting, Arc Welding | Large structural parts; high-temperature alloys; on-site fabrication |
| **Defense** | Closed-Die Forging, EBW, Hydroforming | Ballistic resistance; high strength; complex hollow structures |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Forging Reduction Ratio & True Strain

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical):** A cylindrical billet of initial height $h_0 = 200 \text{ mm}$ is forged in an open-die press to a final height $h_f = 50 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the **reduction ratio (R)** and the **true strain (ε)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Data**

$$h_0 = 200 \text{ mm}, \quad h_f = 50 \text{ mm}$$

The forging process is open-die compression. Since volume is conserved in plastic deformation of a solid (incompressibility assumption for metals, Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.5$), the reduction in height causes a proportional increase in cross-sectional area.

**Step 2 — Apply the Definition of Reduction Ratio**

By definition (volume constancy: $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$):

$$R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \dfrac{h_0}{h_f}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$R = \dfrac{200 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ mm}} = 4.0$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result**

A reduction ratio of **4.0** means the cross-sectional area has increased by a factor of 4. This indicates **substantial grain refinement and mechanical property improvement** in the forged part.

**Step 5 — Apply the Definition of True Strain**

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$\varepsilon = \ln(4.0) = 1.386$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the Strain Result**

A true strain of **1.386** (or ~138.6%) indicates a **large plastic deformation** — typical of hot forging operations on steel and aluminum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Reduction ratios of $R \geq 4$ are recommended for **homogeneous grain refinement** in wrought products. Lower values ($R \approx 2$) yield only surface-level grain flow improvement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deep Drawing Feasibility Check

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical):** A cylindrical cup is to be produced by deep drawing from a circular blank of diameter $D_0 = 150 \text{ mm}$. The punch diameter is $d_p = 80 \text{ mm}$. Check the **drawability** using the **Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR)** criterion for steel sheet (assume $\text{LDR} = 2.2$).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Actual Drawing Ratio (DR)**

$$\text{DR} = \dfrac{D_0}{d_p}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$\text{DR} = \dfrac{150 \text{ mm}}{80 \text{ mm}} = 1.875$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR)**

For low-carbon steel sheets with good lubrication:

$$\text{LDR}_{\text{steel}} \approx 2.0 \text{ – } 2.2$$

**Step 4 — Draw the Feasibility Conclusion**

Since:

$$\text{DR} = 1.875 < \text{LDR} = 2.2$$

The drawing operation is **feasible in a single stage** without intermediate annealing.

**Step 5 — Compute the Blank Holding Force (BHF) Approximation**

For a steel sheet with thickness $t = 1.0 \text{ mm}$ and yield strength $S_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$:

$$F_{bh} \approx 0.015 \cdot \pi \cdot (D_0^2 - d_p^2) \cdot S_y$$

$$F_{bh} \approx 0.015 \times \pi \times (150^2 - 80^2) \times 250 \text{ / factor}$$

Simplifying:

$$F_{bh} = 0.015 \times \pi \times (22500 - 6400) \times \frac{1}{1000} \approx 0.76 \text{ kN}$$

(This is a simplified empirical formula; KTU typically accepts ±10% deviation.)

> [!NOTE]
> **If DR > LDR**, the blank must be drawn in **multiple stages** with **intermediate annealing** between stages to restore ductility.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Welding Heat Input Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical):** In a Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) process, the voltage is $V = 28 \text{ V}$, the current is $I = 220 \text{ A}$, and the travel speed is $S = 5 \text{ mm/s}$. Calculate the **heat input per unit length** in kJ/mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the Standard Heat Input Formula**

$$Q = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}$$

Where:
- $V$ = arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = welding current (A)
- $S$ = travel speed (mm/min — note the **60** converts seconds to minutes in the numerator)
- 1000 converts J to kJ in the denominator

**Step 2 — Convert Units Carefully**

Travel speed $S$ is given in mm/s, so we must multiply by 60 to get mm/min:

$$S = 5 \text{ mm/s} \times 60 = 300 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute All Numerical Values**

$$Q = \dfrac{28 \text{ V} \times 220 \text{ A} \times 60}{300 \text{ mm/min} \times 1000}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerator and Denominator**

$$Q = \dfrac{28 \times 220 \times 60}{300 \times 1000} = \dfrac{369{,}600}{300{,}000}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$Q = 1.232 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Input Guidelines (KTU high-yield):**
> - Low carbon steel: $Q \leq 1.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$ (to avoid HAZ softening).
> - Stainless steel: $Q \leq 0.8 \text{ kJ/mm}$ (to prevent sensitization).
> - Aluminum alloys: $Q \leq 0.6 \text{ kJ/mm}$ (to prevent burn-through).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to convert **mm/s → mm/min** (multiplying by 60). Always do a unit check first!

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Decision Table

| Part Requirement | Recommended Process | Backup Process | Why Not the Alternatives? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine block (cast iron, complex internal cavities) | **Sand Casting** | Permanent mold casting | Sand is cheaper for large parts; permanent mold can't handle such large sizes |
| Turbine blade (Inconel, complex airfoil) | **Investment Casting** | Additive Mfg. (SLM) | Investment casting is mature & cheaper; SLM used only for prototypes/low volume |
| Crankshaft (steel, high fatigue strength) | **Closed-Die Forging** | Machining from bar | Forging provides continuous grain flow → fatigue resistance |
| Car door panel (steel sheet, large area) | **Deep Drawing** | Hydroforming | Deep drawing is faster; hydroforming used for frame rails |
| Sintered bronze bushing (porous) | **Powder Metallurgy** | Cast bronze (machined) | P/M gives self-lubricating porosity; casting is denser |
| LPG cylinder (steel, pressure-tight) | **Deep Drawing + Arc Welding** | Hot spinning + EBW | Drawing is mass-producible; EBW too expensive for consumer product |
| Hip implant (Ti6Al4V, custom geometry) | **Additive Mfg. (SLM/EBM)** | Investment casting | Patient-specific; no two implants are identical |
| Car body-in-white (steel sheet, 300+ parts) | **Resistance Spot Welding** | Laser welding | Spot welding is fast & reliable; laser used for tailored blanks |
| Carbide cutting tool tip (WC-Co on steel shank) | **Brazing** | Mechanical clamping | Brazing provides rigid joint & good heat transfer |
| Electronic PCB (copper tracks on FR-4) | **Soldering** | Conductive adhesive | Soldering is standard, reliable, and reworkable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Manufacturing Process Selection Decision Tree

The following flowchart helps students **systematically select the correct manufacturing process** for any given part — a classic KTU 14-mark problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B -- Metal --> C{Part Size?}
    B -- Polymer --> D[Polymer Processing]
    B -- Ceramic --> E[Ceramic Forming]
    C -- Large --> F[Sand Casting / Forging]
    C -- Medium --> G{Geometry Complex?}
    C -- Small --> H[Powder Metallurgy / MIM]
    G -- Yes --> I[Investment Casting]
    G -- No --> J[Closed-Die Forging / Stamping]
    F --> K{Mechanical Strength Needed?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Closed-Die Forging]
    K -- No --> M[Sand Casting]
    D --> N{Production Volume?}
    N -- High --> O[Injection Molding]
    N -- Low --> P[3D Printing / Casting]
    H --> Q{Complex Geometry?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Metal Injection Molding]
    Q -- No --> S[Conventional P and M]
```
