# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define drawing. List any three differences between rod drawing and wire drawing.**

**Model Answer:**

Drawing is a metal forming process in which a workpiece is plastically pulled through a converging die to reduce its cross-section and increase its length. It is a cold-working operation characterized by tensile axial stress and compressive die contact stress.

| Parameter | Rod Drawing | Wire Drawing |
|---|---|---|
| Stock size | Large diameter rods (> 5 mm) | Fine wire (< 5 mm) |
| Equipment | Draw bench (linear) | Bull block / multi-die continuous machine |
| Typical product | Bright bars, shafts | Electrical conductors, cables |
| Length per piece | Few meters | Kilometers (coil to coil) |
| Lubrication | Heavy grease/oil | Soap/sodium stearate powder |

*(Any 3 differences for 3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differences.)*

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of the redundant work factor (Φ) in the drawing equation. What is its typical range?**

**Model Answer:**

The redundant work factor (Φ) accounts for the **non-uniform (heterogeneous) strain distribution** within the workpiece cross-section during drawing. Material near the surface of the rod deforms more than the material at the center; this internal shearing consumes additional energy that is not represented by the ideal homogeneous work term.

Mathematically, the effective strain is amplified:
$$\varepsilon_{eff} = \Phi \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{A_o}{A_f}\right)$$

**Typical range:** $\Phi = 1.05 \text{ to } 1.30$ for axisymmetric drawing.
Empirical correlation: $\Phi \approx 1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\sin\alpha}$.

*(3 marks: 1 mark role + 1 mark formula + 1 mark value.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> *Internal choice format — answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.*

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **ideal drawing stress** in a rod drawing operation using slab method analysis, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A copper rod of initial diameter **20 mm** is drawn through a die of semi-angle **10°** to a final diameter of **15 mm**. The flow stress of copper is **220 MPa**, the friction coefficient is **0.08**, and the redundant work factor is **1.20**. Calculate: **(i)** the drawing stress, **(ii)** the drawing force, and **(iii)** verify whether drawing is feasible if the ultimate tensile strength of the drawn wire is **260 MPa**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the **derivation in Section 3.1** above for the slab-method derivation of:
$$\sigma_d = Y_f \ln\!\left(\frac{A_o}{A_f}\right) \quad \text{(Ideal case, no friction, no redundant work)}$$

**Assumptions to state explicitly (1 mark each, total 4 marks):**
1. Material is perfectly plastic (no work hardening) — i.e., constant $Y_f$.
2. Die–work interface is **frictionless** ($\mu = 0$).
3. Strain distribution is **homogeneous** (no redundant deformation) — $\Phi = 1$.
4. Plane sections remain plane; no barreling.

Final 3 marks: Equation formation, integration, and final result.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D_o = 20$ mm, $D_f = 15$ mm, $\alpha = 10°$, $Y_f = 220$ MPa, $\mu = 0.08$, $\Phi = 1.20$, $\text{UTS}_f = 260$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Areas:**
$$A_o = \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(15)^2 = 176.71 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Reduction and true strain:**
$$R = \frac{314.16 - 176.71}{314.16} = 0.4375 \;(43.75\%)$$
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{A_o}{A_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{314.16}{176.71}\right) = \ln(1.778) = 0.5754$$

**Step 3 — Convert die angle to radians and compute cot:**
$$\alpha = 10° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.1745 \text{ rad}$$
$$\cot\alpha = \frac{1}{\tan(10°)} = \frac{1}{0.1763} = 5.671$$

**Step 4 — Drawing stress (using master equation):**
$$\sigma_d = Y_f \cdot \Phi \cdot (1 + \mu\cot\alpha) \cdot \ln(A_o/A_f)$$
$$\sigma_d = 220 \times 1.20 \times (1 + 0.08 \times 5.671) \times 0.5754$$
$$\sigma_d = 220 \times 1.20 \times (1 + 0.4537) \times 0.5754$$
$$\sigma_d = 220 \times 1.20 \times 1.4537 \times 0.5754$$
$$\sigma_d = 220.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Drawing force:**
$$F_d = \sigma_d \cdot A_f = 220.7 \times 176.71 = 39{,}001 \text{ N} \approx 39.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Feasibility check:**
$$\sigma_d = 220.7 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{UTS}_f = 260 \text{ MPa}$$
Since $220.7 < 260$ → **Drawing is feasible** ✔ (with a safety margin of ~15%).

**Maximum permissible reduction** (fracture check):
$$\ln(A_o/A_f)_{max} = \frac{260}{220 \times 1.20 \times 1.4537} = 0.6770$$
$$R_{max} = 1 - e^{-0.6770} = 0.492 \;(49.2\%)$$
Our $R = 43.75\% < 49.2\%$ → Safe. ✔

**Final Answer (boxed):**
$$\boxed{\sigma_d = 220.7 \text{ MPa}, \quad F_d \approx 39.0 \text{ kN}, \quad \text{Drawing is FEASIBLE.}}$$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **mechanics of tube drawing** using a **fixed plug/mandrel**. Compare it with **sinking** and **floating plug** drawing. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A steel wire of diameter **5.0 mm** is to be drawn to **3.0 mm** in a single pass. The average flow stress is **350 MPa**, $\mu = 0.10$, die semi-angle $\alpha = 6°$, and $\Phi = 1.10$. Find the **drawing force per unit final area** and the **actual drawing force in kN**. Comment on whether the pass is safe if $\text{UTS}_f = 480$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

*(Describe the tube drawing operation with sketch — refer to Section 4.2 block diagram. Allocation: 3 marks for plug drawing description, 2 marks for sinking, 2 marks for comparison.)*

**Plug/Mandrel Tube Drawing (3 marks):**
- A long **mandrel rod** (fixed) or a **plug** (free, in floating plug) is positioned inside the die.
- The tube is pulled over this internal tool.
- The **mandrel controls the inner diameter (ID)** while the die controls the **outer diameter (OD)**.
- Both ID and wall thickness are precisely controlled.
- Used for **seamless tubes** in heat exchangers, hydraulic lines, and boiler tubes.

**Sinking (2 marks):**
- No internal mandrel.
- The tube is simply pulled through the die.
- **Wall thickness INCREASES** because metal flows inward (towards the centerline) under die pressure, similar to backward extrusion.
- Used for **rough sizing** of large-diameter tubes; OD is reduced but ID is not controlled.

**Floating Plug (2 marks — included in comparison):**
- The plug is "self-positioned" by the pressure difference across it.
- Allows **long drawing lengths** without a long mandrel rod.
- Used in **continuous tube mills** for mass production.

**Comparison table (any 3 differences for 1 mark):**
| Feature | Sinking | Plug Drawing | Floating Plug |
|---|---|---|---|
| ID control | No | Yes (precise) | Yes (precise) |
| Wall change | Thickens | Controlled thinning | Controlled thinning |
| Tooling cost | Lowest | Highest (mandrel) | Medium |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D_o = 5.0$ mm, $D_f = 3.0$ mm, $Y_f = 350$ MPa, $\mu = 0.10$, $\alpha = 6°$, $\Phi = 1.10$, $\text{UTS}_f = 480$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Areas:**
$$A_o = \frac{\pi}{4}(5.0)^2 = 19.635 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(3.0)^2 = 7.069 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — True strain:**
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{A_o}{A_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{19.635}{7.069}\right) = \ln(2.778) = 1.0217$$

**Step 3 — Cot α:**
$$\alpha = 6° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.1047 \text{ rad}$$
$$\cot\alpha = \frac{1}{\tan(6°)} = \frac{1}{0.1051} = 9.514$$

**Step 4 — Drawing stress:**
$$\sigma_d = 350 \times 1.10 \times (1 + 0.10 \times 9.514) \times 1.0217$$
$$\sigma_d = 350 \times 1.10 \times (1 + 0.9514) \times 1.0217$$
$$\sigma_d = 350 \times 1.10 \times 1.9514 \times 1.0217$$
$$\sigma_d = 767.2 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Drawing force:**
$$F_d = \sigma_d \times A_f = 767.2 \times 7.069 = 5{,}423 \text{ N} \approx 5.42 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Feasibility check:**
$$\sigma_d = 767.2 \text{ MPa} > \text{UTS}_f = 480 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{⚠ FAILURE}$$
Since drawing stress **exceeds UTS**, the wire **WILL BREAK** at the die exit. The pass is **NOT safe**.

**Step 7 — Required correction:**
To make it safe, the reduction per pass must be reduced. Maximum permissible true strain:
$$\ln(A_o/A_f)_{max} = \frac{480}{350 \times 1.10 \times 1.9514} = 0.6394$$
$$(A_o/A_f)_{max} = e^{0.6394} = 1.895 \Rightarrow (D_o/D_f)_{max} = \sqrt{1.895} = 1.377$$
$$D_{f,max} = \frac{5.0}{1.377} = 3.63 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**
$$\boxed{F_d/\text{unit area} = 767.2 \text{ MPa}, \quad F_d \approx 5.42 \text{ kN}, \quad \text{Pass is UNSAFE — use multi-pass drawing.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert α from degrees to radians** when computing $\cot\alpha$. Lose ~1 mark.
> 2. **Using initial area $A_o$** instead of final area $A_f$ when computing drawing force. The force acts on the *exit* material. Lose ~2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the redundant work factor Φ** — examiners often test this with a "comment" or "what-if" follow-up. Lose ~1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the feasibility check** ($\sigma_d$ vs UTS) at the end of every drawing problem. Lose ~1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up rod drawing and tube drawing equations** — tube drawing with plug uses the **Sommerfeld/Plug equation**, NOT the rod equation. Read the question carefully!

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Drawing** = tensile pulling through a die; **cold working** process; improves strength via strain hardening.
- ✅ **Three variants:** Rod (>5 mm), Wire (<5 mm), Tube (hollow). Wire drawing uses a **bull block**; rod uses a **draw bench**; tube uses a **mandrel/plug** for ID control.
- ✅ **Master equation (the one equation to rule them all):**
  $$\sigma_d = Y_f \, \Phi \, (1 + \mu \cot\alpha) \ln\!\left(\frac{A_o}{A_f}\right)$$
- ✅ **Drawing force** = $\sigma_d \times A_f$ — **use exit area, not entry area**.
- ✅ **Average flow stress** $Y_f = (Y_o + Y_f^{final})/2$ — assume linear work hardening.
- ✅ **Die angle conversion:** Always convert α to **radians** before substituting.
- ✅ **Friction factor** $(1 + \mu\cot\alpha)$ → increases with smaller α and higher μ.
- ✅ **Redundant factor** $\Phi \approx 1 + \mu/\sin\alpha$ → increases with smaller α and higher μ.
- ✅ **Feasibility rule:** $\sigma_d \leq \text{UTS}_f$ (exit material). If violated → **centerline / chevron cracking** at die exit.
- ✅ **Max reduction per pass** $R_{max} = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{\text{UTS}_f}{Y_f \, \Phi \, (1+\mu\cot\alpha)}\right)$.
- ✅ **Tube drawing types:** (i) **Sinking** (no support — wall thickens), (ii) **Plug drawing** (best ID control), (iii) **Floating plug** (long lengths, self-locked), (iv) **Mandrel drawing** (long mandrel rod, lower productivity).
- ✅ **Optimal die semi-angle** for rod: **6°–15°** (tradeoff between friction and redundant work).
- ✅ **Reduction** in a single pass is typically **20%–35%** for steel; multiple passes (with intermediate **annealing**) can take total reduction beyond **90%**.
- ✅ **Centerline cracking** — caused by high tensile axial stress at the center of the rod exceeding the UTS — *this* is the **#1 defect** in drawing.
- ✅ **Lubricants** in drawing: soaps, sodium stearate (wire), oils/greases (rod), phosphate coatings (steel rods — *bonderizing*).

> **Final Exam Mantra (write this in your answer sheet's margin):**
> *"Drawing Force = Average Flow Stress × Redundant Factor × Friction Factor × True Strain × Exit Area."*
> *(4 multiplicative factors + 1 area — never forget any of them.)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drawing Force and Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drawing Force and Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Drawing** is a metal forming operation in which a workpiece (sheet, wire, or tube) is pulled through a die to reduce its cross-section, elongate it, or impart a specific shape. The deformation is achieved by applying a **tensile (drawing) force** at the exit side of the die, while the material undergoes plastic deformation in the deformation zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Drawing is a tensile-compressive bulk/plastic deformation process in which the work material is pulled through a die orifice, with the drawing force and power being the critical parameters that govern process feasibility, die life, and product quality.

The three principal variants covered in **Module 4** are:

| Variant | Workpiece Form | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Wire Drawing** | Solid cylindrical rod/wire | Reduced diameter wire |
| **Tube Drawing** | Hollow tube/pipe | Reduced diameter & wall thickness tube |
| **Deep Drawing** | Sheet metal blank | Cup/cylindrical shell |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Toothpaste Tube**
> Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste by pulling its tail end through a narrow ring. The toothpaste (workpiece) elongates and thins as it passes through the restriction. Your hand pulls with a force, doing work against the tube wall (friction) and reshaping the paste (plastic deformation). The harder the paste flows, the more force you need — exactly analogous to how **flow stress**, **friction**, and **reduction ratio** dictate the drawing force in metal forming.

In essence:
- **Reduction** (thinning) requires energy → contributes to the **ideal work** term.
- **Friction at the die-workpiece interface** requires extra energy → contributes to the **friction work** term.
- **Non-uniform deformation** in the deformation zone requires extra energy → contributes to the **redundant work** term.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of steel** ≈ **7850 kg/m³** (used in some power calculations)
- **Coefficient of friction (μ)** in drawing: typically **0.05 to 0.15** (with proper lubricant)
- **Die semi-angle (α)**: typically **6° to 15°** for wire drawing
- **Maximum drawing stress** limit: should not exceed the **ultimate tensile strength** of the drawn material (otherwise fracture occurs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The drawing process can be broken down into **three simultaneous energy demands** that the drawing force must overcome:

### Step 1 — Ideal Homogeneous Deformation
In a perfect world (no friction, no redundant work), the drawing force is governed purely by the **reduction in area** and the **flow stress** of the material.

- True strain: $\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$
- Ideal drawing stress: $\sigma_{d,\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$

### Step 2 — Frictional Resistance
The die wall opposes the flow of material. The friction force is proportional to the **die pressure (p)** and the **coefficient of friction (μ)**. It depends strongly on the **die semi-angle (α)**: smaller angles increase the contact area but reduce normal pressure.

- Friction work contribution adds a factor: $(1 + \mu \cot \alpha)$

### Step 3 — Redundant Work
Material near the die centerline flows faster than material near the surface (or vice versa), creating **internal shear**. This non-uniform flow consumes additional energy beyond ideal deformation.

- Combined friction + redundant work factor: $B$ or $(1 + C)$

### Step 4 — Net Drawing Force
Combining all three contributions:

$$F_{\text{draw}} = \sigma_f \cdot (1 + B) \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot A_f$$

### Step 5 — Power Requirement
The power is simply the rate of work done:

$$P = F_{\text{draw}} \cdot v$$

where $v$ is the drawing velocity (m/s).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU 2024 University Exam**
> The table below lists all essential equations. Use \vert or \mid instead of \vert for absolute values to keep Markdown table syntax intact.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0 / A_f) = 2 \ln(D_0 / D_f)$ | True strain in drawing (area-based or diameter-based) |
| 2 | $r = (A_0 - A_f) / A_0 = 1 - (A_f / A_0)$ | Fractional reduction in area |
| 3 | $\sigma_{d,\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(A_0 / A_f)$ | Ideal drawing stress (no friction, no redundant work) |
| 4 | $\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot (1 + B) \cdot \ln(A_0 / A_f)$ | Actual drawing stress including friction and redundant work |
| 5 | $B = \mu \cot \alpha$ | Friction-redundancy factor (simplified; depends on die geometry) |
| 6 | $F_{\text{draw}} = \sigma_d \cdot A_f$ | Drawing force (wire / tube) |
| 7 | $F_{\text{draw}} = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_d$ | Deep drawing force (cup wall at punch diameter) |
| 8 | $DR = D_b / d_p$ | Drawing ratio (deep drawing) |
| 9 | $r_d = (D_b - d_p) / D_b$ | Deep drawing reduction |
| 10 | $P = F_{\text{draw}} \cdot v$ | Power (watts) |
| 11 | $\sigma_f \approx (\sigma_y + \sigma_{uts}) / 2$ | Average flow stress (often used in exam) |
| 12 | $\sigma_d \leq \sigma_{uts}$ | Feasibility constraint (no fracture) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why These Formulas Matter in Industry**
> - **Equipment Sizing:** Drawing force dictates the **tonnage rating of capstans, draw benches, and hydraulic cylinders**.
> - **Die Design:** Maximum safe reduction per pass is set by the **$\sigma_d \leq \sigma_{uts}$** constraint; violating it causes **center bursts** or **wire breakage**.
> - **Lubrication Strategy:** Reducing $\mu$ (via coatings like phosphate + soap) directly reduces the B-factor and required force.
> - **Energy Audit:** Power calculations help factories size **electric motors** and estimate **kWh consumption** per ton of drawn product.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ideal Drawing Stress (Energy Balance Method)

We start with the **work-energy principle**: the work done by the drawing force equals the ideal plastic work of homogeneous deformation.

### Given:
- Initial area: $A_0$
- Final area: $A_f$
- Average flow stress: $\sigma_f$

### Step 1 — Express the volume of the workpiece
The volume is conserved during plastic deformation:
$$V = A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_f \cdot L_f$$

### Step 2 — Write the ideal plastic work
The ideal plastic work is the product of flow stress, strain, and volume:
$$W_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V$$

where $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0 / A_f)$.

### Step 3 — Substitute volume
$$W_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot A_0 \cdot L_0 = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot A_f \cdot L_f$$

### Step 4 — Apply work-energy equivalence to drawing force
The drawing force acts over the elongation distance $\Delta L = L_f - L_0$:
$$F_{\text{ideal}} \cdot \Delta L = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot A_f \cdot L_f$$

### Step 5 — Use the relation $\Delta L / L_f = \ln(A_0 / A_f)$
A well-known geometric identity for plastic deformation:
$$\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_f} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the ideal drawing force
$$F_{\text{ideal}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot A_f$$

Dividing both sides by $\ln(A_0 / A_f)$:
$$F_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \cdot A_f$$

### Step 7 — Convert force to stress
Dividing by final area $A_f$:
$$\boxed{\sigma_{d,\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drawing Stress with Friction and Redundant Work

The actual drawing stress exceeds the ideal value due to two additive phenomena. We introduce the **B-factor** that captures both.

### Step 1 — Acknowledge the two correction terms
$$\sigma_d = \sigma_{d,\text{ideal}} + \sigma_{d,\text{friction}} + \sigma_{d,\text{redundant}}$$

### Step 2 — Combine into a multiplicative form
For engineering convenience, both correction terms are bundled into a single multiplier $B$:
$$\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot (1 + B) \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Express B from die geometry and friction
Using slab analysis at the die-workpiece interface, the friction contribution depends on **friction coefficient $\mu$** and **die semi-angle $\alpha$**:
$$B \approx \mu \cdot \cot \alpha$$

This reflects that **smaller die angles** → larger $\cot \alpha$ → larger B → higher drawing force.

### Step 4 — More rigorous B (from Schey/Avitzur analysis)
A more detailed treatment (which the KTU examiner may award bonus marks for) gives:
$$B = \dfrac{1}{\mu \tan \alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + \dfrac{2 L_f}{D_f \tan \alpha} - 1$$

However, for the KTU exam, the **simplified $B = \mu \cot \alpha$** is sufficient unless specifically asked.

### Step 5 — Final drawing force
$$\boxed{F_{\text{draw}} = \sigma_f \cdot (1 + \mu \cot \alpha) \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot A_f}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Required for Drawing

### Step 1 — Define mechanical power
Power is the rate of doing work:
$$P = \dfrac{W}{t} = F \cdot v$$

### Step 2 — Identify drawing velocity
For a **continuous drawing** process (e.g., wire drawing), the exit velocity is:
$$v_f = \dfrac{v_0 \cdot A_0}{A_f}$$

But for a **single-stroke draw** (e.g., deep drawing with a punch), the punch velocity $v$ is used directly.

### Step 3 — Substitute drawing force
$$P = \sigma_f \cdot (1 + \mu \cot \alpha) \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot A_f \cdot v$$

### Step 4 — Express in compact form
$$\boxed{P = F_{\text{draw}} \cdot v \;\; \text{(watts, when F in N and v in m/s)}}$$

### Step 5 — Account for efficiency (motors are not 100% efficient)
$$P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{P}{\eta}$$

where $\eta$ is the **mechanical efficiency** (typically 0.7 to 0.9 for industrial drives).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sample KTU-Style Numerical Walk-Through

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical (for self-verification)**

**Problem:** A steel wire of initial diameter 10 mm is drawn through a die to a final diameter of 7 mm. Take $\sigma_f = 400$ MPa, $\mu = 0.1$, $\alpha = 8°$. Drawing speed = 2 m/s. Calculate (a) drawing stress, (b) drawing force, (c) power.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Areas:
$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.010)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.007)^2 = 3.848 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

Step 2 — True strain:
$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{7.854}{3.848}\right) = \ln(2.041) = 0.714$$

Step 3 — B-factor:
$$B = \mu \cot \alpha = 0.1 \times \cot(8°) = 0.1 \times 7.115 = 0.7115$$

Step 4 — Drawing stress:
$$\sigma_d = 400 \times (1 + 0.7115) \times 0.714 = 400 \times 1.7115 \times 0.714 = 488.85 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 5 — Drawing force:
$$F_{\text{draw}} = \sigma_d \times A_f = 488.85 \times 10^6 \times 3.848 \times 10^{-5} = 18{,}813 \text{ N} \approx 18.81 \text{ kN}$$

Step 6 — Power:
$$P = 18{,}813 \times 2 = 37{,}626 \text{ W} \approx 37.6 \text{ kW}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Drawing Process Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Workpiece Initial Area A0] --> B[Entry into Die]
    B --> C[Deformation Zone Cone with semi-angle alpha]
    C --> D{Friction at die-workpiece interface}
    C --> E[Plastic strain epsilon equals ln of A0 over Af]
    C --> F[Redundant work due to non-uniform flow]
    D --> G[Draw Force F equals sigma_f times 1 plus B times ln of A0 over Af times Af]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Exit Product Final Area Af]
    G --> I[Power P equals F times drawing velocity v]
    H --> J[Next Pass or Coiling]
    I --> K[Motor Sizing and Energy Audit]
```

## 4.2 Force Components Decomposition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Drawing Force Fdraw] --> B[Ideal Component sigma_f times ln of A0 over Af]
    A --> C[Friction Component proportional to mu and cot alpha]
    A --> D[Redundant Work Component non-uniform flow]
    B --> E[Feasibility Check sigma_d less than sigma_uts]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F{Within Limit?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Successful Draw]
    F -->|No| H[Center Burst or Wire Fracture]
```

## 4.3 Die Geometry and Stress Distribution Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Entry Region: Undeformed Wire diameter D0] --> B[Approach Zone: Slight Bending]
    B --> C[Deformation Zone: Cone semi-angle alpha, length L]
    C --> D[Die Bearing Surface: Friction acts here, mu times pressure]
    C --> E[Back Relief Zone: Stress Recovery]
    E --> F[Exit: Drawn Wire diameter Df, axial stress sigma_d]
    D --> G[Slab Element Equilibrium: d-sigma and pressure p]
    G --> H[Integrated Form: sigma_d equals sigma_f times 1 plus B times ln of A0 over Af]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a physical region or analytical stage. The arrows indicate the *flow of material* and the *flow of analytical logic* simultaneously. The Mermaid schematic compensates for the inability to render a true engineering drawing of a conical wire-drawing die.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define drawing force. List the factors affecting drawing force in a wire drawing operation.**

**Model Answer:**
Drawing force is the tensile force applied at the exit side of a die to pull the workpiece through the die orifice, causing plastic deformation that reduces its cross-section. It is mathematically expressed as $F_{\text{draw}} = \sigma_d \cdot A_f$.

**Factors affecting drawing force:**
1. **Reduction in area** ($\ln(A_0/A_f)$) — higher reduction → higher force.
2. **Flow stress of material** ($\sigma_f$) — harder material → higher force.
3. **Coefficient of friction** ($\mu$) — poor lubrication → higher force.
4. **Die semi-angle** ($\alpha$) — smaller angle → longer contact → higher force.
5. **Redundant work factor** — non-uniform flow increases force.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**State the feasibility condition for a successful wire drawing operation. What happens if this condition is violated?**

**Model Answer:**
The feasibility condition is:
$$\sigma_d \leq \sigma_{uts}$$
The drawing stress must not exceed the ultimate tensile strength of the drawn material.

**If violated:**
- The wire fractures at the exit of the die.
- A phenomenon called **center burst** (internal Chevron cracking) may occur if $\sigma_d$ approaches $\sigma_{uts}$ but does not exceed it.
- To avoid this, the maximum reduction per pass is limited, and **back tension** may be applied.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **ideal drawing stress** in a wire drawing operation, starting from the work-energy principle. Clearly state the assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A copper wire of initial diameter 12 mm is drawn to a final diameter of 8 mm. Given: average flow stress $\sigma_f = 350$ MPa, coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.08$, die semi-angle $\alpha = 10°$, and drawing speed $v = 1.5$ m/s. Calculate: (i) Ideal drawing stress, (ii) Actual drawing stress, (iii) Drawing force, (iv) Power required.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

1. Assume homogeneous deformation, no friction, no redundant work.
2. Volume constancy: $V = A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$.
3. Ideal plastic work: $W = \sigma_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(A_0/A_f) \cdot A_f \cdot L_f$.
4. Work done by drawing force over elongation: $W = F_d \cdot \Delta L$.
5. Using $\Delta L / L_f = \ln(A_0 / A_f)$, we obtain: $F_d = \sigma_f \cdot A_f$.
6. Therefore: $\boxed{\sigma_{d,\text{ideal}} = F_d / A_f = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(A_0/A_f)}$.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Areas:**
$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(12)^2 = 113.10 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(8)^2 = 50.27 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — True strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{113.10}{50.27}\right) = \ln(2.25) = 0.8109$$

**Step 3 — Ideal drawing stress:**
$$\sigma_{d,\text{ideal}} = 350 \times 0.8109 = 283.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — B-factor:**
$$B = \mu \cot \alpha = 0.08 \times \cot(10°) = 0.08 \times 5.671 = 0.4537$$

**Step 5 — Actual drawing stress:**
$$\sigma_d = 350 \times (1 + 0.4537) \times 0.8109 = 350 \times 1.4537 \times 0.8109 = 412.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Drawing force:**
$$F_d = 412.7 \times 50.27 = 20{,}747 \text{ N} \approx 20.75 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Power:**
$$P = F_d \cdot v = 20{,}747 \times 1.5 = 31{,}120 \text{ W} \approx 31.12 \text{ kW}$$

**Feasibility check:** $\sigma_d = 412.7$ MPa. For copper, $\sigma_{uts} \approx 220$–400 MPa, depending on grade. **This would require multiple passes** in a real process — a single-pass reduction of 50% is rarely feasible for copper.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **redundant work factor** in drawing. How does it arise, and how is it accounted for in the drawing stress equation?

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a **deep drawing operation**, a circular blank of diameter 100 mm is drawn into a cup using a punch of diameter 50 mm. The sheet thickness is 2 mm, and the average drawing stress is 300 MPa. Calculate: (i) Drawing ratio, (ii) Drawing reduction, (iii) Drawing force, (iv) Power if the punch velocity is 0.05 m/s.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Definition:** Redundant work is the energy dissipated in internal shearing due to non-uniform velocity gradients within the deformation zone.
2. **Origin:** Material at the die center flows at a different rate than material at the die walls, creating **internal shear bands**. Even without external friction, this consumes additional energy.
3. **Quantitative impact:** The redundant work factor is typically lumped together with friction into a single $B$ term, but conceptually:
   - Friction work: $W_f \propto \mu \cdot p \cdot A_{\text{contact}}$.
   - Redundant work: $W_r \propto$ strain gradient × viscosity-like material response.
4. **Modified equation:** $\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot (1 + B) \cdot \ln(A_0/A_f)$, where $B$ now captures both effects.
5. **Dependence on die angle:** Redundant work is **maximum at very small die angles** (long deformation zone) and at **very large die angles** (sharp gradients). It is **minimum at an optimal die angle** (typically 6° to 15°).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Drawing ratio:**
$$DR = \dfrac{D_b}{d_p} = \dfrac{100}{50} = 2.0$$

**Step 2 — Drawing reduction:**
$$r_d = \dfrac{D_b - d_p}{D_b} = \dfrac{100 - 50}{100} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

**Step 3 — Drawing force (deep drawing):**
$$F_d = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_d = \pi \times 0.050 \times 0.002 \times 300 \times 10^6$$
$$F_d = \pi \times 0.050 \times 0.002 \times 300 \times 10^6 = 94{,}248 \text{ N} \approx 94.25 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Power:**
$$P = F_d \cdot v = 94{,}248 \times 0.05 = 4712 \text{ W} \approx 4.71 \text{ kW}$$

**Feasibility check:** A drawing ratio of 2.0 is at the upper limit of single-stage deep drawing. For most metals, $DR_{\max} \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$. **Multiple draws with annealing may be required** in industrial practice.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Always convert diameters from mm to m before computing area. The $\sigma_f$ is in Pa (N/m²), and using mm² will give force in MPa·mm² = N × 10⁻⁶, an error of a factor of 10⁶.
> 2. **Forgetting the B-factor:** Marks are reserved for the friction/redundancy correction. Using only the ideal formula will be marked down by 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Angle units:** $\cot \alpha$ requires $\alpha$ in **radians** in the calculator, not degrees. Set calculator to DEG mode for die angles in degrees.
> 4. **Confusing drawing ratio with reduction:** $DR = D_b / d_p$ (ratio, dimensionless) ≠ $r_d$ (fractional reduction, dimensionless but different formula).
> 5. **Feasibility check omitted:** Examiners often expect a one-line statement that the calculated $\sigma_d$ is within the $\sigma_{uts}$ limit. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Drawing Force and Power**

- **Core equation to memorize:** $\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot (1 + \mu \cot \alpha) \cdot \ln(A_0/A_f)$
- **Drawing force:** $F_d = \sigma_d \cdot A_f$ (wire/tube) or $F_d = \pi d_p t \sigma_d$ (deep drawing).
- **Power:** $P = F_d \cdot v$ (always product of force and velocity).
- **True strain in drawing:** $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0/A_f) = 2 \ln(D_0/D_f)$.
- **Friction direction:** Increases $B$ by $\mu \cot \alpha$. Small die angle = large $\cot \alpha$ = more friction work.
- **Feasibility:** $\sigma_d \leq \sigma_{uts}$ — single-pass reductions are limited (typically 20%–40% per pass in wire drawing).
- **Optimal die angle:** $\alpha = 6°$ to $15°$ balances friction and redundant work.
- **Lubrication impact:** Reducing $\mu$ from 0.1 to 0.05 can cut drawing force by **20%–30%**.
- **Deep drawing rule of thumb:** Maximum single-stage drawing ratio $\approx 2.0$ to $2.2$.
- **Average flow stress approximation:** $\sigma_f \approx (\sigma_y + \sigma_{uts})/2$ is acceptable in exams.
- **Energy audit shortcut:** For continuous wire drawing, $P = \sigma_d \cdot A_f \cdot v_f$, where $v_f$ is the **exit velocity** (since work is done as material leaves the die).
- **Always check units** — Pa · m² = N, Pa · m² · (m/s) = W.
- **Drawing vs. Extrusion:** Drawing uses **tensile** force; extrusion uses **compressive** force. Same equation for ideal work, but friction contribution is fundamentally different.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **PBMET304 – Manufacturing Processes (Module 4)**, the term **"Numerical Problems"** refers to the application of analytical and empirical equations derived from the mechanics of **bulk metal forming processes** (Rolling, Forging, and Extrusion) and **casting solidification** to compute key process parameters such as forming force, deformation load, torque, power consumption, extrusion pressure, and solidification time. These problems test the student's ability to translate a physical manufacturing scenario into a set of governing equations, identify the correct formula based on process assumptions (e.g., plane strain, homogeneous deformation, ideal work), and arrive at a numerically accurate solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Numerical problems in this module primarily test the **Upper Bound, Lower Bound, and Slab (Equilibrium) methods** of force/pressure estimation in metal forming, along with the **Chvorinov's Rule** for casting solidification. Mastery of these requires fluency in true stress–true strain relationships, friction factor (m) analysis, and the geometric relationship between die/workpiece contact area.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **squeezing a tube of toothpaste** between your fingers.

- The harder you press (and the larger the contact area between your fingers and the tube), the **more force** you need.
- If the tube's surface is **slippery** (less friction), the paste flows out more easily — just like a low friction factor (m) in metal forming reduces the forming load.
- If the paste is **thick** (high flow stress, K), the resistance to flow increases — analogous to using a high-strength alloy that requires a larger press.
- The **speed** at which you squeeze relates to the strain rate, which in turn dictates the power required by the press.

In the same way, every "Numerical Problem" in Module 4 boils down to one core idea: **Given the material properties (K, n), geometry (h₀, h_f, L, D), friction (μ or m), and process speed, find the force/energy/power required to deform the metal.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The bulk of Module 4 numerical problems follow a fixed four-step pattern:
> 1. **Identify** the forming process and extract the given data.
> 2. **Convert** all units to a consistent SI system.
> 3. **Select** the appropriate governing equation (Slab method, ideal work, Chvorinov, etc.).
> 4. **Substitute and solve** carefully, paying attention to the area ratio (R), strain (ε), and friction hill correction.
> 
> Memorizing this 4-step template will allow you to solve **> 90%** of KTU exam questions on this topic.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following standard values/assumptions are frequently used in KTU Module 4 problems:

- **Strain-hardening exponent (n):** typically 0.0 to 0.5 for most metals.
- **Strength coefficient (K):** typically **100 MPa to 1500 MPa** for steels and aluminum alloys.
- **Friction factor (m):** $0 \leq m \leq 1$; for hot forging with graphite lubricant, m ≈ 0.3 to 0.4.
- **Coefficient of friction (μ):** $0 \leq \mu \leq 0.5$ typical for cold metal forming.
- **In Chvorinov's rule:** mold constant B is empirically determined (typically 3 to 6 min/cm² for sand casting).
- **Geometric factor C in forging:** 1.0 (slab), 0.5 to 1.0 (closed-die forging).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation – The Slab Method of Analysis

The **Slab Method** is the cornerstone of force calculation in bulk metal forming. It involves:

1. **Slicing** the deforming workpiece into thin elemental "slabs" perpendicular to the direction of material flow.
2. **Applying equilibrium** to each slab considering internal stresses, friction shear, and the external applied load.
3. **Integrating** across the contact length to obtain the total forming force.

The generic equilibrium equation on a slab of width w and thickness h is:

$$\frac{d\sigma_x}{dx} \pm \frac{2 \tau_f}{h} = 0$$

where $\tau_f$ is the frictional shear stress acting at the die-workpiece interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sticking Friction Assumption:** When $\mu p \geq \frac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}}$ (Tresca) or when $m \to 1$ in the friction factor formulation, the friction shear becomes constant and equal to $\tau_f = m \cdot \frac{Y_f}{2}$, leading to the famous **friction hill** in forging and extrusion.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes **all the governing equations** that are essential for Module 4 numerical problem-solving in KTU 2024 examinations.

| **Process** | **Parameter** | **Governing Formula** | **Assumptions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rolling** | Roll Separating Force | $F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{m \cdot L}{4 \cdot h_f}\right)$ | Plane strain, sticking friction, homogeneous flow |
| **Rolling** | True Strain | $\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | Volume constancy |
| **Rolling** | Average Flow Stress | $Y_f = \frac{K \cdot \epsilon^n}{1+n}$ | Power-law strain hardening |
| **Rolling** | Roll Torque | $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$ | Each roll carries half the load |
| **Rolling** | Power | $P = 2 \pi N \cdot T$ | N = rotational speed (rev/s) |
| **Rolling** | Contact Length | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | Small draft, R = roll radius |
| **Forging (Slab)** | Forging Force | $F = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2 m r}{3 h_f}\right)$ | Cylinder upset, sticking friction, $r$ = radius |
| **Forging (Slab)** | Forging Pressure | $p = Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2 m r}{3 h_f}\right)$ | Average pressure on platen |
| **Forging (Energy)** | Ideal Work | $W_i = Y_f \cdot V \cdot \epsilon$ | Homogeneous deformation |
| **Forging (Energy)** | Actual Work (Upper Bound) | $W = W_i / \eta$ | $\eta$ = efficiency (0.3 to 0.8) |
| **Extrusion (Direct)** | Extrusion Pressure | $p = Y_f \cdot \left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)^n \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot e^{2m L / D}$ | Friction factor formulation |
| **Extrusion (Direct)** | Extrusion Force | $F = p \cdot A_0$ | Ram force |
| **Extrusion** | Extrusion Ratio (R) | $R = \frac{A_0}{A_f}$ | Ratio of billet to product area |
| **Drawing** | Drawing Stress | $\sigma_d = Y_f \cdot \left[ \left(1 + \frac{\mu}{\tan \alpha}\right) \cdot \left(1 - \left(\frac{A_f}{A_0}\right)^{(1+\mu \cot \alpha)}\right) + \frac{4 \mu L_f}{D_f} \right]$ | Friction at die and back tension |
| **Casting** | Chvorinov's Rule | $t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$ | $t_s$ = solidification time, B = mold constant |
| **Casting** | Shrinkage Allowance | $S_a = \alpha \cdot (T_m - T_0) \cdot L$ | Linear thermal expansion |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all KTU 2024 numerical answers, students **MUST** state the formula, the substituted values (with units), and the final answer with units. Examiners explicitly award partial marks for:
> - Correct formula identification: **1 mark**
> - Correct unit conversion: **1 mark**
> - Correct substitution: **1 mark**
> - Final numerical answer: **remaining marks**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These numerical calculations are **not academic exercises** — they directly drive the following engineering decisions in industry:

- **Press Tonnage Selection:** A 200-tonne automobile forging press must be specified based on the calculated peak forging force.
- **Motor Power Sizing:** Rolling mill motors are sized using the calculated roll torque and rotational speed.
- **Billet Length Optimization:** In extrusion, the maximum billet length is dictated by the ram force capacity vs. required extrusion pressure.
- **Cooling Time Estimation:** Casting foundries use Chvorinov's rule to determine shake-out time and optimize production rate.
- **Die Design:** Friction factor (m) directly informs die surface finish selection, lubrication strategy, and die material choice (e.g., H13 tool steel vs. WC).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked-Out Numerical Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Methodology Note:** Each problem below follows the canonical KTU 14-mark answer template: (1) Given data with units, (2) Assumptions, (3) Governing formula, (4) Step-by-step substitution, (5) Final answer with units, and (6) Engineering interpretation.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1: Rolling Mill — Separating Force, Torque, and Power

### Problem Statement
A steel strip with initial thickness $h_0 = 25 \text{ mm}$ is reduced to $h_f = 20 \text{ mm}$ in a single pass through a rolling mill with roll radius $R = 300 \text{ mm}$. The width of the strip is $w = 200 \text{ mm}$. The strength coefficient $K = 600 \text{ MPa}$ and strain-hardening exponent $n = 0.20$. The friction factor $m = 0.4$, and the rolls rotate at $N = 12 \text{ rpm}$. Compute:
- (a) The roll separating force (F).
- (b) The torque on each roll.
- (c) The power required to drive the mill.

### Given Data
$$h_0 = 25 \text{ mm}, \quad h_f = 20 \text{ mm}, \quad R = 300 \text{ mm}, \quad w = 200 \text{ mm}$$
$$K = 600 \text{ MPa}, \quad n = 0.20, \quad m = 0.4, \quad N = 12 \text{ rpm}$$

### Step 1: Calculate the Draft and Contact Length
The draft (reduction in thickness) is:
$$\Delta h = h_0 - h_f = 25 - 20 = 5 \text{ mm}$$

The contact length (projected length of the arc of contact) is:
$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{300 \times 5} = \sqrt{1500} \approx 38.73 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the True Strain
Using volume constancy for plane strain rolling:
$$\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{25}{20}\right) = \ln(1.25) \approx 0.2231$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Average Flow Stress (Y_f)
For a power-law material $\bar{\sigma} = K \epsilon^n$, the average flow stress across a strain history is:
$$Y_f = \frac{K \cdot \epsilon^n}{1 + n} = \frac{600 \times (0.2231)^{0.20}}{1 + 0.20}$$

Computing the term $(0.2231)^{0.20}$:
$$(0.2231)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln(0.2231)} = e^{0.2 \times (-1.5008)} = e^{-0.3002} \approx 0.7406$$

Substituting:
$$Y_f = \frac{600 \times 0.7406}{1.20} = \frac{444.36}{1.20} \approx 370.3 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Roll Separating Force
Using the friction-hill rolling formula:
$$F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{m \cdot L}{4 \cdot h_f}\right)$$

First, compute the friction-hill term:
$$\frac{m \cdot L}{4 \cdot h_f} = \frac{0.4 \times 38.73}{4 \times 20} = \frac{15.492}{80} \approx 0.1937$$

So the bracketed term is:
$$1 + 0.1937 = 1.1937$$

Substituting all values:
$$F = 38.73 \times 200 \times 370.3 \times 1.1937$$

$$F = 7746 \times 370.3 \times 1.1937$$

$$F \approx 2{,}868{,}064 \times 1.1937 \approx 3{,}423{,}676 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 3424 \text{ kN} \approx 3.42 \text{ MN}}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Torque on Each Roll
Assuming the resultant rolling force acts at the midpoint of the arc of contact (lever arm = L/2):
$$T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 3{,}423{,}676 \times 0.03873$$

$$T \approx 66{,}302 \text{ N·m} \approx 66.3 \text{ kN·m}$$

### Step 6: Calculate the Power Required
The rotational speed must be converted to rev/sec:
$$N = 12 \text{ rpm} = \frac{12}{60} = 0.2 \text{ rev/s}$$

The total power (for two rolls) is:
$$P = 2 \pi N \cdot T \cdot 2 = 4 \pi N \cdot T$$

Wait — let me correct this. The torque T we calculated is **per roll**, and total power accounts for both rolls:
$$P_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 2\pi N \cdot T = 2 \times 2\pi \times 0.2 \times 66{,}302$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 4 \pi \times 0.2 \times 66{,}302 = 2.513 \times 66{,}302$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 166{,}600 \text{ W} \approx 166.6 \text{ kW}}$$

### Engineering Interpretation
A rolling force of **3.42 MN** and power of **166.6 kW** indicates that this rolling pass requires a heavy-duty mill. The friction-hill multiplier (1.19) shows that friction contributes ~19% additional load — significant but within typical operating limits for cold rolling of steel.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2: Forging — Cylinder Upsetting under Slab Method

### Problem Statement
A solid cylindrical billet of diameter $d_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$ and height $h_0 = 75 \text{ mm}$ is upset forged in a press at room temperature. The final height after forging is $h_f = 50 \text{ mm}$. The material has strength coefficient $K = 700 \text{ MPa}$, strain-hardening exponent $n = 0.15$, and the friction factor at the die-workpiece interface is $m = 0.4$. Calculate:
- (a) The true strain in the forging operation.
- (b) The average flow stress $Y_f$.
- (c) The maximum forging force and the average forging pressure.

### Given Data
$$d_0 = 50 \text{ mm}, \quad h_0 = 75 \text{ mm}, \quad h_f = 50 \text{ mm}$$
$$K = 700 \text{ MPa}, \quad n = 0.15, \quad m = 0.4$$

### Step 1: Calculate the True Strain
For upsetting under volume constancy:
$$\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{75}{50}\right) = \ln(1.5) \approx 0.4055$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Final Radius (r)
Using volume constancy $V_0 = V_f$:
$$\frac{\pi}{4} d_0^2 \cdot h_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_f^2 \cdot h_f$$
$$50^2 \times 75 = d_f^2 \times 50$$
$$d_f^2 = \frac{50^2 \times 75}{50} = 50 \times 75 = 3750 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$d_f = \sqrt{3750} \approx 61.24 \text{ mm}$$

Final radius:
$$r_f = \frac{d_f}{2} = \frac{61.24}{2} \approx 30.62 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Average Flow Stress
$$Y_f = \frac{K \cdot \epsilon^n}{1 + n} = \frac{700 \times (0.4055)^{0.15}}{1 + 0.15}$$

Computing $(0.4055)^{0.15}$:
$$(0.4055)^{0.15} = e^{0.15 \ln(0.4055)} = e^{0.15 \times (-0.9027)} = e^{-0.1354} \approx 0.8734$$

Substituting:
$$Y_f = \frac{700 \times 0.8734}{1.15} = \frac{611.4}{1.15} \approx 531.7 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Friction-Hill Multiplier
For cylindrical upsetting using the slab method (assuming sticking friction is the limiting case where m ≤ 0.577, and m = 0.4 < 0.577, so the friction factor formula is valid):
$$1 + \frac{2 m r_f}{3 h_f} = 1 + \frac{2 \times 0.4 \times 30.62}{3 \times 50}$$

$$= 1 + \frac{24.496}{150} = 1 + 0.1633 = 1.1633$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Maximum Forging Pressure
$$p_{\max} = Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2 m r_f}{3 h_f}\right) = 531.7 \times 1.1633 \approx 618.5 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 6: Calculate the Maximum Forging Force
The final cross-sectional area of the billet is:
$$A_f = \pi r_f^2 = \pi \times (30.62)^2 = \pi \times 937.6 \approx 2945.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

Converting to m²: $A_f = 2945.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 2.9455 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

The maximum forging force is:
$$F_{\max} = p_{\max} \cdot A_f = 618.5 \times 10^6 \times 2.9455 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{\max} \approx 1{,}821{,}720 \text{ N} \approx 1822 \text{ kN} \approx 1.82 \text{ MN}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\max} \approx 1.82 \text{ MN}, \quad p_{\max} \approx 618.5 \text{ MPa}}$$

### Engineering Interpretation
The required press capacity is **~182 tonnes** (since 1 tonne ≈ 10 kN). The friction-hill effect contributes ~16% additional load over the ideal homogeneous case. To reduce this, a **graphite-based lubricant** (m ≈ 0.2) or **phosphate coating** (m ≈ 0.1) could be used.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3: Direct Extrusion — Extrusion Pressure and Force

### Problem Statement
A cylindrical billet of aluminum alloy with initial diameter $D_0 = 100 \text{ mm}$ and length $L_0 = 300 \text{ mm}$ is directly extruded at room temperature into a rod of diameter $D_f = 40 \text{ mm}$. The flow stress of the alloy follows the relation $\bar{\sigma} = 240 \epsilon^{0.25}$ (MPa). The friction factor at the billet-die interface is $m = 0.25$. Determine:
- (a) The extrusion ratio (R) and true strain.
- (b) The average flow stress at the entry of the die.
- (c) The extrusion pressure at the start of the operation.
- (d) The maximum ram force required.

### Given Data
$$D_0 = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad L_0 = 300 \text{ mm}, \quad D_f = 40 \text{ mm}, \quad m = 0.25$$
$$K = 240 \text{ MPa}, \quad n = 0.25$$

### Step 1: Calculate the Extrusion Ratio
$$R = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \left(\frac{D_0}{D_f}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{100}{40}\right)^2 = 2.5^2 = 6.25$$

### Step 2: Calculate the True Strain
$$\epsilon = \ln(R) = \ln(6.25) \approx 1.8326$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Average Flow Stress
$$Y_f = \frac{K \cdot \epsilon^n}{1 + n} = \frac{240 \times (1.8326)^{0.25}}{1 + 0.25}$$

Computing $(1.8326)^{0.25}$:
$$(1.8326)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \ln(1.8326)} = e^{0.25 \times 0.6058} = e^{0.1514} \approx 1.1635$$

Substituting:
$$Y_f = \frac{240 \times 1.1635}{1.25} = \frac{279.24}{1.25} \approx 223.4 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Extrusion Pressure at the Start
For direct extrusion, the friction-hill contribution is **maximum at the start** when the billet length is full (L = L_0). The standard extrusion pressure formula is:

$$p = Y_f \cdot \left[\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)^n \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot e^{2mL_0/D_0}\right]$$

Wait — let me use the more standard formulation that is typically expected in KTU exams. The widely-used formula is:

$$p = Y_f \cdot \left[\ln(R) + \frac{2mL_0}{D_0}\right]$$

This is a linearized approximation that combines the ideal work and friction terms. Using this:

Compute friction term:
$$\frac{2mL_0}{D_0} = \frac{2 \times 0.25 \times 300}{100} = \frac{150}{100} = 1.5$$

So:
$$p = 223.4 \times [\ln(6.25) + 1.5] = 223.4 \times [1.8326 + 1.5]$$

$$p = 223.4 \times 3.3326 \approx 744.5 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Maximum Ram Force
The initial billet cross-sectional area is:
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (100)^2 = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

The maximum ram force:
$$F_{\max} = p \cdot A_0 = 744.5 \times 10^6 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{\max} \approx 5{,}847{,}000 \text{ N} \approx 5847 \text{ kN} \approx 5.85 \text{ MN}$$

$$\boxed{p \approx 744.5 \text{ MPa}, \quad F_{\max} \approx 5.85 \text{ MN}}$$

### Engineering Interpretation
A ram force of **5.85 MN** is typical for a 6000-tonne industrial extrusion press. The friction term (1.5) actually **exceeds** the ideal work term (1.83) due to the long billet, indicating that **billet length optimization** and **effective lubrication** are critical. Note: The pressure decreases as the billet is consumed because L decreases (a key feature of direct extrusion known as the **pressure decay curve**).

---

## 3.4 Problem 4: Casting — Chvorinov's Rule and Solidification Time

### Problem Statement
A steel casting has the shape of a flat plate of dimensions $300 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm} \times 25 \text{ mm}$. The casting is poured in a green sand mold. The mold constant for this alloy-mold combination is $B = 4.5 \text{ min/cm}^2$. Estimate the solidification time for:
- (a) The casting as described.
- (b) A redesigned version that is a cylinder of diameter 100 mm and length 300 mm.

Compare and comment on the relative solidification times.

### Given Data
$$L = 300 \text{ mm}, \quad W = 200 \text{ mm}, \quad T = 25 \text{ mm}, \quad B = 4.5 \text{ min/cm}^2$$

### Step 1: Convert All Dimensions to Centimeters
$$L = 30 \text{ cm}, \quad W = 20 \text{ cm}, \quad T = 2.5 \text{ cm}$$

### Part (a): Solidification Time of the Plate

**Volume of the plate:**
$$V_{\text{plate}} = L \times W \times T = 30 \times 20 \times 2.5 = 1500 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Surface area of the plate** (assuming all 6 faces are exposed to the mold — top, bottom, and 4 sides):
$$A_{\text{plate}} = 2(LW + LT + WT) = 2(30 \times 20 + 30 \times 2.5 + 20 \times 2.5)$$
$$= 2(600 + 75 + 50) = 2(725) = 1450 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Modulus (V/A) of the plate:**
$$M_{\text{plate}} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{1500}{1450} \approx 1.0345 \text{ cm}$$

**Solidification time (Chvorinov's Rule):**
$$t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2 = 4.5 \times (1.0345)^2 = 4.5 \times 1.0702$$

$$\boxed{t_{s,\text{plate}} \approx 4.82 \text{ min}}$$

### Part (b): Solidification Time of the Cylinder

**Volume of the cylinder:**
$$V_{\text{cyl}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (10)^2 \times 30 = 750\pi \approx 2356.2 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Surface area of the cylinder** (2 circular ends + lateral surface):
$$A_{\text{cyl}} = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} + \pi D L = \frac{\pi (10)^2}{2} + \pi \times 10 \times 30$$
$$= 50\pi + 300\pi = 350\pi \approx 1099.6 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Modulus (V/A) of the cylinder:**
$$M_{\text{cyl}} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{2356.2}{1099.6} \approx 2.1429 \text{ cm}$$

**Solidification time:**
$$t_{s,\text{cyl}} = 4.5 \times (2.1429)^2 = 4.5 \times 4.5919$$

$$\boxed{t_{s,\text{cyl}} \approx 20.66 \text{ min}}$$

### Step 2: Comparison and Comment
The cylindrical casting takes **~4.3 times longer** to solidify than the plate, despite having a larger volume. This is because the **V/A modulus is ~2× larger** for the cylinder, and since solidification time is proportional to $(V/A)^2$, the time scales as a factor of 4.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** The **V/A modulus** is the most important design parameter in casting. To minimize shrinkage defects and porosity, foundries aim to:
> - Keep the V/A ratio **uniform** throughout the casting (avoid hot spots).
> - Place **risers** at locations of high V/A so they solidify **last** and feed liquid metal to the shrinking casting.
> - Use **chills** at locations of low V/A to accelerate their solidification.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Problem Type Classification

The table below provides a **strategic matrix** for selecting the right approach based on problem keywords, useful for KTU exam time management.

| **Problem Keywords** | **Process Identified** | **Primary Formula to Use** | **Common Pitfall** |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Roll radius, draft, separating force" | Rolling | $F = LwY_f(1 + mL/4h_f)$ | Forgetting the friction-hill term |
| "Upset forging, cylinder, height reduction" | Forging (Slab) | $p = Y_f(1 + 2mr/3h_f)$ | Confusing initial and final radius |
| "Extrusion ratio, billet length, ram force" | Extrusion | $p = Y_f[\ln(R) + 2mL/D_0]$ | Using final L instead of L_0 |
| "Drawing stress, die angle, back tension" | Wire Drawing | $\sigma_d$ formula (see table) | Forgetting back tension |
| "Solidification time, casting, V/A" | Casting (Chvorinov) | $t_s = B(V/A)^2$ | Including riser in V/A calculation |
| "Shrinkage allowance, dimension" | Pattern Design | $S_a = \alpha \Delta T L$ | Unit mismatch in α |
| "Forge energy, efficiency, ideal work" | Forging (Energy) | $W = Y_f V \epsilon / \eta$ | Confusing ideal work with actual work |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Numerical Problem Solving Algorithm

The diagram below maps the **decision-tree workflow** that a KTU student should follow when encountering any numerical problem in Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Problem Statement] --> B{Identify Process Type}
    B -->|Force / Pressure / Power| C[Forming Process: Rolling Forging Extrusion Drawing]
    B -->|Time / Shrinkage / Riser| D[Casting Process: Chvorinov Pattern]
    C --> E{Extract Given Data with Units}
    D --> E
    E --> F[Convert All Units to SI]
    F --> G{Is Friction Sticking or Sliding?}
    G -->|Sticking| H[Use Friction Factor m]
    G -->|Sliding| I[Use Coefficient mu]
    H --> J[Compute Geometric Terms: L, A, R, epsilon]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Average Flow Stress Yf]
    K --> L{Apply Governing Formula}
    L -->|Rolling| M1[Friction Hill Rolling Force]
    L -->|Forging| M2[Slab Method Forging Pressure]
    L -->|Extrusion| M3[Direct Extrusion Pressure]
    D --> N[Compute Volume and Surface Area]
    N --> O[Compute V over A Modulus]
    O --> P[Apply Chvorinov Rule]
    M1 --> Q[Calculate Torque and Power if asked]
    M2 --> R[Multiply by Area to get Force]
    M3 --> R
    Q --> S[Write Final Answer with Units]
    R --> S
    P --> S
    S --> T[Add Engineering Interpretation]
    T --> U[End: Final Solution]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force/Pressure Pathways in Bulk Forming

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Material Properties] -->|K, n| B1[Flow Stress Curve]
    A2[Process Geometry] -->|h0, hf, L, D| B2[Geometric Parameters]
    A3[Friction Conditions] -->|m or mu| B3[Friction Shear]
    B1 --> C[Governing Equation]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D1[Rolling Force]
    C --> D2[Forging Pressure]
    C --> D3[Extrusion Pressure]
    D1 --> E1[Torque and Power]
    D2 --> E2[Forging Force = p x A]
    D3 --> E2
    E1 --> F[Press Sizing]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Equipment Selection]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Slab vs. Energy Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Forging Problem] --> Q1{Is geometry simple and uniform?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, e.g., cylinder| Slab[Use Slab Method]
    Q1 -->|No, complex shape| Energy[Use Upper Bound Energy Method]
    Slab --> S1[Compute Average Flow Stress Yf]
    Slab --> S2[Apply p equals Yf times 1 plus 2mr over 3hf]
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3[Multiply by Area for Force]
    Energy --> E1[Compute Homogeneous Strain]
    Energy --> E2[Compute Ideal Work Wi equals Yf V epsilon]
    E1 --> E2
    Energy --> E3[Divide by Efficiency eta]
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> S3
    S3 --> Final[Compare F values to cross-check]
```
