# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "friction hill" as applicable to metal forming processes. Why does it occur?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **friction hill** is the phenomenon in which the pressure required to deform a metal workpiece increases substantially above the material's flow stress due to frictional resistance at the die-workpiece interface. It occurs primarily in bulk forming processes such as **forging, extrusion, and rolling**, where the deforming material is in **extensive contact** with the die surface.
>
> The pressure required is mathematically given by $p = Y_f \cdot (1 + \text{friction term})$, where the friction term is a function of the friction factor (m) and the geometric ratio of the workpiece (L/h or r/h).
>
> **(1 mark)** Correct definition; **(1 mark)** mention of die-workpiece contact; **(1 mark)** identifying it as a multiplicative increase over flow stress.

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Chvorinov's rule for solidification of a casting. What is the significance of the V/A ratio?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Chvorinov's rule states that the **solidification time ($t_s$)** of a casting is proportional to the **square of the V/A ratio** (volume-to-surface-area ratio), and is given by:
> $$t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$$
> where **B** is the **mold constant** that depends on the metal, mold material, and pouring conditions.
>
> The **V/A ratio (modulus)** is significant because:
> - It determines the relative solidification rates of different sections of a casting.
> - Sections with **higher V/A solidify last** and act as potential **risers** to feed liquid metal to feeding sections.
> - It is the **key design parameter** for riser placement, chill location, and gating design.
>
> **(1 mark)** Correct formula; **(1 mark)** explanation of B; **(1 mark)** significance in casting design.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**(a)** A cylindrical billet of diameter 80 mm and height 100 mm is upset forged in a press to a final height of 60 mm at room temperature. The strength coefficient is $K = 410 \text{ MPa}$ and strain-hardening exponent is $n = 0.25$. The friction factor is $m = 0.3$. Determine:
- (i) The true strain in the operation.
- (ii) The average flow stress.
- (iii) The maximum forging pressure and maximum forging force.

**(b)** A steel strip with width 250 mm is rolled from 20 mm to 15 mm thickness. The roll diameter is 400 mm. The material has $K = 500 \text{ MPa}$ and $n = 0.20$. The coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.1$ and the rolls rotate at 30 rpm. Determine:
- (i) The roll separating force.
- (ii) The torque and power required.

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Forging Solution

**Step 1: True Strain**
$$\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{100}{60}\right) = \ln(1.6667) \approx 0.5108$$

**Step 2: Final Diameter (Volume Constancy)**
$$d_0^2 h_0 = d_f^2 h_f \Rightarrow 80^2 \times 100 = d_f^2 \times 60$$
$$d_f^2 = \frac{640000}{60} = 10666.67 \Rightarrow d_f \approx 103.28 \text{ mm}$$
$$r_f = 51.64 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Average Flow Stress**
$$Y_f = \frac{K \epsilon^n}{1+n} = \frac{410 \times (0.5108)^{0.25}}{1.25}$$

$(0.5108)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \times \ln(0.5108)} = e^{0.25 \times (-0.6717)} = e^{-0.1679} \approx 0.8455$

$$Y_f = \frac{410 \times 0.8455}{1.25} = \frac{346.66}{1.25} \approx 277.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Forging Pressure**
$$p_{\max} = Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2mr_f}{3h_f}\right) = 277.3 \times \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 0.3 \times 51.64}{3 \times 60}\right)$$
$$= 277.3 \times \left(1 + \frac{30.984}{180}\right) = 277.3 \times 1.1721 \approx 325.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Forging Force**
$$A_f = \pi r_f^2 = \pi \times (51.64)^2 \approx 8377.7 \text{ mm}^2 = 8.378 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$F_{\max} = p_{\max} \cdot A_f = 325.0 \times 10^6 \times 8.378 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2{,}722{,}850 \text{ N} \approx 2.72 \text{ MN}$$

#### Part (b) — Rolling Solution

**Step 1: Geometric Parameters**
$$\Delta h = 20 - 15 = 5 \text{ mm}, \quad L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{200 \times 5} = \sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: True Strain and Flow Stress**
$$\epsilon = \ln(20/15) = \ln(1.333) \approx 0.2877$$
$$Y_f = \frac{500 \times (0.2877)^{0.20}}{1.20}$$

$(0.2877)^{0.20} = e^{0.20 \times \ln(0.2877)} = e^{0.20 \times (-1.2457)} = e^{-0.2491} \approx 0.7795$

$$Y_f = \frac{500 \times 0.7795}{1.20} = \frac{389.75}{1.20} \approx 324.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Check Sticking vs. Sliding Friction**
$$\mu p_{\text{avg}} \text{ vs } \frac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}} \text{ (Tresca)}$$

A simpler check: $\mu \cdot L / h_f = 0.1 \times 31.62 / 15 = 0.211 < 1$, so sliding friction is applicable.

For sliding friction, the rolling force formula is:
$$F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right]$$

Wait — correction: The general formula depends on whether sticking or sliding friction prevails. The conventional formula for rolling is:

$$F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right) \quad \text{(sliding)}$$

OR

$$F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{m L}{4 h_f}\right) \quad \text{(sticking)}$$

For sliding friction:
$$1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f} = 1 + \frac{0.1 \times 31.62}{2 \times 15} = 1 + \frac{3.162}{30} = 1 + 0.1054 = 1.1054$$

**Step 4: Roll Separating Force**
$$F = 31.62 \times 250 \times 324.8 \times 1.1054$$
$$F = 7905 \times 324.8 \times 1.1054 \approx 2{,}567{,}400 \times 1.1054 \approx 2{,}838{,}100 \text{ N}$$
$$F \approx 2838 \text{ kN} \approx 2.84 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 5: Torque per Roll**
$$T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 2{,}838{,}100 \times 0.03162 \approx 44{,}870 \text{ N·m} \approx 44.9 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 6: Power (Both Rolls)**
$$N = 30 \text{ rpm} = 0.5 \text{ rev/s}$$
$$P = 2 \times 2\pi N \cdot T = 4\pi \times 0.5 \times 44{,}870 = 2\pi \times 44{,}870 \approx 281{,}900 \text{ W} \approx 281.9 \text{ kW}$$

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**(a)** A direct extrusion operation is performed on a billet of initial diameter 75 mm and length 250 mm to produce a rod of diameter 30 mm. The flow stress of the material follows $\bar{\sigma} = 350 \epsilon^{0.15}$ MPa. The friction factor is $m = 0.3$. Calculate:
- (i) The extrusion ratio and the true strain.
- (ii) The maximum extrusion pressure (at the start of the stroke).
- (iii) The maximum ram force required.

**(b)** Two castings are to be produced — one is a sphere of diameter 200 mm, and the other is a cube of side 200 mm. Using Chvorinov's rule with mold constant $B = 3.5 \text{ min/cm}^2$, determine the solidification time for each. State which design is better from a casting perspective and justify.

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Extrusion Solution

**Step 1: Extrusion Ratio and True Strain**
$$R = \left(\frac{D_0}{D_f}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{75}{30}\right)^2 = 2.5^2 = 6.25$$
$$\epsilon = \ln(R) = \ln(6.25) \approx 1.8326$$

**Step 2: Average Flow Stress**
$$Y_f = \frac{K \epsilon^n}{1+n} = \frac{350 \times (1.8326)^{0.15}}{1.15}$$

$(1.8326)^{0.15} = e^{0.15 \times \ln(1.8326)} = e^{0.15 \times 0.6058} = e^{0.0909} \approx 1.0951$

$$Y_f = \frac{350 \times 1.0951}{1.15} = \frac{383.29}{1.15} \approx 333.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Extrusion Pressure (Start of Stroke, L = L_0)**
$$p_{\max} = Y_f \cdot \left[\ln(R) + \frac{2m L_0}{D_0}\right] = 333.3 \times \left[1.8326 + \frac{2 \times 0.3 \times 250}{75}\right]$$
$$= 333.3 \times \left[1.8326 + 2.0\right] = 333.3 \times 3.8326 \approx 1277.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Maximum Ram Force**
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 75^2 = 4417.86 \text{ mm}^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$F_{\max} = p_{\max} \cdot A_0 = 1277.4 \times 10^6 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \approx 5{,}643{,}500 \text{ N} \approx 5.64 \text{ MN}$$

#### Part (b) — Chvorinov's Rule Comparison

**Convert dimensions to cm:** All dimensions = 20 cm.

**For the Sphere:**
- Volume: $V_s = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi (10)^3 = 4188.79 \text{ cm}^3$
- Surface Area: $A_s = 4\pi r^2 = 4\pi (10)^2 = 1256.64 \text{ cm}^2$
- Modulus: $M_s = V_s/A_s = 4188.79/1256.64 = 3.333 \text{ cm}$
- Solidification time: $t_s = 3.5 \times (3.333)^2 = 3.5 \times 11.111 = 38.89 \text{ min}$

**For the Cube:**
- Volume: $V_c = 20^3 = 8000 \text{ cm}^3$
- Surface Area: $A_c = 6 \times 20^2 = 2400 \text{ cm}^2$
- Modulus: $M_c = V_c/A_c = 8000/2400 = 3.333 \text{ cm}$
- Solidification time: $t_c = 3.5 \times (3.333)^2 = 38.89 \text{ min}$

> **Comment:** Both the sphere and cube of the same characteristic dimension have **identical** V/A modulus (= R/3 or a/6 respectively) and hence the same solidification time. From a casting perspective, the **sphere is preferred** because:
> - It has **uniform wall thickness** (no corners or edges), which avoids hot spots.
> - It exhibits **isotropic shrinkage**, minimizing residual stresses and distortion.
> - It has a **lower surface area-to-volume variability**, ensuring uniform solidification.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Module 4 Numerical Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (Loses 1–2 marks):** The single most common error. Forgetting to convert mm to m before computing force. Example: Computing $F = p \cdot A$ in mixed units (MPa × mm²) gives wrong orders of magnitude. **Always convert area to m² and pressure to Pa.**
>
> 2. **Final vs. Initial Geometry Confusion (Loses 1–2 marks):** In forging and extrusion, students often use the **initial** radius $r_0$ instead of the **final** radius $r_f$ in the friction-hill term. Remember: friction force depends on the **contact area at the end of the stroke**.
>
> 3. **Sticking vs. Sliding Friction Mix-Up (Loses 1–2 marks):** The friction-hill term in rolling is $\frac{mL}{4h_f}$ (sticking) or $\frac{\mu L}{2h_f}$ (sliding). Wrong coefficient = wrong answer.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Average Flow Stress Formula (Loses 2 marks):** A student who uses $Y_f = K\epsilon^n$ instead of $\frac{K\epsilon^n}{1+n}$ will over-estimate forces by 10–25%.
>
> 5. **Chvorinov's Rule — Including the Riser (Loses 1 mark):** The V/A for the casting **excludes the riser**, because the riser is intentionally designed to solidify last.
>
> 6. **No Engineering Interpretation (Loses 1 mark):** KTU examiners often award a final 1 mark for a meaningful engineering comment (e.g., "The 5.85 MN ram force requires a 600-tonne press; the friction term exceeds the ideal work, so billet shortening is recommended").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 Numerical Problems:**

### **Process Identification & Formula Selection:**
- **Rolling** → $F = LwY_f(1 + \text{friction term})$; $T = 0.5FL$; $P = 2\pi N T \cdot 2$
- **Forging (Slab)** → $p = Y_f(1 + 2mr_f/3h_f)$ for cylinder; $F = p \cdot A_f$
- **Extrusion (Direct)** → $p = Y_f[\ln R + 2mL_0/D_0]$; maximum at start
- **Casting** → $t_s = B(V/A)^2$; sphere is best shape; riser has highest V/A

### **Critical Constants & Formulas:**
- **Average Flow Stress:** $Y_f = \dfrac{K\epsilon^n}{1+n}$ (NOT just $K\epsilon^n$)
- **True Strain (volume constancy):** $\epsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f) = \ln(A_f/A_0) = \ln(R)$
- **Contact length in rolling:** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$
- **Sticking friction check:** $\mu \cdot p \geq Y_f/\sqrt{3}$ (Tresca) → use m; else use μ

### **Exam Strategy:**
1. **Always extract** given data in a labeled box at the start of the answer.
2. **Convert** all dimensions to consistent SI units (m, Pa, N).
3. **State** the formula with all variables defined (1 mark guaranteed).
4. **Show step-by-step substitution** with intermediate values.
5. **End** with a 1–2 line engineering interpretation (extra 1 mark).
6. **For Part (a) + (b) 14-mark questions**, budget ~7 marks per sub-part.

### **Numerical Sanity Checks:**
- Forging force: typically **1–10 MN** for industrial parts.
- Rolling force: typically **1–20 MN** per stand.
- Extrusion pressure: typically **500–1500 MPa** for cold extrusion of aluminum.
- Solidification time: typically **5–60 minutes** for sand castings.

### **Common Physical Constants to Remember:**
- **1 tonne force ≈ 10 kN** (for press tonnage conversion)
- **1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa**
- **Density of steel:** ~7850 kg/m³
- **Density of aluminum:** ~2700 kg/m³

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drawing Defects - Deep Drawing. Bending - Details of Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drawing Defects - Deep Drawing. Bending - Details of Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Deep Drawing — Formal Definition

**Deep Drawing** is a sheet metal forming process in which a flat sheet metal blank (called a *blank* or *slug*) is forced by a punch into a die cavity to produce a hollow, cup-shaped or box-shaped part of a depth greater than its diameter (or width). The metal undergoes plastic deformation primarily in the **flange region**, where the blank is drawn radially inward over the die entry radius to become the wall of the cup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Deep drawing is distinguished from **shallow drawing** (or *cupping*) by the depth-to-diameter ratio. If depth ≥ diameter, it is **deep drawing**; if depth < diameter, it is **shallow drawing/cupping**.

**Key Process Parameters:**
- **Drawing Ratio (DR)** = $D/d$ (Blank diameter / Punch diameter)
- **Reduction (r)** = $\frac{D - d}{D} \times 100\%$
- **Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR)** = Maximum $D/d$ that can be drawn in one operation without failure
- **Drawing Force (F)** = Force required by the punch to perform the draw
- **Blank-Holding Force (BHF)** = Force applied by the blank holder to prevent wrinkling

---

## 1.2 Drawing Defects — Formal Definition

**Drawing Defects** are the undesirable irregularities, geometric distortions, or failures that occur in a deep drawn product due to improper process parameters, unsuitable material properties, or faulty die design. KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mandates the study of the following defects:

| # | Defect | Brief Description |
|---|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Wrinkling** | Buckling/waving of the flange |
| 2 | **Tearing / Fracture** | Rupture of the cup wall (typically near punch nose) |
| 3 | **Orange Peel** | Pebbled surface texture |
| 4 | **Earing** | Uneven cup rim with wavy (scalloped) edge |
| 5 | **Fish-tailing / Tailing** | Wavy bottom edge |
| 6 | **Stretcher Strains (Lüders bands)** | Localized rough bands on the surface |
| 7 | **Thickness Variation** | Non-uniform wall thickness |
| 8 | **Bottom Cracking** | Crack at the punch nose radius |
| 9 | **Die Lines / Scoring** | Scratches from die/punch contact |

---

## 1.3 Bending — Formal Definition

**Bending** is a sheet metal forming process in which a straight metal sheet is plastically deformed about a linear axis to obtain a curved, angular, or curved surface shape. The neutral axis is displaced toward the inside of the bend, and the outer fibers undergo tension while inner fibers undergo compression.

**Common Bending Operations:**
- **V-Bending** — Die and punch form a V-groove
- **U-Bending** — Forms a U-channel
- **Roll Bending** — Three rolls form a cylindrical curve
- **Air Bending / Coining** — Partial vs. full punch contact
- **Hemming / Flanging** — Folding the edge over

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept — Springback:** When the bending load is released, the elastic portion of the strain is recovered, causing the bend angle to *open up* slightly. This is called **springback** and is a critical defect in bending.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogies for Students

### 🍕 Deep Drawing Analogy
> *"Imagine pushing a flat pizza dough into a small cup using a small cylindrical block. The dough stretches down and forms the cup walls. If you push too hard, the wall tears. If you don't hold the rim, the rim buckles into waves (wrinkles). If the dough is too thick, its surface looks rough (orange peel)."*

### 📄 Bending Analogy
> *"Take a thick paperback book and bend its cover. The outer cover stretches (tension), the inner cover squeezes (compression), and the middle pages barely move (neutral axis). When you let go, the cover doesn't stay perfectly bent — it springs back a little. That's springback."*

---

## 1.5 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR) for steel:** $LDR \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$
- **Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR) for aluminum:** $LDR \approx 1.8$ to $2.0$
- **Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR) for brass:** $LDR \approx 2.1$ to $2.4$
- **Maximum Reduction per draw (steel):** ~**40% to 45%**
- **Maximum Reduction per draw (aluminum):** ~**35% to 40%**
- **Normal Anisotropy value (R̄) for good drawability:** $R̄ > 1.0$ (ideally 1.5–2.0)
- **Springback angle (mild steel, 90° bend):** ~**2° to 4°**
- **Minimum Bend Radius (MBR):** $R_{min} \approx t \times (50\% \text{ elongation})$ for ductile materials
- **Strain Hardening Exponent (n):** For good formability, $n = 0.20$ to $0.30$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** A higher **n-value** (strain hardening exponent) and higher **R̄-value** (normal anisotropy) indicate **superior drawability**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Analysis of Deep Drawing Process

### Step-by-Step Mechanics of the Deep Draw

1. **Blank Placement:** A flat circular blank is placed over the die cavity, resting on the **blank holder**.
2. **Blank Holder Closure:** The blank holder applies a controlled **Blank Holding Force (BHF)** to grip the flange.
3. **Punch Descent:** The punch moves downward, contacting the center of the blank.
4. **Flange Drawing:** The flange (outer ring) is drawn radially inward over the **die entry radius (rd)**. This is the **primary deformation zone** — metal flows plastically.
5. **Wall Formation:** The drawn material wraps around the punch nose, becoming the vertical **cup wall**.
6. **Bottom Formation:** The original center of the blank becomes the **cup bottom** (under the punch), undergoing little deformation.
7. **Bottom Corner Bending:** Material bends over the punch nose radius ($r_p$) — this is the **secondary deformation zone**.
8. **Ejection:** Punch withdraws; blank holder releases.

### Why Defects Occur — The "Why" Behind Each Failure

| Defect | Root Cause ("Why") | Mechanism ("How") |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Wrinkling** | Insufficient BHF | Flange is too "loose" — compressive hoop stress causes buckling |
| **Tearing** | Excessive drawing force OR excessive BHF | Wall region exceeds tensile strength |
| **Orange Peel** | Coarse grain size relative to sheet thickness | Individual grains deform differently, creating surface roughness |
| **Earing** | Anisotropy (planar variation in properties) | Directional R-values in rolling plane cause uneven rim heights |
| **Stretcher Strains** | Yield-point elongation phenomenon | Localized discontinuous yielding in low-carbon steel |
| **Bottom Cracking** | Sharp punch nose radius | Excessive thinning at bend-over radius |

---

## 2.2 Detailed Study of Each Drawing Defect

### 🔴 Defect 1: Wrinkling
- **Location:** Flange region
- **Cause:** Circumferential compressive stress ($\sigma_\theta$) > critical buckling stress
- **Prevention:**
  - Apply adequate **Blank Holding Force (BHF)**
  - Use **bead** (draw beads) on blank holder
  - Increase die entry radius
  - Use multiple draws with intermediate annealing

### 🔴 Defect 2: Tearing / Wall Fracture
- **Location:** Lower wall (near punch nose radius)
- **Cause:** Tensile stress in wall > tensile strength of material (post work-hardening)
- **Prevention:**
  - Reduce drawing ratio per stage
  - Reduce BHF slightly
  - Increase punch nose radius
  - Use a **draw redraw** operation

### 🔴 Defect 3: Orange Peel
- **Location:** Entire surface (visible after forming)
- **Cause:** Grain size $d_g > $ some multiple of sheet thickness
- **Rule of thumb:** If **average grain diameter** > $\frac{t}{10}$ (where $t$ = sheet thickness), orange peel becomes visible
- **Prevention:** Use fine-grained material

### 🔴 Defect 4: Earing
- **Location:** Top rim of the cup
- **Cause:** **Planar anisotropy** ($\Delta R$) — R-value varies with direction in the rolling plane
- **Measurement:** $\Delta R = \frac{R_{max} - R_{min}}{2}$
- **Prevention:** Use material with low $\Delta R$ (isotropic in plane)

### 🔴 Defect 5: Fish-tailing
- **Cause:** Combination of flange thinning at edges and metal flow
- **Prevention:** Proper blank design (using *slippery* die profile)

### 🔴 Defect 6: Stretcher Strains (Lüders Bands)
- **Cause:** Discontinuous yielding in low-carbon steel; carbon content 0.05–0.15%
- **Prevention:** Use **temper-rolled** (skin-rolled) sheet; add aluminum killing

---

## 2.3 Analysis of Bending Process

### Step-by-Step Mechanics of Bending

1. **Positioning:** Sheet is placed over the die opening.
2. **Punch Descent:** Punch pushes the sheet into the V-groove.
3. **Elastic-Plastic Deformation:** Inner fibers compress; outer fibers stretch; neutral axis (N.A.) shifts toward inside.
4. **Plastic Flow:** Material flows plastically beyond the elastic limit.
5. **Springback:** Upon punch release, the elastic strain recovers, and the bend angle **opens up** by an amount $\Delta\theta$.

### Neutral Axis Shift — The Heart of Bending

In pure elastic bending, the neutral axis is at the **geometric center** of the cross-section. In **plastic bending**, the neutral axis shifts **inward (toward the inside of the bend)** because the inner fibers have less area to resist compressive stress after yielding.

$$K = \frac{\text{Location of neutral axis from inner surface}}{t}$$

- For $R/t > 10$: $K \approx 0.5$ (elastic)
- For $R/t < 5$: $K \approx 0.33$ to $0.45$ (plastic)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD for KTU 2024 ESE — memorize these.**

### 📋 Deep Drawing Formulas

| # | Parameter | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Drawing Ratio** | $DR = D/d$ | $D$ = blank diameter, $d$ = punch diameter |
| 2 | **Reduction %** | $r = \dfrac{D - d}{D} \times 100$ | Maximum ~45% per draw for steel |
| 3 | **LDR for first draw** | $LDR = 1 + \eta$ | $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 4 | **Drawing Force (E.g. Sieber's)** | $F_d = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{uts} \cdot (DR - 0.5)$ | Simplified empirical |
| 5 | **Drawing Force (Alt)** | $F_d = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{draw}$ | $\sigma_{draw} \approx (0.7 \text{ to } 1.0) \times \sigma_{uts}$ |
| 6 | **Blank Holding Force** | $BHF = 0.015 \times \sigma_{uts} \times \pi (D^2 - d_p^2)/4$ | Empirical (Sachs) |
| 7 | **BHF (Eigen)** | $BHF = 0.002 \times \pi (D^2 - d^2) \times (t + 0.005 D) \times \sigma_{uts}$ | For aluminum |
| 8 | **Total Straining Force (redraw)** | $F_t = F_d + F_b + F_f$ | $F_b$ = bending, $F_f$ = friction |
| 9 | **Earing height** | $\Delta h \propto \vert \Delta R \vert$ | Proportional to planar anisotropy |
| 10 | **Cup wall thickness** | $t_f = t_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon_r}$ | Exponential thinning |

### 📋 Bending Formulas

| # | Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | **Bend Allowance (BA)** | $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot (R + K \cdot t) \cdot \theta$ | Length of neutral axis in bend |
| 2 | **Bend Deduction (BD)** | $BD = 2(R + t) - BA$ | Used in flat-pattern layout |
| 3 | **Springback angle** | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t)}$ | Simplified Sachs |
| 4 | **Springback (Alt)** | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{M_p \cdot L}{E \cdot I} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ | $M_p$ = plastic moment, $I$ = moment of inertia |
| 5 | **Minimum Bend Radius** | $R_{min} = t \cdot (50\% \text{ elongation} - \text{ offset})$ | For ductile sheets |
| 6 | **Bending Force (V-die)** | $F_b = \dfrac{K_{bf} \cdot L \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_{uts}}{W}$ | $W$ = die width, $L$ = bend length |
| 7 | **Bending Force (coining)** | $F_b = L \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_{uts}$ | Approximation |
| 8 | **Neutral axis factor (K)** | $K = 0.33 + 0.03 \cdot (R/t) + 0.001 \cdot (R/t)^2$ | Empirical (Wood) |
| 9 | **Inside bend radius / Outside** | $R_i = R - t/2$, $R_o = R + t/2$ | Geometry |
| 10 | **Tensile strain at outer fiber** | $\varepsilon_t = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2R/t}$ | True strain |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL: When writing formulas in markdown tables, NEVER use `|`. Use `\vert` instead to avoid breaking the table syntax.**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Deep drawing** is used to manufacture: beverage cans, kitchen sinks, automotive fuel tanks, oil pans, aerosol cans, cookware, battery cases.
- **Bending** is used for: structural frames, brackets, chassis components, HVAC ducts, electrical enclosures, ship hulls, aircraft skins.
- Defect analysis is critical in **quality control** of press shop operations and **die design** in automotive industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR)

### Theoretical Basis

Consider a circular blank of diameter $D$ being drawn into a cup of diameter $d$. In the flange region, an element of metal is subjected to:
- **Radial tensile stress** $\sigma_r$ (drawing direction)
- **Circumferential (hoop) compressive stress** $\sigma_\theta$

Using **Hill's anisotropic yield criterion** for sheet metal:

$$\sigma_r^2 + \sigma_\theta^2 - \left(\dfrac{2R}{1+R}\right) \sigma_r \sigma_\theta = \sigma_y^2$$

For a thin flange in the plastic state, equilibrium gives:

$$\dfrac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \dfrac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0$$

With the yield condition and boundary conditions:
- At outer edge ($r = D/2$): $\sigma_r = 0$
- At inner edge ($r = d/2$): $\sigma_r = \sigma_{wall}$ (drawing stress in wall)

Solving and integrating:

$$\sigma_{wall} = \sigma_y \cdot \left[ \left(1 + \dfrac{R}{1+R}\right) \cdot \ln(DR) \right]^{1/2}$$

For **isotropic material** ($R = 1$):

$$\sigma_{wall} = \sigma_y \cdot \left[ 1.5 \cdot \ln(DR) \right]^{1/2}$$

### Limiting Condition

Failure occurs when $\sigma_{wall}$ reaches the **ultimate tensile strength** (post-work-hardening, $\sigma_{uts,eff}$). Therefore, **LDR** is obtained when:

$$\sigma_{uts,eff} = \sigma_y \cdot \sqrt{1.5 \cdot \ln(LDR)}$$

Solving for LDR:

$$\boxed{\ln(LDR) = \dfrac{1}{1.5} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\sigma_{uts,eff}}{\sigma_y}\right)^2}$$

$$LDR = \exp\left[\dfrac{1}{1.5} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\sigma_{uts,eff}}{\sigma_y}\right)^2\right]$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Deep Drawing

**Problem:** A cylindrical cup is to be drawn from a circular blank of **mild steel sheet**.

**Given:**
- Sheet thickness, $t_0 = 1.2$ mm
- Cup diameter, $d = 80$ mm
- Ultimate tensile strength, $\sigma_{uts} = 400$ MPa
- Drawing stress, $\sigma_d = 0.75 \times \sigma_{uts} = 300$ MPa
- Fudge factor for bending + friction, $K = 1.3$

**Find:** (a) Maximum blank diameter $D_{max}$ (assume LDR = 2.0); (b) Drawing force $F_d$; (c) Blank holding force $BHF$.

### Solution

#### Part (a): Maximum Blank Diameter

$$D_{max} = LDR \times d = 2.0 \times 80 = 160 \text{ mm}$$

**Actual reduction:**
$$r = \dfrac{D - d}{D} \times 100 = \dfrac{160 - 80}{160} \times 100 = 50\%$$

Since 50% > 45% (limit for one draw in steel), we need a **two-stage draw**.

**For two stages** (using 40% reduction each):
- After stage 1: $D_1 = 80 / (1 - 0.4) = 80 / 0.6 = 133.3$ mm
- After stage 2: $D_2 = 80$ mm ✓

**Final blank diameter = 133.3 mm** (with intermediate annealing between stages).

#### Part (b): Drawing Force

Using simplified formula:
$$F_d = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{draw} \cdot (DR - 0.5)$$

$$F_d = \pi \times 80 \times 1.2 \times 300 \times (133.3/80 - 0.5)$$

$$F_d = \pi \times 80 \times 1.2 \times 300 \times (1.666 - 0.5)$$

$$F_d = \pi \times 80 \times 1.2 \times 300 \times 1.166$$

Calculating step by step:
- $80 \times 1.2 = 96$
- $96 \times 300 = 28{,}800$
- $28{,}800 \times 1.166 = 33{,}580.8$
- $33{,}580.8 \times \pi = 33{,}580.8 \times 3.1416 \approx 105{,}490$ N

$$\boxed{F_d \approx 105.5 \text{ kN}}$$

#### Part (c): Blank Holding Force

Using Sachs formula:
$$BHF = 0.015 \times \sigma_{uts} \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$$

$$BHF = 0.015 \times 400 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}(133.3^2 - 80^2)$$

Step by step:
- $133.3^2 = 17{,}768.89$
- $80^2 = 6{,}400$
- $17{,}768.89 - 6{,}400 = 11{,}368.89$
- $\dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 11{,}368.89 = 0.7854 \times 11{,}368.89 = 8{,}929.1$ mm²
- $0.015 \times 400 = 6$
- $6 \times 8{,}929.1 = 53{,}574.5$ N

$$\boxed{BHF \approx 53.6 \text{ kN}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Springback Angle

### Setup

A sheet of thickness $t$ is bent over a die of radius $R$. The outer fiber strain is:
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{2R/t + 1}$$

When the moment is released, the elastic portion recovers. The springback angle is given by the ratio of elastic curvature change to plastic curvature.

### Sachs Springback Formula

$$\dfrac{R'}{R} = 1 - 3 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{R}{t} \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{R}{t}\right)^2\right]$$

For small strains, simplified:
$$\Delta\theta = \theta_{\text{desired}} - \theta_{\text{actual after springback}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180^\circ}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t)}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example 2 — Bending

**Problem:** A **2 mm** thick **mild steel** sheet is bent to a **90° included angle** over a die radius of **6 mm**.

**Given:**
- $t = 2$ mm
- $R = 6$ mm
- $\theta_{\text{desired}} = 90°$
- $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa
- $E = 200$ GPa = 200,000 MPa

**Find:** (a) Springback angle; (b) Required punch angle to achieve 90° final bend.

### Solution

#### Part (a): Springback

$$\dfrac{R}{t} = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3$$

$$\Delta\theta = \dfrac{250}{200{,}000} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3}$$

Step by step:
- $\dfrac{250}{200{,}000} = 0.00125$
- $\dfrac{180}{\pi} = 57.2958$
- $0.00125 \times 57.2958 = 0.07162$
- $0.07162 / 3 = 0.02387°$

$$\boxed{\Delta\theta \approx 0.024° \text{ (negligible)}}$$

**Note:** With $R/t = 3$ and mild steel, springback is minimal. For **aluminum** or **spring steel**, springback is much larger (5°–10°).

#### Part (b): Punch Angle

$$\theta_{punch} = 90° - 0.024° \approx 89.98°$$

**Interpretation:** Springback is small here because $R/t$ is small (sharp bend = more plastic deformation). For larger $R/t$ (gentle bend), springback can be **2° to 10°**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Bend Allowance

**Problem:** A sheet metal part requires a **bend of 60°** with an inside radius of **8 mm** and a sheet thickness of **3 mm**. Find the **Bend Allowance (BA)**.

**Given:**
- $\theta = 60°$
- $R_i = 8$ mm (inside radius)
- $t = 3$ mm
- Assume $K = 0.5$ (since $R/t = 8/3 \approx 2.67$, plastic)

**Find:** Bend Allowance

### Solution

Inside radius $R = R_i + t/2 = 8 + 1.5 = 9.5$ mm (radius to neutral axis assuming $K=0.5$).

$$BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot (R + K \cdot t) \cdot \theta$$

$$BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot (8 + 0.5 \times 3) \cdot 60$$

Wait — the formula uses $(R + K \cdot t)$ where $R$ is the **inside bend radius** and $K \cdot t$ is the **shift of neutral axis from inside surface**.

$$BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot (R_i + K \cdot t) \cdot \theta$$

$$BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot (8 + 0.5 \times 3) \cdot 60$$

$$BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot (8 + 1.5) \cdot 60$$

$$BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot 9.5 \cdot 60$$

Step by step:
- $\dfrac{\pi}{180} = 0.01745$
- $9.5 \times 60 = 570$
- $570 \times 0.01745 = 9.9465$

$$\boxed{BA \approx 9.95 \text{ mm}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Situation | Recommended Approach | Reason |
|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| Single draw, mild steel | LDR ≈ 2.0, single stage | Standard material |
| Deep cup (depth > dia) | Multi-stage with annealing | Avoid wall fracture |
| Soft material (Al, Cu) | Higher BHF fraction | Lower buckling resistance |
| Sharp bend (R < 2t) | Coining, minimal springback | Plastic bending dominant |
| Gentle bend (R > 10t) | Air bending, account for springback | Elastic recovery significant |
| High-strength steel | Larger bend radius needed | Reduced ductility |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATS

## 4.1 Deep Drawing Process — Block Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Step 1: Flat Circular Blank] --> stepB[Step 2: Position on Die Cavity]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Blank Holder Closes with BHF]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Punch Descends]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Flange Draws Over Die Entry Radius]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Cup Wall Forms Around Punch]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Bottom Forms at Punch Face]
    stepG --> stepH{Step 8: Quality Check}
    stepH -->|Pass| stepI[Step 9: Eject Finished Cup]
    stepH -->|Wrinkling| stepJ[Defect: Increase BHF, Add Beads]
    stepH -->|Tearing| stepK[Defect: Reduce DR, Multi-stage Draw]
    stepH -->|Earing| stepL[Defect: Use Low Delta-R Material]
    stepH -->|Orange Peel| stepM[Defect: Use Fine-Grain Sheet]
    stepJ --> stepH
    stepK --> stepH
    stepL --> stepH
    stepM --> stepH
    stepI --> finishA[Finished Deep Drawn Part]
```

## 4.2 Deep Drawing Defect Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Deep Drawing Defects] --> branch1[Flange Defects]
    rootA --> branch2[Wall Defects]
    rootA --> branch3[Bottom Defects]
    rootA --> branch4[Rim Defects]
    rootA --> branch5[Surface Defects]

    branch1 --> def1A[Wrinkling]
    branch1 --> def1B[Flange Thinning]
    
    branch2 --> def2A[Wall Tearing]
    branch2 --> def2B[Wall Thinning]
    branch2 --> def2C[Bottom Corner Crack]
    
    branch3 --> def3A[Bottom Cracking]
    branch3 --> def3B[Center Burst]
    
    branch4 --> def4A[Earing]
    branch4 --> def4B[Fish Tailing]
    branch4 --> def4C[Uneven Height]
    
    branch5 --> def5A[Orange Peel]
    branch5 --> def5B[Stretcher Strains]
    branch5 --> def5C[Die Lines]
```

## 4.3 Bending Process — Block Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB[Step 1: Flat Sheet Placement] --> bend1[Step 2: Sheet Sits on V-Die]
    bend1 --> bend2[Step 3: Punch Contacts Sheet]
    bend2 --> bend3[Step 4: Elastic Deformation Begins]
    bend3 --> bend4[Step 5: Plastic Deformation at Bend Line]
    bend4 --> bend6[Step 6: Outer Fibers Stretch, Inner Compress]
    bend6 --> bend7[Step 7: Neutral Axis Shifts Inward]
    bend7 --> bend8[Step 8: Punch Reaches Bottom of Stroke]
    bend8 --> bend9[Step 9: Punch Retracts]
    bend9 --> bend10[Step 10: Springback Occurs]
    bend10 --> bend11[Step 11: Final Bend Angle Settles]
    bend11 --> bend12{Springback Within Tolerance?}
    bend12 -->|Yes| bend13[Step 12: Accept Part]
    bend12 -->|No| bend14[Defect: Excessive Springback]
    bend14 --> bend15[Solution: Overbend, Use Bottoming, Use Higher YS Material]
    bend15 --> bend12
    bend13 --> endB[Finished Bent Part]
```

## 4.4 Stress State in the Flange During Deep Drawing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    flange[Flange Region] --> s1[Radial Direction: sigma_r TENSION]
    flange --> s2[Hoop Direction: sigma_theta COMPRESSION]
    flange --> s3[Thickness Direction: Near Zero]
    s2 --> s2a[Causes Wrinkling if BHF Insufficient]
    s1 --> s1a[Drives Material into Cup Wall]
    s1a --> wall[Wall Region]
    wall --> s4[Hoop Stress sigma_theta TENSION]
    wall --> s5[Radial Stress sigma_r TENSION]
    s4 --> s4a[Maximum at Punch Nose Radius]
    s4a --> s4b[Causes Wall Tearing if Exceeds UTS]
```

## 4.5 Bending Strain Distribution — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sheet[Sheet Cross-Section in Bend] --> zone1[Outer Fiber: MAX TENSION]
    sheet --> zone2[Neutral Axis: ZERO STRAIN]
    sheet --> zone3[Inner Fiber: MAX COMPRESSION]
    zone1 --> strain1[Epsilon = plus 1 / 1 plus 2R per t]
    zone2 --> strain2[Epsilon = 0]
    zone3 --> strain3[Epsilon = minus 1 / 1 plus 2R per t]
    strain1 --> sb1[On Unloading: Elastic Recovery = Springback]
    strain3 --> sb2[On Unloading: Elastic Recovery = Springback]
    sb1 --> sbe[Net Result: Bend Angle Opens by Delta Theta]
    sb2 --> sbe
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]**

**Q: Define "Limiting Drawing Ratio" (LDR) and state its typical values for mild steel and aluminum.**

**Model Answer:**

**Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR)** is defined as the **maximum ratio of blank diameter ($D$) to punch diameter ($d_p$)** that can be successfully drawn in a single operation without failure (i.e., without tearing the wall or wrinkling the flange).

$$\boxed{LDR = \dfrac{D_{max}}{d_p}}$$

**Typical Values:**
- **Mild Steel:** $LDR \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$ (Maximum reduction $\approx 45\%$)
- **Aluminum:** $LDR \approx 1.8$ to $2.0$ (Maximum reduction $\approx 35\%$)
- **Brass:** $LDR \approx 2.1$ to $2.4$ (Maximum reduction $\approx 50\%$)

If the required drawing ratio exceeds LDR, **multiple draws with intermediate annealing** are necessary.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q: What is "Springback" in sheet metal bending? List two methods to compensate for it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Springback** is the elastic recovery of a sheet metal part after the bending load is released. Since plastic deformation is permanent but elastic deformation is not, the bent sheet partially "unbends," causing the **bend angle to increase (open up)** by an amount $\Delta\theta$.

$$\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t)}$$

**Methods to Compensate for Springback:**
1. **Overbending** — Bend the part to an angle slightly less than required (e.g., bend to 88° if 90° is needed) so that after springback, the angle is 90°.
2. **Bottoming / Coining** — Force the punch to the bottom of the die, applying high local pressure to induce extra plastic strain and reduce elastic recovery.
3. **Use of higher yield-strength material** — Lower elastic strain recovery.
4. **Stretch bending** — Apply tension during bending to shift the neutral axis and reduce springback.
5. **Adjusting die radius** — Smaller die radius gives less springback.

---

## 📘 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: List and explain any five major defects encountered in deep drawing with neat sketches. State their causes and remedies.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Wrinkling**
- **Sketch concept:** Wavy/buckled flange of the cup.
- **Cause:** Insufficient Blank Holding Force (BHF); circumferential compressive stress in the flange exceeds the critical buckling stress.
- **Remedy:** Increase BHF; introduce **draw beads** on the blank holder; increase die entry radius; use multiple draws with intermediate annealing.

**2. Tearing (Wall Fracture)**
- **Sketch concept:** Vertical crack in the lower wall, typically near the punch nose.
- **Cause:** Drawing stress in the wall exceeds the ultimate tensile strength of the work-hardened material.
- **Remedy:** Reduce drawing ratio; multi-stage drawing; reduce BHF (just enough to prevent wrinkling); increase punch nose radius.

**3. Orange Peel**
- **Sketch concept:** Pebbled, orange-skin-like rough surface.
- **Cause:** Coarse grain size (grain diameter > $t/10$); individual grains deform differently due to varying orientations.
- **Remedy:** Use fine-grained sheet metal.

**4. Earing**
- **Sketch concept:** Cup rim with alternating high and low points (scalloped).
- **Cause:** **Planar anisotropy** ($\Delta R$); the R-value varies in different directions in the rolling plane, leading to directionally non-uniform metal flow.
- **Remedy:** Use material with low $\Delta R$ (better in-plane isotropy); proper blank orientation (45° to rolling direction).

**5. Stretcher Strains (Lüders Bands)**
- **Sketch concept:** Localized rough bands on the cup surface.
- **Cause:** Discontinuous yielding in low-carbon steel; carbon content 0.05–0.15%; sharp yield point.
- **Remedy:** Use **temper-rolled** (skin-rolled) sheet; aluminum-killed steel; strain beyond the yield point elongation before forming.

**6. Bottom Cracking (Optional sixth)**
- **Cause:** Sharp punch nose radius causes excessive local thinning.
- **Remedy:** Increase punch nose radius to 5–10 times sheet thickness.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: A cylindrical cup of 100 mm diameter and 120 mm height is to be drawn from a 2 mm thick cold-rolled steel sheet. The ultimate tensile strength is 350 MPa, and the drawing stress is 70% of UTS. Calculate: (i) Maximum blank diameter if LDR = 2.0; (ii) Drawing force; (iii) Blank holding force. Take Sachs' constant for BHF as 0.015.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Cup diameter, $d = 100$ mm
- Sheet thickness, $t = 2$ mm
- $\sigma_{uts} = 350$ MPa
- $\sigma_{draw} = 0.7 \times 350 = 245$ MPa
- LDR = 2.0
- Sachs' constant = 0.015

**(i) Maximum Blank Diameter:**

$$D_{max} = LDR \times d = 2.0 \times 100 = 200 \text{ mm}$$

Reduction:
$$r = \dfrac{D - d}{D} \times 100 = \dfrac{200 - 100}{200} \times 100 = 50\%$$

Since 50% > 45% (limit for one draw), **two-stage drawing is required**.

**For two stages** (40% per stage):
- After stage 1: $D_1 = 100 / 0.6 = 166.67$ mm
- This would need $D_{max}$ for stage 1: $D_1 = 166.67$ mm (intermediate)
- The original blank: $D_0 = D_1 / 0.6 = 277.78$ mm (or based on overall ~50% in 2 stages)

For our problem, assume **single-stage theoretical** then refine:
$$\boxed{D_{max} = 200 \text{ mm (single stage)}}$$

**(ii) Drawing Force:**

$$F_d = \pi \cdot d_p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{draw} \cdot (DR - 0.5)$$

$$F_d = \pi \times 100 \times 2 \times 245 \times (200/100 - 0.5)$$

$$F_d = \pi \times 100 \times 2 \times 245 \times (2 - 0.5)$$

$$F_d = \pi \times 100 \times 2 \times 245 \times 1.5$$

Step by step:
- $100 \times 2 = 200$
- $200 \times 245 = 49{,}000$
- $49{,}000 \times 1.5 = 73{,}500$
- $73{,}500 \times \pi = 73{,}500 \times 3.1416 = 230{,}907$ N

$$\boxed{F_d \approx 230.9 \text{ kN}}$$

**(iii) Blank Holding Force:**

$$BHF = 0.015 \times \sigma_{uts} \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$$

$$BHF = 0.015 \times 350 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}(200^2 - 100^2)$$

Step by step:
- $200^2 = 40{,}000$
- $100^2 = 10{,}000$
- $40{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 30{,}000$
- $\dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 30{,}000 = 0.7854 \times 30{,}000 = 23{,}562$ mm²
- $0.015 \times 350 = 5.25$
- $5.25 \times 23{,}562 = 123{,}700$ N

$$\boxed{BHF \approx 123.7 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the sheet metal bending process with a neat sketch. Derive the formula for Bend Allowance and discuss the factors affecting springback.**

**Model Answer:**

**Sheet Metal Bending Process:**

In bending, a straight sheet is plastically deformed about a straight axis to form an angle. The process uses a **punch and die** (commonly V-die). The sheet is placed over the die opening, and the punch forces the sheet into the V-groove, plastically bending it.

**Deformation Zones:**
- **Outer fibers** (away from die center): Tensile strain, $\varepsilon = +\dfrac{1}{1 + 2R/t}$
- **Inner fibers** (toward die center): Compressive strain, $\varepsilon = -\dfrac{1}{1 + 2R/t}$
- **Neutral axis**: Strain = 0, located at $K \cdot t$ from the inner surface, where $K = 0.33$ to $0.5$.

**Sketch Concept (Functional Description):**
- A sheet is placed over a V-die (V-opening of width $W$, included angle $\theta_d$).
- Punch descends with width $p$ and pushes sheet into V.
- Material bends over die edges (radius $R$).
- The bend angle after the operation is $\theta$.

**Bend Allowance Derivation:**

The **Bend Allowance (BA)** is the length of the neutral axis through the bend region. For a bend of:
- Included angle $\theta$ (in degrees)
- Inside bend radius $R_i$
- Sheet thickness $t$
- Neutral axis location $K \cdot t$ from inside surface

The neutral axis radius is:
$$R_{NA} = R_i + K \cdot t$$

The arc length of the neutral axis through the bend (in mm):
$$BA = \dfrac{\theta}{360} \times 2\pi \cdot R_{NA} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180} \cdot (R_i + K \cdot t)$$

$$\boxed{BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot (R_i + K \cdot t) \cdot \theta}$$

**For $K = 0.5$** (typical when $R/t > 10$, elastic bending):
$$BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot (R_i + 0.5t) \cdot \theta$$

**Factors Affecting Springback:**

1. **Material properties** — Higher $\sigma_y/E$ ratio → more springback.
2. **Bend radius to thickness ratio ($R/t$)** — Larger $R/t$ → more springback.
3. **Bend angle** — Larger bend angle → more springback in degrees.
4. **Tooling type** — Air bending has more springback than bottoming/coining.
5. **Friction** — High friction at die contact reduces springback slightly.
6. **Strain hardening exponent $n$** — Higher $n$ → less springback.
7. **Temperature** — Cold working has more springback; warm forming reduces it.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: A 1.5 mm thick aluminum sheet is bent through 90° over a die of radius 4 mm. The yield strength of aluminum is 120 MPa and Young's modulus is 70 GPa. Calculate: (i) Springback angle; (ii) Required punch angle; (iii) Bend Allowance (use $K = 0.45$).**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $t = 1.5$ mm
- $R = 4$ mm (inside radius)
- $\theta = 90°$
- $\sigma_y = 120$ MPa
- $E = 70$ GPa = 70,000 MPa
- $K = 0.45$

**(i) Springback Angle:**

$$\dfrac{R}{t} = \dfrac{4}{1.5} = 2.667$$

$$\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(R/t)}$$

$$\Delta\theta = \dfrac{120}{70{,}000} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2.667}$$

Step by step:
- $\dfrac{120}{70{,}000} = 0.001714$
- $\dfrac{180}{\pi} = 57.2958$
- $0.001714 \times 57.2958 = 0.09822$
- $0.09822 / 2.667 = 0.03683°$

$$\boxed{\Delta\theta \approx 0.037°}$$

**(ii) Required Punch Angle:**

$$\theta_{punch} = 90° - 0.037° = 89.963° \approx 89.96°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{punch} \approx 89.96°}$$

**(iii) Bend Allowance:**

$$BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot (R + K \cdot t) \cdot \theta$$

$$BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot (4 + 0.45 \times 1.5) \cdot 90$$

$$BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot (4 + 0.675) \cdot 90$$

$$BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \cdot 4.675 \cdot 90$$

Step by step:
- $4.675 \times 90 = 420.75$
- $420.75 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 420.75 \times 0.01745 = 7.342$ mm

$$\boxed{BA \approx 7.34 \text{ mm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Inside Radius" and "Neutral Axis Radius"** — In the bend allowance formula, students often write $(R_i + 0.5t)$ but use $R$ as punch radius instead of inside die radius. **Always specify $R_i$ = inside bend radius**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $\pi$ from radian to degree** — In the springback formula, the factor $\dfrac{180}{\pi}$ is **MUST be included** when the desired answer is in degrees. Skipping this is a 1-mark loss.
> 
> 3. **Not including the fudge factor for BHF** — Sachs' formula uses 0.015, Eigen's uses 0.002 — **memorize both constants**.
> 
> 4. **Mixing $K$-value and inside radius** — In $BA = \pi/180 \cdot (R_i + K \cdot t) \cdot \theta$, the term $(R_i + K \cdot t)$ is **NOT** the punch radius; it is the **neutral axis radius**. This is the most common KTU error.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the need for multiple draws** — When the calculated reduction > 45% for steel, students often proceed with a single stage and lose 1–2 marks for not justifying multi-stage drawing.
> 
> 6. **Wrong unit of E** — Springback requires E in **same units as $\sigma_y$** (both in MPa or both in GPa). Mixed units = 0 marks.
> 
> 7. **Listing defects without stating remedies** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates **defect + cause + remedy** in tabular form for full marks.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Critical Points to Remember Before Exam:**

### ✅ Deep Drawing — Must Know

- **LDR** for steel ≈ 2.0; aluminum ≈ 1.8–2.0; brass ≈ 2.1–2.4
- **Maximum reduction per draw (steel):** ~45%
- **Sachs' BHF constant:** 0.015; **Eigen's BHF constant (Al):** 0.002
- **Drawing force formula:** $F_d = \pi d_p t \sigma_{draw} (DR - 0.5)$
- **Higher $n$ and $R̄$** = better drawability
- **Planar anisotropy $\Delta R$** causes earing
- **Coarse grain** → orange peel (grain size > $t/10$)
- **Multi-stage drawing** with intermediate annealing for deep cups

### ✅ Drawing Defects — Quick Recall Table

| Defect | Region | Key Cause | Quick Fix |
|--------|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Wrinkling | Flange | Low BHF | Increase BHF, draw beads |
| Tearing | Wall | High stress | Multi-draw, lower DR |
| Orange Peel | Surface | Coarse grain | Fine-grain sheet |
| Earing | Rim | Planar anisotropy | Low $\Delta R$ material |
| Stretcher Strains | Surface | Yield-point elongation | Temper-rolled sheet |
| Bottom Crack | Bottom corner | Sharp punch nose | Larger $r_p$ |

### ✅ Bending — Must Know

- **Bend Allowance:** $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180}(R_i + K \cdot t) \cdot \theta$
- **Springback:** $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R/t}$
- **Higher $R/t$** → more springback
- **Higher $\sigma_y/E$** → more springback
- **Overbending, coining, bottoming** reduce springback
- **$K = 0.5$** for elastic; $K = 0.33$–$0.45$ for plastic
- **Minimum bend radius** depends on % elongation
- **Neutral axis shifts inward** during plastic bending

### ✅ Top 10 Formulas to Memorize (KTU 2024 Priority)

1. $LDR = D_{max}/d_p$
2. $r = (D-d)/D \times 100$
3. $F_d = \pi d_p t \sigma_{draw}(DR - 0.5)$
4. $BHF = 0.015 \cdot \sigma_{uts} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$
5. $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180}(R_i + K \cdot t) \cdot \theta$
6. $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R/t}$
7. $\varepsilon_{outer} = 1/(1 + 2R/t)$
8. $F_b = K_{bf} L t^2 \sigma_{uts}/W$ (V-bending)
9. $\Delta R = (R_{max} - R_{min})/2$
10. $R_{min} \approx t \times (\% \text{ elongation} - \text{offset})$

### ✅ Examiner's Mental Model — What KTU Wants

- **Definition + classification** of defects (3–4 marks)
- **Causes + remedies** of at least 5 defects (4–5 marks)
- **Numerical problem** on drawing force and BHF (4–5 marks)
- **Derivation/sketches** of bending process (3–4 marks)
- **Springback numerical** with proper unit conversion (3–4 marks)

> **🎓 Final Tip:** When answering KTU 2024 questions, always draw a **labeled sketch** (even a simple block diagram) for full marks on 14-mark questions. Use **tables for defect-cause-remedy** — it is the most mark-efficient format.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Work Load
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Determination of Work Load

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In bulk metal forming processes, the **work load (or forming load)** refers to the total external force or pressure that must be applied by the machine tool (press, hammer, rolling mill, or extrusion press) to plastically deform the workpiece into the desired shape. It is a critical design parameter because it governs the **tonnage capacity** of the equipment, the **energy consumption**, and the **die/punch life**.

Mathematically, work load represents the integral of the average flow stress of the material over the deformed (projected) area:

$$P = \int_{A} \sigma_f \, dA \approx \sigma_{f,avg} \times A_{projected}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 focuses on **Bulk Deformation Processes (Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Wire Drawing)**. The determination of work/load in each of these processes is a *High-Weightage, Numerically-Intensive* topic and is frequently asked for 7–14 mark problems in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pressing a metal disc with your palm to flatten it. The force you apply is resisted not just by the metal's internal atomic bonds (flow stress), but also by the **friction** between your palm and the disc. The more friction, the more force you need — even though the metal itself hasn't gotten stronger. This is the essence of the **"Friction Hill"** concept in forging.

Similarly, when you roll a ball of dough with a rolling pin, you need a horizontal force to push the pin forward. The harder the dough (higher flow stress) and the wider the pin (contact area), the more force is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Universal Components of Forming Load:**
> 1. **Ideal Work Load** — Force required to deform the metal ignoring friction (just overcoming flow stress).
> 2. **Friction Load** — Additional force due to tool-workpiece interface friction.
> 3. **Redundant Work Load** — Force needed to deform metal in non-homogeneous strain paths (e.g., during drawing through a die).

The **Total Work Load = Ideal Work + Friction Work + Redundant Work**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Flow Stress (σ_f)** — Instantaneous resistance of metal to plastic deformation (in **MPa** or **N/mm²**).
- **Average Flow Stress (σ_f,avg)** — Mean value of flow stress over the strain path.
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ)** — Ratio of frictional shear to normal pressure.
- **Die Geometry Parameters** — R, h, L, d, D, α (in **mm** or **m**).
- **Reduction Ratio (r)** — Ratio of area change, $\vert A_0 - A_1 \vert / A_0$.
- **Extrusion Ratio (R)** — Ratio of billet to product area, $A_0 / A_1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why and How: The Slab Method (Foundation of All Load Calculations)

The **Slab Method of Analysis** is the cornerstone of all bulk forming load derivations. It is used because the exact analytical solution of plastic deformation in 3D is mathematically intractable.

**Core Logic Steps:**

1. **Slice the workpiece** into thin elemental slabs along the direction of metal flow.
2. **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** of each slab — apply equilibrium equations.
3. **Yield Criterion** (von Mises or Tresca): Equate the differential normal stress to shear stress on the tool interface.
4. **Integrate** the stress equation across the geometry to obtain the average pressure.
5. **Multiply** average pressure by the projected (cross-sectional) area to get total load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Friction:**
> - **Sticking Friction:** τ = m × σ_f (where m is the friction factor, typically 0.05–0.4).
> - **Sliding Friction:** τ = μ × σ_n (Coulomb's law, μ ≈ 0.05–0.2).
> - **No Friction (Ideal):** τ = 0.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Process** | **Work Load Formula** | **Geometric Variables** | **Typical Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open-Die Upsetting (Flat Disc, Sliding Friction)** | $P = \sigma_{f,avg} \cdot \pi R^2 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu R}{3h}\right)$ | R = radius, h = instantaneous height | N (Newtons) |
| **Open-Die Upsetting (Flat Disc, Sticking Friction)** | $P = \sigma_{f,avg} \cdot \pi R^2 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{2h}{3R}\right)$ | R = radius, h = instantaneous height | N |
| **Open-Die Upsetting (Cylinder, No Friction — Ideal)** | $P = \sigma_{f,avg} \cdot \pi R^2$ | — | N |
| **Forging in Plane-Strain (Slab, Sticking)** | $\bar{p} = \sigma_f \left(1 + \dfrac{w}{4h}\right)$ | w = width, h = thickness | MPa |
| **Forging in Plane-Strain (Slab, Sliding)** | $\bar{p} = \sigma_f \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{\mu w}{2h}\right)}$ | w, h as above | MPa |
| **Rolling Load (Simplified Hitchcock)** | $F = \sigma_{f,avg} \cdot L_p \cdot w$ | $L_p = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | N |
| **Rolling Load (Sim's Equation — Hill's Solution)** | $F = 2 \sigma_f \cdot L_p \cdot w \cdot Q_p$ | $Q_p$ is a geometric factor (≈ 0.78 to 1.0) | N |
| **Rolling Torque** | $T = 2 F \cdot R \cdot \psi$ | ψ = angle of bite factor (≈ 0.5) | N·m |
| **Direct Extrusion (Slab Method)** | $F_0 = A_0 \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R) + \dfrac{2 L}{D} \cdot A_0 \cdot \sigma_f$ | R = extrusion ratio, L = billet length, D = billet diameter | N |
| **Indirect Extrusion (Slab Method)** | $F_0 = A_0 \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R)$ | R = extrusion ratio | N |
| **Wire Drawing / Tube Drawing** | $F_d = A_1 \cdot \sigma_f \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\tan \alpha}\right) \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_1}\right)$ | α = die semi-angle, μ = friction coeff. | N |
| **Maximum Reduction in Drawing** | $r_{max} = 1 - e^{\left(-2 f \sin \alpha\right)}$ | f = μ (friction) | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always check whether the friction condition is **sliding** or **sticking** before choosing the formula. This is the #1 reason students lose marks in numerical problems.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Press Selection:** A 200-ton forging press is chosen based on the *maximum upsetting load* for the heaviest workpiece.
- **Mill Design:** Rolling mills are rated by *separating force* (e.g., 5000-ton hot strip mill).
- **Die Wear Prediction:** Higher friction factor → higher load → faster die wear.
- **Energy Audit:** Forging energy = ∫P·dh; helps in power press motor sizing.
- **Defect Diagnosis:** If the calculated load exceeds the press capacity, defects like **under-forging** or **die jamming** occur.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Friction Hill for Open-Die Upsetting of a Cylindrical Disc

**Problem Setup:** A cylindrical billet of initial radius $R_0$ and height $h_0$ is compressed axially. Consider an instantaneous element at radius $r$ with infinitesimal thickness $dh$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Element:**

At any instant, the disc has radius $R$ and height $h$. By **volume constancy** (plastic deformation, no porosity change):

$$\pi R_0^2 h_0 = \pi R^2 h \implies R = R_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{h_0}{h}}$$

**Step 2 — Force Equilibrium on the Slab:**

Consider a thin cylindrical shell at radius $r$, thickness $dr$, and height $h$. Forces acting on it:
- Top: axial compressive force $(σ + dσ) \cdot 2π r \, dr$
- Bottom: axial compressive force $σ \cdot 2π r \, dr$
- Lateral (radial): radial pressure σ_r acting on the curved surface area.

The radial stress σ_r at the periphery equals the **frictional shear stress τ** divided by the friction law being used. For a **thick disc with sticking friction**:

$$\sigma_r = \dfrac{2 \tau}{h} \cdot r \quad \text{where } \tau = m \cdot \sigma_f$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Equation:**

The horizontal equilibrium of the thin ring gives:

$$\frac{d(\sigma_r \cdot h)}{dr} = \dfrac{2 \tau}{r} \cdot h$$

Wait, this is for the radial stress. The classical result is obtained by **integrating the pressure profile** over the cross-section.

**Step 4 — Pressure Distribution (Friction Hill):**

For **sticking friction**, the local axial pressure at radius $r$ is:

$$\sigma_z(r) = \sigma_f \left[1 + \dfrac{m}{h}(R - r)\right] \cdot 2 \quad \text{(approx., plane-strain analog)}$$

The classical "friction hill" result for a **thin disc with sticking friction** (where axial pressure is uniform radially but enhanced by friction at the dies):

$$\sigma_z = \sigma_f \left(1 + \dfrac{\pi R h}{2 A_c}\right)$$

where $A_c$ is the contact area. For a circular disc ($A_c = \pi R^2$):

$$\boxed{\sigma_z = \sigma_f \left(1 + \dfrac{h}{2R}\right)}$$

But this is the **centerline pressure** (peak). The **average** pressure is:

$$\sigma_{avg} = \sigma_f \left(1 + \dfrac{2h}{3R}\right) \quad \text{(Sticking)}$$

For **sliding friction** (μ small, low reduction):

$$\sigma_{avg} = \sigma_f \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu R}{3h}\right) \quad \text{(Sliding)}$$

**Step 5 — Total Upsetting Load:**

The total load is obtained by multiplying the average pressure by the instantaneous cross-sectional area:

$$P = \sigma_{avg} \times A = \sigma_{avg} \times \pi R^2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{sticking} = \sigma_f \cdot \pi R^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{2h}{3R}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{P_{sliding} = \sigma_f \cdot \pi R^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu R}{3h}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Volume Constancy Substitution:**

Using $R = R_0 \sqrt{h_0/h}$, we can express the load in terms of the *instantaneous height* $h$ for integration over the stroke (to compute **forging energy**).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Rolling Load (Hitchcock's Formula)

**Problem Setup:** A strip of width $w$ and initial thickness $h_0$ passes through two rolls of radius $R$. The exit thickness is $h_f$, so the draft is $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$.

**Step 1 — Projected Contact Length:**

The geometry of the roll bite forms a circular arc. The horizontal projection of the contact arc is:

$$L_p = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$$

(derived from Pythagoras: $L_p^2 + (R - \Delta h/2)^2 = R^2 \implies L_p \approx \sqrt{R \Delta h}$ for small draft)

**Step 2 — Pressure Distribution Assumption:**

In the **simplified Hitchcock model**, the roll pressure is assumed uniform across the contact length and equal to the average flow stress $\sigma_{f,avg}$.

**Step 3 — Vertical Force Balance:**

The vertical (separating) force on the roll is the pressure acting over the projected contact area:

$$F_{roll} = \sigma_{f,avg} \times L_p \times w$$

$$\boxed{F = \sigma_{f,avg} \cdot w \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}}$$

**Step 4 — Roll Torque:**

The torque required to drive each roll is the force times the effective lever arm. The lever arm is approximately half the contact length (L_p/2), but corrected by a factor ψ ≈ 0.5 (due to non-uniform pressure):

$$T = 2 F \cdot R \cdot \psi = F \cdot L_p \cdot \psi$$

For symmetric double-roll stand: $T_{total} = 2 F \cdot R \cdot \psi$

**Step 5 — Rolling Power:**

$$P_{power} = 2 \pi N \cdot T = \omega \cdot T$$

where $N$ is the rotational speed (rev/s) and $\omega$ is angular velocity (rad/s).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Extrusion Load (Direct vs Indirect)

**Problem Setup:** A billet of cross-section $A_0$ and length $L$ is forced through a die to produce a product of cross-section $A_1$. The extrusion ratio is $R_{ext} = A_0 / A_1$.

**Step 1 — Ideal Work (Homogeneous Deformation):**

For homogeneous plastic strain $\varepsilon = \ln(R_{ext})$, the ideal work per unit volume is:

$$w_{ideal} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R_{ext})$$

The ideal extrusion force is:

$$F_{ideal} = A_0 \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R_{ext})$$

**Step 2 — Friction Contribution (Direct Extrusion):**

In **direct extrusion**, the billet slides against the container wall, generating friction. The frictional shear stress is $\tau = \mu \sigma_f$ (sliding) or $\tau = m \sigma_f$ (sticking). For a cylindrical billet of diameter $D$:

$$F_{friction} = \pi D L \cdot \tau = \pi D L \cdot \mu \sigma_f$$

Expressed using $A_0 = \pi D^2 / 4$:

$$F_{friction} = \dfrac{4 L}{D} \cdot A_0 \cdot \mu \sigma_f$$

**Step 3 — Total Direct Extrusion Load:**

$$\boxed{F_{direct} = A_0 \sigma_f \ln(R_{ext}) + \dfrac{2 L}{D} A_0 \sigma_f \quad \text{(using } \mu = 0.5 \text{ for sticking peak)}}$$

Or in simpler form for sliding friction (μ):

$$F_{direct} = A_0 \sigma_f \left[\ln(R_{ext}) + \dfrac{2 \mu L}{D}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Indirect Extrusion Load:**

In **indirect extrusion**, the die moves into the billet and there is **no relative motion** at the container wall, so friction is essentially zero:

$$\boxed{F_{indirect} = A_0 \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R_{ext})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Trap:** Direct extrusion load is typically **30–40% higher** than indirect for the same reduction. Examiners love asking: *"Why is indirect extrusion preferred for high-reduction operations?"*

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Wire Drawing Load

**Problem Setup:** A wire of initial area $A_0$ is drawn through a conical die of semi-angle α to a final area $A_1$. The drawing force is applied at the exit.

**Step 1 — Strain in Drawing:**

The true strain is $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0 / A_1) = \ln(R_{ext})$.

**Step 2 — Slab Equilibrium on the Die Cone:**

For a die with semi-angle α, the slab on the die surface experiences:
- Normal pressure p (from die).
- Frictional shear τ = μ·p (acting against wire motion).

**Step 3 — Frictional Component:**

The factor that multiplies the ideal work due to friction is:

$$K_{friction} = 1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\tan \alpha} = 1 + \mu \cot \alpha$$

(For sticking friction, the factor becomes: $1 + (2m \cdot L_{die}) / (d_f \cdot \sin \alpha)$ where $L_{die}$ is the die land length.)

**Step 4 — Total Drawing Force:**

$$\boxed{F_d = A_1 \cdot \sigma_{f,avg} \left(1 + \mu \cot \alpha\right) \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_1}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Reduction in Drawing:**

To avoid wire breakage, the back tension (if any) plus the die friction stress must not exceed the material's tensile strength. The maximum reduction is given by:

$$\boxed{r_{max} = 1 - e^{(-2 f \sin \alpha)}}$$

where $f$ is the friction coefficient and α is the die semi-angle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimum Die Angle:** The total drawing force is **minimum** when $\alpha_{opt} = \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{\mu})$ for given reduction. Beyond this angle, redundant work increases; below it, friction work increases.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Processes

| **Parameter** | **Forging (Upset)** | **Rolling** | **Extrusion** | **Wire Drawing** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| State of Stress | Triaxial compression | Biaxial compression + tension | Triaxial compression | Biaxial compression + tension |
| Friction Effect | **High** (friction hill) | Moderate (entry/exit friction hill) | High in direct, low in indirect | High (small die angle) |
| Typical Reduction per Pass | 30–50% | 10–40% (cold), 30–60% (hot) | 70–95% (direct) | 20–40% (cold) |
| Load Magnitude (Relative) | High | Moderate | Very High | Low–Moderate |
| KTU Formula Hook | $\sigma_f(1 + 2h/3R)$ | $\sigma_f w \sqrt{R \Delta h}$ | $A_0 \sigma_f \ln(R_{ext})$ | $A_1 \sigma_f (1 + \mu \cot \alpha) \ln(A_0/A_1)$ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Friction Hill" in metal forming.**

**Model Answer:**
The "Friction Hill" refers to the pressure distribution in a workpiece during compression (e.g., open-die forging) where the local pressure is **highest at the center** of the contact area and **decreases towards the periphery**. This is due to the radial outward flow of metal being restrained by friction at the die-workpiece interface. The peak pressure at the center can be 2 to 3 times the material's flow stress, even though the average pressure used for load calculation is lower. The concept was first developed by Siebel and is fundamental to understanding the effect of friction in bulk forming.

> **Total Marks Breakdown:** Definition (1.5) + Physical explanation (1) + Example (0.5) = 3 Marks.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between Ideal Work, Friction Work, and Redundant Work in metal forming.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Type of Work** | **Cause** | **Magnitude** | **Remedial Measures** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Work** | Homogeneous deformation of the bulk material | Depends only on flow stress and strain | Cannot be eliminated (fundamental) |
| **Friction Work** | Tool-workpiece interface friction | Depends on μ, contact area, sliding distance | Use good lubrication, polish tools |
| **Redundant Work** | Non-uniform strain paths (internal shearing) | Depends on die geometry (e.g., small α in drawing) | Optimize die angle |

**Total Work = Ideal Work + Friction Work + Redundant Work.**

> **Total Marks Breakdown:** Three definitions (2) + One equation showing total (1) = 3 Marks.

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **average forging pressure in open-die upsetting of a cylindrical disc with sticking friction**, starting from the slab method. State clearly all assumptions used. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A steel cylinder of diameter **40 mm and height 60 mm** is upset in an open-die forging operation to a height of **30 mm**. The material has a flow stress of **σ_f = 350 MPa** and a friction factor **m = 0.25**. Determine: (i) The maximum forging load, and (ii) The forging load assuming ideal (frictionless) conditions. Compare both values. (7 Marks)

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:** (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. Plane-strain deformation (uniform axial compression).
2. Material is rigid-perfectly plastic (constant σ_f).
3. Sticking friction: τ = m·σ_f.
4. Homogeneous strain distribution (slab method).
5. No barreling (uniform deformation).

**Derivation Outline (Slab Method):**

Consider a cylindrical disc of instantaneous radius $R$ and height $h$. Apply equilibrium to a thin slab at radius $r$:

$$d\sigma_r + \frac{2 \tau}{h} dr = 0 \quad \text{(in equilibrium)}$$

Integrating with boundary condition $\sigma_r = 0$ at $r = R$:

$$\sigma_r(r) = \dfrac{2 m \sigma_f}{h}(R - r)$$

The local axial stress is found using the **Tresca yield criterion** $\sigma_z = \sigma_f + \sigma_r$:

$$\sigma_z(r) = \sigma_f \left[1 + \dfrac{2m}{h}(R - r)\right]$$

The average pressure is obtained by integrating over the disc area:

$$\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R \sigma_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_f}{R^2} \int_0^R r \left[1 + \dfrac{2m}{h}(R - r)\right] dr$$

After integration:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{avg} = \sigma_f \left(1 + \dfrac{2 m R}{3 h}\right)}$$

Multiplying by area $A = \pi R^2$:

$$\boxed{P = \sigma_f \cdot \pi R^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{2 m R}{3 h}\right)}$$

*(Note: This is the more general form; in plane-strain forging of a slab, the equivalent form is $\sigma_f(1 + w/4h)$.)*

**Distribution of Marks:** Assumptions (1.5) + FBD (1) + Equilibrium equation (1.5) + Integration (2) + Final expression (1) = **7 Marks**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Initial diameter $D_0 = 40$ mm → $R_0 = 20$ mm
- Initial height $h_0 = 60$ mm
- Final height $h_f = 30$ mm
- Flow stress $\sigma_f = 350$ MPa
- Friction factor $m = 0.25$

**Step 1 — Final Radius from Volume Constancy:**

$$A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f \implies \pi R_0^2 h_0 = \pi R_f^2 h_f$$

$$R_f = R_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}} = 20 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{60}{30}} = 20 \sqrt{2} \approx 28.28 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Forging Load with Sticking Friction:**

$$P = \sigma_f \cdot \pi R_f^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{2 m R_f}{3 h_f}\right)$$

$$P = 350 \times \pi \times (28.28)^2 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{2 \times 0.25 \times 28.28}{3 \times 30}\right)$$

Compute each term:
- $\pi R_f^2 = \pi \times 800 = 2513.27$ mm²
- $2 m R_f / (3 h_f) = 2 \times 0.25 \times 28.28 / 90 = 0.1571$
- Bracket: $1 + 0.1571 = 1.1571$

$$P = 350 \times 2513.27 \times 1.1571 \approx 1,017,720 \text{ N} \approx 1018 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 1.02 \text{ MN} \approx 104 \text{ tonnes-force}}$$

**Step 3 — Forging Load under Ideal (Frictionless) Conditions:**

$$P_{ideal} = \sigma_f \cdot \pi R_f^2 = 350 \times 2513.27 \approx 879,645 \text{ N} \approx 880 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{P_{ideal} \approx 0.88 \text{ MN} \approx 90 \text{ tonnes-force}}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and Interpretation:**

$$\dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{ideal}} = \dfrac{1018}{880} \approx 1.157$$

This means friction increases the load by approximately **15.7%**. This additional load is dissipated as heat at the tool-workpiece interface, accelerating die wear.

**Distribution of Marks:** Volume constancy (1) + Area calculation (1) + Friction load formula (2) + Numerical evaluation (2) + Comparison and interpretation (1) = **7 Marks**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the **Sim's equation for the rolling load** of a strip passing through a two-high rolling mill. State the assumptions clearly. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **2 mm thick aluminum strip** of width **200 mm** is rolled to **1.5 mm** in one pass using rolls of diameter **300 mm**. The roll speed is **30 rpm** and the average flow stress of aluminum is **180 MPa**. Calculate: (i) The rolling load, (ii) The rolling torque, and (iii) The rolling power required. Assume a lever arm factor of **ψ = 0.5** and a Sims' geometric factor of **Q_p = 0.85**. (7 Marks)

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Sim's Equation Derivation:** (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. Plane-strain compression in the deformation zone.
2. Material is rigid-plastic with constant flow stress.
3. Friction follows Coulomb's law (sliding).
4. The roll is a rigid body.
5. Strip width remains constant (no spread).
6. Homogeneous deformation in the bite (Sim's assumption — but the actual analysis accounts for non-uniform strain).

**Derivation Outline:**

The horizontal projection of the contact arc is:

$$L_p = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} \quad \text{where } \Delta h = h_0 - h_f$$

Sim's exact solution (from the integration of slab equilibrium with friction hill at entry and exit) gives the **roll separating force** as:

$$F = 2 \sigma_f \cdot L_p \cdot w \cdot Q_p$$

where $Q_p$ is a geometric factor:

$$Q_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} + \dfrac{L_p}{4h_f} \ln\left(\dfrac{h_x}{h_f}\right) - \text{simplified for thin strips to} \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 1.0$$

For thin strips where the bite is small compared to the roll radius:

$$\boxed{F \approx \sigma_{f,avg} \cdot L_p \cdot w = \sigma_{f,avg} \cdot w \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}}$$

This is the **simplified Hitchcock's formula**, often used in KTU problems for cold rolling of thin strips.

**Distribution of Marks:** Assumptions (1.5) + Geometry of bite (1.5) + Derivation steps (3) + Final formula (1) = **7 Marks**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Initial thickness $h_0 = 2$ mm
- Final thickness $h_f = 1.5$ mm
- Strip width $w = 200$ mm
- Roll diameter = 300 mm → $R = 150$ mm
- Roll speed $N = 30$ rpm
- $\sigma_{f,avg} = 180$ MPa
- $Q_p = 0.85$, $\psi = 0.5$

**Step 1 — Draft and Projected Contact Length:**

$$\Delta h = h_0 - h_f = 2 - 1.5 = 0.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_p = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{150 \times 0.5} = \sqrt{75} \approx 8.66 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Rolling Load (Sim's Equation):**

$$F = 2 \sigma_f \cdot L_p \cdot w \cdot Q_p$$

$$F = 2 \times 180 \times 8.66 \times 200 \times 0.85$$

Step-by-step:
- $2 \times 180 = 360$
- $360 \times 8.66 = 3117.6$
- $3117.6 \times 200 = 623,520$
- $623,520 \times 0.85 = 529,992$ N

$$\boxed{F \approx 530 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Rolling Torque:**

For a two-high mill, total torque (both rolls):

$$T = 2 F \cdot R \cdot \psi = 2 \times 530{,}000 \times 0.150 \times 0.5$$

$$T = 2 \times 530{,}000 \times 0.075 = 79{,}500 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 79.5 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Rolling Power:**

Angular velocity: $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 30}{60} = \pi \approx 3.1416$ rad/s

$$P_{power} = T \cdot \omega = 79{,}500 \times 3.1416 \approx 249{,}760 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{power} \approx 250 \text{ kW} \approx 335 \text{ HP}}$$

**Distribution of Marks:** Draft and $L_p$ (1.5) + Load formula application (1.5) + Torque (1.5) + Power (1.5) + Final summary (1) = **7 Marks**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Volume Constancy:** In upsetting, students often use $R_0$ instead of $R_f$ in the load formula. *Deduct 2 marks* if volume constancy is not applied.
> 2. **Wrong Friction Law:** Using sticking friction formula when sliding is given, or vice-versa. *Deduct 1 mark* per error.
> 3. **Ignoring Units:** Mixing mm and m in load calculations. *Deduct 0.5 mark* per occurrence.
> 4. **Not Stating Assumptions:** In derivations, the assumptions are worth 1.5–2 marks. *Always list 4–5 assumptions explicitly.*
> 5. **Single Roll vs Two Rolls:** In rolling torque, students often forget to multiply by 2. *Deduct 1 mark.*
> 6. **Friction Hill Confusion:** Mixing the "average pressure" formula with the "centerline pressure" formula. *Read the question carefully.*
> 7. **Logarithmic Strain vs Engineering Strain:** In extrusion/drawing, always use $\ln(A_0/A_1)$, **not** $(A_0 - A_1)/A_0$.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- The **Slab Method** is the foundation of all forming load derivations in Module 4.
- **Work Load = Ideal Work + Friction Work + Redundant Work**.
- The **Friction Hill** effect can increase forging pressure by **2× to 3×** the flow stress, especially with poor lubrication.
- For **upsetting**: $P = \sigma_f \pi R^2 (1 + \text{friction term})$. Use $\mu R/(3h)$ for sliding; $(2h/3R)$ for sticking.
- For **rolling**: $F = 2 \sigma_f L_p w Q_p$ (Sim's) or $F = \sigma_f w \sqrt{R \Delta h}$ (Hitchcock's simplified).
- For **extrusion**: Direct = $A_0 \sigma_f [\ln R + 2\mu L/D]$; Indirect = $A_0 \sigma_f \ln R$. Indirect is **30–40%** lower load.
- For **drawing**: $F_d = A_1 \sigma_f (1 + \mu \cot \alpha) \ln(A_0/A_1)$. Optimum die angle: $\alpha_{opt} = \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{\mu})$.
- **Always apply volume constancy** in forging and extrusion to find geometric changes.
- **Average flow stress** is used (not peak) because the material strain-hardens through the process.
- **Power (W) = Torque (N·m) × Angular Velocity (rad/s)**.
- Typical KTU marks distribution: Numerical Problem = 7–10 marks, Theory/Definition = 3–5 marks per question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Spring Back
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Spring Back

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Spring back** is defined as the *elastic recovery* of a sheet metal component that occurs after the forming load is removed. During a forming operation (bending, drawing, stretch forming, etc.), the metal undergoes both **elastic** and **plastic** deformation. When the tooling is withdrawn, the elastic portion of the strain is recovered, causing the part to change its shape relative to the tool geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard Wording):**
> "Spring back is the change in shape (radius of curvature and/or angle) of a formed sheet-metal component that occurs upon removal of the forming forces, due to the elastic recovery of the material from its plastically deformed state."

Mathematically, the change in curvature during spring back is expressed as:

$$\Delta K = K_f - K_p$$

where:
- $K_f$ = Curvature during forming (just before unloading) = $\dfrac{1}{R_f}$
- $K_p$ = Curvature after spring back (final part curvature) = $\dfrac{1}{R_p}$
- $R_f$ = Forming radius (punch radius)
- $R_p$ = Final radius after elastic recovery

> [!NOTE]
> When $\Delta K > 0$ (most metals): the part **opens up** — radius increases, angle decreases. This is the standard case.
> When $\Delta K < 0$ (**spring-forward** or **negative spring back**, rare): observed in materials with very high $E/\sigma_Y$ ratio such as high-strength steels under tension. The radius decreases after unloading.

---
