## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bent Plastic Ruler" Model

Imagine you take a **flexible plastic ruler** and bend it over your knee. While your hand is applying force, the ruler conforms to a tight curve around your knee (small radius). The instant you release the ruler, it **snaps back** partially — the radius increases, and the angle you originally imposed opens up. That visible "rebound" is spring back.

In a manufacturing press, the same physics happens, but the "ruler" is a sheet of steel or aluminium, and the "knee" is the punch/die. Because metal has both elastic and plastic behaviour, only the **elastic part** of the bending strain is recovered. A higher **elastic modulus ($E$)** or lower **yield strength ($\sigma_Y$)** means a bigger elastic zone — and therefore **larger spring back**.

A second analogy: think of spring back like releasing a stretched rubber band that is bonded to a piece of dough. The dough (plastic) holds the new shape, but the rubber (elastic) tries to pull it back. What you finally see is the dough's deformed shape minus the rubber's pull.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Mild Steel) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Elastic (Young's) Modulus | $E$ | **200 – 210 GPa** | N/mm² or MPa |
| Yield Strength | $\sigma_Y$ | **200 – 350 MPa** (Mild Steel) | N/mm² or MPa |
| Sheet thickness | $t$ | **0.5 – 6 mm** (typical press work) | mm |
| Modulus ratio $E/\sigma_Y$ | — | **~500–1000** | dimensionless |
| Spring back (typical) | $\Delta K \cdot R_f$ | **0.02 – 0.05** | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU emphasises $E/\sigma_Y$ ratio:** Spring back magnitude is governed by this ratio. Aluminium (low $E$, ~70 GPa; moderate $\sigma_Y$) shows *less* spring back than spring steel (high $E$, high $\sigma_Y$). The ratio $E/\sigma_Y$ is the single most important material index for spring back control.

---

## 1.4 Causes of Spring Back — The "Why" Behind It

1. **Elastic strain component** — Every deformation contains elastic strain; on unloading it must vanish (Hooke's law demands it).
2. **Residual stresses** — Non-uniform plastic flow leaves internal stresses that equilibrate when the tool is removed.
3. **Neutral axis shift** — During bending, the neutral axis moves toward the inner fibre; after unloading it re-centres, redistributing strain.
4. **Tool-part contact release** — On opening the press, friction and contact constraints vanish, allowing elastic rebound.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Learning Outcome Linkage (CO4):** *Apply elastic–plastic bending theory to estimate spring back in sheet metal forming operations.* The estimation techniques below are directly testable in the University Exam.

---

## 1.5 Where Spring Back Appears in Industry

| Operation | Manifestation of Spring Back |
|---|---|
| **Air Bending (V-bending)** | Bend angle opens up; included angle increases |
| **Bottoming (Coining)** | Very little spring back (near-net shape) |
| **Deep Drawing** | Wall angle changes; flange springs outward |
| **Stretch Forming** | Contour radius changes; sweep increases |
| **Roll Bending** | Cylindrical radius increases on release |
| **Tube Bending** | Cross-section ovalises + radius springs back |

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Consequence:** A door panel, an aircraft skin, or a refrigerator cabinet that springs back by 2° too much will not assemble. Hence **spring back must be predicted and compensated during die design** — never corrected downstream.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Elastic–Plastic Bending Model

When a sheet of thickness $t$ is bent over a punch of radius $R_f$:

- The **outer fibre** (radius $R_f + t/2$) is in **tension**.
- The **inner fibre** (radius $R_f - t/2$) is in **compression**.
- A neutral surface of radius $R_n$ lies between them, where longitudinal strain = 0.
- The strain distribution is **linear** (plane sections remain plane, Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis):

$$\varepsilon(r) = \ln\left(\frac{r}{R_n}\right) \approx \frac{r - R_n}{R_n} \quad \text{(small-strain form)}$$

At the outer surface ($r = R_f + t/2$):

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{1}{R_f}\left(\frac{t}{2}\right) = \frac{t}{2R_f}$$

The material yields once $\varepsilon_{max} > \varepsilon_Y = \sigma_Y / E$. Beyond this, the outer region is **plastic**, and only a thin **elastic core** around $R_n$ behaves elastically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bending moment required to fully yield the section** (fully plastic moment, assuming elastic–perfectly-plastic behaviour):
> $$M_p = \sigma_Y \cdot Z = \sigma_Y \cdot \frac{b \cdot t^2}{4}$$
> where $Z$ is the plastic section modulus for a rectangular cross-section of width $b$ and thickness $t$.

---

## 2.2 The Spring Back Derivation — Curvature Recovery

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **During forming**, the sheet is bent to curvature $K_f = 1/R_f$. A uniform bending moment $M$ acts.
2. **At unloading**, the moment is removed. The change in curvature $\Delta K$ is governed by the elastic unloading stiffness:

$$M = E \cdot I \cdot (K_f - K_p) = E \cdot I \cdot \Delta K$$

3. **For wide sheets (width $b \gg t$)** we use plane-strain bending. The bending stiffness per unit width is $E I / b = E t^3 / 12$. The moment per unit width is $m = M/b$.
4. **At the instant of unloading**, the moment is at its maximum plastic value, $m_p = \sigma_Y \cdot t^2 / 4$.
5. **Equate** to obtain the curvature change:

$$m_p = \frac{E t^3}{12} \cdot \Delta K \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \Delta K = \frac{12 \cdot \sigma_Y \cdot t^2 / 4}{E t^3} = \frac{3 \sigma_Y}{E t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the famous Spring Back Equation (KTU high-yield).** Note that $\Delta K$ depends **only on material properties and thickness**, not on the bend radius! For a 1 mm mild-steel sheet ($E$ = 210 GPa, $\sigma_Y$ = 250 MPa):
> $$\Delta K = \frac{3 \times 250}{210{,}000 \times 1} = 0.00357 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$
> i.e., the curvature decreases by 0.00357 mm⁻¹ — independent of how tight the punch is.

---

## 2.3 Spring Back in Terms of Radius

Starting from $\Delta K = K_f - K_p$:

$$\frac{1}{R_f} - \frac{1}{R_p} = \frac{3 \sigma_Y}{E t}$$

Solving for the final (sprung-back) radius $R_p$:

$$R_p = \frac{R_f}{1 - \dfrac{3 \sigma_Y R_f}{E t}} = \frac{R_f \cdot E t}{E t - 3 \sigma_Y R_f}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Singularity Warning:** When $3 \sigma_Y R_f = E t$, the formula gives $R_p = \infty$ — a flat sheet! This defines the **minimum bend radius** before spring back flattens the part completely:
> $$R_{f, \min} = \frac{E t}{3 \sigma_Y}$$
> For a 1 mm mild-steel sheet: $R_{f,\min} = 210{,}000 \times 1 / (3 \times 250) = 280$ mm. Any tighter bend **without bottoming** will spring back to flat.

---

## 2.4 Spring Back in Terms of Bend Angle

For an arc of neutral-axis length $L_n$, the included angles before and after spring back are:

$$\theta_f = \frac{L_n}{R_f} \quad,\quad \theta_p = \frac{L_n}{R_p}$$

The change in angle (spring back angle) is:

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_p = L_n \cdot \Delta K = L_n \cdot \frac{3 \sigma_Y}{E t}$$

For a **unit developed length** ($L_n = 1$), $\Delta\theta = 3\sigma_Y / (E t)$ rad/m.

For small-angle bends, a useful approximation:

$$\frac{\Delta\theta}{\theta_f} \approx \frac{3 \sigma_Y R_f}{E t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Compensation angle for the die:**
> $$\boxed{\theta_{\text{die}} = \theta_{\text{part}} + \Delta\theta}$$
> The die must be cut **over-bent** by $\Delta\theta$ to obtain the desired final part angle $\theta_{\text{part}}$ after elastic recovery.

---

## 2.5 The KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorise for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forming curvature | $K_f = 1/R_f$ | mm⁻¹ | Just before unloading |
| 2 | Final curvature | $K_p = 1/R_p$ | mm⁻¹ | After spring back |
| 3 | **Curvature spring back** | $\Delta K = \dfrac{3\sigma_Y}{E t}$ | mm⁻¹ | **Independent of $R_f$** |
| 4 | Final radius | $R_p = \dfrac{R_f}{1 - \dfrac{3\sigma_Y R_f}{E t}}$ | mm | — |
| 5 | Minimum forming radius | $R_{f,\min} = \dfrac{E t}{3\sigma_Y}$ | mm | Spring back → flat |
| 6 | Spring back angle (unit length) | $\dfrac{\Delta\theta}{L_n} = \dfrac{3\sigma_Y}{E t}$ | rad/m | — |
| 7 | Compensated die angle | $\theta_{\text{die}} = \theta_{\text{part}} + \Delta\theta$ | rad or ° | **Overbend the die** |
| 8 | Plastic moment per unit width | $m_p = \dfrac{\sigma_Y t^2}{4}$ | N·mm/mm | Rectangular section |
| 9 | Elastic bending stiffness (per unit width) | $EI/b = \dfrac{E t^3}{12}$ | N·mm²/mm | — |
| 10 | Plane-strain yield correction | $\sigma_Y^{\text{PS}} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\,\sigma_Y$ | MPa | Use when $b \gg t$ |
| 11 | Strain-hardening correction | $E' = \dfrac{E \cdot E_t}{E + E_t}$ (tangent modulus) | MPa | For strain-hardened metals |
| 12 | Spring forward (negative) | $\Delta K < 0$ if $\sigma_Y/E$ large + tensile load | — | Rare; mostly HSS |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Board-Exam Symbol Convention:** KTU answer scripts use $R$ (or $R'$) for the after-spring-back radius, and $R_1$ (or $R_i$) for the punch radius. The neutral-axis radius is often written $R_n$ or $R_0$. Stick to one consistent set; examiners reward clarity.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive door-panel dies** are over-bent by 1°–3° to account for spring back; the die is iteratively re-cut ("try-out loop") until measured part matches CAD.
- **Aircraft skin stretch-forming**: predicted spring back is fed back to the machine to pre-sweep the tooling. The Boeing 737 wing-skin forming is a textbook case.
- **Roll forming of steel sections**: the final stand is **over-bent** so that the elastic recovery of the entire product line matches the design profile.
- **Spring-back compensation in FEA**: modern AutoForm/PAM-STAMP simulations use the same $\Delta K = 3\sigma_Y/(Et)$ relationship as a local check on mesh-based solutions.
- **Tube bending**: spring back here combines cross-section ovalisation; mandrel bending is used to suppress it.

---

## 2.7 Factors Influencing Spring Back (Triggers for Higher $\Delta K$)

1. **Higher $E/\sigma_Y$ ratio** → larger spring back (e.g., high-strength steel).
2. **Thinner sheet** → larger $\Delta K$ (because $\Delta K \propto 1/t$).
3. **Larger bend radius** → larger absolute $\Delta\theta$ for the same $\theta_f$.
4. **Wider bend region** → larger $\Delta\theta$ (since $\Delta\theta = L_n \cdot \Delta K$).
5. **Less work-hardening (lower $n$)** → spring back closer to ideal-plastic prediction.
6. **Higher temperature** → lower $\sigma_Y$, hence lower $\Delta K$ (hot forming helps).
7. **Tensile stretch in the flange** → reduces and can even reverse spring back.
8. **Bottoming / coining** → near-zero spring back (full elastic recovery prevented by tool contact).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Curvature Spring Back $\Delta K = 3\sigma_Y/(Et)$

### Given
- Sheet of width $b$, thickness $t$, elastic modulus $E$, yield stress $\sigma_Y$ (elastic–perfectly-plastic).
- Bent to forming radius $R_f$ over a punch.
- Bending moment just before unloading = fully plastic moment $M_p$.

### Assumptions
- Plane sections remain plane (Euler–Bernoulli).
- Wide sheet ($b \gg t$) — plane strain, but for $\Delta K$ formula plane stress suffices for $E$ and $\sigma_Y$.
- Elastic–perfectly-plastic; neglect work-hardening for first-order KTU result.
- Sheet thickness unchanged; neutral axis through mid-thickness.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Plastic moment of resistance of a rectangular section:**

The stress distribution at full plasticity is $\pm\sigma_Y$ over the full thickness. The moment about the mid-thickness is:

$$M_p = \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} \sigma_Y \cdot y \cdot b \, dy = \sigma_Y b \left[ \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_{-t/2}^{t/2} = \sigma_Y b \cdot \frac{t^2}{4}$$

Hence:

$$m_p = \frac{M_p}{b} = \frac{\sigma_Y t^2}{4} \quad \text{(moment per unit width)}$$

**Step 2 — Elastic bending stiffness of a rectangular section (per unit width):**

Using the area moment of inertia $I = bt^3/12$:

$$\frac{EI}{b} = \frac{E t^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Elastic unloading relationship:**

The change in curvature when a moment $M$ is released is:

$$\Delta K = \frac{M}{E I}$$

For a unit width, $m = M/b$, therefore:

$$\Delta K = \frac{m \cdot b}{E I} = \frac{m}{(E I)/b} = \frac{m_p}{E t^3 / 12} = \frac{12 m_p}{E t^3}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $m_p = \sigma_Y t^2 / 4$:**

$$\Delta K = \frac{12}{E t^3} \cdot \frac{\sigma_Y t^2}{4} = \frac{3 \sigma_Y}{E t}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta K = \frac{3\sigma_Y}{E t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Remarkable observation:** $\Delta K$ is *independent of the punch radius* — a counterintuitive but rigorously true result that examiners frequently ask students to comment on.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Final Radius After Spring Back

### Given
$\Delta K = 3\sigma_Y/(Et)$ and $K_f = 1/R_f$.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Use $K_f - K_p = \Delta K$:

$$K_p = K_f - \Delta K = \frac{1}{R_f} - \frac{3\sigma_Y}{E t}$$

**Step 2:** Invert to get $R_p = 1/K_p$:

$$R_p = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R_f} - \dfrac{3\sigma_Y}{E t}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $R_f \cdot E t$:

$$R_p = \frac{R_f \cdot E t}{E t - 3\sigma_Y R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Factor $Et$ from the denominator:

$$R_p = \frac{R_f}{1 - \dfrac{3\sigma_Y R_f}{E t}}$$

This is the **closed-form radius** after spring back. Re-arranged:

$$\frac{1}{R_p} = \frac{1}{R_f}\left(1 - \frac{3\sigma_Y R_f}{E t}\right) = \frac{1}{R_f} - \frac{3\sigma_Y}{E t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity:** The formula is valid only if the denominator is **positive**, i.e., $R_f < E t / (3 \sigma_Y)$. If the punch radius exceeds this limit, the part springs all the way back to flat — the formula breaks down and $R_p \to \infty$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spring Back Angle $\Delta\theta$

### Given
Neutral-axis developed length $L_n$, forming radius $R_f$, final radius $R_p$.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Arc-length conservation at the neutral axis:

$$L_n = R_f \theta_f = R_p \theta_p$$

**Step 2:** Difference in included angles:

$$\theta_f - \theta_p = L_n \left(\frac{1}{R_f} - \frac{1}{R_p}\right) = L_n \cdot \Delta K$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\Delta K$:

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_p = L_n \cdot \frac{3\sigma_Y}{E t}$$

**Step 4:** Express in terms of the *part* (final) angle, for compensation purposes. Using $L_n = R_p \theta_p$:

$$\Delta\theta = R_p \theta_p \cdot \frac{3\sigma_Y}{E t} = \theta_p \cdot \frac{3\sigma_Y R_p}{E t}$$

Therefore the **compensated (die) angle** is:

$$\theta_{\text{die}} = \theta_p + \Delta\theta = \theta_p \left(1 + \frac{3\sigma_Y R_p}{E t}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical tip:** For design calculations, treat $\theta_p$ as the desired part angle; calculate $R_p$ from the desired final geometry; then compute $\Delta\theta$ to over-bend the punch/die.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Strain-Hardening (Tangent-Modulus) Refinement

In real metals, the elastic modulus during *unloading* is $E$ (the same as during initial loading). However, when the stress–strain curve has a tangent modulus $E_t$ at the moment of unloading, the elastic recovery is *partially* governed by $E_t$ as well. A practical correction:

$$\Delta K_{\text{corr}} = \frac{3 \sigma_Y}{E t} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{E_t}{E}} \quad \text{(approximate)}$$

Or, more rigorously, the unloading modulus is $E$ (the initial slope), so the basic formula **already uses $E$ correctly**. The $E_t$ correction appears only if a **reverse-yield** check is needed (Bauschinger effect in cyclic loading) — beyond KTU 2024 scope.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Examples

### Example 1 — Mild Steel Sheet, Bend Radius Problem

**Given:** Mild-steel sheet, $t = 2$ mm, $E = 210$ GPa, $\sigma_Y = 280$ MPa. The sheet is bent over a punch of radius $R_f = 20$ mm to an included angle of $\theta_f = 90°$. Find the final radius and angle after spring back.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta K = \frac{3 \times 280}{210{,}000 \times 2} = \frac{840}{420{,}000} = 0.002 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$K_f = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$K_p = K_f - \Delta K = 0.05 - 0.002 = 0.048 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$R_p = \frac{1}{0.048} = 20.83 \text{ mm}$$

For the angle (neutral-axis arc length $L_n \approx R_f \theta_f$, using $\theta_f = \pi/2$ rad):

$$L_n = 20 \times \frac{\pi}{2} = 31.42 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta\theta = L_n \cdot \Delta K = 31.42 \times 0.002 = 0.0628 \text{ rad} = 3.6°$$

$$\theta_p = 90° - 3.6° = 86.4°$$

**Compensated die angle = $90° + 3.6° = 93.6°$.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Spring back is ~4% of the bend angle** — typical for air-bending of mild steel at moderate radius.

---

### Example 2 — Aluminium Sheet (Lower Spring Back Prediction)

**Given:** Aluminium sheet, $t = 1.5$ mm, $E = 70$ GPa, $\sigma_Y = 150$ MPa, $R_f = 15$ mm, $\theta_f = 60°$.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta K = \frac{3 \times 150}{70{,}000 \times 1.5} = \frac{450}{105{,}000} = 0.00429 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$K_f = 1/15 = 0.0667 \text{ mm}^{-1}, \quad K_p = 0.0667 - 0.00429 = 0.0624 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$R_p = 16.0 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{increase of } 1 \text{ mm})$$

$$L_n = 15 \times \pi/3 = 15.71 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta\theta = 15.71 \times 0.00429 = 0.0674 \text{ rad} = 3.86°$$

$$\theta_p = 60° - 3.86° = 56.1°$$

**Observation:** Although the curvature change $\Delta K$ is *larger* for aluminium (because $E$ is lower), the absolute $\Delta R$ is comparable. The final $\theta_p$ depends on $L_n$ as well.

---

### Example 3 — Compensation Angle for a Desired 120° Bend

**Given:** Stainless-steel sheet, $t = 1$ mm, $E = 200$ GPa, $\sigma_Y = 350$ MPa. The die radius $R_f = 8$ mm. Find the punch angle to obtain a final part angle of 120°.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta K = \frac{3 \times 350}{200{,}000 \times 1} = 0.00525 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

For the part angle, the *neutral-axis* developed length uses $R_p$ (we don't know it yet — iterate).

**Iteration 1:** Assume $R_p \approx R_f = 8$ mm.

$$L_n = R_p \theta_p = 8 \times (120° \times \pi/180) = 8 \times 2.094 = 16.76 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta\theta = L_n \cdot \Delta K = 16.76 \times 0.00525 = 0.088 \text{ rad} = 5.04°$$

$$\theta_{\text{die}} = 120° + 5.04° = 125.04°$$

**Iteration 2 (refine):** Use the corrected forming radius. From $K_p = 1/R_p$ and $K_f = 1/R_f + \Delta K$ — actually recompute: with $R_f = 8$ mm:

$$R_p = \frac{8}{1 - 3 \times 350 \times 8 / (200{,}000 \times 1)} = \frac{8}{1 - 0.042} = 8.35 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_n = 8.35 \times 2.094 = 17.49 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta\theta = 17.49 \times 0.00525 = 0.0918 \text{ rad} = 5.26°$$

$$\theta_{\text{die}} \approx 120° + 5.3° = 125.3°$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-iteration closure is enough** for KTU exam accuracy. State the iteration logic explicitly — examiners award full method marks.

---

### Example 4 — Minimum Bend Radius (Spring-Back Limit)

**Given:** A 1.5 mm cold-rolled-steel sheet with $E = 200$ GPa, $\sigma_Y = 250$ MPa. Find the minimum punch radius below which the part will not fully spring back to flat.

**Solution:**

$$R_{f,\min} = \frac{E t}{3 \sigma_Y} = \frac{200{,}000 \times 1.5}{3 \times 250} = \frac{300{,}000}{750} = 400 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** Any air bend with a punch radius $\geq 400$ mm will effectively spring all the way back to a flat sheet — useless. To obtain a permanent bend, the punch radius must be **less than 400 mm**, *or* the sheet must be bottomed / coined.

---

### Example 5 — Spring Back Fraction (Quick Mental Check)

**Quick formula** for the *fractional* radius increase:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R_f} = \frac{R_p - R_f}{R_f} = \frac{3 \sigma_Y R_f}{E t - 3 \sigma_Y R_f}$$

For Example 1: $\Delta R / R_f = (3 \times 280 \times 20)/(420{,}000 - 16{,}800) = 16{,}800/403{,}200 = 0.0417$ → ~4.2% radius increase. Matches.

---

## 3.6 The Decision-Matrix for Spring Back Estimation

| If you know... | Compute using... | To find... |
|---|---|---|
| Material ($E$, $\sigma_Y$), $t$ | $\Delta K = 3\sigma_Y/(Et)$ | Curvature change |
| $R_f$ | $R_p = R_f/(1 - 3\sigma_Y R_f/Et)$ | Final radius |
| $\theta_f$, $R_f$ | $\Delta\theta = R_f \theta_f \cdot 3\sigma_Y/(Et)$ | Angle change |
| $\theta_p$ (desired) | $\theta_{\text{die}} = \theta_p(1 + 3\sigma_Y R_p/Et)$ | Compensation angle |
| All of the above | FEA (AutoForm / PAM-STAMP) | Realistic part shape |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Elastic–Plastic Bending & Spring Back Geometry

> The physical "bending over a punch" geometry is best shown as a functional schematic. The Mermaid block below traces the sequence of curvature states a sheet metal goes through during a bending operation — from initial flat, to loaded (forming), to unloaded (sprung back). This is the conceptual flow every KTU examiner expects to see in a high-scoring answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["State A: Flat Sheet\nCurvature K = 0\nRadius R = infinity"] --> B["Loading Phase\nPunch applies bending moment M"]
    B --> C["State B: Formed (Loaded)\nCurvature Kf = 1 over Rf\nBending moment M at Mp"]
    C --> D{"Unloading\nM released\nElastically"}
    D --> E["State C: Sprung Back\nCurvature Kp = 1 over Rp\nwhere Kp less than Kf"]
    E --> F["Final Part Geometry\nPart ready for assembly"]
    F --> G{"Delta K positive?\nStandard spring back"}
    G --> H["Radius increases\nAngle opens up\nCompensate die"]
    G --> I["Spring forward\nRare case\nTensile stress state"]

    style A fill:#dbe9ff,stroke:#1f4e89,color:#0b2545
    style C fill:#ffe2b3,stroke:#b35c00,color:#5a2d00
    style E fill:#c8f0c2,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#103e10
    style F fill:#f3d1f3,stroke:#7a1f7a,color:#3a0a3a
    style H fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#a07000,color:#3d2a00
    style I fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a02020,color:#500000
```

**How to read this diagram in the exam:**

- The **horizontal axis** represents the progression of the operation: load → form → unload → final part.
- The **three curvature states** ($K = 0$, $K_f$, $K_p$) correspond to flat, formed, and sprung-back.
- The **branch on the right** distinguishes standard spring back from the rare spring-forward case.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Spring Back Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Parameters"]
        I1["Material Data\nE and sigmaY"]
        I2["Sheet Thickness t"]
        I3["Target Part Geometry\ntheta_p, R_p"]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE["Computation Engine"]
        C1["Calculate Delta K\nequals 3 sigmaY over E t"]
        C2["Calculate Delta theta\nequals Ln times Delta K"]
        C3["Calculate Die Radius Rf\nfrom desired Rp"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Die Tooling Output"]
        O1["Compensated Punch Angle\ntheta_die = theta_p + Delta theta"]
        O2["Over-bent Punch Radius\nRf"]
        O3["Iterative Try-out\nAdjust if measured part deviates"]
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACK["Feedback Loop"]
        F1["Measure actual part angle\nCompare with theta_p"]
        F2["Recompute Delta theta\nRe-cut die"]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I1 --> C2
    I2 --> C2
    I3 --> C3
    C1 --> C3
    C3 --> O2
    C2 --> O1
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> O1

    style INPUT fill:#e7f0ff,stroke:#1f4e89,color:#0b2545
    style COMPUTE fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b8860b,color:#5c3d00
    style OUTPUT fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#103e10
    style FEEDBACK fill:#fde0e0,stroke:#a02020,color:#500000
```

**Interpretation for the student:**

- **Input block** — every compensation calculation starts with $E$, $\sigma_Y$, $t$, and the **target** part geometry (final, post-spring-back values).
- **Computation block** — applies the three core KTU formulas in sequence.
- **Output block** — produces the over-bent die angle and the punch radius to be cut.
- **Feedback block** — represents the **die try-out loop** practised in industry. The first try-out rarely matches; iterative re-cutting (typically 2–4 iterations) is the norm.

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Stress Distribution Across the Bent Section

> A side-view schematic of a bent sheet showing the elastic core and plastic zones is the most-missed diagram in KTU scripts. The Mermaid below renders the stress-state zonation as a labelled block diagram (the *physical* drawing is impossible in Mermaid; the block-level abstraction carries the same information).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Outer fibre r equals Rf plus t over 2\nTensile plastic zone\nStress equals plus sigmaY"] --> B["Transition r greater than Rn\nElastic unloading zone\nStress linear in r"]
    B --> C["Neutral surface r equals Rn\nStress equals 0\nPurely elastic hinge"]
    C --> D["Transition r less than Rn\nElastic unloading zone\nStress linear in r"]
    D --> E["Inner fibre r equals Rf minus t over 2\nCompressive plastic zone\nStress equals minus sigmaY"]

    A -.->|On unload| A1["Elastic strain recovers\nTensile stress drops to zero"]
    E -.->|On unload| E1["Elastic strain recovers\nCompressive stress drops to zero"]
    B -.->|On unload| B1["Unloads along slope E\nReaches zero residual stress"]
    C -.->|On unload| C1["Remains at zero stress\nStrain becomes residual"]

    style A fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a02020,color:#500000
    style E fill:#d6e0ff,stroke:#2030a0,color:#101a50
    style B fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#a07000,color:#3d2a00
    style D fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#a07000,color:#3d2a00
    style C fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#404040,color:#101010
```

**Reading guide:**

- **Top block (red)** = plastically *tensile* outer fibre, fully yielded.
- **Middle block (grey)** = neutral surface — no longitudinal stress, but a *strain* remains after unloading.
- **Bottom block (blue)** = plastically *compressive* inner fibre.
- **Yellow side blocks** = elastic core — the only zones that *recover* on unloading.
- The dashed arrows on the right show the **unloading sequence**: each zone returns to its residual stress (zero for elastic zones; the plastic zones stay at the residual stress that existed at the moment of unloading).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define spring back in sheet metal forming. State the formula for the change in curvature on unloading and explain why it is independent of the bend radius."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Spring back is the elastic recovery of a sheet metal component that occurs after the forming load is removed, resulting in a change in radius and/or angle from the tool geometry.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$\Delta K = \frac{3\sigma_Y}{E t}$$

**Independence of $R_f$ (1 mark):** The change in curvature depends only on material properties ($E$, $\sigma_Y$) and sheet thickness $t$. This is because the elastic unloading stiffness $EI/b = E t^3/12$ is **inversely proportional to $t^3$**, while the plastic moment $m_p = \sigma_Y t^2 / 4$ is **proportional to $t^2$** — the ratio $\Delta K = 12 m_p/(E t^3)$ retains no radius dependence, because the bending moment at the instant of unloading is set by the *material's plastic capacity*, not by the geometry of the bend.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"List any four factors that increase spring back in a sheet-metal bending operation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each, or ¾ + ¾ + ¾ + ¾):**

1. **High $E/\sigma_Y$ ratio** of the material (e.g., high-strength steel, titanium).
2. **Thinner sheet** (since $\Delta K \propto 1/t$).
3. **Larger bend radius / larger developed length** of the bend (since $\Delta\theta = L_n \cdot \Delta K$).
4. **Lower work-hardening exponent** (more ideal-plastic behaviour).
5. **Air-bending (no bottoming)** — bottoming / coining suppresses spring back.
6. **Lower forming temperature** (hot forming reduces $\sigma_Y$ and hence $\Delta K$).

(Any four valid points; full marks.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Module-4 Internal-Choice Format:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

A cold-rolled mild-steel sheet of thickness $t = 1.5$ mm, elastic modulus $E = 210$ GPa, and yield strength $\sigma_Y = 260$ MPa is bent over a punch of radius $R_f = 12$ mm through a forming angle of $90°$.

**(a)** Derive the formula for the change in curvature $\Delta K$ during spring back, clearly stating the assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate (i) the final radius $R_p$, (ii) the spring back angle $\Delta\theta$ in degrees, and (iii) the die angle that should be cut to obtain a final part angle of $90°$. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions (2 marks):*
1. Elastic–perfectly-plastic material; neglect work-hardening.
2. Plane sections remain plane (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis).
3. Wide sheet (plane stress, since $b \gg t$).
4. Neutral axis at mid-thickness during both loading and unloading.
5. Bending moment at the instant of unloading equals the fully plastic moment $M_p$.

*Derivation (5 marks):*

The fully plastic moment of a rectangular cross-section (width $b$, thickness $t$) is:

$$M_p = \sigma_Y \cdot Z = \sigma_Y \cdot \frac{b t^2}{4}$$

Per unit width:

$$m_p = \frac{M_p}{b} = \frac{\sigma_Y t^2}{4} \quad \cdots (1)$$

The elastic bending stiffness per unit width (using $I = bt^3/12$):

$$\frac{EI}{b} = \frac{E t^3}{12} \quad \cdots (2)$$

On unloading, the change in curvature is governed by the elastic relationship:

$$\Delta K = \frac{m_p}{(EI)/b} = \frac{\sigma_Y t^2/4}{E t^3/12} = \frac{3 \sigma_Y}{E t} \quad \blacksquare \quad \cdots (3)$$

This is independent of $R_f$ because the plastic moment is a *material property* at full yield, not a geometric quantity.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

*Given:* $t = 1.5$ mm, $E = 210$ GPa = $2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\sigma_Y = 260$ MPa, $R_f = 12$ mm, $\theta_f = 90° = \pi/2$ rad.

**(i) Final radius (2 marks):**

$$\Delta K = \frac{3 \times 260}{2.1 \times 10^5 \times 1.5} = \frac{780}{3.15 \times 10^5} = 2.476 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$K_f = \frac{1}{R_f} = \frac{1}{12} = 0.0833 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$K_p = K_f - \Delta K = 0.0833 - 0.002476 = 0.0809 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$R_p = \frac{1}{K_p} = 12.37 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Spring back angle (3 marks):**

Neutral-axis developed length:

$$L_n = R_f \cdot \theta_f = 12 \times \frac{\pi}{2} = 18.85 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta\theta = L_n \cdot \Delta K = 18.85 \times 2.476 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0467 \text{ rad}$$

Convert to degrees:

$$\Delta\theta = 0.0467 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 2.67°$$

Final part angle: $\theta_p = 90° - 2.67° = 87.33°$

**(iii) Compensation angle (2 marks):**

To obtain a final part angle of $90°$, the die must be over-bent by $2.67°$:

$$\theta_{\text{die}} = 90° + 2.67° = 92.67°$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common mistakes that cost marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** $E$ in GPa and $\sigma_Y$ in MPa — students often forget to convert and get a 1000× error.
> 2. **Confusing $R_f$ with $R_p$:** The punch radius is *not* the final radius. Always compute $R_p$ explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert radians to degrees** (or vice versa) in the final answer.
> 4. **Using $R_f$ instead of $R_p$** in the neutral-axis arc length for compensation; the *correct* arc length uses the final (post-spring-back) geometry.
> 5. **Sign error in spring-forward cases** — the formula is $K_f - K_p = +\Delta K$ in the standard case; reversing it gives a negative $R_p$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the phenomenon of spring back in a sheet metal bending operation. Discuss the effect of the $E/\sigma_Y$ ratio on spring back magnitude. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 1.0 mm thick aluminium sheet is to be bent to a final included angle of $60°$ with a final inside radius of 8 mm. The material properties are $E = 70$ GPa, $\sigma_Y = 180$ MPa. Determine: (i) the change in curvature, (ii) the punch (forming) radius required, and (iii) the punch (forming) angle required. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks):**

*Sketch / Description (3 marks):* [Refer to the Mermaid schematic in Section 4.1 — a flat sheet is bent over a punch to curvature $K_f = 1/R_f$. On release, the curvature drops to $K_p = 1/R_p$, with the included angle changing from $\theta_f$ to $\theta_p$. The outer fibre was plastically stretched; the inner fibre plastically compressed. The elastic component (in the elastic core and the elastic part of the outer/inner fibres) recovers.]

*Mechanism of spring back (2 marks):* On unloading, the elastic strain $\varepsilon_e$ at every fibre vanishes, leaving only the residual plastic strain $\varepsilon_p = \varepsilon_{total} - \varepsilon_e$. Because plastic strain varies linearly across the thickness, the final shape is a circular arc of *larger* radius and *smaller* included angle than the punch geometry.

*Effect of $E/\sigma_Y$ (2 marks):*

$$\Delta K = \frac{3 \sigma_Y}{E t} = \frac{3}{t} \cdot \frac{\sigma_Y}{E}$$

The ratio $\sigma_Y/E$ is the **yield strain** $\varepsilon_Y$. Therefore:

$$\Delta K = \frac{3 \varepsilon_Y}{t}$$

- A **high $E/\sigma_Y$ ratio** (low yield strain) means a small elastic zone and *small* $\Delta K$? — Wait, careful: actually **low $E/\sigma_Y$** (i.e., $\sigma_Y/E$ large) means large $\Delta K$ and large spring back. Equivalently, **high $E/\sigma_Y$** (the modulus is large compared to yield, e.g., $E$ = 200 GPa, $\sigma_Y$ = 200 MPa) means $\Delta K$ is small.

  > **Correction for clarity:** Spring back $\Delta K \propto \sigma_Y/E$. So materials with **low $\sigma_Y/E$** (i.e., high $E/\sigma_Y$ ratio) have **less** spring back. Aluminium ($E/\sigma_Y \approx 70/0.18 \approx 389$) has *less* spring back than high-strength steel ($E/\sigma_Y \approx 210/1.0 = 210$)... let me recompute:
  >
  > For aluminium: $\sigma_Y/E = 180/70000 = 0.00257$
  > For HSS: $\sigma_Y/E = 1000/210000 = 0.00476$
  >
  > HSS has higher $\sigma_Y/E$, hence **more** spring back. So **higher $\sigma_Y/E$ → more spring back**; **higher $E/\sigma_Y$ → less spring back**.

  > [!NOTE]
  > **Final correct interpretation:** A **high $E/\sigma_Y$ ratio** corresponds to a *stiff* material that yields easily relative to its stiffness — this gives **less** spring back. A **low $E/\sigma_Y$** (high $\sigma_Y/E$) means a *strong* material relative to its stiffness, producing **more** spring back.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

*Given:* $t = 1.0$ mm, $E = 70$ GPa = $7 \times 10^4$ MPa, $\sigma_Y = 180$ MPa, $R_p = 8$ mm (final inside radius), $\theta_p = 60° = \pi/3$ rad (final included angle).

**(i) Change in curvature (2 marks):**

$$\Delta K = \frac{3 \times 180}{7 \times 10^4 \times 1.0} = \frac{540}{70000} = 7.714 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Punch (forming) radius (2 marks):**

From $K_p = K_f - \Delta K$:

$$K_f = K_p + \Delta K = \frac{1}{8} + 7.714 \times 10^{-3} = 0.125 + 0.007714 = 0.1327 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

$$R_f = \frac{1}{K_f} = \frac{1}{0.1327} = 7.535 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Punch (forming) angle (3 marks):**

Final neutral-axis arc length:

$$L_n = R_p \cdot \theta_p = 8 \times \frac{\pi}{3} = 8.378 \text{ mm}$$

Spring back angle:

$$\Delta\theta = L_n \cdot \Delta K = 8.378 \times 7.714 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0646 \text{ rad} = 3.70°$$

Punch (die) angle:

$$\theta_f = \theta_p + \Delta\theta = 60° + 3.70° = 63.7°$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Board-Exam Pitfall — Question B Specific:**
> 1. Students often **invert the formula** when the question asks for $R_f$ given $R_p$. Remember: $K_f = K_p + \Delta K$ (not $-$).
> 2. When asked for the punch angle, **over-bend** the die — the punch angle is *larger* than the desired part angle.
> 3. The neutral-axis arc length is the *invariant* quantity in a bending problem — not the punch arc length, not the final inside-arc length. State this explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Spring Back: 60-Second Recap**

- **Definition:** Spring back = elastic recovery after unloading → radius **increases**, angle **decreases** (standard case).
- **Core Equation:** $\Delta K = \dfrac{3\sigma_Y}{E t}$ — depends on **material + thickness only**, *not* on radius.
- **Final radius formula:** $R_p = \dfrac{R_f}{1 - 3\sigma_Y R_f/(E t)}$
- **Minimum bend radius** (before part springs flat): $R_{f,\min} = E t / (3 \sigma_Y)$
- **Spring back angle:** $\Delta\theta = L_n \cdot \Delta K$ where $L_n$ = neutral-axis developed length.
- **Compensation rule:** $\theta_{\text{die}} = \theta_{\text{part}} + \Delta\theta$ (over-bend the die).
- **Plane-strain correction** (very wide sheets): replace $\sigma_Y$ by $2\sigma_Y/\sqrt{3}$ in the $\Delta K$ formula.
- **Higher $E/\sigma_Y$ ratio → less spring back**; lower ratio → more spring back.
- **Spring forward** is the rare case where $\Delta K < 0$ (very high tensile in-plane stress, some HSS).
- **Practical suppressors of spring back:** bottoming / coining, over-bending the die, stretch-bend (tensile flange load), warm/hot forming, proper lubrication, sequenced multi-stage bending.
- **Units to watch:** $E$ in MPa (not GPa) when $\sigma_Y$ is in MPa — this is the #1 marks-loser in numerical answers.
- **Radians vs. degrees:** always state which one; convert consistently using $\pi/180$ or $180/\pi$.
- **Industry analogue:** Spring back estimation is what determines the iterative die try-out cost in automotive panel manufacturing (2–4 iterations are typical).
- **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "estimate" spring back, *show the formula derivation in 3–4 lines, then plug numbers*. The derivation is the method; numbers alone fetch only partial credit.

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Estimation of Spring Back**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems. Extrusion - Metal Flow
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Numerical Problems on Extrusion – Metal Flow

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Extrusion** is a compression-based metal forming process in which a billet of material is forced to flow plastically through a shaped die orifice under high compressive stress, producing a product of uniform cross-section that mirrors the die profile. The deformation is achieved by applying force through a punch (in *indirect/cold* extrusion) or a ram (in *direct/hot* extrusion) acting on a confined billet enclosed within a container.

**Sub-classifications required for KTU 2024 Module 4:**

| Type | Process Characteristics |
|------|--------------------------|
| **Direct (Forward) Extrusion** | Ram and product move in the *same* direction; billet slides relative to container walls. |
| **Indirect (Backward) Extrusion** | Die is mounted on a hollow ram; product moves *opposite* to the ram; no billet–container friction. |
| **Hot Extrusion** | Performed *above* the recrystallization temperature (e.g., Aluminum at 350–500 °C). |
| **Cold Extrusion** | Performed at *room temperature* (e.g., steel, copper cold heading). |
| **Hydrostatic Extrusion** | Billet is pressurized by a fluid; eliminates ram–billet friction entirely. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 – Core Focus:** The syllabus explicitly prioritizes (a) derivation of extrusion pressure/force, (b) the *defects caused by non-uniform metal flow*, and (c) solving numerical problems on extrusion force using the **Johnson, Siebel, and modified Sachs equations**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pushing a tube of toothpaste from the back. The paste inside the tube is the **billet**, the opening at the tip is the **die orifice**, and your hand is the **ram**. The paste *flows plastically* and takes the shape of the opening.

- If the opening is small (high *extrusion ratio*), your hand must apply **greater force** — this is precisely why extrusion pressure increases with the extrusion ratio.
- If the inside walls of the tube were rough (high **container friction**), you'd need even *more force* to push the paste out — mirroring the difference between *direct* (friction present) and *indirect* (friction absent) extrusion.
- If you pushed the paste too fast, it might "snag" or form a pipe-like void in the center — analogous to the **central burst defect** (a critical KTU question topic).

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Standardized)

- **Extrusion Ratio (R)** = $R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \dfrac{D_0^2}{D_f^2}$ (for round sections)
- **Reduction (%)** = $(1 - \dfrac{1}{R}) \times 100$
- **True Strain (ε)** = $\ln(R)$ (also called *extrusion strain*)
- **Die Angle (2α)** — included semi-angle typically 30° to 60° for best balance
- **Flow Stress ($\sigma_f$)** — the average plastic flow stress of the billet material at the working temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** Always convert extrusion ratio to *true strain* using $\varepsilon = \ln R$ before substituting into any pressure formula. Students frequently lose marks by directly using the *engineering strain* $(R-1)$ instead of *true strain* $\ln R$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Metal Flow in Extrusion

Metal flow in extrusion is **inherently non-uniform** because of three simultaneous factors:

1. **Friction at the container walls** (only in *direct* extrusion) — outer layers lag behind the center.
2. **Redundant work at the die corners** — the metal shears and re-orients around the die cone.
3. **Dead metal zone formation** — a stagnant region forms at the die entry, *insulating* active flow.

### 2.1.1 Stages of Metal Flow (KTU Frequently Tested)

When the ram advances, the deformation proceeds in three well-defined stages:

- **Stage 1 — Upsetting / Initial Compression:** The billet shortens, fills the container radially. Flow is *uneven*; **dead metal zone begins to form** at the corners.
- **Stage 2 — Steady-State Flow:** A stable funnel-shaped flow pattern is established. The dead-metal zone remains roughly constant. This is the stage during which **90% of the product length is extruded**.
- **Stage 3 — Piping / Tail-End Defect:** As the ram approaches the die, the central core of the billet flows out *faster* than the periphery, forming an inverted "V"-shaped cavity called the **extrusion pipe defect**. To prevent this, a *dummy block* (or *bolster*) is used to retain the discard.

### 2.1.2 Why the Dead Metal Zone Matters

The dead metal zone is **not actually stationary** at the microscopic level — it is a region of intense, localized shear where material is in *turbulent* flow. It behaves like a *natural die* with its own effective angle, and it **increases extrusion pressure** because it adds a redundant shear strain component on top of the ideal homogeneous deformation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Almost every KTU numerical problem on extrusion includes either (a) the dead-metal zone effect on pressure or (b) the die-angle optimization to minimize the redundant work. You must know *why* die angle has an optimum at 2α ≈ 60°.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

The following table consolidates **every formula** you need to solve a Module 4 extrusion numerical. Memorize them.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Applicable To |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Ideal Homogeneous Pressure (no friction, no redundant work)** | $P_{id} = \sigma_f \ln R$ | $P_{id}$ in MPa; $\sigma_f$ in MPa; $R$ dimensionless | Lower-bound estimate |
| 2 | **Siebel's Equation (with friction, no redundant work)** | $P = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{2L}{D_0} \cdot \dfrac{\mu}{\sin\alpha} \right)$ | $L$ = billet length; $D_0$ = billet diameter; $\mu$ = friction factor; $2\alpha$ = die semi-angle | Direct extrusion, simple estimate |
| 3 | **Johnson's Formula (with container friction AND redundant work)** | $P = \sigma_f \left( a + b \ln R \right)$ | $a, b$ are empirical constants (b ≈ 0.8 to 1.0; a depends on geometry) | Most KTU problems |
| 4 | **Modified Sachs Equation (for cone die with dead-metal zone)** | $P = \sigma_f \left[ \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \ln R + \dfrac{2\mu L}{D_0 - D_f} \right]$ | Used for *forward* extrusion through conical die | Direct hot extrusion |
| 5 | **Extrusion Force (Ram Force)** | $F = P \cdot A_0$ | $F$ in kN or N; $A_0$ = initial billet area | Any extrusion |
| 6 | **True Strain in Extrusion** | $\varepsilon = \ln R = \ln \left( \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} \right)$ | Dimensionless | All problems |
| 7 | **Average Flow Stress (when σ₀ and σ_f are given separately)** | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{\sigma_0 + \sigma_f^{end}}{2}$ | Used for long billets | Hot extrusion |
| 8 | **Power Required** | $\text{Power} = F \cdot v_{ram}$ | $v_{ram}$ = ram speed (m/s) | Indirect KTU question |
| 9 | **Die Pressure (Cone Die, ignoring friction)** | $P_d = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} \right)$ | $2\alpha$ in radians | Die-angle optimization |
| 10 | **Optimum Die Angle Condition** | $\dfrac{dP}{d\alpha} = 0 \Rightarrow 2\alpha \approx 60°$ | Empirical optimum | KTU theory |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Tip Used Above:** I have used `\dfrac` (display-style fraction) and `\dfrac` inside all inline formulas to ensure consistent rendering in markdown. Where vertical lines occur (e.g., friction term `2L/D₀`), they are written as `\vert` or in non-vertical contexts to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Aluminum Profile Manufacturing:** Automotive and aerospace companies use the **Siebel equation** to size hydraulic presses for producing extruded aluminum window frames, heat sinks, and structural sections.
- **Copper Tube Production:** Indirect extrusion with **Johnson's formula** is used to design presses for seamless copper tubes used in HVAC and refrigeration.
- **Steel Cold Extrusion:** Forged connecting rods and gear blanks are produced via cold extrusion; the **modified Sachs equation** predicts ram loads accurately because of the conical die geometry.
- **Lead/Cable Sheathing:** Continuous hydrostatic extrusion (no friction) is governed purely by the **ideal pressure $P_{id} = \sigma_f \ln R$** — used as a baseline benchmark in industry.

## 2.4 Defects Caused by Non-Uniform Metal Flow (KTU 2024 – High Weightage)

| Defect | Cause | Visual Pattern | Cure |
|--------|-------|----------------|------|
| **Central Burst (Chevron Crack)** | Excessive redundant work at low die angles; high friction | Internal star-shaped crack in the middle of the product | Increase die angle; reduce friction; use proper lubrication |
| **Extrusion Pipe (Tail-End Defect)** | Steady-state flow disrupted at end of stroke | Inverted V-shaped cavity at billet tail | Use dummy block; discard the tail |
| **Surface Cracking ("Orange Peel")** | Hot shortness; tensile stresses on surface at high temp | Rough, scaly surface | Reduce temperature; increase speed |
| **Die-Line / Scratches** | Surface of die is rough or scratched | Longitudinal lines on product | Polish die; use proper lubrication |
| **Bursting (in indirect extrusion)** | Tensile stresses at the back face of the billet | Crack at rear face | Reduce ram speed; use proper die angle |
| **Flow lines / striations** | Non-uniform grain flow | Visible bands on etched cross-section | Use proper die angle, lubrication |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The **central burst** defect is *inversely* related to die angle. At very small die angles (2α < 30°), the redundant work becomes excessive, generating tensile hydrostatic stresses at the center → central burst. At very large die angles (2α > 90°), friction and bending stresses dominate → surface cracking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ideal Homogeneous Extrusion Pressure (Slab Method)

**Assumption:** No friction, no redundant work. The deformation is a *pure, uniform compression* in the axial direction from area $A_0$ to area $A_f$.

Consider a slab of billet at axial position $z$, with cross-section $A_0$. As the ram pushes, a virtual element undergoes a differential strain $d\varepsilon$.

The incremental pressure to cause this increment is:

$$dP = \sigma_f \, d\varepsilon$$

The true strain in extrusion is:

$$\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln R$$

Therefore, integrating from zero strain to the final strain $\ln R$:

$$P_{id} = \int_0^{\ln R} \sigma_f \, d\varepsilon = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R$$

Hence, the **ideal homogeneous pressure** is:

$$\boxed{P_{id} = \sigma_f \ln R}$$

> This is the *lower-bound* (best-case) pressure. Real extrusion pressure will always be higher because of friction and redundant work.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Siebel's Equation (Friction Included)

**Assumption:** Slab method; friction at the *container walls* acting on the entire billet length $L$. Die is conical with semi-angle $\alpha$.

### Step 1: Force balance on a slab of length $dz$

Let $P$ be the local pressure at axial position $z$. The slab of cross-section $A_0$ and length $dz$ experiences:

- Pressure on the left face: $P \cdot A_0$ (pushes right)
- Pressure on the right face: $(P + dP) \cdot A_0$ (pushes left)
- Shear friction on the cylindrical wall: $\tau \cdot \pi D_0 \cdot dz$
- Where $\tau = \mu P$ (Coulomb friction)

### Step 2: Equilibrium equation

$$P \cdot A_0 = (P + dP) \cdot A_0 + \mu P \cdot \pi D_0 \cdot dz$$

### Step 3: Simplify

$$-A_0 \, dP = \mu P \cdot \pi D_0 \, dz$$

$$\dfrac{dP}{P} = -\dfrac{\mu \pi D_0}{A_0} \, dz$$

For a circular billet, $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi D_0^2}{4}$, so $\dfrac{\pi D_0}{A_0} = \dfrac{4}{D_0}$.

$$\dfrac{dP}{P} = -\dfrac{4\mu}{D_0} \, dz$$

### Step 4: Integrate from ram end (z = 0) to die entry (z = L)

$$\int_{P_{ram}}^{P_{die}} \dfrac{dP}{P} = -\int_0^L \dfrac{4\mu}{D_0} \, dz$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{die}}{P_{ram}}\right) = -\dfrac{4\mu L}{D_0}$$

$$P_{ram} = P_{die} \cdot e^{\frac{4\mu L}{D_0}}$$

### Step 5: The die pressure $P_{die}$ includes both the ideal deformation and redundant work over the die cone

For a conical die with semi-angle $\alpha$ and effective shear along the cone:

$$P_{die} = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} \right)$$

### Step 6: Combine to get Siebel's full equation

$$\boxed{P = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} \right) e^{\frac{4\mu L}{D_0}}}$$

> **Simplified KTU-friendly form** (using $\sin 2\alpha \approx 1$ for the typical $2\alpha \approx 60°$ die):

$$P \approx \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{4\mu L}{D_0} \right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Johnson's Empirical Formula

Johnson observed that for hot extrusion, the extrusion pressure $P$ is *approximately linear* in $\ln R$:

$$P = \sigma_f (a + b \ln R)$$

Where:
- $a \approx 0.8$ to $1.5$ (depends on friction and die angle)
- $b \approx 0.8$ to $1.0$ (close to 1 for low-friction indirect extrusion)

For a typical aluminum hot extrusion with conical die:

$$\boxed{P = \sigma_f (0.8 + 1.0 \ln R)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is preferred in KTU problems:** Johnson's formula is the *most frequently tested* in university exams because it requires only two constants $a, b$ and a single $\ln R$ term. The examiner can give you $\sigma_f$, $R$, and constants, and expects a single-line numerical answer.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effect of Die Angle on Redundant Work

The redundant shear strain along the die cone (semi-angle $\alpha$) is:

$$\varepsilon_{red} = \dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha}$$

This function has a minimum at $2\alpha = 60°$:

$$\dfrac{d}{d\alpha}\left( \dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} \right) = 0 \Rightarrow \tan 2\alpha = 2\alpha$$

Numerically solving: $2\alpha \approx 60°$.

Substituting back:

$$\varepsilon_{red, \min} = \dfrac{60° \cdot \pi/180°}{\sin 60°} = \dfrac{1.047}{0.866} \approx 1.21$$

> **Interpretation:** The *minimum* redundant shear strain is ~1.21. Below 2α = 60°, redundant work rises sharply (causing central burst); above 60°, friction and bending stresses rise (causing surface cracking).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problems (Analytical Matrix)

### Problem 1: Direct Extrusion Force Calculation (Johnson's Formula)

> **Problem:** A billet of aluminum (flow stress $\sigma_f = 80$ MPa) is hot-extruded with extrusion ratio $R = 16$. Using Johnson's formula with $a = 0.8$ and $b = 1.0$, calculate the extrusion pressure. If the billet diameter is $D_0 = 100$ mm, find the extrusion force.

**Step 1 — Compute $\ln R$:**

$$\ln R = \ln 16 = 2.773$$

**Step 2 — Apply Johnson's equation:**

$$P = \sigma_f (a + b \ln R) = 80 \times (0.8 + 1.0 \times 2.773)$$

$$P = 80 \times 3.573 = 285.84 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute billet cross-section area:**

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi D_0^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (100)^2}{4} = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute extrusion force:**

$$F = P \cdot A_0 = 285.84 \times 7853.98 = 2.245 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 2245 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### Problem 2: Siebel's Equation with Friction

> **Problem:** A brass billet ($D_0 = 75$ mm, $L = 200$ mm) is extruded through a conical die (semi-angle $\alpha = 30°$) to a final diameter $D_f = 25$ mm. Average flow stress $\sigma_f = 200$ MPa; friction factor $\mu = 0.08$. Estimate the extrusion pressure using Siebel's equation.

**Step 1 — Extrusion ratio:**

$$R = \left( \dfrac{D_0}{D_f} \right)^2 = \left( \dfrac{75}{25} \right)^2 = 9$$

**Step 2 — True strain:**

$$\ln R = \ln 9 = 2.197$$

**Step 3 — Redundant shear term:**

$$\dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} = \dfrac{2 \times 30° \times \pi/180°}{\sin 60°} = \dfrac{1.047}{0.866} = 1.209$$

**Step 4 — Friction exponent term:**

$$e^{\frac{4\mu L}{D_0}} = e^{\frac{4 \times 0.08 \times 200}{75}} = e^{0.853} = 2.347$$

**Step 5 — Combine all terms in Siebel's equation:**

$$P = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} \right) e^{\frac{4\mu L}{D_0}}$$

$$P = 200 \times (2.197 + 1.209) \times 2.347$$

$$P = 200 \times 3.406 \times 2.347 = 1598.8 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 1599 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** This is a very high pressure. In real practice, hot extrusion of brass uses lower $\sigma_f$ (~100 MPa) and better lubrication. The numerical above is designed to test your ability to chain multiple terms correctly.

---

### Problem 3: Direct vs Indirect Extrusion Force Comparison

> **Problem:** A 6061 aluminum billet ($\sigma_f = 100$ MPa, $\mu = 0.1$, $D_0 = 120$ mm, $L = 250$ mm) is extruded to $D_f = 30$ mm through a 60° die. Calculate: (a) Force for direct extrusion, (b) Force for indirect extrusion, (c) % saving by using indirect.

**Step 1 — Extrusion ratio and true strain:**

$$R = (120/30)^2 = 16 \Rightarrow \ln R = 2.773$$

**Step 2 — Redundant work for 60° die:**

$$\dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} = \dfrac{60° \times \pi/180°}{\sin 60°} = \dfrac{1.047}{0.866} = 1.209$$

**Step 3 — Direct extrusion (with container friction):**

$$P_{dir} = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + 1.209 \right) e^{\frac{4\mu L}{D_0}}$$

$$P_{dir} = 100 \times (2.773 + 1.209) \times e^{\frac{4 \times 0.1 \times 250}{120}}$$

$$P_{dir} = 100 \times 3.982 \times e^{0.833} = 100 \times 3.982 \times 2.301 = 916.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Indirect extrusion (no container friction):**

$$P_{indir} = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + 1.209 \right) \times 1 = 100 \times 3.982 = 398.2 \text{ MPa}$$

> (No exponential term because there is no billet–container relative motion.)

**Step 5 — Force calculations:**

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi (120)^2}{4} = 11309.7 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F_{dir} = 916.4 \times 11309.7 = 10.36 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 10360 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{indir} = 398.2 \times 11309.7 = 4.50 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 4502 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage saving:**

$$\% \text{ saving} = \dfrac{F_{dir} - F_{indir}}{F_{dir}} \times 100 = \dfrac{10360 - 4502}{10360} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ saving} \approx 56.5\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Insight:** A 50–60% force reduction is *typical* when switching from direct to indirect extrusion. This is why modern aluminum extrusion presses overwhelmingly prefer the indirect (tubular) configuration despite its higher die-cost.

---

### Problem 4: Optimum Die Angle for Minimum Pressure

> **Problem:** A copper billet ($\sigma_f = 250$ MPa) is extruded with $R = 10$. Compare the extrusion pressure at 2α = 30°, 60°, and 90°, ignoring friction.

**Step 1 — Compute $\ln R$:**

$$\ln R = \ln 10 = 2.303$$

**Step 2 — Pressure formula (no friction):**

$$P = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Case A: 2α = 30°:**

$$\dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} = \dfrac{30° \times \pi/180°}{\sin 30°} = \dfrac{0.5236}{0.5} = 1.047$$

$$P_A = 250 \times (2.303 + 1.047) = 250 \times 3.350 = 837.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Case B: 2α = 60°:**

$$\dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} = \dfrac{1.047}{0.866} = 1.209$$

$$P_B = 250 \times (2.303 + 1.209) = 250 \times 3.512 = 878.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Case C: 2α = 90°:**

$$\dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} = \dfrac{1.571}{1.0} = 1.571$$

$$P_C = 250 \times (2.303 + 1.571) = 250 \times 3.874 = 968.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison:**

| Die Angle 2α | Redundant Term | Total Pressure (MPa) |
|:------------:|:--------------:|:--------------------:|
| 30° | 1.047 | 837.5 |
| **60° (Optimum)** | **1.209** | **878.0** |
| 90° | 1.571 | 968.5 |

> **Important:** Although $P$ is *minimum* at the *smallest* die angle in the frictionless case, **defects** (central burst) become severe. In practice, 2α ≈ 60° is the *engineering optimum* — balancing pressure, defect avoidance, and product quality.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Extrusion Process — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Billet Loading] --> B[Container Preheating]
    B --> C[Ram Engagement]
    C --> D{Extrusion Type}
    D -- Direct --> E1[Ram Force on Billet]
    D -- Indirect --> E2[Die Mounted on Hollow Ram]
    E1 --> F1[Container Wall Friction Active]
    E2 --> F2[No Container Friction]
    F1 --> G[Metal Flow Through Die Cone]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Dead Metal Zone Formation]
    H --> I[Product Emerges from Die]
    I --> J{Quality Check}
    J -- Pass --> K[Cutoff and Cool]
    J -- Fail --> L[Defect: Central Burst / Pipe / Crack]
    K --> M[Finished Extruded Section]
    L --> N[Process Parameter Adjustment]
    N --> C
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Stages of Metal Flow in Direct Extrusion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stage 1: Upsetting] --> B[Billets Fills Container]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Steady-State Flow]
    C --> D[Funnel-Shaped Velocity Profile]
    D --> E[Stage 3: Piping at Tail]
    E --> F[Dummy Block Retains Discard]
    F --> G[Ram Retracts]
    G --> H[Next Cycle]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Extrusion Pressure Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Total Extrusion Pressure P] --> I[Ideal Homogeneous Work]
    P --> R[Redundant Work at Die Cone]
    P --> F[Friction Work at Container]
    I --> I1[sigma_f x ln R]
    R --> R1[sigma_f x 2alpha over sin 2alpha]
    F --> F1[Exponent of 4 mu L over D0]
    I1 --> Sum[Summed in Siebel/Johnson Equation]
    R1 --> Sum
    F1 --> Sum
    Sum --> Out[Final Ram Force F = P x A0]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Direct vs Indirect Extrusion (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DIRECT["DIRECT EXTRUSION"]
        D1[Ram] --> D2[Force on Billet]
        D2 --> D3[Billet in Container]
        D3 --> D4[Container Wall Friction]
        D4 --> D5[Die at Container End]
        D5 --> D6[Product Moves Same Direction as Ram]
    end
    
    subgraph INDIRECT["INDIRECT EXTRUSION"]
        I1[Hollow Ram] --> I2[Die Mounted on Ram]
        I2 --> I3[Billet in Container]
        I3 --> I4[No Container Wall Friction]
        I4 --> I5[Product Moves Opposite to Ram]
    end
```

## 4.5 Defect-Cause-Effect Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Non-Uniform Metal Flow] --> B[Central Burst]
    A --> C[Extrusion Pipe]
    A --> D[Surface Cracking]
    A --> E[Die Lines]
    B --> B1[Cure: Increase Die Angle]
    C --> C1[Cure: Use Dummy Block]
    D --> D1[Cure: Lower Temperature]
    E --> E1[Cure: Polish Die]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1` style) to comply with Mermaid syntax safeguards. No reserved keywords are used as node names. Labels are kept clean — no markdown formatting inside the node text — for maximum Mermaid rendering reliability.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define extrusion and distinguish between direct and indirect extrusion.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Extrusion is a compressive metal forming process in which a billet is forced through a die orifice to produce a product of uniform cross-section.
- **Direct (1 mark):** Ram pushes the billet directly toward a stationary die; billet slides along the container wall → friction present.
- **Indirect (1 mark):** Die is mounted on a hollow ram; the product moves *opposite* to the ram direction; no relative motion between billet and container → friction absent.

### Question 2: What is the dead metal zone in extrusion? State its significance.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The dead metal zone is a stagnant region that forms at the corners between the billet and the die cone where material does not flow into the die.
- **Significance (2 marks):** (a) It acts as a *natural extension* of the die, increasing the effective die angle. (b) It contributes to *redundant work*, raising the extrusion pressure. (c) It promotes *uniform flow* in the central region of the billet.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Derive Siebel's equation for extrusion pressure and apply it to a numerical problem.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) Derivation of Siebel's equation (7 marks):**

Starting with the slab method, consider a differential element of the billet at distance $z$ from the ram:

**Step 1:** Equilibrium of slab (thickness dz, area A₀):

$$P \cdot A_0 = (P + dP) A_0 + \mu P \cdot \pi D_0 \, dz$$

**Step 2:** Simplify and separate variables:

$$-dP = \dfrac{4\mu P}{D_0} dz \Rightarrow \dfrac{dP}{P} = -\dfrac{4\mu}{D_0} dz$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from $z = 0$ (ram) to $z = L$ (die entry):

$$\int_{P_{ram}}^{P_{die}} \dfrac{dP}{P} = -\int_0^L \dfrac{4\mu}{D_0} dz$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{die}}{P_{ram}}\right) = -\dfrac{4\mu L}{D_0}$$

$$P_{ram} = P_{die} \cdot e^{\frac{4\mu L}{D_0}}$$

**Step 4:** Pressure at die entry = ideal deformation + redundant shear:

$$P_{die} = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$\boxed{P = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} \right) e^{\frac{4\mu L}{D_0}}}$$

**(b) Numerical application (7 marks):**

> **Problem:** A steel billet ($D_0 = 100$ mm, $L = 300$ mm, $\sigma_f = 350$ MPa, $\mu = 0.05$) is extruded through a 60° die to $D_f = 25$ mm. Calculate the extrusion pressure using Siebel's equation.

**Solution:**

$$R = (100/25)^2 = 16 \Rightarrow \ln R = 2.773$$

$$\dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} = \dfrac{1.047}{0.866} = 1.209$$

$$e^{\frac{4 \times 0.05 \times 300}{100}} = e^{0.6} = 1.822$$

$$P = 350 \times (2.773 + 1.209) \times 1.822 = 350 \times 3.982 \times 1.822$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 2540 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Ram Force:**

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi (100)^2}{4} = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F = P \cdot A_0 = 2540 \times 7853.98 \approx 1.995 \times 10^7 \text{ N} = 19,950 \text{ kN}$$

---

### Question B: Explain the various defects in extrusion caused by non-uniform metal flow. How are they controlled?
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) Defects and their causes (7 marks):**

| Defect | Location | Cause |
|--------|----------|-------|
| **Central Burst (Chevron Crack)** | Inside the product, near center axis | Tensile hydrostatic stress at center due to high redundant work (low die angle) |
| **Extrusion Pipe** | Tail end of billet | Dead metal zone collapses at end of stroke, drawing outer material inward |
| **Surface Cracking** | Outer surface of product | Excessive die temperature or improper lubrication; high tensile surface stress |
| **Die Lines** | Longitudinal lines on product | Die surface roughness or contamination |
| **Blistering** | Subsurface | Dissolved gases expanding at high temperature |

**(b) Control measures (7 marks):**

- **Central Burst (2 marks):** Use die angle 2α ≈ 60°; employ effective lubrication; reduce redundant work; if material is hard, increase homogenization time.
- **Extrusion Pipe (2 marks):** Use a *dummy block* and discard the tail portion; maintain proper billet length to length-to-diameter ratio ($L/D_0 \leq 4$).
- **Surface Cracking (1.5 marks):** Control billet temperature; use proper lubrication (e.g., graphite for hot extrusion); use conical or streamline dies.
- **Die Lines (1.5 marks):** Polish the die land; clean the die cavity before each run; use nitrided or hardened die surfaces.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 
> **Most common mark-losing mistake in this question:**
> 1. Students confuse **central burst** with **surface cracking**. Central burst is *internal*; surface cracking is *external*. The KTU key explicitly allocates marks for stating the correct *location* of each defect.
> 2. Students forget to mention the **die-angle dependency** of central burst. If you write "central burst is caused by friction" you will lose 1.5 marks. The *correct* cause is excessive **redundant work** due to *small die angle*.
> 3. Students omit the **dummy block** cure for the extrusion pipe defect. This is a recurring 1-mark loss.
> 4. In numerical problems, students use the *engineering strain* $(R-1)$ instead of *true strain* $\ln R$. This single substitution error leads to ~10% error in the final answer and full marks are not awarded.
> 5. For Siebel's equation, students often forget the $\dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha}$ term and only use $\ln R$. Always state the *complete* equation on your answer sheet.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Exam-Ready Memory Map — KTU Module 4: Extrusion Metal Flow**

- [ ] **Extrusion definition:** Compression forming through a die orifice; produces uniform cross-section product.
- [ ] **Direct vs Indirect:** Direct has container friction; Indirect has no friction (force saving ~50%).
- [ ] **Hot vs Cold:** Hot is above recrystallization (Al, Cu, brass); Cold is at room temperature (steel, ductile metals).
- [ ] **Three stages of metal flow:** Upsetting → Steady-state flow → Piping at tail end.
- [ ] **Dead metal zone:** Stagnant region at die corner; increases redundant work and extrusion pressure.
- [ ] **Extrusion ratio:** $R = A_0 / A_f = (D_0/D_f)^2$ for round sections.
- [ ] **True strain:** $\varepsilon = \ln R$ — *always* use this in pressure formulas.
- [ ] **Ideal pressure:** $P_{id} = \sigma_f \ln R$ (theoretical minimum).
- [ ] **Siebel's equation:** $P = \sigma_f \left( \ln R + \dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha} \right) e^{\frac{4\mu L}{D_0}}$ — most common in KTU.
- [ ] **Johnson's formula:** $P = \sigma_f (a + b \ln R)$ — empirical, requires given constants.
- [ ] **Optimum die angle:** 2α ≈ 60° (balances redundant work and surface friction).
- [ ] **Central burst:** Internal crack; caused by small die angle + high friction.
- [ ] **Extrusion pipe:** Tail-end cavity; cured by dummy block.
- [ ] **Surface cracking:** External defect; cured by proper temperature and lubrication.
- [ ] **Force = Pressure × Area:** $F = P \cdot A_0$ — always the final step in a numerical.
- [ ] **Power = Force × Ram Speed:** Useful for indirect sub-questions on energy.
- [ ] **Unit check:** $\sigma_f$ in MPa, $A_0$ in mm² → F in N. Convert to kN by dividing by 1000.
- [ ] **Typical values to remember:** Aluminum hot extrusion: $\sigma_f \approx 80$–150 MPa, $\mu \approx 0.1$–0.2, $R$ up to 100.
- [ ] **Industry context:** Indirect extrusion saves ~50% force; used for aluminum and copper tubes.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When you see a question on extrusion force, immediately write down the *five* building blocks in this order:
> 1. Compute $R$ and $\ln R$
> 2. Identify which formula the question wants (Ideal / Siebel / Johnson)
> 3. Compute friction term $e^{4\mu L / D_0}$ *only if direct extrusion*
> 4. Compute redundant term $\dfrac{2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha}$ *only if Siebel*
> 5. Multiply $P$ by $A_0$ to get the ram force.
> 
> Following this checklist guarantees you will not skip any term and lose marks unnecessarily.

---

*End of Module 4 Notes — Numerical Problems on Extrusion Metal Flow (KTU 2024 Scheme, Course Code: PBMET304)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanics of Extrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mechanics of Extrusion # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Extrusion **Extrusion** is a compressive metal forming process in which a billet (or slug) of material is forced to flow plastically through a shaped die opening, producing a continuous product of constant cross-section that matches the die profile. The deformation is achieved by applying an axial compressive force via a ram (or punch) on the billet confined within a container. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "Extrusion is a metal forming process in which a block of metal (billet) is forced by compression to flow through a die orifice of the desired cross-section, thereby producing a long product of uniform cross-section." ### Classification of Extrusion (As per KTU Module 4) | Parameter | Types | |---|---| | Direction of metal flow | **Direct (Forward) Extrusion** & **Indirect (Reverse/Backward) Extrusion** | | Temperature of working | **Hot Extrusion** & **Cold Extrusion** | | Tooling arrangement | **Rod Extrusion** & **Tube/Seamless Pipe Extrusion** | | Die geometry | **Conical Die**, **Flat Die (Square Die)** & **Streamlined Die** | ## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Toothpaste Tube:** > Think of how toothpaste comes out of a tube. When you squeeze the closed end of the tube, the paste flows forward (towards the open nozzle) and exits in a continuous ribbon whose shape matches the nozzle opening. > > - The **tube body** = the **container/billet holder** of the extruder. > - Your **fingers squeezing** = the **ram applying compressive force**. > - The **paste flowing out** = the **billet deforming plastically**. > - The **nozzle** = the **die** (which determines the final cross-section). > > If you were to fix the nozzle and push the paste backwards (impossible with toothpaste, but possible in industry), that would be **indirect extrusion**. ## 1.3 Why is Mechanics of Extrusion Important? Understanding the mechanics allows engineers to: 1. **Predict the extrusion load** required (size of the hydraulic press). 2. **Select the correct die angle** to minimize power consumption. 3. **Identify the optimum billet length-to-diameter ratio** to avoid defects. 4. **Estimate die life** by knowing contact pressure and friction distribution. 5. **Choose between direct and indirect extrusion** based on material, length, and quality requirements. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Standard Values Used in Mechanics of Extrusion:** > - **Homogeneous Deformation Strain** is given by: $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ > - **Extrusion Ratio (R)** = $R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$ where $A_0$ is billet cross-section and $A_f$ is final product cross-section. > - **Reduction in Area (%)** = $\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{R}\right) \times 100$ > - **Typical Hot Extrusion Temperature for Aluminum Alloys:** $350^\circ C$ to $500^\circ C$ > - **Typical Friction Factor (m) in hot extrusion:** $0.05$ to $0.25$ > - **Mean Flow Stress ($\sigma_f$ or $Y_f$):** Average of initial and final yield stresses at the working temperature. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanics of Direct (Forward) Extrusion — Stage-wise Analysis In direct extrusion, the ram and the metal flow in the **same direction**. The process is divided into three distinct stages: ### Stage 1: Upsetting (Initial Compression) - The billet is placed in the container. - As the ram descends, the billet is upsetted (shortened and bulged sideways due to Poisson's effect). - The metal fills the container completely and starts to extrude through the die. - **No extrusion occurs yet** — only compressive deformation. ### Stage 2: Steady-State Extrusion (Steady Flow) - Metal flows steadily through the die. - The extrusion load is **maximum** in this stage (peak load). - The deformation zone remains roughly constant in size and shape — it travels *up* the billet as extrusion proceeds. - A dead metal zone (DMZ) forms at the corners of the container, against the die face. ### Stage 3: Pipe Formation / Defect Stage - Towards the end of the billet, the central portion of the metal flows faster than the outer portion (which is slowed by friction at the container walls). - This non-uniform flow creates a **central burst or "piping" defect** at the rear end of the extruded product. - The remaining disc of metal (the "butt" or "disc") is discarded. ## 2.2 Mechanics of Indirect (Reverse/Backward) Extrusion - The die is mounted on a **hollow ram**, and the container moves instead of the ram. - There is **no relative motion** between the billet and the container walls. - **No friction at the billet-container interface** — significantly lower extrusion force (~25–30% lower than direct). - **No dead metal zone** is formed. - **No piping defect** is produced. - **Limitation:** Limited product length (constrained by the hollow ram length) and lower stiffness of the hollow ram. ## 2.3 The Ideal Work of Extrusion (Homogeneous Deformation) If we assume **no friction** and **no redundant work**, the work done in extrusion is the ideal work — the minimum theoretical work needed for the deformation. **Step-by-step logic for ideal extrusion work:** 1. Consider a small element of length $dx$ at a position $x$ from the die exit. 2. The cross-sectional area at this element is $A_x$ (decreases from $A_0$ to $A_f$). 3. The flow stress at this point is $Y_x$ (which may be constant or change with strain). 4. For an infinitesimal strain increment, $d\varepsilon = -\dfrac{dA_x}{A_x}$. 5. Integrating from $A_0$ to $A_f$: $$W_{ideal} = \int_{A_f}^{A_0} Y_x \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot dA \bigg/ A$$ For a **constant flow stress** $Y_f$: $$\boxed{W_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = Y_f \cdot V_0 \cdot \ln(R)}$$ where $V_0 = A_0 \cdot L_0$ is the initial volume of the billet. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the master formula reference for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Mechanics of Extrusion.** | S.No. | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |:---:|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Extrusion Ratio | $R = A_0 / A_f$ | dimensionless | $A_0$ = billet area, $A_f$ = product area | | 2 | True (Homogeneous) Strain | $\varepsilon = \ln R = \ln(A_0 / A_f)$ | dimensionless | Always positive | | 3 | Reduction in Area | $\% RA = (1 - 1/R) \times 100$ | % | Common form for exam | | 4 | Ideal Work (constant $Y_f$) | $W_i = Y_f A_0 \ln R$ | J (or N·m) | Minimum work needed | | 5 | Ideal Work (variable flow stress) | $W_i = V_0 \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} Y_f \, d\varepsilon$ | J | Use flow curve | | 6 | Extrusion Pressure (Ideal) | $p_i = Y_f \ln R$ | Pa or N/mm² | Ram pressure | | 7 | Johnson’s Approx. Extrusion Pressure | $p = Y_f \left[ a + b \ln R \right]$ | Pa | Empirical; $a$, $b$ depend on friction, die angle, redundant work | | 8 | Average Pressure (with friction) | $p = Y_f \left[ (1 + \ln R) + \dfrac{2L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{m}{\sqrt{3}} \right]$ | Pa | Direct extrusion, conical die | | 9 | Extrusion Force (Direct) | $F = p \cdot A_0$ | N | $A_0$ = ram area | | 10 | Extrusion Force (Indirect) | $F = Y_f A_0 \left[ \ln R + \dfrac{2L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{m}{\sqrt{3}} \right] - F_{friction}$ | N | No container friction | | 11 | Power Required | $P_{power} = F \cdot v_{ram}$ | W (or kW) | $v_{ram}$ = ram velocity | | 12 | Optimum Die Angle (Cone) | $\alpha_{opt} \approx 60^\circ$ for hot, $\approx 45^\circ$ for cold | degrees | Minimizes total work | | 13 | Deformation Zone Length (cone) | $\ell = (D_0 - D_f) / (2 \tan \alpha)$ | mm | Cone die geometry | | 14 | Friction Hill (Forward Extrusion) | $\Delta p = \dfrac{4 Y_f L m}{D \sqrt{3}}$ | Pa | Extra pressure due to container friction | > [!NOTE] > **Why the $\dfrac{2L}{D}$ term appears:** In the formula, $L$ is the **remaining billet length** in the container and $D$ is the container diameter. As extrusion proceeds, $L$ decreases continuously, so the friction hill also decreases — this is why the ram pressure is **highest at the start of steady flow** and falls to a near-constant value. ## 2.5 Effect of Process Parameters on Extrusion Pressure | Parameter | Effect on Extrusion Pressure | KTU 2024 Key Insight | |---|---|---| | **Extrusion Ratio (R)** | Pressure increases with $\ln R$ | Doubling R increases work only modestly (logarithmic) | | **Die Angle ($\alpha$)** | Minimum pressure at $\alpha \approx 45^\circ$ to $60^\circ$ | Smaller angle → more redundant work; larger angle → more friction | | **Friction (m)** | Increases pressure linearly | Container friction dominates in long billets | | **Temperature** | Hot extrusion → much lower pressure | But hot → surface oxidation, shorter die life | | **Strain Hardening** | Higher $Y_f$ → higher pressure | Cold extrusion needs much greater force | | **Billet Length (L)** | Longer billet → more friction → higher pressure | Indirect extrusion eliminates this | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Automotive industry** — Aluminum alloy pistons, body panels, and chassis sections are produced by hot direct extrusion. 2. **Aerospace** — Titanium and Inconel turbine blade preforms and fuselage stringers use hot indirect extrusion. 3. **Construction** — Aluminum window frames, door sections, and heat sinks are continuous direct extrusions. 4. **Civil engineering** — Lead pipes, copper tubes (later drawn) start as extruded slugs. 5. **Consumer goods** — PVC pipes, aluminum foil (after further rolling) — extrusion principles govern both metals and polymers. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Extrusion Pressure for Direct Extrusion Through a Conical Die (with Friction) > [!NOTE] > This is a **high-weightage KTU 2024 derivation** — frequently asked for 7–14 marks. ### Assumptions 1. Billet is a rigid-perfectly plastic material (no strain hardening) at flow stress $Y_f$. 2. Coefficient of friction $\mu$ is constant (or friction factor $m$). 3. Conical die with semi-cone angle $\alpha$. 4. Deformation zone is a truncated cone of length $\ell$. 5.
