Redundant work is incorporated via the empirical factor. ### Setup Consider a conical die of entry diameter $D_0$ and exit diameter $D_f$, semi-cone angle $\alpha$. **Geometry of the deformation zone:** $$\ell = \frac{D_0 - D_f}{2 \tan \alpha}$$ **Billet is a cylinder of length $L$ remaining and diameter $D$ inside the container.** ### Step 1: Elemental Force Balance (Slab Method) Consider an elemental slice of the billet at distance $x$ from the die entry, of thickness $dx$. The slice has area $A_x$ (varies along the cone). Force balance on the slice: - Compressive force on left face: $F_x$ - Compressive force on right face: $F_x + dF_x$ - Pressure from die wall (normal): $p \cdot dA_s$ where $dA_s$ is the slant surface area. - Friction shear: $\tau \cdot dA_s = m \cdot Y_f \cdot dA_s$ For a conical surface element: $$dA_s = \frac{\pi D_x \, dx}{\cos \alpha} \cdot (\text{circumferential area projected on slant})$$ In differential form along the axis: $$dF_x = Y_f \cdot dA_x + p \cdot \pi D_x \tan \alpha \, dx + m Y_f \cdot \pi D_x \, dx / \cos \alpha$$ After simplification and using the continuity equation $A_x v_x = A_0 v_{ram}$ (incompressibility): ### Step 2: Integrating Across the Die For a cone, $D_x$ varies linearly from $D_0$ to $D_f$: $$D_x = D_0 - \frac{(D_0 - D_f)}{\ell} x$$ Integrating across the deformation zone length $\ell$: $$p_{die} = Y_f \left[ 1 + \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{m}{\sin \alpha} \cdot \ln R \right]$$ This is the **die friction contribution**. ### Step 3: Adding the Homogeneous Work and Redundant Work The total extrusion pressure has **three contributions**: $$p_{total} = p_{ideal} + p_{die\,friction} + p_{container\,friction} + p_{redundant}$$ In the simplified **Johnson's approximation** form: $$\boxed{p = Y_f \left[ (a + b) + \ln R + \frac{2L}{D} \cdot \frac{m}{\sqrt{3}} \right]}$$ where: - $a$ = contribution from redundant work (depends on die angle) - $b$ = contribution from die friction (depends on $\alpha$ and $m$) - $\dfrac{2L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{m}{\sqrt{3}}$ = container friction (bill friction) - $\ln R$ = ideal work / homogeneous work ### Step 4: Final Form of Average Pressure (KTU Standard Form) $$\boxed{p_{avg} = Y_f \left[ \ln R + \frac{2L}{D} \cdot \frac{m}{\sqrt{3}} \right] \text{ (for ideal conical die, no redundant)}}$$ $$\boxed{p_{avg} = Y_f \left[ 1 + \ln R + \frac{2L}{D} \cdot \frac{m}{\sqrt{3}} \right] \text{ (including redundant work, } a \approx 1\text{)}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimum Die Angle for Minimum Extrusion Pressure > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Expected Question:** "Derive the condition for optimum die angle in extrusion." The total extrusion pressure as a function of die angle $\alpha$ has two competing terms: - **Friction term** in the die → increases as $\alpha$ decreases (longer contact length). - **Redundant work term** → increases as $\alpha$ increases (more bending of flow lines). ### Step-by-step: Let $p(\alpha) = Y_f \left[ \ln R + C_1 \cdot f(\alpha)_{friction} + C_2 \cdot g(\alpha)_{redundant} \right]$ For minimum pressure: $$\frac{dp}{d\alpha} = 0$$ For a conical die, the redundant work contribution is empirically given as: $$W_{redundant} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} Y_f V_0 \cdot \frac{\cot \alpha}{\sqrt{1 + \cot^2 \alpha}}$$ For small-to-moderate $\alpha$, redundant work $\propto \cot \alpha$ → increases as $\alpha$ decreases. For friction, total die contact length $\ell = \dfrac{D_0 - D_f}{2 \tan \alpha}$ → increases as $\alpha$ decreases. Differentiating and equating to zero: $$\frac{d}{d\alpha}\left[\frac{\cot \alpha}{\sqrt{1 + \cot^2 \alpha}}\right] = \frac{d}{d\alpha}\left[\frac{2m \ln R}{\sin \alpha}\right]$$ For hot extrusion (low $m$, high $\ln R$): $\boxed{\alpha_{opt} \approx 60^\circ}$ For cold extrusion (higher $m$): $\boxed{\alpha_{opt} \approx 45^\circ}$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Extrusion Force and Power for a Practical Problem > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (KTU Exam Style):** > > **Problem:** An aluminum billet of diameter 100 mm and length 200 mm is extruded at $400^\circ C$ through a die of exit diameter 40 mm. The mean flow stress at this temperature is $Y_f = 60$ MPa. The friction factor at the container wall is $m = 0.1$. The ram velocity is 20 mm/s. Calculate: > (a) Extrusion ratio > (b) Homogeneous strain > (c) Average extrusion pressure > (d) Maximum extrusion force > (e) Power required ### Given Data - $D_0 = 100$ mm, $D_f = 40$ mm - $L_0 = 200$ mm (initial billet length) - $Y_f = 60$ MPa = 60 N/mm² - $m = 0.1$ - $v_{ram} = 20$ mm/s - Assume redundant work factor $a = 1$ (default in KTU problems) ### Solution **Step (a): Extrusion Ratio** $$R = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \frac{D_0^2}{D_f^2} = \frac{100^2}{40^2} = \frac{10000}{1600} = 6.25$$ **Step (b): Homogeneous (True) Strain** $$\varepsilon = \ln R = \ln(6.25) = 1.8326$$ **Step (c): Average Extrusion Pressure** Using the standard KTU formula (including redundant work and friction): $$p_{avg} = Y_f \left[ 1 + \ln R + \frac{2L_0}{D_0} \cdot \frac{m}{\sqrt{3}} \right]$$ Substituting: $$p_{avg} = 60 \left[ 1 + 1.8326 + \frac{2 \times 200}{100} \cdot \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{3}} \right]$$ Calculate the friction term: $$\frac{2 \times 200}{100} \cdot \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{3}} = 4 \times 0.0577 = 0.2309$$ Therefore: $$p_{avg} = 60 \times (1 + 1.8326 + 0.2309) = 60 \times 3.0635 = 183.81 \, \text{MPa}$$ **Step (d): Maximum Extrusion Force** $$F_{max} = p_{avg} \times A_0 = 183.81 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 100^2$$ $$F_{max} = 183.81 \times 7853.98 = 1{,}443{,}471 \, \text{N} \approx 1443.5 \, \text{kN}$$ **Step (e): Power Required** $$P_{power} = F_{max} \times v_{ram} = 1{,}443{,}471 \times 20 \, \text{mm/s}$$ $$P_{power} = 28{,}869{,}420 \, \text{N·mm/s} = 28.87 \, \text{kW}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** Always check whether the problem asks for **maximum** force (start of steady state, full billet length) or **average** force (over the entire stroke). Maximum force is at the beginning of the steady flow stage. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Direct vs Indirect Extrusion | Parameter | Direct (Forward) Extrusion | Indirect (Reverse) Extrusion | |---|---|---| | Relative motion between billet & container | Yes — friction present | None — no friction | | Container friction term | $\dfrac{2L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{m}{\sqrt{3}}$ added | **Absent** | | Friction hill / peak | Pronounced | Minimal | | Extrusion force | Higher (baseline) | **25–30% lower** | | Product length | Unlimited (continuous) | Limited by hollow ram length | | Dead metal zone | Yes — at die corners | No | | Piping defect | Possible at end | Avoided | | Ram stiffness | Solid (high) | Hollow (lower) | | Billet preparation | Simple | More complex (lubrication critical) | | Typical applications | Long structural sections | High-purity tubes, short precision parts | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Extrusion Press > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of a hydraulic extrusion press system — an alternative to physical drawings which cannot be rendered in text. ```mermaid flowchart TD billetA[Billet
Heated to Working Temp] containerB[Heated Container

Continuous Section] discardF[Discard Butt
Remnant Disc] dmzG[Dead Metal Zone
At Die Corners] frictionH[Container Wall Friction
Opposes Motion] billetA --> containerB containerB --> ramC ramC -->|"Axial Compression"| dieAssemblyD dieAssemblyD -->|"Plastic Flow"| extrudedE billetA -.->|"Last 5-10%"| discardF containerB -.->|"Forms at corners"| dmzG ramC -.->|"Shear stress mYf"| frictionH frictionH -.->|"Increases ram load"| ramC dmzG -.->|"Defines flow path"| dieAssemblyD ``` ## 4.2 Stage-Wise Load Diagram (Extrusion Force vs Ram Stroke) > [!NOTE] > This Mermaid flowchart represents the **three classical stages** of direct extrusion and how the extrusion force evolves during each. ```mermaid flowchart LR s1A[Stage 1: Upsetting
Load rises rapidly] s1B[Billett bulges and fills container] s2A[Stage 2: Steady Flow

Load drops sharply at the end] s3B[Central burst defect forms] s1A --> s1B s1B --> s2A s2A --> s2B s2B --> s3A s3A --> s3B ``` ## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Mechanics of Extrusion (Cause-Effect Map) ```mermaid flowchart TD inputA[Process Inputs] pA1[Billet Geometry L0 D0] pA2[Material Flow Stress Yf] pA3[Die Geometry alpha Df] pA4[Friction Factor m] pA5[Ram Velocity v] processB[Mechanics Engine] pB1[Ideal Work Yf V0 ln R] pB2[Container Friction Term 2L m by D sqrt3] pB3[Redundant Work from Die Angle] pB4[Total Extrusion Pressure p] outputC[Engineering Outputs] pC1[Required Ram Force F] pC2[Press Tonnage Selection] pC3[Power kW Rating] pC4[Defect Risk Assessment] pC5[Die Life Prediction] inputA --> processB pA1 --> pB1 pA1 --> pB2 pA2 --> pB1 pA3 --> pB3 pA4 --> pB2 pA4 --> pB3 pB1 --> pB4 pB2 --> pB4 pB3 --> pB4 pB4 --> outputC pC1 --> pC2 pC4 --> pC5 ``` ## 4.4 Direct vs Indirect Extrusion — Comparative Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DirectExt["DIRECT EXTRUSION - Ram and Flow in Same Direction"] D1[Container fixed] D2[Ram pushes forward] D3[Billet-container friction PRESENT] D4[Dead metal zone FORMS] D5[Peak load HIGHER] end subgraph IndirectExt["INDIRECT EXTRUSION - Ram and Flow in Opposite Directions"] I1[Container moves] I2[Die on hollow ram] I3[NO billet-container friction] I4[NO dead metal zone] I5[Peak load LOWER by 25-30%] end DirectExt -.->|"Trade-off: Higher force, no length limit"| IndirectExt ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]** **Define extrusion. List any four defects that occur in direct extrusion.** **Model Answer:** **Extrusion** is a compression metal forming process in which a billet is forced under high compressive load to flow through a die orifice of desired cross-section, producing a long product of uniform cross-section. **Four defects in direct extrusion:** 1. **Piping defect (central burst)** — non-uniform flow at the end of the billet. 2. **Surface cracking** — due to excessive die friction or low temperature. 3. **Surface lines / die lines** — from surface scoring by the die. 4. **Hot shortness / tearing** — when extrusion temperature is too close to the incipient melting point of low-melting constituents. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Compare direct and indirect extrusion on the basis of (i) friction and (ii) product length.** **Model Answer:** | Criterion | Direct Extrusion | Indirect Extrusion | |---|---|---| | (i) Friction | Billet moves relative to container — **friction present** at the container wall, contributing a friction hill | Die moves with billet — **no relative motion**, no container friction | | (ii) Product length | **Unlimited** (continuous) since the die is fixed and the ram can travel long distances | **Limited** by the length of the hollow ram that holds the die | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Derive the average extrusion pressure expression for direct extrusion through a conical die, clearly showing the contributions of (i) ideal/homogeneous work, (ii) redundant work, and (iii) container friction. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** An aluminum alloy billet of diameter 75 mm and length 300 mm is hot extruded at $450^\circ C$ through a conical die with exit diameter 25 mm. The mean flow stress is 50 MPa and the friction factor at the container wall is $m = 0.12$. Taking the redundant work factor as unity, calculate the (i) extrusion ratio, (ii) average extrusion pressure, and (iii) maximum ram force. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution to A(a): **Setup:** Consider a cylindrical billet of initial length $L_0$ and diameter $D_0$ in a container. The conical die has semi-cone angle $\alpha$, entry diameter $D_0$, and exit diameter $D_f$. **Total average extrusion pressure** has three components: $$p_{total} = p_{ideal} + p_{redundant} + p_{friction}$$ **Component 1 — Ideal / Homogeneous Work:** Assuming uniform deformation, the work per unit volume is the true strain: $$p_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot \varepsilon = Y_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = Y_f \cdot \ln R$$ This represents the **minimum theoretical pressure** assuming no friction and no redundant work. **Component 2 — Redundant Work:** Flow lines inside the die are not straight — they curve near the entry and exit. This causes additional internal shear, called **redundant work**. For a conical die, empirically: $$p_{redundant} \approx Y_f \cdot \cot \alpha \cdot \frac{\text{geometry factor}}{\sqrt{3}}$$ Simplified (KTU standard form), this is absorbed as a constant $\approx Y_f$: $$p_{redundant} \approx Y_f$$ **Component 3 — Container (Bill) Friction:** Consider a billet of length $L$ remaining in the container of diameter $D$. The wall shear stress (using friction factor $m$): $$\tau = m \cdot \frac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}}$$ Total friction force on the cylindrical wall: $$F_{friction} = \tau \cdot \pi D \cdot L = \frac{m Y_f \pi D L}{\sqrt{3}}$$ Pressure equivalent over the billet cross-section $A_0 = \pi D^2 / 4$: $$p_{friction} = \frac{F_{friction}}{A_0} = \frac{4 m Y_f L}{D \sqrt{3}} = \frac{2 m Y_f}{ \sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{2L}{D}$$ Simplifying: $$p_{friction} = Y_f \cdot \frac{2L}{D} \cdot \frac{m}{\sqrt{3}}$$ **Final Combined Expression:** $$\boxed{p_{avg} = Y_f \left[ 1 + \ln R + \frac{2L}{D} \cdot \frac{m}{\sqrt{3}} \right]}$$ #### Model Solution to A(b): **Given:** - $D_0 = 75$ mm, $L_0 = 300$ mm, $D_f = 25$ mm - $Y_f = 50$ MPa = 50 N/mm² - $m = 0.12$, redundant factor = 1 **(i) Extrusion Ratio:** $$R = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \frac{D_0^2}{D_f^2} = \frac{75^2}{25^2} = \frac{5625}{625} = 9.0$$ **(ii) Average Extrusion Pressure:** $$p_{avg} = Y_f \left[ 1 + \ln R + \frac{2L_0}{D_0} \cdot \frac{m}{\sqrt{3}} \right]$$ Calculate each term: - $\ln R = \ln(9.0) = 2.1972$ - $\dfrac{2L_0}{D_0} = \dfrac{2 \times 300}{75} = 8.0$ - $\dfrac{m}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{0.12}{1.732} = 0.0693$ - Friction term: $8.0 \times 0.0693 = 0.5542$ Sum: $1 + 2.1972 + 0.5542 = 3.7514$ Therefore: $$p_{avg} = 50 \times 3.7514 = 187.57 \, \text{MPa}$$ **(iii) Maximum Ram Force:** $$F_{max} = p_{avg} \times A_0 = 187.57 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 75^2$$ $$F_{max} = 187.57 \times 4417.86 = 828{,}617 \, \text{N} \approx 828.6 \, \text{kN}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Question]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the mechanics of indirect extrusion. Highlight (i) the absence of dead metal zone, (ii) absence of friction hill, and (iii) absence of piping defect. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** What is the optimum die angle in extrusion? Explain the competing effects of die friction and redundant work that lead to the optimum value. A copper billet of diameter 50 mm is extruded to a 15 mm diameter rod through a $50^\circ$ conical die. The mean flow stress is 250 MPa, the friction factor is 0.08, and the billet length is 150 mm. Calculate the extrusion force. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution to B(a): **Mechanics of Indirect (Reverse) Extrusion:** In indirect extrusion, the die is mounted on a **hollow, reciprocating ram**, and the container holds the billet stationary or moves against the ram. The metal flows in a direction **opposite** to the ram motion. **Schematic flow of forces:** - The hollow ram carries the die at its end. - The container seals the rear end with a dummy block. - As the ram pushes **into** the container, the die is pushed **backward** relative to the billet, but the product emerges from the die in the **same direction as the ram advances** (i.e., the product travels through the hollow ram). **Three key mechanical features:** **(i) Absence of Dead Metal Zone (DMZ):** In direct extrusion, the DMZ forms at the die corners because the billet is pushed past the stationary die face. In indirect extrusion, the die itself moves with the billet — there is no relative shearing at the die-billet interface, so no metal "sticks" to the corners. **(ii) Absence of Friction Hill:** Since there is **no relative motion** between the billet and the container walls, the shear stress at the wall is zero. Therefore, the friction term $\dfrac{2L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{m}{\sqrt{3}}$ vanishes, and the ram pressure curve is **flat** (no peak then decline). **(iii) Absence of Piping Defect:** In direct extrusion, the central portion of the billet exits faster than the outer portion at the very end, causing a central burst. In indirect extrusion, the flow is more uniform and the entire billet is consumed — no discard butt is produced, so no piping defect occurs. #### Model Solution to B(b): **Concept of Optimum Die Angle:** The total extrusion pressure varies with the die semi-cone angle $\alpha$: - As $\alpha$ **decreases** (more streamlined die) → die contact length increases → **die friction increases**. - As $\alpha$ **increases** (more blunt die) → flow lines bend more sharply → **redundant work increases**. The optimum die angle is the value of $\alpha$ that **minimizes the sum** of friction and redundant work. Typically: - Hot extrusion: $\alpha_{opt} \approx 60^\circ$ - Cold extrusion: $\alpha_{opt} \approx 45^\circ$ **Calculation:** **Given:** - $D_0 = 50$ mm, $D_f = 15$ mm, $\alpha = 50^\circ$, $L_0 = 150$ mm - $Y_f = 250$ MPa, $m = 0.08$ **Step 1: Extrusion Ratio** $$R = \frac{D_0^2}{D_f^2} = \frac{2500}{225} = 11.111$$ **Step 2: Homogeneous Strain** $$\ln R = \ln(11.111) = 2.4079$$ **Step 3: Average Pressure (using KTU standard formula with redundant factor = 1)** $$p_{avg} = Y_f \left[ 1 + \ln R + \frac{2L_0}{D_0} \cdot \frac{m}{\sqrt{3}} \right]$$ Calculate friction term: - $\dfrac{2L_0}{D_0} = \dfrac{2 \times 150}{50} = 6.0$ - $\dfrac{m}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{0.08}{1.732} = 0.0462$ - Friction term: $6.0 \times 0.0462 = 0.2771$ Sum: $1 + 2.4079 + 0.2771 = 3.6850$ $$p_{avg} = 250 \times 3.685 = 921.25 \, \text{MPa}$$ **Step 4: Extrusion Force** $$F = p_{avg} \times A_0 = 921.25 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 50^2$$ $$F = 921.25 \times 1963.5 = 1{,}808{,}855 \, \text{N} \approx 1809 \, \text{kN}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the redundant work term** (the leading "1" in the formula). Many students write $p = Y_f[\ln R + \text{friction}]$ only. KTU awards 2 marks separately for the redundant work contribution. > > 2. **Using initial billet length $L_0$ instead of remaining length $L$ in the friction term.** At the start of steady flow, $L = L_0$ (maximum), so $L_0$ is correct for **peak** force — but if the question asks for **average** force, the examiner may deduct marks unless this is justified. > > 3. **Confusing $\ln R$ with $(R - 1)$ or $(1 - 1/R)$.** The KTU answer key strictly uses $\ln(A_0/A_f)$. Substitution of an incorrect strain form leads to 0 marks for the entire calculation sub-part. > > 4. **Not converting units consistently.** If $Y_f$ is in MPa and dimensions in mm, the area in mm² gives force in N directly. Mixing cm and mm is a frequent error. > > 5. **Omitting the "1" redundant factor for a conical die at $\alpha \approx 45^\circ$–$60^\circ$.** This is the **default assumption** in KTU problems unless otherwise stated. > > 6. **In derivation questions, students often skip the slab method force balance.** Always write the force balance on an elemental disc explicitly: $F_x + dF_x = F_x + Y_f dA_x + \text{friction terms}$. > > 7. **Drawing the "Die Lines" defect under the wrong category.** Die lines belong to **surface defects**, not internal defects. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Mechanics of Extrusion (KTU Module 4)** ### 🔑 Must-Memorize Core Definitions - **Extrusion:** Compressive flow of metal through a die of desired cross-section. - **Billet:** The starting cylindrical slug of metal. - **Butt / Disc:** The leftover disc of metal at the end of the cycle. - **Dead Metal Zone (DMZ):** Region of metal at die corners that does not flow. - **Friction Hill:** The rise in extrusion pressure at the start of steady flow due to friction. - **Piping Defect:** Central burst at the end of the extruded product. ### 🔑 Must-Memorize Master Formula (THE single most important equation) $$p_{avg} = Y_f \left[ 1 + \ln R + \frac{2L}{D} \cdot \frac{m}{\sqrt{3}} \right]$$ - The "**1**" = redundant work factor (for a well-designed conical die). - The "**$\ln R$**" = ideal / homogeneous work. - The "**$\dfrac{2L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{m}{\sqrt{3}}$**" = container friction. ### 🔑 Key Numerical Values to Remember - $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ - $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$, $\ln(3) \approx 1.099$, $\ln(5) \approx 1.609$, $\ln(10) \approx 2.303$ - Optimum die angle: **60° (hot), 45° (cold)** - Friction factor in hot extrusion: **$m = 0.05$ to $0.25$** - Indirect extrusion saves: **25–30% force** over direct ### 🔑 Key Comparisons (Frequently Asked) - **Direct vs Indirect** — friction, defects, product length, ram stiffness. - **Hot vs Cold** — flow stress, surface finish, dimensional accuracy, die life. - **Conical vs Flat Die** — flow uniformity, redundant work, die life. ### 🔑 Common KTU Exam Question Patterns 1. **"Define extrusion and explain its types."** — 7 mark descriptive. 2. **"Derive the extrusion pressure for direct extrusion."** — 7 or 14 mark derivation. 3. **"A billet of ... is extruded. Calculate the force/power."** — 7 mark numerical. 4. **"Explain the defects in extrusion with diagrams."** — 7 mark descriptive. 5. **"Discuss the effect of die angle / friction / extrusion ratio on pressure."** — 7 mark short notes. ### 🔑 Key Relationships to Recall - $R = (D_0/D_f)^2$ for round billets - $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln R$ - $F = p \times A_0$ (force = pressure × ram area) - $P_{power} = F \times v_{ram}$ (in consistent units) - $W_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot V_0 \cdot \ln R$ (ideal work, constant flow stress)

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **PBMET304 – Manufacturing Processes (Module 4)**, the term **"Numerical Problems"** refers to the application of analytical and empirical equations derived from the mechanics of **bulk metal forming processes** (Rolling, Forging, and Extrusion) and **casting solidification** to compute key process parameters such as forming force, deformation load, torque, power consumption, extrusion pressure, and solidification time. These problems test the student's ability to translate a physical manufacturing scenario into a set of governing equations, identify the correct formula based on process assumptions (e.g., plane strain, homogeneous deformation, ideal work), and arrive at a numerically accurate solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Numerical problems in this module primarily test the **Upper Bound, Lower Bound, and Slab (Equilibrium) methods** of force/pressure estimation in metal forming, along with the **Chvorinov's Rule** for casting solidification. Mastery of these requires fluency in true stress–true strain relationships, friction factor (m) analysis, and the geometric relationship between die/workpiece contact area.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **squeezing a tube of toothpaste** between your fingers.

- The harder you press (and the larger the contact area between your fingers and the tube), the **more force** you need.
- If the tube's surface is **slippery** (less friction), the paste flows out more easily — just like a low friction factor (m) in metal forming reduces the forming load.
- If the paste is **thick** (high flow stress, K), the resistance to flow increases — analogous to using a high-strength alloy that requires a larger press.
- The **speed** at which you squeeze relates to the strain rate, which in turn dictates the power required by the press.

In the same way, every "Numerical Problem" in Module 4 boils down to one core idea: **Given the material properties (K, n), geometry (h₀, h_f, L, D), friction (μ or m), and process speed, find the force/energy/power required to deform the metal.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The bulk of Module 4 numerical problems follow a fixed four-step pattern:
> 1. **Identify** the forming process and extract the given data.
> 2. **Convert** all units to a consistent SI system.
> 3. **Select** the appropriate governing equation (Slab method, ideal work, Chvorinov, etc.).
> 4. **Substitute and solve** carefully, paying attention to the area ratio (R), strain (ε), and friction hill correction.
> 
> Memorizing this 4-step template will allow you to solve **> 90%** of KTU exam questions on this topic.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following standard values/assumptions are frequently used in KTU Module 4 problems:

- **Strain-hardening exponent (n):** typically 0.0 to 0.5 for most metals.
- **Strength coefficient (K):** typically **100 MPa to 1500 MPa** for steels and aluminum alloys.
- **Friction factor (m):** $0 \leq m \leq 1$; for hot forging with graphite lubricant, m ≈ 0.3 to 0.4.
- **Coefficient of friction (μ):** $0 \leq \mu \leq 0.5$ typical for cold metal forming.
- **In Chvorinov's rule:** mold constant B is empirically determined (typically 3 to 6 min/cm² for sand casting).
- **Geometric factor C in forging:** 1.0 (slab), 0.5 to 1.0 (closed-die forging).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation – The Slab Method of Analysis

The **Slab Method** is the cornerstone of force calculation in bulk metal forming. It involves:

1. **Slicing** the deforming workpiece into thin elemental "slabs" perpendicular to the direction of material flow.
2. **Applying equilibrium** to each slab considering internal stresses, friction shear, and the external applied load.
3. **Integrating** across the contact length to obtain the total forming force.

The generic equilibrium equation on a slab of width w and thickness h is:

$$\frac{d\sigma_x}{dx} \pm \frac{2 \tau_f}{h} = 0$$

where $\tau_f$ is the frictional shear stress acting at the die-workpiece interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sticking Friction Assumption:** When $\mu p \geq \frac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}}$ (Tresca) or when $m \to 1$ in the friction factor formulation, the friction shear becomes constant and equal to $\tau_f = m \cdot \frac{Y_f}{2}$, leading to the famous **friction hill** in forging and extrusion.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes **all the governing equations** that are essential for Module 4 numerical problem-solving in KTU 2024 examinations.

| **Process** | **Parameter** | **Governing Formula** | **Assumptions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rolling** | Roll Separating Force | $F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{m \cdot L}{4 \cdot h_f}\right)$ | Plane strain, sticking friction, homogeneous flow |
| **Rolling** | True Strain | $\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | Volume constancy |
| **Rolling** | Average Flow Stress | $Y_f = \frac{K \cdot \epsilon^n}{1+n}$ | Power-law strain hardening |
| **Rolling** | Roll Torque | $T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L$ | Each roll carries half the load |
| **Rolling** | Power | $P = 2 \pi N \cdot T$ | N = rotational speed (rev/s) |
| **Rolling** | Contact Length | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | Small draft, R = roll radius |
| **Forging (Slab)** | Forging Force | $F = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2 m r}{3 h_f}\right)$ | Cylinder upset, sticking friction, $r$ = radius |
| **Forging (Slab)** | Forging Pressure | $p = Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2 m r}{3 h_f}\right)$ | Average pressure on platen |
| **Forging (Energy)** | Ideal Work | $W_i = Y_f \cdot V \cdot \epsilon$ | Homogeneous deformation |
| **Forging (Energy)** | Actual Work (Upper Bound) | $W = W_i / \eta$ | $\eta$ = efficiency (0.3 to 0.8) |
| **Extrusion (Direct)** | Extrusion Pressure | $p = Y_f \cdot \left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)^n \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot e^{2m L / D}$ | Friction factor formulation |
| **Extrusion (Direct)** | Extrusion Force | $F = p \cdot A_0$ | Ram force |
| **Extrusion** | Extrusion Ratio (R) | $R = \frac{A_0}{A_f}$ | Ratio of billet to product area |
| **Drawing** | Drawing Stress | $\sigma_d = Y_f \cdot \left[ \left(1 + \frac{\mu}{\tan \alpha}\right) \cdot \left(1 - \left(\frac{A_f}{A_0}\right)^{(1+\mu \cot \alpha)}\right) + \frac{4 \mu L_f}{D_f} \right]$ | Friction at die and back tension |
| **Casting** | Chvorinov's Rule | $t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$ | $t_s$ = solidification time, B = mold constant |
| **Casting** | Shrinkage Allowance | $S_a = \alpha \cdot (T_m - T_0) \cdot L$ | Linear thermal expansion |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all KTU 2024 numerical answers, students **MUST** state the formula, the substituted values (with units), and the final answer with units. Examiners explicitly award partial marks for:
> - Correct formula identification: **1 mark**
> - Correct unit conversion: **1 mark**
> - Correct substitution: **1 mark**
> - Final numerical answer: **remaining marks**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These numerical calculations are **not academic exercises** — they directly drive the following engineering decisions in industry:

- **Press Tonnage Selection:** A 200-tonne automobile forging press must be specified based on the calculated peak forging force.
- **Motor Power Sizing:** Rolling mill motors are sized using the calculated roll torque and rotational speed.
- **Billet Length Optimization:** In extrusion, the maximum billet length is dictated by the ram force capacity vs. required extrusion pressure.
- **Cooling Time Estimation:** Casting foundries use Chvorinov's rule to determine shake-out time and optimize production rate.
- **Die Design:** Friction factor (m) directly informs die surface finish selection, lubrication strategy, and die material choice (e.g., H13 tool steel vs. WC).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked-Out Numerical Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Methodology Note:** Each problem below follows the canonical KTU 14-mark answer template: (1) Given data with units, (2) Assumptions, (3) Governing formula, (4) Step-by-step substitution, (5) Final answer with units, and (6) Engineering interpretation.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1: Rolling Mill — Separating Force, Torque, and Power

### Problem Statement
A steel strip with initial thickness $h_0 = 25 \text{ mm}$ is reduced to $h_f = 20 \text{ mm}$ in a single pass through a rolling mill with roll radius $R = 300 \text{ mm}$. The width of the strip is $w = 200 \text{ mm}$. The strength coefficient $K = 600 \text{ MPa}$ and strain-hardening exponent $n = 0.20$. The friction factor $m = 0.4$, and the rolls rotate at $N = 12 \text{ rpm}$. Compute:
- (a) The roll separating force (F).
- (b) The torque on each roll.
- (c) The power required to drive the mill.

### Given Data
$$h_0 = 25 \text{ mm}, \quad h_f = 20 \text{ mm}, \quad R = 300 \text{ mm}, \quad w = 200 \text{ mm}$$
$$K = 600 \text{ MPa}, \quad n = 0.20, \quad m = 0.4, \quad N = 12 \text{ rpm}$$

### Step 1: Calculate the Draft and Contact Length
The draft (reduction in thickness) is:
$$\Delta h = h_0 - h_f = 25 - 20 = 5 \text{ mm}$$

The contact length (projected length of the arc of contact) is:
$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{300 \times 5} = \sqrt{1500} \approx 38.73 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the True Strain
Using volume constancy for plane strain rolling:
$$\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{25}{20}\right) = \ln(1.25) \approx 0.2231$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Average Flow Stress (Y_f)
For a power-law material $\bar{\sigma} = K \epsilon^n$, the average flow stress across a strain history is:
$$Y_f = \frac{K \cdot \epsilon^n}{1 + n} = \frac{600 \times (0.2231)^{0.20}}{1 + 0.20}$$

Computing the term $(0.2231)^{0.20}$:
$$(0.2231)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln(0.2231)} = e^{0.2 \times (-1.5008)} = e^{-0.3002} \approx 0.7406$$

Substituting:
$$Y_f = \frac{600 \times 0.7406}{1.20} = \frac{444.36}{1.20} \approx 370.3 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Roll Separating Force
Using the friction-hill rolling formula:
$$F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{m \cdot L}{4 \cdot h_f}\right)$$

First, compute the friction-hill term:
$$\frac{m \cdot L}{4 \cdot h_f} = \frac{0.4 \times 38.73}{4 \times 20} = \frac{15.492}{80} \approx 0.1937$$

So the bracketed term is:
$$1 + 0.1937 = 1.1937$$

Substituting all values:
$$F = 38.73 \times 200 \times 370.3 \times 1.1937$$

$$F = 7746 \times 370.3 \times 1.1937$$

$$F \approx 2{,}868{,}064 \times 1.1937 \approx 3{,}423{,}676 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 3424 \text{ kN} \approx 3.42 \text{ MN}}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Torque on Each Roll
Assuming the resultant rolling force acts at the midpoint of the arc of contact (lever arm = L/2):
$$T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 3{,}423{,}676 \times 0.03873$$

$$T \approx 66{,}302 \text{ N·m} \approx 66.3 \text{ kN·m}$$

### Step 6: Calculate the Power Required
The rotational speed must be converted to rev/sec:
$$N = 12 \text{ rpm} = \frac{12}{60} = 0.2 \text{ rev/s}$$

The total power (for two rolls) is:
$$P = 2 \pi N \cdot T \cdot 2 = 4 \pi N \cdot T$$

Wait — let me correct this. The torque T we calculated is **per roll**, and total power accounts for both rolls:
$$P_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 2\pi N \cdot T = 2 \times 2\pi \times 0.2 \times 66{,}302$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 4 \pi \times 0.2 \times 66{,}302 = 2.513 \times 66{,}302$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 166{,}600 \text{ W} \approx 166.6 \text{ kW}}$$

### Engineering Interpretation
A rolling force of **3.42 MN** and power of **166.6 kW** indicates that this rolling pass requires a heavy-duty mill. The friction-hill multiplier (1.19) shows that friction contributes ~19% additional load — significant but within typical operating limits for cold rolling of steel.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2: Forging — Cylinder Upsetting under Slab Method

### Problem Statement
A solid cylindrical billet of diameter $d_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$ and height $h_0 = 75 \text{ mm}$ is upset forged in a press at room temperature. The final height after forging is $h_f = 50 \text{ mm}$. The material has strength coefficient $K = 700 \text{ MPa}$, strain-hardening exponent $n = 0.15$, and the friction factor at the die-workpiece interface is $m = 0.4$. Calculate:
- (a) The true strain in the forging operation.
- (b) The average flow stress $Y_f$.
- (c) The maximum forging force and the average forging pressure.

### Given Data
$$d_0 = 50 \text{ mm}, \quad h_0 = 75 \text{ mm}, \quad h_f = 50 \text{ mm}$$
$$K = 700 \text{ MPa}, \quad n = 0.15, \quad m = 0.4$$

### Step 1: Calculate the True Strain
For upsetting under volume constancy:
$$\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{75}{50}\right) = \ln(1.5) \approx 0.4055$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Final Radius (r)
Using volume constancy $V_0 = V_f$:
$$\frac{\pi}{4} d_0^2 \cdot h_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_f^2 \cdot h_f$$
$$50^2 \times 75 = d_f^2 \times 50$$
$$d_f^2 = \frac{50^2 \times 75}{50} = 50 \times 75 = 3750 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$d_f = \sqrt{3750} \approx 61.24 \text{ mm}$$

Final radius:
$$r_f = \frac{d_f}{2} = \frac{61.24}{2} \approx 30.62 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Average Flow Stress
$$Y_f = \frac{K \cdot \epsilon^n}{1 + n} = \frac{700 \times (0.4055)^{0.15}}{1 + 0.15}$$

Computing $(0.4055)^{0.15}$:
$$(0.4055)^{0.15} = e^{0.15 \ln(0.4055)} = e^{0.15 \times (-0.9027)} = e^{-0.1354} \approx 0.8734$$

Substituting:
$$Y_f = \frac{700 \times 0.8734}{1.15} = \frac{611.4}{1.15} \approx 531.7 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Friction-Hill Multiplier
For cylindrical upsetting using the slab method (assuming sticking friction is the limiting case where m ≤ 0.577, and m = 0.4 < 0.577, so the friction factor formula is valid):
$$1 + \frac{2 m r_f}{3 h_f} = 1 + \frac{2 \times 0.4 \times 30.62}{3 \times 50}$$

$$= 1 + \frac{24.496}{150} = 1 + 0.1633 = 1.1633$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Maximum Forging Pressure
$$p_{\max} = Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2 m r_f}{3 h_f}\right) = 531.7 \times 1.1633 \approx 618.5 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 6: Calculate the Maximum Forging Force
The final cross-sectional area of the billet is:
$$A_f = \pi r_f^2 = \pi \times (30.62)^2 = \pi \times 937.6 \approx 2945.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

Converting to m²: $A_f = 2945.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 2.9455 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

The maximum forging force is:
$$F_{\max} = p_{\max} \cdot A_f = 618.5 \times 10^6 \times 2.9455 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{\max} \approx 1{,}821{,}720 \text{ N} \approx 1822 \text{ kN} \approx 1.82 \text{ MN}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\max} \approx 1.82 \text{ MN}, \quad p_{\max} \approx 618.5 \text{ MPa}}$$

### Engineering Interpretation
The required press capacity is **~182 tonnes** (since 1 tonne ≈ 10 kN). The friction-hill effect contributes ~16% additional load over the ideal homogeneous case. To reduce this, a **graphite-based lubricant** (m ≈ 0.2) or **phosphate coating** (m ≈ 0.1) could be used.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3: Direct Extrusion — Extrusion Pressure and Force

### Problem Statement
A cylindrical billet of aluminum alloy with initial diameter $D_0 = 100 \text{ mm}$ and length $L_0 = 300 \text{ mm}$ is directly extruded at room temperature into a rod of diameter $D_f = 40 \text{ mm}$. The flow stress of the alloy follows the relation $\bar{\sigma} = 240 \epsilon^{0.25}$ (MPa). The friction factor at the billet-die interface is $m = 0.25$. Determine:
- (a) The extrusion ratio (R) and true strain.
- (b) The average flow stress at the entry of the die.
- (c) The extrusion pressure at the start of the operation.
- (d) The maximum ram force required.

### Given Data
$$D_0 = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad L_0 = 300 \text{ mm}, \quad D_f = 40 \text{ mm}, \quad m = 0.25$$
$$K = 240 \text{ MPa}, \quad n = 0.25$$

### Step 1: Calculate the Extrusion Ratio
$$R = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \left(\frac{D_0}{D_f}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{100}{40}\right)^2 = 2.5^2 = 6.25$$

### Step 2: Calculate the True Strain
$$\epsilon = \ln(R) = \ln(6.25) \approx 1.8326$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Average Flow Stress
$$Y_f = \frac{K \cdot \epsilon^n}{1 + n} = \frac{240 \times (1.8326)^{0.25}}{1 + 0.25}$$

Computing $(1.8326)^{0.25}$:
$$(1.8326)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \ln(1.8326)} = e^{0.25 \times 0.6058} = e^{0.1514} \approx 1.1635$$

Substituting:
$$Y_f = \frac{240 \times 1.1635}{1.25} = \frac{279.24}{1.25} \approx 223.4 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Extrusion Pressure at the Start
For direct extrusion, the friction-hill contribution is **maximum at the start** when the billet length is full (L = L_0). The standard extrusion pressure formula is:

$$p = Y_f \cdot \left[\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)^n \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot e^{2mL_0/D_0}\right]$$

Wait — let me use the more standard formulation that is typically expected in KTU exams. The widely-used formula is:

$$p = Y_f \cdot \left[\ln(R) + \frac{2mL_0}{D_0}\right]$$

This is a linearized approximation that combines the ideal work and friction terms. Using this:

Compute friction term:
$$\frac{2mL_0}{D_0} = \frac{2 \times 0.25 \times 300}{100} = \frac{150}{100} = 1.5$$

So:
$$p = 223.4 \times [\ln(6.25) + 1.5] = 223.4 \times [1.8326 + 1.5]$$

$$p = 223.4 \times 3.3326 \approx 744.5 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Maximum Ram Force
The initial billet cross-sectional area is:
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (100)^2 = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

The maximum ram force:
$$F_{\max} = p \cdot A_0 = 744.5 \times 10^6 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{\max} \approx 5{,}847{,}000 \text{ N} \approx 5847 \text{ kN} \approx 5.85 \text{ MN}$$

$$\boxed{p \approx 744.5 \text{ MPa}, \quad F_{\max} \approx 5.85 \text{ MN}}$$

### Engineering Interpretation
A ram force of **5.85 MN** is typical for a 6000-tonne industrial extrusion press. The friction term (1.5) actually **exceeds** the ideal work term (1.83) due to the long billet, indicating that **billet length optimization** and **effective lubrication** are critical. Note: The pressure decreases as the billet is consumed because L decreases (a key feature of direct extrusion known as the **pressure decay curve**).

---

## 3.4 Problem 4: Casting — Chvorinov's Rule and Solidification Time

### Problem Statement
A steel casting has the shape of a flat plate of dimensions $300 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm} \times 25 \text{ mm}$. The casting is poured in a green sand mold. The mold constant for this alloy-mold combination is $B = 4.5 \text{ min/cm}^2$. Estimate the solidification time for:
- (a) The casting as described.
- (b) A redesigned version that is a cylinder of diameter 100 mm and length 300 mm.

Compare and comment on the relative solidification times.

### Given Data
$$L = 300 \text{ mm}, \quad W = 200 \text{ mm}, \quad T = 25 \text{ mm}, \quad B = 4.5 \text{ min/cm}^2$$

### Step 1: Convert All Dimensions to Centimeters
$$L = 30 \text{ cm}, \quad W = 20 \text{ cm}, \quad T = 2.5 \text{ cm}$$

### Part (a): Solidification Time of the Plate

**Volume of the plate:**
$$V_{\text{plate}} = L \times W \times T = 30 \times 20 \times 2.5 = 1500 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Surface area of the plate** (assuming all 6 faces are exposed to the mold — top, bottom, and 4 sides):
$$A_{\text{plate}} = 2(LW + LT + WT) = 2(30 \times 20 + 30 \times 2.5 + 20 \times 2.5)$$
$$= 2(600 + 75 + 50) = 2(725) = 1450 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Modulus (V/A) of the plate:**
$$M_{\text{plate}} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{1500}{1450} \approx 1.0345 \text{ cm}$$

**Solidification time (Chvorinov's Rule):**
$$t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2 = 4.5 \times (1.0345)^2 = 4.5 \times 1.0702$$

$$\boxed{t_{s,\text{plate}} \approx 4.82 \text{ min}}$$

### Part (b): Solidification Time of the Cylinder

**Volume of the cylinder:**
$$V_{\text{cyl}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (10)^2 \times 30 = 750\pi \approx 2356.2 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Surface area of the cylinder** (2 circular ends + lateral surface):
$$A_{\text{cyl}} = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} + \pi D L = \frac{\pi (10)^2}{2} + \pi \times 10 \times 30$$
$$= 50\pi + 300\pi = 350\pi \approx 1099.6 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Modulus (V/A) of the cylinder:**
$$M_{\text{cyl}} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{2356.2}{1099.6} \approx 2.1429 \text{ cm}$$

**Solidification time:**
$$t_{s,\text{cyl}} = 4.5 \times (2.1429)^2 = 4.5 \times 4.5919$$

$$\boxed{t_{s,\text{cyl}} \approx 20.66 \text{ min}}$$

### Step 2: Comparison and Comment
The cylindrical casting takes **~4.3 times longer** to solidify than the plate, despite having a larger volume. This is because the **V/A modulus is ~2× larger** for the cylinder, and since solidification time is proportional to $(V/A)^2$, the time scales as a factor of 4.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** The **V/A modulus** is the most important design parameter in casting. To minimize shrinkage defects and porosity, foundries aim to:
> - Keep the V/A ratio **uniform** throughout the casting (avoid hot spots).
> - Place **risers** at locations of high V/A so they solidify **last** and feed liquid metal to the shrinking casting.
> - Use **chills** at locations of low V/A to accelerate their solidification.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Problem Type Classification

The table below provides a **strategic matrix** for selecting the right approach based on problem keywords, useful for KTU exam time management.

| **Problem Keywords** | **Process Identified** | **Primary Formula to Use** | **Common Pitfall** |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Roll radius, draft, separating force" | Rolling | $F = LwY_f(1 + mL/4h_f)$ | Forgetting the friction-hill term |
| "Upset forging, cylinder, height reduction" | Forging (Slab) | $p = Y_f(1 + 2mr/3h_f)$ | Confusing initial and final radius |
| "Extrusion ratio, billet length, ram force" | Extrusion | $p = Y_f[\ln(R) + 2mL/D_0]$ | Using final L instead of L_0 |
| "Drawing stress, die angle, back tension" | Wire Drawing | $\sigma_d$ formula (see table) | Forgetting back tension |
| "Solidification time, casting, V/A" | Casting (Chvorinov) | $t_s = B(V/A)^2$ | Including riser in V/A calculation |
| "Shrinkage allowance, dimension" | Pattern Design | $S_a = \alpha \Delta T L$ | Unit mismatch in α |
| "Forge energy, efficiency, ideal work" | Forging (Energy) | $W = Y_f V \epsilon / \eta$ | Confusing ideal work with actual work |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Numerical Problem Solving Algorithm

The diagram below maps the **decision-tree workflow** that a KTU student should follow when encountering any numerical problem in Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Problem Statement] --> B{Identify Process Type}
    B -->|Force / Pressure / Power| C[Forming Process: Rolling Forging Extrusion Drawing]
    B -->|Time / Shrinkage / Riser| D[Casting Process: Chvorinov Pattern]
    C --> E{Extract Given Data with Units}
    D --> E
    E --> F[Convert All Units to SI]
    F --> G{Is Friction Sticking or Sliding?}
    G -->|Sticking| H[Use Friction Factor m]
    G -->|Sliding| I[Use Coefficient mu]
    H --> J[Compute Geometric Terms: L, A, R, epsilon]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Average Flow Stress Yf]
    K --> L{Apply Governing Formula}
    L -->|Rolling| M1[Friction Hill Rolling Force]
    L -->|Forging| M2[Slab Method Forging Pressure]
    L -->|Extrusion| M3[Direct Extrusion Pressure]
    D --> N[Compute Volume and Surface Area]
    N --> O[Compute V over A Modulus]
    O --> P[Apply Chvorinov Rule]
    M1 --> Q[Calculate Torque and Power if asked]
    M2 --> R[Multiply by Area to get Force]
    M3 --> R
    Q --> S[Write Final Answer with Units]
    R --> S
    P --> S
    S --> T[Add Engineering Interpretation]
    T --> U[End: Final Solution]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force/Pressure Pathways in Bulk Forming

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Material Properties] -->|K, n| B1[Flow Stress Curve]
    A2[Process Geometry] -->|h0, hf, L, D| B2[Geometric Parameters]
    A3[Friction Conditions] -->|m or mu| B3[Friction Shear]
    B1 --> C[Governing Equation]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D1[Rolling Force]
    C --> D2[Forging Pressure]
    C --> D3[Extrusion Pressure]
    D1 --> E1[Torque and Power]
    D2 --> E2[Forging Force = p x A]
    D3 --> E2
    E1 --> F[Press Sizing]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Equipment Selection]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Slab vs. Energy Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Forging Problem] --> Q1{Is geometry simple and uniform?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, e.g., cylinder| Slab[Use Slab Method]
    Q1 -->|No, complex shape| Energy[Use Upper Bound Energy Method]
    Slab --> S1[Compute Average Flow Stress Yf]
    Slab --> S2[Apply p equals Yf times 1 plus 2mr over 3hf]
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3[Multiply by Area for Force]
    Energy --> E1[Compute Homogeneous Strain]
    Energy --> E2[Compute Ideal Work Wi equals Yf V epsilon]
    E1 --> E2
    Energy --> E3[Divide by Efficiency eta]
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> S3
    S3 --> Final[Compare F values to cross-check]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "friction hill" as applicable to metal forming processes. Why does it occur?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **friction hill** is the phenomenon in which the pressure required to deform a metal workpiece increases substantially above the material's flow stress due to frictional resistance at the die-workpiece interface. It occurs primarily in bulk forming processes such as **forging, extrusion, and rolling**, where the deforming material is in **extensive contact** with the die surface.
>
> The pressure required is mathematically given by $p = Y_f \cdot (1 + \text{friction term})$, where the friction term is a function of the friction factor (m) and the geometric ratio of the workpiece (L/h or r/h).
>
> **(1 mark)** Correct definition; **(1 mark)** mention of die-workpiece contact; **(1 mark)** identifying it as a multiplicative increase over flow stress.

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Chvorinov's rule for solidification of a casting. What is the significance of the V/A ratio?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Chvorinov's rule states that the **solidification time ($t_s$)** of a casting is proportional to the **square of the V/A ratio** (volume-to-surface-area ratio), and is given by:
> $$t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$$
> where **B** is the **mold constant** that depends on the metal, mold material, and pouring conditions.
>
> The **V/A ratio (modulus)** is significant because:
> - It determines the relative solidification rates of different sections of a casting.
> - Sections with **higher V/A solidify last** and act as potential **risers** to feed liquid metal to feeding sections.
> - It is the **key design parameter** for riser placement, chill location, and gating design.
>
> **(1 mark)** Correct formula; **(1 mark)** explanation of B; **(1 mark)** significance in casting design.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**(a)** A cylindrical billet of diameter 80 mm and height 100 mm is upset forged in a press to a final height of 60 mm at room temperature. The strength coefficient is $K = 410 \text{ MPa}$ and strain-hardening exponent is $n = 0.25$. The friction factor is $m = 0.3$. Determine:
- (i) The true strain in the operation.
- (ii) The average flow stress.
- (iii) The maximum forging pressure and maximum forging force.

**(b)** A steel strip with width 250 mm is rolled from 20 mm to 15 mm thickness. The roll diameter is 400 mm. The material has $K = 500 \text{ MPa}$ and $n = 0.20$. The coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.1$ and the rolls rotate at 30 rpm. Determine:
- (i) The roll separating force.
- (ii) The torque and power required.

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Forging Solution

**Step 1: True Strain**
$$\epsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{100}{60}\right) = \ln(1.6667) \approx 0.5108$$

**Step 2: Final Diameter (Volume Constancy)**
$$d_0^2 h_0 = d_f^2 h_f \Rightarrow 80^2 \times 100 = d_f^2 \times 60$$
$$d_f^2 = \frac{640000}{60} = 10666.67 \Rightarrow d_f \approx 103.28 \text{ mm}$$
$$r_f = 51.64 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Average Flow Stress**
$$Y_f = \frac{K \epsilon^n}{1+n} = \frac{410 \times (0.5108)^{0.25}}{1.25}$$

$(0.5108)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \times \ln(0.5108)} = e^{0.25 \times (-0.6717)} = e^{-0.1679} \approx 0.8455$

$$Y_f = \frac{410 \times 0.8455}{1.25} = \frac{346.66}{1.25} \approx 277.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Forging Pressure**
$$p_{\max} = Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2mr_f}{3h_f}\right) = 277.3 \times \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 0.3 \times 51.64}{3 \times 60}\right)$$
$$= 277.3 \times \left(1 + \frac{30.984}{180}\right) = 277.3 \times 1.1721 \approx 325.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Forging Force**
$$A_f = \pi r_f^2 = \pi \times (51.64)^2 \approx 8377.7 \text{ mm}^2 = 8.378 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$F_{\max} = p_{\max} \cdot A_f = 325.0 \times 10^6 \times 8.378 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2{,}722{,}850 \text{ N} \approx 2.72 \text{ MN}$$

#### Part (b) — Rolling Solution

**Step 1: Geometric Parameters**
$$\Delta h = 20 - 15 = 5 \text{ mm}, \quad L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{200 \times 5} = \sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: True Strain and Flow Stress**
$$\epsilon = \ln(20/15) = \ln(1.333) \approx 0.2877$$
$$Y_f = \frac{500 \times (0.2877)^{0.20}}{1.20}$$

$(0.2877)^{0.20} = e^{0.20 \times \ln(0.2877)} = e^{0.20 \times (-1.2457)} = e^{-0.2491} \approx 0.7795$

$$Y_f = \frac{500 \times 0.7795}{1.20} = \frac{389.75}{1.20} \approx 324.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Check Sticking vs. Sliding Friction**
$$\mu p_{\text{avg}} \text{ vs } \frac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}} \text{ (Tresca)}$$

A simpler check: $\mu \cdot L / h_f = 0.1 \times 31.62 / 15 = 0.211 < 1$, so sliding friction is applicable.

For sliding friction, the rolling force formula is:
$$F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right]$$

Wait — correction: The general formula depends on whether sticking or sliding friction prevails. The conventional formula for rolling is:

$$F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f}\right) \quad \text{(sliding)}$$

OR

$$F = L \cdot w \cdot Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{m L}{4 h_f}\right) \quad \text{(sticking)}$$

For sliding friction:
$$1 + \frac{\mu L}{2 h_f} = 1 + \frac{0.1 \times 31.62}{2 \times 15} = 1 + \frac{3.162}{30} = 1 + 0.1054 = 1.1054$$

**Step 4: Roll Separating Force**
$$F = 31.62 \times 250 \times 324.8 \times 1.1054$$
$$F = 7905 \times 324.8 \times 1.1054 \approx 2{,}567{,}400 \times 1.1054 \approx 2{,}838{,}100 \text{ N}$$
$$F \approx 2838 \text{ kN} \approx 2.84 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 5: Torque per Roll**
$$T = 0.5 \cdot F \cdot L = 0.5 \times 2{,}838{,}100 \times 0.03162 \approx 44{,}870 \text{ N·m} \approx 44.9 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 6: Power (Both Rolls)**
$$N = 30 \text{ rpm} = 0.5 \text{ rev/s}$$
$$P = 2 \times 2\pi N \cdot T = 4\pi \times 0.5 \times 44{,}870 = 2\pi \times 44{,}870 \approx 281{,}900 \text{ W} \approx 281.9 \text{ kW}$$

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**(a)** A direct extrusion operation is performed on a billet of initial diameter 75 mm and length 250 mm to produce a rod of diameter 30 mm. The flow stress of the material follows $\bar{\sigma} = 350 \epsilon^{0.15}$ MPa. The friction factor is $m = 0.3$. Calculate:
- (i) The extrusion ratio and the true strain.
- (ii) The maximum extrusion pressure (at the start of the stroke).
- (iii) The maximum ram force required.

**(b)** Two castings are to be produced — one is a sphere of diameter 200 mm, and the other is a cube of side 200 mm. Using Chvorinov's rule with mold constant $B = 3.5 \text{ min/cm}^2$, determine the solidification time for each. State which design is better from a casting perspective and justify.

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Extrusion Solution

**Step 1: Extrusion Ratio and True Strain**
$$R = \left(\frac{D_0}{D_f}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{75}{30}\right)^2 = 2.5^2 = 6.25$$
$$\epsilon = \ln(R) = \ln(6.25) \approx 1.8326$$

**Step 2: Average Flow Stress**
$$Y_f = \frac{K \epsilon^n}{1+n} = \frac{350 \times (1.8326)^{0.15}}{1.15}$$

$(1.8326)^{0.15} = e^{0.15 \times \ln(1.8326)} = e^{0.15 \times 0.6058} = e^{0.0909} \approx 1.0951$

$$Y_f = \frac{350 \times 1.0951}{1.15} = \frac{383.29}{1.15} \approx 333.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Extrusion Pressure (Start of Stroke, L = L_0)**
$$p_{\max} = Y_f \cdot \left[\ln(R) + \frac{2m L_0}{D_0}\right] = 333.3 \times \left[1.8326 + \frac{2 \times 0.3 \times 250}{75}\right]$$
$$= 333.3 \times \left[1.8326 + 2.0\right] = 333.3 \times 3.8326 \approx 1277.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Maximum Ram Force**
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 75^2 = 4417.86 \text{ mm}^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$F_{\max} = p_{\max} \cdot A_0 = 1277.4 \times 10^6 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \approx 5{,}643{,}500 \text{ N} \approx 5.64 \text{ MN}$$

#### Part (b) — Chvorinov's Rule Comparison

**Convert dimensions to cm:** All dimensions = 20 cm.

**For the Sphere:**
- Volume: $V_s = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi (10)^3 = 4188.79 \text{ cm}^3$
- Surface Area: $A_s = 4\pi r^2 = 4\pi (10)^2 = 1256.64 \text{ cm}^2$
- Modulus: $M_s = V_s/A_s = 4188.79/1256.64 = 3.333 \text{ cm}$
- Solidification time: $t_s = 3.5 \times (3.333)^2 = 3.5 \times 11.111 = 38.89 \text{ min}$

**For the Cube:**
- Volume: $V_c = 20^3 = 8000 \text{ cm}^3$
- Surface Area: $A_c = 6 \times 20^2 = 2400 \text{ cm}^2$
- Modulus: $M_c = V_c/A_c = 8000/2400 = 3.333 \text{ cm}$
- Solidification time: $t_c = 3.5 \times (3.333)^2 = 38.89 \text{ min}$

> **Comment:** Both the sphere and cube of the same characteristic dimension have **identical** V/A modulus (= R/3 or a/6 respectively) and hence the same solidification time. From a casting perspective, the **sphere is preferred** because:
> - It has **uniform wall thickness** (no corners or edges), which avoids hot spots.
> - It exhibits **isotropic shrinkage**, minimizing residual stresses and distortion.
> - It has a **lower surface area-to-volume variability**, ensuring uniform solidification.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Module 4 Numerical Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (Loses 1–2 marks):** The single most common error. Forgetting to convert mm to m before computing force. Example: Computing $F = p \cdot A$ in mixed units (MPa × mm²) gives wrong orders of magnitude. **Always convert area to m² and pressure to Pa.**
>
> 2. **Final vs. Initial Geometry Confusion (Loses 1–2 marks):** In forging and extrusion, students often use the **initial** radius $r_0$ instead of the **final** radius $r_f$ in the friction-hill term. Remember: friction force depends on the **contact area at the end of the stroke**.
>
> 3. **Sticking vs. Sliding Friction Mix-Up (Loses 1–2 marks):** The friction-hill term in rolling is $\frac{mL}{4h_f}$ (sticking) or $\frac{\mu L}{2h_f}$ (sliding). Wrong coefficient = wrong answer.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Average Flow Stress Formula (Loses 2 marks):** A student who uses $Y_f = K\epsilon^n$ instead of $\frac{K\epsilon^n}{1+n}$ will over-estimate forces by 10–25%.
>
> 5. **Chvorinov's Rule — Including the Riser (Loses 1 mark):** The V/A for the casting **excludes the riser**, because the riser is intentionally designed to solidify last.
>
> 6. **No Engineering Interpretation (Loses 1 mark):** KTU examiners often award a final 1 mark for a meaningful engineering comment (e.g., "The 5.85 MN ram force requires a 600-tonne press; the friction term exceeds the ideal work, so billet shortening is recommended").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 Numerical Problems:**

### **Process Identification & Formula Selection:**
- **Rolling** → $F = LwY_f(1 + \text{friction term})$; $T = 0.5FL$; $P = 2\pi N T \cdot 2$
- **Forging (Slab)** → $p = Y_f(1 + 2mr_f/3h_f)$ for cylinder; $F = p \cdot A_f$
- **Extrusion (Direct)** → $p = Y_f[\ln R + 2mL_0/D_0]$; maximum at start
- **Casting** → $t_s = B(V/A)^2$; sphere is best shape; riser has highest V/A

### **Critical Constants & Formulas:**
- **Average Flow Stress:** $Y_f = \dfrac{K\epsilon^n}{1+n}$ (NOT just $K\epsilon^n$)
- **True Strain (volume constancy):** $\epsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f) = \ln(A_f/A_0) = \ln(R)$
- **Contact length in rolling:** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$
- **Sticking friction check:** $\mu \cdot p \geq Y_f/\sqrt{3}$ (Tresca) → use m; else use μ

### **Exam Strategy:**
1. **Always extract** given data in a labeled box at the start of the answer.
2. **Convert** all dimensions to consistent SI units (m, Pa, N).
3. **State** the formula with all variables defined (1 mark guaranteed).
4. **Show step-by-step substitution** with intermediate values.
5. **End** with a 1–2 line engineering interpretation (extra 1 mark).
6. **For Part (a) + (b) 14-mark questions**, budget ~7 marks per sub-part.

### **Numerical Sanity Checks:**
- Forging force: typically **1–10 MN** for industrial parts.
- Rolling force: typically **1–20 MN** per stand.
- Extrusion pressure: typically **500–1500 MPa** for cold extrusion of aluminum.
- Solidification time: typically **5–60 minutes** for sand castings.

### **Common Physical Constants to Remember:**
- **1 tonne force ≈ 10 kN** (for press tonnage conversion)
- **1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa**
- **Density of steel:** ~7850 kg/m³
- **Density of aluminum:** ~2700 kg/m³

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Miscellaneous Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Miscellaneous Processes" in Manufacturing?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PBMET304 – Manufacturing Processes**, the term **"Miscellaneous Processes"** groups together a set of *non-traditional, near-net-shape, and material-specific manufacturing routes* that do not fall cleanly under bulk deformation (forging, rolling), bulk removal (machining), or conventional joining (welding). These processes have become essential because modern engineering products increasingly demand:

- Complex internal geometries (lattice structures, conformal cooling channels)
- Lightweight, customized, low-volume parts
- Materials that are difficult-to-machine (superalloys, titanium, refractory metals, technical ceramics, bio-compatible polymers)
- High material utilization with minimal scrap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examiner-Wording):**  
> *Miscellaneous manufacturing processes are non-conventional, non-traditional or specialized material-shaping techniques — such as Additive Manufacturing (AM), Powder Metallurgy (P/M), and Polymer/Plastics Processing — that produce parts by layer-wise addition, particulate consolidation, or melt-flow forming, rather than by chip removal or bulk plastic deformation.*

The three pillars of this module (as per the KTU 2024 Module-4 syllabus) are:

| Pillar | Core Idea | Typical Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Additive Manufacturing (AM / 3D Printing)** | Build part layer-by-layer from a digital CAD model | Aerospace, biomedical, automotive prototyping |
| **Powder Metallurgy (P/M)** | Produce parts from metal powders via pressing + sintering | Gears, bushes, self-lubricating bearings, cemented carbide cutting tools |
| **Polymer / Plastics Processing** | Shape thermoplastics/thermosets using heat, pressure and moulds | Consumer goods, packaging, automotive dashboards, pipes |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bakery, the Clay-Kiln, and the Injection"

Imagine three different ways a baker makes shapes:

1. **Additive Manufacturing** → Like a **cake decorated with piped icing**. The baker follows a digital pattern, depositing one thin horizontal stripe of icing on top of the previous one until the full 3D rose emerges. No material is cut away; everything deposited stays in the final cake. → This is exactly what an **AM printer does**: it deposits (or fuses) one thin layer on top of the previous one.

2. **Powder Metallurgy** → Like making a **sugar cube**. You press granulated sugar tightly inside a small mould, then gently bake it so the grains bond at their contact points without melting fully. → This is how **P/M parts** are made: metal powder is compacted in a die and then sintered below its melting point.

3. **Plastics Processing (Injection Moulding)** → Like using a **syringe of melted chocolate**. You melt chocolate, force it into a shaped cavity under pressure, let it solidify, and eject a perfect truffle. → That is precisely what an **injection moulding machine** does with molten thermoplastic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are they grouped as "Miscellaneous"?**  
> Because they are *process-families* rather than single processes. Each family contains 5–10 sub-processes (e.g., AM has FDM, SLA, SLS, DMLS, EBM; P/M has conventional P/M, Metal Injection Moulding, Hot Isostatic Pressing; Plastics has injection, blow, extrusion, thermoforming, rotational moulding). The KTU module expects you to know the **principle, working, advantages, limitations, and applications** of each major sub-process.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants You Must Remember

| Metric / Constant | Value / Definition | Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Dimensional accuracy of FDM** | ± 0.2 mm (typical) | AM |
| **Layer thickness (AM)** | **0.05 – 0.4 mm** | AM |
| **Sintering temperature** | **(0.7 – 0.9) × T<sub>melting</sub>** in Kelvin | P/M |
| **Compaction pressure (P/M)** | **200 – 800 MPa** | P/M |
| **Glass transition temperature (T<sub>g</sub>)** | Temperature above which amorphous polymer becomes rubbery | Plastics |
| **Melt Flow Index (MFI)** | Mass of polymer (g) flowing in **10 minutes** through a standard die at standard T & load | Plastics |
| **Shrinkage in injection moulding** | **0.3 % – 3 %** typical | Plastics |

---

## 1.4 The Big Picture — Where Each Process Sits

```
Raw Material Form  ────────────────►  Final Part
      │
      ├─── Wire / Powder / Resin ──► Layer-by-Layer (AM)
      ├─── Metal Powder ───────────► Press + Sinter (P/M)
      └─── Thermoplastic Granules ──► Melt + Solidify (Plastics)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board-exam success tip:** When asked to *compare* processes, always use a **4-row table** covering: Principle, Material form, Dimensional accuracy, Typical applications. Examiners award easy marks for clean tabular answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ADITIVE MANUFACTURING (AM / Rapid Prototyping / 3D Printing)

### 2.1.1 ISO/ASTM 52900 Standard Classification

The international standard (and what KTU examiners expect) classifies AM into **seven process categories**. The four most-asked in KTU are:

| Process | Full Name | Energy Source | Material State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VPP (SLA)** | Vat Photopolymerization (Stereolithography) | UV laser | Liquid photopolymer resin |
| **PBF (SLS / DMLS / SLM)** | Powder Bed Fusion | Laser / Electron beam | Powder (polymer / metal) |
| **MJF** | Material Jetting (PolyJet) | UV lamp + inkjet head | Liquid photopolymer droplets |
| **MEX (FDM / FFF)** | Material Extrusion | Heated nozzle (thermal) | Thermoplastic filament/wire |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiners frequently test:** "Differentiate between SLA and SLS" and "Explain FDM with neat block diagram." Be ready.

### 2.1.2 Generic AM Workflow (Every Process Follows This)

1. **CAD model** is created in any 3D CAD software (SolidWorks, CATIA, Fusion 360).
2. Model is exported as **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** — a triangulated mesh.
3. STL is imported into **slicer software** (Cura, PrusaSlicer), which slices the model into **2-D horizontal layers** of thickness *h*.
4. Slicer generates **G-code** containing tool-path and process parameters.
5. The AM machine builds the part **layer by layer** in a **build chamber**.
6. Post-processing: removal of support structures, surface finishing, heat treatment, HIP.

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Principle of Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)

- A **thermoplastic filament** (PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon) is fed from a spool.
- Filament passes through a **heated liquefier** (200–260 °C) where it melts.
- The molten polymer is extruded through a **nozzle** (typical Ø = **0.2 – 0.8 mm**).
- The nozzle, mounted on a **gantry (X-Y motion)**, deposits a thin **bead (road)** of semi-molten material on the build platform.
- The platform descends by one **layer thickness (Z step)** and the next road is deposited on top.
- Adjacent roads **bond** through **welding and diffusion** as the material re-solidifies.

### 2.1.4 Why and How Each Step Matters

- **Heating above T<sub>melting</sub>** → reduces viscosity for flow (η drops by ≈ an order of magnitude per 10 °C).
- **Small nozzle diameter** → better resolution but slower build.
- **Layer thickness h** → trades off speed (larger h) against surface finish and stair-stepping (smaller h).
- **Support structures** → mandatory for overhangs greater than **45°** to prevent collapse.

---

## 2.2 POWDER METALLURGY (P/M)

### 2.2.1 The Four Mandatory Steps

| Step | Operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Powder Production** | Atomization, reduction, electrolysis, mechanical crushing | Obtain powder of required purity, size, shape |
| 2. **Powder Mixing / Blending** | Blend with lubricant (e.g., zinc stearate) and alloying elements | Homogeneous distribution |
| 3. **Compaction** | Press in rigid die at **200–800 MPa** | Form "green compact" with sufficient strength for handling |
| 4. **Sintering** | Heat in controlled atmosphere furnace at **(0.7–0.9) T<sub>mp</sub>** | Diffusive bonding of particles, densification |

### 2.2.2 Why "Sintering" Works — The Diffusion Mechanism

During sintering, atoms diffuse across particle–particle contact points (necks) via:
- **Surface diffusion** (low T, no shrinkage)
- **Volume / lattice diffusion** (mid T)
- **Grain-boundary diffusion**
- **Plastic flow / creep** (high T, near T<sub>mp</sub>)

The driving force is the **reduction of total surface free energy** — small particles have huge surface-area-to-volume ratios and the system "wants" to minimize that energy by growing necks between particles.

### 2.2.3 Critical P/M Parameters

- **Apparent density** ρ<sub>a</sub> = mass / volume of loose powder
- **Tap density** ρ<sub>t</sub> = mass / volume after standardized tapping
- **Flow rate** (Hall flowmeter): time for **50 g** powder to flow through a **2.54 mm** orifice
- **Green strength** = strength of the compacted-but-not-sintered part
- **Sintered density** typically **80 – 95 %** of theoretical (the residual **porosity 5 – 20 %** is intentional for self-lubricating bearings)

---

## 2.3 POLYMER / PLASTICS PROCESSING

### 2.3.1 Injection Moulding — The Most-Asked Sub-Process

**Working principle:**
1. **Hopper** feeds thermoplastic granules into the barrel.
2. A **screw** (rotating and acting as plunger) transports, melts (via external heater bands) and meters the melt.
3. Molten plastic is **injected** through a **nozzle** into a closed, water-cooled **mould** under high pressure (**35 – 140 MPa**).
4. Melt **solidifies** in the cavity, **shrinkage** is compensated by additional melt from the screw (packing & holding phase).
5. Mould opens, **ejector pins** push the solidified part out, and the cycle repeats (typical cycle: **10 – 60 s**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners' favourite question:** *"Explain injection moulding with a neat sketch. List any 4 defects with causes."* The four classic defects to memorize are: **Sink marks, Flash, Weld lines, Short shots, Voids, Warpage** (pick four).

### 2.3.2 Other Important Plastics Processes (Brief)

| Process | One-Line Principle | Typical Product |
|---|---|---|
| **Extrusion** | Continuous forcing of melt through a die | Pipes, rods, sheets, films |
| **Blow Moulding** | Air inflates a molten parison inside a mould | Bottles, tanks |
| **Thermoforming** | Heated plastic sheet vacuum-formed over a mould | Trays, cups, dashboards |
| **Rotational Moulding** | Powder tumbles inside rotating heated mould | Large hollow tanks, kayaks |
| **Compression Moulding** | Thermoset powder pressed in heated mould | Electrical switchboards, brake pads |
| **Transfer Moulding** | Pre-heated thermoset forced into closed mould | Encapsulation of electronic components |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **direct, exam-grade**, and have appeared in past KTU papers.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_a = \dfrac{m}{V_a}$ | Apparent density (powder metallurgy) | P/M |
| 2 | $\rho_t = \dfrac{m}{V_t}$ | Tap density | P/M |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Newton's law of viscosity (polymer melt) | Plastics rheology |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Compaction pressure (P/M) | P/M |
| 5 | $T_{sint} = (0.7 \text{ to } 0.9) \cdot T_{mp}$ | Sintering temperature (in Kelvin) | P/M |
| 6 | $V_{inj} = V_{part} \times (1 + s)$ | Injection volume with shrinkage compensation | Plastics |
| 7 | $\text{MFI} = \dfrac{m \, (g)}{10 \, \text{min}}$ | Melt Flow Index (g/10 min) | Plastics |
| 8 | $R_a \approx \dfrac{h}{2}$ | Approximate stair-stepping roughness ≈ half of layer thickness (AM) | AM |
| 9 | $C = 2\pi r h$ | Surface area of road (cylindrical bead) in FDM | AM |
| 10 | $Q = v \cdot A$ | Volumetric flow rate of extrudate | AM & Plastics |
| 11 | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{sint} - \rho_{green}}{\rho_{th}} \times 100$ | Sintered shrinkage (%) | P/M |
| 12 | $\Delta V = V_{cavity} - V_{part}$ | Volumetric shrinkage allowance | Plastics |
| 13 | $\eta_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) viscosity model for non-Newtonian polymer melts | Plastics |
| 14 | $P_c = P_{inj} \times \left(\dfrac{D_{cyl}}{d_{noz}}\right)^{2}$ | Pressure intensification in injection cylinder | Plastics |
| 15 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + K \varepsilon^n$ | Hollomon's equation for plastic flow | General |

### 2.4.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AM** is used by **GE Aviation** to print fuel nozzles for the LEAP engine (TiAl6V4, DMLS) — a single piece replaces 18 parts, **25 % weight saving**.
- **P/M** makes **self-lubricating bearings** with controlled porosity (15–25 %) that absorb oil; used in **electric motors, washing machines**.
- **Injection moulding** produces **billions of parts per year** (every Lego brick is injection-moulded ABS).
