# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Manufacturing Processes (Mechanical) does not have the same level of formal mathematical derivation as Engineering Maths or Fluid Mechanics. However, KTU examiners **do** test **formula-based numerical problems** and **process-parameter derivations**. The following worked examples cover the **highest-yield numerical questions** from the KTU 2024 module.

---

## 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — FDM Bead Geometry & Build Time

**Question (3-mark KTU typical):**
An FDM printer extrudes a thermoplastic road of circular cross-section of diameter **0.6 mm** at a feed rate of **8 mm³/s**. The print head moves at **30 mm/s**. Determine:
(a) the volumetric flow rate actually deposited,
(b) whether the feed matches the deposition rate,
(c) the time to print a single layer of area **120 mm × 120 mm** with **20 % infill** (assume roads are laid as parallel lines spaced at 0.6 mm centre-to-centre).

### Solution

**(a) Volumetric flow rate of deposition**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{dep} &= v \times A_{road} \\
A_{road} &= \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.6)^{2}}{4} \\
A_{road} &= \frac{\pi \times 0.36}{4} = 0.2827 \text{ mm}^{2} \\
Q_{dep} &= 30 \;\text{mm/s} \times 0.2827 \;\text{mm}^{2} \\
Q_{dep} &= 8.482 \;\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Compare with feed rate**

$$
Q_{feed} = 8 \;\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s} \quad \text{vs} \quad Q_{dep} = 8.482 \;\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Conclusion:** The extruder feed is **~5.7 % too low** → the printer will experience **under-extrusion**; the operator must increase the extrusion multiplier or reduce the print speed.

**(c) Time to print one layer**

Total length of roads = Area × infill / road width

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= \frac{A_{layer} \times f_{infill}}{w} \\
&= \frac{120 \times 120 \times 0.20}{0.6} \\
&= \frac{2880}{0.6} = 4800 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Time per layer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{layer} &= \frac{L_{total}}{v} = \frac{4800}{30} \\
t_{layer} &= 160 \;\text{s} \;\approx\; 2 \text{ min } 40 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** One layer takes ≈ 160 seconds.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — Powder Metallurgy Compaction & Sintering Shrinkage

**Question (7-mark KTU typical):**
A cylindrical P/M part of **green diameter 25.0 mm** and **green length 50.0 mm** is compacted from an iron powder of apparent density **3.2 g/cm³**. After compaction the green density is **6.8 g/cm³**, and after sintering the part shrinks uniformly by **2.0 %** linearly.
Determine:
(a) the mass of iron powder required,
(b) the green porosity,
(c) the final (sintered) dimensions,
(d) the sintered density assuming no mass loss.

**Given:** theoretical density of iron ρ<sub>th,Fe</sub> = **7.87 g/cm³**.

### Solution

**(a) Mass of iron powder**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{green} &= \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (2.5)^{2} \times 5.0 \\
V_{green} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6.25 \times 5.0 = 24.544 \;\text{cm}^{3} \\
m &= \rho_{green} \times V_{green} = 6.8 \times 24.544 \\
m &= 166.9 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Green porosity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{green} &= \frac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{green}}{\rho_{th}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7.87 - 6.8}{7.87} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1.07}{7.87} \times 100 = 13.6 \;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Sintered dimensions (uniform 2 % linear shrinkage)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{sint} &= 25.0 \times (1 - 0.02) = 24.5 \;\text{mm} \\
L_{sint} &= 50.0 \times (1 - 0.02) = 49.0 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Sintered density** (mass conserved, volume shrinks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sint} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (2.45)^{2} \times 4.9 = 23.103 \;\text{cm}^{3} \\
\rho_{sint} &= \frac{m}{V_{sint}} = \frac{166.9}{23.103} \\
\rho_{sint} &= 7.22 \;\text{g/cm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equivalent porosity after sintering:**

$$
\varepsilon_{sint} = \frac{7.87 - 7.22}{7.87} \times 100 = 8.26 \;\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Sintering reduced porosity from **13.6 % → 8.3 %**; the part became **denser and smaller** as expected.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 3 — Injection Moulding Cycle Time & Shrinkage Allowance

**Question (7-mark KTU typical):**
An injection moulding machine produces a polystyrene part of volume **60 cm³** with a moulding shrinkage of **0.5 %**. The machine delivers an injection flow rate of **80 cm³/s**, injection time is 1.2 s, cooling time is 18 s, and mould open/eject time is 3 s. Determine:
(a) the required cavity volume,
(b) the injection pressure at the cylinder if the cylinder bore is 40 mm and the nozzle bore is 4 mm, given the cavity pressure is 20 MPa,
(c) the total cycle time,
(d) the production rate per hour.

### Solution

**(a) Cavity volume** (accounting for shrinkage)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cavity} &= V_{part} \times (1 + s) = 60 \times (1 + 0.005) \\
V_{cavity} &= 60 \times 1.005 = 60.3 \;\text{cm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Injection cylinder pressure** using pressure intensification

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cyl} &= P_{cav} \times \left(\frac{d_{noz}}{D_{cyl}}\right)^{2} \\
&= 20 \;\text{MPa} \times \left(\frac{4}{40}\right)^{2} \\
&= 20 \times 0.01 = 0.20 \;\text{MPa} = 200 \;\text{kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why so low?** Because the hydraulic cylinder is huge compared with the nozzle → small hydraulic pressure generates huge cavity pressure. This is the **hydraulic intensifier principle** — *force in = force out* (Pascal's law).

**(c) Total cycle time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cycle} &= t_{inj} + t_{cool} + t_{open/eject} \\
&= 1.2 + 18 + 3 = 22.2 \;\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Production rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{hour} &= \frac{3600}{t_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{22.2} \\
N_{hour} &= 162.16 \;\text{parts/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** ≈ **162 parts per hour**, or about **3 900 parts per 24-hour day** on continuous operation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection Cheat-Sheet

| Selection Criterion | Best Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Complex internal lattice (bone implant) | **AM (DMLS / EBM in Ti)** | Impossible to machine internally |
| Self-lubricating oil-impregnated bushing | **P/M with 15–25 % porosity** | Controlled porosity is intentional |
| 1 km of PVC pipe (continuous length) | **Extrusion** | Continuous process, low cost |
| 10 000 identical Lego bricks | **Injection moulding** | High volume, low per-part cost |
| 1 prototype of a complex aerospace bracket in 2 days | **AM (SLA or FDM)** | No tooling, fast iteration |
| Hardmetal cutting insert (WC-Co) | **P/M (cemented carbide route)** | Tungsten carbide cannot be cast/machined |
| Hollow 200-litre water tank | **Rotational moulding** | Large seamless hollow part, low tooling cost |
| Phone cover with 0.05 mm features | **AM (SLA / DLP)** | Highest resolution of all AM methods |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are written **strictly in KTU 2024 Scheme style** — two-mark sub-parts, 7 + 7 pattern for 14-mark questions, and the *Internal-Choice* rule for Part B (you attempt either OR). Model answers are given for **full-mark** scoring.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 marks each, typically 1-mark per concept)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Additive Manufacturing (AM). List any two ASTM/ISO categories of AM processes.**

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Additive Manufacturing is the process of joining materials to make objects from **3D model data**, usually **layer upon layer**, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies (ISO/ASTM 52900).
- **Two categories (½ mark each):**
  1. **Vat Photopolymerization (VPP)** — e.g., Stereolithography (SLA)
  2. **Material Extrusion (MEX)** — e.g., Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)
  *(Other acceptable: Powder Bed Fusion, Material Jetting, Binder Jetting, Directed Energy Deposition, Sheet Lamination.)*

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Sintering? State the typical sintering temperature range for iron powder.**

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
- **Sintering (1 mark):** Sintering is a thermal process in which metal powder particles are bonded together by **diffusion** of atoms across particle contact points, at a temperature **below the melting point** of the main constituent.
- **Temperature (1 mark):** For iron (T<sub>mp</sub> ≈ 1538 °C = 1811 K), sintering is carried out at **(0.7 – 0.9) × 1811 = 1268 – 1630 K ≈ 1000 – 1350 °C** in a protective atmosphere (dissociated ammonia or endothermic gas).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and has an internal choice — attempt **either Q(A) OR Q(B)**. Each choice is split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]

**Q. (A) (a)** Explain the **Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)** process with a neat block diagram. List its **advantages and limitations**.
**Q. (A) (b)** Discuss the **different methods of powder production** in powder metallurgy.

#### Model Answer — (a) 7 marks

**Principle (2 marks):**
FDM is a Material-Extrusion AM process in which a thermoplastic filament is melted in a heated liquefier and extruded through a nozzle that traces the cross-sectional profile of each layer, depositing material on a build platform. The platform descends by one layer thickness and the next layer is deposited on top, fusing to the previous layer on cooling.

**Working steps (2 marks):**
1. Filament is fed from a spool into a **heated liquefier head (200 – 260 °C)**.
2. The molten polymer is extruded through a **fine nozzle (Ø 0.2 – 0.8 mm)**.
3. The nozzle is moved in **X–Y** by a gantry following the sliced tool-path.
4. The **build platform** lowers by one **layer thickness (0.1 – 0.3 mm)** in Z.
5. The process repeats until the part is complete; **support structures** are generated automatically for overhangs > 45°.

**Block diagram (1 mark):** (use the diagram in SECTION 4.1 above)

**Advantages (1 mark):** Low cost, simple operation, wide material range (PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon), minimal post-processing.
**Limitations (1 mark):** Layer-line stair-stepping on curved surfaces, slow build speed, anisotropic strength (weaker in Z direction), limited accuracy (≈ ± 0.2 mm).

#### Model Answer — (b) 7 marks

**Powder production methods (any 5 of the following 6 — 1 mark each, 1 mark for neat classification):**

1. **Atomisation** (most common for Fe, Cu, Al powders): A molten metal stream is broken up by a high-velocity jet of **gas (gas atomisation)** or **water (water atomisation)** into fine droplets that solidify as spherical/irregular particles. Yields fine, irregular powder.
2. **Reduction of oxides:** Finely divided metal oxide (e.g., Fe₂O₃, CuO) is reduced by **H₂ or CO** gas at high temperature → spongy, porous powder. Used for iron, copper, tungsten.
3. **Electrolytic deposition:** Metal is deposited from a salt solution onto a cathode as a brittle, dendritic powder which is then crushed, washed and dried. High-purity powder (Fe, Cu).
4. **Mechanical crushing/milling:** Brittle metals (bismuth, antimony) or pre-alloyed ingots are crushed in ball mills, hammer mills or stamp mills. Produces irregular, angular particles.
5. **Chemical precipitation:** Used for very fine, pure powders of refractory metals (W, Mo) and oxides (Al₂O₃, SiC).
6. **Other modern methods:** Plasma spheroidisation, carbonyl decomposition (very pure Fe and Ni powders via Fe(CO)₅ / Ni(CO)₄).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]

**Q. (B) (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Injection Moulding process** for plastics. List **any four moulding defects** with their causes.
**Q. (B) (b)** Describe the **construction and working of a single-screw extruder** with a labelled diagram.

#### Model Answer — (a) 7 marks

**Principle (2 marks):**
Injection moulding is a closed-mould process in which a **thermoplastic** (or, in reaction injection moulding, a thermoset) is heated to a molten state and **injected under high pressure** (35 – 140 MPa) into a closed, water-cooled mould cavity. The melt solidifies, the mould opens, and the solidified part is ejected by ejector pins. The cycle is **10 – 60 s** depending on wall thickness.

**Block diagram (1 mark):** (use the diagram in SECTION 4.4 above)

**Stages of cycle (2 marks):**
1. **Filling** — melt flows into cavity.
2. **Packing/Holding** — additional melt forced in to compensate shrinkage.
3. **Cooling** — solidification; longest phase.
4. **Ejection** — mould opens, ejector pins push part out.

**Four defects (2 marks — ½ each):**

| Defect | Cause |
|---|---|
| **Sink mark** | Insufficient packing/holding pressure or local thick section |
| **Flash** | Excessive injection pressure, worn mould parting line, or low clamp force |
| **Weld line** | Two melt fronts meet but don't fuse fully (low T, low P) |
| **Short shot** | Insufficient material or inadequate injection pressure; mould not full |
| **Warpage** | Uneven cooling / non-uniform shrinkage |
| **Voids** | Insufficient packing of thick sections; air entrapment |

#### Model Answer — (b) 7 marks

**Single-screw extruder — construction (3 marks):**
The single-screw extruder has the following components:
1. **Hopper** — feeds polymer granules into the barrel.
2. **Barrel** — heated externally by **3 to 5 heater bands**; L/D ratio = **20 – 30**.
3. **Screw** — has **three zones**:
   - **Feed zone** (≈ 25 % length) — conveys solid pellets.
   - **Compression / melting zone** (≈ 50 % length) — depth decreases from 3–4 D to ~ 1 D, melt forms.
   - **Metering zone** (≈ 25 % length) — homogenises melt and pumps at constant rate.
4. **Breaker plate + screen pack** — at screw tip, filters contaminants, builds back-pressure.
5. **Die** — at the front, shapes the melt (pipe die, sheet die, film die, profile die).
6. **Heater bands, thermocouples, and cooling fans** — temperature control along barrel.

**Working (3 marks):**
- Solid polymer from the hopper is conveyed by the rotating screw (typically 20 – 150 rpm).
- The polymer is **gradually melted** by **conduction** from barrel heaters and **mechanical shear** between screw and barrel.
- The **melt is pressurised and metered** at a constant rate by the metering zone.
- The melt is forced through the **screen pack** (purification) and through the **die** (shaping).
- The extrudate is **cooled** (air, water bath or chill rolls) and **cut** (for profiles) or **wound** (for films).

**One labelled diagram (1 mark):** Show the barrel, screw (with three zones labelled), hopper, breaker plate, die, and heater bands.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most-commonly LOSE marks in this module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SLA with SLS.** SLA uses **liquid resin + UV laser**; SLS uses **powder bed + laser** to sinter. If you swap them, you lose 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Writing "sintering" as "melting" in P/M.** Sintering is **solid-state** bonding, not melting. Examiners deduct marks.
> 3. **Forgetting "atmosphere control" in sintering.** Sintering must be done in a **reducing or inert atmosphere** (H₂, N₂, dissociated ammonia, endothermic gas) to prevent oxidation. Missing this = 1 mark lost.
> 4. **In injection moulding, omitting the "packing/holding" phase.** Many students only mention filling + cooling. The packing phase compensates for shrinkage; without it you cannot explain how the part is full and dense.
> 5. **Units errors in P/M numerics:** mixing **cm³** and **mm³**, or g/cm³ with kg/m³ (× 1000). Always carry units through.
> 6. **Not stating typical values** (e.g., compaction pressure 200–800 MPa, sintering 0.7–0.9 T<sub>mp</sub>, injection pressure 35–140 MPa). Examiners love these numerical ranges.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Print this section. Read it 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.

### A. Additive Manufacturing (AM)
- ☐ AM is **layer-by-layer** material addition from a **3D CAD model** (ISO/ASTM 52900).
- ☐ **FDM (MEX):** thermoplastic filament + heated nozzle; cheapest, lowest accuracy.
- ☐ **SLA (VPP):** liquid photopolymer + UV laser; best surface finish of plastics AM.
- ☐ **SLS (PBF, polymer):** powder + laser to sinter; no support needed.
- ☐ **DMLS / SLM (PBF, metal):** metal powder fully melted by laser; aerospace & medical.
- ☐ **EBM:** metal powder + electron beam in vacuum; high power, less residual stress.
- ☐ Layer thickness **0.05 – 0.4 mm**; stair-stepping roughness R<sub>a</sub> ≈ h/2.
- ☐ Support structures needed for **overhangs > 45°** in FDM.
- ☐ Key applications: **prototypes, implants (Ti), lattice structures, conformal cooling**, aerospace brackets.

### B. Powder Metallurgy (P/M)
- ☐ Four mandatory steps: **Powder → Mixing → Compaction → Sintering**.
- ☐ Compaction pressure: **200 – 800 MPa**; green part has green strength.
- ☐ Sintering temperature: **(0.7 – 0.9) × T<sub>mp</sub> in Kelvin**; **solid-state** bonding by **diffusion**; **reducing/inert atmosphere** mandatory.
- ☐ Powder production: **atomisation, reduction, electrolysis, crushing, chemical precipitation, carbonyl process**.
- ☐ Sintered density typically **80 – 95 %** of theoretical; residual **porosity 5 – 20 %** (intentional for self-lub bearings).
- ☐ Key products: **gears, bushes, oil-impregnated bearings, cemented carbide (WC-Co), automotive synchroniser hubs**.

### C. Plastics / Polymer Processing
- ☐ **Injection moulding** is the **king of mass production** for plastics.
- ☐ Pressures: **injection 35 – 140 MPa**; hydraulic cylinder much larger bore than nozzle → pressure intensification.
- ☐ Cycle stages: **Filling → Packing → Cooling → Ejection**.
- ☐ Shrinkage allowance: **0.3 – 3 %** → cavity volume = V<sub>part</sub> × (1 + s).
- ☐ Defects: **sink marks, flash, weld lines, short shots, voids, warpage**.
- ☐ Other processes: **Extrusion, Blow moulding, Thermoforming, Rotational moulding, Compression moulding, Transfer moulding** — match each to its product.
- ☐ **MFI** measures polymer flow behaviour in **g/10 min**.

### D. Universal Exam Tips
- ☐ Always end the answer with **applications** — examiners allocate **1–2 marks** here.
- ☐ Use **neat labelled block diagrams** (mimic the SECTION-4 diagrams) — diagrams carry **at least 25 % of marks** in any process-based question.
- ☐ When comparing, **always use a 4-column table** with header columns: **Parameter / Process A / Process B / Remarks**.
- ☐ Numerical answers: **carry units throughout**; round to 2–3 significant figures only at the end.
- ☐ Memorise the **ASTM/ISO 7 categories of AM**; this is a guaranteed 2-mark question almost every KTU cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defects in Extrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Defects in Extrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defects in Extrusion** are internal or surface discontinuities, cracks, voids, or geometric irregularities that develop in the extruded product due to unfavourable combinations of process parameters such as extrusion ratio, die semi-angle, billet temperature, ram speed, lubrication, and material flow characteristics. They represent a deviation from the desired sound, defect-free, dimensionally accurate extruded profile.

In the context of KTU 2024 (PBMET304), the extrusion defect analysis is a high-yield topic typically covered under hot and cold working, falling within the broader module of metal forming processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study extrusion defects?**
> Extrusion is widely used for manufacturing long, uniform cross-section products (tubes, rods, channels, I-sections) from both non-ferrous (Al, Cu, brass) and ferrous alloys. Even minor defects reduce the mechanical strength, fatigue life, surface finish, and functional reliability of the part. Identifying the *cause* of each defect is critical for KTU design/production-type questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste with a small hole at the tip.

- If you squeeze **too hard (high extrusion ratio)** → the toothpaste at the **centre** (the last to come out) develops internal splitting (centre burst).
- If you squeeze when the tube is **almost empty (billet too short)** → a funnel-shaped cavity forms at the back (piping defect).
- If the **tip (die) is rough or hot spots exist** → the surface comes out with scratches (die lines, blistering).
- If the surrounding wall is **too cold (improper temperature)** → the outer surface tears (surface cracking).

The metal behaves exactly like the toothpaste — under the right conditions it flows beautifully, but with wrong parameters, it cracks, splits, or forms voids.

## 1.3 Classification of Extrusion Defects

Extrusion defects are broadly classified into three categories based on their location and origin:

| Category | Location | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Defects** | Inside the extruded product | Centre burst (Chevron cracking), Piping |
| **Surface Defects** | On the outer surface | Die lines, Surface cracking, Blistering, Tearing |
| **Geometric/Shape Defects** | Overall form of product | Barrelling, twisting, bending, uneven flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric (KTU Highlight):** The most important non-dimensional parameter governing extrusion defects is the **Extrusion Ratio (R)**, which for a round billet is defined as:
>
> $$R = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \left(\frac{D_0}{D_f}\right)^2$$
>
> where $A_0, D_0$ are the initial cross-sectional area and diameter, and $A_f, D_f$ are the final cross-sectional area and diameter. Typical industrial values of **R lie between 10 and 100**. Higher R ⇒ greater tendency for internal cracking.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Study of Major Extrusion Defects

### 2.1.1 Centre Burst (Chevron Cracking) — *Internal Defect*

- **Appearance:** A central, internal, zig-zag or V-shaped crack located along the longitudinal axis of the extruded product. The pattern resembles a chevron (>) when the section is split open.
- **Location:** Always at the **central core** of the billet/product.
- **Root Cause:** The central zone of the billet experiences **tensile (hoop) stresses** due to non-uniform radial flow of metal. As the outer material flows faster than the core, a void nucleates at the centre.
- **Primary Influencing Parameters:**
  1. **High Extrusion Ratio (R)** — promotes non-uniform flow.
  2. **Large Die Semi-Angle (α)** — increases dead-metal zone and tensile region size.
  3. **High Friction at Container–Billet interface** — accelerates uneven velocity gradient.
  4. **Low billet temperature / high ram speed** — reduces material ductility, promoting brittle failure.
- **Why and How:** The redundancy of work + radial pressure gradient produces a hydrostatic tensile zone at the centre, opposing the compressive hydrostatic stress that suppresses porosity. When tensile radial stress exceeds the material's hot fracture strength, central cracking initiates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept — Critical Extrusion Ratio:**
> Beyond a certain extrusion ratio (typically R > 5 for non-lubricated hot extrusion of aluminium), centre burst becomes almost unavoidable unless die angle and friction are carefully controlled.

### 2.1.2 Piping Defect — *End-of-Stroke Internal Defect*

- **Appearance:** A funnel-shaped or cone-shaped void that forms at the **back end (rear)** of the extruded product.
- **Root Cause:** When the ram approaches the end of the stroke, the **oxide-rich outer skin of the billet (the discard)** folds inward toward the central axis. The remaining thin core material flows outward radially instead of being extruded through the die. This inward folding creates a pipe-shaped cavity.
- **Primary Influencing Parameters:**
  1. **High Extrusion Ratio (R)** — amplifies the inward folding.
  2. **High Friction** at the container wall.
  3. **Improper discard handling** — discard length is set to 10–20% of billet length, but if too short, piping propagates into the product.
  4. **Use of direct (forward) extrusion** — reverse extrusion is far less prone to piping.
- **Why and How:** Velocity discontinuity at the rear face, combined with the dead-metal zone at the rear, forces circumferential inward flow, leading to material overlap and cavity formation.

### 2.1.3 Surface Cracking

- **Appearance:** Longitudinal or transverse surface tears, often in a "fir-tree" or "crocodile-skin" pattern.
- **Root Cause:** Tensile stresses act on the **outer surface** of the extruded product when it emerges from the die, due to the **residual shear strain** and **temperature drop** as the hot surface contacts cooler air/die land.
- **Primary Influencing Parameters:**
  1. **Excessive die semi-angle (α > 60°)** — outer fibres overstretch.
  2. **High extrusion speed** at low billet temperature.
  3. **Excessively high temperature** of die land causing surface hot shortness.
  4. **Insufficient billet homogenisation** for alloys like Al–Zn–Mg.

### 2.1.4 Blistering

- **Appearance:** Small surface or sub-surface bubbles/blisters on the extruded product.
- **Root Cause:** Dissolved gases (especially hydrogen) in the molten/cast billet come out of solution during heating and form gas pockets.
- **Primary Influencing Parameters:**
  1. Inadequate degassing of the melt before billet casting.
  2. Improper homogenisation treatment.

### 2.1.5 Die Lines and Surface Scratches

- **Appearance:** Longitudinal straight lines or scratches parallel to the extrusion direction.
- **Root Cause:** Surface roughness, scratches, or adhered metal on the die bearing surface; or hard particles in the billet skin.
- **Prevention:** Polishing die bearing; using proper die bearing length and entry radius.

### 2.1.6 Cold Shuts (Lap Defects)

- **Appearance:** Seam-like discontinuities on the surface, often at corners or thick-thin junctions.
- **Root Cause:** Two metal streams meeting at low temperature fail to weld together properly.

### 2.1.7 Twisting and Bending

- **Appearance:** The extruded product twists about its longitudinal axis or bends sideways.
- **Root Cause:** Asymmetric flow due to non-uniform billet temperature, die misalignment, or non-uniform friction.

## 2.2 The Six Most Frequently Tested Defects (KTU Quick List)

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Centre Burst / Chevron Cracking**
> 2. **Piping Defect**
> 3. **Surface Cracking / Tearing**
> 4. **Blistering**
> 5. **Die Lines**
> 6. **Cold Shuts (Laps)**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units / Typical Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Extrusion Ratio (R)** | $R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \left(\dfrac{D_0}{D_f}\right)^{2}$ | Dimensionless; 10 – 100 | Quantifies deformation severity |
| 2 | **True Strain in Extrusion** | $\varepsilon = \ln R = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | Dimensionless | Used in flow stress / work calculations |
| 3 | **Optimum Die Semi-Angle (Avitzur, upper bound)** | $\alpha_{opt} = \cos^{-1}\!\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{1 + \tfrac{2\mu L}{D_0}}}$ | radians / degrees | Minimises redundant work & centre burst |
| 4 | **Ideal Work (no friction, no redundant work)** | $W_i = Y_f \cdot A_f \cdot L_f \cdot \ln R$ | Joules (J) | Baseline deformation energy |
| 5 | **Homogeneous Strain Rate (axisymmetric extrusion)** | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{2 V_{ram} \tan\alpha}{D_0 - D_f} \cdot \ln R$ | $s^{-1}$ | Drives flow stress and defect formation |
| 6 | **Ram Pressure (ideal, no friction)** | $p = Y_f \ln R$ | Pa or MPa | Required container pressure |
| 7 | **Ram Pressure (with friction, direct extrusion)** | $p = Y_f \ln R \left(1 + \dfrac{2\mu L_b}{D_0}\right)$ | Pa or MPa | Direct (forward) extrusion pressure |
| 8 | **Billet L/D ratio (safe limit)** | $L_b / D_0 \le 3$ to 4 | Dimensionless | Prevents buckling and piping |
| 9 | **Discard Length** | $L_{disc} = 10$ to $20\%$ of billet length | mm | Eliminates piping into the product |
| 10 | **Critical Extrusion Ratio (centre burst onset)** | $R_{crit} \approx 5$ (unlubricated Al hot extrusion) | Dimensionless | Design boundary for defect-free extrusion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to remember for KTU derivations:**
> - Homogeneous deformation: frictionless, no redundant work.
> - Sticking friction: $\tau = \dfrac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}}$ at billet–die and billet–container interface.
> - For **optimum die angle**, the redundant work factor $\phi$ equals the friction work factor $\frac{2L}{D_0}\mu$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimum Die Semi-Angle (Avitzur Upper Bound)

This is a **high-value derivation** in KTU 2024 Scheme Manufacturing Processes.

### Step 1: Set up velocity field
Avitzur assumed a conical velocity field for axisymmetric extrusion, where a particle on the die cone surface moves with velocity $V_d$ along the die wall:

$$V_d = V_{ram} \cdot \frac{\pi D_0^2 / 4}{\pi D^2 / 4} = V_{ram}\left(\frac{D_0}{D}\right)^2$$

where $D$ is the instantaneous diameter at the die entry.

### Step 2: Write upper-bound total power
The total power $\dot{W}$ consists of ideal power $\dot{W}_i$, redundant power $\dot{W}_r$, and friction power $\dot{W}_f$:

$$\dot{W} = \dot{W}_i + \dot{W}_r + \dot{W}_f$$

Ideal power:

$$\dot{W}_i = \sigma_0 \cdot A_f \cdot V_f \cdot \ln R$$

where $\sigma_0 = \dfrac{2 Y_f}{\sqrt{3}}$ is the effective flow stress.

Friction power at the die–billet conical surface (with friction factor $m$):

$$\dot{W}_f = \dfrac{m \sigma_0}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot A_d \cdot V_d$$

where $A_d$ is the slant area of the die cone.

Redundant power from the velocity discontinuity (using the sine-gap model):

$$\dot{W}_r = \dfrac{2 \sigma_0}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot A_d \cdot V_d \cdot \left[\cot\alpha - \tan\alpha\right] \cdot \dfrac{f(\alpha)}{1 - f(\alpha)}$$

where $f(\alpha) = \dfrac{1 - \sin\alpha}{2 \cos\alpha}$ and $\alpha$ is the die semi-angle.

### Step 3: Total extrusion pressure
The total extrusion pressure $p_{ext}$ equals total power divided by ram velocity:

$$p_{ext} = \frac{\dot{W}}{V_{ram} A_0}$$

### Step 4: Minimise $p_{ext}$ with respect to die semi-angle
Setting $\dfrac{\partial p_{ext}}{\partial \alpha} = 0$ and equating friction contribution to redundant work contribution:

$$\boxed{\alpha_{opt} = \cos^{-1}\sqrt{\frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2 \mu L}{D_0}}}}$$

> **Interpretation:**
> - Larger friction coefficient $\mu$ → smaller $\alpha_{opt}$ (sharper die).
> - Larger die land length $L$ → smaller $\alpha_{opt}$.
> - Larger billet diameter $D_0$ → $\alpha_{opt}$ approaches 90° (blunter die acceptable).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Condition for Centre Burst (Hill's Criterion)

Hill (1948) showed that centre burst occurs when the **mean hydrostatic stress at the centre becomes tensile**.

For axisymmetric extrusion, the radial stress $\sigma_r$ at the centre along the axis satisfies the equilibrium:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dz} + \frac{2 \tau_{rz}}{r} = 0$$

For sticking friction at the container wall, $\tau_{rz} = \dfrac{Y_f}{\sqrt{3}}$, and integrating from the die entry to a point at the axis:

$$-\sigma_r = \int \frac{2 \tau_{rz}}{r} dz$$

This radial stress becomes **tensile** if the friction-integral exceeds the material's flow strength. Hill proposed the critical condition:

$$\boxed{\frac{2 L_b}{D_0} > 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Centre burst likely}}$$

with $L_b$ being the remaining billet length being extruded. As the stroke proceeds, $L_b$ decreases, so centre burst risk is highest **mid-stroke** and lowest at the beginning.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cause–Effect Mapping for Each Defect

| Defect | Primary Cause | Secondary Cause | Affected Zone | Cure / Prevention |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Centre Burst** | High R, large die angle | High friction, low T | Centre core | Lower R, use $\alpha_{opt}$, good lubrication |
| **Piping** | Folding of billet skin at end of stroke | High R, high friction | Rear of product | Use longer discard; use indirect extrusion |
| **Surface Cracking** | Tensile stress on exit surface | High speed, low T, large $\alpha$ | Outer surface | Lower speed; use sharper die; pre-heat die |
| **Blistering** | Dissolved gas in billet | Poor degassing | Sub-surface | Degassing; proper homogenisation |
| **Die Lines** | Die bearing damage | Adhered metal; hard particles | Outer surface | Polish die; clean die bearing |
| **Cold Shuts** | Low weldability of streams | Low T, low pressure | Corners, junctions | Increase T, increase ram pressure |
| **Twisting/Bending** | Asymmetric flow | T gradient, die misalignment | Whole product | Uniform pre-heat; align die |

## 3.4 Numerical-Style Analytical Reasoning (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A cylindrical aluminium billet of diameter $D_0 = 100\,\text{mm}$ and length $L_b = 200\,\text{mm}$ is extruded at 450 °C to a final diameter $D_f = 25\,\text{mm}$. The flow stress is $Y_f = 80\,\text{MPa}$, and the friction factor $m = 0.3$. The die semi-angle is $\alpha = 45°$. Determine whether centre burst is likely, and compute the optimum die angle.

**Step 1 — Extrusion ratio:**

$$R = \left(\frac{D_0}{D_f}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{100}{25}\right)^2 = 16$$

**Step 2 — True strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \ln R = \ln(16) = 2.77$$

**Step 3 — Ideal extrusion pressure:**

$$p_i = Y_f \cdot \ln R = 80 \times 2.77 = 221.6 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Hill's criterion (mid-stroke):**

$$\frac{2 L_b}{D_0} = \frac{2 \times 200}{100} = 4.0 > 1$$

> Hence **centre burst is highly likely** under these conditions. Billet length-to-diameter ratio must be reduced, or friction lowered.

**Step 5 — Optimum die angle (using $L \approx 0.3 D_0$ for land length):**

$$\alpha_{opt} = \cos^{-1}\sqrt{\frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2 \times 0.3 \times 30}{100}}} = \cos^{-1}\sqrt{\frac{1}{1.18}}$$

$$= \cos^{-1}(0.921) = 22.9°$$

> The given $\alpha = 45°$ is **far above the optimum**, which is another reason centre burst is likely. Recommended action: use $\alpha = 22.9°$ and apply efficient lubrication.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Defect-Formation Flow Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Billet Loaded into Container"] --> B["Ram Applies Compressive Load"]
    B --> C["Metal Flows Through Die Cone"]
    C --> D{"Process Parameters\nwithin Safe Range?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Sound Defect Free Extrudate"]
    D -- No --> F["Identify Defect Category"]
    F --> G1["Centre Burst Chevron Crack"]
    F --> G2["Piping at Rear End"]
    F --> G3["Surface Cracking Tearing"]
    F --> G4["Blistering Sub surface"]
    F --> G5["Die Lines Surface Scratches"]
    F --> G6["Cold Shuts Laps"]
    G1 --> H1["Cause: High R, Large Alpha, Friction"]
    G2 --> H2["Cause: Oxide Skin Inward Folding"]
    G3 --> H3["Cause: Tensile Surface Stress"]
    G4 --> H4["Cause: Dissolved Gas in Billet"]
    G5 --> H5["Cause: Die Bearing Damage"]
    G6 --> H6["Cause: Incomplete Metal Welding"]
    H1 --> I1["Fix: Optimum Alpha, Lubrication, Lower R"]
    H2 --> I2["Fix: Longer Discard, Indirect Extrusion"]
    H3 --> I3["Fix: Lower Speed, Preheat Die"]
    H4 --> I4["Fix: Degassing, Homogenisation"]
    H5 --> I5["Fix: Polish Die, Clean Bearing"]
    H6 --> I6["Fix: Higher T, Higher Pressure"]
    I1 --> J["Acceptable Defect Free Product"]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    I6 --> J
```

## 4.2 Stress-State Architecture Around the Die (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONE["Die Cone Region"]
        S1["Surface Zone: Tensile Hoop Stress sigma_t > 0"]
        S2["Intermediate Zone: Compressive Hydrostatic sigma_h  approx  -Y_f 3"]
        S3["Centre Axis: Tensile Radial Stress sigma_r > 0 → CENTRE BURST"]
    end
    CONE -- "Material flows outward" --> OUT["Extrudate Exits Die Land"]
    OUT -- "Surface cools" --> SURF["Surface Tensile Stress → SURFACE CRACKING"]
```

## 4.3 Billet-End Architecture (Piping Formation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start of Stroke: Uniform Flow"] --> B["Mid Stroke: Stable Extrusion"]
    B --> C["End of Stroke: Oxide Skin Approaches Die"]
    C --> D["Outer Skin Folds Inward"]
    D --> E["Piping Defect Funnel Shaped Void Forms"]
    E --> F["Discard Slices Off the Defect"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** These block-architecture flows capture the *cause → defect → consequence* relationship visually. In KTU board exam answers, always accompany text with such labelled block diagrams for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]
**What is centre burst defect in extrusion? Mention its two main causes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Centre burst, also called **chevron cracking**, is an internal longitudinal crack that forms at the central core of an extruded product. The two main causes are:
1. **High extrusion ratio (R)** combined with non-uniform radial flow that creates tensile radial stress at the centre.
2. **Large die semi-angle (α)** and **high friction at container wall** that increase redundant work and tensile zone size.

(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each cause = 3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]
**Distinguish between direct and indirect extrusion in the context of piping defect.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Direct Extrusion | Indirect Extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| Billet–Container relative motion | Yes (friction exists) | No (billet–container static) |
| **Piping defect** | **Severe** (skin folds at rear) | **Negligible / absent** |
| Discard required | Long discard (10–20% L_b) | Minimal or no discard |
| Ram load | Higher (friction) | Lower (no friction) |

(1 mark for piping mention in each, 1 mark for discard distinction = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model QP, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain **centre burst defect** in extrusion. Discuss the process parameters that influence its formation and the remedies to prevent it.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the **optimum die semi-angle** in axisymmetric extrusion using the upper-bound (Avitzur) approach, and explain how this optimum helps to minimise extrusion defects.

#### Model Solution A(a) — Centre Burst Defect

1. **Sketch (2 marks):** Conical die with V-shaped internal crack shown on the axis of the extruded rod.
2. **Definition (1 mark):** Internal V-shaped crack at the centre of the extrudate.
3. **Mechanism (2 marks):** Non-uniform radial flow → tensile radial stress at centre → hydrostatic tension overcomes flow strength → void nucleates and grows into chevron crack.
4. **Influencing parameters (1 mark):** Extrusion ratio, die angle, friction, temperature, ram speed.
5. **Remedies (1 mark):** Reduce R, use optimum die angle, apply lubricant, control ram speed, preheat billet and die.

#### Model Solution A(b) — Optimum Die Semi-Angle Derivation

1. State upper-bound theorem and Avitzur conical velocity field (1 mark).
2. Write expressions for ideal, redundant, and friction powers (2 marks).
3. Differentiate total pressure with respect to α and set to zero (2 marks).
4. Arrive at $\alpha_{opt} = \cos^{-1}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{1 + \tfrac{2\mu L}{D_0}}}$ (1 mark).
5. Conclude: at $\alpha_{opt}$, redundant work balances friction work, giving minimum pressure and minimum centre burst tendency (1 mark).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Model QP, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **piping defect** in direct extrusion with a labelled diagram. Discuss the role of the **discard** in eliminating this defect and compare piping in direct vs indirect extrusion.

**(b) [7 marks]** A cylindrical aluminium billet of diameter 75 mm is extruded at 400 °C through a die to produce a rod of 20 mm diameter. The flow stress at this temperature is 60 MPa. The container friction factor is $m = 0.2$ and the die semi-angle is 60°. Calculate:
(i) The extrusion ratio and true strain.
(ii) The ideal extrusion pressure.
(iii) Whether this die angle is suitable if the optimum angle works out to 25°. Justify your answer.

#### Model Solution B(a) — Piping Defect

1. **Diagram (2 marks):** Billet with rear cone-shaped cavity inside, oxide skin fold shown.
2. **Mechanism (2 marks):** As ram nears end of stroke, the outer oxide-rich skin folds inward due to the conical dead-metal zone at the rear; this folded material creates a void.
3. **Discard function (1 mark):** Discard length (10–20% of billet length) is sliced off to remove the piped portion.
4. **Direct vs Indirect (2 marks):** Direct extrusion → severe piping (skin moves relative to container). Indirect extrusion → no relative motion → no piping. Hence indirect extrusion eliminates the need for a long discard.

#### Model Solution B(b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Extrusion Ratio:**

$$R = \left(\frac{75}{20}\right)^2 = 14.06$$

**Step 2 — True Strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \ln(14.06) = 2.64$$

**Step 3 — Ideal Pressure:**

$$p_i = Y_f \cdot \varepsilon = 60 \times 2.64 = 158.6 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Suitability of Die Angle:**

Given $\alpha = 60°$, optimum $\alpha_{opt} = 25°$. Since $\alpha \gg \alpha_{opt}$, the die is **too blunt**. The current die will cause:
- Excessive redundant work.
- Large centre burst probability.
- Higher ram pressure than necessary.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The die should be re-machined to a semi-angle close to **25°** to obtain a defect-free extrudate with minimum energy consumption.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Extrusion Defects:**
> 1. **Writing only defect names without causes** — KTU examiners expect at least 1 cause + 1 prevention for each defect. Skipping these = 1 mark lost per defect.
> 2. **No diagram in long answers** — A neat, labelled diagram of centre burst or piping is worth 2 marks by itself. Always draw it.
> 3. **Confusing direct vs indirect extrusion** — In direct extrusion, the *billet moves*; in indirect extrusion, the *die moves*. This reversal of motion changes friction, piping, and discard requirements. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 4. **Wrong formula for extrusion pressure** — Using $p = Y_f \cdot R$ instead of $p = Y_f \cdot \ln R$ is a common error. The correct expression uses the *logarithmic* strain.
> 5. **Optimum die angle derivation — missing $\mu L / D_0$ term** — Many students forget the friction-and-land-length group. The result must explicitly contain $\mu$, $L$, and $D_0$.
> 6. **Forgetting the discard's role in piping** — A piping answer without mentioning discard slicing loses at least 1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] Extrusion ratio $R = (D_0/D_f)^2$ is the single most important parameter.
- [ ] True strain $\varepsilon = \ln R$; pressure $p = Y_f \ln R$ (ideal).
- [ ] **Centre burst** → internal V-crack → caused by tensile radial stress at centre.
- [ ] **Piping** → funnel-shaped void at rear → caused by inward folding of oxide skin.
- [ ] **Surface cracking** → outer surface tearing → caused by tensile stress on exit.
- [ ] **Blistering** → dissolved gas → cure by degassing.
- [ ] **Die lines** → die bearing damage → cure by polishing.
- [ ] **Cold shuts** → incomplete metal welding at junctions.
- [ ] Direct extrusion has higher friction and piping; indirect extrusion has neither.
- [ ] **Hill's criterion** for centre burst: $2 L_b / D_0 > 1$ ⇒ centre burst likely.
- [ ] **Avitzur's optimum die angle** formula: $\alpha_{opt} = \cos^{-1}\sqrt{1/(1 + 2\mu L/D_0)}$.
- [ ] Discard length = 10–20% of billet length eliminates piping in direct extrusion.
- [ ] Higher $R$ and larger $\alpha$ ⇒ higher defect probability.
- [ ] Lower friction, pre-heated billet and die, controlled ram speed ⇒ defect-free extrusion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET304 - MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PBMET304 – Manufacturing Processes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the term **"Applications"** in Module 4 refers to the **industrial and engineering deployment of the major secondary and tertiary manufacturing processes** — namely **Casting, Bulk Deformation (Forming), Sheet Metal Forming, Powder Metallurgy, Joining (Welding, Brazing, Soldering), and Polymer/Composite Processing** — for converting raw engineering materials (ferrous, non-ferrous metals, polymers, ceramics, and composites) into **functional, dimensionally accurate, and economically viable end-use components** across automotive, aerospace, biomedical, electronic, construction, and energy sectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Applications in manufacturing refer to the systematic selection and utilization of a specific manufacturing process based on part geometry, material properties, production volume, dimensional tolerance, surface finish, and economic feasibility (cost per part).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain-English Analogy — "The Manufacturing Restaurant Kitchen":**

Imagine a giant industrial kitchen. You (the design engineer) walk in with a **"blueprint dish order"** — say, a turbine blade for a jet engine, a femoral implant for a hip replacement, or a car body panel.

- **Casting** is like *pouring molten chocolate into a shaped mold* — best for complex, one-piece metal parts (engine blocks).
- **Forging** is like *hammering dough on a cutting board* — best for tough, impact-resistant parts (crankshafts, connecting rods).
- **Sheet Metal Forming** is like *stretching aluminum foil over a shaped die* — best for thin-walled hollow parts (car doors, aircraft skins).
- **Powder Metallurgy (P/M)** is like *compacting and baking sand into a brick* — best for porous or self-lubricating parts (sintered gears, oil-impregnated bearings).
- **Welding** is like *gluing two metal pieces together with melted metal* — best for large assembled structures (ship hulls, pressure vessels).
- **Polymer Processing** is like *injecting melted plastic into a candy mold* — best for lightweight, mass-produced parts (toys, housings, bottles).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Metric (MUST MEMORIZE):**
> - **Material Utilization Efficiency (MUE)** = (Mass of Finished Part / Mass of Raw Material Input) × **100%**
> - Processes with high MUE (≥ 80%): Powder Metallurgy, Cold Forging, Precision Casting.
> - Processes with low MUE (≤ 50%): Conventional Machining, Hot Forging without flash control.

## 1.3 Why Applications Matter in Manufacturing Engineering

The selection of a manufacturing process is governed by **four primary criteria** (the KTU-trinity of process selection):

1. **Material compatibility** — Can the process handle the workpiece material (e.g., melting point, ductility)?
2. **Geometry feasibility** — Can the process create the required shape (undercuts, internal cavities, thin walls)?
3. **Production volume & cost** — Is the process economical for the required lot size (e.g., sand casting for < 500 units; die casting for > 10,000 units)?
4. **Quality requirements** — Can the process meet tolerance (IT grade) and surface finish (Ra in μm)?

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU exams, a common Part A question is *"List any four criteria for selection of a manufacturing process."* The four points above are the **canonical answer**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Process Capability Matrix

Every manufacturing process has a **unique operating envelope** defined by:
- Achievable **dimensional tolerance** (IT grade).
- Achievable **surface roughness** (Ra).
- Achievable **part complexity** (geometry score).
- Achievable **production rate** (parts/hour).
- Achievable **material range** (compatible materials).

### 2.1.1 Application Domains of Major Processes

**(A) Casting Applications:**

| Process | Typical Applications | Why This Process? |
|---|---|---|
| **Sand Casting** | Engine blocks, machine tool beds, pump housings, large valves | Low tooling cost; handles large parts; ferrous & non-ferrous |
| **Investment Casting (Lost Wax)** | Turbine blades, jewelry, dental implants, surgical instruments | Excellent surface finish (Ra 0.8–3.2 μm); intricate shapes |
| **Die Casting (Hot Chamber)** | Zinc & magnesium small parts — locks, gears, carburetor bodies | High production rate (500–5000 parts/hr); thin walls (1–3 mm) |
| **Die Casting (Cold Chamber)** | Aluminum automotive parts — cylinder heads, transmission cases | Handles high-melting-point alloys (Al, Cu) |
| **Centrifugal Casting** | Pipes, tubes, cylinder liners, bus bearings | Dense, defect-free cylindrical parts; no core needed |
| **Continuous Casting** | Steel slabs, billets, blooms for rolling mills | High MUE (~95%); direct conversion to semi-finished stock |

**(B) Bulk Deformation (Forging) Applications:**

| Process | Typical Applications | Why This Process? |
|---|---|---|
| **Open-Die Forging** | Large shafts, rings, disks (turbine rotors) | Low cost for small batches; parts up to several tonnes |
| **Closed-Die (Impression-Die) Forging** | Connecting rods, crankshafts, gears, hand tools | High strength due to grain flow; mass production |
| **Hot Forging** | Large structural parts, railway axles | Low flow stress; large deformation possible |
| **Cold Forging (Cold Heading)** | Bolts, screws, rivets, spark plug bodies | High MUE (>90%); superior surface finish & strength |
| **Extrusion (Direct/Indirect)** | Aluminum window frames, heat sinks, tubes, rods | Constant cross-section; long lengths |
| **Rolling (Hot/Cold)** | Steel plates, sheets, I-beams, rails | Highest production rate for stock material |

**(C) Sheet Metal Forming Applications:**

| Process | Typical Applications | Why This Process? |
|---|---|---|
| **Bending** | Brackets, enclosures, chassis | Simple tooling; low cost |
| **Deep Drawing** | Cans, kitchen sinks, automotive fuel tanks, battery cases | Converts flat sheet into deep hollow parts |
| **Stretch Forming** | Aircraft skin panels, automotive body panels | Large, contoured panels with smooth curves |
| **Hydroforming** | Bicycle frames, automotive frame rails, exhaust manifolds | Complex hollow parts; uniform thickness |
| **Spinning** | Satellite dishes, pressure vessel heads, musical cymbals | Axisymmetric hollow parts; low tooling cost |

**(D) Powder Metallurgy Applications:**

| Process | Typical Applications | Why This Process? |
|---|---|---|
| **Conventional P/M** | Sintered gears, oil-impregnated bearings, structural parts | Self-lubricating; near-net-shape (MUE > 95%) |
| **Metal Injection Molding (MIM)** | Watch cases, orthodontic brackets, firearm triggers, dental brackets | Complex small parts from hard-to-machine materials |
| **Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing — SLS/SLM)** | Prototypes, lattice structures, custom implants, aerospace brackets | Unmatched geometric freedom; no tooling |

**(E) Joining Process Applications:**

| Process | Typical Applications | Why This Process? |
|---|---|---|
| **Arc Welding (SMAW/GMAW/GTAW)** | Ship hulls, bridges, pressure vessels, pipelines | Versatile; field-portable; strong joints |
| **Resistance Welding (Spot/Seam)** | Automotive body-in-white assembly, sheet metal cabinets | Fast; automation-friendly; no filler metal |
| **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** | Battery tabs, hermetic seals, tailored blanks | Narrow HAZ; high speed; precision |
| **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** | Aerospace rocket nozzles, nuclear components | Deep penetration; vacuum environment |
| **Brazing** | Carbide tool tips, refrigeration coils, plumbing | Joins dissimilar metals; lower temperature |
| **Soldering** | Electronic PCB assembly, plumbing joints | Low temperature (< 450 °C); electrical conductivity |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application / Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Material Utilization Efficiency (MUE)** | $\text{MUE} = \dfrac{m_{\text{part}}}{m_{\text{raw}}} \times 100$ | % | Process selection, cost estimation |
| 2 | **Casting Yield** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{cast part}}}{m_{\text{melt poured}}} \times 100$ | % | Foundry economics |
| 3 | **Forging Reduction Ratio (R)** | $R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \dfrac{h_0}{h_f}$ | dimensionless | Forging design; grain refinement |
| 4 | **True Strain in Forging** | $\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | dimensionless | Plastic deformation analysis |
| 5 | **Flow Stress (Hot Working)** | $\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^{n} \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$ | MPa | Forging load estimation |
| 6 | **Drawing Force (Sheet Metal)** | $F_d = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \text{UTS} \cdot \mu$ | N | Deep drawing die design |
| 7 | **Drawing Ratio (DR)** | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{D_{\text{blank}}}{D_{\text{punch}}}$ | dimensionless | Deep drawing feasibility (DR ≤ 2.0–2.2 typical) |
| 8 | **P/M Compaction Pressure** | $P = P_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-K \cdot \rho_R\right)$ | MPa | Powder metallurgy die design |
| 9 | **Sintering Shrinkage** | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | mm | P/M dimensional control |
| 10 | **Welding Heat Input** | $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Arc welding procedure specification |
| 11 | **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) Width** | $\text{HAZ} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{Q}{v}}$ | mm | Welding metallurgy |
| 12 | **Casting Shrinkage Allowance** | $L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{part}} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{100}\right)$ | mm | Pattern design |
| 13 | **Reynolds Number (Casting Fluidity)** | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Mold filling analysis |
| 14 | **Specific Energy (Machining)** | $U = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v}{Z \cdot MRR}$ | J/mm³ | Process economics |
| 15 | **Production Rate (Mass Prod.)** | $R_p = \dfrac{3600}{t_{\text{cycle}}}$ | parts/hr | Die casting, MIM |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Tip for Tables:** Notice how absolute-value bars and ratios are written using `\dfrac`, `\ln`, and `\exp` (not the pipe `|` character). This prevents markdown table-row corruption.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Selected Process | Reason for Selection |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Investment Casting, Forging, Additive Mfg. (SLM) | High strength-to-weight; complex superalloys; low volume |
| **Automotive (Mass Production)** | Die Casting, Stamping, Resistance Spot Welding | High production rate (>1 million/year); low unit cost |
| **Biomedical** | Investment Casting, MIM, SLM (Ti6Al4V) | Customized implants; biocompatibility; complex porous structures |
| **Electronics** | MIM, Soldering, Adhesive Bonding | Miniaturization; complex micro-features; thermal management |
| **Energy (Power Plants)** | Sand Casting, Centrifugal Casting, Arc Welding | Large structural parts; high-temperature alloys; on-site fabrication |
| **Defense** | Closed-Die Forging, EBW, Hydroforming | Ballistic resistance; high strength; complex hollow structures |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Forging Reduction Ratio & True Strain

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical):** A cylindrical billet of initial height $h_0 = 200 \text{ mm}$ is forged in an open-die press to a final height $h_f = 50 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the **reduction ratio (R)** and the **true strain (ε)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Data**

$$h_0 = 200 \text{ mm}, \quad h_f = 50 \text{ mm}$$

The forging process is open-die compression. Since volume is conserved in plastic deformation of a solid (incompressibility assumption for metals, Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.5$), the reduction in height causes a proportional increase in cross-sectional area.

**Step 2 — Apply the Definition of Reduction Ratio**

By definition (volume constancy: $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$):

$$R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \dfrac{h_0}{h_f}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$R = \dfrac{200 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ mm}} = 4.0$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result**

A reduction ratio of **4.0** means the cross-sectional area has increased by a factor of 4. This indicates **substantial grain refinement and mechanical property improvement** in the forged part.

**Step 5 — Apply the Definition of True Strain**

$$\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$\varepsilon = \ln(4.0) = 1.386$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the Strain Result**

A true strain of **1.386** (or ~138.6%) indicates a **large plastic deformation** — typical of hot forging operations on steel and aluminum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Reduction ratios of $R \geq 4$ are recommended for **homogeneous grain refinement** in wrought products. Lower values ($R \approx 2$) yield only surface-level grain flow improvement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deep Drawing Feasibility Check

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical):** A cylindrical cup is to be produced by deep drawing from a circular blank of diameter $D_0 = 150 \text{ mm}$. The punch diameter is $d_p = 80 \text{ mm}$. Check the **drawability** using the **Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR)** criterion for steel sheet (assume $\text{LDR} = 2.2$).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Actual Drawing Ratio (DR)**

$$\text{DR} = \dfrac{D_0}{d_p}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$\text{DR} = \dfrac{150 \text{ mm}}{80 \text{ mm}} = 1.875$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Limiting Drawing Ratio (LDR)**

For low-carbon steel sheets with good lubrication:

$$\text{LDR}_{\text{steel}} \approx 2.0 \text{ – } 2.2$$

**Step 4 — Draw the Feasibility Conclusion**

Since:

$$\text{DR} = 1.875 < \text{LDR} = 2.2$$

The drawing operation is **feasible in a single stage** without intermediate annealing.

**Step 5 — Compute the Blank Holding Force (BHF) Approximation**

For a steel sheet with thickness $t = 1.0 \text{ mm}$ and yield strength $S_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$:

$$F_{bh} \approx 0.015 \cdot \pi \cdot (D_0^2 - d_p^2) \cdot S_y$$

$$F_{bh} \approx 0.015 \times \pi \times (150^2 - 80^2) \times 250 \text{ / factor}$$

Simplifying:

$$F_{bh} = 0.015 \times \pi \times (22500 - 6400) \times \frac{1}{1000} \approx 0.76 \text{ kN}$$

(This is a simplified empirical formula; KTU typically accepts ±10% deviation.)

> [!NOTE]
> **If DR > LDR**, the blank must be drawn in **multiple stages** with **intermediate annealing** between stages to restore ductility.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Welding Heat Input Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical):** In a Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) process, the voltage is $V = 28 \text{ V}$, the current is $I = 220 \text{ A}$, and the travel speed is $S = 5 \text{ mm/s}$. Calculate the **heat input per unit length** in kJ/mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the Standard Heat Input Formula**

$$Q = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}$$

Where:
- $V$ = arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = welding current (A)
- $S$ = travel speed (mm/min — note the **60** converts seconds to minutes in the numerator)
- 1000 converts J to kJ in the denominator

**Step 2 — Convert Units Carefully**

Travel speed $S$ is given in mm/s, so we must multiply by 60 to get mm/min:

$$S = 5 \text{ mm/s} \times 60 = 300 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute All Numerical Values**

$$Q = \dfrac{28 \text{ V} \times 220 \text{ A} \times 60}{300 \text{ mm/min} \times 1000}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerator and Denominator**

$$Q = \dfrac{28 \times 220 \times 60}{300 \times 1000} = \dfrac{369{,}600}{300{,}000}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$Q = 1.232 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Input Guidelines (KTU high-yield):**
> - Low carbon steel: $Q \leq 1.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$ (to avoid HAZ softening).
> - Stainless steel: $Q \leq 0.8 \text{ kJ/mm}$ (to prevent sensitization).
> - Aluminum alloys: $Q \leq 0.6 \text{ kJ/mm}$ (to prevent burn-through).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to convert **mm/s → mm/min** (multiplying by 60). Always do a unit check first!

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Decision Table

| Part Requirement | Recommended Process | Backup Process | Why Not the Alternatives? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine block (cast iron, complex internal cavities) | **Sand Casting** | Permanent mold casting | Sand is cheaper for large parts; permanent mold can't handle such large sizes |
| Turbine blade (Inconel, complex airfoil) | **Investment Casting** | Additive Mfg. (SLM) | Investment casting is mature & cheaper; SLM used only for prototypes/low volume |
| Crankshaft (steel, high fatigue strength) | **Closed-Die Forging** | Machining from bar | Forging provides continuous grain flow → fatigue resistance |
| Car door panel (steel sheet, large area) | **Deep Drawing** | Hydroforming | Deep drawing is faster; hydroforming used for frame rails |
| Sintered bronze bushing (porous) | **Powder Metallurgy** | Cast bronze (machined) | P/M gives self-lubricating porosity; casting is denser |
| LPG cylinder (steel, pressure-tight) | **Deep Drawing + Arc Welding** | Hot spinning + EBW | Drawing is mass-producible; EBW too expensive for consumer product |
| Hip implant (Ti6Al4V, custom geometry) | **Additive Mfg. (SLM/EBM)** | Investment casting | Patient-specific; no two implants are identical |
| Car body-in-white (steel sheet, 300+ parts) | **Resistance Spot Welding** | Laser welding | Spot welding is fast & reliable; laser used for tailored blanks |
| Carbide cutting tool tip (WC-Co on steel shank) | **Brazing** | Mechanical clamping | Brazing provides rigid joint & good heat transfer |
| Electronic PCB (copper tracks on FR-4) | **Soldering** | Conductive adhesive | Soldering is standard, reliable, and reworkable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Manufacturing Process Selection Decision Tree

The following flowchart helps students **systematically select the correct manufacturing process** for any given part — a classic KTU 14-mark problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Part Requirement] --> B{Material Type?}
    B -- Metal --> C{Part Size?}
    B -- Polymer --> D[Polymer Processing]
    B -- Ceramic --> E[Ceramic Forming]
    C -- Large --> F[Sand Casting / Forging]
    C -- Medium --> G{Geometry Complex?}
    C -- Small --> H[Powder Metallurgy / MIM]
    G -- Yes --> I[Investment Casting]
    G -- No --> J[Closed-Die Forging / Stamping]
    F --> K{Mechanical Strength Needed?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Closed-Die Forging]
    K -- No --> M[Sand Casting]
    D --> N{Production Volume?}
    N -- High --> O[Injection Molding]
    N -- Low --> P[3D Printing / Casting]
    H --> Q{Complex Geometry?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Metal Injection Molding]
    Q -- No --> S[Conventional P and M]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Start at `A` (Part Requirement).
- Branch by **Material Type** → then by **Part Size** and **Geometry Complexity**.
- Each terminal node gives the **recommended KTU-approved process**.

## 4.2 Comparative Process Capability Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Casting
        C1[Sand Casting]
        C2[Investment Casting]
        C3[Die Casting]
    end
    subgraph Forming
        F1[Open-Die Forging]
        F2[Closed-Die Forging]
        F3[Extrusion]
    end
    subgraph SheetMetal
        S1[Deep Drawing]
        S2[Hydroforming]
        S3[Stamping]
    end
    subgraph PowderMet
        P1[Conventional PM]
        P2[MIM]
        P3[Additive Mfg]
    end
    subgraph Joining
        J1[Arc Welding]
        J2[Spot Welding]
        J3[Laser Welding]
    end
    Casting --> App1[Aerospace, Automotive, Pumps]
    Forming --> App2[Aerospace, Defense, Tools]
    SheetMetal --> App3[Automotive, Aerospace, Consumer]
    PowderMet --> App4[Gears, Implants, Prototypes]
    Joining --> App5[Shipbuilding, Bridges, Cars]
```

## 4.3 Welding Process Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Welding Process] --> W1[Arc Welding]
    W --> W2[Resistance Welding]
    W --> W3[High-Energy Beam Welding]
    W --> W4[Solid-State Welding]
    W1 --> W1a[SMAW]
    W1 --> W1b[GMAW]
    W1 --> W1c[GTAW]
    W1 --> W1d[Plasma Arc]
    W2 --> W2a[Spot Welding]
    W2 --> W2b[Seam Welding]
    W2 --> W2c[Projection Welding]
    W3 --> W3a[Laser Beam LBW]
    W3 --> W3b[Electron Beam EBW]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: List any four criteria for the selection of a suitable manufacturing process for a given component.**

**Model Answer (4 points × 0.75 ≈ 3 marks):**

1. **Material compatibility** — The process must be capable of handling the workpiece material (e.g., melting point, ductility, reactivity).
2. **Geometric complexity** — The process should be able to produce the required shape (undercuts, internal cavities, thin walls, tolerance).
3. **Production volume / Economic batch size** — Sand casting for < 500 units; die casting or stamping for > 10,000 units.
4. **Quality requirements** — Achievable dimensional tolerance (IT grade) and surface finish (Ra in μm).
5. *(Optional 5th point for full marks)* Available **equipment and tooling infrastructure** in the industry.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between hot working and cold working of metals. Give two examples of products made by each process.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Above recrystallization temperature (~0.6 × T_melting in K) | Below recrystallization temperature |
| **Strength** | Lower yield strength, higher ductility | Higher yield strength (work hardening) |
| **Surface finish** | Poor (oxide scale) | Excellent (smooth, bright) |
| **Dimensional accuracy** | Poor (scale, thermal distortion) | High (close tolerances) |
| **Examples** | Hot rolled steel plates, hot forged crankshafts | Cold drawn wires, cold headed bolts, deep drawn cans |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **Investment Casting (Lost Wax) process** with a neat block diagram. List **six industrial applications** of investment casting with the material used for each.
**Marks: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**

**(a) Investment Casting Process — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

1. **Pattern Making:** A wax (or polystyrene) pattern is injection-molded to the exact shape of the desired part. Multiple patterns are assembled onto a wax sprue to form a "tree."
2. **Shell Building:** The wax tree is repeatedly dipped into a ceramic slurry, followed by stuccoing with fine refractory sand. This is repeated 6–8 times to build a strong ceramic shell (~6–15 mm thick).
3. **Dewaxing (Burnout):** The ceramic-coated tree is placed in an autoclave or flash-dewax furnace at ~150–200 °C. The wax melts and drains out rapidly, leaving a perfect ceramic mold cavity.
4. **Mold Firing:** The ceramic shell is fired at 900–1100 °C to burn out residual wax, sinter the ceramic, and develop high strength.
5. **Pouring:** Molten metal (superheated by 100–200 °C above its melting point) is poured into the preheated shell under gravity or vacuum.
6. **Cooling & Solidification:** The metal solidifies inside the shell, replicating the wax pattern's geometry.
7. **Shell Removal & Finishing:** The ceramic shell is broken away (vibrated or hammered), the parts are cut from the tree, and final grinding/polishing is done.

**Six Industrial Applications (each 1 mark ≈ 7 marks):**

1. **Aerospace turbine blades** (Ni-based superalloys like Inconel 718) — for high-temperature strength.
2. **Dental crowns and bridges** (Co-Cr alloys, titanium) — for biocompatibility and precision.
3. **Jewelry** (gold, silver, platinum) — for intricate filigree patterns.
4. **Surgical instruments** (stainless steel 316L) — for sharp edges and complex shapes.
5. **Firearm triggers and receivers** (17-4 PH stainless steel) — for high strength and fine detail.
6. **Impellers for pumps and compressors** (bronze, stainless steel) — for smooth fluid flow passages.

---

### Question A — Option 1 (b) [Alternative 7 marks]
**(b) Compare Sand Casting, Die Casting, and Investment Casting on the basis of (i) mold material, (ii) tolerance achievable, (iii) surface finish, (iv) production rate, (v) typical applications, and (vi) cost per part.**

| Parameter | Sand Casting | Die Casting | Investment Casting |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mold material** | Silica sand + binder (clay/resin) | Hardened tool steel (H13) | Ceramic shell (alumina + silica) |
| **Tolerance (mm)** | ± 0.5 to ± 2.0 | ± 0.05 to ± 0.2 | ± 0.1 to ± 0.3 |
| **Surface finish Ra (μm)** | 6.3 – 25 | 0.8 – 3.2 | 0.8 – 3.2 |
| **Production rate (parts/hr)** | 5 – 20 | 200 – 1000 | 10 – 50 |
| **Typical applications** | Engine blocks, machine beds | Automotive housings, locks | Turbine blades, jewelry, implants |
| **Cost per part** | Low (small batches) | Very low (mass production) | High (low-to-medium volume) |

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the **Power Metallurgy (P/M) process**. List **five advantages** and **three limitations** of P/M.
**Marks: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**

**(a) Powder Metallurgy Process — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

1. **Powder Production:** Metal powders are produced by atomization (gas/water), reduction of oxides, electrolysis, or mechanical crushing. Typical particle size: 10–200 μm.
2. **Powder Blending:** Different powders (e.g., Fe + Cu + C + Ni) are blended in tumbling mills to achieve homogeneity. Lubricants (e.g., zinc stearate) may be added.
3. **Compaction:** The blended powder is poured into a hardened steel die and compacted at 200–700 MPa in a mechanical or hydraulic press. The powder is densified to ~80–90% theoretical density.
4. **Sintering:** The green compact is heated in a controlled-atmosphere furnace (N₂, H₂, or vacuum) at 70–90% of the metal's melting point for 30–120 minutes. Atomic diffusion bonds the particles, increasing strength and density to ~85–95%.
5. **Secondary Operations (Optional):** Re-pressing, sizing, coining, impregnation (oil/PTFE), heat treatment, machining, or surface finishing.

**Block Diagram (text representation for exam):**

```
Raw Material → Powder Production → Sieving & Classification
                                              ↓
Powder Blending & Lubrication → Compaction in Die → Green Compact
                                                              ↓
                            Sintering Furnace → Sintered Part
                                                              ↓
                          Secondary Operations → Finished Part
```

**Five Advantages of P/M (each 1 mark):**
1. **Near-net-shape manufacturing** — MUE > 95%, minimal machining.
2. **Self-lubricating parts** — Porous bearings retain oil (10–25% by volume).
3. **Complex geometries** — Internal flutes, threads, and back-grooves are possible.
4. **Wide material range** — Metals, alloys, ceramics, cermets, composites.
5. **Excellent dimensional control** — Tolerance ± 0.025 mm achievable.

**Three Limitations of P/M:**
1. **High initial tooling cost** — Dies are expensive (justified only for large volumes).
2. **Limited part size** — Maximum part size ~0.5 m × 0.5 m (press capacity constraint).
3. **Porosity reduces ductility and impact strength** — Not suitable for highly loaded dynamic parts.

---

**(b) A cylindrical P/M compact of diameter 25 mm and height 30 mm is sintered in a furnace. The compact shrinks uniformly by 2% in each dimension. Calculate the **final sintered dimensions** and the **percentage change in density** (assume mass is conserved).** (7 marks)

**Given:**
$$d_0 = 25 \text{ mm}, \quad h_0 = 30 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Shrinkage} = 2\% \text{ per dimension}$$

**Step 1 — Calculate Final Dimensions**

$$d_f = d_0 \times (1 - 0.02) = 25 \times 0.98 = 24.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$h_f = h_0 \times (1 - 0.02) = 30 \times 0.98 = 29.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Initial and Final Volumes**

$$V_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_0^2 \cdot h_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (25)^2 \times 30 = 14{,}726.2 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$V_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_f^2 \cdot h_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (24.5)^2 \times 29.4 = 13{,}855.4 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Initial and Final Densities (Mass Conserved)**

$$\rho_0 = \dfrac{m}{V_0}, \quad \rho_f = \dfrac{m}{V_f}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Density Ratio**

$$\dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_0} = \dfrac{V_0}{V_f} = \dfrac{14{,}726.2}{13{,}855.4} = 1.0628$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Change in Density**

$$\Delta \rho\, (\%) = (\rho_f / \rho_0 - 1) \times 100 = 6.28\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $d_f = 24.5 \text{ mm}$, $h_f = 29.4 \text{ mm}$, **Density increases by ≈ 6.28%** due to pore closure during sintering.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in "Applications" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Generic answers:** Writing "used in aerospace" without naming the **specific part** and **specific material** costs 2–3 marks. Always pair a process with **(a) the exact part name** and **(b) the exact material grade**.
>
> 2. **Confusing P/M and MIM:** Powder Metallurgy (P/M) is for *larger* parts with simple geometry (gears, bearings). **Metal Injection Molding (MIM)** is for *small* (< 100 g) parts with *complex* geometry (watch cases, brackets). Many students interchange them — this is a sure 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the heat-input unit conversion:** The factor **60** in the welding heat-input formula is to convert **seconds → minutes** of travel speed. Forgetting this is the #1 mistake in welding numericals.
>
> 4. **Not stating the MUE** when comparing processes: Examiners love to see numerical justification (e.g., "MUE of P/M ≈ 95% vs. MUE of sand casting ≈ 60%"). Students who skip this lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Mixing up "casting yield" and "MUE":** Casting yield uses the **mass poured**; MUE uses the **mass of raw material input (including risers, runners)**. Use the correct denominator.
>
> 6. **Diagram without labels:** A block diagram drawn without process names inside the boxes fetches **0 marks** for the diagram. Always label *each block* clearly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Applications**
>
> ✅ **Casting Applications:** Sand → large rough parts; Investment → complex precise parts; Die → mass-produced small parts; Centrifugal → cylindrical parts; Continuous → semi-finished stock.
>
> ✅ **Forging Applications:** Open-die → large simple shapes; Closed-die → small/medium complex parts; Cold heading → fasteners; Extrusion → constant cross-section profiles; Rolling → sheet/plate stock.
>
> ✅ **Sheet Metal Forming Applications:** Deep drawing → hollow cups/tanks; Hydroforming → complex hollow structural rails; Stretch forming → large contoured panels; Spinning → axisymmetric dishes.
>
> ✅ **Powder Metallurgy Applications:** Conventional P/M → gears, self-lubricating bushings; MIM → small complex watch/dental parts; Additive Mfg. → prototypes, custom implants, lattice structures.
>
> ✅ **Welding Applications:** Arc → shipbuilding, bridges; Spot → automotive body-in-white; Laser → battery, electronics; EBW → aerospace rocket nozzles; Brazing → carbide tool tips.
>
> ✅ **Polymer Processing Applications:** Injection molding → mass-produced housings, toys; Blow molding → bottles, containers; Extrusion → pipes, films, sheets; Thermoforming → disposable cups, trays.
>
> ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
> - MUE = (m_part / m_raw) × 100
> - Forging Reduction R = h₀ / h_f; True Strain ε = ln(h₀/h_f)
> - Drawing Ratio DR = D₀ / d_p (must be < LDR)
> - Welding Heat Input Q = (V·I·60) / (S·1000) kJ/mm
> - Shrinkage Allowance L_pattern = L_part × (1 + S/100)
>
> ✅ **Selection Criteria (must list 4 in any 3-mark question):** Material, Geometry, Volume, Quality, Cost, Equipment.
>
> ✅ **Key Industrial Numbers:**
> - Die casting production rate: 200–1000 parts/hr
> - Investment casting Ra: 0.8–3.2 μm
> - P/M MUE: ≥ 95%
> - Sand casting tolerance: ± 0.5 to ± 2.0 mm
> - Welding heat input limit for mild steel: ≤ 1.5 kJ/mm
